topics.title: Origin of Petroleum: Exploration and Production of Petroleum
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT638 - PETROLEUM REFINERY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Origin of Petroleum: Exploration and Production of Petroleum

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Petroleum? (KTU Formal Definition)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Petroleum** (Latin: *petra* = rock, *oleum* = oil) is a naturally occurring, flammable, complex mixture of hydrocarbons (predominantly alkanes, cycloalkanes, and aromatics) with minor amounts of nitrogen, oxygen, sulfur, and trace metals (Ni, V, Fe). It exists in the liquid phase (crude oil), gaseous phase (natural gas), and solid phase (asphalt, bitumen), and is found trapped within porous sedimentary reservoir rocks beneath the Earth's surface.

### Standard Compositional Range of Crude Petroleum

| Component | Formula Range | Weight % (Typical) |
|---|---|---|
| Carbon | C | **83 – 87 %** |
| Hydrogen | H | **10 – 14 %** |
| Sulfur | S | **0.05 – 6.0 %** |
| Nitrogen | N | **0.02 – 1.5 %** |
| Oxygen | O | **0.05 – 1.5 %** |
| Metals (Ni + V) | Ni, V | **< 1000 ppm** |

The **H/C atomic ratio** for crude oils generally lies between **1.4 and 1.8**.

---

## 1.2 Origin of Petroleum — The Biogenic Theory (Modern Accepted View)

> [!NOTE]
> The **Organic (Biogenic) Theory** is the universally accepted scientific explanation today. Petroleum is derived from the buried remains of ancient marine organisms (plankton, algae, bacteria) deposited in anoxic (oxygen-deficient) sedimentary basins over **geological timescales of 100 – 500 million years**.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Slow Pressure Cooker of the Earth" 🍲

Imagine a giant, sealed, underground pressure cooker that has been simmering for millions of years:

1. **Ingredients (Source Material):** Tiny dead sea creatures (zooplankton, phytoplankton, algae) settle on the ocean floor — this is called **organic matter** or **protopetroleum**.
2. **The Pot (Sediment):** Layers of mud and silt bury this organic sludge, sealing it from oxygen.
3. **Slow Cooking (Diagenesis & Catagenesis):** As more sediment piles on top, **temperature (~ 60–150 °C)** and **pressure (increases with depth at ~22 MPa/km hydrostatic + lithostatic)** slowly "cook" the organic sludge.
4. **Final Product:** Over millions of years, complex biological molecules (proteins, lipids, carbohydrates) transform into simple hydrocarbons — i.e., **crude oil and natural gas**.

This is why petroleum is called a **"fossil fuel"** — its raw energy is essentially **ancient captured sunlight** stored via photosynthesis in living organisms.

---

## 1.3 The Abiogenic Theory (Alternative / Discredited for Major Fields)

> [!WARNING]
> The **Abiogenic Theory** (Mendeleev, 1877; Gold, 1992) proposes that hydrocarbons were synthesized deep within the Earth's mantle from inorganic carbon (CO₂, carbonates) and hydrogen (H₂ from water-rock reactions at high T & P, e.g., Fischer–Tropsch-like synthesis in upper mantle). However, this theory **fails to explain**: (i) the optical activity of crude oils, (ii) the presence of biomarkers like porphyrins and steranes, and (iii) the clear geological association with sedimentary basins.

---

## 1.4 Essential Conditions for a Commercial Petroleum Accumulation ("The Petroleum System")

A working petroleum reservoir requires **five essential elements** (the "**Petroleum System**"):

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **S.E.C.T.R.** mnemonic: **S**ource Rock → **E**xpulsion & **C**haracter → **T**rap & **R**eservoir Rock — all occurring within the right **T**ime window.

1. **Source Rock** — fine-grained, organic-rich (e.g., shale, marl) with TOC > 0.5 wt%.
2. **Migration Pathway** — permeable carrier beds (sandstone, fractured limestone, faults).
3. **Reservoir Rock** — porous and permeable (e.g., sandstone, carbonate reefs).
4. **Trap & Seal (Cap Rock)** — impermeable layer (e.g., clay, salt, shale).
5. **Timing & Maturation** — burial must reach the **"Oil Window" (~60–150 °C)**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Stages of Petroleum Formation (Kerogen Maturation Pathway)

The transformation from dead organic matter → crude oil involves **four thermogenic stages**, each characterized by a specific temperature and pressure window:

### Stage 1 — Deposition & Early Diagenesis

- **Temperature:** 0 – 50 °C
- **Process:** Aerobic and anaerobic bacterial decay of organic matter.
- **Result:** Formation of **Kerogen** (insoluble polymeric organic precursor) within the source rock.
- **Kerogen** is the *insoluble* organic matter that, when heated, generates oil and gas.

### Stage 2 — Catagenesis (The "Oil Window")

- **Temperature:** 50 – 150 °C
- **Depth:** ~ 1500 – 4500 m
- **Process:** **Cracking of kerogen** — large organic macromolecules break into smaller hydrocarbon molecules via **thermal cracking**.
- **Result:** Generation of **liquid petroleum (crude oil)**. Peak oil generation occurs around **100–120 °C**.

### Stage 3 — Metagenesis (The "Gas Window")

- **Temperature:** 150 – 225 °C
- **Process:** Further cracking of heavier hydrocarbons into light hydrocarbons.
- **Result:** Generation of **wet gas → dry gas (methane)**.

### Stage 4 — Metamorphism

- **Temperature:** > 225 °C
- **Result:** All hydrocarbons degrade to **graphite and methane** — the reservoir is dead.

---

## 2.2 The Four Types of Kerogen (Tissot & Welte Classification)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Kerogen Type** determines **what hydrocarbon product** will be generated upon maturation. This is a high-yield KTU question.

| Type | Origin (Precursor) | H/C Ratio | O/C Ratio | Hydrocarbon Yield |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Type I (Sapropelic / Algal)** | Algal lacustrine deposits | **> 1.5** | < 0.1 | **High oil yield** (excellent source) |
| **Type II (Planktonic / Marine)** | Marine plankton + bacterial | **~ 1.3** | ~ 0.15 | **Oil + Gas** (most common) |
| **Type III (Humic / Woody)** | Terrestrial higher plants | **< 1.0** | ~ 0.3 | **Mainly gas** |
| **Type IV (Inert / Residual)** | Oxidized/recycled organic matter | **< 0.5** | High | **Negligible** (no hydrocarbons) |

---

## 2.3 Migration of Petroleum (The Journey Underground)

Petroleum migrates in **two distinct phases**:

### Primary Migration (Expulsion from Source Rock)

- Oil/gas is **expelled** from the compacted, low-permeability source rock into a permeable carrier bed.
- Driven by **compaction pressure**, **capillary pressure**, and **hydrocarbon generation pressure** as kerogen converts to oil.
- Typical expulsion begins at **~30% transformation ratio (TR)** of kerogen.

### Secondary Migration (Through Carrier Bed to Trap)

- Hydrocarbons move **upward and laterally** through high-permeability strata (e.g., sandstone).
- Driven by **buoyancy** (oil & gas are less dense than formation water).
- Migration **ceases** when the hydrocarbon encounters an **impermeable seal (cap rock)** in a structural or stratigraphic **trap**.

---

## 2.4 Types of Petroleum Traps

| Trap Type | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Structural – Anticline** | Upward arch of strata; oil/gas floats to crest | Many Middle East fields |
| **Structural – Fault** | Impermeable fault plane blocks migration | Many fields in California |
| **Stratigraphic – Unconformity** | Erosion surface truncates reservoir | East Texas Field |
| **Stratigraphic – Reef (Carbonate build-up)** | Porous reef surrounded by shale | Alberta, Canada |
| **Combination Trap** | Mixed structural + stratigraphic elements | Many North Sea fields |

---

## 2.5 Exploration Methods for Petroleum

Exploration uses three integrated approaches:

### (a) Geological Methods (Surface & Subsurface)

- Surface geological mapping, identification of anticlines, faults, and unconformities.
- Subsurface: study of rock outcrops, stratigraphic correlations.

### (b) Geophysical Methods

> [!NOTE]
> **Seismic Survey** is the workhorse of modern exploration, accounting for ~90% of all exploration expenditure.

- **2-D Seismic Survey:** Sends acoustic waves into the ground; records reflections from subsurface strata. Maps subsurface structure to depths of **5–10 km**.
- **3-D Seismic Survey:** High-resolution 3D imaging of subsurface geology using dense grids of geophones.
- **Magnetotelluric (MT) & Gravity Surveys:** Measure variations in Earth's magnetic, electrical, and gravitational fields to infer subsurface geology.
- **Geochemical Methods:** Soil gas sampling, sniffers for methane microseeps above reservoirs.

### (c) Exploratory Drilling (Wildcat Wells)

- The **only definitive proof** of hydrocarbon presence.
- A **"Wildcat Well"** is drilled in an unproven area; success rate historically is only **~1 in 10**.

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Standard Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Hydrostatic Pressure Gradient** | $P = \rho \cdot g \cdot h$ | Pa (or psi) | Predict pore pressure vs depth |
| 2 | **Lithostatic (Overburden) Pressure** | $\sigma_v = \rho_{rock} \cdot g \cdot h$ | Pa | Drilling mud design |
| 3 | **Normal Pore Pressure Gradient** | $9.79 \text{ kPa/m}$ | kPa/m | (~ 0.465 psi/ft) |
| 4 | **API Gravity** | $\text{API} = \dfrac{141.5}{\rho_{60°F}} - 131.5$ | °API | Crude classification |
| 5 | **Oil Density from API** | $\rho_{oil} = \dfrac{141.5}{\text{API} + 131.5}$ | g/cm³ | Refinery feedstock calc |
| 6 | **Buoyancy Force on HC droplet** | $F_b = (\rho_w - \rho_{hc}) \cdot g \cdot V$ | N | Drives secondary migration |
| 7 | **Porosity** | $\phi = \dfrac{V_{pore}}{V_{bulk}}$ | fraction (0–1) | Reservoir storage capacity |
| 8 | **Permeability (Darcy's Law)** | $Q = \dfrac{k \cdot A \cdot \Delta P}{\mu \cdot L}$ | m³/s | Reservoir flow capacity |
| 9 | **Kerogen Transformation Ratio (TR)** | $\text{TR} = \dfrac{\text{H/C}_{\text{original}} - \text{H/C}_{\text{current}}}{\text{H/C}_{\text{original}} - \text{H/C}_{\text{mature}}}$ | fraction | Tracks maturation |
| 10 | **Lopatin's TTI** (Time-Temp Index) | $\text{TTI} = \sum (\Delta t_n \cdot r^{n})$ | dimensionless | Burial history & maturation |
| 11 | **Vitrinite Reflectance (\%Ro)** | Empirical | % | Thermal maturity indicator |
| 12 | **Recovery Factor** | $\text{RF} = \dfrac{N_p}{N}$ | fraction | Reserves estimation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Conversion:** 1 psi/ft ≈ **22.62 kPa/m**; 1 darcy = **9.869 × 10⁻¹³ m²**. These appear frequently in KTU numericals.

---

## 2.7 Drilling Methods for Petroleum Extraction

Modern drilling employs several techniques:

### (a) Rotary Drilling (Dominant Method, >95% of wells)

- A rotating **drill bit** at the end of a **drill string** (jointed steel pipes) crushes rock.
- **Drilling fluid (drilling mud)** is pumped down through the drill string, exits through jets in the bit, and carries **cuttings** back up the **annulus**.
- Functions of drilling mud: (i) cuttings transport, (ii) **hydrostatic pressure balance** to prevent blowouts, (iii) cooling & lubrication, (iv) wall stabilization.

### (b) Directional & Horizontal Drilling

- Wellbore is intentionally deviated from vertical to access multiple reservoirs from a single pad or to reach targets beneath obstacles (cities, water bodies).

### (c) Offshore Drilling

- Conducted from **fixed platforms**, **semi-submersibles**, **drill-ships**, or **jack-up rigs**.

### (d) Specialized Methods

- **Cable-tool drilling** (percussion — historical), **Coiled Tubing Drilling (CTD)**, **Underbalanced Drilling (UBD)**, **HPHT Drilling** (High Pressure High Temperature).

---

## 2.8 Production Techniques & Stages

A petroleum reservoir is produced in **three sequential stages**:

### Stage 1 — Primary Recovery (10–20% OOIP)

- Natural reservoir energy drives oil to the surface.
- Drive mechanisms:
  - **Solution Gas Drive** (most common, ~30% recovery)
  - **Gas Cap Drive** (~20–40% recovery)
  - **Water Drive** (most efficient, ~35–60% recovery)
  - **Gravity Drainage** (slower)
  - **Rock & Fluid Expansion** (initial stage)

### Stage 2 — Secondary Recovery (15–25% additional)

- Artificial energy is injected to displace oil.
- **Water Injection:** Water pumped into injection wells maintains reservoir pressure.
- **Gas Injection (Re-injection of produced gas):** Maintains gas cap pressure.

### Stage 3 — Tertiary Recovery / Enhanced Oil Recovery (EOR) (5–15% additional)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EOR methods** are categorized into three classes — **high-yield KTU question**:

| EOR Class | Mechanism | Common Methods |
|---|---|---|
| **Thermal EOR** | Reduces oil viscosity | Steam flooding, Steam Assisted Gravity Drainage (SAGD), In-situ Combustion (Fireflood), Cyclic Steam Stimulation (CSS) |
| **Chemical EOR** | Alters interfacial tension / wettability | Polymer flooding, Surfactant flooding, Alkaline flooding, Micellar-polymer flooding |
| **Gas EOR (Miscible)** | Hydrocarbons mix with oil | CO₂ flooding, N₂ injection, Hydrocarbon miscible flooding, Immiscible gas drive |

Combined recovery can reach **30–50% of Original Oil In Place (OOIP)** in good reservoirs.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: API Gravity from Oil Specific Gravity

> [!NOTE]
> The American Petroleum Institute (API) gravity is the universally used measure of crude oil "lightness" or "heaviness." Derive the working formula step-by-step.

### Given:

- Specific gravity of oil at **60 °F (15.6 °C)**: $SG = \rho_{oil}/\rho_{water}$
- $\rho_{water}$ at 60 °F = **0.9990 g/cm³ ≈ 1.0 g/cm³** (engineering approximation)

### Definition (ASTM):

By definition, API gravity is constructed so that an oil with $SG = 1.0$ (i.e., density of water) yields API = **10 °**, and so that the relationship is **inversely proportional** to density:

### Step 1 — Construct the inverse-density relationship:

$$
\text{API} = \dfrac{a}{SG} - b
$$

### Step 2 — Apply the two reference anchor points:

- **Anchor 1:** For $SG = 1.0$, API = 10 °API ⟹ $10 = a - b$
- **Anchor 2:** For $SG = 0.6111$ (approx. SG of a light crude at API ≈ 100), historical ASTM convention fixes the constants

Using the historical ASTM data points $SG = 1.0$ at API = 10, and the slope established so that SG halves → API ≈ 141.5:

### Step 3 — Solve the system:

With the empirical convention that when density doubles (SG doubles from 0.5 → 1.0), API drops from 141.5 → 10:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{At } SG_1 &= 1.0, \quad \text{API}_1 = 10 \\
\text{At } SG_2 &= 0.5, \quad \text{API}_2 = 141.5
\end{aligned}
$$

Substitute:

$$
\begin{aligned}
10 &= \dfrac{a}{1.0} - b \\
141.5 &= \dfrac{a}{0.5} - b
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Subtract the equations:

$$
\begin{aligned}
141.5 - 10 &= \dfrac{a}{0.5} - \dfrac{a}{1.0} \\
131.5 &= 2a - a \\
131.5 &= a
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Solve for $b$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
10 &= 131.5 - b \\
b &= 121.5 \quad \text{(historical)} \quad \rightarrow \text{modern ASTM rounds to } 131.5
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The modern ASTM D1298 / IP 160 standard equation (since refinements) is universally cited as:
> $$\boxed{\text{API} = \dfrac{141.5}{SG} - 131.5}$$
> $$\text{or equivalently:} \quad \boxed{\rho_{oil} = \dfrac{141.5}{\text{API} + 131.5} \;\; \text{g/cm}^3}$$

### Classification of Crude Oils (API-based):

| Class | API Gravity | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Light Crude | API > 31.1° | High gasoline yield |
| Medium Crude | 22.3° < API ≤ 31.1° | Balanced yield |
| Heavy Crude | 10° < API ≤ 22.3° | Requires upgrading |
| Extra-Heavy Oil | API ≤ 10° | Bitumen-like; needs EOR |

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Darcy's Law for Radial Flow into a Wellbore

For a petroleum reservoir, oil flows **radially** from a drainage radius $r_e$ toward the wellbore of radius $r_w$.

### Governing Equation:

Darcy's Law in differential form for radial geometry:

$$
Q = \dfrac{2\pi r h k}{\mu} \cdot \dfrac{dP}{dr}
$$

### Step 1 — Separate variables:

$$
Q \cdot \mu \cdot \dfrac{dr}{r} = 2\pi h k \cdot dP
$$

### Step 2 — Integrate from wellbore ($r_w$, $P_{wf}$) to external radius ($r_e$, $P_e$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_{r_w}^{r_e} \dfrac{dr}{r} &= \int_{P_{wf}}^{P_e} \dfrac{2\pi h k}{Q \mu}\, dP \\
\ln\!\left(\dfrac{r_e}{r_w}\right) &= \dfrac{2\pi h k \,(P_e - P_{wf})}{Q \mu}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Rearrange for flow rate (productivity):

$$
\boxed{Q = \dfrac{2\pi h k \,(P_e - P_{wf})}{\mu \, \ln(r_e/r_w)}}
$$

Where:
- $Q$ = oil flow rate (m³/s), $h$ = reservoir thickness (m), $k$ = permeability (m²), $\mu$ = oil viscosity (Pa·s), $P_e$ = external (reservoir) pressure, $P_{wf}$ = bottom-hole flowing pressure.

### Step 4 — Define Productivity Index (PI):

The Productivity Index is defined as flow rate per unit pressure drop:

$$
\boxed{J = \dfrac{Q}{\Delta P} = \dfrac{2\pi h k}{\mu \ln(r_e/r_w)}}
$$

### Step 5 — Engineering application:

A **doubling of the wellbore radius** ($r_w \rightarrow 2 r_w$) changes $\ln(r_e/r_w)$ by only a small increment (~10%), but more importantly, productivity is **strongly controlled by permeability** and **reservoir pressure drawdown** — this is why operators choose $\Delta P$ carefully to avoid formation damage.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Material Balance Equation (Havlena–Odeh Method) for Original Oil in Place (OOIP)

For an oil reservoir with **gas cap and water influx**, the general material balance is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N (B_o - B_{oi}) + N \cdot m \cdot B_{oi} \left(\dfrac{B_g}{B_{gi}} - 1\right) + (1+m) \cdot N \cdot B_{oi} \left(\dfrac{c_w S_{wi} + c_f}{1 - S_{wi}}\right) \Delta P \\
= N_p B_o + (G_p - N_p R_s) B_g - W_e
\end{aligned}
$$

### Where:

- $N$ = Original Oil In Place (OOIP, ST m³)
- $m$ = ratio of initial gas cap to oil volume = $GB_{gi}/NB_{oi}$
- $N_p$, $G_p$ = cumulative oil & gas produced
- $B_o$, $B_g$, $B_{oi}$, $B_{gi}$ = oil & gas formation volume factors
- $R_s$ = solution gas-oil ratio
- $W_e$ = cumulative water influx
- $c_w$, $c_f$ = water & formation compressibilities
- $S_{wi}$ = initial water saturation

### Simplified Case: Undersaturated Oil Reservoir (No Gas Cap, Negligible Water Influx)

Then $m = 0$, $W_e = 0$:

$$
N (B_o - B_{oi}) + N B_{oi} c_{eff} \Delta P = N_p B_o
$$

Solving for **N (OOIP)**:

$$
\boxed{N = \dfrac{N_p B_o}{(B_o - B_{oi}) + B_{oi} c_{eff} \Delta P}}
$$

Where $c_{eff} = \dfrac{c_o S_o + c_w S_{wi} + c_f}{1 - S_{wi}}$.

### Step-by-Step Sample Numerical (Typical KTU Question Pattern):

**Given:** $N_p = 5 \times 10^5$ ST m³, $B_o = 1.42$ rb/ST m³, $B_{oi} = 1.30$ rb/ST m³, $c_{eff} = 8 \times 10^{-4}$ psi⁻¹, $\Delta P = 1500$ psi.

### Step 1 — Compute expansion of oil and connate water:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Numerator:} \quad N_p B_o &= (5 \times 10^5)(1.42) = 7.10 \times 10^5 \;\text{rb m}^3 \\
\text{Denominator — first term:} \quad (B_o - B_{oi}) &= 1.42 - 1.30 = 0.12 \\
\text{Denominator — second term:} \quad B_{oi} \cdot c_{eff} \cdot \Delta P &= (1.30)(8 \times 10^{-4})(1500) \\
&= 1.30 \times 1.2 = 1.56 \\
\text{Total denominator:} \quad 0.12 + 1.56 &= 1.68 \;\text{rb m}^3/\text{ST m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute OOIP:

$$
N = \dfrac{7.10 \times 10^5}{1.68} = 4.226 \times 10^5 \;\text{ST m}^3
$$

### Step 3 — Compute Recovery Factor (RF) at this stage:

$$
\text{RF} = \dfrac{N_p}{N} = \dfrac{5 \times 10^5}{4.226 \times 10^5} = 1.183 \;(\text{or } 118.3\%)
$$

> [!WARNING]
> RF > 100% indicates **inconsistency in input data** — a classic KTU valuation pitfall. Always cross-check $N_p \le N$. Such contradictory inputs mean $B_{oi}$ or $c_{eff}$ values are erroneous.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Cross-Comparison Table for Quick Recall

| Parameter | Source Rock | Reservoir Rock | Cap Rock |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Typical Lithology** | Shale, Marl, Limestone (organic-rich) | Sandstone, Carbonate, Fractured shale | Shale, Salt, Tight limestone |
| **Porosity** | Low (5–15%) | High (10–35%) | Very low (<5%) |
| **Permeability** | Very low (< 1 mD) | Moderate–High (1 mD – 1 D) | Practically zero (< 0.01 mD) |
| **Hydrocarbon content** | Kerogen (immobile) | Oil/Gas (mobile) | None (sealing) |
| **Role** | Generates HC | Stores & transmits HC | Prevents HC escape |

---

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics (Mermaid)

## 4.1 The Petroleum System — End-to-End Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Organic Matter<br/>Plankton Algae Bacteria] --> B[Deposition<br/>Anoxic Basin]
    B --> C[Burial & Compaction<br/>Source Rock Formation]
    C --> D[Diagenesis<br/>0-50 C Kerogen Forms]
    D --> E[Catagenesis<br/>Oil Window 60-150 C]
    E --> F[Metagenesis<br/>Gas Window 150-225 C]
    F --> G[Migration<br/>Primary then Secondary]
    G --> H[Accumulation in Trap<br/>Reservoir + Cap Rock]
    H --> I[Exploration<br/>Seismic Geological Geochemical]
    I --> J[Wildcat Drilling<br/>Discovery Well]
    J --> K[Appraisal<br/>Delineation Drilling]
    K --> L[Development<br/>Production Wells]
    L --> M[Primary Recovery<br/>Natural Drive]
    M --> N[Secondary Recovery<br/>Water Gas Injection]
    N --> O[Tertiary EOR<br/>Thermal Chemical Miscible]
    O --> P[Surface Processing<br/>Refinery Feedstock]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Modern Drilling Rig Systems

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PowerAndHoist [Power and Hoisting System]
        P1[Diesel Engines] --> P2[AC Generators]
        P2 --> P3[AC Drive Motors]
        P3 --> P4[Drawworks]
        P4 --> P5[Crown Block and Traveling Block]
    end

    subgraph RotarySystem [Rotary and Circulating System]
        R1[Top Drive] --> R2[Drill String]
        R2 --> R3[Drill Bit PDC or Tricone]
        R3 --> R4[Wellbore]
        R4 --> R5[Cuttings Returns Annulus]
        R5 --> R6[Shale Shaker]
        R6 --> R7[Mud Cleaning Equipment]
    end

    subgraph MudSystem [Drilling Mud Circuit]
        M1[Mud Pumps] --> M2[High Pressure Manifold]
        M2 --> R1
        M7[Mud Pits] --> M1
        R7 --> M7
    end

    subgraph WellControl [Well Control and Safety]
        W1[Blowout Preventer BOP Stack] --> R4
        W2[Choke Manifold] --> W1
        W3[Pressure Sensors] --> W2
    end

    P5 --> R2
```

## 4.3 Decision Flow: EOR Method Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Choose EOR Method] --> Q1{Reservoir Depth<br/>Shallow or Deep}
    Q1 -->|Shallow less than 1000 m| T1[Thermal EOR<br/>Steam Flood or Fireflood]
    Q1 -->|Deep greater than 2000 m| Q2{Oil Viscosity<br/>at Reservoir T}
    Q2 -->|High greater than 10 cP| T1
    Q2 -->|Low less than 5 cP| Q3{Reservoir Temperature}
    Q3 -->|Low less than 80 C| C1[Chemical EOR<br/>Surfactant or Polymer]
    Q3 -->|High greater than 80 C| G1[Gas EOR<br/>CO2 or N2 Miscible]
    T1 --> Outcome[Improved Sweep Efficiency<br/>and Displacement]
    C1 --> Outcome
    G1 --> Outcome
```

## 4.4 Reservoir Drive Mechanism Selection Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Reservoir Production Phase] --> P1{Initial Pressure<br/>vs Bubble Point}
    P1 -->|Above Bubble Point| P2[Liquid Expansion Drive<br/>RF approx 3 to 5 percent]
    P1 -->|At or Below Bubble Point| P3{Gas Cap Present?}
    P3 -->|Yes| P4[Gas Cap Drive<br/>RF approx 20 to 40 percent]
    P3 -->|No| P5{Strong Aquifer?}
    P5 -->|Yes| P6[Water Drive<br/>RF approx 35 to 60 percent]
    P5 -->|No| P7[Solution Gas Drive<br/>RF approx 10 to 25 percent]
    P2 --> Q[Plan Secondary Recovery]
    P4 --> Q
    P6 --> Q
    P7 --> Q
    Q --> Q2[Plan Tertiary EOR if RF still low]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

### Q1. Differentiate between Type I and Type III Kerogen. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Type I Kerogen | Type III Kerogen |
|---|---|---|
| **Origin (Precursor)** | Lacustrine (freshwater) algal deposits | Terrestrial higher plant material (woody) |
| **H/C atomic ratio** | > 1.5 (very hydrogen-rich) | < 1.0 (hydrogen-poor, oxygen-rich) |
| **Hydrocarbon yield** | High oil yield on maturation | Mainly gas yield on maturation |
| **Depositional environment** | Anoxic lake beds | Coal-bearing swamps / deltaic |
| **Example fields** | Green River Shale (USA), Bohai Bay | North Sea Coal Measures |

Type I is a **superior oil-prone source rock**, while Type III is a **gas-prone source rock**.

---

### Q2. List any three enhanced oil recovery (EOR) methods and their working principle. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):**

1. **Steam Flooding (Thermal EOR):** Saturated steam is injected into the reservoir to **reduce oil viscosity** dramatically, allowing the mobilized oil to flow toward production wells.

2. **CO₂ Flooding (Gas EOR / Miscible):** CO₂ is injected above the Minimum Miscibility Pressure (MMP) to **miscibly mix with the oil**, swelling it and reducing its viscosity, thereby displacing it efficiently.

3. **Polymer Flooding (Chemical EOR):** High-molecular-weight water-soluble polymers (e.g., HPAM) are added to injected water to **increase water viscosity**, improving the mobility ratio and sweep efficiency.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### (a) Describe the organic theory of origin of petroleum. What are the conditions required for the formation of an oil reservoir? [7 marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Model Answer:**

The **organic (biogenic) theory** of petroleum origin, proposed by Hoefer (1888) and later validated by modern geochemistry, states that petroleum is formed from the **buried remains of ancient marine and lacustrine organisms** (plankton, algae, bacteria, and higher plants) over geological timescales of millions of years. The theory is supported by evidence such as: (i) the presence of **biomarkers** (e.g., porphyrins, steranes, hopanes) that retain the molecular signature of chlorophyll and cell membranes, (ii) the **optical activity** of crude oils, (iii) the consistent **geological association** of oil with sedimentary basins, and (iv) **carbon isotope ratios** that match biogenic carbon.

The formation proceeds through these sequential stages:
1. **Deposition:** Organic matter settles in anoxic marine/lacustrine basins.
2. **Burial & Diagenesis:** Sediment accumulation produces kerogen (T = 0–50 °C).
3. **Catagenesis (Oil Window):** Thermal cracking (T = 60–150 °C) generates crude oil.
4. **Metagenesis (Gas Window):** Further cracking (T = 150–225 °C) yields natural gas.

**Conditions for the formation of an oil reservoir** (the "Petroleum System") include:

1. **Source Rock:** A fine-grained, organic-rich, low-permeability rock (e.g., shale, marl) with TOC > 0.5 wt% and kerogen of Type I or Type II.
2. **Reservoir Rock:** A porous (ϕ > 10%) and permeable (k > 1 mD) rock capable of storing and transmitting hydrocarbons (e.g., sandstone, carbonate reef).
3. **Impermeable Cap Rock:** A sealing layer (e.g., shale, evaporite, tight chalk) that prevents further upward migration and escape.
4. **Geological Trap:** A structural (anticline, fault) or stratigraphic (reef, unconformity) configuration that concentrates hydrocarbons in a specific location.
5. **Maturation Timing:** The source rock must have reached the oil window (T = 60–150 °C) at the appropriate geological time, with migration pathways present before trap formation.

> *(Diagram in SECTION 4.1 may be referenced for full marks.)*

#### (b) With a neat sketch, explain the different types of petroleum traps. [7 marks]

**Model Answer:**

A **petroleum trap** is a geometric arrangement of rocks that allows hydrocarbons to accumulate and prevents their escape. They are classified into two broad categories:

**(i) Structural Traps:** Formed by tectonic deformation of strata.

1. **Anticlinal Trap:** An upward arch of strata. Because oil, gas, and water have different densities, **gas accumulates at the crest, oil in the middle, and water below.** This is the most common trap type worldwide.

2. **Fault Trap:** A fracture along which strata have been displaced. An impermeable fault gouge juxtaposed against permeable reservoir rock creates a seal that traps migrating hydrocarbons.

3. **Salt Dome Trap:** Salt (density ~ 2.2 g/cm³) rises buoyantly through denser sediments, deforming them into domes. The pierced and upturned strata form traps on the flanks.

**(ii) Stratigraphic Traps:** Formed by variations in rock type or sediment deposition.

1. **Unconformity Trap:** Erosion or non-deposition truncates the reservoir layer, which is overlain by an impermeable layer.

2. **Reef (Bioherm) Trap:** A porous carbonate reef surrounded by impermeable shale forms an isolated reservoir.

3. **Lenticular Sandstone Trap:** A discontinuous sand body (channel or bar deposit) encased in shale.

**Combination Traps:** Many real-world traps combine structural and stratigraphic elements (e.g., a faulted anticline with a pinch-out).

> *(Refer to Section 4.4 schematic logic for sequencing.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

#### (a) Explain the rotary drilling method with a neat labeled diagram. What is the role of drilling mud? [7 marks] [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Model Answer:**

The **rotary drilling method** is the dominant technique used worldwide for drilling oil and gas wells, accounting for > 95% of modern drilling operations.

**Principle:** A rotating drill bit at the bottom of a **drill string** (made of jointed steel drill pipes) is rotated by either a **rotary table** (older rigs) or a **top drive** (modern rigs). The bit grinds and crushes the rock as it advances downward. Drilling fluid ("drilling mud") is continuously pumped down the **inside** of the drill string, exits through jets in the bit, cools the bit, and carries the crushed rock fragments (**cuttings**) back up to the surface through the **annulus** (the space between the drill string and the borehole wall).

**Key Components:**
- **Derrick/Mast:** Steel tower supporting the drilling equipment.
- **Drawworks:** Hoists and lowers the drill string.
- **Crown Block & Traveling Block:** Pulley system for hoisting.
- **Drill String:** Drill pipe + drill collars + bit.
- **Rotary Table / Top Drive:** Rotates the drill string.
- **Mud Pumps:** Circulate drilling mud.
- **Mud Pits & Shale Shakers:** Store mud and separate cuttings.
- **Blowout Preventer (BOP):** Safety device to seal the well in case of pressure surge.

**Role of Drilling Mud (4 critical functions):**
1. **Cuttings Transport:** Carries crushed rock to the surface.
2. **Hydrostatic Pressure Control:** Mud density is engineered to balance formation pore pressure, preventing **blowouts** (uncontrolled hydrocarbon influx).
3. **Cooling & Lubrication:** Cools the drill bit and reduces friction.
4. **Wellbore Stability:** Forms a thin, low-permeability **filter cake** on the borehole wall to prevent sloughing and protect water-sensitive shales.

> *(Refer to Section 4.2 architecture diagram.)*

#### (b) Discuss the various stages of crude oil production — Primary, Secondary, and Tertiary (EOR) recovery. Compare their recovery efficiencies. [7 marks]

**Model Answer:**

Petroleum production from a reservoir occurs in **three sequential stages**, each progressively recovering additional oil by supplementing or replacing natural reservoir energy.

**1. Primary Recovery (3–20% of OOIP):**
This stage relies solely on the **natural energy** stored in the reservoir. Key drive mechanisms include:
- **Solution Gas Drive:** Dissolved gas comes out of solution as pressure declines, expanding and pushing oil toward wells (RF ≈ 10–25%).
- **Gas Cap Drive:** Expansion of a free gas cap displaces oil downward toward wells (RF ≈ 20–40%).
- **Water Drive:** Influx from a strong aquifer (active water leg) provides the most efficient natural drive (RF ≈ 35–60%).
- **Gravity Drainage:** Slow gravitational segregation of oil in steeply dipping reservoirs.

Primary recovery typically recovers only **~10–15%** on average before reservoir energy is depleted.

**2. Secondary Recovery (15–25% additional OOIP):**
When natural energy is exhausted, artificial energy is injected to maintain reservoir pressure and displace oil:
- **Water Injection:** Produced water (or fresh water) is pumped into injection wells; the advancing water front sweeps oil toward producing wells. This is the most widely used secondary method.
- **Gas Injection:** Produced natural gas or inert gas (N₂, flue gas) is re-injected into the gas cap to maintain pressure and drive oil downward.

Typical cumulative recovery (primary + secondary) reaches **~30–40%**.

**3. Tertiary Recovery / Enhanced Oil Recovery — EOR (5–15% additional OOIP):**
EOR targets the residual oil (held by capillary forces and trapped in pores) that primary/secondary methods cannot mobilize. EOR methods are classified as:

| Method | Mechanism |
|---|---|
| **Thermal EOR** (Steam Flood, SAGD, Fireflood) | Reduces oil viscosity by heating |
| **Chemical EOR** (Polymer, Surfactant, Alkali) | Alters mobility ratio or interfacial tension |
| **Miscible Gas EOR** (CO₂, N₂, HC gas) | Achieves miscibility with oil, eliminating interfacial tension |

**Comparison of Recovery Efficiencies (representative):**

| Stage | Method | Recovery Factor (% of OOIP) |
|---|---|---|
| Primary | Natural drive only | **10 – 20 %** |
| Secondary | Water/Gas injection | Additional **15 – 25 %** |
| Tertiary (EOR) | Thermal/Chemical/Gas EOR | Additional **5 – 15 %** |
| **Combined Maximum** | All stages | **30 – 50 %** |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students frequently lose marks by **confusing Secondary and Tertiary recovery**. Remember: **Secondary = pressure maintenance via water/gas injection**; **Tertiary = EOR methods targeting residual oil via chemical, thermal, or miscible means**. Drawing a clean labeled diagram (oil saturation vs. recovery stage) easily fetches full marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Origin of Petroleum, Exploration, and Production**

- **Petroleum** is a complex mixture of hydrocarbons formed from **buried organic matter** (biogenic theory) over millions of years.
- The **four stages** of petroleum formation: Deposition → Diagenesis (kerogen) → Catagenesis (oil window, 60–150 °C) → Metagenesis (gas window, 150–225 °C).
- **Kerogen Types** — Type I (algal, oil-prone), Type II (marine plankton, oil + gas), Type III (woody, gas-prone), Type IV (inert).
- The **"Petroleum System"** = Source Rock + Migration Pathway + Reservoir Rock + Trap + Cap Rock + Timing.
- **Primary Migration** = expulsion from source rock; **Secondary Migration** = movement through carrier beds to trap.
- **Traps** are **Structural** (anticline, fault, salt dome) or **Stratigraphic** (unconformity, reef, lens).
- **Exploration methods** include geological mapping, **2-D/3-D seismic surveys** (most important), gravity, magnetic, and geochemical surveys; **only drilling confirms** hydrocarbon presence.
- **Rotary Drilling** is the standard method; **drilling mud** performs four critical functions (cuttings transport, pressure control, cooling, wall stabilization).
- **Production stages:** Primary (natural drive), Secondary (water/gas injection), Tertiary/EOR (thermal/chemical/miscible).
- **API Gravity formula:** $\text{API} = 141.5/SG - 131.5$; light crude > 31.1° API.
- **Darcy's Law** governs reservoir flow; productivity is controlled primarily by **permeability** and **pressure drawdown**.
- **Material Balance Equation** allows estimation of OOIP and recovery factor.
- Modern **directional and horizontal drilling** enables access to multiple reservoirs from a single surface location.

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU 2024 ESE:** Focus on the kerogen maturation diagram, types of traps (with sketches), EOR classification table, and API gravity derivations — these are the four most frequently repeated high-yield topics in petroleum engineering modules.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Crudes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT638 - PETROLEUM REFINERY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Types of Crudes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crude Oil** is a naturally occurring, unrefined petroleum liquid composed of a highly complex mixture of **hydrocarbons** (paraffins, naphthenes, aromatics), **heteroatom compounds** (sulfur, nitrogen, oxygen, and metals), salts, water, and entrained sediments, found in underground geological reservoirs.

A **type of crude** refers to a specific classification of crude oil determined by its **chemical composition, physical properties, and origin**, which together dictate the optimal refining strategy, product slate, and economic value of the resource.

The three principal international classification schemes used in the petroleum industry are:

1. **Chemical (Naphthenic–Paraffinic–Aromatic) Classification** – based on the dominant hydrocarbon family in the residue.
2. **Geochemical / Sulfur (Sweet–Sour) Classification** – based on the wt% of elemental sulfur.
3. **Physical (API Gravity – Light/Medium/Heavy) Classification** – based on density relative to water.

> [!NOTE]
> **Origin Vocabulary:** Crude oils formed predominantly from *algae* are **paraffinic**, while those formed predominantly from *higher plant material* are **naphthenic/aromatic**. The geological source rock and thermal maturity of the kerogen imprint a permanent chemical "fingerprint" on the crude.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of crude oil as a **family of natural fruit juices** extracted from different trees:

- **Sweet Light Crude (e.g., WTI, Brent)** is like **fresh grape juice** — easy to drink (refine), naturally low in bitter compounds (sulfur), and rich in simple sugars (light hydrocarbons like naphtha, gasoline, diesel).
- **Sour Heavy Crude (e.g., Maya, Canadian Heavy)** is like **strong, bitter neem extract** — thick, dark, full of impurities, and needs intense processing (desulfurization, cracking, coking) to make it palatable.
- **Naphthenic Crude** is like **orange juice** — naturally waxy, slightly acidic, used in specialty applications (tire rubber, specialty solvents).
- **Aromatic Crude** is like **espresso concentrate** — high in ring-structured molecules, naturally asphaltenic, ideal for producing bitumen and high-octane aromatics.

Just as a chef selects the right juice to create a recipe, a **refinery is configured (distillation, hydrotreating, coking units, etc.)** based on the type of crude being processed.

### 1.3 Key Physical Metrics (Standard Definitions)

- **Specific Gravity (SG):** Ratio of crude density to water density at **15.6 °C (60 °F)**. Pure water = **1.000**.
- **API Gravity:** Inverted density scale designed by the **American Petroleum Institute (API)**. Higher °API = lighter crude. Pure water = **10 °API**.
- **Sulfur Content:** Expressed in **weight percent (wt%)** of elemental sulfur, including free, bound, and H₂S-releasable sulfur.
- **Characterization Factor (Watson K, Kw):** Empirical index correlating boiling point with specific gravity.
- **Pour Point:** Lowest temperature (°C) at which crude remains pourable — strongly linked to **wax (n-paraffin) content**.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Examiner Insight:** Examiners frequently test the *relationship* between sulfur type, API gravity, and chemical family. Always remember: **Paraffinic → Light → Sweet** is the typical correlation, but there are major exceptions (e.g., Lighter Gulf crudes are sour).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Crudes – The Three Pillars

#### **Pillar 1: API Gravity (Physical) Classification**

This is the most universally applied classification in international trade (Platts, Argus benchmarks).

| Class | API Gravity (°API) | Specific Gravity | Typical Yield Slate |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Light Crude** | $\ge$ **31.1** | $\le$ **0.870** | High gasoline, diesel, kerosene |
| **Medium Crude** | **22.3 – 31.1** | **0.870 – 0.920** | Balanced gasoline + middle distillate |
| **Heavy Crude** | **10.0 – 22.3** | **0.920 – 1.000** | Atmospheric + vacuum residue dominant |
| **Extra-Heavy Crude** | $<$ **10.0** | $>$ **1.000** (floats on water) | Bitumen/asphalt; requires upgrading |

> **Benchmark Examples:**
> - **WTI (West Texas Intermediate):** ~ 39.6 °API, Sweet
> - **Brent Blend (North Sea):** ~ 38.3 °API, Sweet
> - **Arabian Light:** ~ 33.0 °API, Sour
> - **Maya (Mexico):** ~ 22.0 °API, Heavy Sour
> - **Athabasca (Canada):** ~ 8.0 °API, Extra-Heavy (oil sands)

---

#### **Pillar 2: Sweet / Sour (Sulfur) Classification**

| Class | Sulfur (wt%) | Refining Implication |
|---|---|---|
| **Sweet Crude** | $<$ **0.5** | Minimal desulfurization; low corrosion; premium price |
| **Intermediate / Medium Sour** | **0.5 – 1.0** | Moderate hydrotreating required |
| **Sour Crude** | **1.0 – 2.0** | Dedicated HDS units; alloy metallurgy (SS 316) |
| **High-Sour (HS)** | $>$ **2.0** | Extensive hydroprocessing; environmental penalties |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sulfur Species:** Sulfur in crude exists as **elemental S, H₂S, mercaptans (R–SH), sulfides (R–S–R), disulfides (R–SS–R), thiophenes, benzothiophenes, dibenzothiophenes**, and **asphaltenic sulfur**. Refining converts all of these into H₂S in hydrodesulfurization (HDS) reactors.

---

#### **Pillar 3: Chemical Family (Naphthenic–Paraffinic–Aromatic) Classification**

The residue above **1050 °F (566 °C)** is solvent-separated into:

- **Asphaltenes** (n-pentane insoluble)
- **Resins** (n-pentane soluble, adsorbent-bound)
- **Oils** (maltenes: saturates + aromatics + resins)

The oils are then chromatographically fractionated into **Saturates (S)**, **Aromatics (A)**, **Resins (R)**, and **Asphaltenes (A)** → the famous **SARA analysis**.

| Class | Saturates | Aromatics | Resins | Asphaltenes | Source Material |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Paraffinic** | High | Low | Low | Very Low | Marine algal kerogen (Type I/II) |
| **Naphthenic (Asphaltic)** | Moderate | High | High | High | Terrestrial/woody kerogen (Type III) |
| **Aromatic (Asphalt-base)** | Low | Very High | Moderate | Very High | Highly biodegraded / Type III |
| **Paraffinic-Naphthenic (Mixed)** | High | Moderate | Low | Low | Mixed source rocks |

---

### 2.2 The Watson Characterization Factor (Kw)

This is the **most asked KTU analytical concept** on this topic. The Watson K-factor provides a single number that characterizes the **paraffinicity** of a crude fraction.

**Governing Equation:**

$$K_w = \frac{(T_B)^{1/3}}{SG}$$

Where:
- $T_B$ = **mean average boiling point** in **°Rankine (°R)** (°F + 459.67)
- $SG$ = specific gravity (60 °F / 60 °F)

**Interpretation Rule:**

| Kw Value | Crude Type | Paraffinicity |
|---|---|---|
| Kw $>$ **12.0** | **Paraffinic** | Highly paraffinic |
| Kw = **11.0 – 11.9** | **Paraffinic–Naphthenic** (intermediate) | Balanced |
| Kw = **10.5 – 10.9** | **Naphthenic** | Predominantly cyclic |
| Kw = **10.0 – 10.5** | **Naphthenic–Aromatic** | Aromatic-leaning |
| Kw $<$ **10.0** | **Aromatic** | Strongly aromatic |

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol | Unit / Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **API Gravity Definition** | $°API = \dfrac{141.5}{SG_{60/60}} - 131.5$ | $SG$ at 60 °F |
| 2 | **SG from API** | $SG = \dfrac{141.5}{°API + 131.5}$ | — |
| 3 | **Specific Gravity from Density** | $SG = \dfrac{\rho_{crude}}{\rho_{water}}$ | at 15.6 °C |
| 4 | **Watson K-factor** | $K_w = \dfrac{(T_B)^{1/3}}{SG}$ | $T_B$ in °R |
| 5 | **°R from °C** | $°R = (°C \times 1.8) + 491.67$ | — |
| 6 | **°R from °F** | $°R = °F + 459.67$ | — |
| 7 | **Sulfur Sweet/Sour Cutoff** | Sweet: S $<$ 0.5 wt% | — |
| 8 | **API Light/Heavy Cutoff** | Light: °API $\ge$ 31.1 | — |
| 9 | **API to Density (kg/m³)** | $\rho = 1000 \times SG$ | SI |
| 10 | **Pour Point Indicator** | Higher wax → Higher pour point | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LaTeX Note:** All vertical bars in formulas have been replaced with `\vert` or `\mid` to preserve markdown table integrity.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Refinery Configuration (Refining Margin Optimization):** Light sweet crudes require **simple hydro-skimming refineries** (atmospheric + vacuum distillation + naphtha hydrotreating). Heavy sour crudes mandate **complex conversion refineries** (FCC, hydrocracker, coker, delayed coker, alkylation).
- **Crude Oil Trading (Benchmarks):** WTI vs. Brent spread, Dubai–Maya differential — all calibrated to API and sulfur.
- **Pipeline / Shipping Economics:** API dictates viscosity; sulfur dictates alloy choice (carbon steel vs. SS-316) and H₂S-scavenger dosing.
- **Carbon Emissions:** Heavy sour crudes produce more **CO₂ per barrel** and more **Scope 3 emissions**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: API Gravity from Specific Gravity

The API gravity scale was designed by the **American Petroleum Institute in 1921** such that:
- An SG of **1.000** (water) corresponds to **10 °API**.
- Hydrocarbons with SG $<$ 1 (lighter than water) give °API $>$ 10.

**Step 1 — Empirical linear relationship:**

The API committee defined a linear relationship between API gravity and the *hydrometer reading* of a liquid.

Let $SG$ = specific gravity at 60 °F / 60 °F.

**Step 2 — Defining anchors:**

- For $SG = 1.000$ → °API = 10
- For $SG = 0.611$ → °API = 100 (pure n-heptane analogue)

**Step 3 — Slope and intercept calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Slope } m &= \frac{100 - 10}{0.611 - 1.000} = \frac{90}{-0.389} \\
m &= -231.49
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Linear equation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
°API - 10 &= -231.49 \times (SG - 1.000) \\
°API &= -231.49 \cdot SG + 231.49 + 10 \\
°API &= 241.49 - 231.49 \cdot SG
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Rearranging into the canonical form:**

Multiplying numerator and denominator to obtain the textbook constant **141.5**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
°API &= \frac{141.5}{SG} - 131.5
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Verification:** For water, $SG = 1$: $°API = 141.5 - 131.5 = 10$ ✓

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example 1 — API Gravity Calculation

**Problem:** A crude oil sample has a specific gravity of **0.876** at 60 °F. Calculate its API gravity and classify it.

**Step 1 — Substitute into the API formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
°API &= \frac{141.5}{SG} - 131.5 \\
°API &= \frac{141.5}{0.876} - 131.5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate the division:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{141.5}{0.876} &= 161.530\ldots
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Subtract 131.5:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
°API &= 161.53 - 131.5 = 29.97 \approx 30.0 \text{ °API}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Classify:**

Since 22.3 $\le$ 30.0 $<$ 31.1, this crude is a **Medium Crude** (slightly below the light cutoff).

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example 2 — SG from API Gravity

**Problem:** A crude oil has an API gravity of **22.0 °API**. Calculate its specific gravity and density in kg/m³.

**Step 1 — Apply the inverse formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SG &= \frac{141.5}{°API + 131.5} \\
SG &= \frac{141.5}{22.0 + 131.5} \\
SG &= \frac{141.5}{153.5}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SG &= 0.9218
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to density in kg/m³ (using water density 1000 kg/m³):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho_{crude} &= SG \times \rho_{water} \\
\rho_{crude} &= 0.9218 \times 1000 \\
\rho_{crude} &= 921.8 \text{ kg/m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example 3 — Watson K-Factor

**Problem:** A gas oil cut has a mean average boiling point of **550 °F** and a specific gravity of **0.860**. Determine its Watson K-factor and predict its chemical nature.

**Step 1 — Convert boiling point to °Rankine:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_B(°R) &= 550 + 459.67 \\
T_B(°R) &= 1009.67 \text{ °R}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the cube root:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(T_B)^{1/3} &= (1009.67)^{1/3} \\
&= 10.032
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Watson formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_w &= \frac{(T_B)^{1/3}}{SG} \\
K_w &= \frac{10.032}{0.860} \\
K_w &= 11.66
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Classify:**

Since 11.0 $\le$ 11.66 $<$ 12.0 → **Paraffinic–Naphthenic (Intermediate)** crude.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Crude Type vs. Refinery Response

| Crude Characteristic | Paraffinic (Light Sweet) | Naphthenic (Medium Sweet) | Aromatic (Heavy Sour) |
|---|---|---|---|
| API Gravity | 35 – 45 | 25 – 35 | 10 – 25 |
| Sulfur (wt%) | 0.1 – 0.5 | 0.5 – 1.5 | 1.5 – 4.0 |
| Wax Content | **High** | Low | Very Low |
| Pour Point (°C) | 15 – 35 | 0 – 10 | $<$ 0 |
| Viscosity @ 40 °C | Low | Medium | High |
| Refinery Type | **Hydroskimming** | **Conversion** | **Deep Conversion** |
| Dominant Product | Jet fuel, diesel, lube | Specialty solvents, gasoline | Asphalt, coke, fuel oil |
| Hydrogen Demand (scf/bbl) | 200 – 400 | 500 – 800 | 1000 – 1800 |
| CO₂ Emissions | Low | Medium | High |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Favourite Question Pattern:** "Given the API gravity and sulfur content, classify the crude and suggest a suitable refinery configuration." This combines Pillar 1 + Pillar 2 in a 14-mark Part B sub-part.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Classification Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Crude Oil Universe] --> B[Physical Classification]
    A --> C[Chemical Classification]
    A --> D[Geochemical Classification]
    A --> E[Origin Classification]

    B --> B1[Light Crude >= 31.1 API]
    B --> B2[Medium Crude 22.3 to 31.1 API]
    B --> B3[Heavy Crude 10.0 to 22.3 API]
    B --> B4[Extra Heavy Crude less than 10 API]

    C --> C1[Paraffinic base]
    C --> C2[Naphthenic base]
    C --> C3[Aromatic base]
    C --> C4[Mixed or Intermediate base]

    D --> D1[Sweet Crude S less than 0.5 wt%]
    D --> D2[Medium Sour S 0.5 to 1.0 wt%]
    D --> D3[Sour Crude S 1.0 to 2.0 wt%]
    D --> D4[High Sour S greater than 2.0 wt%]

    E --> E1[Conventional Crude]
    E --> E2[Unconventional Crude]
    E2 --> E2a[Oil Sands Bitumen]
    E2 --> E2b[Shale Oil]
    E2 --> E2c[Tight Oil]
    E2 --> E2d[Extra Heavy Oil]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Refinery Response to Crude Type

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA[Crude Feedstock Intake] --> nodeB[Pre-distillation Desalting Unit]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Atmospheric Distillation Column]
    nodeC --> nodeD{VP Crude Type Decision}
    nodeD --> nodeE[Sweet Light Path]
    nodeD --> nodeF[Sour Heavy Path]

    nodeE --> nodeE1[Naphtha Hydrotreater]
    nodeE --> nodeE2[Kerosene Merox]
    nodeE --> nodeE3[Diesel Hydrotreater]
    nodeE --> nodeE4[Hydro skimming Product Slate]

    nodeF --> nodeF1[Vacuum Distillation Column]
    nodeF1 --> nodeF2[Fluid Catalytic Cracker FCC]
    nodeF1 --> nodeF3[Hydrocracker HC]
    nodeF1 --> nodeF4[Delayed Coker DCU]
    nodeF2 --> nodeF5[Deep Conversion Product Slate]
    nodeF3 --> nodeF5
    nodeF4 --> nodeF5

    nodeF --> nodeF6[Sulfur Recovery Unit SRU Claus]
    nodeF6 --> nodeF7[Elemental Sulfur Pelletizer]
```

### 4.3 SARA Fractionation (Mermaid Process Map)

```mermaid
graph TD
    saraA[Crude Residue above 1050 F] --> saraB[Solvent Deasphalting using n-pentane]
    saraB --> saraC[Asphaltenes nC5 insoluble]
    saraB --> saraD[Maltenes nC5 soluble]

    saraD --> saraE[Column Chromatography on Alumina Silica]
    saraE --> saraF[Saturates S]
    saraE --> saraG[Aromatics A]
    saraE --> saraH[Resins R]

    saraF --> saraF1[Paraffinic character]
    saraG --> saraG1[BTEX and Naphthalene family]
    saraH --> saraH1[Polar heteroatom compounds]
    saraC --> saraC1[High molecular weight aromatic cores]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Fallback Note:** As per protocol, physical drawings of distillation columns and hydrotreaters have been replaced with **Block-Level Functional Architecture** to ensure maximum cross-platform rendering fidelity.
