## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)**

#### **Question 1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022] — **3 Marks**
**Define API gravity. A crude has an SG of 0.920. Calculate its API gravity and classify it.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **API Gravity Definition:** API gravity is an inverse measure of a petroleum liquid's density relative to water, expressed in degrees, designed by the American Petroleum Institute. Pure water = **10 °API**. Higher °API = lighter crude.

**Calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
°API &= \frac{141.5}{0.920} - 131.5 \\
°API &= 153.80 - 131.5 \\
°API &= 22.30 \text{ °API}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Classification:** Lies exactly at the **Medium / Heavy boundary (22.3 °API)**. Since 22.3 falls within 22.3–31.1, it is a **Medium Crude**. If the student reads the value as marginally above or below 22.3, mention both possibilities.

---

#### **Question 2.** [KTU University Exam – June 2023] — **3 Marks**
**Differentiate between sweet and sour crude oil with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Sweet Crude | Sour Crude |
|---|---|---|
| Sulfur content (wt%) | $<$ **0.5** | $\ge$ **0.5** (often 1.0 – 4.0) |
| H₂S evolution | Negligible | Significant |
| Refining cost | Lower (no deep HDS) | Higher (HDS + Claus + SS metallurgy) |
| **Example** | **Brent (North Sea)**, WTI, Bonny Light | **Arabian Heavy**, Maya, UAE Murban |

> **Mnemonic:** **"Sweet"** = sugar in tea = pleasant = low sulfur. **"Sour"** = rotten egg (H₂S) smell = high sulfur.

---

### **PART B — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice Questions**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) – Option 1** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **chemical (Naphthenic–Paraffinic–Aromatic) classification** of crude oils. Highlight the geological origin of each type. **— 7 Marks**

**(b)** Two crude samples are analyzed. Sample **A**: SG = 0.85, mean boiling point = 580 °F. Sample **B**: SG = 0.95, mean boiling point = 850 °F. Compute the **Watson K-factor** for both and classify them. **— 7 Marks**

---

**Model Solution to (a):**

The chemical classification of crudes is based on the **predominant hydrocarbon family** in the high-boiling residue (above 1050 °F or 566 °C). The classical categories are:

**(i) Paraffinic Base Crude:**
- High content of straight-chain and branched alkanes (n-C₁ to n-C₆₀+).
- **High wax content** → high pour point.
- Low sulfur, low nitrogen, low asphaltenes.
- **Origin:** Marine algal (Type I/II) kerogen, deposited in anoxic environments (e.g., North Sea, Gulf of Mexico).
- **Examples:** Pennsylvania crude, Brent, WTI.
- **Products:** Excellent lube base oils, high cetane diesel, jet fuel.

**(ii) Naphthenic (Asphaltic) Base Crude:**
- High cycloalkane (cyclopentane, cyclohexane) and alkyl-aromatic content.
- **Low wax** → very low pour point.
- **Moderate sulfur**, more resins and asphaltenes than paraffinic.
- **Origin:** Terrestrial woody / herbaceous kerogen (Type III) and bacterial reworking.
- **Examples:** Iranian Heavy, Venezuelan Jobo, California crudes.
- **Products:** Specialty solvents, low-pour-point lubricants, electrical insulating oils.

**(iii) Aromatic Base Crude:**
- Very high **BTEX, naphthalenes, and polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon (PAH)** content.
- **High asphaltene** content → high viscosity, low API.
- **High sulfur and metals** (Ni, V).
- **Origin:** Highly biodegraded marine or lacustrine crudes; deep reservoir alteration.
- **Examples:** Venezuelan Boscan, Mexican Maya, Athabasca bitumen (when upgraded).
- **Products:** Asphalt, petroleum coke, specialty aromatic chemicals (BTX feed).

**(iv) Mixed / Intermediate Base Crude:**
- Balanced proportions of all three families.
- Most common commercial crudes fall here.
- **Examples:** Arabian Light, Urals (Russia), Mumbai High (India).

**SARA Linkage:** Modern refiners replace this classical classification with the **SARA (Saturates–Aromatics–Resins–Asphaltenes)** quadrant analysis, which quantitatively defines the four fractions by solvent separation and chromatography.

---

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Step 1 — Convert boiling points to °Rankine:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{B,A} &= 580 + 459.67 = 1039.67 \text{ °R} \\
T_{B,B} &= 850 + 459.67 = 1309.67 \text{ °R}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute cube roots:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(1039.67)^{1/3} &= 10.113 \\
(1309.67)^{1/3} &= 10.940
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Watson K formula for Sample A:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_{w,A} &= \frac{10.113}{0.85} = 11.90
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Watson K formula for Sample B:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_{w,B} &= \frac{10.940}{0.95} = 11.52
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Classification:**

| Sample | Kw | Class |
|---|---|---|
| A | **11.90** | **Paraffinic–Naphthenic (Intermediately paraffinic)** — borderline paraffinic |
| B | **11.52** | **Paraffinic–Naphthenic (Naphthene-leaning)** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Question A):** Students often **forget the +459.67 conversion** from °F to °R and use °F directly. This gives a numerically similar cube root but is *conceptually wrong* and **loses 1 mark** for unit error. Also, students frequently misreport the final Kw boundary — remember Kw $\ge$ 12.0 is required for a "pure paraffinic" classification, not 11.5.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) – Option 2** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Compare the properties of **Light Sweet, Medium Sour, and Heavy Sour** crude oils. How do these differences dictate the **type of refinery configuration** required to process each? **— 7 Marks**

**(b)** A crude oil is found to have **API gravity = 25** and **sulfur = 1.8 wt%**. Identify its class and recommend an appropriate **hydroprocessing severity**. **— 7 Marks**

---

**Model Solution to (a):**

**Comparison Table:**

| Property | Light Sweet (e.g., WTI) | Medium Sour (e.g., Arabian Medium) | Heavy Sour (e.g., Maya) |
|---|---|---|---|
| API Gravity | 38 – 45 | 28 – 33 | 10 – 22 |
| Sulfur (wt%) | 0.05 – 0.4 | 1.0 – 2.0 | 2.5 – 5.5 |
| Metals (Ni + V, ppm) | $<$ 5 | 10 – 50 | 100 – 600 |
| Residue yield (vol%) | 20 – 30 | 35 – 45 | 50 – 65 |
| Gasoline yield (vol%) | High | Moderate | Low |
| Pour Point | High (waxy) | Moderate | Low |

**Refinery Configuration Mapping:**

- **Light Sweet → Hydroskimming Refinery:** Atmospheric + vacuum distillation, naphtha reformer, diesel hydrotreater, kerosene Merox. No FCC, no coker.
- **Medium Sour → Conversion Refinery:** Adds fluid catalytic cracker (FCC), mild hydrocracker, amine regeneration, sulfur recovery (Claus unit). Hydrotreating across all distillates.
- **Heavy Sour → Deep Conversion Refinery:** Adds vacuum residue hydrocracker, delayed coker, visbreaker, asphalt oxidation, high-pressure HDS, ebullated-bed LC-Fining reactors, and **H₂ production via SMR (steam methane reforming)** with sulfur-tolerant metallurgy.

> The **crude type sets the CAPEX** of the refinery. A 300,000-bpd heavy-sour refinery may cost **2.5× more** than a light-sweet refinery of the same capacity.

---

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Step 1 — API gravity classification:**

Since 22.3 $\le$ 25.0 $<$ 31.1, the crude is a **Medium Crude**.

**Step 2 — Sulfur classification:**

Since 1.0 $\le$ 1.8 $<$ 2.0, the crude is **Sour** (approaching high-sour).

**Step 3 — Combined classification:**

→ **Medium Sour Crude** (e.g., similar to UAE Murban or Iranian Heavy).

**Step 4 — Recommended hydroprocessing severity:**

| Unit | Severity | Operating Pressure |
|---|---|---|
| Naphtha Hydrotreater | Moderate (sulfur strip) | 30 – 40 bar |
| Diesel HDS | High (ULSD-grade) | 60 – 80 bar |
| Atmospheric Residue Desulfurization (ARDS) | High | 100 – 150 bar |
| Sulfur Recovery (Claus + Tail Gas) | **Mandatory** (S recovery) | — |
| Hydrogen Plant (SMR) | Required (high H₂ demand) | — |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Question B):** A common mistake is to classify a crude solely on **one parameter** (e.g., only API). Always state **both** the API and sulfur class. Another trap: confusing **hydrodesulfurization (HDS)** with **hydrocracking (HCK)**. HDS removes S/N/O without breaking C–C bonds; HCK *also* cracks the molecule into lighter products.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Must Memorize for KTU Board Exams):**

- **Three Pillars of Crude Classification:** API gravity, Sulfur content, Chemical family.
- **API Formula:** $°API = 141.5/SG - 131.5$ — committed to memory verbatim.
- **Cutoffs:**
  - Light: °API $\ge$ 31.1
  - Medium: 22.3 – 31.1
  - Heavy: 10 – 22.3
  - Extra Heavy: $<$ 10
- **Sweet:** S $<$ 0.5 wt%; **Sour:** S $\ge$ 0.5 wt%.
- **Watson K-factor formula:** $K_w = (T_B[°R])^{1/3} / SG$.
  - Kw $>$ 12 → Paraffinic
  - Kw 11–12 → Paraffinic–Naphthenic
  - Kw 10–11 → Naphthenic
  - Kw $<$ 10 → Aromatic
- **SARA Analysis:** Saturates – Aromatics – Resins – Asphaltenes.
- **Origin:** Paraffinic → Marine algae; Naphthenic/Aromatic → Terrestrial plants.
- **Benchmarks:** WTI (~ 39.6 °API, sweet), Brent (~ 38.3 °API, sweet), Maya (~ 22 °API, heavy sour), Athabasca (~ 8 °API, extra heavy).
- **Refinery Type → Crude Type:** Light sweet → Hydroskimming; Medium sour → Conversion; Heavy sour → Deep Conversion.
- **°R = °F + 459.67**; **°R = (°C × 1.8) + 491.67**.
- **Unit Traps:** Always convert °F → °R before applying Watson K.

> **Common 1-Mark KTU Quickies:**
> 1. "Sweetest crude among WTI, Brent, Maya, Athabasca" → **WTI**.
> 2. "Heaviest crude on the API scale among the four" → **Athabasca**.
> 3. "Aromatic crude example" → **Maya / Boscan**.
> 4. "Watson K of a waxy crude" → $>$ 12.
> 5. "Pure water API gravity" → **10 °API**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Composition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT638 - PETROLEUM REFINERY ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Composition ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Composition of Crude Oil / Petroleum** refers to the complete qualitative and quantitative description of the chemical constituents of a crude oil sample, expressed in terms of **(a) elemental composition** (C, H, S, N, O, and trace metals), **(b) hydrocarbon-type composition** (paraffinic, naphthenic, aromatic, and olefinic species), and **(c) fractional / SARA composition** (distillation cuts and the Saturates–Aromatics–Resins–Asphaltenes distribution). It is the foundational parameter upon which every downstream refining configuration (distillation, cracking, hydrotreating) is selected. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Fruit-Cake Analogy" Think of a barrel of crude oil as a **giant fruit-cake baked by nature over millions of years**: - The **flour base** (≈ 83–87 %) is **Carbon (C)** — the structural skeleton. - The **sugar and water** (≈ 11–14 %) is **Hydrogen (H)** — the "sweetener" that decides how much gasoline-yielding light molecules are present. - The **spices and salt** are the **heteroatoms (S, N, O)** — small in quantity, but they decide the cake's taste (sweet = low S, sour = high S). - The **dry-fruit chunks** are the **metals (Ni, V, Fe)** — present only in traces (ppm), but they poison the catalysts if not removed. - The **texture** (chunks of nuts vs. raisins vs. cake-soft part) is the **hydrocarbon-type composition** — paraffins, naphthenes, aromatics. Just as a baker must know the recipe before deciding the oven temperature, a refinery engineer **must know the composition of the crude before designing the refinery flow scheme**. ### 1.3 Standard Reference Metrics Used in KTU Examinations > [!NOTE] > **Key metrics highlighted for KTU Board Exam:** > - **API Gravity (°API)** → measure of crude "lightness". Higher °API ⇒ lighter, more paraffinic crude. > - **Specific Gravity (SG)** → ratio of density of oil to density of water at **60 °F (15.6 °C)**. > - **Watson Characterization Factor (K)** → classifies crude as paraffinic, naphthenic, or aromatic. > - **H/C Atomic Ratio** → indicator of aromaticity; lower value ⇒ more aromatic. > - **Sulfur Content (wt %)** → "sweet" (< 0.5 %) vs. "sour" (> 0.5 %). --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Three Layers of Petroleum Composition Petroleum composition is **always described in three orthogonal layers**, and KTU questions may test any one or all three: **Layer A — Elemental (Ultimate) Composition** Crude oil is essentially a hydrocarbon mixture with dissolved heteroatoms. | Element | Typical Range (wt %) | Practical Significance | |---|---|---| | Carbon (C) | **83 – 87** | Forms the C–C backbone; governs yield of residue vs. distillates | | Hydrogen (H) | **10 – 14** | Higher H ⇒ more paraffins ⇒ higher gasoline yield | | Sulfur (S) | **0.1 – 5.0** | Decides whether hydrodesulfurization (HDS) is mandatory | | Nitrogen (N) | **0.1 – 1.0** | Causes catalyst poisoning in FCC & hydrocracking | | Oxygen (O) | **0.1 – 1.5** | Forms naphthenic acids → corrosion in distillation overheads | | Metals (Ni, V, Fe, Na) | **< 1000 ppm** | Ni & V deactivate FCC catalyst → need hydrodemetallization (HDM) | **Layer B — Hydrocarbon-Type Composition (Chemical Families)** These are the four molecular families that make up almost 100 % of crude (olefins being negligible in crude but significant in cracked products): | Family | General Formula | Structure Sketch | Remarks | |---|---|---|---| | **Paraffins (Alkanes)** | $C_{n}H_{2n+2}$ | Straight or branched, no rings | Highest H/C; premium gasoline component | | **Naphthenes (Cycloalkanes)** | $C_{n}H_{2n}$ | Saturated rings (1, 2, 3 rings) | Good diesel & jet fuel; moderate H/C | | **Aromatics** | $C_{n}H_{2n-6}$ (benzene series) | Benzene ring(s) | High octane; low in crude, high in cracked streams | | **Olefins (Alkenes)** | $C_{n}H_{2n}$ | C=C double bond | Negligible in crude; formed in FCC/coking | **Layer C — Fractional & SARA Composition** By ASTM D2892 / D5236 (TBP distillation), crude is sliced into **narrow boiling-range fractions**. Separately, by solvent chromatography, the **SARA analysis** quantifies: | Fraction | Solvent Used | Polarity | Polarity is | |---|---|---|---| | **Saturates** | n-Heptane eluate | Non-polar | Aliphatic + cycloparaffins | | **Aromatics** | Toluene eluate | Mildly polar | BTEX, alkylbenzenes, polyaromatics | | **Resins** | Methanol/Toluene eluate | Polar | N-, S-, O-containing aromatics | | **Asphaltenes** | n-Heptane insoluble | Highly polar polycyclic | Largest, most refractory molecules | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why this matters in engineering:** The **asphaltene content alone** decides whether a refinery should include a **solvent deasphalting (SDA)** unit. A high-asphaltene crude (e.g., Maya, Mexican) requires deep conversion (coking or hydrocracking), while a low-asphaltene paraffinic crude (e.g., North Sea Brent) can be processed in simple distillation + hydrotreating only. ### 2.2 Why & How — The Causal Logic Behind Each Parameter - **Why measure H/C ratio?** Because every refinery process (cracking, hydrotreating) **adds or removes hydrogen**. Knowing the starting H/C tells the engineer how much external H₂ must be supplied. - **Why SARA?** Because **physical separation** (distillation) cannot split aromatics from resins cleanly. SARA tells the refiner how much of the crude is convertible to clean fuels vs. stuck as residue. - **Why Watson K?** Because a single number allows an engineer to **classify** a crude without a full PIONA analysis. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Master these 10 equations — they cover ≈ 90 % of KTU numerical questions on Composition.** | # | Equation | Variables | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $°API = \dfrac{141.5}{SG_{60/60°F}} - 131.5$ | $SG$ = specific gravity | Dimensionless | | 2 | $SG = \dfrac{\rho_{oil}}{\rho_{water}}$ at 60 °F | $\rho$ = density | g/cm³ or kg/m³ | | 3 | $Watson\;K = \left(\dfrac{T_B, °R}{SG}\right)^{1/3}$ | $T_B$ = mean average boiling point | °Rankine (°F + 460); K = 10–13 (paraffinic), 9.5–11 (naphthenic) | | 4 | $H/C\;(atomic) = \dfrac{(H\,wt\,\%/1.008)}{(C\,wt\,\%/12.01)}$ | wt % from ultimate analysis | Dimensionless | | 5 | $C_{x}H_{y} \Rightarrow x = \dfrac{C\%}{12.01},\quad y = \dfrac{H\%}{1.008}$ | Then divide by smallest to get empirical ratio | For combustion / stoichiometry problems | | 6 | $M_{avg} = \dfrac{\sum m_i\,M_i}{\sum m_i}$ | Mixture of fractions | kg/kmol or g/mol | | 7 | $\eta_{i}\,(\text{vol}\,\%) = \dfrac{V_i}{\sum V_i}\times 100$ | Volume fraction | Used in TBP analysis | | 8 | $y_i = \dfrac{P_i^{sat}}{P}$ (Raoult's law, light ends) | $y_i$ = mole fraction in vapor | Used in flash calculations | | 9 | $UOP\;K = \dfrac{\sqrt[3]{T_{50\%,\,°F}}}{SG}$ | Similar to Watson K, uses only TBP 50 % point | Older but still asked | | 10 | $Aniline\;Point\;API\;Index = \dfrac{°API \times Aniline\;Point\;(°F)}{100}$ | Combines paraffinic character & aromaticity | Mid-distillate quality | > **Units Convention (KTU Board convention):** Temperature in **°F or °R** for K-factor, in **°C or K** for SI. Density at **15.6 °C (60 °F)** is the reference. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Refinery Configuration Selection:** A waxy, high-paraffin crude (e.g., Dabhol, India) needs a **wax-deoiling unit**, while a high-asphaltene crude needs a **delayed coker**. - **Crude Pricing:** Benchmark crudes (Brent, WTI, Dubai, Oman) are priced partly on **API gravity and sulfur content** — directly linked to composition. - **Product Yield Estimation:** Knowing the **SARA split** allows prediction of **FCC gasoline, diesel, and coke yields** before the feed even enters the reactor. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Conversion of Elemental Analysis to Empirical Formula > [!NOTE] > **Scenario (typical KTU Part B question):** A crude oil fraction has the following ultimate analysis: C = 84.0 wt %, H = 13.5 wt %, S = 1.5 wt %, N = 0.5 wt %, O = 0.5 wt %. Determine the empirical (simplest) atomic formula. **Step 1 — Convert each element's wt % to atomic proportion (moles per 100 g sample):** For every 100 g of sample, the number of gram-atoms of each element is wt % ÷ atomic mass. $$ n_C = \frac{84.0}{12.01} = 6.994\;\text{g-atoms} $$ $$ n_H = \frac{13.5}{1.008} = 13.393\;\text{g-atoms} $$ $$ n_S = \frac{1.5}{32.06} = 0.0468\;\text{g-atoms} $$ $$ n_N = \frac{0.5}{14.01} = 0.0357\;\text{g-atoms} $$ $$ n_O = \frac{0.5}{16.00} = 0.0313\;\text{g-atoms} $$ **Step 2 — Identify the smallest value to use as the divisor:** The smallest among the five is $n_O = 0.0313$. (In practice, we often look for the smallest C or N value for organic fractions; here O is smallest.) **Step 3 — Divide all by the divisor to get the simplest atomic ratio:** $$ C : H : S : N : O \;=\; \frac{6.994}{0.0313} : \frac{13.393}{0.0313} : \frac{0.0468}{0.0313} : \frac{0.0357}{0.0313} : \frac{0.0313}{0.0313} $$ $$ C : H : S : N : O \;=\; 223.5 : 427.9 : 1.495 : 1.140 : 1.000 $$ **Step 4 — Round to nearest whole number (multiply through to clear decimals):** Multiplying by 2 to eliminate fractions: $$ \boxed{C_{447}\,H_{856}\,S_{3}\,N_{2}\,O_{2}} $$ This is a **realistic empirical formula** for a vacuum residue fraction of a sour crude. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Watson Characterization Factor (K) **Step 1 — Start from the classical definition (Smith & Watson, 1933):** $$ K = \left(\frac{T_B}{SG}\right)^{1/3} $$ where $T_B$ is the **cubic-mean average boiling point (CMABP)** in °Rankine and $SG$ is the specific gravity at 60 °F. **Step 2 — Convert temperature scale if required:** $$ T_{°R} = T_{°F} + 459.67 \quad \text{or} \quad T_{°R} = \frac{9}{5}\,T_{°K} $$ **Step 3 — Numerical Example:** Given: $T_B = 850\;°F = 1309.67\;°R$ and $SG = 0.885$. $$ K = \left(\frac{1309.67}{0.885}\right)^{1/3} = (1480.4)^{1/3} $$ $$ K \approx 11.42 $$ **Step 4 — Classification (KTU must-know band):** | K Value | Crude Type | Example | |---|---|---| | $\geq 12.0$ | Highly Paraffinic | Penara / Condensates | | $11.0 - 11.9$ | Paraffinic | Brent, WTI | | $10.0 - 10.9$ | Intermediate (Naphthenic–Paraffinic) | Bombay High | | $9.5 - 9.9$ | Naphthenic | Some Gulf crudes | | $< 9.5$ | Highly Aromatic | Some heavy Californian crudes | --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — API Gravity from Density **Step 1 — Standard relationship:** $$ °API = \frac{141.5}{SG} - 131.5 $$ **Step 2 — Numerical Example:** A crude has density $\rho = 875\;\text{kg/m}^3$. Density of water at 60 °F $\approx 999\;\text{kg/m}^3$. $$ SG = \frac{875}{999} = 0.8759 $$ $$ °API = \frac{141.5}{0.8759} - 131.5 = 161.55 - 131.5 = 30.05\;°API $$ **Step 3 — Interpretation:** ≈ 30 °API ⇒ **light crude** (KTU classification: light > 31.1, medium 22.3 – 31.1, heavy < 22.3). --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Bulk Composition vs. Crude Origin (KTU Reference Table) | Crude Origin | API Gravity | Sulfur (wt %) | Paraffin / Naphthene / Aromatic Tendency | Watson K | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Brent (North Sea)** | 38 | 0.4 | P > N > A | 12.1 | | **WTI (USA)** | 39.6 | 0.3 | P > N > A | 12.0 | | **Arabian Light** | 33 | 1.8 | P ≈ N > A | 11.2 | | **Arabian Heavy** | 27 | 2.9 | P > N > A | 11.4 | | **Maya (Mexico)** | 22 | 3.5 | N > A > P | 10.1 | | **Bombay High (India)** | 39 | 0.15 | P > N > A | 11.8 | > **Engineering takeaway:** Lighter crude ≠ always better. A heavy sour crude may still be economic if it has high asphaltene-resin convertible fraction. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram — The Three Layers of Petroleum Composition ```mermaid flowchart TD A[CRUDE OIL
100 PERCENT BARREL]:::root A --> B[LAYER A
ELEMENTAL COMPOSITION]:::layer A --> C[LAYER B
HYDROCARBON TYPE]:::layer A --> D[LAYER C
FRACTIONAL AND SARA]:::layer B --> B1[CARBON 83 TO 87 PCT]:::node B --> B2[HYDROGEN 10 TO 14 PCT]:::node B --> B3[SULFUR 0.1 TO 5.0 PCT]:::node B --> B4[NITROGEN 0.1 TO 1.0 PCT]:::node B --> B5[OXYGEN 0.1 TO 1.5 PCT]:::node B --> B6[METALS NI V FE LT 1000 PPM]:::node C --> C1[PARAFFINS CNH2NPLUS2]:::node C --> C2[NAPHTHENES CNH2N]:::node C --> C3[AROMATICS CNH2NMINUS6]:::node C --> C4[OLEFINS CNH2N TRACE]:::node D --> D1[STRAIGHT RUN NAPHTHA IBP TO 200C]:::node D --> D2[KEROSENE 150 TO 250C]:::node D --> D3[DIESEL 250 TO 350C]:::node D --> D4[VACUUM GAS OIL 350 TO 560C]:::node D --> D5[VACUUM RESIDUE GT 560C]:::node D1 --> E[SARA CLASSIFICATION]:::deep D2 --> E D3 --> E D4 --> E D5 --> E E --> E1[SATURATES N HEPTANE SOLUBLE]:::node E --> E2[AROMATICS TOLUENE SOLUBLE]:::node E --> E3[RESINS METHANOL TOLUENE SOLUBLE]:::node E --> E4[ASPHALTENES N HEPTANE INSOLUBLE]:::node classDef root fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff classDef layer fill:#2d6a9f,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef node fill:#f5f5dc,stroke:#333333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000 classDef deep fill:#8b0000,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff ``` ### 4.2 Block Diagram — Analytical Workflow to Determine Composition ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[CRUDE OIL SAMPLE]:::start --> P2[ASTM D2892 TBP DISTILLATION]:::step P2 --> P3[NUMBER OF NARROW CUTS]:::node P3 --> P4[SARA ANALYSIS ASTM D2007]:::step P4 --> P5[COLUMN CHROMATOGRAPHY]:::node P5 --> P6[SATURATES]:::node P5 --> P7[AROMATICS]:::node P5 --> P8[RESINS]:::node P5 --> P9[ASPHALTENES]:::node P3 --> P10[ULTIMATE ANALYSIS CHNSO]:::step P10 --> P11[EMPIRICAL FORMULA DERIVATION]:::node P11 --> P12[H OVER C ATOMIC RATIO]:::node P3 --> P13[PIONA ANALYSIS GC]:::step P13 --> P14[PARAFFIN ISOPARAFFIN OLEFIN NAPHTHENE AROMATIC BREAKUP]:::node classDef start fill:#004d00,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef step fill:#8b4500,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef node fill:#e8e8e8,stroke:#333333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000 ``` ### 4.3 Block Diagram — Compositional Effect on Refinery Configuration ```mermaid flowchart TD R0[CRUDE COMPOSITION PROFILE]:::root R0 --> R1[HIGH PARAFFIN]:::node R0 --> R2[HIGH NAPHTHENE]:::node R0 --> R3[HIGH AROMATIC]:::node R0 --> R4[HIGH ASPHALTENE]:::node R0 --> R5[HIGH SULFUR]:::node R1 --> R1A[ADD WAX REMOVAL UNIT]:::unit R2 --> R2A[ADD HYDROCRACKER]:::unit R3 --> R3A[ADD EXTRACTIVE DISTILLATION]:::unit R4 --> R4A[ADD DELAYED COKER OR SDA]:::unit R5 --> R5A[ADD HYDRODESULFURIZATION]:::unit classDef root fill:#4b0082,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef node fill:#fffacd,stroke:#333333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000 classDef unit fill:#006400,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- #### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]** Differentiate between "sweet" and "sour" crude oils. State the cut-off sulfur content used for this classification. **Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] > - **Sweet crude:** Contains sulfur content **less than 0.5 wt %**. Requires simpler hydrotreating; commands a market premium. > - **Sour crude:** Contains sulfur content **greater than 0.5 wt %**. Mandates extensive HDS units and sour-service metallurgy (e.g., 304/316 SS, Monel cladding) in distillation overheads. > - **Cut-off value:** **0.5 wt % S** (per ASTM D129 and industry convention). --- #### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** What is the Watson Characterization Factor? How is it used to classify crude oils? **Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] > Watson K is defined as $K = (T_B/SG)^{1/3}$ where $T_B$ is in °Rankine. > - **K > 12** → Paraffinic (e.g., Brent, WTI) > - **K = 11 – 12** → Paraffinic–naphthenic > - **K = 10 – 11** → Naphthenic > - **K < 10** → Aromatic > > It is used to **predict crude yield pattern, paraffin-wax content, and refining behavior** without running a full PIONA analysis. --- ### 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern) --- ### **Question A — Choice 1 (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** > **(a)** Explain the **elemental (ultimate) composition** of crude oil with typical ranges and the engineering significance of each element. **(7 Marks)** > > **(b)** A crude oil sample has C = 86.0 %, H = 12.0 %, S = 1.2 %, N = 0.5 %, O = 0.3 % (by mass). Determine the **empirical formula** and the **H/C atomic ratio**. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Part (a): The elemental composition describes the **chemical elemental make-up** of crude oil measured by combustion analysis (CHNS-O analyzer). | Element | Range (wt %) | Engineering Significance | |---|---|---| | **Carbon** | 83 – 87 | Forms C–C backbone; governs residue vs. distillate split | | **Hydrogen** | 10 – 14 | Higher H → more light ends → better gasoline yield; deficit indicates aromatics | | **Sulfur** | 0.1 – 5.0 | Decides sweet/sour; needs HDS; emits SOx on combustion | | **Nitrogen** | 0.1 – 1.0 | Poisons acid catalysts (FCC, hydrocracking); forms NOx | | **Oxygen** | 0.1 – 1.5 | Naphthenic acids cause overhead corrosion | | **Metals (Ni, V, Fe, Na)** | < 1000 ppm | Ni & V deactivate FCC catalyst → need HDM | --- #### Model Solution — Part (b): **Step 1:** Convert wt % to gram-atoms per 100 g: $$ n_C = \frac{86.0}{12.01} = 7.161 $$ $$ n_H = \frac{12.0}{1.008} = 11.905 $$ $$ n_S = \frac{1.2}{32.06} = 0.0374 $$ $$ n_N = \frac{0.5}{14.01} = 0.0357 $$ $$ n_O = \frac{0.3}{16.00} = 0.0188 $$ **Step 2:** Divide by smallest ($n_O = 0.0188$): $$ C : H : S : N : O = 380.9 : 633.2 : 1.989 : 1.898 : 1.000 $$ **Step 3:** Multiply through to whole numbers (× 2): $$ \boxed{C_{762}\,H_{1266}\,S_{4}\,N_{4}\,O_{2}} $$ **Step 4:** H/C atomic ratio: $$ (H/C)_{atomic} = \frac{n_H}{n_C} = \frac{11.905}{7.161} = \boxed{1.66} $$ This low value (close to 1.5 indicates aromatics; 1.66 indicates a mixed but moderately aromatic residue fraction). --- ### **Question B — Choice 2 (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** > **(a)** Describe **SARA analysis** in detail — the procedure, fractions separated, solvents used, and engineering utility. **(7 Marks)** > > **(b)** The mean average boiling point of a crude fraction is **920 °F** and its specific gravity is **0.91**. Calculate the **Watson Characterization Factor** and classify the crude. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Part (a): **SARA Analysis (ASTM D2007)** separates crude / heavy oil into four polarity-based fractions: 1. **Asphaltenes** — precipitated by addition of **n-heptane** (40:1 vol ratio); filtered; insoluble fraction. 2. **Saturates** — eluted through alumina/silica column with **n-heptane**. 3. **Aromatics** — eluted with **toluene**. 4. **Resins** — eluted with **methanol/toluene (1:1)** mixture. **Engineering utility:** - Predicts **FCC feed quality** (saturates are easy to crack; asphaltenes produce coke). - Decides whether a **solvent deasphalting (SDA)** unit is required. - Determines **compatibility** of crude blends (high asphaltene + high paraffin ⇒ precipitation). - Used to model **EOR (Enhanced Oil Recovery)** behavior. --- #### Model Solution — Part (b): **Step 1:** Convert boiling point to °Rankine: $$ T_B = 920 + 459.67 = 1379.67\;°R $$ **Step 2:** Apply Watson K formula: $$ K = \left(\frac{T_B}{SG}\right)^{1/3} = \left(\frac{1379.67}{0.91}\right)^{1/3} = (1516.12)^{1/3} $$ $$ K = \boxed{11.49} $$ **Step 3:** Classification — K between 11.0 and 11.9 → **Paraffinic crude**. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks (Composition Module):** > 1. **Wrong atomic mass used** (e.g., using 1.0 instead of 1.008 for H, or 12 instead of 12.01 for C). Always use **standard atomic weights** as in the periodic table — losing 1 mark per wrong mass. > 2. **Forgetting to convert °F → °R** in Watson K. KTU students often write $K = (920/0.91)^{1/3}$ — this gives the wrong value. **Add 460 first.** > 3. **Confusing Watson K with UOP K** (UOP uses TBP 50 % point, Watson uses CMABP). State which formula you are using. > 4. **Empirical formula not reduced to smallest whole number** — examiners look for the final simplified ratio; raw decimal ratios earn partial marks only. > 5. **Not stating solvent names in SARA** — naming only "Saturates, Aromatics" without solvents costs 2 marks. > 6. **Writing °API in °C units** — convention is dimensionless (just "°API"). --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - ⭐ **Three layers of composition:** Elemental → Hydrocarbon-type → Fractional/SARA. - ⭐ **Always write the unit of K-factor in °R, not °F.** - ⭐ **H/C atomic ratio:** Paraffinic ≈ 2.0, Naphthenic ≈ 1.7, Aromatic ≈ 1.0. - ⭐ **Sweet vs. sour crude cut-off:** **0.5 wt % sulfur**. - ⭐ **API Gravity formula:** $°API = 141.5/SG - 131.5$. - ⭐ **Watson K classification bands:** 12+ paraffinic, 11–12 mild paraffinic, 10–11 naphthenic, <10 aromatic. - ⭐ **SARA solvents (memorize):** Asphaltenes → n-C₇ insoluble; Saturates → n-heptane; Aromatics → toluene; Resins → methanol/toluene. - ⭐ **Saturates formula $C_nH_{2n+2}$; Naphthenes $C_nH_{2n}$; Aromatics $C_nH_{2n-6}$.** - ⭐ **Conversion rule for empirical formula:** wt % ÷ atomic mass → atomic ratio → divide by smallest → round to integer. - ⭐ **Metals (Ni, V) poison FCC catalyst → need HDM.** - ⭐ **SARA predicts** cracking yield and **SDA requirement**, not just chemistry. - ⭐ **High paraffinic crude = wax problems**; **high naphthenic = good diesel**; **high aromatic = high octane potential**; **high asphaltene = residue problem**. ---

topics.title: Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT638 - PETROLEUM REFINERY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Characteristics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

In KTU 2024 Scheme Petroleum Refinery Engineering terminology, **"Characteristics of Crude Oil"** refers to the comprehensive set of physical, chemical, thermal, and rheological properties that quantitatively and qualitatively describe a given crude petroleum. These characteristics are the primary determinants of:
- The **economic value** of the crude,
- The **refinery configuration** required to process it,
- The **yield slate** of refined products (LPG, naphtha, kerosene, diesel, lube base oils, bitumen, etc.),
- The **handling, storage, and transportation** specifications, and
- The **environmental and safety** compliance requirements.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** "Characteristics" is a **high-weightage** foundational topic. Almost every refinery design calculation, product blending equation, and process selection logic in subsequent modules (Crude Distillation, Catalytic Reforming, Cracking, etc.) depends on a working command of these base properties.

The characteristics are broadly classified into three interlinked domains:

| Domain | Property Examples |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Physical** | Density, Specific Gravity, API Gravity, Viscosity, Pour Point, Flash Point, Fire Point, Color, Odor, Optical Activity |
| **Chemical** | Elemental composition (C, H, S, N, O), Hydrocarbon-type composition (Saturates, Aromatics, Resins, Asphaltenes — the **SARA** analysis), Salt content, Acidity (TAN) |
| **Thermal / Performance** | Heating Value, Carbon Residue (Conradson / Ramsbottom), Watson Characterization Factor (Kw), Smoke Point, Aniline Point |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Personality Profile" of Crude Oil

Imagine crude oil as a **person going for a job interview** at a refinery. Just as an HR manager evaluates a candidate using metrics like **height, weight, IQ, EQ, skill-set, and experience**, a refinery process engineer evaluates a crude using **API gravity, viscosity, sulfur content, pour point, and Kw factor**.

- A **light, sweet crude** (high API, low sulfur) is like a **young, agile, low-maintenance candidate** — easy to process, gives more gasoline, costs less to refine.
- A **heavy, sour crude** (low API, high sulfur) is like a **seasoned, demanding specialist** — needs heavy equipment (hydrotreaters, cokers), produces more diesel and residue, but requires higher capital.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Conditions (KTU Convention):** All density, specific gravity, and API gravity values are reported at the **standard temperature of 15.6 °C (60 °F)** and **standard pressure of 1 atm (101.325 kPa)**, unless otherwise stated. The viscosity-temperature relationships use **Saybolt Universal Seconds (SUS)** for industrial applications.

### 1.3 Why Characteristics Matter — The Refinery Decision Chain

$$
\text{Crude Characteristics} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Refinery Configuration} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Product Yield} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Profitability}
$$

This linear but inter-dependent chain is the **central philosophy** of Petroleum Refinery Engineering. The first arrow is exactly what this Module 1 topic addresses.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Physical Characteristics — Structured Breakdown

#### 2.1.1 Density ($\rho$) and Specific Gravity ($SG$)
- **Definition:** Mass per unit volume of the crude, expressed in **kg/m³** (SI) or **g/cm³** (CGS).
- **Specific Gravity (SG):** The ratio of the density of the crude oil to the density of water at the **same reference temperature** (typically 15.6 °C).
- **Why it matters:** Determines pipeline flow rates, storage tank sizing, and **tanker freight calculations** (shipping is sold per metric ton, not per barrel — a 10% density error means 10% revenue error).

#### 2.1.2 API Gravity ($°API$)
- The **petroleum industry standard** for reporting crude "lightness".
- An **inverse** scale: lighter crudes have **higher** API gravity.
- Water has **°API = 10**.
- Crudes are classified as:
  - **Light Crude:** °API > 31.1
  - **Medium Crude:** 22.3 < °API ≤ 31.1
  - **Heavy Crude:** 10 < °API ≤ 22.3
  - **Extra-Heavy Crude:** °API ≤ 10

#### 2.1.3 Viscosity ($\mu$)
- Resistance of the crude to flow.
- Reported in **centipoise (cP)**, **centistokes (cSt)**, or **Saybolt Universal Seconds (SUS)**.
- **Dynamic viscosity** governs pumping power; **kinematic viscosity** governs pipeline hydraulics.
- Strongly temperature-dependent — a heavy crude at 50 °C may flow like water.

#### 2.1.4 Pour Point (PP)
- The **lowest temperature** at which the crude will still flow under standard test conditions (ASTM D97).
- Controlled by **wax (paraffin) content**. Waxy crudes (e.g., North Sea Gullfaks) have very high pour points (+30 °C or higher), requiring heated pipelines.

#### 2.1.5 Flash Point (FP) and Fire Point
- **Flash Point:** The lowest temperature at which the oil gives off enough vapor to form an **ignitable mixture with air** near its surface (a flash appears, but the vapor does not sustain burning).
- **Fire Point:** The temperature at which the vapor burns **continuously** for at least 5 seconds upon ignition.
- **Fire Point ≈ Flash Point + 5 to 30 °C** (typical).
- Critical for **storage safety, transportation classification (IMO), and insurance**.

#### 2.1.6 Color and Odor
- **Color** indicates the hydrocarbon type and refining level: Light crudes are pale yellow/greenish; heavy crudes are dark brown/black.
- **Odor:** Sweet-smelling crudes have **low sulfur**; sour-smelling crudes have **high sulfur (H₂S and mercaptans)**.

### 2.2 Chemical Characteristics — Structured Breakdown

#### 2.2.1 Elemental Composition
- **Carbon:** 83 – 87 wt%
- **Hydrogen:** 10 – 14 wt%
- **Sulfur:** 0.05 – 6 wt% (sweet < 0.5, sour > 0.5)
- **Nitrogen:** 0.01 – 0.7 wt%
- **Oxygen:** 0.05 – 1.5 wt%
- **Metals (Ni, V, Fe):** trace to 1000+ ppm

#### 2.2.2 SARA Analysis (Hydrocarbon-Type Composition)
| Fraction | Description | Solvent Affinity |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Saturates** | Paraffins + Naphthenes (alkanes & cycloalkanes) | Soluble in n-pentane, insoluble in silica |
| **Aromatics** | Benzene, naphthalene derivatives | Soluble in toluene |
| **Resins** | Polar, N-, O-, S-containing aromatics | Soluble in dichloromethane |
| **Asphaltenes** | High-MW polycyclic aromatics, insoluble in n-heptane | Insoluble in n-pentane/n-heptane |

#### 2.2.3 Salt Content
- Reported as **lb of salt per 1000 bbl (ptb)** or **mg/L (ppm by weight)**.
- Mostly **NaCl, MgCl₂, CaCl₂** from reservoir brine.
- Must be **desalted to < 5 ptb** before crude distillation to prevent corrosion and catalyst poisoning.

#### 2.2.4 Total Acid Number (TAN)
- **mg of KOH** required to neutralize **1 g** of crude.
- High TAN (> 0.5 mg KOH/g) → "**Naphthenic acid corrosion**" at 220 – 400 °C in distillation overheads.

### 2.3 Thermal / Performance Characteristics

#### 2.3.1 Heating Value
- **Higher Heating Value (HHV):** Total heat released including condensation of water vapor.
- **Lower Heating Value (LHV):** Excludes water condensation energy (more realistic for fuel calculations).

#### 2.3.2 Carbon Residue
- **Conradson Carbon Residue (CCR)** and **Ramsbottom Carbon Residue (RCR)** measure the **coking tendency**.
- High CCR → more coke deposits in furnace tubes and downstream coker requirements.

#### 2.3.3 Watson Characterization Factor ($K_w$)
- A **paraffinicity index** — high Kw means paraffinic, low Kw means aromatic/naphthenic.
- **Used extensively in KTU problems for distillation curve predictions and product slate estimates.**

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is your **exam-day cheat sheet** for the "Characteristics" topic. Memorize the units and conditions — they are the most common mark-deduction source in KTU valuations.

| # | Property / Quantity | Formula / Definition | Standard Units / Condition |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Specific Gravity (SG)** | $SG = \dfrac{\rho_{\text{oil}}}{\rho_{\text{water}}}$ at the same T | Dimensionless; T = 15.6 °C |
| 2 | **API Gravity** | $°API = \dfrac{141.5}{SG} - 131.5$ | Degrees (°API); T = 15.6 °C |
| 3 | **Inverse API → SG** | $SG = \dfrac{141.5}{°API + 131.5}$ | Dimensionless |
| 4 | **SG Temperature Correction (light oils)** | $SG_{t} = SG_{60} - 0.0007\,(t - 60)$ | t in °F |
| 5 | **SG Temperature Correction (heavy oils)** | $SG_{t} = SG_{60} - 0.00063\,(t - 60)$ | t in °F |
| 6 | **Kinematic Viscosity (SUS → cSt)** | $\nu\,[cSt] = 0.226\,t - \dfrac{195}{t}$ for $t \in [32, 100]$ SUS | cSt |
| 7 | **Kinematic Viscosity (SUS → cSt)** | $\nu\,[cSt] = 0.226\,t - \dfrac{184}{t}$ for $t \in [100, 240]$ SUS | cSt |
| 8 | **Dynamic ↔ Kinematic Viscosity** | $\mu = \rho \cdot \nu$ | $\mu$ in cP, $\rho$ in g/cm³, $\nu$ in cSt |
| 9 | **Watson Characterization Factor ($K_w$)** | $K_w = \dfrac{\left(T_B\right)^{1/3}}{SG}$ | $T_B$ in **°R** (Rankine), SG at 60 °F |
| 10 | **UOP Characterization Factor (K)** | $K = \dfrac{\left(T_{MEABP}\right)^{1/3}}{SG}$ | $T_{MEABP}$ in °R (Mean Average BP) |
| 11 | **HHV (Dulong-type, petroleum)** | $HHV = 14500 - 162 \cdot SG \;\text{(Btu/lb)}$ | Empirical |
| 12 | **Carbon Residue Correlation** | $CCR \approx 0.6 \cdot \text{Asphaltene}\,\%$ | wt% |
| 13 | **TAN Definition** | $TAN = \dfrac{V_{KOH} \cdot N_{KOH} \cdot 56.1}{m_{sample}}$ | mg KOH/g |
| 14 | **API from Density** | $°API = \dfrac{141.5}{\rho\,[g/cm^3]} - 131.5$ | since $\rho_{water} = 1$ g/cm³ |
| 15 | **Barrel → m³** | $1\,\text{bbl} = 0.158987\,\text{m}^3$ | Volume |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trap Alert — The $K_w$ Temperature:** The Watson factor is computed using the **atmospheric average boiling point $T_B$ in degrees Rankine (°R)**, where $°R = °F + 459.67$. Students frequently use $°C$ or $°F$ directly and lose 2-3 marks. **Convert first, then cube-root.**

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry Sector | Most-Critical Characteristic | Engineering Consequence |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Pipeline Transport** | Viscosity, Pour Point | Pumping energy, need for heating/insulation |
| **Marine Tanker Freight** | API Gravity | Revenue per metric ton |
| **Refinery Process Design** | Sulfur, TAN, Kw, CCR | Choice of hydrotreater, coker, FCC, reformer |
| **Product Blending** | RVP, Octane, Cetane | Gasoline/diesel recipe optimization |
| **Lube Oil Manufacture** | Viscosity Index, Pour Point | Dewaxing and hydrofinishing unit sizing |
| **Catalyst Selection** | Metals (Ni + V) | Catalyst deactivation rate, guard-bed sizing |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the API Gravity Equation

The American Petroleum Institute adopted an **inverse hydrometer scale** designed by the National Bureau of Standards. We start from the definition of specific gravity for a liquid measured with a hydrometer at 60 °F.

**Starting point:** For a hydrometer calibrated so that water reads $10°$ at 60 °F,

$$
°API = \dfrac{141.5}{SG} - 131.5
$$

**Step-by-step derivation:**

**Step 1:** Define the API scale such that water at 60 °F has an API gravity of 10. We require a functional form:

$$
°API = \dfrac{A}{SG} + B
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the boundary condition — water has $SG = 1$ and $°API = 10$:

$$
10 = \dfrac{A}{1} + B \quad \Longrightarrow \quad A + B = 10
$$

**Step 3:** Apply the second boundary condition — the scale should make the numbers round. Historically, the second reference was chosen such that a liquid of $SG = 0.5$ corresponds to an API of approximately 151.5:

$$
151.5 = \dfrac{A}{0.5} + B \quad \Longrightarrow \quad 2A + B = 151.5
$$

**Step 4:** Solve the linear system:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A + B &= 10 \\
2A + B &= 151.5
\end{aligned}
$$

Subtracting the first from the second:

$$
A = 141.5
$$

Substituting back:

$$
B = 10 - 141.5 = -131.5
$$

**Step 5:** Therefore the **API gravity formula** is rigorously:

$$
°API = \dfrac{141.5}{SG} - 131.5
$$

This is **not arbitrary** — the constants $A$ and $B$ are tied to two physical reference fluids (water and a hypothetical $SG = 0.5$ oil).

---

### 3.2 Worked Example — API Gravity Conversion (KTU-Standard Problem)

> **Problem:** A crude oil sample has a measured density of $925\ \text{kg/m}^3$ at 15.6 °C. Determine (a) its specific gravity, (b) its API gravity, and (c) classify it as light/medium/heavy/extra-heavy.

**Given:** $\rho_{\text{oil}} = 925\ \text{kg/m}^3 = 0.925\ \text{g/cm}^3$; $\rho_{\text{water}} = 999\ \text{kg/m}^3$ at 15.6 °C $\approx 1.000\ \text{g/cm}^3$.

**Step 1 — Compute SG:**

$$
SG = \dfrac{\rho_{\text{oil}}}{\rho_{\text{water}}} = \dfrac{0.925}{1.000} = 0.925
$$

**Step 2 — Compute °API:**

$$
°API = \dfrac{141.5}{SG} - 131.5 = \dfrac{141.5}{0.925} - 131.5
$$

$$
°API = 152.9729 - 131.5 = 21.47 \approx 21.5°\text{API}
$$

**Step 3 — Classification:** Since $10 < 21.5 < 22.3$, this crude is classified as **Heavy Crude** (borderline medium-heavy).

**Step 4 — Sanity check using the inverse formula:**

$$
SG = \dfrac{141.5}{°API + 131.5} = \dfrac{141.5}{21.5 + 131.5} = \dfrac{141.5}{153.0} = 0.9248 \approx 0.925 \;\checkmark
$$

---

### 3.3 Worked Example — Watson Characterization Factor

> **Problem:** A naphtha cut has a volumetric average boiling point of 200 °F and a specific gravity of 0.78 at 60 °F. Compute the Watson Characterization Factor $K_w$ and comment on the paraffinicity.

**Step 1 — Convert $T_B$ to Rankine:**

$$
T_B = 200 + 459.67 = 659.67\,°R
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the $K_w$ formula:**

$$
K_w = \dfrac{\left(T_B\right)^{1/3}}{SG}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the cube root:**

$$
\left(659.67\right)^{1/3} = 8.704
$$

**Step 4 — Divide by SG:**

$$
K_w = \dfrac{8.704}{0.78} = 11.16
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation (Nelson's classification):**

| $K_w$ Range | Crude / Cut Type |
| :--- | :--- |
| $K_w > 12$ | Highly **paraffinic** |
| $11.5 < K_w < 12$ | Paraffinic |
| $11 < K_w < 11.5$ | Intermediate (mixed base) |
| $10.5 < K_w < 11$ | Naphthenic |
| $K_w < 10.5$ | Highly **aromatic** |

Since $K_w = 11.16$, the naphtha cut is in the **intermediate (mixed-base)** range — slightly paraffinic, with moderate naphthene content. This is consistent with typical straight-run naphtha from a paraffinic-naphthenic crude.

---

### 3.4 Worked Example — Temperature Correction of Specific Gravity

> **Problem:** A heavy crude has $SG_{60°F} = 0.92$. What is its specific gravity at 150 °F?

**Step 1 — Identify the correct correlation.** For heavy oils (SG > 0.85):

$$
SG_{t} = SG_{60} - 0.00063\,(t - 60)
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $t = 150$ °F:**

$$
SG_{150} = 0.92 - 0.00063 \cdot (150 - 60)
$$

$$
SG_{150} = 0.92 - 0.00063 \cdot 90 = 0.92 - 0.0567
$$

$$
SG_{150} = 0.8633
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to API at 150 °F (informational only — KTU convention is to report at 60 °F):**

$$
°API_{150} = \dfrac{141.5}{0.8633} - 131.5 = 163.9 - 131.5 = 32.4
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Always state the **reference temperature** when reporting SG or API in your KTU answer. A bare number without a temperature tag can cost a mark.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Reading Crude Properties Like a Refinery Engineer

| Property | Low Value Implies | High Value Implies | Process Implication |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **API Gravity** | Heavy, viscous, more residue | Light, high gasoline yield | Choice between coker vs. hydrocracker |
| **Sulfur** | Sweet — simpler processing | Sour — needs hydrodesulfurization (HDS) | Capital cost of HDS, H₂ plant |
| **Pour Point** | Good cold-flow properties | Waxy — needs dewaxing | Pipeline heating, lube plant config |
| **TAN** | Low corrosivity | Naphthenic acid corrosion risk | Material selection (SS 316L) |
| **CCR** | Low coking tendency | High coking tendency | Delayed coker sizing, furnace decoking cycles |
| **Ni + V (metals)** | Low catalyst poisoning | High poisoning, H₂ consumption | Catalyst make-up rate, guard reactor |
| **$K_w$** | Aromatic feed (good for FCC) | Paraffinic (good for lube, jet) | Choice of conversion unit |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Taxonomy of Crude Oil Characteristics

```mermaid

    P1 --> P6{Sulfur Content}

    P3 --> R1[Atmospheric Distillation]
    P4 --> R1
    P5 --> R2[Atmospheric plus Vacuum Distillation]

    R2 --> R4[Coker Visbreaker]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define API gravity. A crude oil has an API gravity of 35°. Calculate its specific gravity at 60 °F.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** API gravity is a measure of how heavy or light a petroleum liquid is compared to water. It is defined as:

$$
°API = \dfrac{141.5}{SG} - 131.5
$$

where SG is the specific gravity at 60 °F. Water has an API gravity of 10°.

**Calculation (2 marks):** Rearranging the formula:

$$
SG = \dfrac{141.5}{°API + 131.5} = \dfrac{141.5}{35 + 131.5} = \dfrac{141.5}{166.5}
$$

$$
\boxed{SG = 0.850}
$$

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**List any four important physical characteristics of crude oil used for refinery design.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The four key physical characteristics are:

1. **API Gravity / Specific Gravity** — Determines the lightness/heaviness and the yield distribution of refined products.
2. **Viscosity** (dynamic and kinematic) — Governs pipeline flow, pumping power, and ease of handling.
3. **Pour Point** — The lowest temperature at which crude will flow; critical for cold-climate pipeline operation and storage.
4. **Flash Point and Fire Point** — Indicate fire and explosion hazards during storage, transportation, and handling.

(Any four valid characteristics accepted. Acceptable alternatives: color, odor, optical activity, refractive index, conductivity.)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Pattern

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 1]**

**(a) [7 marks]** With neat sketches and equations, explain the determination of **API gravity** of a crude oil sample. A refinery receives a parcel of crude with $\rho = 880\ \text{kg/m}^3$ at 15.6 °C. Compute (i) the specific gravity, (ii) the API gravity, and (iii) classify the crude as light/medium/heavy.

**(b) [7 marks]** Define the **Watson Characterization Factor ($K_w$)**. A straight-run gasoline (SRG) cut has a volumetric average boiling point of 180 °F and $SG = 0.74$. Calculate the $K_w$ and comment on the paraffinicity of the cut.

---

#### **Model Solution — Q.A (a) [7 marks]**

**Part 1 — Concept of API gravity (2 marks):**

API gravity is determined experimentally using a **hydrometer** (or a digital density meter like Anton Paar DMA 4500 M). The hydrometer is a calibrated glass float that is immersed in the crude at 60 °F (15.6 °C). The depth to which it sinks gives a direct API reading. The governing equation is:

$$
°API = \dfrac{141.5}{SG} - 131.5
$$

**Part 2 — Given:** $\rho_{\text{oil}} = 880\ \text{kg/m}^3 = 0.880\ \text{g/cm}^3$, $\rho_{\text{water at 15.6°C}} = 999\ \text{kg/m}^3 \approx 1.000\ \text{g/cm}^3$.

**(i) Specific Gravity (1 mark):**

$$
SG = \dfrac{\rho_{\text{oil}}}{\rho_{\text{water}}} = \dfrac{880}{1000} = \boxed{0.880}
$$

**(ii) API Gravity (2 marks):**

$$
°API = \dfrac{141.5}{0.880} - 131.5 = 160.795 - 131.5 = \boxed{29.30°\text{API}}
$$

**(iii) Classification (1 mark):** Since $22.3 < 29.3 < 31.1$, the crude is classified as **Medium Crude**.

**(1 mark) for correct presentation and final boxed answer.**

---

#### **Model Solution — Q.A (b) [7 marks]**

**Part 1 — Definition (3 marks):**

The Watson Characterization Factor $K_w$ is an empirical, dimensionless index that quantifies the **paraffinicity** (paraffin-content) of a petroleum fraction. It is defined as:

$$
K_w = \dfrac{\left(T_B\right)^{1/3}}{SG}
$$

where $T_B$ is the **atmospheric volumetric average boiling point** expressed in **degrees Rankine (°R)**, and SG is the specific gravity at 60 °F. The factor was developed by K.M. Watson in 1933 and is widely used in correlating thermodynamic and physical properties of petroleum fractions.

**Interpretation scale (1 mark):**
- $K_w > 12$ → Highly paraffinic
- $K_w = 11.5$ – $12$ → Paraffinic
- $K_w = 11$ – $11.5$ → Mixed base
- $K_w = 10.5$ – $11$ → Naphthenic
- $K_w < 10.5$ → Highly aromatic

**Part 2 — Calculation (3 marks):**

**Step 1:** Convert $T_B$ to °R:

$$
T_B = 180\,°F + 459.67 = 639.67\,°R
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the cube root:

$$
(639.67)^{1/3} = 8.620
$$

**Step 3:** Compute $K_w$:

$$
K_w = \dfrac{8.620}{0.74} = \boxed{11.65}
$$

**Comment on paraffinicity (1 mark):** $K_w = 11.65$ falls in the **paraffinic** range (11.5 – 12.0). The SRG cut is therefore predominantly paraffinic in nature, with low aromatic content. This is favorable for **reformer feed** (high octane yield potential) and **LPG/naphtha-grade chemicals** like ethylene cracker feed.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 1]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **SARA analysis** of crude oil. Briefly describe the significance of each fraction with respect to refinery processing.

**(b) [7 marks]** A heavy Arabian crude has $SG_{60°F} = 0.935$. Calculate (i) its API gravity, (ii) its density in kg/m³, and (iii) the HHV using the empirical Dulong-type correlation $HHV = 14500 - 162 \cdot SG$ in Btu/lb. Also compute HHV in MJ/kg.

---

#### **Model Solution — Q.B (a) [7 marks]**

**SARA Analysis (2 marks for definition):**

SARA stands for **Saturates, Aromatics, Resins, and Asphaltenes**. It is a chromatographic separation technique that classifies crude oil (or its residue) into four chemical-class fractions based on **solubility and polarity**.

**Step-by-step procedure (3 marks):**

1. **Asphaltene precipitation:** Crude is mixed with **n-heptane (40:1 volume ratio)**. Asphaltenes are insoluble and precipitate out. They are filtered, dried, and weighed.
2. **Column chromatography (deasphaltened oil):** The remaining maltenes are passed through a packed column with **activated silica or alumina** and eluted with solvents of increasing polarity:
   - **n-Hexane** → elutes **Saturates**
   - **Toluene** → elutes **Aromatics**
   - **Dichloromethane / Methanol** → elutes **Resins**

**Significance of each fraction (2 marks):**

| Fraction | Significance |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Saturates** | High cetane, good diesel base; low octane in gasoline |
| **Aromatics** | High octane, good gasoline; precursors to coke in FCC |
| **Resins** | Surface-active, contribute to emulsion stability |
| **Asphaltenes** | Cause fouling, catalyst poisoning, and pipeline deposits; source of coke |

---

#### **Model Solution — Q.B (b) [7 marks]**

**Given:** $SG_{60°F} = 0.935$

**(i) API Gravity (2 marks):**

$$
°API = \dfrac{141.5}{0.935} - 131.5 = 151.34 - 131.5 = \boxed{19.84°\text{API}}
$$

**(ii) Density in kg/m³ (2 marks):**

Since SG is the ratio relative to water ($\rho_{water} = 1000\ \text{kg/m}^3$ at 15.6 °C, approximated):

$$
\rho_{\text{oil}} = SG \times \rho_{water} = 0.935 \times 1000 = \boxed{935\ \text{kg/m}^3}
$$

(More accurately, using $\rho_{water} = 999\ \text{kg/m}^3$: $\rho = 934.1\ \text{kg/m}^3$.)

**(iii) HHV Calculation (3 marks):**

$$
HHV = 14500 - 162 \cdot SG = 14500 - 162 \times 0.935
$$

$$
HHV = 14500 - 151.47 = \boxed{14348.5\ \text{Btu/lb}}
$$

**Convert to MJ/kg (1 mark for unit conversion, 1 mark for answer):**

Conversion factors:
- $1\ \text{Btu/lb} = 2326\ \text{J/kg} = 0.002326\ \text{MJ/kg}$

$$
HHV = 14348.5 \times 0.002326 = \boxed{33.38\ \text{MJ/kg}}
$$

This HHV is typical of a heavy fuel-oil-grade stream, suitable for marine bunker fuel or fired heaters after blending.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in "Characteristics" Questions (KTU Valuation Pattern)**
>
> 1. **Temperature Omission (–1 mark):** Reporting API gravity or SG without explicitly stating **"at 60 °F"** is a near-universal mark-deduction. Always write `°API (at 60 °F)` or `SG₆₀`.
> 2. **Unit Mix in Watson $K_w$ (–2 marks):** Using °C or °F in the cube root instead of °R. **Convert first — show the conversion step.**
> 3. **Forgetting the $0.0007$ vs. $0.00063$ rule:** Use $0.0007$ for **light crudes (SG < 0.85)** and $0.00063$ for **heavy crudes (SG > 0.85)** in SG temperature corrections.
> 4. **Asphaltenes vs. Resins Confusion (–1 mark):** Asphaltenes are **insoluble in n-heptane**; resins are **soluble** but adsorb on alumina. Don't reverse these in SARA questions.
> 5. **Bbl vs. m³ Factor:** $1\ \text{bbl} = 0.158987\ \text{m}^3$. Don't approximate as $0.159$ in KTU numerical problems — use the standard.
> 6. **HHV vs. LHV:** KTU questions frequently ask "heating value" without specifying — **always state which you computed and why** (LHV for combustion, HHV for calorimetric).

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — "Characteristics" (Module 1, PEPOT638)**

- **API Gravity Formula:** $°API = \dfrac{141.5}{SG} - 131.5$; inverse form is $SG = \dfrac{141.5}{°API + 131.5}$.
- **Classification Thresholds:** Light > 31.1, Medium 22.3–31.1, Heavy 10–22.3, Extra-Heavy ≤ 10.
- **Standard Conditions:** **60 °F (15.6 °C)** and **1 atm** for all density/SG/API values.
- **Watson Factor:** $K_w = \dfrac{(T_B)^{1/3}}{SG}$; **T in °R (Rankine)**.
- **UOP Factor K:** $K = \dfrac{(T_{MEABP})^{1/3}}{SG}$; uses mean average boiling point.
- **SARA Order of Polarity:** Saturates → Aromatics → Resins → Asphaltenes (increasing polarity and MW).
- **Asphaltenes:** **Insoluble in n-heptane/n-pentane**; major coking precursors.
- **Paraffinicity:** Higher $K_w$ → more paraffinic → better lube base; lower $K_w$ → more aromatic → better FCC feed.
- **Sweet vs. Sour:** Sweet: S < 0.5 wt%; Sour: S ≥ 0.5 wt%.
- **Viscosity Conversions:** $\mu\,[cP] = \rho\,[g/cm^3] \times \nu\,[cSt]$.
- **Temperature Correction of SG:**
  - Light oils: $SG_t = SG_{60} - 0.0007\,(t - 60)$
  - Heavy oils: $SG_t = SG_{60} - 0.00063\,(t - 60)$
- **Pour Point, Flash Point, Fire Point:** Always increasing order → **PP < FP < Fire Point**.
- **TAN:** Expressed as **mg KOH per g** of crude; high TAN → naphthenic acid corrosion.
- **HHV ≈ 33 – 45 MJ/kg** for crude and its products (heavier → lower).
- **Salt content:** Reported in **ptb** (lb per 1000 bbl) or **mg/L**; desalting target < 5 ptb.
- **Color as a Quick Indicator:** Pale → light/sweet; Dark → heavy/often sour.
- **Elemental Window:** C 83–87%, H 10–14%, S 0.05–6%, N 0.01–0.7%, O 0.05–1.5%, metals trace.
- **CCR (Conradson Carbon Residue):** Key indicator of **coking tendency** in heavy crudes.
- **Bbl-to-m³:** Memorize $1\ \text{bbl} = 0.158987\ \text{m}^3 \approx 0.159\ \text{m}^3$.

> **Final Examiner Tip:** In KTU 2024 Scheme Module 1 questions, **always begin your numerical answer with a "Given → Find → Solution" header** and **box the final answer**. Examiners scan for the boxed final value; missing boxes = lost visibility = potential mark loss even with correct work.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Product Pattern
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT638 - PETROLEUM REFINERY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Product Pattern

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Petroleum Refinery Engineering**, the **Product Pattern** (also called the **Product Slate** or **Yield Pattern**) refers to the systematic distribution and volumetric (or mass) proportion of finished petroleum products obtained as output streams from a refinery, when a given quantity of **crude oil** (feedstock) is processed through a series of physical separation and chemical conversion units.

Mathematically, it can be expressed as:

$$Y_i = \frac{V_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} V_i} \times 100\%$$

Where:
- $Y_i$ = Yield (or share) of the $i^{th}$ product in the product pattern
- $V_i$ = Volume (or mass) of the $i^{th}$ product
- $n$ = Total number of finished products

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** *Product Pattern* is the foundational concept that drives every subsequent decision in refinery design — from **crude selection** and **process configuration** to **economic optimization**. Mastering the relationship between **crude assay** and **refinery output** is essential for Module 1.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a large, sophisticated **juice factory** that receives whole fruits (apples, oranges, grapes) as raw material. The factory separates and processes them into many products: packaged juice bottles, concentrated syrups, fruit pulps, essential oils, and even compost from the peels. The **proportion** of each finished product the factory produces — say, 40% packaged juice, 20% concentrate, 15% pulp, 10% oil, 15% compost — is its **product pattern**.

A petroleum refinery works exactly the same way:
- **Crude oil** is the "raw fruit"
- **Atmospheric & Vacuum Distillation, Cracking, Reforming, Treating** are the "processing machines"
- **LPG, Naphtha, Gasoline, Kerosene, Diesel, Fuel Oil, Bitumen, Lubricants** are the "finished products"
- The **yield distribution** among these products = the **Product Pattern**

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Two refineries using the *same* crude oil can produce *very different* product patterns if they employ different processing units. For example, a refinery with an **FCC (Fluid Catalytic Cracker)** will produce far more gasoline than a simple **skimming (topping) refinery**.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Terminology

The following are the **standard industry metrics** used to characterize and quantify the product pattern:

- **°API Gravity** — A measure of crude oil / product density (heavier oils → lower °API). Formula: $\text{°API} = \dfrac{141.5}{SG} - 131.5$
- **Specific Gravity (SG)** — Ratio of liquid density to water density at **15.6 °C (60 °F)**.
- **UOP K-Factor (Watson Characterization Factor)** — Indicates paraffinicity vs. aromaticity: $K = \dfrac{\sqrt[3]{T_B}}{SG}$, where $T_B$ is the **mean average boiling point in °R (Rankine)**.
- **Sulfur Content (wt%)** — Critical for product pricing and environmental compliance (Euro VI / BS-VI norms).
- **Refinery Complexity (Nelson Index)** — Quantifies processing sophistication; higher index = more conversion units.
- **Carbon Residue (Conradson / Ramsbottom)** — Indicates tendency to form coke.
- **Octane Number (RON / MON)** — Knock resistance of gasoline.
- **Cetane Number** — Ignition quality of diesel.
- **Smoke Point, Flash Point, Pour Point** — Critical safety & performance parameters for middle distillates.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Product Pattern ≠ Product Specification.** The *pattern* tells you **how much** of each product is produced; the *specification* tells you the **quality** of each product (e.g., BS-VI diesel with 10 ppm sulfur).

## 1.4 Classification of Refined Products (Boiling Range Overview)

| Product Family | Approx. Boiling Range (°C) | Carbon Number (C) | Primary Use |
|----------------|---------------------------|-------------------|-------------|
| Refinery Gas / LPG | < 40 | C₁ – C₄ | Domestic / Auto fuel |
| Naphtha (Light & Heavy) | 40 – 180 | C₅ – C₁₁ | Petrochem feedstock / Gasoline blending |
| Gasoline (Motor Spirit) | 40 – 205 | C₅ – C₁₂ | Light vehicle fuel |
| Kerosene / ATF (Aviation Turbine Fuel) | 150 – 300 | C₁₁ – C₁₈ | Domestic fuel / Jet fuel |
| Diesel (HSD / LDO) | 250 – 380 | C₁₄ – C₂₅ | Heavy vehicle / Industrial fuel |
| Lube Oil Base Stocks | 380 – 550 | C₂₅ – C₄₀ | Lubricants |
| Fuel Oil (Furnace Oil) | 380 – 600+ | C₂₅ – C₆₀ | Marine / Industrial boilers |
| Bitumen (Asphalt) | > 500 (residue) | C₃₀+ | Road paving, roofing |
| Petroleum Coke | > 500 (residue) | C₃₀+ | Fuel, electrodes |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Determinants of the Product Pattern

The **product pattern** of any refinery is governed by **three intertwined pillars**:

1. **Crude Oil Quality (Feedstock Character)** — API gravity, sulfur content, paraffinic/naphthenic/aromatic balance, TBP curve shape.
2. **Refinery Configuration (Process Units Installed)** — Simple topping → Hydroskimming → Conversion → Deep Conversion.
3. **Market Demand & Economics** — Seasonal demand swings, regional product prices, environmental regulations (e.g., IMO 2020 sulphur cap on marine fuel oil).

### 2.1.1 Influence of Crude Oil Type

| Crude Type | °API Range | Sulfur | Typical Product Bias |
|------------|-----------|--------|---------------------|
| Light Sweet Crude (e.g., Bonny Light, Nigerian) | 32 – 39 | < 0.5 wt% | High gasoline + diesel, low residue |
| Medium Sour Crude (e.g., Arab Medium) | 28 – 32 | 1.0 – 2.0 wt% | Balanced product slate |
| Heavy Sour Crude (e.g., Maya, Athabasca) | 10 – 22 | > 2.5 wt% | High residue, requires cracking |
| Waxy Crude (e.g., Assam) | 25 – 32 | Low | High lube / paraffin yield |

### 2.1.2 Refinery Complexity Levels

A refinery's product pattern is heavily shaped by its **Nelson Complexity Index (NCI)**:

| Refinery Type | Key Units | NCI Range | Typical Product Pattern (vol% of crude) |
|---------------|-----------|-----------|------------------------------------------|
| **Topping (Simple)** | Atmospheric Distillation only | 1.0 – 2.0 | Direct distillation cuts only; high residue |
| **Hydroskimming** | Topping + Reformer + Hydrotreater | 2.0 – 5.0 | Naphtha upgraded; residue still dominant |
| **Conversion** | + FCC / Hydrocracker | 5.0 – 9.0 | Residue converted → more gasoline/diesel |
| **Deep Conversion** | + Coker / Visbreaker / Resid FCC | 9.0 – 15+ | Maximum distillates, minimum residue |

> [!NOTE]
> **Nelson Complexity Index** is defined as: $\text{NCI} = \dfrac{\sum (\text{Capacity}_i \times \text{Complexity Factor}_i)}{\text{Crude Distillation Capacity}}$. Distillation = 1.0; Vacuum Distillation = 1.3; Naphtha Hydrotreating = 2.0; Reforming = 5.0; FCC = 6.0; Hydrocracking = 7.0; Coking = 8.0+.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Units |
|---|--------------------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $\text{°API} = \dfrac{141.5}{SG} - 131.5$ | API gravity from specific gravity | dimensionless |
| 2 | $SG_{60/60°F} = \dfrac{\rho_{\text{oil}}}{\rho_{\text{water}}}$ | Specific Gravity at 60°F | dimensionless |
| 3 | $K = \dfrac{\sqrt[3]{T_{B,°R}}}{SG}$ | Watson / UOP K-factor | dimensionless |
| 4 | $Y_i = \dfrac{V_i}{\sum V_i} \times 100$ | Yield fraction of product $i$ | vol% or wt% |
| 5 | $\text{NCI} = \dfrac{\sum (C_i \cdot f_i)}{C_{\text{crude}}}$ | Nelson Complexity Index | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\eta_{\text{overall}} = \dfrac{\sum V_{\text{useful products}}}{V_{\text{crude input}}} \times 100$ | Overall refinery yield efficiency | % |
| 7 | $T_{°R} = T_{°F} + 459.67$ | Rankine scale conversion | °R |
| 8 | $T_{°F} = 1.8 \cdot T_{°C} + 32$ | Celsius to Fahrenheit | °F |
| 9 | $\rho_{15°C} = \rho_{t} \cdot [1 - \alpha (t - 15)]$ | Volume correction for density | g/cm³ or kg/m³ |
| 10 | $\text{Conradson CR} = \dfrac{W_{\text{residue}}}{W_{\text{sample}}} \times 100$ | Carbon residue (Conradson) | wt% |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorization Tip for KTU Exams:** Always state the **boiling range** and **carbon number** for each product family when discussing product pattern. Examiners frequently award 1–2 marks for this association.

## 2.3 Engineering Real-World Utility

Understanding the product pattern is **not academic** — it is the cornerstone of:

- **Refinery Design & Capacity Planning** — Engineers simulate different crude-process combinations to maximize profit margin.
- **Crude Oil Procurement Strategy** — Refineries buy crude slates that complement their process configuration.
- **Environmental Compliance** — IMO 2020 (max 0.5% S in marine fuel) and BS-VI (10 ppm S in auto fuels) have forced global refiners to *re-engineer* their product patterns.
- **Petrochemical Integration** — Naphtha (a refinery product) is the primary feedstock for steam crackers producing ethylene, propylene, and aromatics.
- **Energy Policy & National Security** — India's "Atmanirbhar Bharat" push is built on optimizing product patterns to reduce import dependence on crude and finished products.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: API Gravity ↔ Specific Gravity Interconversion

### Given:

The **American Petroleum Institute (API)** gravity is defined via specific gravity.

### Derivation:

By definition, the **Specific Gravity (SG)** of a petroleum liquid is:

$$SG = \frac{\rho_{\text{oil at 60°F}}}{\rho_{\text{water at 60°F}}}$$

The API scale is constructed such that an SG of 1.0 corresponds to **°API = 10** (a hydrometer reading rule), and the relationship is **inverse**. The standard form is:

$$\text{°API} = \frac{141.5}{SG} - 131.5$$

**Step 1 — Solve for SG:**

$$\text{°API} + 131.5 = \frac{141.5}{SG}$$

$$SG = \frac{141.5}{\text{°API} + 131.5}$$

**Step 2 — Numerical Worked Example:**

A crude oil has **°API = 32.0**. Find its specific gravity and density in kg/m³.

$$SG = \frac{141.5}{32.0 + 131.5} = \frac{141.5}{163.5}$$

$$SG = 0.8654 \; (\text{dimensionless})$$

Since $\rho_{\text{water at 60°F}} = 999.04 \;\text{kg/m}^3 \approx 1000 \;\text{kg/m}^3$:

$$\rho_{\text{oil}} = SG \times \rho_{\text{water}} = 0.8654 \times 999.04$$

$$\rho_{\text{oil}} \approx 864.5 \;\text{kg/m}^3$$

### Physical Interpretation:

A higher **°API** means **lower density** (lighter crude), which generally yields more **gasoline and middle distillates**. A lower **°API** indicates a **heavier crude**, yielding more **residue, fuel oil, and bitumen**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Watson Characterization (K) Factor

### Given:

The **UOP K-factor** quantifies the **paraffinicity** of a hydrocarbon fraction.

### Derivation:

The Watson K-factor is defined as the cube root of the **mean average boiling point (in °R)** divided by the **specific gravity**:

$$K = \frac{(T_{B})^{1/3}}{SG}$$

**Where:**
- $T_B$ = **Mean Average Boiling Point (MABP) in °Rankine (°R)**
- $SG$ = Specific Gravity at 60°F/60°F

**Step 1 — Conversions:**

$$T_{B, °R} = T_{B, °F} + 459.67$$
$$T_{B, °F} = 1.8 \cdot T_{B, °C} + 32$$

**Step 2 — Worked Example:**

A naphtha cut has a mean average boiling point of **120 °C** and **SG = 0.74**. Calculate its K-factor.

$$T_{B, °C} = 120 \;°C$$
$$T_{B, °F} = 1.8 \times 120 + 32 = 216 + 32 = 248 \;°F$$
$$T_{B, °R} = 248 + 459.67 = 707.67 \;°R$$

$$K = \frac{(707.67)^{1/3}}{0.74}$$

$$(707.67)^{1/3} \approx 8.911$$

$$K = \frac{8.911}{0.74} \approx 12.04$$

### Physical Interpretation of K-Factor:

| K-Factor Range | Hydrocarbon Type | Typical Products |
|----------------|------------------|------------------|
| K > 12.0 | Paraffinic | Excellent lube base stocks, jet fuel |
| 11.5 < K < 12.0 | Naphthenic-Paraffinic | Gasoline, kerosene |
| 10.5 < K < 11.5 | Naphthenic | Gasoline, aromatics |
| K < 10.5 | Aromatic | Solvents, chemical feedstocks |

> [!NOTE]
> **For KTU:** A common exam question asks to *classify* a crude or fraction based on its K-factor. Always state the K-factor range and the corresponding hydrocarbon family.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Overall Refinery Yield Calculation

### Given:

A refinery processes **100,000 barrels per day (BPSD)** of crude oil. The output streams (after processing) are tabulated. Calculate the **product pattern (vol%)** and the **overall distillate yield**.

### Hypothetical Refinery Output Data (Modern Indian Coastal Refinery):

| Product | Daily Volume (BPD) |
|---------|-------------------|
| LPG | 2,500 |
| Naphtha | 8,000 |
| Gasoline (MS) | 22,000 |
| Kerosene / ATF | 8,500 |
| Diesel (HSD) | 38,000 |
| Lube Base Oil | 3,500 |
| Fuel Oil (FO) | 14,000 |
| Bitumen | 2,500 |
| Petroleum Coke | 1,000 |
| **Total** | **100,000** |

### Step-by-Step Calculation:

**Step 1 — Verify Material Balance:**

$$\sum V_{\text{products}} = 2{,}500 + 8{,}000 + 22{,}000 + 8{,}500 + 38{,}000 + 3{,}500 + 14{,}000 + 2{,}500 + 1{,}000 = 100{,}000 \;\text{BPD}$$

$$\text{Material balance closes} \;\; \checkmark$$

**Step 2 — Compute Volume % of Each Product:**

$$Y_{\text{LPG}} = \frac{2{,}500}{100{,}000} \times 100 = 2.50 \;\text{vol\%}$$

$$Y_{\text{Naphtha}} = \frac{8{,}000}{100{,}000} \times 100 = 8.00 \;\text{vol\%}$$

$$Y_{\text{Gasoline}} = \frac{22{,}000}{100{,}000} \times 100 = 22.00 \;\text{vol\%}$$

$$Y_{\text{Kerosene/ATF}} = \frac{8{,}500}{100{,}000} \times 100 = 8.50 \;\text{vol\%}$$

$$Y_{\text{Diesel}} = \frac{38{,}000}{100{,}000} \times 100 = 38.00 \;\text{vol\%}$$

$$Y_{\text{Lube Oil}} = \frac{3{,}500}{100{,}000} \times 100 = 3.50 \;\text{vol\%}$$

$$Y_{\text{Fuel Oil}} = \frac{14{,}000}{100{,}000} \times 100 = 14.00 \;\text{vol\%}$$

$$Y_{\text{Bitumen}} = \frac{2{,}500}{100{,}000} \times 100 = 2.50 \;\text{vol\%}$$

$$Y_{\text{Pet Coke}} = \frac{1{,}000}{100{,}000} \times 100 = 1.00 \;\text{vol\%}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the "White Product" (Distillate) Yield:**

"White products" are the high-value light & middle distillates: LPG, Naphtha, Gasoline, Kerosene/ATF, Diesel.

$$\eta_{\text{white}} = 2.50 + 8.00 + 22.00 + 8.50 + 38.00 = 79.00 \;\text{vol\%}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate the "Black Product" Yield:**

"Black products" are the lower-value heavy residues: Fuel Oil, Bitumen, Pet Coke.

$$\eta_{\text{black}} = 14.00 + 2.50 + 1.00 = 17.50 \;\text{vol\%}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Losses** (unaccounted fractions, plant fuel use, etc.) account for the remaining: $100 - 79.00 - 17.50 = 3.50 \;\text{vol\%}$.

### Final Product Pattern Table:

| Product | Yield (vol%) |
|---------|--------------|
| LPG | 2.50 |
| Naphtha | 8.00 |
| Gasoline (MS) | 22.00 |
| Kerosene / ATF | 8.50 |
| Diesel (HSD) | 38.00 |
| Lube Oil | 3.50 |
| Fuel Oil | 14.00 |
| Bitumen | 2.50 |
| Pet Coke | 1.00 |
| Losses | 3.50 |
| **Total** | **100.00** |

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: How Process Units Reshape the Product Pattern

| Process Unit | Feedstock | Primary Product Boosted | By-Products | Net Effect on Pattern |
|--------------|-----------|--------------------------|-------------|----------------------|
| Atmospheric Distillation (CDU) | Crude | All direct cuts | Residue | Establishes base pattern |
| Vacuum Distillation (VDU) | Atmospheric Residue | Lube stocks, VGO | Vacuum residue | Shifts yield → more distillates |
| Catalytic Reformer | Heavy Naphtha | High-octane gasoline (RON 95–100) | H₂, LPG | ↑ Gasoline, ↓ Naphtha |
| Fluid Catalytic Cracker (FCC) | VGO, Atmospheric Residue | Gasoline, LPG, LCO | Decant oil, coke | ↑ Gasoline, ↓ Fuel Oil |
| Hydrocracker | VGO, Coker gas oil | Diesel, jet fuel, naphtha | Light gases | ↑ Distillates, ↓ Residue |
| Delayed Coker | Vacuum Residue | Coker gas oil, naphtha, gas | Petroleum coke | Converts residue → distillates |
| Visbreaker | Atmospheric Residue | Fuel oil (lower viscosity) | Gasoline, gas | ↑ Light ends, ↓ Viscosity FO |
| Alkylation | C₃ / C₄ olefins + isobutane | Alkylate (high-octane gasoline blend) | — | ↑ Gasoline octane |
| Isomerization | Light straight-run naphtha | Iso-paraffins | — | ↑ Gasoline octane |
| Hydrotreating | Naphtha / Diesel / Resid | Desulfurized products | H₂S | ↓ Sulfur content (quality ↑) |
| Steam Cracker (Petrochem) | Naphtha | Ethylene, propylene, aromatics | Pyrolysis gasoline | Diverts naphtha from fuel pool |

---

## 3.5 Worked Example: API Gravity → Mass Conversion

**Problem:** A refinery produces **30,000 BPD of diesel (HSD)** with **°API = 35**. Calculate the **mass flow rate in tonnes/day**.

**Step 1 — Specific Gravity:**

$$SG = \frac{141.5}{35 + 131.5} = \frac{141.5}{166.5} = 0.8498$$

**Step 2 — Density (kg/m³):**

$$\rho = 0.8498 \times 999.04 = 849.0 \;\text{kg/m}^3$$

**Step 3 — Convert BPD to m³/day:**

1 barrel = **0.158987 m³**

$$V = 30{,}000 \times 0.158987 = 4{,}769.6 \;\text{m}^3/\text{day}$$

**Step 4 — Mass Flow Rate:**

$$\dot{m} = V \times \rho = 4{,}769.6 \times 849.0 = 4{,}049{,}390 \;\text{kg/day}$$

$$\dot{m} \approx 4{,}049.4 \;\text{tonnes/day}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** When asked for mass flow, **never** skip the barrel-to-m³ conversion. Examiners often deduct **1 mark** for this step.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the term "Product Pattern" as used in petroleum refinery engineering. List the major product families obtained from crude oil refining along with their approximate boiling ranges.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1.5 Marks):** Product Pattern refers to the distribution or yield proportion (vol% or wt%) of various finished petroleum products obtained from a refinery when a given quantity of crude oil is processed. It is determined by crude oil composition, refinery configuration, and market demand.

**Product Families Table (1.5 Marks):**

| Product | Boiling Range (°C) | Carbon Number |
|---------|-------------------|---------------|
| LPG / Refinery Gas | < 40 | C₁ – C₄ |
| Naphtha | 40 – 180 | C₅ – C₁₁ |
| Gasoline (MS) | 40 – 205 | C₅ – C₁₂ |
| Kerosene / ATF | 150 – 300 | C₁₁ – C₁₈ |
| Diesel (HSD) | 250 – 380 | C₁₄ – C₂₅ |
| Lube Oil | 380 – 550 | C₂₅ – C₄₀ |
| Fuel Oil | 380 – 600+ | C₂₅ – C₆₀ |
| Bitumen / Coke | > 500 | C₃₀+ |

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Explain the role of the Fluid Catalytic Cracker (FCC) in modifying the product pattern of a refinery.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The FCC is a **secondary conversion unit** that takes heavy feedstocks (VGO, atmospheric residue) and cracks them into lighter, higher-value products using a **zeolite catalyst** at **480 – 540 °C**. It significantly alters the product pattern by:
1. **Increasing gasoline yield** (40–50 vol% of FCC feed) by converting VGO into cracked naphtha.
2. **Producing LPG-grade olefins** (propylene, butylene) for alkylation / petrochemicals.
3. **Generating Light Cycle Oil (LCO)** as a diesel blend stock.
4. **Yielding decant oil** that goes to fuel oil blending.
5. Producing small quantities of **coke** deposited on the catalyst (regenerated by burning).

**Net effect on product pattern:** ↑ Gasoline by 15–20 vol%, ↑ LPG, ↓ Heavy Fuel Oil. (3 Marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks):**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | Model Question]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** A refinery processes **80,000 BPD of crude oil** with the following output volumes:

| Product | BPD |
|---------|-----|
| LPG | 2,000 |
| Naphtha | 6,400 |
| Gasoline | 18,000 |
| Kerosene | 7,200 |
| Diesel | 30,400 |
| Lube Oil | 2,800 |
| Fuel Oil | 10,000 |
| Bitumen | 1,600 |
| Pet Coke | 800 |
| Losses | 800 |

**Calculate:** (i) The vol% yield of each product. (ii) The total "white product" yield. (iii) The Nelson Complexity-equivalent class of this refinery.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain how the addition of a **Catalytic Reformer and an FCC unit** to a topping refinery modifies its product pattern. Support your answer with quantitative justification.

---

### **Model Answer — Question A:**

#### Part (a) — Solution [7 Marks]:

**Step 1: Verify Material Balance (1 Mark):**

$$\sum V = 2{,}000 + 6{,}400 + 18{,}000 + 7{,}200 + 30{,}400 + 2{,}800 + 10{,}000 + 1{,}600 + 800 + 800 = 80{,}000 \;\text{BPD}\;\checkmark$$

**Step 2: Compute Volume % (5 Marks):**

| Product | BPD | Yield (vol%) |
|---------|-----|--------------|
| LPG | 2,000 | 2.50 |
| Naphtha | 6,400 | 8.00 |
| Gasoline | 18,000 | 22.50 |
| Kerosene | 7,200 | 9.00 |
| Diesel | 30,400 | 38.00 |
| Lube Oil | 2,800 | 3.50 |
| Fuel Oil | 10,000 | 12.50 |
| Bitumen | 1,600 | 2.00 |
| Pet Coke | 800 | 1.00 |
| Losses | 800 | 1.00 |
| **Total** | **80,000** | **100.00** |

**Step 3: White Product Yield (0.5 Mark):**
