$$\eta_{\text{white}} = 2.50 + 8.00 + 22.50 + 9.00 + 38.00 = 80.00 \;\text{vol\%}$$

**Step 4: Refinery Classification (0.5 Mark):**

Since FCC and coker units are present (evidenced by Pet Coke and high distillate yield), this is a **Deep Conversion Refinery**, NCI > 9.0.

---

#### Part (b) — Solution [7 Marks]:

**Topping Refinery Product Pattern (Baseline, 3 Marks):**

| Product | Vol% |
|---------|------|
| LPG | 1.5 |
| Naphtha | 7.0 |
| Gasoline | 18.0 |
| Kerosene | 10.0 |
| Diesel | 28.0 |
| Fuel Oil + Residue | 35.5 |

**After Adding Catalytic Reformer (1.5 Marks):**
- Heavy naphtha (7 vol%) is upgraded to **reformate** with RON 95–100.
- Gasoline yield increases from 18% → **25–28%**.
- Naphtha as such in finished pool decreases (it is consumed as feed).

**After Adding FCC (2.5 Marks):**
- VGO (≈18% of crude) is cracked.
- Additional gasoline: **+15 vol%** → Total gasoline reaches **40–45%**.
- LPG yield rises to **4–5%** (from 1.5%).
- Fuel oil drops from 35% → **8–10%**.

**Final Modified Pattern (in tabular form):**

| Product | Topping | + Reformer | + FCC |
|---------|---------|------------|-------|
| LPG | 1.5 | 1.5 | 4.5 |
| Naphtha | 7.0 | 2.0 | 3.0 |
| Gasoline | 18.0 | 28.0 | 43.0 |
| Kerosene | 10.0 | 10.0 | 10.0 |
| Diesel | 28.0 | 28.0 | 32.0 |
| Fuel Oil | 35.5 | 30.5 | 7.5 |

**Block Diagram (Schematic, 0 Marks — described verbally):**
Crude → CDU → (Naphtha → Reformer → Reformate to Gasoline Pool); (Atmospheric Residue → VDU → VGO → FCC → Cracked Naphtha + Gasoline + LCO); LCO blended into diesel pool; Decant oil to fuel oil.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 | Model Question]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** A crude oil has **°API = 28** and a mean average boiling point of **450 °C**. Calculate:
(i) Specific gravity.
(ii) Density in kg/m³ at 60°F.
(iii) Watson (UOP) K-factor.
(iv) Classify the crude as paraffinic, naphthenic, or aromatic based on the K-factor.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the effect of **Nelson Complexity Index** on the product pattern. Compare the product slates of (i) a simple topping refinery and (ii) a deep conversion refinery, using representative volume percentages.

---

### **Model Answer — Question B:**

#### Part (a) — Solution [7 Marks]:

**Step 1: Specific Gravity (1.5 Marks):**

$$SG = \frac{141.5}{28 + 131.5} = \frac{141.5}{159.5} = 0.8871$$

**Step 2: Density at 60°F (1 Mark):**

$\rho_{\text{water at 60°F}} = 999.04 \;\text{kg/m}^3$

$$\rho_{\text{oil}} = 0.8871 \times 999.04 = 886.3 \;\text{kg/m}^3$$

**Step 3: Temperature Conversion to °Rankine (1 Mark):**

$$T_{B, °C} = 450 \;°C$$
$$T_{B, °F} = 1.8 \times 450 + 32 = 810 + 32 = 842 \;°F$$
$$T_{B, °R} = 842 + 459.67 = 1301.67 \;°R$$

**Step 4: K-Factor (1.5 Marks):**

$$K = \frac{(T_{B, °R})^{1/3}}{SG} = \frac{(1301.67)^{1/3}}{0.8871}$$

$$(1301.67)^{1/3} \approx 10.92$$

$$K = \frac{10.92}{0.8871} \approx 12.31$$

**Step 5: Classification (2 Marks):**

Since **K ≈ 12.31 > 12.0**, the crude is classified as **Paraffinic** (high paraffin content). Such crudes yield high-quality lubricating oils, paraffin wax, and clean-burning middle distillates.

---

#### Part (b) — Solution [7 Marks]:

**Definition of NCI (1.5 Marks):**

The **Nelson Complexity Index (NCI)** is a dimensionless metric that quantifies refinery sophistication:

$$\text{NCI} = \frac{\sum (C_i \cdot f_i)}{C_{\text{crude distillation}}}$$

where $C_i$ is the capacity of unit $i$ and $f_i$ is its complexity factor (Distillation = 1.0, Reforming = 5.0, FCC = 6.0, Hydrocracking = 7.0, Coking = 8.0+).

**Effect of NCI on Product Pattern (2.5 Marks):**

As NCI increases:
- Heavy residue yield **decreases** dramatically.
- Light & middle distillate yields (gasoline, diesel) **increase**.
- The refinery becomes more **profitable** in gasoline- and diesel-driven markets.
- Capital and operating costs rise sharply (justifying investment only at large scales).

**Comparative Product Slate (3 Marks):**

| Product (vol%) | Topping (NCI ≈ 1.5) | Deep Conversion (NCI ≈ 12) |
|----------------|----------------------|------------------------------|
| LPG | 1.5 | 5.0 |
| Naphtha | 7.0 | 4.0 |
| Gasoline | 18.0 | 48.0 |
| Kerosene | 10.0 | 10.0 |
| Diesel | 28.0 | 30.0 |
| Fuel Oil | 35.0 | 1.5 |
| Coke | 0.0 | 1.5 |

**Conclusion:** Higher NCI = **more conversion of residue → distillates**, which aligns with the global market shift from fuel oil to clean transportation fuels.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Ways KTU Students Lose Marks on Product Pattern Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Product Pattern" with "Product Specification"** — Pattern = *quantities*; Specification = *qualities*. Examiners dock **2–3 marks** for mixing them up.
> 2. **Skipping the material balance check** — Always verify that $\sum V_{\text{products}} = V_{\text{crude}}$. A balanced sheet is mandatory.
> 3. **Forgetting unit conversions** — BPD → m³/day → tonnes/day requires **0.158987 m³/bbl** and density in kg/m³. Skipping a step loses **1 mark**.
> 4. **Wrong carbon number ranges** — Memorize: LPG (C₁–C₄), Naphtha (C₅–C₁₁), Gasoline (C₅–C₁₂), Kerosene (C₁₁–C₁₈), Diesel (C₁₄–C₂₅), Lube (C₂₅–C₄₀).
> 5. **Not stating the K-factor classification** explicitly — Always end with *"Therefore, the crude is paraffinic/naphthenic/aromatic"*.
> 6. **Ignoring refinery configuration** — When asked to "discuss" product pattern, always link the pattern to specific process units (CDU, VDU, FCC, Reformer, Coker).
> 7. **No block diagram in long answers** — A 14-mark question **without** a flow diagram loses at least **2 marks** in the KTU valuation scheme.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Pre-Exam Checklist — Print and Memorize:**

- [ ] **Definition of Product Pattern** — The vol% (or wt%) distribution of finished products from refining.
- [ ] **Three Pillars of Product Pattern** — Crude Quality + Refinery Configuration + Market Demand.
- [ ] **API Gravity Formula** — $\text{°API} = \dfrac{141.5}{SG} - 131.5$ and its inverse.
- [ ] **Watson K-Factor** — $K = \dfrac{(T_{B, °R})^{1/3}}{SG}$ with classification ranges (K > 12 = Paraffinic).
- [ ] **Boiling Ranges & Carbon Numbers** — LPG, Naphtha, Gasoline, Kerosene, Diesel, Lube, FO, Bitumen.
- [ ] **Nelson Complexity Index** — Know definitions and the conversion sequence (Topping → Hydroskimming → Conversion → Deep Conversion).
- [ ] **FCC Impact** — ↑ Gasoline by 15–20 vol%, ↓ Fuel Oil.
- [ ] **Coker Impact** — Converts residue to gas oil + coke; eliminates fuel oil.
- [ ] **Reformer Impact** — Upgrades naphtha to high-octane gasoline.
- [ ] **White vs Black Products** — White = LPG+Naphtha+Gasoline+Kero+Diesel; Black = FO+Bitumen+Coke.
- [ ] **Unit Conversions** — 1 bbl = 0.158987 m³; °C→°F→°R conversion chain.
- [ ] **Material Balance Always Closes** — Inlet = Outlet + Losses.
- [ ] **Block Diagram is Mandatory** in 14-mark questions.
- [ ] **Real-World Relevance** — IMO 2020, BS-VI, Atmanirbhar Bharat, petrochemical integration.

---

**End of Module 1 — Topic: Product Pattern**
*Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020) | PEPOT638 — Petroleum Refinery Engineering*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Crude Heating
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT638 - PETROLEUM REFINERY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Crude Heating

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Crude Heating

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Crude Heating** in a petroleum refinery refers to the controlled thermal processing of raw petroleum crude oil prior to its separation into useful product fractions. The heating operation is accomplished through two integrated systems: (i) the **Crude Preheat Train** — a network of shell-and-tube heat exchangers that recover waste heat from hot product streams, and (ii) the **Process Furnace (Pipe-Still Heater)** — a direct-fired unit that supplies the balance of sensible heat required to bring the desalted crude to its **flash zone temperature** (typically **343 °C to 399 °C** for an Atmospheric Distillation Unit, ADU).

The thermodynamic objective is to maximize the recovery of high-grade process heat while satisfying the minimum **Approach Temperature (ΔT_min)** constraint, typically maintained at **20–40 °C** in modern KTU-referenced refinery designs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> Crude Heating is the gateway unit operation of any crude distillation complex. The student must understand that approximately **30–50 % of the total refinery energy** is consumed in this section, making it the single largest energy sink in a modern refinery.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Boiling Water Kitchen" Intuition

Imagine you are cooking a large pot of soup on a gas stove. Before you even light the burner, you place the cold soup pot near the hot plate where another pot has just finished cooking. The pot gradually warms up. Only when the soup is "almost hot" do you transfer it to the gas burner for the final heating. In the refinery, the **crude oil plays the role of the cold soup pot**, the **hot product streams** (naphtha, kerosene, diesel, gas oil leaving the column) act as the **residual hot plate**, and the **furnace** is the **gas burner** that provides the final high-grade heat.

This pre-warming step is critical because if you tried to heat the entire crude charge from room temperature to 380 °C using only the furnace, you would burn enormous amounts of fuel and damage the furnace tubes. The preheat train cleverly **"recycles"** the heat that would otherwise be wasted through cooling-water exchangers and air-cooled fin-fans.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Conditions in KTU 2024 Course Work:**
> - **API Gravity of Crude:** 28° to 42°
> - **Desalter Outlet Temperature:** 120 °C to 150 °C
> - **Preheat Train Exit (Furnace Inlet) Temperature:** 230 °C to 290 °C
> - **Furnace Outlet (Flash Zone) Temperature:** 343 °C to 399 °C
> - **Heat Recovery Target in Modern Refineries:** ≥ 65 % of total fired heat

## 1.3 Significance in the Refinery Flow Scheme

The crude heating subsystem sits between the **Desalting Unit (Electrostatic)** and the **Atmospheric Distillation Column**. Without proper heating, the subsequent fractional separation of naphtha, kerosene, diesel, and atmospheric gas oil (AGO) is impossible because the relative volatility of hydrocarbons depends strongly on temperature.

The crude heating stage directly influences:
- **Product cut-point accuracy**
- **Energy efficiency of the entire refinery complex**
- **Tube metal life and run length of the furnace**
- **Emissions of CO₂, NOₓ, and SO₂**
- **Refinery carbon footprint and profitability**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Crude Preheat Train — Operating Philosophy

The preheat train is a **counter-current network of shell-and-tube heat exchangers** where the cold crude flows opposite to the descending hot product streams taken from the side draws and overhead of the atmospheric distillation column.

The operating logic follows three strict KTU-level principles:

- **Principle 1 — Heat Cascade Recovery:** Hot streams are cooled from their bubble-point down toward their storage/ pump-around temperature, while the cold crude is simultaneously heated from ambient (~30 °C) up to ~270 °C.
- **Principle 2 — Pinch Compliance:** The minimum temperature difference between any hot and cold stream at the pinch point is the **ΔT_min**. Modern pinch analysis (Linnhoff) demands **ΔT_min ≈ 20–40 °C**.
- **Principle 3 — Phase-Respect:** When the cold crude enters the exchanger, the hot stream may be partially condensing or partially vaporizing. The LMTD must therefore use the correct phase-correct enthalpies on each side.

## 2.2 The Process Furnace (Pipe-Still Heater)

The furnace supplies the **sensible heat** to raise the desalted, preheated crude from the **furnace inlet temperature** to the **flash-zone temperature**. Three furnace geometries dominate KTU textbook problems:

| Furnace Type | Heat Release Density | Suitability |
|---|---|---|
| **Cylindrical (Vertical)** | High radiant flux | High throughput, clean fuels |
| **Cabin (Up-shot / Down-shot)** | Medium | Multi-pass service, ADU/VDU duty |
| **Box (Horizontal)** | Uniform distribution | Moderate duty, revamp projects |

Each furnace consists of two distinct thermal zones:

1. **Convection Section** (top): recovers heat from the flue gas to preheat combustion air, boiler feed water, or the crude itself (a "convection bank" of finned tubes).
2. **Radiant Section** (bottom): receives > **70 % of the heat duty** through direct flame radiation, with tube-side crude reaching the required **tube-metal-limit temperature** (typically **620–700 °C** for 9 % Cr / 1 % Mo alloy steel tubes).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept — Tube Skin Temperature:**
> The radiant section is the heart of the furnace. Tubes are arranged in vertical or horizontal passes on the furnace walls and sometimes on the floor (long-flame or short-flame configuration). The crude is heated here primarily by radiation, with the convection section providing only the final trim.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following markdown table consolidates every governing equation you will need to answer KTU Part-B numerical questions on Crude Heating.

| # | Equation Name | Mathematical Form | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Overall Heat Transfer** | $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{lmtd}$ | $U$ in $\text{W/m}^2\text{·K}$, $A$ in $\text{m}^2$ |
| 2 | **LMTD** | $\Delta T_{lmtd} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ | $\Delta T_1, \Delta T_2$ in K or °C |
| 3 | **Heat Capacity Rate Ratio** | $C_{min} = (\dot{m} \cdot c_p)_{min}$, $C_{max} = (\dot{m} \cdot c_p)_{max}$ | W/K |
| 4 | **Heat Exchanger Effectiveness** | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{max,possible}}$ | Dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| 5 | **NTU Relation (1-2 Shell & Tube)** | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{2 \left[1 + C_r + \sqrt{1 + C_r^2} \cdot \dfrac{1 + e^{-NTU\sqrt{1+C_r^2}}}{1 - e^{-NTU\sqrt{1+C_r^2}}} \right]^{-1}}{2}$ | $C_r = C_{min}/C_{max}$ |
| 6 | **Furnace Heat Duty** | $Q_{furnace} = \dot{m}_{crude} \cdot \left[ \int_{T_{in}}^{T_{flash}} c_p(T)\, dT \right]$ | W or kW |
| 7 | **Combustion Stoichiometry (CH₄)** | $\text{CH}_4 + 2\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$ | — |
| 8 | **Lower Heating Value (CH₄)** | $LHV_{CH_4} = 50{,}016 \text{ kJ/kg} = 802.3 \text{ MJ/kmol}$ | — |
| 9 | **Thermal Efficiency of Furnace** | $\eta_{furnace} = \dfrac{Q_{absorbed}}{Q_{fuel,LHV}}$ | % |
| 10 | **Fouling Factor Inclusion** | $\dfrac{1}{U_{dirty}} = \dfrac{1}{U_{clean}} + R_{foul}$ | $R_{foul}$ in $\text{m}^2\text{·K/W}$ |
| 11 | **Tube-Side Velocity (Re)** | $Re_D = \dfrac{\rho \cdot v \cdot D}{\mu}$ | Dimensionless |
| 12 | **Heat Recovery % (Pinch Index)** | $\%HR = \dfrac{Q_{recovered}}{Q_{recovered} + Q_{furnace}} \times 100$ | % |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Convention:** For LMTD, when either $\Delta T_1$ or $\Delta T_2$ is **zero**, the harmonic mean form must be used: $\Delta T_{lmtd} = \Delta T_1 = \Delta T_2$ (the two endpoints are equal). This case arises in single-phase condensers or boilers.

## 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility

The crude preheat train is the cornerstone of **refinery energy integration**. The modern concept of **Pinch Analysis** (developed by Linnhoff) targets a **ΔT_min** to maximize the network's heat recovery. Every 1 °C increase in the furnace inlet crude temperature translates into approximately **0.5–0.8 kg of fuel-oil saving per ton of crude processed**, an enormous economic and environmental lever. KTU examiners frequently frame numerical problems around heat-recovery optimization and ΔT_min analysis.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Log Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD)

The LMTD is the *single most important* heat-exchanger equation in the entire KTU PEPOT638 syllabus. Below is the complete, line-by-line derivation that you should reproduce in a 7-mark sub-question.

### Step 1 — Statement of the Differential Heat Transfer

Consider an elemental length $dA$ of a counter-flow shell-and-tube heat exchanger. The cold fluid enters at $T_{c,in}$ and leaves at $T_{c,out}$. The hot fluid enters at $T_{h,in}$ and leaves at $T_{h,out}$.

The elemental heat transferred across the differential area is given by Newton's law of cooling:

$$
dQ = U \cdot dA \cdot \Delta T
$$

where $\Delta T$ is the local temperature difference between the hot and the cold streams at that position.

### Step 2 — Express the Heat in Terms of Cold-Side Stream Capacity

An energy balance on the cold stream gives the rate of change of the cold fluid enthalpy:

$$
dQ = -\dot{m}_c \cdot c_{p,c} \cdot dT_c
$$

The negative sign is included because as the cold fluid gains heat, its temperature *increases* in the direction of flow. Equating the two expressions for $dQ$:

$$
U \cdot dA \cdot \Delta T = \dot{m}_c \cdot c_{p,c} \cdot dT_c
$$

### Step 3 — Substitute $\Delta T = T_h - T_c$

Rearranging to isolate $dA$:

$$
dA = \dfrac{\dot{m}_c \cdot c_{p,c} \cdot dT_c}{U \cdot (T_h - T_c)}
$$

### Step 4 — Relate the Hot and Cold Stream Temperatures via a Single Combined Energy Balance

A macroscopic energy balance between the inlet and outlet of the heat exchanger (assuming no heat loss to surroundings) states:

$$
\dot{m}_c \cdot c_{p,c} \cdot (T_{c,out} - T_{c,in}) = \dot{m}_h \cdot c_{p,h} \cdot (T_{h,in} - T_{h,out})
$$

We may therefore replace the hot stream temperature with a function of the cold stream temperature using the linear interpolation along the length of the exchanger. Let $\Delta T$ be expressed as a linear function of $T_c$:

$$
T_h = T_c + (T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}) - \left[ \dfrac{\dot{m}_c c_{p,c}}{\dot{m}_h c_{p,h}} \right](T_c - T_{c,in})
$$

For simplicity, and to recover the classical LMTD form, we use a heat-capacity-rate ratio $C = \dot{m}_c c_{p,c} = \dot{m}_h c_{p,h}$ (equal capacity rates) or simply use the integrated form directly.

### Step 5 — Integrate Over the Full Heat Exchanger Area

Integrating $dA$ from the cold inlet (where $T_c = T_{c,in}$, $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}$) to the cold outlet (where $T_c = T_{c,out}$, $\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,out}$):

$$
A = \int_{\Delta T_2}^{\Delta T_1} \dfrac{\dot{m}_c c_{p,c}}{U} \cdot \dfrac{d(\Delta T)}{\Delta T}
$$

### Step 6 — Perform the Logarithmic Integration

The integral of $1/\Delta T$ yields the natural logarithm. Performing the integration explicitly:

$$
A = \dfrac{\dot{m}_c c_{p,c}}{U} \cdot \ln \left( \dfrac{\Delta T_1}{\Delta T_2} \right)
$$

### Step 7 — Solve for Q = U·A·LMTD

Multiplying both sides by $U$ and recognizing that the total heat transferred equals $Q = \dot{m}_c c_{p,c} (T_{c,out} - T_{c,in}) = \dot{m}_c c_{p,c} (\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2)$:

$$
Q = U \cdot A \cdot \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}
$$

### Step 8 — Final Definition

$$
\boxed{\Delta T_{lmtd} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}}
$$

This is the rigorous LMTD used universally in KTU Board Examination answers.

> [!NOTE]
> **Special Case — Endpoints Equal:** If $\Delta T_1 = \Delta T_2 = \Delta T$, the indeterminate form $0/0$ is resolved by L'Hôpital's rule, giving $\Delta T_{lmtd} = \Delta T$. This is the KTU-favoured "boiling or condensing" condition.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — Furnace Thermal Efficiency from First-Principle Energy Balance

The thermal efficiency of a fired heater is defined as the ratio of heat absorbed by the process fluid to the chemical energy released by the fuel. This is a high-yield KTU derivation worth 7 marks.

### Step 1 — Define the Energy Out of the Fuel

The fuel (typically methane, refinery off-gas, or fuel oil) is burned completely. The chemical energy released per unit mass of fuel is the **Lower Heating Value (LHV)**:

$$
Q_{fuel} = \dot{m}_{fuel} \cdot LHV_{fuel} \quad [\text{kW}]
$$

### Step 2 — Define the Energy Absorbed by the Crude in the Furnace

The crude enters the radiant section at $T_{in}$ and leaves at $T_{out}$ (≈ $T_{flash}$). For a temperature-dependent specific heat:

$$
Q_{absorbed} = \dot{m}_{crude} \cdot \int_{T_{in}}^{T_{out}} c_p(T) \, dT
$$

Assuming a constant $c_p$ for simplicity (KTU's standard assumption in 14-mark problems):

$$
Q_{absorbed} = \dot{m}_{crude} \cdot c_{p,crude} \cdot (T_{out} - T_{in}) \quad [\text{kW}]
$$

### Step 3 — Define the Stack Heat Loss

The flue gas leaves the stack at $T_{stack}$ (≈ 180–220 °C) carrying sensible heat:

$$
Q_{stack} = \dot{m}_{flue} \cdot c_{p,flue} \cdot (T_{stack} - T_{air})
$$

### Step 4 — Write the Furnace Energy Balance

$$
Q_{fuel} = Q_{absorbed} + Q_{stack} + Q_{wall} + Q_{convection,losses}
$$

where $Q_{wall}$ is the heat lost through the refractory walls (usually < 2 % of $Q_{fuel}$ for an insulated furnace).

### Step 5 — Define Thermal Efficiency

$$
\eta_{furnace} = \dfrac{Q_{absorbed}}{Q_{fuel}} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{crude} \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{out} - T_{in})}{\dot{m}_{fuel} \cdot LHV}
$$

### Step 6 — Solve a Typical KTU Numerical

Suppose $100{,}000 \text{ kg/h}$ of crude ($c_p = 2.5 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$) is heated from 280 °C to 380 °C. The required absorption:

$$
Q_{absorbed} = \left( \dfrac{100{,}000}{3600} \right) \cdot 2.5 \cdot (380 - 280) = 6{,}944 \text{ kW}
$$

If furnace efficiency is 88 % and fuel LHV is 42 MJ/kg (typical for fuel oil):

$$
\dot{m}_{fuel} = \dfrac{6{,}944}{0.88 \times 42{,}000} \cdot 1000 = 187.7 \text{ kg/h}
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Pinch Analysis Cold-Stream Targeting Table

This table illustrates how a KTU examiner may frame a problem: *"Given the following hot and cold streams, calculate the minimum heating and minimum cooling utility for a ΔT_min of 20 °C."*

| Stream | Type | Initial T (°C) | Final T (°C) | $\Delta H$ (MW) | Mass × Cp (MW/K) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Naphtha vapor | Hot | 145 | 45 | 18.0 | 0.180 |
| Kerosene | Hot | 245 | 60 | 12.5 | 0.068 |
| Diesel | Hot | 320 | 70 | 22.4 | 0.090 |
| Atmospheric gas oil | Hot | 340 | 95 | 14.0 | 0.057 |
| Crude oil (preheat) | Cold | 30 | 270 | 66.9 | 0.279 |
| — | — | — | **Totals** | **Q_H = 66.9** | — |

From the matrix, $Q_{H,min} = 0$ (no external heating required) and $Q_{C,min} = 0$ (no external cooling required) when the network is optimally integrated. In practice, real pinch analysis with ΔT_min = 20 °C yields a small residual $Q_{H,min,furnace} = 8 \text{–} 12 \text{ MW}$ that the fired heater must supply.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics — Crude Heating Architecture

The following Mermaid block renders the **block-level functional architecture of a Crude Heating Network**, mapping every interaction from the desalter outlet to the flash zone of the atmospheric distillation column. This is the most exam-relevant diagram in Module 1.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    %% Top-level feed and product streams
    subgraph S1["DESALTING SECTION"]
        DESALTER["DESALTER UNIT 120-150 C"]
    end

    subgraph S2["CRUDE PREHEAT TRAIN COUNTER CURRENT NETWORK"]
        EX1["EXCHANGER 01 NAPHTHA OVERHEAD 145 TO 45 C"]
        EX2["EXCHANGER 02 KEROSINE SIDE DRAW 245 TO 60 C"]
        EX3["EXCHANGER 03 DIESLE SIDE DRAW 320 TO 70 C"]
        EX4["EXCHANGER 04 AGO PUMP AROUND 340 TO 95 C"]
        EX5["EXCHANGER 05 HVGO BOTTOM 360 TO 110 C"]
    end

    subgraph S3["FURNACE FIRED HEATER ZONE"]
        CONV["CONVECTION SECTION FINNED TUBES"]
        RAD["RADIANT SECTION DIRECT FLAME 70 PERCENT OF DUTY"]
        STACK["STACK FLUE GAS 180 TO 220 C EXIT"]
    end

    subgraph S4["ATMOSPHERIC DISTILLATION COLUMN"]
        FLASH["FLASH ZONE TARGET 370 C PLUS OR MINUS 10"]
        OVHD["OVERHEAD NAPHTHA VAPOR"]
        KEROSIDE["KEROSINE SIDE DRAW"]
        DIESIDE["DIESLE SIDE DRAW"]
        AGOSIDE["AGO SIDE DRAW"]
        RESIDUE["REDUCED CRUDE BOTTOM TO VDU"]
    end

    %% Crude flow path
    CRUDEIN(["CRUDE INLET 30 C"]) --> DESALTER
    DESALTER --> EX1
    EX1 --> EX2
    EX2 --> EX3
    EX3 --> EX4
    EX4 --> EX5
    EX5 --> CONV
    CONV --> RAD
    RAD --> FLASH
    FLASH --> RESIDUE

    %% Hot-stream counter-flow returns
    OVHD -.-> EX1
    KEROSIDE -.-> EX2
    DIESIDE -.-> EX3
    AGOSIDE -.-> EX4

    %% Flue gas path
    RAD --> STACK

    %% Fuel supply to furnace
    FUEL(["REFINERY FUEL GAS OR FUEL OIL"]) --> RAD

    %% Combustion air supply
    AIR(["AMBIENT AIR PREHEATED IN CONVECTION BANK"]) --> RAD
```

### Diagram Reading Guide for the KTU Examiner

- **Solid arrows** represent the **crude (cold) path** moving from ambient through the preheat train to the flash zone.
- **Dashed arrows** represent the **hot product streams** returning to heat the cold crude.
- The **furnace** is the only energy-input node. All other exchangers are *heat-recovery* units.
- The desalter is electrically heated, not thermally. Its inclusion sets the boundary of the heating network.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this diagram matters:** KTU 2024 ESE (End-Semester Examination) problems frequently ask students to *"sketch the crude preheat train and identify the hot and cold streams."* Memorize the structure of the four primary side-draws and the counter-current flow direction.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

The following question bank is constructed to mirror the exact weightage, sub-part division, and cognitive level (Revised Bloom's Taxonomy) of the KTU University End-Semester Examination. Each question is tagged with a synthetic KTU past-year stamp for realism.

---

## PART A — Short-Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Define crude preheat train. List the major hot streams used for crude preheating in a typical atmospheric distillation unit.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **crude preheat train** is a network of shell-and-tube heat exchangers that recovers sensible heat from the hot product streams of the atmospheric distillation column to raise the temperature of the incoming crude oil before it enters the fired furnace. (1.5 marks)

The major hot streams tapped from the ADU and routed through the preheat train are:

1. **Naphtha vapor** from the overhead receiver (145 °C → 45 °C) (0.5 mark)
2. **Kerosene side draw** (245 °C → 60 °C) (0.5 mark)
3. **Diesel side draw** (320 °C → 70 °C)
4. **Atmospheric gas oil (AGO) side draw or pump-around** (340 °C → 95 °C)

These streams collectively raise the crude temperature from ~30 °C to ~270 °C. (0.5 mark)

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Differentiate between the radiant section and the convection section of a pipe-still furnace.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Radiant Section | Convection Section |
|---|---|---|
| **Heat transfer mode** | Direct flame radiation (≈ 70 % of duty) | Convective heat transfer from hot flue gas (≈ 30 %) |
| **Tube surface** | Bare tubes, plain | External finned tubes (extended surface) |
| **Location** | Lower, near burner | Above the radiant section, before the stack |
| **Tube temperature** | Tube skin may exceed 600 °C | Tube skin typically 200–400 °C |
| **Function** | Final heating to flash-zone T | Preheating combustion air, BFW, or trim heating of crude |

(3 marks awarded for any three distinguishing features with one-mark emphasis on radiation vs convection.)

---

## PART B — Long-Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice) (1 × 14 = 14 Marks)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Convention:** Each 14-mark question carries exactly two sub-parts: **Part (a) for 7 marks** and **Part (b) for 7 marks**. Students answer *either* Question A *or* Question B in full.

---

### ❑ Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**
A shell-and-tube heat exchanger in a crude preheat train receives crude oil at **30 °C** and heats it to **150 °C** using a hot kerosene stream that cools from **245 °C** to **160 °C**. The overall heat transfer coefficient is **U = 450 W/m²·K** and the heat duty is **Q = 4.5 MW**. Calculate:
(i) the Log Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD), and
(ii) the required heat-transfer area.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Identify temperature endpoints for counter-current flow.**

For a counter-flow arrangement, the temperature differences at the two ends are:

$$
\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out} = 245 - 150 = 95 \text{ °C}
$$

$$
\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in} = 160 - 30 = 130 \text{ °C}
$$

(1 mark)

**Step 2 — Apply the LMTD formula.**

$$
\Delta T_{lmtd} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)} = \dfrac{95 - 130}{\ln(95 / 130)} = \dfrac{-35}{\ln(0.7308)}
$$

$$
= \dfrac{-35}{-0.3137} = 111.6 \text{ °C}
$$

(2 marks)

**Step 3 — Compute heat-transfer area using Q = U·A·LMTD.**

$$
A = \dfrac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T_{lmtd}} = \dfrac{4.5 \times 10^6}{450 \times 111.6} = \dfrac{4.5 \times 10^6}{50{,}220}
$$

$$
A = 89.6 \text{ m}^2
$$

(2 marks)

**Step 4 — Add a 10 % design margin (KTU best practice).**

$$
A_{design} = 1.10 \times 89.6 = 98.6 \text{ m}^2 \approx 100 \text{ m}^2
$$

(2 marks)

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**
Explain the concept of **Pinch Analysis** as applied to crude preheat train design. What is meant by the term **ΔT_min** and how does it influence the network's heat-recovery target?

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Pinch Analysis** is a systematic thermodynamic methodology developed by Linnhoff (1978) for designing heat-exchanger networks that maximize heat recovery at the minimum total annualized cost. (1 mark)

The methodology proceeds as follows:

1. **Stream data extraction:** All hot and cold process streams are tabulated with their supply/target temperatures, mass flow rates, and specific heats. (1 mark)
2. **Construction of composite curves:** Hot streams are combined into a single "hot composite curve" and cold streams into a "cold composite curve," plotting enthalpy (kW) versus temperature (°C). (1 mark)
3. **Identification of the pinch point:** The two composite curves are brought together horizontally until they touch at exactly one point. This is the **pinch**. (1 mark)
4. **ΔT_min interpretation:** The vertical distance between the two curves at the pinch is the **ΔT_min**. Choosing a small ΔT_min (e.g., 10 °C) increases the heat-recovery target but requires very large heat-exchanger areas, increasing capital cost. A larger ΔT_min (e.g., 40 °C) lowers the capital cost but forces more heat to be supplied by the furnace, increasing operating cost. (2 marks)
5. **Pinch design rules:** No heat should be transferred across the pinch. The minimum hot utility (= furnace duty) equals the enthalpy deficit of the hot composite above the pinch. The minimum cold utility equals the enthalpy excess of the cold composite below the pinch. (1 mark)

For a typical refinery crude preheat train, the economic optimum lies at **ΔT_min ≈ 20–30 °C**, balancing the capital cost of additional exchangers against the fuel savings in the fired heater. (1 mark)

---

### ❑ Question B (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**
A pipe-still furnace in a refinery ADU heats **80,000 kg/h** of desalted crude from **270 °C** to **365 °C**. The crude has an average specific heat of **2.6 kJ/kg·K**. The furnace is fired on refinery fuel gas (assume pure methane, LHV = 50,016 kJ/kg) with a thermal efficiency of **88 %**. Calculate:
(i) the heat duty absorbed by the crude,
(ii) the fuel consumption rate, and
(iii) the volumetric flow rate of air required for stoichiometric combustion at 30 % excess air, given that air has a density of 1.177 kg/m³ at NTP.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Heat duty absorbed by crude.**

$$
Q_{abs} = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T = \left(\dfrac{80{,}000}{3600}\right) \cdot 2.6 \cdot (365 - 270)
$$

$$
= 22.222 \cdot 2.6 \cdot 95 = 5488.9 \text{ kW} \quad (\approx 5.49 \text{ MW})
$$

(2 marks)

**Step 2 — Fuel consumption from furnace efficiency.**

$$
Q_{fuel,input} = \dfrac{Q_{abs}}{\eta_{furnace}} = \dfrac{5488.9}{0.88} = 6237.4 \text{ kW}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{fuel} = \dfrac{6237.4}{50{,}016} \cdot 3600 = 449.0 \text{ kg/h}
$$

(2 marks)

**Step 3 — Stoichiometric oxygen requirement for CH₄ combustion.**

$$
\text{CH}_4 + 2\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

Molar mass of CH₄ = 16 kg/kmol. Stoichiometric O₂ = 2 kmol O₂ per kmol CH₄ = 64 kg O₂ per 16 kg CH₄ = **4 kg O₂ per kg CH₄.** (1 mark)

**Step 4 — Air requirement with 30 % excess.**

Mass of O₂ actually supplied = $4 \times 1.30 = 5.2$ kg O₂ per kg fuel.

Oxygen is 23.2 % by mass in air, so:

$$
\dot{m}_{air} = \dot{m}_{fuel} \times \dfrac{5.2}{0.232} = 449.0 \times 22.41 = 10{,}062 \text{ kg/h}
$$

(1 mark)

**Step 5 — Volumetric flow rate of air.**

$$
\dot{V}_{air} = \dfrac{10{,}062}{1.177} = 8549 \text{ m}^3/\text{h} = 142.5 \text{ m}^3/\text{min}
$$

(1 mark)

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**
Describe the **heat transfer mechanisms** occurring in the radiant and convection sections of a pipe-still furnace. How do these mechanisms affect the selection of tube material and tube-skin temperature limits?

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Radiant Section (5–7 marks if expanded):**
The radiant section receives heat from the **luminous flame** of the burner. The dominant mode of heat transfer is **thermal radiation** governed by the Stefan–Boltzmann law:

$$
q = \sigma \cdot \varepsilon \cdot (T_{flame}^4 - T_{tube}^4)
$$

where $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \text{ W/m}^2\text{·K}^4$ is the Stefan–Boltzmann constant and $\varepsilon$ is the effective emissivity of the flame-tube system. (1.5 marks)

Because $T_{flame}$ is in the range 1500–1800 °C, even with a low emissivity of 0.3, the radiant heat flux on the tube skin can reach **30–45 kW/m²**. This extreme flux dictates the choice of **9 % Cr / 1 % Mo alloy steel tubes** (e.g., ASTM A335 P9) that can withstand continuous tube-skin temperatures up to **620 °C** without creep rupture. (1.5 marks)

**Convection Section (2–3 marks):**
Above the radiant section, the partially cooled flue gas (≈ 700–900 °C) flows over the **finned tubes** of the convection bank. Heat is transferred by **forced convection** following:

$$
Nu = \dfrac{h \cdot D}{k} = C \cdot Re^m \cdot Pr^n
$$

(1.5 marks)

Finned tubes (typically 2–4 fins per cm, height 12–16 mm) extend the external surface area by 5–9×, allowing economical heat recovery at much lower temperature driving forces. Tube material can be **carbon steel** (ASTM A106) because tube-skin temperatures rarely exceed **400 °C**. (1 mark)

**Selection Implication (1 mark):**
The combined effect of the two mechanisms forces a graded material selection — high-grade alloy in the radiant zone, carbon steel in the convection zone — and a controlled firing rate to keep the tube-skin temperature below the metallurgical creep limit, ensuring a furnace run length of 4–6 years between scheduled decoking operations.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign convention in LMTD:** Students often forget to take the **absolute value** of $\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2$. Always verify that the numerator and denominator carry the same sign; otherwise your LMTD will be negative, which is physically meaningless.
> 2. **Counter-flow vs co-flow swap:** If the assignment specifies counter-current, the endpoints are $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out}$ and $\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in}$. Many students invert these and report a wrong LMTD.
> 3. **Units consistency:** LMTD is in **°C or K** (the difference is numerically the same, but write the unit!). Heat duty must be in **watts (W)** when $U$ is in $\text{W/m}^2\text{·K}$.
> 4. **Specific heat assumption:** A constant $c_p$ is a *KTU-simplified* assumption. In a viva or 14-mark derivational problem, expect a follow-up: *"What would change if c_p = a + bT?"* — answer with the integrated form.
> 5. **ΔT_min vs ΔT_min,approach:** Many textbooks mix these up. **ΔT_min** is the *vertical* gap on the composite-curve diagram; **ΔT_min,approach** is the *horizontal* gap at any single exchanger. The two are related but **not equal**.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Crude Heating = Preheat Train + Fired Furnace.** The preheat train recovers waste heat from product streams; the furnace supplies the *make-up* heat to reach the flash zone.
- **Flash-zone temperature** is **343–399 °C** for the atmospheric column. Exceeding **400 °C** initiates **thermal cracking** in the furnace tubes, reducing product yield and damaging tube metal.
- **LMTD formula:** $\Delta T_{lmtd} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$. Indeterminate form when $\Delta T_1 = \Delta T_2$; use L'Hôpital's rule.
- **Overall heat transfer equation:** $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{lmtd}$. Include the **fouling factor** $R_{foul}$ in real designs: $1/U_{dirty} = 1/U_{clean} + R_{foul}$.
- **Furnace efficiency** typically lies between **85 % and 92 %** in modern refineries; **stack temperature** is the dominant loss.
- **Tube metallurgy:** 9 % Cr / 1 % Mo alloy steel for the radiant section (high temperature, high stress); carbon steel (ASTM A106) for the convection section.
- **Combustion stoichiometry for CH₄:** 1 kmol CH₄ requires 2 kmol O₂ (or 9.52 kmol air). With 30 % excess air, the air-fuel ratio is ≈ 12.4 (mass basis) for methane.
- **Pinch Analysis** targets a **ΔT_min ≈ 20–30 °C** for the preheat train. The pinch separates the network into two thermodynamic regions: above the pinch (no cooling utility allowed) and below the pinch (no heating utility allowed).
- **Heat recovery percentage** in a state-of-the-art refinery: **65–75 %** of the total fired heat is recovered by the preheat train.
- **Differentiate furnace types:** Cylindrical (high radiant flux), Cabin (multi-pass, large duty), Box (uniform heating, revamps).
- **Energy benchmark:** Every **1 °C** increase in the furnace-inlet crude temperature saves approximately **0.5–0.8 kg of fuel-oil per ton of crude**.
- **Mandatory assumptions in KTU 14-mark numericals:** (i) Constant $c_p$ for crude, (ii) Negligible heat loss to surroundings, (iii) Steady-state operation, (iv) 1-D heat transfer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Distillation Principles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT638 - PETROLEUM REFINERY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Distillation Principles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

**Distillation** is a *unit operation* used universally in the petroleum refining industry to separate liquid mixtures (primarily hydrocarbons) into fractions of distinct boiling ranges. It exploits the fundamental thermodynamic principle that components in a liquid mixture exert **different partial vapor pressures** based on their inherent volatility when subjected to heat.

In the context of Petroleum Refinery Engineering (Course Code: PEPOT638), distillation is defined as:

> The physical separation process in which a liquid mixture of hydrocarbons is partially vaporized, and the resultant vapor phase is subsequently condensed to yield products of differing composition, exploiting the differences in **volatility** (or equivalently, differences in boiling points) of the constituent fractions.

**KTU Board Definition (Standardized):**
*Distillation is the separation of a liquid mixture into fractions of different boiling points by vaporization and subsequent condensation of the vapor.*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 1 mandates that students must comprehend the principles governing **Crude Distillation**, **Atmospheric Distillation Unit (ADU)**, and **Vacuum Distillation Unit (VDU)**. The underlying thermodynamic engine for all three is the **Vapor–Liquid Equilibrium (VLE)** behavior of multicomponent hydrocarbon systems.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine a kettle of seawater mixed with alcohol. When you gently heat it, you smell alcohol vapor long before the water starts boiling vigorously. Why? Because alcohol molecules are "lighter" in the sense of *escaping* the liquid surface more easily — they have higher **volatility**.

If you capture that escaping alcohol vapor with a cold lid (a condenser), the droplets that form are richer in alcohol than the original mixture. You have just performed a **single-stage flash distillation**.

Now imagine a tall vertical column with many "mini-distillations" stacked one above the other. Each level purifies the vapor a bit more as it rises and the liquid a bit more as it descends. The vapor becomes richer in light (low-boiling) components and the liquid richer in heavy (high-boiling) components. This is the heart of every refinery's **fractionating tower**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of distillation like sorting pebbles and sand by shaking them up in a tube of water. The lighter sand rises with the upward water current (vapor), while the heavier pebbles settle downward (liquid reflux). In a refinery column, the "water current" is the rising vapor and the "settling pebbles" are the descending liquid reflux.

---

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following physical constants and standard metrics are **mandatory** for KTU numerical problems:

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Range (Crude Fractions) | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Standard Atmospheric Pressure | $P$ | **101.325 kPa** (1 atm) | kPa, atm |
| Boiling Point Range (Naphtha) | $T_b$ | **30 – 200 °C** | °C, K |
| Boiling Point Range (Diesel/ATF) | $T_b$ | **200 – 360 °C** | °C, K |
| Boiling Point Range (Residue) | $T_b$ | **> 360 °C** | °C, K |
| Relative Volatility (α) | $\alpha$ | **1.2 – 5.0** for easy separations | dimensionless |
| Reflux Ratio | $R$ | **0.5 – 5.0** | dimensionless |
| Vacuum Pressure (VDU) | $P$ | **1 – 10 kPa** | kPa, mmHg |
| Heat of Vaporization (typical crude) | $\Delta H_{vap}$ | **~ 230 – 350 kJ/kg** | kJ/kg |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boiling Point vs. Vapor Pressure:** Two physically equivalent ways to think about volatility. *Lower boiling point = Higher vapor pressure at a given temperature = Higher volatility.*

---

### 1.4 Fundamental Vocabulary Required for Module 1

The following terms **must** be mastered verbatim for KTU ESE and internal assessments:

- **Feed (F):** The crude oil or hydrocarbon mixture entering the distillation column.
- **Overhead / Distillate (D):** The vapor leaving the top of the column, condensed to liquid product.
- **Bottoms / Residue (B or W):** The unvaporized liquid leaving the bottom of the column.
- **Reflux (L):** A portion of condensed overhead liquid returned to the column to enhance separation.
- **Reflux Ratio (R = L/D):** A critical design and operating parameter.
- **Plate / Stage (N):** A theoretical contact point where vapor–liquid mass transfer occurs.
- **Reboiler:** Heat exchanger at the column bottom that vaporizes part of the bottoms.
- **Condenser:** Heat exchanger at the column top that condenses the overhead vapor.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Thermodynamic Foundation — The "Why" Behind Distillation

Distillation is mathematically grounded in **Vapor–Liquid Equilibrium (VLE)**. Two cornerstone physical laws govern this:

#### A. Dalton's Law of Partial Pressures
The total pressure exerted by an ideal gas/vapor mixture is the sum of the partial pressures of each component.

$$P_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i$$

where $P_i$ is the partial pressure of component $i$ in the vapor phase.

#### B. Raoult's Law
For an ideal solution, the partial pressure of component $i$ in the vapor phase equals its mole fraction in the liquid multiplied by its pure-component vapor pressure.

$$P_i = x_i \cdot P_i^{sat}$$

where:
- $x_i$ = mole fraction of component $i$ in the liquid phase
- $P_i^{sat}$ = saturation (pure-component) vapor pressure of component $i$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hydrocarbon systems closely obey Raoult's Law**, which is why this law is the workhorse of all refinery distillation calculations (Crude, Naphtha, Kerosene, Diesel, Lube oil).

---

### 2.2 Relative Volatility (α) — The Separation "Engine"

Relative volatility quantifies the *ease* of separation between two components. It is the **single most important KTU numerical parameter**.

For a binary pair $(i, j)$:

$$\alpha_{ij} = \frac{K_i}{K_j} = \frac{y_i / x_i}{y_j / x_j} = \frac{P_i^{sat}}{P_j^{sat}}$$

**Interpretation:**
- $\alpha > 1$ → Separation is feasible (component $i$ more volatile).
- $\alpha = 1$ → Separation by simple distillation is **impossible** (azeotrope formation).
- $\alpha \gg 1$ → Easy, economical separation.
- $\alpha < 1$ → Swap indices; component $j$ is the lighter key.

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of Thumb (KTU Examiner's favorite):** For "easy" separations, $\alpha \geq 1.5$. For "difficult" separations, $1.1 \leq \alpha < 1.5$. Below 1.1, extractive or azeotropic distillation must be considered.

---

### 2.3 Material and Energy Balances on a Distillation Column

#### Overall Material Balance
$$F = D + B$$

#### Component Material Balance (for any component $i$)
$$F \cdot z_i = D \cdot y_{i,D} + B \cdot x_{i,B}$$

where:
- $F, D, B$ = molar flow rates of feed, distillate, bottoms
- $z_i, y_{i,D}, x_{i,B}$ = mole fractions of component $i$ in feed, distillate, bottoms

#### Reflux Ratio Definition
$$R = \frac{L}{D}$$

where $L$ is the molar flow rate of reflux returned to the column.

---

### 2.4 Types of Distillation (Refinery-Specific)

| Type | Pressure Range | Feedstock | Key Product | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Atmospheric Distillation** | 1 atm | Desalted crude | Naphtha, Kerosene, Diesel | Primary refinery step |
| **Vacuum Distillation** | 1 – 10 kPa | Atmospheric residue | Lubo, VGO, Bitumen | Avoids cracking > 360 °C |
| **Steam Distillation** | Sub-atmospheric | Heavy crude | Heavy naphtha | Reduces partial pressure |
| **Azeotropic Distillation** | 1 atm | Close-boiling mixtures | Pure alcohol, BTX | Breaks azeotropes |
| **Extractive Distillation** | 1 atm | Close-boiling mixtures | Pure isomers | Adds solvent to alter $\alpha$ |

---

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet — Master Reference Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this table.** Every KTU problem on Distillation will use at least 3 of these formulas.

| # | Formula | Variables | Conditions / Boundary |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_i = x_i P_i^{sat}$ | Partial pressure | Raoult's Law (ideal solution) |
| 2 | $P_i = y_i P_{total}$ | Partial pressure | Dalton's Law (ideal gas) |
| 3 | $K_i = y_i / x_i = P_i^{sat} / P_{total}$ | K-value | Ideal solution VLE |
| 4 | $\alpha_{ij} = K_i / K_j = P_i^{sat} / P_j^{sat}$ | Relative volatility | Ideal binary |
| 5 | $F = D + B$ | Molar flows | Total mass balance |
| 6 | $F z_i = D y_i + B x_i$ | Mole fractions | Component balance |
| 7 | $R = L / D$ | Reflux flows | Operating parameter |
| 8 | $q = \frac{\text{Heat to vaporize 1 mol feed}}{\Delta H_{vap}}$ | Feed thermal condition | $q=1$: saturated liquid; $q=0$: saturated vapor |
| 9 | $N_{min} = \frac{\log\left[\left(\frac{x_D}{x_B}\right)\left(\frac{1-x_B}{1-x_D}\right)\right]}{\log \alpha_{avg}}$ | Minimum stages | Fenske equation (total reflux) |
| 10 | $R_{min} = \frac{x_D - y_q}{y_q - x_q}$ | Minimum reflux | Underwood (q-line intersection) |
| 11 | $\frac{L}{V} = \frac{R}{R+1}$ | Internal L/V ratio | Rectifying section |
| 12 | $\ln\left(\frac{x_F}{x_W}\right) = \int \frac{dy}{y-x}$ | Rayleigh batch eqn. | Differential/batch distillation |
| 13 | $T_{boil} \downarrow \Rightarrow P^{sat} \uparrow$ | Clausius–Clapeyron | Volatility principle |
| 14 | $\eta = \frac{N_{ideal}}{N_{actual}}$ | Murphree efficiency | $0 < \eta < 1$ |
| 15 | $y_{n+1} = \frac{R}{R+1} x_n + \frac{x_D}{R+1}$ | Operating line | Rectifying section (McCabe–Thiele) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Pipe Table Caveat:** Whenever absolute value bars appear in equations (e.g., $\vert x \vert$), use the LaTeX `\vert` command — never the literal `|` character — to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

---

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Distillation principles are foundational to:
- **Crude Distillation Unit (CDU)** — primary refinery unit producing naphtha, kerosene, diesel.
- **FCC Main Fractionator** — separates cracked products by boiling range.
- **Lubricating Oil Dewaxing** — solvent + vacuum distillation.
- **Cryogenic NGL Recovery** — low-temperature distillation of C1–C4 hydrocarbons.
- **Aromatics Extraction** — separating benzene, toluene, xylene.

> [!NOTE]
> Distillation columns consume **~ 30 – 50%** of the total process energy in a typical refinery. Optimizing reflux ratio and reboiler duty is therefore a *major economic* engineering concern — frequently appearing in KTU problems.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Dalton's + Raoult's Combined VLE Relation

Starting from first principles:

**Step 1:** Write Dalton's Law for component $i$:

$$P_i = y_i \cdot P_{total}$$

**Step 2:** Write Raoult's Law for component $i$:

$$P_i = x_i \cdot P_i^{sat}$$

**Step 3:** Equate the two expressions (they describe the same partial pressure):

$$y_i \cdot P_{total} = x_i \cdot P_i^{sat}$$

**Step 4:** Solve for the vapor-phase mole fraction $y_i$:

$$y_i = \frac{x_i \cdot P_i^{sat}}{P_{total}}$$

**Step 5:** Recognize the right-hand ratio as the **K-value** (equilibrium constant):

$$\boxed{K_i = \frac{y_i}{x_i} = \frac{P_i^{sat}}{P_{total}}}$$

> **Engineering Insight:** This compact equation is the *master key* to every VLE calculation. Given $x_i$ and $P_i^{sat}(T)$, you can directly find $y_i$ at temperature $T$ and total pressure $P_{total}$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Overall and Component Material Balance

Consider a distillation column with feed $F$, distillate $D$, bottoms $B$.

**Step 1: Total Mass Balance**
Total moles entering must equal total moles leaving:

$$F = D + B$$

**Step 2: Component Mass Balance**
For component $i$, the moles entering must equal moles leaving:

$$F \cdot z_i = D \cdot y_{i,D} + B \cdot x_{i,B}$$

**Step 3: Solve for $D$ and $B$**
Substitute $B = F - D$ into the component balance:

$$F z_i = D y_{i,D} + (F - D) x_{i,B}$$

Expand:

$$F z_i = D y_{i,D} + F x_{i,B} - D x_{i,B}$$

Collect the $D$ terms:

$$F z_i - F x_{i,B} = D y_{i,D} - D x_{i,B}$$

Factor:

$$F(z_i - x_{i,B}) = D(y_{i,D} - x_{i,B})$$

Solve for $D$:

$$D = F \cdot \frac{z_i - x_{i,B}}{y_{i,D} - x_{i,B}}$$

Similarly, $B = F - D$.

> **Result Interpretation:** This formula lets you size the distillate and bottoms streams directly from known compositions.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Rayleigh Equation for Differential (Batch) Distillation

In differential distillation, vapor is continuously removed as it forms (no reflux).

**Step 1:** Component balance on the still pot at instant $t$:

$$d(L \cdot x) = -y \cdot dV$$

where:
- $L$ = moles of liquid remaining in pot
- $x$ = mole fraction of light component in liquid
- $V$ = moles of vapor removed (cumulative)
- $y$ = mole fraction in equilibrium vapor

**Step 2:** Total mass balance:

$$dL = -dV \quad \Rightarrow \quad V = L_0 - L$$

**Step 3:** Combine:

$$L \, dx + x \, dL = -y \, dL$$

$$L \, dx = -(y - x) \, dL$$

$$\frac{dL}{L} = \frac{dx}{y - x}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute $y = K x / (1 + (K-1)x)$ for binary VLE:

$$\boxed{\ln\!\left(\frac{L_0}{L}\right) = \int_{x_0}^{x} \frac{dx}{y - x}}$$

This is the **Rayleigh Equation** — used to predict residue composition in batch refinery operations.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Operating Line Equation (Rectifying Section)

**Step 1:** Take a control volume around the top of the column (from plate $n+1$ down to condenser).

**Step 2:** Total molar balance:

$$V_{n+1} = L_n + D$$

**Step 3:** Component molar balance:

$$V_{n+1} y_{n+1} = L_n x_n + D x_D$$

**Step 4:** Use $L = R \cdot D$ and $V = L + D = (R+1)D$:

$$(R+1)D \, y_{n+1} = R D \, x_n + D x_D$$

Cancel $D$:

$$(R+1) y_{n+1} = R x_n + x_D$$

**Step 5:** Solve for $y_{n+1}$:

$$\boxed{y_{n+1} = \frac{R}{R+1} x_n + \frac{x_D}{R+1}}$$

> **Engineering Use:** Plot this straight line on the $y$–$x$ diagram with slope $R/(R+1)$ and $y$-intercept $x_D/(R+1)$. The staircase between this line and the equilibrium curve gives the **number of theoretical stages**.

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A binary hydrocarbon mixture contains 50 mol% n-pentane and 50 mol% n-hexane at 1 atm. The vapor pressures at the operating temperature are $P_{C5}^{sat} = 400$ kPa and $P_{C6}^{sat} = 120$ kPa. Calculate:
(a) The K-values of each component.
(b) The vapor-phase composition in equilibrium.
(c) The relative volatility.

**Solution:**

**(a) K-values** (using $K_i = P_i^{sat}/P_{total}$, with $P_{total} = 101.325$ kPa):

$$K_{C5} = \frac{400}{101.325} = 3.948$$

$$K_{C6} = \frac{120}{101.325} = 1.184$$

**(b) Vapor-phase composition** (using $y_i = K_i x_i$):

$$y_{C5} = 3.948 \times 0.50 = 1.974$$

$$y_{C6} = 1.184 \times 0.50 = 0.592$$

Sum: $1.974 + 0.592 = 2.566$. Normalize:

$$y_{C5} = \frac{1.974}{2.566} = 0.769$$

$$y_{C6} = \frac{0.592}{2.566} = 0.231$$

**(c) Relative volatility:**

$$\alpha_{C5,C6} = \frac{K_{C5}}{K_{C6}} = \frac{3.948}{1.184} = 3.33$$

> **Conclusion:** $\alpha = 3.33 > 1.5$, so this is an *easy* separation. Pentane concentrates preferentially in the vapor phase (76.9 mol%) — consistent with its lower boiling point (36 °C vs. 69 °C for hexane).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Atmospheric Distillation Unit (ADU)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["CRUDE OIL STORAGE TANK"] --> nodeB["DESALTER UNIT"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["PREHEAT TRAIN E-100 SERIES"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["FURNACE F-101"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["ATMOSPHERIC DISTILLATION COLUMN T-101"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["OVERHEAD CONDENSER E-102"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["RECEIVER DRUM V-101"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["NAPHTHA PRODUCT TO STABILIZER"]
    nodeG --> nodeI["REFLEX RETURN TO T-101"]
    nodeE --> nodeJ["SIDE DRAW 1 KEROSENE JP-1"]
    nodeE --> nodeK["SIDE DRAW 2 DIESEL HSD"]
    nodeE --> nodeL["SIDE DRAW 3 ATMOSPHERIC GAS OIL"]
    nodeE --> nodeM["COLUMN BOTTOMS REDUCED CRUDE TO VDU"]
    nodeM --> nodeN["VACUUM DISTILLATION UNIT T-102"]
    nodeN --> nodeO["VACUUM GAS OIL VGO TO FCC"]
    nodeN --> nodeP["VACUUM RESIDUE TO COKER OR ASPHALT"]
```

**Legend / Functional Notes:**
- **T-101** is the heart of the refinery. Internals typically include 30–50 fractionation trays.
- Side draws (J, K, L) are taken from trays where the local temperature matches the desired product cut.
- Bottoms (M) feed the VDU for further separation under reduced pressure.

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Vapor–Liquid Equilibrium Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    feedIn["FEED F"] --> preheat["PREHEATER"]
    preheat --> flashDrum["FLASH DRUM V-100"]
    flashDrum --> vapOut["VAPOR V RISES TO TRAY N+1"]
    flashDrum --> liqOut["LIQUID L DESCENDS TO TRAY N-1"]
    vapOut --> trayMass["MASS TRANSFER ON TRAY"]
    liqOut --> trayMass
    trayMass --> condTop["CONDENSER E-200"]
    condTop --> accu["REFLUX ACCUMULATOR"]
    accu --> refluxRet["REFLUX L RETURNS"]
    accu --> distillate["DISTILLATE D PRODUCT"]
    trayMass --> reboiler["REBOILER E-300 HEAT INPUT"]
    reboiler --> bottomsOut["BOTTOMS B PRODUCT"]
```

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Schematic — McCabe–Thiele Stage-to-Stage Construction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeP1["STEP 1 PLOT EQUILIBRIUM CURVE y VS x"]
    nodeP1 --> nodeP2["STEP 2 PLOT 45 DEGREE DIAGONAL"]
    nodeP2 --> nodeP3["STEP 3 PLOT Q-LINE FROM xF yF"]
    nodeP3 --> nodeP4["STEP 4 PLOT RECTIFYING OPERATING LINE SLOPE R OVER R+1"]
    nodeP4 --> nodeP5["STEP 5 PLOT STRIPPING OPERATING LINE FROM xB"]
    nodeP5 --> nodeP6["STEP 6 START AT xD HORIZONTAL TO EQUILIBRIUM"]
    nodeP6 --> nodeP7["STEP 7 VERTICAL DOWN TO OPERATING LINE"]
    nodeP7 --> nodeP8["STEP 8 REPEAT STAIRCASE TO COUNT STAGES"]
    nodeP8 --> nodeP9["STEP 9 ADJUST REFLUX RATIO FOR OPTIMUM STAGES"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to "explain the McCabe–Thiele method," always reproduce this 9-step staircase construction. Examiners award marks for **methodology**, not just the final stage count.

---

### 4.4 Mermaid Schematic — Reflux Ratio Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    caseA["TOTAL REFLUX R INFINITE"] --> stagesA["MINIMUM STAGES NMIN"]
    caseB["OPTIMUM REFLUX R OPT 1.2 TO 1.5 RMIN"] --> stagesB["BALANCED STAGES AND HEAT DUTY"]
    caseC["MINIMUM REFLUX RMIN"] --> stagesC["INFINITE STAGES"]
    caseA --> compare["COMPARE TRADE OFF CAPITAL VS OPERATING COST"]
    caseB --> compare
    caseC --> compare
```

> **Engineering Trade-off:** Higher $R$ → fewer stages (cheaper column) but higher reboiler duty (more energy cost). Lower $R$ → more stages (taller column) but less energy. The *optimum* $R \approx 1.2 \text{ to } 1.5 \times R_{min}$.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question A1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 | Module 1]**

**Q: Define distillation. State Raoult's Law and explain its significance in petroleum refining.**

**Model Answer:**

*Distillation is a physical separation process that exploits differences in volatility (boiling points) of components in a liquid mixture by partial vaporization followed by condensation of the vapor.*

*Raoult's Law states that the partial pressure of component $i$ in the vapor phase is equal to its mole fraction in the liquid phase multiplied by its pure-component vapor pressure at the same temperature: $P_i = x_i \cdot P_i^{sat}$.*

*Significance in Petroleum Refining:*
- Hydrocarbon mixtures closely obey Raoult's Law (near-ideal behavior).
- Enables prediction of vapor–liquid equilibrium in crude distillation columns.
- Forms the theoretical foundation for calculating K-values, bubble/dew points, and stage requirements.
- Essential for designing ADU/VDU and predicting product cut points.

*(Full marks: 3 — typically 1 mark for definition + 1 mark Raoult's Law + 1 mark significance.)*

---

#### Question A2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 | Module 1]**

**Q: What is relative volatility? How does it influence the design of a distillation column?**

**Model Answer:**

*Relative volatility ($\alpha_{ij}$) is the ratio of the K-values (or vapor pressures) of two components $i$ and $j$ in a mixture. It is defined as:*

$$\alpha_{ij} = \frac{K_i}{K_j} = \frac{P_i^{sat}}{P_j^{sat}}$$

*Influence on Column Design:*
1. **Ease of Separation:** If $\alpha \gg 1$ (e.g., > 1.5), separation is easy and requires fewer stages.
2. **Stage Count (Fenske Equation):** $N_{min} \propto \frac{1}{\log \alpha}$. Larger $\alpha$ → fewer theoretical stages.
3. **Reflux Demand:** Larger $\alpha$ → lower minimum reflux ratio → lower energy costs.
4. **Economics:** High $\alpha$ = short, cheap column; low $\alpha$ ($<1.1$) = economically infeasible simple distillation, requires azeotropic/extractive methods.

*(Full marks: 3.)*

---

### PART B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### ⭐ OPTION A — QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme | Module 1]**

**Q (a)** Explain the principle of differential distillation with a neat sketch. Derive the Rayleigh equation. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

*Principle:* In differential (simple) batch distillation, the liquid mixture is heated in a still pot, and the vapor formed is *continuously* removed and condensed. Since the vapor is removed as soon as it forms, the liquid composition changes with time — becoming progressively richer in the heavier (less volatile) component.

*Sketch (describe verbally):* A closed pot heated from below; vapor leaves through a pipe at the top, passes through a condenser, and is collected in a receiver. No reflux is returned.

*Derivation of Rayleigh Equation:*

**Step 1:** At any instant, let $L$ = moles of liquid in the pot, $x$ = mole fraction of light component in the liquid.

**Step 2:** A small amount of vapor $dV$ is removed with composition $y$ (in equilibrium with $x$).

**Step 3:** Total moles balance on the pot:
$$L + dL = L - dV \quad \Rightarrow \quad dL = -dV$$

**Step 4:** Component balance:
$$L \cdot x = (L + dL)(x + dx) + y \, dV$$

Expanding and ignoring the second-order term $dL \cdot dx$:
$$Lx = Lx + L \, dx + x \, dL + y \, dV$$

$$0 = L \, dx + x \, dL + y \, dL$$

**Step 5:** Substitute $dL = -dV$:
$$0 = L \, dx - x \, dV - y \, dV$$
$$L \, dx = (y - x) dV = -(y - x) dL$$

**Step 6:** Separate variables and integrate:
$$\int_{L_0}^{L} \frac{dL}{L} = \int_{x_0}^{x} \frac{dx}{x - y}$$

$$\boxed{\ln\!\left(\frac{L_0}{L}\right) = \int_{x_0}^{x} \frac{dx}{y - x}}$$

This is the Rayleigh equation, used to predict the final residue composition $x$ starting from initial $L_0$ moles at composition $x_0$. (7 Marks — 3 for principle/sketch + 4 for derivation)

---

**Q (b)** A binary mixture of 60 mol% benzene and 40 mol% toluene is to be distilled. The vapor pressures at the column operating temperature are $P_{benzene}^{sat} = 200$ kPa and $P_{toluene}^{sat} = 80$ kPa. The total pressure is 101.325 kPa. Compute:
(i) The K-values of both components.
(ii) The vapor-phase composition.
(iii) The relative volatility and comment on the ease of separation. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(i) K-values** using $K_i = P_i^{sat}/P$:

$$K_B = \frac{200}{101.325} = 1.974$$

$$K_T = \frac{80}{101.325} = 0.790$$

**(ii) Vapor-phase composition:**

$$y_B = K_B \cdot x_B = 1.974 \times 0.60 = 1.184$$

$$y_T = K_T \cdot x_T = 0.790 \times 0.40 = 0.316$$

Sum $= 1.184 + 0.316 = 1.500$

Normalized:

$$y_B = \frac{1.184}{1.500} = 0.789$$

$$y_T = \frac{0.316}{1.500} = 0.211$$

**(iii) Relative volatility:**

$$\alpha_{B,T} = \frac{K_B}{K_T} = \frac{1.974}{0.790} = 2.498 \approx 2.50$$

**Comment:** Since $\alpha = 2.50 > 1.5$, the separation is **easy and economical**, requiring a moderate number of theoretical stages and standard reflux ratios. (7 Marks — 2 + 3 + 2)

---

#### ⭐ OPTION B — QUESTION B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme | Module 1]**

**Q (a)** Define reflux ratio. Derive the equation of the rectifying section operating line used in the McCabe–Thiele method. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

*Definition:* The **reflux ratio** $R$ is defined as the ratio of the liquid returned to the column (reflux $L$) to the liquid product withdrawn as distillate ($D$):

$$R = \frac{L}{D}$$

It is a key operating parameter that controls both separation sharpness and energy consumption.

*Derivation of Rectifying Operating Line:*

**Step 1:** Draw a control envelope around the top of the column, from an arbitrary tray $n$ in the rectifying section up through the condenser.

**Step 2:** Total molar balance across the envelope:

$$V_{n+1} = L_n + D$$

**Step 3:** Component molar balance:

$$V_{n+1} \, y_{n+1} = L_n \, x_n + D \, x_D$$

**Step 4:** Assume constant molal overflow (CMO), so $V_{n+1} = V$ and $L_n = L$ are constant. Also $L = R D$ and $V = L + D = (R+1) D$.

**Step 5:** Substitute:

$$(R+1) D \, y_{n+1} = R D \, x_n + D \, x_D$$

Cancel $D$:

$$(R+1) y_{n+1} = R x_n + x_D$$

**Step 6:** Solve for $y_{n+1}$:

$$\boxed{y_{n+1} = \frac{R}{R+1} \, x_n + \frac{x_D}{R+1}}$$

This straight line has slope $\frac{R}{R+1}$ and y-intercept $\frac{x_D}{R+1}$. When plotted on the $y$–$x$ diagram, the staircase between this line and the equilibrium curve gives the number of theoretical stages in the rectifying section. (7 Marks — 2 for definition + 5 for derivation)

---

**Q (b)** A refinery's atmospheric distillation column processes 100,000 kg/hr of desalted crude oil. The feed composition is 35 mol% light key (naphtha fraction) and 65 mol% heavy key. The distillate (naphtha) contains 95 mol% light key, and the bottoms (reduced crude) contains 5 mol% light key. Calculate:
(i) The flow rates of distillate and bottoms in kg-mol/hr (assume MW ≈ 120 kg/kmol for all streams).
(ii) The reflux ratio if the reflux flow is 80,000 kg/hr.
(iii) The internal liquid and vapor flow rates in the rectifying section. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

Given:
- $F = 100{,}000$ kg/hr $\div 120$ kg/kmol $= 833.33$ kmol/hr
- $z_F = 0.35$, $x_D = 0.95$, $x_B = 0.05$

**(i) Distillate and Bottoms flow rates:**

Using $D = F \cdot \frac{z_F - x_B}{x_D - x_B}$:

$$D = 833.33 \times \frac{0.35 - 0.05}{0.95 - 0.05} = 833.33 \times \frac{0.30}{0.90} = 277.78 \text{ kmol/hr}$$

Converting to kg/hr: $D = 277.78 \times 120 = 33{,}333$ kg/hr

$$B = F - D = 833.33 - 277.78 = 555.55 \text{ kmol/hr} = 66{,}667 \text{ kg/hr}$$

**(ii) Reflux Ratio:**

Convert reflux: $L = 80{,}000$ kg/hr $\div 120$ kg/kmol $= 666.67$ kmol/hr

$$R = \frac{L}{D} = \frac{666.67}{277.78} = 2.40$$

**(iii) Internal Vapor and Liquid flows:**

$$V = L + D = 666.67 + 277.78 = 944.45 \text{ kmol/hr}$$

So $V = 944.45 \times 120 = 113{,}333$ kg/hr.

**Comment:** $R = 2.4$ is within the typical refinery operating range (1.5 – 4.0), indicating an energy-efficient design.

(7 Marks — 2 + 2 + 3)

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students LOSE marks on Distillation Problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting to normalize** $y_i$ values after computing $y_i = K_i x_i$. Always check that $\sum y_i = 1$. K-fractions will exceed 1 if you skip this.
> 2. **Mixing up relative volatility** direction. $\alpha_{ij} = K_i/K_j$ requires the **lighter (more volatile) component to be the numerator**. Reversing gives $\alpha < 1$, leading to wrong stage counts.
> 3. **Using mass fractions instead of mole fractions** in Raoult's Law. Raoult's Law strictly requires *mole* fractions.
> 4. **Confusing q-line definition.** $q$ represents the *fraction of feed that is liquid*, NOT the heat added. A common error is to set $q=0$ for hot feed (should be $q=1$ for cold liquid feed entering a hot column).
> 5. **Not stating the assumption of constant molal overflow (CMO)** when deriving operating lines. KTU examiners deduct 1 mark for this omission.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these 12 points before entering the KTU exam hall:**

- ☐ **Distillation = Volatility-based separation** via VLE between vapor and liquid phases.
- ☐ **Raoult's Law:** $P_i = x_i P_i^{sat}$ — valid for ideal hydrocarbon mixtures (the refinery norm).
- ☐ **Dalton's Law:** $P_i = y_i P_{total}$ — valid for ideal gas/vapor.
- ☐ **Combined VLE:** $y_i = \dfrac{x_i P_i^{sat}}{P_{total}}$ — the master equation.
- ☐ **K-value:** $K_i = y_i/x_i = P_i^{sat}/P_{total}$.
- ☐ **Relative volatility:** $\alpha_{ij} = K_i/K_j$. Easy separation if $\alpha \geq 1.5$.
- ☐ **Overall mass balance:** $F = D + B$. Component balance: $Fz_i = Dy_i + Bx_i$.
- ☐ **Reflux ratio:** $R = L/D$. Optimum $R \approx 1.2 \text{ to } 1.5 \times R_{min}$.
- ☐ **Operating line (rectifying):** $y_{n+1} = \dfrac{R}{R+1} x_n + \dfrac{x_D}{R+1}$.
- ☐ **Rayleigh equation** for batch: $\ln(L_0/L) = \int_{x_0}^{x} \dfrac{dx}{y-x}$.
- ☐ **Fenske equation:** $N_{min} = \dfrac{\log\left[\left(\frac{x_D}{x_B}\right)\left(\frac{1-x_B}{1-x_D}\right)\right]}{\log \alpha_{avg}}$.
- ☐ **Refinery columns:** ADU (1 atm) for naphtha/diesel; VDU (1–10 kPa) for residue; both rooted in the same VLE principles.

> **Final Word:** Distillation is the *spine* of petroleum refining. Master Module 1's VLE, material balances, and McCabe–Thiele logic — and the rest of the semester becomes a layered extension of these principles.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stripping
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT638 - PETROLEUM REFINERY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Stripping

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stripping** is a mass-transfer unit operation in which one or more volatile components are *removed* from a liquid mixture by intimate contact with a **stripping agent** (typically steam, nitrogen, fuel gas, or reboiled vapors) that is relatively non-volatile and does not dissolve appreciably in the liquid. It is the physical *inverse* of gas absorption.

In petroleum refinery engineering, stripping is performed to:
- Remove light ends (C1–C4) from heavier naphthas, kerosenes, diesel, and atmospheric residue.
- Strip H2S, ammonia, and light HC gases from hydrotreater reactor effluents.
- Strip sour water of H2S and NH3 in a sour water stripper (SWS).
- Strip acid gases from amine/DEA solutions in regeneration columns.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Stripping is grouped alongside *absorption* and *distillation* as a primary equilibrium-stage contact operation. Expect direct questions on the **Kremser equation**, **stripping factor**, and the **operating line** of a stripper.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Open Lid on a Boiling Pot":**
> Imagine a pot of thick dal (lentils) on the stove. When you remove the lid, water vapor (the stripping vapor) escapes upward, carrying away the volatile smell and some dissolved gases, leaving the dal behind. If you blow a fan across the top (forced stripping vapor), the smell disappears even faster. The "fan wind" is the **stripping agent**, the dal is the **heavy liquid**, and the volatile smell is the **solute to be removed**.
>
> The more wind you blow (↑ stripping-gas flow), the more efficiently the smell is stripped. The hotter the dal (↑ temperature), the more smell escapes naturally (↑ vapor pressure of solute). This is exactly how a refinery stripper works — steam rises through a descending liquid, selectively picking up the *light* (volatile) components and leaving the *heavy* oil.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Standard Operating Pressure:** 1 atm (101.325 kPa) for most refinery strippers; some vacuum residue strippers operate at **5–50 kPa (50–375 mmHg)**.
- **Stripping Steam:** Saturated steam at **3–10 barg (≈ 134–184 °C)** is typical.
- **Steam-to-Oil Ratio (refinery rule of thumb):** **2.3 – 4.5 kg steam / m³ oil** for atmospheric side-stream strippers.
- **Stripping Factor (S):** Dimensionless; defined as $S = \dfrac{mV}{L}$, where $m$ is the slope of the equilibrium line, $V$ is the vapor molar flow, and $L$ is the liquid molar flow.
- **Tray Efficiency (Murphree):** Typically **0.6 – 0.8** for refinery stripper trays.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — The Why and the How

A stripper is essentially a **tray or packed column** in which:
1. The **liquid feed** enters at the **top** (counter-current to the vapor in absorption, but in stripping the liquid is the *continuous descending phase* and the stripping vapor rises from the bottom).
2. The **stripping medium** (steam or reboiled vapor) enters at the **bottom** and rises upward, contacting the descending liquid on each tray/packing section.
3. Because the partial pressure of the volatile solute in the rising vapor stream is kept **lower** than its equilibrium vapor pressure above the liquid, the solute continuously transfers from the liquid → vapor phase.
4. The **lean (stripped) oil** exits the bottom, while the **rich (loaded) stripping vapor + stripped gases** exits the top.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Driving Force** for stripping is the **concentration (or partial pressure) gradient** between the bulk liquid and the gas-liquid interface, just as in absorption — but the direction of transfer is reversed in the sense that the *goal* is to deplete the liquid of its volatile content.

## 2.2 The Stripping Operating Line

For a dilute solution where the equilibrium relation is linear ($y = m x$), the **stripping operating line** is:

$$y_{n+1} = \frac{L}{V} x_n - \frac{L}{V} x_{\text{bot}} + y_{\text{bot}}$$

This line passes through the point $\left(x_{\text{bot}}, y_{\text{bot}}\right)$ with a **slope of $L/V$**, which is the *reciprocal* of the absorption operating-line slope $L/V$. In a stripper, $L/V < 1$ typically (because we use a *large* vapor flow to drive stripping).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol Meaning | Typical Units | KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $S = \dfrac{mV}{L}$ | $m$ = equil. slope, $V$ = vapor molar flow, $L$ = liquid molar flow | dimensionless | Stripping factor |
| 2 | $A = \dfrac{L}{mV} = \dfrac{1}{S}$ | Absorption factor | dimensionless | Reciprocal of $S$ |
| 3 | $\dfrac{x_N - x_{\text{bot}}}{x_N - x_{\text{bot}}^*} = \dfrac{A^{N+1}-A}{A^{N+1}-1}$ | Kremser eqn (stripping form) | dimensionless | $N$ stages given $x_{\text{bot}}$ |
| 4 | $\dfrac{y_1 - y_{N+1}}{y_1 - y_{N+1}^*} = \dfrac{S^{N+1}-S}{S^{N+1}-1}$ | Kremser eqn (gas-phase form) | dimensionless | Solving for $y_1$ |
| 5 | $\text{RR} = \dfrac{V_{\text{steam}}}{L_{\text{oil}}}$ | Steam-to-oil ratio | kg/m³ or mol/mol | Refinery design rule |
| 6 | $E_{M} = \dfrac{y_{n}-y_{n+1}}{y_n^* - y_{n+1}}$ | Murphree vapor efficiency | dimensionless | Tray design |
| 7 | $K_i = \dfrac{y_i^*}{x_i}$ | K-value (VLE constant) | — | Used in $m$ for multicomp. |
| 8 | $\text{Reb duty} = V \cdot \Delta H_{\text{vap}}$ | Reboiler duty | kW or MJ/h | Energy balance |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU 2024 EXAM TIP:** You will almost certainly be asked to **derive the Kremser equation for a stripper** OR to **solve a numerical** in which you are given $L$, $V$, $m$, and $N$ and asked to find the exit liquid concentration. Memorize the *form* (stripping, not absorption) and the *limiting cases* (very large $S \Rightarrow x_{\text{bot}} \to 0$; $S = 1 \Rightarrow$ linear).

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Petroleum Refineries

- **Crude Atmospheric Distillation:** The naphtha, kerosene, and diesel side draws each pass through a **side-stream stripper** (3–6 trays) with 2–4 kg steam per m³ oil to control their **flash point** and **initial boiling point (IBP)**.
- **Vacuum Distillation:** Vacuum residue strippers remove entrained light ends before the residue goes to a coker or visbreaker.
- **Fluidized Catalytic Cracking (FCC):** The slurry settler / main fractionator overhead stripper removes catalyst fines and entrained hydrocarbons.
- **Hydrodesulfurization (HDS):** Reactor effluent is stripped of H2S and NH3 in a **reactor-effluent air-cooler (REAC) / hot separator** followed by a **stabilizer/stripper column**.
- **Sour Water Stripping:** H2S and NH3 are removed from refinery wastewater before biotreatment.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Stripping Kremser Equation

The **Kremser equation** gives the relationship between the *number of equilibrium stages* ($N$), the *stripping factor* ($S$), and the *terminal compositions* of the liquid streams in a counter-current stripper.

### 3.1.1 Setup and Component Mass Balance

Consider a stripper with $N$ equilibrium stages, numbered from the top down. Let:
- $L$ = molar flow rate of the liquid (assumed constant, dilute solution).
- $V$ = molar flow rate of the stripping vapor (assumed constant, solute-free inlet).
- $x_n$ = mole fraction of solute in the liquid leaving stage $n$.
- $y_n$ = mole fraction of solute in the vapor leaving stage $n$.
- Top stage = stage 1, bottom stage = stage $N$.
- Liquid feed enters at top: $x_0 = x_{\text{top}}$ (or simply denoted $x_F$ for the feed entering the *top*).
- Stripping vapor enters at bottom: $y_{N+1} = y_{\text{bot}}$ (solute-free steam gives $y_{N+1} = 0$).

A **component (solute) mass balance around stage $n$** gives, for $n = 1, 2, \ldots, N$:

$$L x_{n-1} + V y_{n+1} = L x_n + V y_n$$

Rearranging:

$$L(x_{n-1} - x_n) = V(y_n - y_{n+1})$$

### 3.1.2 Equilibrium Relation (Linear Approximation)

For a dilute system with constant relative volatility, the equilibrium line is:

$$y_n = m x_n$$

This converts the operating-line equation into a *single-variable recurrence* in $x_n$:

$$L(x_{n-1} - x_n) = V(m x_n - y_{n+1})$$

### 3.1.3 Recurrence Relation in $x_n$

Solve for $x_{n-1}$:

$$x_{n-1} = \left(1 + \frac{mV}{L}\right) x_n - \frac{V}{L} y_{n+1}$$

Define the **stripping factor** $S = \dfrac{mV}{L}$. Then:

$$x_{n-1} = (1 + S) x_n - \frac{V}{L} y_{n+1}$$

To make this purely recursive in $x$, substitute the equilibrium relation $y_{n+1} = m x_{n+1}$:

$$x_{n-1} = (1 + S) x_n - S \cdot x_{n+1}$$

This is a **second-order linear difference equation**. It is more convenient to eliminate $x_{n+1}$ in favor of $x_n$ and $x_{n-1}$ — or, equivalently, to use the standard Kremser derivation (see McCabe–Smith–Harriott, *Unit Operations of Chemical Engineering*, Chapter 10).

### 3.1.4 Closed-Form Solution (Standard Kremser Derivation)

A more elegant approach (used in KTU-model answers) is to perform a **component balance over the entire column** plus an **incremental stage balance**.

**Overall column balance** (solute in feed liquid + solute in stripping vapor = solute in bottoms liquid + solute in overhead vapor):

$$L x_F + V y_{N+1} = L x_N + V y_1$$

Define:
- $x_F$ = solute mole fraction in **liquid feed** (entering top).
- $x_N = x_{\text{bot}}$ = solute mole fraction in **bottoms** (leaving bottom — what we want to minimize).
- $y_1$ = solute mole fraction in **overhead vapor** (leaving top).
- $y_{N+1}$ = solute mole fraction in **stripping vapor inlet** (typically $\approx 0$ for steam).

For an **ideal stage**, $y_1 = m x_1$. Substituting in the overall balance:

$$L x_F + V y_{N+1} = L x_N + V m x_1$$

The detailed derivation (combining the stage-by-stage balances) yields the canonical Kremser **stripping** form:

$$\frac{x_N - x_N^*}{x_F - x_N^*} = \frac{1}{S} \cdot \frac{S^{N+1} - 1}{S^{N+1} - S}$$

where $x_N^*$ is the liquid composition that would be in equilibrium with the entering stripping vapor, $x_N^* = y_{N+1}/m$.

The **most-used rearrangement** (with $y_{N+1} = 0 \Rightarrow x_N^* = 0$) is:

$$\boxed{\;\frac{x_N}{x_F} = \frac{S - 1}{S^{N+1} - 1}\;}$$

or, equivalently, when $y_{N+1} = 0$ and we use the absorption factor $A = 1/S$:

$$\boxed{\;\frac{x_N - x_N^*}{x_F - x_N^*} = \frac{A^{N+1} - A}{A^{N+1} - 1}\;}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The two boxed forms are equivalent.** The KTU exam may present the equation in either form. Be fluent in *both* and confirm the sign convention (some textbooks use $\dfrac{x_N - y_{N+1}/m}{x_F - y_{N+1}/m}$).

### 3.1.5 Limiting Cases (Board-Exam Favorites)

**Case 1: Infinite stages ($N \to \infty$).**
As $N \to \infty$, $S^{N+1} \to \infty$ (for $S > 1$). Then:

$$\frac{x_N}{x_F} \to \frac{S}{S} \cdot \frac{1}{S^N} \to 0$$

i.e., the bottoms become **infinitely lean** — physically impossible due to equilibrium, but the limit shows that stripping improves monotonically with $N$.

**Case 2: $S = 1$ (exactly).**
