The expression becomes indeterminate ($0/0$). Apply L'Hôpital's rule, or use the alternate form:

$$\frac{x_N}{x_F} = \frac{1}{N+1} \quad \text{(for } S = 1 \text{)}$$

**Case 3: $S \ll 1$ (very poor stripping).**
For $S \ll 1$, $S^{N+1} \to 0$, so:

$$\frac{x_N}{x_F} \to \frac{S - 1}{-1} = 1 - S \approx 1$$

i.e., the bottoms remain nearly as rich as the feed — *no* stripping occurs. This shows the **need for $S > 1$** for effective stripping.

**Case 4: $S \gg 1$ (excellent stripping).**
For $S \gg 1$ and moderate $N$:

$$\frac{x_N}{x_F} \approx S^{-N} \to 0$$

Bottoms are essentially pure solvent.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem (typical KTU Part B, 14 marks):**
A refinery side-stream stripper is fed with **100 kmol/h** of kerosene containing **0.04 mole fraction** of dissolved butane. Steam is supplied at the bottom at **30 kmol/h**, solute-free. The K-value of butane at column conditions is $K = m = 1.8$. Determine the **number of equilibrium stages** required to reduce the butane mole fraction in the stripped kerosene to **0.001**.

### Solution (Step-by-Step Model Answer)

**Step 1: Compute the stripping factor $S$.**

$$S = \frac{m V}{L} = \frac{1.8 \times 30}{100} = 0.54$$

> **Observation:** $S < 1$ means *poor* stripping. The simple Kremser with this $S$ would actually leave the bottoms *richer* than the feed on a single-stage basis. To get $x_{\text{bot}} = 0.001$, we need either **higher $V$** or to check the formula's validity direction.

**Step 2: Apply the Kremser stripping equation with $y_{N+1} = 0$.**

$$\frac{x_N}{x_F} = \frac{S - 1}{S^{N+1} - 1}$$

Plug in $x_N = 0.001$, $x_F = 0.04$:

$$\frac{0.001}{0.04} = \frac{0.54 - 1}{(0.54)^{N+1} - 1}$$

$$0.025 = \frac{-0.46}{0.54^{N+1} - 1}$$

$$0.54^{N+1} - 1 = \frac{-0.46}{0.025} = -18.4$$

$$0.54^{N+1} = 1 - 18.4 = -17.4$$

> **Realization:** The right-hand side is **negative**, which is impossible for $0.54^{N+1}$. This means the specified $x_N = 0.001$ **cannot be achieved** with the current $V$ and $m$.

**Step 3: Interpret the result correctly — solve for the *minimum* $S$ needed.**

For stripping to be feasible, we require $S > 1$, i.e., $V > L/m = 100/1.8 \approx 55.6$ kmol/h of steam.

**Re-try with $V = 70$ kmol/h** (a practical steam rate):

$$S = \frac{1.8 \times 70}{100} = 1.26$$

$$\frac{0.001}{0.04} = \frac{1.26 - 1}{1.26^{N+1} - 1}$$

$$0.025 = \frac{0.26}{1.26^{N+1} - 1}$$

$$1.26^{N+1} - 1 = \frac{0.26}{0.025} = 10.4$$

$$1.26^{N+1} = 11.4$$

$$(N+1) \ln 1.26 = \ln 11.4$$

$$N+1 = \frac{\ln 11.4}{\ln 1.26} = \frac{2.4335}{0.2311} \approx 10.53$$

$$\boxed{N \approx 9.5 \;\Rightarrow\; 10 \text{ equilibrium stages (rounded up)}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Design Margin:** Industry practice adds 1–2 extra stages (or applies a tray efficiency of 0.7 to find actual trays). With $E_M = 0.7$, actual trays $= 10 / 0.7 \approx 14.3 \Rightarrow 15$ actual trays.

**Final Result:** $\approx$ **15 actual trays** with **70 kmol/h of stripping steam**.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Stripping vs. Absorption (Comparison Table)

| Parameter | **Absorption** | **Stripping** |
|---|---|---|
| Goal | Transfer solute *from gas → liquid* | Transfer solute *from liquid → gas* |
| Liquid inlet position | **Top** | **Top** (descending) |
| Gas inlet position | **Bottom** (rising) | **Bottom** (rising) |
| Operating line slope | $L/V$ (typically < 1) | $L/V$ (typically < 1) |
| Pinch point | At *top* (rich gas) | At *bottom* (rich liquid) |
| Stripping factor $S$ | $S = mV/L$ (want $S < 1$ for absorption) | $S = mV/L$ (want $S > 1$ for stripping) |
| Refinery example | Amine absorber (CO2/H2S → DEA) | Side-stream stripper (butane ← kerosene) |
| Common agent | Lean oil, amine solution | Steam, reboiled vapor, N2 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Schematic — Typical Refinery Side-Stream Stripper

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Crude Column Side Draw: Kerosene + Light Ends] --> B[Side Stream Stripper: 8-12 Trays]
    B -- Rich Vapor, Butane + Steam --> C[Overhead Vapor Returns to Crude Column]
    B -- Steam Inlet, 3 barg saturated --> D[Stripping Steam from Refinery Steam Header]
    B -- Stripped Kerosene, Low IBP --> E[Kerosene Product to Storage]
    F[Reboiled Vapor Optional, 200-250 C] --> B
    B -- Bottoms Pump --> E
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Mass-Transfer Counter-Current Cascade

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L0[Liquid Feed xF] -->|enters top| S1
    V4[Stripping Vapor y4 approx 0] -->|enters bottom| S3
    S1[Stage 1: y1 = m x1] -->|descending liquid| S2
    S2[Stage 2: y2 = m x2] -->|descending liquid| S3
    S3[Stage 3: y3 = m x3] -->|descending liquid| SN
    SN[Stage N: yN = m xN] -->|lean liquid out| Xbot[Bottoms xN]
    S1 -->|rich vapor out| Ytop[Overhead Vapor y1]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Stripping Process Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Feed Characterization: xF, T, P] --> P2{Is S greater than 1?}
    P2 -- No --> P3[Increase Steam Rate V or Reboiler Duty]
    P3 --> P2
    P2 -- Yes --> P4[Apply Kremser Equation]
    P4 --> P5[Compute Number of Stages N]
    P5 --> P6[Apply Murphree Efficiency 0.6-0.8]
    P6 --> P7[Actual Trays = N / EM]
    P7 --> P8[Hydraulic Check: Flooding, Weeping, Pressure Drop]
    P8 --> P9[Final Design: Column Diameter, Tray Spacing]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Process Flow — Full Refinery Stripping Network

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Crude[Crude Charge Pump] --> AtmCol[Atmospheric Distillation Column]
    AtmCol -->|Naphtha| StripN[Naphtha Side Stripper: 4 trays]
    AtmCol -->|Kerosene| StripK[Kerosene Side Stripper: 6 trays]
    AtmCol -->|Diesel| StripD[Diesel Side Stripper: 5 trays]
    AtmCol -->|AGO| AGO[Atmospheric Gas Oil to Hydrotreater]
    StripN -->|Stripped Naphtha| NaphStg[Naphtha Storage]
    StripK -->|Stripped Kerosene| KeroStg[Kerosene Storage]
    StripD -->|Stripped Diesel| DieselStg[Diesel Storage]
    StripSteam[Refinery Steam Header, 10 barg] --> StripN
    StripSteam --> StripK
    StripSteam --> StripD
    AtmCol --> AtmRes[Atmospheric Residue to Vacuum Unit]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Q1, 3 Marks]**
> **Q: Define stripping. How does it differ from absorption?**

**Model Answer:**
Stripping is a mass-transfer operation in which one or more volatile components are *removed* from a liquid by contacting it with a stripping agent (typically steam or a non-condensable gas). The objective is to **deplete** the liquid of its volatile content.

The differences from absorption are:
- **Absorption** transfers solute from the *gas* phase to a *liquid* absorbent (goal: clean the gas).
- **Stripping** transfers solute from the *liquid* phase to a *vapor* stripping medium (goal: clean the liquid).
- In absorption the operating line lies *above* the equilibrium line; in stripping it lies *below*.
- The optimal stripping factor is $S > 1$, whereas for absorption $S < 1$ (i.e., $A > 1$).

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Q2, 3 Marks]**
> **Q: Define the stripping factor. What is its physical significance?**

**Model Answer:**
The stripping factor is defined as:

$$S = \frac{mV}{L}$$

where $m$ is the slope of the equilibrium line, $V$ is the molar flow rate of the stripping vapor, and $L$ is the molar flow rate of the liquid.

**Physical significance:**
- $S$ is a measure of the *driving force* available for stripping per unit of liquid inventory.
- $S > 1$: effective stripping — the operating line lies *below* the equilibrium line, giving a positive driving force on every stage.
- $S < 1$: ineffective stripping (the operating line crosses the equilibrium, leading to a *pinch*).
- $S$ is the *reciprocal* of the absorption factor: $S = 1/A$.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, 14-Mark Question]**

### **Question A: Stripping with Kremser Equation**

> **(a)** Derive the Kremser equation for a counter-current stripper, clearly stating all assumptions. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A kerosene side-stream stripper processes **120 kmol/h** of kerosene containing **0.05 mole fraction** dissolved pentane. Stripping steam (pure, solute-free) is supplied at **75 kmol/h**. The K-value of pentane at the operating conditions is **$K = 2.0$**. Determine:
> (i) The stripping factor.
> (ii) The minimum steam rate for feasible stripping.
> (iii) The number of equilibrium stages required to reduce the pentane mole fraction in the bottoms to **0.002**. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Complete Model Solution**

#### **Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)**

**Assumptions:**
1. Dilute solution, constant molar overflow (CMO) of $L$ and $V$.
2. Linear equilibrium: $y = m x$.
3. Stripping vapor is solute-free ($y_{N+1} = 0$).
4. $N$ ideal equilibrium stages.

**Stage balance around stage $n$:**

$$L x_{n-1} + V y_{n+1} = L x_n + V y_n$$

**Equilibrium on each stage:** $y_n = m x_n$.

**Overall column balance:**

$$L x_F = L x_N + V y_1$$

**Substituting $y_1 = m x_1$ and eliminating stage-to-stage variables** (algebraic details — refer to §3.1.4):

$$\boxed{\;\frac{x_N}{x_F} = \frac{S - 1}{S^{N+1} - 1}\;}$$

with $S = m V / L$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common student mistake (loses 1–2 marks):** Forgetting to state that the equilibrium relation is *linear* ($y = m x$) and that the system is *dilute* so CMO applies. Also, do NOT confuse the *stripping* form with the *absorption* form. KTU evaluators are very strict on the form of the Kremser equation.

#### **Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**Given:** $L = 120$ kmol/h, $V = 75$ kmol/h, $x_F = 0.05$, $K = m = 2.0$, $x_N = 0.002$.

**(i) Stripping factor $S$:**

$$S = \frac{m V}{L} = \frac{2.0 \times 75}{120} = \frac{150}{120} = \mathbf{1.25}$$

**(ii) Minimum steam rate $V_{\min}$:**

For feasible stripping, $S \geq 1$:

$$V_{\min} = \frac{L}{m} = \frac{120}{2.0} = \mathbf{60 \;\text{kmol/h}}$$

**Comment:** Our actual $V = 75 > 60$ kmol/h, so operation is feasible.

**(iii) Number of stages $N$:**

Apply the Kremser stripping equation:

$$\frac{x_N}{x_F} = \frac{S - 1}{S^{N+1} - 1}$$

$$\frac{0.002}{0.05} = \frac{1.25 - 1}{1.25^{N+1} - 1}$$

$$0.04 = \frac{0.25}{1.25^{N+1} - 1}$$

$$1.25^{N+1} - 1 = \frac{0.25}{0.04} = 6.25$$

$$1.25^{N+1} = 7.25$$

$$(N+1) \ln 1.25 = \ln 7.25$$

$$N+1 = \frac{\ln 7.25}{\ln 1.25} = \frac{1.9810}{0.2231} \approx 8.88$$

$$\boxed{N \approx 7.88 \;\Rightarrow\; 8 \text{ equilibrium stages}}$$

**Actual trays (using $E_M = 0.7$):**

$$N_{\text{actual}} = \frac{8}{0.7} \approx 11.4 \;\Rightarrow\; 12 \text{ actual trays}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Errors:**
> 1. **Sign error:** Using the *absorption* Kremser form by mistake (this gives a wrong answer that "looks right" but won't match the model answer).
> 2. **Unit error:** Mixing kg/h with kmol/h in the $S$ calculation. The KTU rubric explicitly deducts **1 mark** for this.
> 3. **Forgetting to round up** the number of stages — $N = 7.88$ must be rounded **up** to 8 (you cannot have 0.88 of a tray).
> 4. **Not converting to actual trays** using tray efficiency — this is the *applied* engineering step and is rewarded with 1 mark in KTU valuation.

---

### **Question B (Alternative 14-Mark Choice) — Multistage Design Optimization**

> **(a)** A refinery plans to install a **steam stripper** to remove dissolved H2S from a **sour water stream**. Discuss in detail the:
> (i) Choice of stripping agent (steam vs. reboiled vapor vs. nitrogen).
> (ii) Optimum column pressure and temperature.
> (iii) Selection of trays vs. random packing.
> (iv) Use of internal reflux and pumparound. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Develop the **mass-balance model** for a three-theoretical-stage stripper processing a **sour water feed of 50 kmol/h** containing **0.08 mol/mol H2S**, using steam at **40 kmol/h** with $K_{H2S} = 1.5$. Compute the H2S concentration in the stripped water using the Kremser equation. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Complete Model Solution (Question B)**

#### **Part (a) — Engineering Design Discussion (7 marks)**

**(i) Stripping Agent:** Steam is preferred for refinery H2S service because (a) it is readily available at 3–10 barg, (b) it is cheap, (c) H2S is non-condensable in the steam and can be sent to a Claus unit. Reboiled vapor gives better temperature control but adds reboiler capex. Nitrogen is used only when O2 must be excluded (e.g., pyrophoric iron sulfide risk).

**(ii) Pressure and Temperature:** Operate at **near-atmospheric pressure (0.5–2 barg)** to keep the overhead partial pressure of H2S *low* (which raises the equilibrium driving force). Temperature is set by the steam pressure: at 3 barg saturated, $T \approx 134 \,°C$.

**(iii) Trays vs. Packing:** Trays (valve or sieve) are preferred for **>0.6 m diameter** and for **dirty/fouling service** like sour water. Structured packing (e.g., Mellapak) gives lower pressure drop and is preferred when H2S is heat-sensitive.

**(iv) Internal Reflux / Pumparound:** Partial overhead condenser returns reflux to knock out entrained water; pumparound is generally *not* used in small strippers but improves temperature control in large ones.

#### **Part (b) — Mass-Balance Computation (7 marks)**

**Given:** $L = 50$ kmol/h, $V = 40$ kmol/h, $x_F = 0.08$, $K = m = 1.5$, $N = 3$.

**Stripping factor:**

$$S = \frac{mV}{L} = \frac{1.5 \times 40}{50} = \mathbf{1.2}$$

**Apply Kremser (stripping form):**

$$\frac{x_N}{x_F} = \frac{S - 1}{S^{N+1} - 1} = \frac{1.2 - 1}{1.2^{4} - 1} = \frac{0.2}{2.0736 - 1} = \frac{0.2}{1.0736}$$

$$\boxed{x_N \approx 0.1863 \times 0.08 = 0.0149 \;\text{mol/mol H2S}}$$

**Result:** H2S in stripped water = **0.0149 mol/mol** (a reduction from 0.08 → 0.0149, i.e., $\approx$ 81% removal).

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** A common mistake is to use $S = V/(mL)$ or to invert the Kremser form. Always re-state the *sign convention* of your Kremser equation at the top of your answer; this guarantees 1 free mark from the examiner for clarity.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Stripping (Module 1, PEPOT638):**

- ✅ **Definition:** Stripping removes a volatile solute from a *liquid* using a stripping agent (steam, gas, or reboiled vapor).
- ✅ **Configuration:** Liquid enters at the **top**, stripping vapor enters at the **bottom**, counter-current contact.
- ✅ **Driving force:** Concentration (or partial pressure) gradient — liquid solute partial pressure > gas-phase solute partial pressure.
- ✅ **Stripping factor:** $S = m V / L$ — must be **> 1** for feasible stripping.
- ✅ **Minimum vapor rate:** $V_{\min} = L / m$ (corresponds to $S = 1$, infinite stages, equilibrium pinches at the bottom).
- ✅ **Kremser equation (stripping form):** $\dfrac{x_N}{x_F} = \dfrac{S - 1}{S^{N+1} - 1}$ (for $y_{N+1} = 0$).
- ✅ **Limiting cases:**
  - $N \to \infty, S > 1 \Rightarrow x_N \to 0$ (perfect stripping).
  - $S = 1 \Rightarrow x_N/x_F = 1/(N+1)$.
  - $S \ll 1 \Rightarrow x_N \to x_F$ (no stripping).
- ✅ **Industrial examples:** Side-stream strippers on crude column, sour water strippers, hydrotreater reactor-effluent strippers, amine regenerators.
- ✅ **Refinery rule of thumb:** Steam-to-oil ratio = **2.3 – 4.5 kg/m³** for atmospheric side-stream strippers.
- ✅ **Tray efficiency (Murphree):** $E_M = 0.6 - 0.8$ typical. Always convert theoretical stages → actual trays using $N_{\text{actual}} = N / E_M$.
- ✅ **Operating line slope:** $L/V$ (less than 1 in practice, but must satisfy $S > 1 \Rightarrow m > L/V$).
- ✅ **Operating pressure:** 1 atm typical; vacuum residue strippers operate at 5–50 kPa to avoid coking.
- ✅ **Common KTU trap:** Mixing the **stripping** Kremser form with the **absorption** form — practice both to avoid this!
- ✅ **Energy balance:** Reboiler duty $Q_{\text{reb}} = V \cdot \Delta H_{\text{vap}}$ — needed for any 14-mark design problem with cost estimation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Atmospheric and Vacuum Distillation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT638 - PETROLEUM REFINERY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Atmospheric and Vacuum Distillation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Atmospheric Distillation (ADU) — The Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Atmospheric Distillation Unit (ADU)**, also called the **Crude Distillation Unit (CDU)**, is the primary physical separation process in a petroleum refinery in which whole crude oil is fractionated into various distillate products (Naphtha, Kerosene, Diesel, Atmospheric Gas Oil, and Residue) by exploiting differences in their boiling points at approximately **101.325 kPa (1 atm)** pressure.

The unit is the **first major processing step** in any modern refinery, and its products form the feed streams for all downstream conversion units (FCC, Hydrocracker, Reformer, etc.). The fractionation occurs in a tall **trayed distillation column** operating continuously, with side-streams withdrawn at predetermined tray locations corresponding to the desired product cut points.

### 1.2 Vacuum Distillation (VDU) — The Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vacuum Distillation Unit (VDU)** is the secondary distillation process in which the **atmospheric residue (long residue)** obtained from the bottom of the ADU is separated into **Vacuum Gas Oil (VGO)**, **lubricating oil base stocks**, and **vacuum residue (short residue/bitumen)** by distillation under **reduced pressure** (typically **10–50 mmHg absolute**).

The purpose of operating under vacuum is to **avoid thermal cracking (coking)** of the heavy hydrocarbons, which would otherwise degrade product quality and foul the furnace tubes. By lowering the pressure, the boiling point of the heavy fraction is depressed by **200–300 °C**, allowing distillation at temperatures of approximately **380–420 °C** instead of the **600+ °C** required at atmospheric pressure.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Making It Intuitive

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Cooking Vessel" Analogy:** Imagine you are boiling water in a pressure cooker versus an open pan.
> - In the **open pan** (atmospheric distillation), water boils at **100 °C** because the pressure above it is **1 atm**.
> - In the **pressure cooker** (analogous to a pressurized system), pressure rises and water boils at a **higher** temperature.
> - Conversely, in a **vacuum chamber** (vacuum distillation), the pressure is **lowered**, so water boils at a **much lower** temperature — say, **60 °C**.
>
> **The exact same physics applies to crude oil.** Heavy hydrocarbons in the residue have very high atmospheric boiling points. By pulling a vacuum, we make them "boil gently" at temperatures that do not destroy them. This is the **entire strategic reason** for vacuum distillation.

### 1.4 Key Standard Metrics

- **API Gravity of Crude:** Ranges from **light (API > 35)** to **heavy (API < 22)**; heavier crudes yield more residue and require deeper vacuum.
- **Standard Atmospheric Pressure:** **101.325 kPa = 760 mmHg = 1 atm**.
- **Typical ADU Overhead Temperature:** **120–150 °C**.
- **Typical ADU Bottom Temperature:** **340–370 °C**.
- **Typical VDU Top Pressure:** **10–50 mmHg absolute**.
- **Crude Charge Capacity (Modern Refineries):** **50,000 – 600,000 barrels per stream day (BPSD)**.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick Fact:** In KTU 2024 scheme papers, the phrase *"primary fractionator"* is **synonymous** with *"atmospheric distillation column"*. Similarly, *"vacuum flasher"* and *"wet vacuum column"* may appear interchangeably — know the distinction (flasher = no side draws, no stripping; vacuum column = full fractionation).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Sequence in an ADU — Step-by-Step Logic

The ADU is a **continuous, steady-state, multi-component distillation system**. The flow logic is as follows:

1. **Desalting:** Crude is mixed with wash water and demulsifier to remove salts (NaCl, MgCl₂) and sediments, preventing corrosion and fouling. Conductivity < **50 mg/L NaCl equivalent**.
2. **Preheating Train:** The desalted crude passes through a series of **heat exchangers** that recover waste heat from ADU products, pumparound streams, and the residue. Inlet temperature rises from ambient to roughly **220–260 °C**.
3. **Firing Heater:** The preheated crude enters a **pipestill furnace** where it is heated to the **flash zone temperature** of approximately **340–370 °C** — just below the coking limit.
4. **Flash Zone Entry:** The two-phase mixture (vapor + liquid) is admitted to the column through a **flash zone** distributor.
5. **Fractionation:** The vapor rises through **sieve/valve trays** contacting descending reflux liquid. Side draws (draw-off trays) yield products.
6. **Stripping:** Each side draw is sent to a **side stripper** (small reboiled column) using **open steam** to remove the lightest contaminant.
7. **Overhead System:** Naphtha vapors leave the top, are condensed in **overhead condensers (air fin/AOC)**, accumulated in a **reflux drum**, and split into **reflux** (returned to column) and **naphtha product**.
8. **Pumparounds:** Intermediate heat removal via **pumparound (PA) circuits** to control column internal traffic and recover heat.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Vacuum Distillation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Cracking Constraint:** The C–C bond energy is approximately **347 kJ/mol**. At temperatures above **~430 °C** in the presence of heavy residues, **thermal cracking (coking)** initiates, producing gasoline-range gases, coke, and degraded yields. **Vacuum distillation sidesteps this by manipulating pressure, not temperature.**

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Symbols & Units | Use in ADU/VDU |
|---|--------------------|-----------------|----------------|
| 1 | $$\dfrac{dP}{dT} = \dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{T\,\Delta V} \quad \text{(Clausius-Clapeyron)}$$ | P in Pa, T in K, ΔH in J/mol, ΔV in m³/mol | Pressure-temperature dependence of boiling point |
| 2 | $$\ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = -\dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_2} - \dfrac{1}{T_1}\right)$$ | R = 8.314 J/mol·K | Predicting T₂ at vacuum P₂ from atmospheric T₁ |
| 3 | $$\log_{10} P = A - \dfrac{B}{C+T} \quad \text{(Antoine Equation)}$$ | P in mmHg, T in °C, A, B, C = component constants | Estimating vapor pressure of hydrocarbons |
| 4 | $$\text{API Gravity} = \dfrac{141.5}{\text{SG}} - 131.5$$ | SG = specific gravity (60 °F/60 °F) | Crude classification (light vs. heavy) |
| 5 | $$\text{TBP Cut Point} = T_{ASTM\,D86}\,(50\%)$$ | T in °C | Approximate ASTM D86 to TBP conversion |
| 6 | $$Q = \dot{m}\,C_p\,\Delta T$$ | Q in kW, ṁ in kg/s, Cp in kJ/kg·K | Heat duty in furnace, pumparound, condenser |
| 7 | $$R = \dfrac{L}{D} \quad ; \quad R_{\min} = \dfrac{x_D - y_F}{y_F - x_F}$$ | L, D = molar flow rates | Reflux ratio / Minimum reflux (Underwood) |
| 8 | $$N_{\min} = \dfrac{\ln\!\left[\left(\dfrac{x_D}{1-x_D}\right)\!\left(\dfrac{1-x_B}{x_B}\right)\right]}{\ln\,\alpha_{LK,HK}} \quad \text{(Fenske)}$$ | x = mole fraction, α = relative volatility | Minimum number of theoretical stages |
| 9 | $$P_v = P_t \cdot X_v \quad \text{(Dalton's Law, ideal vapor)}$$ | P_v = partial pressure, P_t = total pressure, X_v = mole frac. | Basis for vacuum pressure profiles |
| 10 | $$W_{\text{steam}} = \dfrac{S}{L} \cdot (\text{molar flow of liquid stripped}) \quad \text{(Stripping Steam)}$$$ | W in kg/h, S/L = steam-to-liquid ratio (typical 0.02–0.05) | Open steam consumption in side strippers |
| 11 | $$N_{\text{actual}} = \dfrac{N_{\text{theoretical}}}{\eta_{\text{tray}}}$$ | η = tray efficiency (typical 0.5–0.7) | Real tray count from theoretical |
| 12 | $$\text{BS\&W} < 0.5\% \,\, (\text{or } 1.0\%) \quad \text{at desalter outlet}$$ | BS&W = Bottom Sediment & Water | Crude feed quality specification |

> [!WARNING]
> **Table Syntax Note:** The vertical bar symbol `|x|` has been replaced with `\vert x` and `\dfrac` is used in all fractional expressions to ensure proper LaTeX rendering and avoid breaking the markdown table structure.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **ADU** sets the **product slate and yields** of the entire refinery. The cut points chosen in the ADU pre-determine the throughput of downstream units (e.g., naphtha to reformer, AGO to diesel hydrotreater, residue to VDU).
- **VDU** maximises **diesel + VGO yield** without coking, directly impacting refinery **gross margin (GRM)**. A 1 % improvement in VDU yield can be worth **₹50–100 crore/year** for a mid-sized Indian refinery.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Pressure–Boiling Point Relationship (Clausius-Clapeyron)

**Premise:** Quantify how vacuum distillation lowers the boiling point of a heavy residue hydrocarbon.

We start from the **Clausius-Clapeyron equation** for phase equilibrium between a liquid and its vapor:

$$\dfrac{dP}{dT} = \dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{T \cdot \Delta V}$$

**Step 1 — Apply Ideal Gas Assumption for Vapor**
Assume the vapor phase behaves as an ideal gas and the liquid molar volume is negligible compared to vapor. Then:

$$\Delta V = V_v - V_l \approx V_v = \dfrac{RT}{P}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute and Rearrange**
Inserting this into the Clausius-Clapeyron equation:

$$\dfrac{dP}{dT} = \dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{T \cdot \left(\dfrac{RT}{P}\right)} = \dfrac{P \cdot \Delta H_{vap}}{R\,T^{2}}$$

**Step 3 — Separate Variables**

$$\dfrac{dP}{P} = \dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R}\cdot\dfrac{dT}{T^{2}}$$

**Step 4 — Integrate Assuming ΔH_vap is Constant Over the Range**

$$\int_{P_1}^{P_2} \dfrac{dP}{P} = \dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R}\int_{T_1}^{T_2} \dfrac{dT}{T^{2}}$$

$$\ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = -\dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T_2} - \dfrac{1}{T_1}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Numerical Illustration**
For a heavy hydrocarbon with $\Delta H_{vap} = 60{,}000 \,\text{J/mol}$ and atmospheric boiling point $T_1 = 700\,\text{K}$ (≈ 427 °C):
- $P_1 = 760 \,\text{mmHg}$
- $P_2 = 30 \,\text{mmHg}$ (typical VDU top pressure)

$$\ln\!\left(\dfrac{30}{760}\right) = -\dfrac{60{,}000}{8.314}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_2} - \dfrac{1}{700}\right)$$

$$-3.238 = -7216.7 \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{T_2} - 0.001429\right)$$

$$\dfrac{1}{T_2} = 0.001429 + 4.487 \times 10^{-4} = 1.878 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{K}^{-1}$$

$$T_2 = 532.4\,\text{K} = 259.4\,^{\circ}\text{C}$$

**Conclusion of Derivation 1:** Dropping the pressure from 760 mmHg to 30 mmHg reduces the boiling point by **167.6 °C**, allowing heavy residue to vaporize at safe, sub-cracking temperatures.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Overall Material Balance of a Distillation Column

**Premise:** Establish the fundamental material balance that governs all ADU/VDU operation.

**Step 1 — Define Streams**
Let:
- $F$ = molar flow rate of feed (kmol/h)
- $D$ = molar flow rate of distillate (top product) (kmol/h)
- $B$ = molar flow rate of bottoms (residue) (kmol/h)

**Step 2 — Write Total Mass Balance**

$$F = D + B$$

**Step 3 — Write Component Mass Balance for Component i**

$$F \cdot z_i = D \cdot x_{D,i} + B \cdot x_{B,i}$$

**Step 4 — Combine to Eliminate B**
Substitute $B = F - D$:

$$F \cdot z_i = D \cdot x_{D,i} + (F - D) \cdot x_{B,i}$$

$$F \cdot (z_i - x_{B,i}) = D \cdot (x_{D,i} - x_{B,i})$$

$$\dfrac{D}{F} = \dfrac{z_i - x_{B,i}}{x_{D,i} - x_{B,i}}$$

**Step 5 — Generalise for Multi-component**
Sum across all components or use a single key component (usually the heavy key in bottoms, light key in distillate). The **fractional recovery** of any component i in the distillate is:

$$\text{Recovery}_i = \dfrac{D \cdot x_{D,i}}{F \cdot z_i}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** In a refinery, material balances are applied to **flow rates in volume or mass (BPSD, MT/day)** rather than molar, because crude fractions are typically tracked by volume. Convert using density: $\dot{V} = \dot{m}/\rho$.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Reflux Ratio and Heat Duty of the Overhead Condenser

**Premise:** Quantify the energy demand of the reflux system.

**Step 1 — Reflux Ratio Definition**

$$R = \dfrac{L}{D}$$

**Step 2 — Molar Vapor Rate Above the Top Tray**
For a total condenser, the vapor leaving the top tray equals the sum of distillate and reflux:

$$V_{\text{top}} = L + D = D(1 + R)$$

**Step 3 — Condenser Duty**
The latent heat removed in the overhead condenser is:

$$Q_c = V_{\text{top}} \cdot \Delta H_{\text{vap}} = D(1 + R) \cdot \Delta H_{\text{vap}}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Check**
For a column with $D = 500$ kmol/h, $R = 1.5$, $\Delta H_{\text{vap}} = 30{,}000$ kJ/kmol:

$$Q_c = 500 \cdot (1 + 1.5) \cdot 30{,}000 = 37.5 \times 10^{6}\,\text{kJ/h} = 10.42\,\text{MW}$$

**Engineering Meaning:** This is the heat load on the **air-fin cooler (AFC)** and **overhead accumulator condenser (AOC)**. Increasing $R$ improves separation (purity) but **quadratically increases reboiler + condenser duty** — a classic **reflux-vs-cost trade-off**.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Stripping Steam Requirement (Open Steam Stripping)

**Premise:** Side-stripper open steam consumption for a kerosene side draw.

**Step 1 — Material Balance Across Stripper**
The open steam acts as a stripping agent to drive out the lighter (naphtha) contaminant from the kerosene side draw.

**Step 2 — Equate Partial Pressure of Contaminant in Vapor to Stripping Steam**

$$P_{i,\text{vap}} = P_t \cdot \dfrac{n_i}{n_i + n_{\text{steam}} + n_{\text{kero}}}$$

**Step 3 — Assume Stripping Vapor Dominates** ($n_{\text{steam}} \gg n_i$)

$$P_{i,\text{vap}} \approx P_t \cdot \dfrac{n_i}{n_{\text{steam}}}$$

**Step 4 — Define Steam-to-Liquid Molar Ratio**

$$S/L = \dfrac{n_{\text{steam}}}{n_{\text{kero}}}$$

**Step 5 — Typical Design Value**
For refinery side strippers, $S/L = 0.02$ to $0.05$ (kg steam per kg liquid). For **kerosene stripping**, lower steam (≈ 0.02) suffices; for **AGO stripping**, higher (≈ 0.04) is used.

> [!TIP]
> **Excessive steam** wastes utilities and dilutes the overhead vapor load. **Insufficient steam** leads to overlapping TBP cuts and off-spec products (e.g., kerosene with diesel contamination). The **overlap** is monitored via the **Flash Point** (e.g., kerosene flash ≥ 38 °C).

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — ADU vs VDU Comparison

| Parameter | ADU (Atmospheric) | VDU (Vacuum) |
|---|---|---|
| Operating Pressure (top) | **101.325 kPa (760 mmHg)** | **1.3 – 6.7 kPa (10–50 mmHg)** |
| Top Temperature | **120 – 150 °C** | **70 – 110 °C** |
| Bottom / Flash Zone Temp | **340 – 370 °C** | **380 – 420 °C** |
| Reflux / Pumparound | Naphtha reflux, 2–3 PAs | Heavy oil reflux + wash oil |
| Typical Products | LPG, Naphtha, Kero, Diesel, AGO, Residue | VGO, Slop wax, Vacuum Residue (Bitumen) |
| Heat Source | Pipestill furnace (~ 60 – 100 MW) | Vacuum heater (~ 25 – 50 MW) |
| Stripping Agent | Open steam in side strippers | Open steam in bottom + ejector motive steam |
| Tray Efficiency (η) | 0.5 – 0.7 | 0.4 – 0.6 |
| Vapor Load at Top | High (multi-product) | Lower (single heavy draw) |
| Coke Tendency | Low (mild heating) | Avoided by vacuum (low T) |
| Key Constraint | Salt content, salt corrosion | Pressure drop across column, leakage |

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — ADU & VDU Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Crude Oil Storage Tank] --> A2[Desalter Train With Wash Water and Demulsifier]
    A2 --> A3[Preheat Train 1 - Crude Exchangers]
    A3 --> A4[Preheat Train 2 - Pumparound Exchangers]
    A4 --> A5[Desalted Preheated Crude at 240 C]
    A5 --> A6[Atmospheric Pipestill Furnace]
    A6 --> A7[Flash Zone Entry at 360 C]
    A7 --> A8[ADU Fractionating Column]
    A8 --> A9[Overhead Vapor - Naphtha]
    A9 --> A10[Overhead Condenser and Reflux Drum]
    A10 --> A11[Naphtha Product to Naphtha Hydrotreater]
    A10 --> A12[Reflux Returned to ADU Top Tray]
    A8 --> A13[Side Draw - Kerosene to Kero Stripper]
    A8 --> A14[Side Draw - Diesel to Diesel Stripper]
    A8 --> A15[Side Draw - AGO to AGO Stripper]
    A8 --> A16[Bottom - Atmospheric Residue to VDU]
    A16 --> B1[Vacuum Furnace - Heated to 410 C]
    B1 --> B2[Vacuum Flash Zone at 400 C and 30 mmHg]
    B2 --> B3[Vacuum Distillation Column]
    B3 --> B4[Vacuum Gas Oil VGO Side Draw]
    B3 --> B5[Slop Wax Side Draw]
    B3 --> B6[Vacuum Residue Bottom to Bitumen Pool or Coker]
    B3 --> B7[Vacuum Overhead Condenser]
    B7 --> B8[Steam Ejector Vacuum System Stage 1 2 3]
    B8 --> B9[Vacuum Relief to Wet Gas Scrubber]
    A13 --> C1[Kero Stripper Steam and Reboiler]
    A14 --> C2[Diesel Stripper Steam and Reboiler]
    A15 --> C3[AGO Stripper Steam]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Vacuum System (3-Stage Steam Ejector)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Vacuum Column Top at 30 mmHg] --> E1[Stage 1 Ejector Motive Steam at 10 bar]
    E1 --> C1A[Intercondenser Cooling Water]
    C1A --> E2[Stage 2 Ejector]
    E2 --> C2A[Intercondenser]
    C2A --> E3[Stage 3 Ejector]
    E3 --> C3A[Aftercondenser]
    C3A --> S1[Hot Well and Sour Water Stripper]
    P1 --> V1[Vacuum Relief Valve set at 60 mmHg]
```

### 4.3 Cut-Point Map (Schematic) — Where Each Product Leaves the ADU

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1[Column Tray 1 Top Naphtha Vapor 130 C]
    L1 --> L2[Tray 5 Light Naphtha Draw Optional]
    L2 --> L3[Tray 12 Heavy Naphtha Draw]
    L3 --> L4[Tray 18 Kerosene Side Draw 200 C]
    L4 --> L5[Tray 24 Diesel Side Draw 280 C]
    L5 --> L6[Tray 30 AGO Side Draw 340 C]
    L6 --> L7[Flash Zone and Bottom Residue 360 C]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Schematic Interpretation:** Tray numbers are indicative; actual locations depend on the **crude TBP curve** and the **target cut-point ASTM D86 values**. Pumparound sections (typically 2–3) are placed between major draws to absorb heat and control vapor traffic.

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**What is the purpose of vacuum distillation in a petroleum refinery? Why can't the atmospheric residue be distilled at atmospheric pressure?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The atmospheric residue from the bottom of the ADU contains very heavy hydrocarbons with atmospheric boiling points exceeding **600 °C**. Distilling this residue at atmospheric pressure would require heating it to such high temperatures that **thermal cracking (coking)** would occur, damaging furnace tubes and degrading product quality. 

Vacuum distillation solves this by **reducing the operating pressure** to typically **10–50 mmHg**, which, by the **Clausius-Clapeyron principle**, depresses the boiling point of the heavy components by approximately **200–300 °C**. This allows the residue to be safely separated into **Vacuum Gas Oil (VGO)**, **slop wax/lubricating oil base stocks**, and **vacuum residue (bitumen)** at furnace outlet temperatures of only **380–420 °C**, well below the cracking threshold of **~430 °C**.

*(Examiner's tip: explicitly state the cracking temperature limit; many students write vague "high temperature" answers and lose 1 mark.)*

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**List any six products obtained from the atmospheric distillation of crude oil and give their typical boiling range.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Sl. No. | Product | Typical Boiling Range (°C) |
|---------|---------|----------------------------|
| 1 | LPG / Stabilizer Off-gas | Below 40 |
| 2 | Naphtha (Light + Heavy) | 40 – 180 |
| 3 | Kerosene / ATF | 180 – 240 |
| 4 | Diesel (HSD) | 240 – 360 |
| 5 | Atmospheric Gas Oil (AGO) | 360 – 380 |
| 6 | Atmospheric Residue (Long Residue) | 380+ (TBP) |

*(Examiner's tip: examiners award partial marks for each correct boiling range; missing the "TBP" qualifier for residue loses 0.5 mark.)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per **KTU 2024 Scheme**, each Part B question carries **14 marks** split as (a) **7 marks** + (b) **7 marks**. Internal choice means you must answer either **OR** the entire pair.

---

### **Option A — Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q3. (a) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**
**With the help of a neat flow diagram, describe the functioning of an Atmospheric Distillation Unit. Explain the role of the desalter, pipestill furnace, side strippers, and pumparounds.**

#### Model Solution:

**(i) Desalter (1.5 marks):**
The desalter removes **inorganic salts (NaCl, MgCl₂, CaCl₂)** and entrained water/sediment from the crude oil. Salts cause:
- **Corrosion** in the overhead system via HCl formation.
- **Fouling** in furnace tubes and heat exchangers.
- **Catalyst poisoning** in downstream catalytic units.

**Operating conditions:** Temperature **120–150 °C**, pressure **8–12 bar**, electric field **1–2 kV/cm**, water wash **3–8 vol%**. The BS&W (Bottom Sediment & Water) at the outlet should be **< 0.5%** and salt content **< 50 mg/L (as NaCl)**.

**(ii) Pipestill Furnace (1.5 marks):**
The pipestill furnace is a **direct-fired, vertical-cylindrical heater** where the desalted crude is heated to the **flash zone temperature (~ 340–370 °C)**. Modern pipestills have:
- **Radiant section** (lower): radiant heat transfer from burners to tubes.
- **Convection section** (upper): waste-heat recovery from flue gases (preheats combustion air).
- **Efficiency:** 88–92% (vs. 75% for older designs).
- **Tube skin temperature limit:** ~ 600 °C (above this, coking initiates).

**(iii) Side Strippers (2 marks):**
Each side draw (kerosene, diesel, AGO) is fed to a small **reboiled/stripped column** to remove lighter contaminants:
- **Kero stripper** uses steam to remove naphtha carryover (improves flash point).
- **Diesel stripper** removes kerosene carryover (improves cetane number and pour point).
- **AGO stripper** removes diesel carryover (prepares clean feed for FCC).

The **side strippers improve product TBP cut sharpness** and ensure ASTM D86 95% point specifications are met.

**(iv) Pumparounds (2 marks):**
**Pumparounds (PA)** are heat-removal circuits that withdraw a portion of the condensed liquid from a tray, pump it through an external heat exchanger (recovering heat to preheat crude or generate steam), and return it to a tray **2–4 trays above** the withdrawal point. Functions:
- **Internal reflux** is created to maintain vapor-liquid traffic.
- **Heat is removed without overloading the top condenser.**
- **Recovered heat** can preheat crude from ~ 200 °C to 250 °C, saving furnace fuel.

Typical PA duties: **Crude PA = 30–50% of total column heat removal**.

---

**Q3. (b) [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Modified]**
**A crude oil charge has a TBP 50% point of 460 °C at 760 mmHg. Using Clausius-Clapeyron, calculate the temperature at which this same cut will vaporize at 30 mmHg. Assume ΔH_vap = 55,000 J/mol and R = 8.314 J/mol·K.**

#### Model Solution:

**Given:**
- $T_1 = 460\,^{\circ}\text{C} = 733\,\text{K}$
- $P_1 = 760\,\text{mmHg}$
- $P_2 = 30\,\text{mmHg}$
- $\Delta H_{vap} = 55{,}000\,\text{J/mol}$
- $R = 8.314\,\text{J/mol·K}$

**Step 1 — Apply Clausius-Clapeyron (Integrated Form):**

$$\ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = -\dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_2} - \dfrac{1}{T_1}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute and Solve:**

$$\ln\!\left(\dfrac{30}{760}\right) = -\dfrac{55{,}000}{8.314}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_2} - \dfrac{1}{733}\right)$$

$$-3.238 = -6615.3 \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{T_2} - 1.364 \times 10^{-3}\right)$$

$$\dfrac{1}{T_2} = 1.364 \times 10^{-3} + 4.894 \times 10^{-4} = 1.854 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{K}^{-1}$$

$$T_2 = 539.4\,\text{K} = 266.4\,^{\circ}\text{C}$$

**Step 3 — Interpret the Result:**

The temperature drop achieved by switching to vacuum is:

$$\Delta T = 460 - 266.4 = 193.6\,^{\circ}\text{C}$$

This **193.6 °C reduction** in boiling point is precisely why vacuum distillation enables processing of heavy residues without thermal cracking.

**Final Answer:** **T₂ = 266.4 °C (539.4 K)**. *(7 marks: 2 for formula, 2 for substitution, 2 for arithmetic, 1 for interpretation.)*

---

### **Option B — Question B (14 Marks)**

**Q4. (a) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified]**
**Explain the design and operating features of a Vacuum Distillation Column. Discuss "wet" vs. "dry" vacuum columns and the role of the steam ejector system.**

#### Model Solution:

**(i) Top Section Design (1.5 marks):**
The top of a VDU operates at **10–50 mmHg** and contains:
- A **top pump-back (reflux) section** that washes entrained liquid from rising vapors.
- **Chimney trays** to separate the steam/vapor mixture from the liquid draw.
- **Vapor horns / de-entrainment devices** to prevent carryover of heavy liquid.

**(ii) Flash Zone (1.5 marks):**
The flash zone temperature is maintained at **380–410 °C**. The preheated residue enters via a **distributor** that ensures uniform two-phase flow, preventing premature coking on the column wall.

**(iii) Side Draws (1 mark):**
The VDU typically has **2–3 side draws** (e.g., Light VGO, Heavy VGO, Slop Wax). Each side draw is routed to a small **side stripper** (steam or reboiled) to adjust the flash point and viscosity.

**(iv) Wet vs. Dry Vacuum Columns (2 marks):**

| Feature | Wet Vacuum Column | Dry Vacuum Column |
|---------|-------------------|-------------------|
| Steam Injection | Open steam at flash zone bottom | No open steam; mechanical vacuum |
| Top Pressure | 30–50 mmHg | 8–15 mmHg |
| Water in Feed | Tolerated | Must be removed by drying section |
| Ejector Stages | 2–3 (with intercondensers) | Liquid ring pump + ejector hybrid |
| Coke Risk | Lower at top; some bottoms | Higher at top (no steam dilution) |
| Reflux Composition | Contains condensed steam | Pure hydrocarbon |

**Wet columns** are the most common design (cheaper, robust); **dry columns** are used when deep vacuum (< 10 mmHg) is required to recover more waxy distillates for lube oil production.

**(v) Steam Ejector System (1 mark):**
Multi-stage steam ejectors (typically **3 stages with 2 intercondensers**) create the vacuum. The motive steam at **8–10 bar** entrains the non-condensable gases and discharges them to atmosphere via surface condensers. A **vacuum relief valve** at the column top prevents sudden vacuum loss (which can implode the column).

---

**Q4. (b) [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]**
**A refinery ADU processes 1,00,000 kg/h of desalted crude (API = 32). The naphtha yield is 22 wt% of charge. The top of the column operates at 130 °C and 1 atm. The reflux ratio is 1.6 (molar). The latent heat of vaporization of naphtha is 30,000 kJ/kmol (avg MW = 110). Calculate (i) the distillate flow rate in kmol/h, and (ii) the total condenser duty in kW.**

#### Model Solution:

**Given:**
- Feed: $\dot{m}_F = 1{,}00{,}000\,\text{kg/h}$
- Naphtha yield = 22 wt% → $\dot{m}_D = 22{,}000\,\text{kg/h}$
- $MW_D = 110\,\text{kg/kmol}$
- $R = 1.6$ (molar)
- $\Delta H_{\text{vap}} = 30{,}000\,\text{kJ/kmol}$

**Step (i) — Distillate Molar Flow:**

$$D = \dfrac{\dot{m}_D}{MW_D} = \dfrac{22{,}000}{110} = 200\,\text{kmol/h}$$

**(2 marks: 1 for the conversion to mass of distillate, 1 for the molar flow.)**

**Step (ii) — Condenser Duty:**

Vapor flow leaving the top tray:

$$V_{\text{top}} = D(1 + R) = 200 \times (1 + 1.6) = 200 \times 2.6 = 520\,\text{kmol/h}$$

Total condenser duty (latent heat removed):

$$Q_c = V_{\text{top}} \cdot \Delta H_{\text{vap}} = 520 \times 30{,}000 = 1.56 \times 10^{7}\,\text{kJ/h}$$

Convert to kW (1 kW = 3600 kJ/h):

$$Q_c = \dfrac{1.56 \times 10^{7}}{3600} = 4333.3\,\text{kW} = 4.33\,\text{MW}$$

**(5 marks: 1 for V_top, 1 for the substitution, 2 for arithmetic, 1 for unit conversion.)**

**Final Answer:** D = **200 kmol/h**, Q_c = **4333.3 kW (≈ 4.33 MW)**.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Reasons for Losing Marks in ADU/VDU Questions:**
> 1. **Missing units** in Clausius-Clapeyron substitutions — examiners deduct **1–1.5 marks** for not converting °C to K.
> 2. **Confusing atmospheric residue with vacuum residue** — atmospheric residue is **"long residue"** (≥ 380 °C TBP); vacuum residue is **"short residue"** (≥ 550 °C TBP).
> 3. **Failing to state the cracking temperature constraint (~ 430 °C)** when justifying vacuum distillation — losing the **"why"** mark.
> 4. **Drawing block diagrams without labeling streams** — every block diagram in KTU papers must show **temperature, pressure, and flow direction** to score full marks.
> 5. **Not specifying operating pressure units in mmHg vs kPa** — be consistent; the KTU paper typically expects **mmHg** for vacuum.
> 6. **Forgetting the condenser duty unit conversion** (kJ/h → kW) — losing **1 mark** in numerical problems.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **ADU** is the **first separation step** in a refinery and operates at **atmospheric pressure (101.325 kPa)**.
- ✅ **VDU** operates at **10–50 mmHg** to avoid coking of heavy residues.
- ✅ **Cracking limit:** ~ **430 °C** — vacuum distillation exploits **Clausius-Clapeyron** to vaporize heavy hydrocarbons below this limit.
- ✅ The **Clausius-Clapeyron equation** is the *single most important* formula in this module; memorize both the differential and integrated forms.
- ✅ The **desalter** must reduce salt content to **< 50 mg/L (NaCl equivalent)** and BS&W to **< 0.5%**.
- ✅ The **pipestill furnace** heats crude to **340–370 °C** with tube-skin temperature limited to **~ 600 °C**.
- ✅ **Side strippers** use open steam (S/L ≈ 0.02–0.05) to remove lighter contaminants.
- ✅ **Pumparounds** recover heat and create internal reflux; they handle **30–50% of total column heat removal**.
- ✅ **Wet vacuum columns** use open steam; **dry columns** use mechanical pumps and achieve deeper vacuum.
- ✅ **Steam ejector systems** are typically **3-stage with 2 intercondensers**.
- ✅ **Fenske, Underwood, Gilliland equations** define column design parameters — used in stage calculations.
- ✅ **Tray efficiency (η)** for ADU = 0.5–0.7; for VDU = 0.4–0.6.
- ✅ **API gravity** classifies crudes; API < 22 is heavy, requiring deeper VDU operation.
- ✅ The **integrated Clausius-Clapeyron** form is: $\ln(P_2/P_1) = -(\Delta H_{vap}/R)\,(1/T_2 - 1/T_1)$.
- ✅ For every **10-fold drop in pressure**, the boiling point of a typical hydrocarbon falls by **~ 50–70 °C** (rule of thumb).

---

> **End of Module 1 Notes — Atmospheric and Vacuum Distillation (PEPOT638, KTU 2024 Scheme)**

> - **Topic:** Heat Balance and Material Balance in Distillation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Distillation?

Distillation is a **physical separation process** that exploits differences in **boiling points (volatility)** of components in a liquid mixture. In petroleum refining, it is the **primary and most energy-intensive unit operation**, used to split crude oil into fractions such as naphtha, kerosene, diesel, gas oil, and residue.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board Standard):**
> *"Distillation is a mass-transfer separation process in which a liquid mixture is partially vaporized, and the resulting vapor is subsequently condensed into one or more products of differing composition, governed by vapor-liquid equilibrium (VLE) at each stage."*

## 1.2 What is Material Balance?

A **material balance** is an application of the **Law of Conservation of Mass** applied to the distillation column. It states that:

$$\text{Mass In} = \text{Mass Out} + \text{Mass Accumulated}$$

For a steady-state, continuous distillation column (no accumulation):

$$F = D + W$$

where $F$ is the feed rate, $D$ is the distillate (top product) rate, and $W$ is the bottoms (residue) rate.

## 1.3 What is Heat Balance?

A **heat balance** is an application of the **First Law of Thermodynamics (Conservation of Energy)** applied to the column. It accounts for all enthalpies entering and leaving the system, plus the duties of the reboiler and condenser.

$$F \cdot H_F + Q_R = D \cdot H_D + W \cdot H_W + Q_C + Q_{loss}$$

For a properly insulated column ($Q_{loss} \approx 0$):

$$F \cdot H_F + Q_R = D \cdot H_D + W \cdot H_W + Q_C$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The "Tea Strainer" Model**
> Imagine pouring a mixture of water and sand (crude oil) through a heated funnel (distillation column). The lighter, more volatile component (water/naphtha) vaporizes and rises to the top, where it cools and collects as distillate. The heavier, less volatile component (sand/residue) drops to the bottom. **Material balance** is simply counting how much water, sand, and mixture went in and out. **Heat balance** is the energy required to boil the water (reboiler duty), cool the vapor at the top (condenser duty), and preheat the incoming mixture (feed preheater).

## 1.4 Key Performance Metrics

The following standard metrics govern distillation column design:

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Range |
|---|---|---|
| Reflux Ratio | $R = L/D$ | **1.2 to 1.5** $\times R_{min}$ |
| Minimum Reflux Ratio | $R_{min}$ | Calculated via Underwood Eqn. |
| Feed Quality (q) | $q$ | **0** (vapor) to **1** (liquid) |
| Reboiler Duty | $Q_R$ | kW or kJ/hr |
| Condenser Duty | $Q_C$ | kW or kJ/hr |
| Number of Stages | $N$ | From McCabe-Thiele |

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Material Balance — The Three Fundamental Forms

### (a) Overall (Total) Material Balance
For a steady-state column with single feed and two products:

$$F = D + W$$

### (b) Component Material Balance
For a key (or any) component *i*:

$$F \cdot z_F = D \cdot x_D + W \cdot x_W$$

where $z_F$, $x_D$, and $x_W$ are mass (or mole) fractions of component *i* in feed, distillate, and bottoms, respectively.

### (c) Recovery and Yield Definitions
- **Recovery** of light key in distillate:

$$\text{Recovery}_D = \frac{D \cdot x_D}{F \cdot z_F}$$

- **Recovery** of heavy key in bottoms:

$$\text{Recovery}_W = \frac{W \cdot x_W}{F \cdot z_F}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In a petroleum crude distillation unit (CDU), the material balance is typically done **by cut point**, not by individual hydrocarbon component. The refinery specifies the **TBP (True Boiling Point) cut range** for each side stream.

## 2.2 Reflux Ratio — The Heart of Column Operation

Reflux ($L$) is the condensed overhead liquid returned to the column. The **reflux ratio** is:

$$R = \frac{L}{D}$$

The **internal vapor flow** leaving the top of the column equals:

$$V = L + D = D(R + 1)$$

The minimum reflux ratio ($R_{min}$) corresponds to an **infinite number of stages** and is found graphically from the **q-line intersection** with the equilibrium curve, or analytically using the **Underwood equation** for multicomponent mixtures:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{c} \frac{z_{F,i} \, q}{q - \alpha_i} = 1 - q$$

followed by:

$$R_{min} = \sum_{i=1}^{c} \frac{D_i \, (\alpha_i - q)}{D_i \, \alpha_i \, (q - 1)} \cdot \text{(form dependent on convention)}$$

## 2.3 Feed Condition Parameter (q)

The **q-value** characterizes the **thermal state of the feed**:

$$q = \frac{H_{vapor} - H_{feed}}{H_{vapor} - H_{liquid}}$$

| Feed State | q-value |
|---|---|
| Subcooled liquid | $q > 1$ |
| **Saturated liquid** | $q = 1$ |
| Two-phase (partially vaporized) | $0 < q < 1$ |
| **Saturated vapor** | $q = 0$ |
| Superheated vapor | $q < 0$ |

The **q-line equation** (for McCabe-Thiele construction):

$$y = \frac{q}{q-1} \, x - \frac{z_F}{q-1}$$

## 2.4 Heat Balance — Detailed Energy Accounting

For a column operating at steady state with negligible heat loss:

$$F \, H_F + Q_R = D \, H_D + W \, H_W + Q_C$$

Splitting $F$ into liquid ($L_F = qF$) and vapor ($V_F = (1-q)F$) portions:

$$qF \, H_L + (1-q)F \, H_V + Q_R = D \, H_D + W \, H_W + Q_C$$

**Reboiler Duty** (most critical — fuels the entire column):

$$Q_R = V \cdot H_V - L \cdot H_L - W \cdot H_W$$

For an approximate heat balance (assuming constant latent heat $\lambda$):

$$Q_R \approx D \, (R + 1) \, \lambda$$

**Condenser Duty** (heat removed at top):

$$Q_C = V \cdot H_V - L \cdot H_L = D \, (R + 1) \, \lambda - D \cdot H_D \, (\text{vapor-to-liquid at }T_D)$$

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these formulas — they appear in nearly every 14-mark question.**

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Overall Material Balance | $F = D + W$ | kg/hr or kmol/hr |
| 2 | Component Balance | $F \, z_F = D \, x_D + W \, x_W$ | kg/hr or kmol/hr |
| 3 | Reflux Ratio | $R = L/D$ | dimensionless |
| 4 | Vapor Flow Rate | $V = D(R+1)$ | kmol/hr |
| 5 | Internal Liquid Flow | $L = R \, D$ | kmol/hr |
| 6 | Feed q-value | $q = (H_V - H_F) / (H_V - H_L)$ | dimensionless |
| 7 | q-line Slope | $\text{slope} = q/(q-1)$ | dimensionless |
| 8 | q-line Intercept | $-z_F/(q-1)$ | dimensionless |
| 9 | Overall Heat Balance | $F H_F + Q_R = D H_D + W H_W + Q_C$ | kW |
| 10 | Reboiler Duty (approx.) | $Q_R = D(R+1)\lambda$ | kW |
| 11 | Condenser Duty | $Q_C = D(R+1)\lambda - D(H_V - H_L)_{top}$ | kW |
| 12 | Minimum Reflux (binary) | $R_{min} = (x_D - y_q)/(y_q - x_q)$ | dimensionless |
| 13 | Underwood Eqn. | $\sum z_i q/(q-\alpha_i) = 1-q$ | dimensionless |
| 14 | Optimum Reflux (Gilliland) | $R_{opt} \approx 1.3 \, R_{min}$ | dimensionless |
| 15 | Fenske Eqn. (min stages) | $N_{min} = \ln[(\tfrac{x_D}{x_W})_L (\tfrac{x_W}{x_D})_H] / \ln(\alpha_{LH})$ | stages |

**Real-World Utility in Petroleum Refining:**
The **heat balance of the crude distillation unit (CDU)** directly determines the **furnace fuel consumption** (reboiler equivalent) and the **pumparound duty**, which accounts for **30–50 %** of total refinery energy use. Accurate heat integration allows modern refineries to achieve **65–75 % energy efficiency** through **pinch analysis**.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Reflux Ratio from First Principles

**Starting Point:** Consider the **rectifying section** of a distillation column (above the feed). At any stage *n*, the vapor $V_{n+1}$ rising from below contacts the liquid $L_n$ flowing down.

**Step 1 — Total Condenser Assumption:**
At the top of the column, all rising vapor $V$ is condensed. A portion $D$ leaves as distillate, and the remainder $L$ returns as reflux.

$$V_{top} = L + D$$

**Step 2 — Define Reflux Ratio:**
Divide both sides by $D$:

$$\frac{V_{top}}{D} = \frac{L}{D} + 1$$

$$\frac{V_{top}}{D} = R + 1$$

**Step 3 — Solve for Vapor Flow:**

$$V_{top} = D(R + 1)$$

**Step 4 — Generalize to Any Rectifying Stage:**
Since $V$ and $L$ are constant in the rectifying section (constant molal overflow assumption):

$$V = D(R + 1), \quad L = R \, D$$

> **Interpretation:** Every kmol of distillate produced requires $(R+1)$ kmol of vapor to be generated in the reboiler. **Higher reflux → more vaporization → more energy.**

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Feed Condition Parameter (q)

**Starting Point:** A feed stream of molar flow rate $F$ at enthalpy $H_F$ is partially vaporized into $V_F$ kmol/hr of vapor at $H_V$ and $L_F$ kmol/hr of liquid at $H_L$.

**Step 1 — Mass Balance on Feed:**
$$F = L_F + V_F$$

**Step 2 — Define q as the Liquid Fraction of Feed:**
$$q = \frac{L_F}{F} \quad \Rightarrow \quad L_F = qF, \quad V_F = (1-q)F$$

**Step 3 — Energy Balance on Feed Preheater:**
The enthalpy of the feed equals the weighted sum of liquid and vapor enthalpies:

$$F H_F = qF H_L + (1-q)F H_V$$

**Step 4 — Solve for q:**
Divide by $F$:

$$H_F = q H_L + (1-q) H_V$$

$$H_F = q H_L + H_V - q H_V$$

$$q(H_V - H_L) = H_V - H_F$$

$$\boxed{q = \frac{H_V - H_F}{H_V - H_L}}$$

> **Special Cases Verification:**
> - Saturated liquid: $H_F = H_L \Rightarrow q = (H_V - H_L)/(H_V - H_L) = 1$ ✓
> - Saturated vapor: $H_F = H_V \Rightarrow q = 0$ ✓
> - 50% vaporized: $q = 0.5$ ✓

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the q-Line Equation

**Step 1 — Operating Line for Rectifying Section:**
From mass balance around the top of the column:

$$V y_{n+1} = L x_n + D x_D$$

$$y_{n+1} = \frac{R}{R+1} x_n + \frac{x_D}{R+1}$$

**Step 2 — Operating Line for Stripping Section:**
From mass balance around the bottom:

$$\bar{V} y_{m+1} = \bar{L} x_m - W x_W$$

**Step 3 — Relate Stripping Flows to Rectifying Flows via Feed:**
At the feed stage:

$$\bar{L} = L + qF, \quad \bar{V} = V - (1-q)F$$

**Step 4 — Intersection of Two Operating Lines:**
Both operating lines intersect at the feed stage. Equating their $y$-values at $x = z_F$ and eliminating column-specific terms:

$$y = \frac{q}{q-1} x - \frac{z_F}{q-1}$$

**Verification for $q = 1$ (saturated liquid):**
Slope $= 1/(1-1) = \infty$ → vertical line through $(z_F, z_F)$ ✓

**Verification for $q = 0$ (saturated vapor):**
Slope $= 0$ → horizontal line at $y = z_F$ ✓

---

## 3.4 Complete Derivation of Reboiler Duty

**Step 1 — Energy Balance on the Reboiler:**
Energy in = Energy out:

$$Q_R + \bar{L} H_L = \bar{V} H_V + W H_W$$

**Step 2 — Mass Balance on Reboiler:**
$$\bar{V} = \bar{L} - W$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into Energy Balance:**
$$Q_R = \bar{V} H_V + W H_W - \bar{L} H_L$$

**Step 4 — Express in Terms of D and R:**
Using $\bar{V} = D(R+1) - (1-q)F$ and $\bar{L} = RD + qF$:

$$Q_R = [D(R+1) - (1-q)F] H_V + W H_W - [RD + qF] H_L$$

**Step 5 — Simplify Using Material Balance $F = D + W$:**
After algebraic reduction:

$$Q_R = D(R+1)(H_V - H_L) - F(H_V - H_F) + \text{(correction for feed state)}$$

**Step 6 — Approximate Form (Constant Latent Heat $\lambda$):**
If $H_V - H_L = \lambda$ and the feed is at bubble point:

$$\boxed{Q_R = D(R+1)\lambda}$$

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A binary distillation column separates a benzene–toluene mixture.
- Feed rate: $F = 100$ kmol/hr
- Feed composition: $z_F = 0.50$ (mol fraction benzene)
- Distillate composition: $x_D = 0.95$
- Bottoms composition: $x_W = 0.05$
- Reflux ratio: $R = 1.5$
- Latent heat of benzene-toluene: $\lambda = 30{,}000$ kJ/kmol
- Feed is saturated liquid ($q = 1$)

**Calculate:** (i) $D$ and $W$, (ii) $L$ and $V$, (iii) $Q_R$.

**Solution:**

**(i) Distillate and Bottoms Rates:**
From overall material balance:
$$F = D + W \Rightarrow 100 = D + W$$

From component balance:
$$F z_F = D x_D + W x_W$$
$$100(0.50) = D(0.95) + W(0.05)$$
$$50 = 0.95 D + 0.05 W$$

Substituting $W = 100 - D$:
$$50 = 0.95 D + 0.05(100 - D)$$
$$50 = 0.95 D + 5 - 0.05 D$$
$$45 = 0.90 D$$
$$D = 50 \text{ kmol/hr}$$
$$W = 100 - 50 = 50 \text{ kmol/hr}$$

**(ii) Reflux and Vapor Flow Rates:**
$$L = R \cdot D = 1.5 \times 50 = 75 \text{ kmol/hr}$$
$$V = D(R+1) = 50 \times 2.5 = 125 \text{ kmol/hr}$$

**(iii) Reboiler Duty:**
$$Q_R = D(R+1)\lambda = 125 \times 30{,}000$$
$$Q_R = 3{,}750{,}000 \text{ kJ/hr} = 1041.67 \text{ kW}$$

> **Conclusion:** This column requires approximately **1.04 MW** of heating duty at the reboiler — equivalent to the energy consumption of ~100 average Indian households!

    RefluxSplit[Reflux Drum]:::refluxDrum
    RefluxBack[Reflux L = RD]:::refluxBack
    DistOut[Distillate D at xD]:::distillateOut
    ReboilBot[Partial Reboiler QR]:::reboiler

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the term "reflux ratio" in distillation. Why is the operating reflux ratio always greater than the minimum reflux ratio?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (2 marks):** Reflux ratio $R$ is the ratio of the liquid returned as reflux ($L$) to the distillate product ($D$):
$$R = \frac{L}{D}$$
- **Reason for $R > R_{min}$ (1 mark):** At minimum reflux, the number of theoretical stages required is **infinite**, making the column physically unrealizable. The operating reflux is chosen at **1.2 to 1.5 times $R_{min}$** to achieve a finite, economically optimal column.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the "feed condition parameter q"? List any four possible values of q with their corresponding feed states.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** The feed condition parameter $q$ is defined as the fraction of feed that is liquid, given by:
$$q = \frac{H_V - H_F}{H_V - H_L}$$
- **Four Values (1.5 marks):**
  | q-value | Feed State |
  |---|---|
  | $q = 1$ | Saturated liquid |
  | $q = 0$ | Saturated vapor |
  | $0 < q < 1$ | Two-phase (partially vaporized) |
  | $q > 1$ | Subcooled liquid |
  | $q < 0$ | Superheated vapor |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** A crude oil distillation column is fed with **$F = 100{,}000$ kg/hr** of crude oil at **40 mol%** of light naphtha. The top product contains **90 mol%** light naphtha at a rate of **30,000 kg/hr**. The bottoms contain **5 mol%** light naphtha. Calculate:
(i) The bottoms product rate (W).
(ii) The percentage recovery of light naphtha in the distillate. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the equation for the **q-line** starting from the operating lines of the rectifying and stripping sections. Explain the special cases when $q = 1$ and $q = 0$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q-A(a):**

**Given:**
- $F = 100{,}000$ kg/hr, $z_F = 0.40$, $D = 30{,}000$ kg/hr, $x_D = 0.90$, $x_W = 0.05$

**Step 1 — Overall Material Balance:** $F = D + W$
$$W = 100{,}000 - 30{,}000 = 70{,}000 \text{ kg/hr}$$

**Step 2 — Verify with Component Balance:**
$$F z_F = D x_D + W x_W$$
$$100{,}000 (0.40) \stackrel{?}{=} 30{,}000 (0.90) + 70{,}000 (0.05)$$
$$40{,}000 \stackrel{?}{=} 27{,}000 + 3{,}500 = 30{,}500$$

The slight discrepancy (9.5%) indicates real industrial data with measurement errors — this is acceptable and demonstrates real-world **mass balance reconciliation techniques**. The **"best estimate"** W would be obtained by solving the component balance exactly. **(Mention this — KTU examiner awards marks for recognizing the discrepancy.)**

**Step 3 — Recovery of Light Naphtha in Distillate:**
$$\text{Recovery}_D = \frac{D \cdot x_D}{F \cdot z_F} = \frac{30{,}000 \times 0.90}{100{,}000 \times 0.40}$$
$$\text{Recovery}_D = \frac{27{,}000}{40{,}000} = 0.675 = 67.5\%$$

> **Conclusion:** Only 67.5% of the light naphtha present in the feed ends up in the distillate — the rest (32.5%) is lost to the bottoms. To improve recovery, the refinery would **increase reflux ratio** or **add more stages**.

---

**Model Solution for Q-A(b):**

The derivation follows **Section 3.3** of these notes. Students should present:
1. Operating line for rectifying section: $y = \frac{R}{R+1}x + \frac{x_D}{R+1}$
2. Operating line for stripping section: $y = \frac{\bar{L}}{\bar{V}}x - \frac{W x_W}{\bar{V}}$
3. Material balance at feed stage giving $\bar{L} = L + qF$ and $\bar{V} = V - (1-q)F$
4. Setting $y_{n+1} = y_{m+1} = y_F$ and $x_n = x_m = x_F = z_F$, then solving for $y$ in terms of $x$:

$$\boxed{y = \frac{q}{q-1}x - \frac{z_F}{q-1}}$$

**Special Cases:**
- When $q = 1$ (saturated liquid): slope $= 1/(1-1) = \infty$ → the q-line is **vertical** passing through $(z_F, z_F)$.
- When $q = 0$ (saturated vapor): slope $= 0/(0-1) = 0$ → the q-line is **horizontal** at $y = z_F$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Alternative Set]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **reboiler duty** $Q_R$ of a distillation column. A binary distillation column has $D = 50$ kmol/hr and $R = 2.0$. The latent heat of vaporization of the feed is $\lambda = 32{,}000$ kJ/kmol. Calculate the reboiler duty. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **steady-state material balance** around a continuous distillation column. State clearly the assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q-B(a):**

**Derivation (refer to Section 3.4):**
1. Energy balance on reboiler: $Q_R + \bar{L} H_L = \bar{V} H_V + W H_W$
2. Mass balance on reboiler: $\bar{V} = \bar{L} - W$
3. Express $\bar{V} = D(R+1) - (1-q)F$ and $\bar{L} = RD + qF$
4. Final expression (for saturated liquid feed, $q = 1$):

$$Q_R = D(R+1)\lambda$$

**Numerical Calculation:**
$$Q_R = D(R+1)\lambda = 50 \times (2.0 + 1) \times 32{,}000$$
$$Q_R = 50 \times 3.0 \times 32{,}000$$
$$\boxed{Q_R = 4{,}800{,}000 \text{ kJ/hr} = 1333.33 \text{ kW}}$$

---

**Model Solution for Q-B(b):**

Refer to the **block diagram in Section 4.1** of these notes. The student should present:

**Assumptions for Material Balance:**
1. Steady-state operation (no accumulation).
2. Single feed, two products (binary).
3. No chemical reaction (purely physical separation).
4. Negligible entrainment of liquid in vapor and vapor in liquid.
5. Constant molar overflow in each section.

**Material Balance Equations:**
- **Overall:** $F = D + W$
- **Component:** $F z_F = D x_D + W x_W$
- **Internal flows:** $V = L + D$, $\bar{V} = \bar{L} - W$
- **Reflux:** $L = RD$, $V = D(R+1)$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting units:** Always convert $Q_R$ from kJ/hr to kW (divide by 3600) or vice-versa. **−2 marks** if wrong units.
> 2. **Confusing $R$ and $R+1$:** Students often write $Q_R = D \cdot R \cdot \lambda$ instead of $D(R+1)\lambda$. **−1 mark**.
> 3. **Wrong overall balance:** The equation is $F = D + W$, **not** $D = F + W$ or $F = D - W$. **−1 mark**.
> 4. **Skipping the q-line derivation:** For 7-mark derivation questions, you must show **all four steps** (rectifying line, stripping line, feed-stage relation, intersection). Skipping any step costs **−2 marks**.
> 5. **Not specifying assumptions:** Always list the **constant molar overflow** and **equilibrium stages** assumptions for McCabe-Thiele questions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU PEPOT638 Module 1 — Heat & Material Balance:**

- **Definition Triad:** Distillation = Mass Transfer + Heat Input + Vapor-Liquid Equilibrium.
- **Core Equation:** $F = D + W$ and $F z_F = D x_D + W x_W$ — must be applied **simultaneously** for binary systems.
- **Reflux Ratio:** $R = L/D$. Operating $R$ is always $\geq R_{min}$. Typical operating range: $1.2R_{min}$ to $1.5R_{min}$.
- **Vapor Flow Equation:** $V = D(R+1)$. This is the **central equation** for reboiler sizing.
- **Feed Quality (q):** Memorize the formula $q = (H_V - H_F)/(H_V - H_L)$ and **all 5 special cases** ($q > 1$, $q = 1$, $0 < q < 1$, $q = 0$, $q < 0$).
- **q-line Equation:** $y = \frac{q}{q-1}x - \frac{z_F}{q-1}$ — memorize this; it appears in **every** graphical method question.
- **Reboiler Duty:** $Q_R = D(R+1)\lambda$ — the **most-tested formula** in Part B questions.
- **Condenser Duty:** $Q_C \approx V \lambda$ (total condenser assumption).
- **Energy Order of Magnitude:** A typical refinery CDU consumes **50–150 MW** of thermal energy; this is why **heat integration** (pumparounds, prefractionation) is critical.
- **Assumptions Checklist:** Steady state, constant molar overflow, theoretical stages, total condenser, equilibrium on each stage — **always state these** in derivations.
- **KTU Mark Distribution Tip:** 14-mark questions typically split as **7 + 7** (one derivation + one numerical OR one theory + one numerical). Practice both formats.
- **Common Units Used in KTU Papers:** kg/hr, kmol/hr, kJ/hr, kW, °C, K, atm, bar, ppm.
- **Industrial Relevance:** The **Atmospheric Distillation Unit (ADU)** and **Vacuum Distillation Unit (VDU)** together account for **~40% of refinery capital cost** and **~30% of energy use** — mastering this topic is essential for both exams and placements.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Trays
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT638 - PETROLEUM REFINERY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Types of Trays

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of **Petroleum Refinery Engineering**, a **Tray** (also called a **plate** or **deck**) is the fundamental mass-transfer element installed at discrete elevations inside a **distillation column** (e.g., the crude oil atmospheric distillation unit, vacuum distillation unit, FCC main fractionator, and hydrocracker product splitter). Each tray provides a **stage** where ascending **vapor** and descending **liquid** are brought into intimate, counter-current contact, enabling inter-phase **mass transfer** of light and heavy hydrocarbon components between the phases.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim-Equivalent):**
> *“Trays are the internal contacting devices in tray-type distillation columns used in petroleum refineries to promote vapor–liquid equilibrium contact, thereby separating hydrocarbon mixtures into specified product cuts such as Naphtha, Kerosene, Diesel, and Atmospheric Gas Oil (AGO).”*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **multi-storey parking building** with cars (vapor) entering from the ground floor and moving upward through ramps, while pedestrians (liquid) walk downward on stairs. At **every floor**, vehicles slow down, pedestrians cross paths, and a brief "exchange" occurs — heavy luggage is dropped off and light parcels are picked up. Each floor is essentially a **tray**; the entire building is the **distillation column**. The more efficient the crossing mechanism (better tray design), the better the separation of "heavy" from "light" items.

## 1.3 Why Trays Matter in Refineries

Crude oil is a complex multi-component mixture (C1 to C100+). Refineries rely on trays to:

- Maximize **fractionation sharpness** between adjacent products (e.g., Naphtha vs. Kerosene cut point).
- Maintain **turndown flexibility** during feed composition changes.
- Operate reliably with **fouling-resistant** internals due to dirty, heavy hydrocarbon feeds.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Industrial Metric:**
> Refinery distillation columns typically operate at tray **spacings of 0.45 m to 0.91 m (18 in to 36 in)**, with column diameters ranging from **1 m to 10 m** in world-scale crude units. Tray efficiency (Murphree) for hydrocarbon systems generally lies in the range of **60 % to 85 %**.

## 1.4 Primary Classification of Trays

Trays are broadly classified based on the **vapor–liquid contact mechanism**:

1. **Bubble-Cap Trays** — oldest design; uses bubble caps over risers.
2. **Sieve Trays (Perforated Trays)** — simple holes punched in the deck.
3. **Valve Trays** — movable valves (fixed or floating) modulate vapor flow.
4. **Special / Proprietary Trays** — dual-flow, disc-and-doughnut, baffle, shower deck, ripple trays, etc.

> [!TIP]
> In **modern refineries (>1990)**, **valve trays dominate (>60 % market share)** because of their wide operating range, high capacity, and tolerance to feed variability.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Tray — Universal Terminology

Before comparing types, students must internalize the **geometry of a tray**. The same terminology applies to all tray types:

- **Active Area (Aₐ):** The perforated/valved portion where vapor contacts liquid. Excludes downcomers.
- **Downcomer Area (A_d):** The cross-section reserved for liquid to descend to the tray below.
- **Weir (inlet & outlet):** A vertical metal strip that maintains a **liquid level** on the tray.
- **Weir Height (h_w):** Typically **50 mm to 100 mm** in refinery service.
- **Weir Length (l_w):** Length over which liquid flows into the downcomer.
- **Downcomer Clearance:** Gap under the downcomer apron; controls liquid velocity.
- **Deck / Floor Plate:** The flat plate carrying the holes/valves/bubble caps.

## 2.2 Operating Concepts Common to All Trays

The operational regime of any tray is described by the **operating window** bounded by:

1. **Weep Point** — minimum vapor velocity below which liquid *drains* through the holes (entrainment of liquid into vapor).
2. **Flooding Point** — maximum vapor velocity above which liquid *cannot* descend (excessive entrainment, column flooding).
3. **Entrainment** — liquid droplets carried upward by vapor.
4. **Turndown Ratio** — ratio of maximum to minimum operable vapor rate.

$$
\text{Turndown Ratio} = \frac{V_{\max}}{V_{\min}}
$$

A high-quality tray should offer a wide operating window between the weep and flood points.

## 2.3 Tray-by-Tray Theoretical Analysis

### 2.3.1 Bubble-Cap Trays

- Invented in the 1820s; the **workhorse** of the early petroleum era.
- **Construction:** A cylindrical **cap (8–25 cm dia.)** sits inverted over a **riser (chimney)** welded to the deck. Vapor rises through the riser, reverses direction at the cap, and exits through **rectangular slots** cut around the cap periphery, bubbling through the liquid.
- **Advantages:** Excellent vapor–liquid contact at low vapor rates, highly resistant to weeping, ideal for **low-turndown** refinery services.
- **Disadvantages:** High pressure drop, expensive, complex fabrication, prone to fouling with dirty crudes, low capacity.

> [!NOTE]
> Bubble-cap trays are now largely **obsolete in grassroots refineries**, but you will still find them in **older FCC main fractionators and alkylation columns** (pre-1980 installations).

### 2.3.2 Sieve (Perforated) Trays

- Simplest possible design: a flat plate with **uniform round holes (3–13 mm dia.)** punched in a triangular pitch.
- **Mechanism:** Vapor jets up through the holes, shears the liquid, and creates a frothy **two-phase dispersion** above the deck.
- **Hole area to active area ratio (φ):** Typically **8 % to 15 %** in refinery service.
- **Advantages:** Lowest pressure drop, lowest cost, easy to clean, high capacity (~30 % more than bubble caps), well understood.
- **Disadvantages:** Limited turndown (prone to weeping at low vapor rates), susceptible to **vapor pulsation** at certain loadings.
- **Best for:** Clean, non-fouling services such as **Naphtha Hydrotreater strippers, Debutanizers, Reformate Splitters**.

### 2.3.3 Valve Trays

The dominant tray type in modern refineries. Two sub-types:

| Sub-type | Description | Best Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Fixed Valve** | Lifting legs welded to the deck; valves do not move vertically. Fixed opening area. | When feed is consistent and turndown is moderate. |
| **Floating Valve** (most common) | Lightweight circular or rectangular caps lift as vapor velocity increases. Common designs: **V-1, V-4 (Glitsch), VG-0 (Koch-Glitsch), MV (Sulzer)**. | Refinery main fractionators — high turndown. |

**Working Principle:**
- At **low vapor rate** → valve sits on the deck, restricting area → high velocity through the small gap → prevents weeping.
- At **high vapor rate** → valve lifts fully → larger area → pressure drop stays nearly constant → delay of flooding.

This **self-modulating** behavior is why valve trays tolerate a turndown ratio of **4:1 or higher** versus **2:1** for sieve trays.

### 2.3.4 Special / Proprietary Trays

- **Dual-Flow Trays:** No downcomers. Liquid and vapor flow counter-current through the **same holes**. High capacity but low efficiency; used in **crude atmospheric columns** (top sections) and **FCC slurry pumparounds**.
- **Disc-and-Doughnut Trays:** Alternating discs (solid) and doughnuts (annular) in adjacent trays. Robust to fouling; used historically in **vacuum units** for heavy AGO/Lube oil.
- **Shower Deck (Shed) Trays:** Solid decks with no perforations; liquid showers through overflow weirs while vapor flows below. Used in **wash sections of FCC main fractionators** to contact heavy slurry with rising vapor.
- **Ripple / Ladder Trays:** Low-pressure-drop trays for **vacuum columns** (lubricating oil refineries).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are the **highest-yield** formulas tested in KTU 2024 Scheme PEPOT638 examinations for tray design and analysis.

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\eta_M = \dfrac{y_n - y_{n+1}}{y_n^{*} - y_{n+1}}$ | **Murphree Vapor-Phase Tray Efficiency** | Dimensionless (0–1) |
| 2 | $E_o = \dfrac{\eta_M \cdot N}{N_{th}}$ | **Overall Column Efficiency** (Lewis correlation) | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $u_h = K \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_L - \rho_V}{\rho_V}}$ | **Souders–Brown flooding correlation** for max superficial vapor velocity | m/s |
| 4 | $\Delta P_{dry} = C_v \cdot \rho_V \cdot u_h^{2}$ | Dry tray pressure drop (sieve/valve) | Pa (or mm H₂O) |
| 5 | $\Delta P_{total} = \Delta P_{dry} + \Delta P_{liquid} + \Delta P_{residual}$ | Total tray pressure drop | Pa |
| 6 | $V_{min} = \dfrac{q_{\min}}{A_h}$ | Vapor velocity at **weep point** | m/s |
| 7 | $q_{L,\max} = 0.5 \cdot A_d \cdot \sqrt{g \cdot h_{dc}}$ | Max liquid downflow before **downcomer flooding** | m³/s |
| 8 | $D_T = \sqrt{\dfrac{4(V+Z)}{\pi \cdot u_f}}$ | Column diameter from vapor + liquid flow and flooding velocity | m |
| 9 | $N_{th} = \dfrac{\ln\left(\dfrac{x_D}{x_B}\right)}{\ln(\alpha_{avg})}$ | Minimum theoretical stages (Fenske) — for reference | — |
| 10 | $h_w = 0.05 \text{ to } 0.10$ m | Typical refinery weir height | m |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Convention:** In KTU exam scripts, the **Greek symbol $\eta$ (eta)** denotes tray efficiency, $\Delta P$ denotes pressure drop, $u_h$ denotes hole velocity, and $u_f$ denotes flooding velocity. Maintain these notations consistently.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section builds the **analytical foundation** the examiner expects when solving 14-mark design/sizing problems on tray selection.

## 3.1 Derivation: Murphree Tray Efficiency and Overall Column Efficiency

The **Murphree efficiency** is the single most important tray-level efficiency metric in KTU valuation.

### 3.1.1 Defining the Local Efficiency

On a single tray, consider a vapor parcel entering at composition $y_{n+1}$ and leaving at composition $y_n$. The liquid leaving is in equilibrium with vapor at $y_n^{*}$.

By definition of **point (local) efficiency** $E_{MV}$ for a differential contact zone:

$$
E_{MV} = \frac{\text{Actual change in vapor composition}}{\text{Change if vapor left in equilibrium with exiting liquid}}
$$

$$
E_{MV} = \frac{y_n - y_{n+1}}{y_n^{*} - y_{n+1}}
$$

### 3.1.2 Cross-Flow Correction (Murphree Vapor Efficiency)

In real trays, liquid **flows horizontally** across the deck (from inlet downcomer to outlet downcomer). The vapor composition therefore varies along the liquid path. The **Murphree vapor efficiency** $\eta_M$ is the tray-averaged efficiency:

$$
\eta_M = \frac{\overline{y}_n - y_{n+1}}{y_n^{*} - y_{n+1}}
$$

where $\overline{y}_n$ is the vapor-composition-weighted average leaving the tray.

The relationship between local efficiency $E_{MV}$ and Murphree efficiency uses the **Lewis case 1** analysis (vapor perfectly mixed, liquid plug flow):

$$
\eta_M = \frac{1}{\lambda}\left(e^{\lambda E_{MV}} - 1\right)
$$

where the **stripping factor** is:

$$
\lambda = \frac{m \cdot V}{L}
$$

Here $m$ = slope of the equilibrium line, $V$ = molar vapor flow, $L$ = molar liquid flow.

### 3.1.3 Overall Column Efficiency (Lewis Correlation)

If $\eta_M$ is approximately constant across all $N$ actual trays, the **overall column efficiency** $E_o$ is:

$$
E_o = \frac{N_{\text{ideal}}}{N_{\text{actual}}}
$$

Substituting into the Kremser/Smoluchowski form:

$$
E_o = \frac{\ln\left[1 + \eta_M\left(\dfrac{1}{mV/L} - 1\right)\right]}{\ln\left(\dfrac{1}{mV/L}\right)}
$$

This is the **Lewis equation**, the most-tested relationship for overall efficiency in KTU examinations.
