## 3.2 Derivation: Souders–Brown Flooding Correlation

This equation determines the **maximum allowable superficial vapor velocity** $u_f$ in the column cross-section. It is derived from a **force balance** on a liquid droplet suspended in upward vapor:

$$
\text{Drag force on droplet} = \text{Gravity force on droplet} - \text{Buoyancy}
$$

For a droplet of diameter $d_p$ and density $\rho_L$ in vapor of density $\rho_V$:

$$
C_D \cdot \frac{\pi d_p^{2}}{4} \cdot \frac{\rho_V u^{2}}{2} = \frac{\pi d_p^{3}}{6} \cdot (\rho_L - \rho_V) g
$$

Solving for $u$:

$$
u = \sqrt{\frac{4 g d_p (\rho_L - \rho_V)}{3 C_D \rho_V}}
$$

Empirically, Souders & Brown collapsed the constants into $K$:

$$
\boxed{u_f = K \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\rho_L - \rho_V}{\rho_V}}}
$$

Typical $K$ values for refinery columns:
- **Bubble-cap trays:** $K \approx 0.08 \text{ m/s}$
- **Sieve trays:** $K \approx 0.10 \text{ m/s}$
- **Valve trays:** $K \approx 0.11 \text{ m/s}$
- **Dual-flow trays:** $K \approx 0.20 \text{ m/s}$

## 3.3 Worked Analytical Problem — Capacity Comparison

**Problem Statement (KTU-Style):**
A refinery **naphtha stabilizer column** operates at top section with the following data:

- Vapor flow rate $V = 0.85 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$
- Liquid flow rate $L = 0.012 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$
- Vapor density $\rho_V = 4.8 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- Liquid density $\rho_L = 720 \text{ kg/m}^3$

**Step 1 — Compute the flooding velocity using Souders–Brown for sieve trays (K = 0.10 m/s):**

$$
u_f = 0.10 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{720 - 4.8}{4.8}}
$$

$$
u_f = 0.10 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{715.2}{4.8}}
$$

$$
u_f = 0.10 \cdot \sqrt{149.0}
$$

$$
u_f = 0.10 \cdot 12.21 = 1.221 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the flooding vapor flow:**

For sieve trays, the flooding capacity is based on the **net cross-sectional area** $A_n$ (total minus one downcomer):

$$
A_n = \frac{V}{u_f} = \frac{0.85}{1.221} = 0.696 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Compute total cross-sectional area (assume downcomer area $A_d = 0.10 A_n$):**

$$
A_T = A_n + A_d = 0.696 + 0.0696 = 0.766 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 4 — Compute column diameter:**

$$
D_T = \sqrt{\frac{4 A_T}{\pi}} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 0.766}{3.1416}} = \sqrt{0.975} = 0.988 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion:**
The column diameter is approximately **0.99 m** (rounded to next standard 1.0 m).

> [!TIP]
> The same problem for **valve trays** ($K = 0.11 \text{ m/s}$) gives a smaller required diameter $\approx 0.93$ m, while for **bubble-cap trays** ($K = 0.08 \text{ m/s}$) the required diameter grows to $\approx 1.18$ m. **This is why valve trays are preferred.**

## 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Performance Metric | Bubble-Cap | Sieve | Valve (Floating) | Dual-Flow |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Capacity (relative)** | 0.80 | 1.00 | 1.10 | 1.25 |
| **Pressure drop** | Highest | Lowest | Low | Lowest |
| **Turndown ratio** | 5:1 (best) | 2:1 (worst) | 4:1 | 1.5:1 |
| **Murphree efficiency** | 75–85 % | 60–75 % | 70–85 % | 50–65 % |
| **Fouling resistance** | Poor | Moderate | Moderate | Best |
| **Capital cost (relative)** | 2.5× | 1.0× | 1.4× | 1.1× |
| **Refinery use today** | Legacy only | Wide | Dominant | Specialty |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Tray Selection Decision Flow for Refineries

The following diagram shows the **decision logic** a refinery process engineer applies when selecting a tray type for a given service.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Define Separation Service] --> B{Feed Contains Fouling Solids or Heavy Crudes?}
    B -- Yes --> C{Downstream Fouling Tolerance is Low?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Select Dual-Flow or Disc-Doughnut Tray]
    C -- No --> E[Select Valve Tray with Push Valves]
    B -- No --> F{Turndown Ratio Required Greater than 3 to 1?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Select Floating Valve Tray V4 or VG0]
    F -- No --> H{Column Diameter Exceeds 6 m?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Select Sieve Tray with Cross Flow]
    H -- No --> J{Refinery Has Legacy Bubble Cap Inventory?}
    J -- Yes --> K[Retain Bubble Cap for Spare Compatibility]
    J -- No --> L[Select Modern Valve Tray as Default]
    D --> M[Output Tray Specification Sheet]
    E --> M
    G --> M
    I --> M
    K --> M
    L --> M
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Vapor–Liquid Flow Path on a Typical Cross-Flow Tray

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ColumnShell[Distillation Column Shell]
        direction TB
        V1[Ascending Vapor from Tray N minus 1] -->|Through Tray Holes or Valves| Deck1[Tray N Deck with Liquid Pool]
        Deck1 -->|Liquid Cross Flow| Weir1[Outlet Weir]
        Weir1 --> Down1[Downcomer to Tray N minus 1]
        Down1 --> Deck1
        V2[Purified Vapor Rises to Tray N plus 1] --> Deck2[Upper Tray N plus 1 Deck]
    end
    Down1 --> PumpAround[Pumparound or Reboiler Returns]
    PumpAround --> Deck1
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture: Tray Type Selection Matrix (Block Level)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Inputs[Process Inputs]
        I1[Feed Composition and Flowrate]
        I2[Column Pressure and Temperature]
        I3[Turndown Requirement]
        I4[Fouling Tendency of Feed]
    end
    subgraph Analysis[Engineering Analysis Block]
        A1[Calculate V and L molar flows]
        A2[Compute Souders Brown flooding velocity]
        A3[Estimate Murphree Efficiency]
        A4[Estimate Tray Pressure Drop]
    end
    subgraph Output[Selected Tray Architecture]
        O1[Bubble Cap Tray for Legacy Service]
        O2[Fixed Valve Tray for Moderate Turndown]
        O3[Floating Valve Tray for High Turndown]
        O4[Dual Flow Tray for Dirty Fouling Service]
    end
    Inputs --> Analysis
    Analysis --> O1
    Analysis --> O2
    Analysis --> O3
    Analysis --> O4
```

> [!NOTE]
> Mermaid is used here as a **functional architecture flow** because the physical 3D geometry of a tray (with bubble caps, risers, valves, downcomers, weirs) is best understood from textbook figures. The block diagram captures the **decision logic and functional flow** that examiners test in viva voce and short notes.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper, Module 1, 3 Marks]**
> *"Differentiate between bubble cap trays and sieve trays used in petroleum refinery distillation columns."*

**Model Answer (Marks Distribution):**
- **Construction (1 mark):** Bubble-cap trays have a chimney/riser and an inverted cap; sieve trays have simple round holes punched in a flat plate.
- **Vapor path (1 mark):** In bubble caps, vapor reverses direction 180° under the cap; in sieve trays, vapor jets straight up through the hole.
- **Performance (1 mark):** Bubble caps offer higher turndown (5:1) but higher pressure drop; sieve trays offer higher capacity and lower cost but limited turndown (~2:1).

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper, Module 1, 3 Marks]**
> *"Why are valve trays preferred over sieve trays in modern refinery distillation columns? Justify with two engineering reasons."*

**Model Answer:**
1. **Superior Turndown Range:** Floating valves self-modulate the open area as vapor velocity changes, giving a turndown ratio of **4:1 or more**, whereas sieve trays weep severely at low vapor rates (turndown ~2:1). *(1.5 marks)*
2. **Flooding Tolerance:** The valve lift response limits the dry-tray pressure drop at high vapor rates, delaying flooding and increasing column capacity by **10–15 %** versus sieve. *(1.5 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **Question A — Choice 1 [KTU University Exam – June 2024, 14 Marks]**
>
> **(a) [7 Marks]** Classify the different types of trays used in petroleum refinery distillation columns. Describe the working principle of a **floating valve tray** with a neat functional sketch and explain why it offers better turndown compared to a sieve tray.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks)** A refinery fractionator operates with the following data:
> - Vapor density $\rho_V = 5.2 \text{ kg/m}^3$
> - Liquid density $\rho_L = 760 \text{ kg/m}^3$
> - Vapor flow rate $V = 0.92 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$
>
> Using the Souders–Brown correlation with $K = 0.10$ m/s, calculate:
> (i) the flooding velocity,
> (ii) the column cross-sectional area, and
> (iii) the column diameter for a sieve tray design.
> Compare the diameter with that required if a valve tray ($K = 0.11$ m/s) were used instead.

### Model Solution — Q1(a)

**Classification (3 marks):**
- **Bubble-cap tray** (legacy).
- **Sieve (perforated) tray** (simple).
- **Valve tray** — fixed and floating.
- **Special trays** — dual-flow, disc-and-doughnut, shower deck.

**Floating Valve Working Principle (2 marks):**
A lightweight circular or rectangular cap rests on a circular hole drilled in the tray deck. The cap is held down by gravity but is free to lift as vapor pressure beneath it increases.
- At **low vapor rate**, the cap sits on the deck — minimum opening area — but vapor still achieves sufficient velocity through the small gap to prevent **weeping**.
- At **high vapor rate**, the cap lifts — opening area increases — pressure drop stabilizes and **flooding is delayed**.

**Turndown Comparison (2 marks):**
- Sieve tray: fixed hole area. At low vapor rates, liquid weeps through holes; at high rates, it floods quickly → turndown ~2:1.
- Floating valve: variable opening. As vapor rate falls, the cap descends and reduces area, maintaining high local velocity → no weeping. Hence turndown 4:1 to 5:1.

### Model Solution — Q1(b)

**Step 1 — Flooding velocity (sieve tray, K = 0.10 m/s):**

$$
u_f = 0.10 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{760 - 5.2}{5.2}} = 0.10 \cdot \sqrt{145.0} = 0.10 \cdot 12.04 = 1.204 \text{ m/s}
$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 2 — Net cross-sectional area:**

$$
A_n = \frac{V}{u_f} = \frac{0.92}{1.204} = 0.764 \text{ m}^2
$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 3 — Total cross-sectional area (downcomer area = 10% of net):**

$$
A_T = 1.10 \times 0.764 = 0.841 \text{ m}^2
$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 4 — Column diameter:**

$$
D_T = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 0.841}{\pi}} = \sqrt{1.071} = 1.035 \text{ m} \approx 1.05 \text{ m}
$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 5 — Recalculate for valve tray (K = 0.11 m/s):**

$$
u_{f,valve} = 0.11 \cdot \sqrt{145.0} = 0.11 \times 12.04 = 1.324 \text{ m/s}
$$

$$
A_{n,valve} = \frac{0.92}{1.324} = 0.695 \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
D_{T,valve} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 1.10 \times 0.695}{\pi}} = \sqrt{0.973} = 0.987 \text{ m} \approx 0.99 \text{ m}
$$

*(2 marks for full comparison and comment)*

**Conclusion:** The valve tray design yields a smaller diameter (~5% reduction), translating to lower column shell cost and better dynamic response.

---

> **Question B — Choice 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, 14 Marks]**
>
> **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the working of (i) sieve tray, (ii) bubble-cap tray, and (iii) dual-flow tray. Compare their **turndown ratios** and typical refinery applications in a tabular form.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** A crude unit atmospheric distillation column has 44 actual trays and operates at an average Murphree vapor efficiency of $\eta_M = 0.72$. The relative volatility between the key components is $\alpha = 1.45$, and the desired top and bottom compositions give a minimum number of theoretical stages (Fenske) $N_{\min} = 18$. Estimate the **overall column efficiency** using the Lewis correlation and discuss what it implies for tray selection.

### Model Solution — Q2(a)

**Working of each tray (1.5 marks each = 4.5 marks):**
- **Sieve:** Vapor passes through uniform holes, shearing the liquid into a froth. Liquid flows cross-wise to the outlet weir.
- **Bubble-cap:** Vapor rises through the riser, hits the cap roof, reverses direction, and exits through peripheral slots — bubbling gently through the liquid.
- **Dual-flow:** No downcomers. Vapor and liquid pass counter-current through the same set of holes; capacity is high but turndown is poor.

**Comparison table (2.5 marks):**

| Tray | Turndown | Typical Refinery Service |
|---|---|---|
| Bubble-cap | 5:1 | Legacy crude columns, alkylation units |
| Sieve | 2:1 | Naphtha hydrotreater strippers, debutanizers |
| Dual-flow | 1.5:1 | Top of atmospheric column, FCC slurry pumparound |

### Model Solution — Q2(b)

**Step 1 — Compute the actual number of theoretical stages:**

$$
N_{actual,th} = \eta_M \times N_{actual} = 0.72 \times 44 = 31.7
$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 2 — Overall column efficiency:**

$$
E_o = \frac{N_{actual,th}}{N_{actual}} \times 100\% = 0.72 \times 100 = 72\%
$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 3 — Compare to Fenske minimum (cross-check):**

The ratio $\dfrac{N_{\min}}{N_{actual,th}} = \dfrac{18}{31.7} = 0.568$ indicates the column is operating at about **1.76× the minimum reflux condition** — a healthy design margin for refinery fractionators.

*(1.5 marks)*

**Step 4 — Discussion — Tray Selection Implication (3.5 marks):**
- $\eta_M = 0.72$ is consistent with a **clean service (sieve or fixed valve)** in a non-fouling hydrocarbon system.
- If crude quality degrades (e.g., higher TAN, more asphaltenes), $\eta_M$ may drop to 0.60 → engineer should switch to **floating valve trays with fixed valve caps** to maintain turndown.
- The Lewis equation simplification holds because $mV/L$ is approximately constant across the column — for refinery columns this is acceptable within ±5 %.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Traps**
>
> 1. **Unit Mistakes (–2 marks):** Students frequently mix up densities. Recall that in the Souders–Brown equation, $\rho_L$ and $\rho_V$ are in **kg/m³** and the result $u_f$ is in **m/s** (not cm/s).
> 2. **Confusing Hole Velocity vs. Superficial Velocity (–2 marks):** $u_h$ (hole velocity) ≠ superficial velocity $u_s$. KTU scripts often penalize when these are used interchangeably. The flooding equation uses **superficial** velocity based on the **net cross-section**, not the hole area.
> 3. **Forgetting the Downcomer Area in Diameter Calculation (–2 marks):** Column diameter is based on $A_T = A_n + A_d$, not just $A_n$. Omitting the downcomer area is a classic KTU trap.
> 4. **Confusing Murphree vs. Overall Efficiency (–2 marks):** $\eta_M$ is per-tray; $E_o$ is for the whole column. Writing one in place of the other costs 2 marks.
> 5. **Using the Wrong K for Tray Type (–1 mark):** Always state the tray type and confirm the appropriate $K$ value (bubble cap: 0.08, sieve: 0.10, valve: 0.11).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU PEPOT638 — Module 1, "Types of Trays"**

- [ ] **Four primary tray families:** Bubble-cap, Sieve, Valve (fixed/floating), Special (dual-flow, disc-and-doughnut, shower deck, ripple).
- [ ] **Bubble-cap tray** = legacy; best turndown; **highest pressure drop**; **lowest capacity**.
- [ ] **Sieve tray** = simplest construction; **hole diameter 3–13 mm**; **φ (hole/active area ratio) = 8–15 %**; turndown ~2:1.
- [ ] **Valve tray** = **industry default** in modern refineries; turndown 4:1 to 5:1; capacity 10 % above sieve.
- [ ] **Dual-flow tray** = no downcomers; **highest capacity**; **lowest efficiency**; used for **dirty/fouling feeds**.
- [ ] **Active area (Aₐ) ≠ Net area (Aₙ) ≠ Total area (A_T)** — know the difference for sizing.
- [ ] **Weir height** in refinery service: **50–100 mm**; downcomer clearance drives liquid residence time.
- [ ] **Murphree efficiency** $\eta_M$ ranges **0.6–0.85** for refinery hydrocarbon systems.
- [ ] **Souders–Brown equation:** $u_f = K \sqrt{(\rho_L - \rho_V)/\rho_V}$ — know $K$ values for each tray type.
- [ ] **Lewis correlation** links Murphree efficiency to overall column efficiency.
- [ ] **Turndown ratio:** $V_{max}/V_{min}$ — critical for refinery flexibility during feed swings.
- [ ] **Tray spacing:** **0.45 m to 0.91 m** in refinery columns; higher spacing → higher capacity, taller column.
- [ ] **Pressure drop order** (highest to lowest): Bubble-cap > Valve > Sieve > Dual-flow.
- [ ] **Always state the tray type** in any KTU answer about $K$-based calculations — examiners deduct marks otherwise.
- [ ] **Common refinery services:** Sieve → clean services; Valve → main fractionators; Dual-flow → fouling/wash sections.
- [ ] **Disc-and-doughnut** is the historical choice for **vacuum columns** with heavy gas-oil service.

> **End of Module 1 — Types of Trays Notes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Refining Processes: Cracking
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT638 - PETROLEUM REFINERY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Refining Processes: Cracking

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Cracking** is defined as the set of secondary refinery processes in which high-molecular-weight hydrocarbons (typically heavy gas oils, residue, vacuum gas oil — VGO, and deasphalted oil) are decomposed into lower-molecular-weight, more valuable products such as gasoline, diesel, LPG, light olefins, and gas oils by the application of **heat (thermal cracking)**, **catalysts (catalytic cracking)**, or a combination of **hydrogen and catalyst (hydrocracking)**.

The word *crack* literally means to break, which perfectly describes the process — large hydrocarbon molecules are **broken (ruptured)** at C–C bonds into smaller fragments. The process is **endothermic** when products are gases and **slightly exothermic** for catalytic cracking due to the hydrogen transfer reactions that saturate olefins.

**Standard Refinery Metrics (bolded for emphasis):**

- **Conversion (%)** $= \dfrac{\text{Feed (vol \%)} - \text{Unconverted Oil (vol \%)}}{\text{Feed (vol \%)}} \times 100$
- **Yield** = Mass or volume of specific product per unit feed (e.g., **wt % gasoline**)
- **Selectivity** $= \dfrac{\text{Desired Product Yield}}{\text{Total Conversion}} \times 100$
- **Operating Severity** is commonly expressed as a function of reactor temperature (**500 °C – 900 °C**), pressure (**1 atm – 70 atm**), and **space velocity (LHSV = 0.2 – 10 h⁻¹**).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy: "Breaking a Long Train"**
> Imagine a long freight train carrying 50 coal wagons. You cannot deliver this efficiently to 50 different small factories. So you "crack" the train at junctions into smaller trains of 5, 10, or 20 wagons — each routed to a specific destination. *Cracking does the same thing with hydrocarbon molecules*. A heavy C₃₀+ hydrocarbon (the long train) is "cracked" at selected C–C bonds to form C₅–C₁₂ gasoline molecules, C₁–C₄ LPG gases, and valuable **light olefins (ethylene, propylene, butylene)** — the building blocks of the petrochemical industry.

### 1.3 Why Cracking is Essential — The Market Demand Gap

Crude oil naturally contains only about **15–25 % gasoline** by volume. However, the market demand profile is:

- **Gasoline (naphtha) demand:** ~35–40 %
- **Diesel + Kerosene (middle distillate) demand:** ~35–40 %
- **Heavy fuel oil demand:** < 15 % (and rapidly declining due to IMO 2020 sulphur regulations)

This **mismatch** between crude supply and product demand is the **fundamental economic driver** of all cracking processes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 PEPOT638 Module 2):** Students must understand the *operational principle*, *reactor design logic*, *feedstock-product relationships*, and *yield patterns* of:
> 1. **Thermal Cracking** (Visbreaking, Steam Cracking, Coking)
> 2. **Catalytic Cracking** (FCC, TCC)
> 3. **Hydrocracking**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Cracking Processes (Hierarchical Logic)

Cracking is broadly classified into three families, distinguished by the *driving force* of bond cleavage:

1. **Thermal Cracking (Homolytic / Free-Radical Mechanism)**
   - Bond cleavage via **heat alone**.
   - Follows the **Rice–Herzfeld free-radical chain mechanism** (Initiation → Propagation → Termination).
   - Sub-processes:
     - **Visbreaking** — mild thermal cracking (440–490 °C) to reduce viscosity of residue.
     - **Steam Cracking** — high-severity (750–900 °C) for olefin production.
     - **Coking** — severe (480–540 °C) for complete carbon rejection:
       - **Delayed Coking**
       - **Fluid Coking**
       - **Flexicoking**

2. **Catalytic Cracking (Heterolytic / Carbocation Mechanism)**
   - Bond cleavage via **acidic solid catalyst** (typically **zeolite Y / ZSM-5 / USY**).
   - Follows the **Whitmore carbocation (carbonium/carbonium ion) mechanism**.
   - Sub-processes:
     - **Fluid Catalytic Cracking (FCC)** — dominant modern process.
     - **Thermofor Catalytic Cracking (TCC)** — obsolete, moving-bed.
     - **Mild Thermal + Catalytic** combinations.

3. **Hydrocracking (Hydrogenation + Cracking Hybrid)**
   - Cracking occurs in a **hydrogen-rich atmosphere** over a **bifunctional catalyst** (acidic support + metal hydrogenation sites like Pt, Pd, Ni-Mo, W).
   - Produces saturated products (no coke, low sulphur/nitrogen).
   - Operates at **high pressure (35–200 bar)** and moderate temperature (**300–450 °C**).

### 2.2 Core Operating Logic — "Why" and "How" Behind Each Step

#### A. Thermal Cracking — Free Radical Mechanism

$$\text{C–C bond} \xrightarrow{\Delta} \text{CH}_3^{\bullet} + \text{R}^{\bullet} \quad \text{(Initiation)}$$

$$\text{R}^{\bullet} + \text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n+2} \rightarrow \text{RH} + \text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n+1}^{\bullet} \quad \text{(Propagation)}$$

$$\text{R}^{\bullet} + \text{R}^{\prime\bullet} \rightarrow \text{R–R}^{\prime} \quad \text{(Termination)}$$

> **Why free radicals?** At high temperatures (>400 °C), C–C bonds (bond energy ~ 347 kJ/mol) are activated homolytically. The β-scission of the radical produces an **α-olefin** (always terminal, Markovnikov-violating) and a smaller radical — this explains why **thermal cracking produces high olefin yields**.

> **How severity controls products:** Higher temperature + longer residence time → more secondary cracking → more gas + coke. Hence **Steam Cracking (high T, short τ)** → olefins; **Coking (moderate T, long τ)** → solid coke.

#### B. Catalytic Cracking — Carbocation Mechanism

$$\text{RH} \xrightarrow{\text{H}^+ \text{ (Brønsted acid site)}} \text{R}^+ + \text{H}_2 \quad \text{(Initiation via hydride abstraction)}$$

$$\text{R}^+ \xrightarrow{\beta\text{-scission}} \text{smaller olefin} + \text{new R}^+ \quad \text{(Propagation)}$$

$$\text{R}^+ + \text{R}^{\prime}\text{H} \rightarrow \text{RH} + \text{R}^{\prime +} \quad \text{(Hydride transfer)}$$

> **Why carbocations?** Solid acids (zeolites) donate protons to olefins (or abstract hydrides from paraffins), forming planar **sp²-hybridized carbocations**. β-scission is facile and proceeds via the *most stable* carbocation — favoring **branched isomers (isobutane, isopentane)** that give **high octane gasoline**.

> **How catalyst deactivates:** Coke (polycyclic aromatic carbon) deposits on acidic sites, blocking pores. This is why **FCC uses a regenerator** where coke is burned off in air, restoring catalyst activity. **Catalyst-to-oil ratio (C/O)** is the key operating handle (typically **5 – 8 kg/kg**).

#### C. Hydrocracking — Bifunctional Logic

The catalyst has two active site types:
- **Metal sites (Ni-Mo, Pt, Pd):** promote **hydrogenation** of olefins and heteroatom removal.
- **Acid sites (zeolite, silica-alumina):** promote **C–C scission** via carbocations.

> **Why hydrogen suppresses coke?** Hydrogenation immediately saturates the olefins formed by β-scission, **preventing the oligomerization → cyclization → aromatization → coke** pathway. Result: **Near-zero coke, very high conversion (>95 %)** and **clean products (S < 10 ppm, N < 1 ppm)**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Quantity / Parameter** | **Formula** | **Typical Numerical Range** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Conversion ($X$) | $X = \dfrac{F_{\text{in}} - F_{\text{unconv}}}{F_{\text{in}}} \times 100$ | 50 – 90 | % (vol) |
| Yield of product $i$ ($Y_i$) | $Y_i = \dfrac{\text{Mass or Volume of product } i}{\text{Mass or Volume of feed}}$ | 30 – 80 | % (wt or vol) |
| Selectivity ($S_i$) | $S_i = \dfrac{Y_i}{X} \times 100$ | 40 – 90 | % |
| Space Velocity (LHSV) | $\text{LHSV} = \dfrac{V_{\text{feed/h}}}{V_{\text{catalyst}}}$ | 0.2 – 10 | h⁻¹ |
| Weight Hourly Space Velocity (WHSV) | $\text{WHSV} = \dfrac{m_{\text{feed/h}}}{m_{\text{catalyst}}}$ | 0.5 – 5 | h⁻¹ |
| Catalyst-to-Oil Ratio (C/O) | $\text{C/O} = \dfrac{\text{Mass catalyst circulated}}{\text{Mass oil feed}}$ | 5 – 8 (FCC) | kg/kg |
| Severity Index (Steam Cracking) | $\text{SI} = f(T, \tau, \text{P})$ — Coats–Bechtel model | 0 – 50 | Dimensionless |
| Cracking Stoichiometry (Paraffin) | $\text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n+2} \rightarrow \text{C}_a\text{H}_{2a+2} + \text{C}_b\text{H}_{2b}$ where $a + b = n$ | n = 10 – 50 | — |
| Hydrogen Balance (Hydrocracker) | $\text{H}_2 \text{ consumption} = 0.02 \text{ to } 0.04$ × feed mass | 200 – 400 | Nm³/m³ feed |
| Coke Yield (FCC) | Typically **3 – 7 wt %** on feed | — | wt % |
| Octane Number of FCC Naphtha | RON = 90 – 95, MON = 80 – 85 | — | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** These formulas are used by **process engineers at IOCL, BPCL, Reliance Jamnagar, and global refineries (ExxonMobil, Shell, UOP, Chevron Lummus)** for daily yield accounting, **LP (Linear Programming) refinery optimization**, and **planning monthly crude diets**. KTU examiners often test the **conversion-yield-selectivity** triplet as a 7-mark problem.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Material Balance Around an FCC Reactor- regenerator

Consider a steady-state FCC unit with feed stream $F$ (kg/h), fresh catalyst makeup $M_c$, and product streams of gasoline $G$, light cycle oil (LCO) $L$, heavy cycle oil (HCO) $H$, dry gas $D$, and coke $C$ deposited on catalyst.

**Step 1: Overall Mass Balance (steady state, no accumulation).**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Input} &= \text{Total Output} \\[4pt]
F + M_c &= G + L + H + D + C + \text{losses}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Defining Conversion in terms of feed boiling above 221 °C (350 °F).**

Let $F_{>221}$ = mass of feed with boiling point > 221 °C. The unconverted oil (UCO) is the bottoms product of the main fractionator, i.e., slurry + clarified oil = HCO.

$$\begin{aligned}
X \,(\%) &= \dfrac{F_{>221} - \text{UCO}_{>221}}{F_{>221}} \times 100 \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{F_{>221} - H}{F_{>221}} \times 100
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Defining Yield.**

For gasoline (boiling range C₅ – 221 °C):

$$Y_G \,(\text{wt \%}) = \dfrac{G}{F} \times 100$$

**Step 4: Selectivity to Gasoline.**

$$S_G = \dfrac{Y_G}{X} \times 100 = \dfrac{G}{F_{>221} - H} \times 100$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Worked Numerical Example (KTU 2018–2023 Pattern)

> **Problem:** A commercial FCC unit processes **50,000 kg/h** of VGO feed. Mass fractions of products measured over 24 hours are:
> - Dry gas (C₁–C₂): 4.0 wt %
> - LPG (C₃–C₄): 14.0 wt %
> - Gasoline (C₅ – 221 °C): 52.0 wt %
> - LCO (221–343 °C): 18.0 wt %
> - Slurry (HCO, > 343 °C): 8.0 wt %
> - Coke: 4.0 wt %
>
> **Calculate:** (a) Total mass balance closure check, (b) Conversion assuming feed has 85 wt % boiling above 221 °C, (c) Selectivity to gasoline.

**Step (a): Mass Balance Check.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Sum of all products} &= 4.0 + 14.0 + 52.0 + 18.0 + 8.0 + 4.0 \\[4pt]
&= 100.0 \text{ wt \%}
\end{aligned}$$

Mass balance closes within 0.1 %, so the data is internally consistent.

**Step (b): Conversion Calculation.**

The unconverted oil (UCO) at 221 °C cut point consists of LCO + Slurry:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{UCO} &= \text{LCO} + \text{Slurry} = 18.0 + 8.0 = 26.0 \text{ wt \% on feed}
\end{aligned}$$

The feed has 85 wt % boiling above 221 °C, so feed heavier than 221 °C = 85 % of 50,000 = 42,500 kg/h.

Unconverted portion of heavy feed:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{UCO}_{>221} &= \text{UCO} \times \text{(fraction of UCO above 221)} \\
&\approx 26.0 \text{ wt \% (all UCO is } > 221 \text{ by definition)} \\
&= 0.26 \times 50{,}000 = 13{,}000 \text{ kg/h}
\end{aligned}$$

Heavy feed:

$$F_{>221} = 0.85 \times 50{,}000 = 42{,}500 \text{ kg/h}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
X &= \dfrac{42{,}500 - 13{,}000}{42{,}500} \times 100 \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{29{,}500}{42{,}500} \times 100 \\[4pt]
&= 69.41 \, \%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step (c): Selectivity to Gasoline.**

$$\begin{aligned}
Y_G &= \dfrac{52.0 \text{ wt \% (on total feed)}}{69.41 \text{ wt \% (on heavy feed)}} \times 100 \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{52.0}{69.41} \times 100 \\[4pt]
&= 74.92 \, \%
\end{aligned}$$

> **Result:** Conversion ≈ **69.4 %** and Gasoline Selectivity ≈ **75 %** — both within typical industrial FCC ranges.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Steam Cracking Severity–Yield Correlation (Coats–Bechtel Model)

For a given hydrocarbon feedstock, the yield of ethylene is empirically correlated with **Kinetic Severity Function (KSF)**:

$$\text{KSF} = \int_0^t k(T) \, dt$$

where $k(T)$ is the first-order rate constant for the primary cracking reaction following Arrhenius form:

$$k(T) = A \cdot \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{RT}\right)$$

For n-hexane steam cracking, typical Arrhenius parameters: $A = 1.0 \times 10^{13} \text{ s}^{-1}$, $E_a = 220 \text{ kJ/mol}$.

> **Example:** Compute the kinetic severity for a coil residence time $\tau = 0.4$ s at $T = 1050$ K.

$$\begin{aligned}
k(1050) &= 1.0 \times 10^{13} \cdot \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{220{,}000}{8.314 \times 1050}\right) \\[6pt]
&= 1.0 \times 10^{13} \cdot \exp(-25.20) \\[6pt]
&= 1.0 \times 10^{13} \times 1.20 \times 10^{-11} \\[6pt]
&= 120 \text{ s}^{-1}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{KSF} &= k \times \tau = 120 \times 0.4 = 48
\end{aligned}$$

This KSF ≈ 48 corresponds to **medium–high severity** steam cracking, producing **ethylene yields around 28–32 wt %** on feed.

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Delayed Coking Mass Balance (Decoking Cycle)

In a semi-batch delayed coker, one cycle = filling + coking + decoking.

Per cycle on a feed charge of $M_f$ kg of vacuum residue:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Gasoline + Naphtha (C}_5 \text{– 200 °C)} &: 8 \text{ – 15 wt \%} \\
\text{Light Gas Oil (200–350 °C)} &: 25 \text{ – 35 wt \%} \\
\text{Heavy Gas Oil (350–500 °C)} &: 15 \text{ – 25 wt \%} \\
\text{Coke} &: 25 \text{ – 35 wt \%} \\
\text{Dry Gas + LPG} &: 5 \text{ – 10 wt \%}
\end{aligned}$$

**Material closure check (typical mid-point values):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Sigma &= 11.5 + 30 + 20 + 30 + 8.5 = 100.0 \text{ wt \%}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Key Engineering Insight:** Delayed coking is a **carbon-rejection process** — it physically removes carbon as coke (a low-value solid), thereby upgrading the *liquid* fraction. The **C/H ratio** of feed (typically ~7.5) drops to ~6.5 in liquid products, with the excess carbon going to coke.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Classification — Cracking Process Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[CRACKING PROCESSES] --> nodeB[THERMAL CRACKING]
    nodeA --> nodeC[CATALYTIC CRACKING]
    nodeA --> nodeD[HYDROCRACKING]

    nodeB --> nodeB1[VISBREAKING]
    nodeB --> nodeB2[STEAM CRACKING]
    nodeB --> nodeB3[COKING]

    nodeB3 --> nodeB3a[DELAYED COKING]
    nodeB3 --> nodeB3b[FLUID COKING]
    nodeB3 --> nodeB3c[FLEXICOKING]

    nodeC --> nodeC1[FCC FLUID CATALYTIC CRACKING]
    nodeC --> nodeC2[TCC MOVING BED OBSOLETE]
    nodeC --> nodeC3[RESID FCC RFCC]

    nodeD --> nodeD1[ONCE THROUGH HYDROCRACKER]
    nodeD --> nodeD2[RECYCLE HYDROCRACKER]
    nodeD --> nodeD3[MILD HYDROCRACKING MHC]
```

### 4.2 FCC Unit — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeFeed[VGO FEED] --> nodeR1[REACTOR RISER]
    nodeReg[REGENERATED CATALYST] --> nodeR1
    nodeR1 --> nodeSep[REACTOR DISENGAGER STRIPPER]
    nodeSep --> nodeFra[MAIN FRACTIONATOR]
    nodeFra --> nodeG[GASOLINE PRODUCT]
    nodeFra --> nodeL[LCO PRODUCT]
    nodeFra --> nodeH[CLARIFIED OIL SLURRY]
    nodeFra --> nodeDG[DRY GAS LPG]
    nodeSep --> nodeS[COKE LADEN SPENT CATALYST]
    nodeS --> nodeRegen[REGENERATOR AIR BURN OFF]
    nodeRegen --> nodeReg
    nodeRegen --> nodeFlue[FLUE GAS CO2 CO HX TO COGEN]
```

### 4.3 Delayed Coking Cycle — Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeVR[VACUUM RESIDUE FEED] --> nodeF1[FRACTIONATOR BOTTOM]
    nodeF1 --> nodeH1[COKER FURNACE 500 °C]
    nodeH1 --> nodeDR1[DRUM A COKING 24 HR]
    nodeH1 --> nodeDR2[DRUM B COOLING DECOK]
    nodeDR1 --> nodeVF[UNCONVERTED VAPORS TO FRACTIONATOR]
    nodeDR1 --> nodeCK[PETROLEUM COKE PRODUCT 30 WT PERCENT]
    nodeVF --> nodeF1
    nodeDR2 --> nodeCK
    nodeDR2 --> nodeDR2a[HYDRO BLAST DECOKING]
```

### 4.4 Hydrocracker — Two-Stage Functional Block

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeH2[H2 MAKE UP AND RECYCLE] --> nodeMIX[MIXER]
    nodeHF[HEAVY FEED VGO OR DAO] --> nodeMIX
    nodeMIX --> nodeE1[HEAT EXCHANGER 300 °C]
    nodeE1 --> nodeF1[FURNACE 380 °C]
    nodeF1 --> nodeRX1[STAGE 1 REACTOR HYDROTREATMENT]
    nodeRX1 --> nodeRX2[STAGE 2 REACTOR HYDROCRACKING]
    nodeRX2 --> nodeHP[HIGH PRESSURE SEPARATOR]
    nodeHP --> nodeLP[LOW PRESSURE SEPARATOR]
    nodeLP --> nodeFRA[PRODUCT FRACTIONATOR]
    nodeFRA --> nodePG[GASOLINE KERO DIESEL PRODUCTS]
    nodeHP --> nodeCOMP[H2 RECYCLE COMPRESSOR]
    nodeCOMP --> nodeMIX
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Tip for KTU Exams:** When asked to draw any process flow, the examiner awards full marks only if (i) the **feed-preheat-reactor-separator-recycle** sequence is shown, (ii) **all major recycles** are marked, and (iii) **regeneration/heat integration** is indicated. A purely "boxes-and-arrows" sketch without these details loses 2–3 marks.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]** Differentiate between thermal cracking and catalytic cracking with respect to mechanism and products.

**Model Answer:**

| **Criterion** | **Thermal Cracking** | **Catalytic Cracking** |
|---|---|---|
| Mechanism | Free radical (homolytic) | Carbocation (heterolytic) |
| Driving Force | Heat only (~ 500 °C) | Acidic catalyst (zeolite) |
| Pressure | High (10–70 bar) | Near-atmospheric (1.2–3 bar) |
| Major Products | Olefins, gasoline, coke | Iso-paraffins, aromatics, gasoline |
| Octane of Naphtha | 60–75 RON | 90–95 RON |
| Coke Tendency | High | Moderate (regenerated) |

*(2 marks for 3 distinct points + 1 mark for conclusion statement)*

---

#### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** What is the role of a catalyst in fluid catalytic cracking (FCC)?

**Model Answer:**
The catalyst (typically **REUSY / USY zeolite** dispersed in a silica-alumina matrix) performs four key functions:
1. **Initiation** — abstracts hydride from paraffins, forming carbocations.
2. **β-Scission** — promotes C–C bond cleavage at the β-position relative to the cation.
3. **Isomerization** — produces branched isomers (high-octane components).
4. **Cyclic / Aromatic formation** — contributes to aromatics in the gasoline pool.

Additionally, the catalyst is **regenerated continuously** by burning off coke in the regenerator, allowing steady-state operation.

*(1 mark per valid point, 3 marks total)*

---

### Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Pattern (Module 2):** Two sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. Internal choice between two full questions.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

#### (a) **[7 Marks]** Describe with a neat flow diagram the working of a **Fluid Catalytic Cracking (FCC)** unit. List the major products and their typical yield ranges.

**Model Solution:**

**1. Process Description:**

The FCC unit consists of three interconnected sections:
- **Riser-Reactor Section** (vertical conduit, 0.5–1 s contact time)
- **Regenerator Section** (fluidized bed burning coke at 700–750 °C)
- **Fractionation Section** (main fractionator separates products by boiling point)

**2. Operating Steps (with rationale):**

(i) **Feed Injection:** Pre-heated VGO (260–370 °C) is atomized into the riser base through feed nozzles.
(ii) **Catalyst Contact:** Hot regenerated catalyst (700–730 °C) contacts feed, vaporizes it instantly, and the endothermic cracking raises the mixture to 500–540 °C.
(iii) **Cracking in Riser:** C–C bond scission occurs via carbocation mechanism; residence time is short to minimize secondary overcracking.
(iv) **Disengaging:** Catalyst and vapors separate in the reactor cyclone; catalyst falls into the stripper where steam strips adsorbed hydrocarbons.
(v) **Regeneration:** Spent catalyst (with 0.8–1.2 wt % coke) flows to the regenerator where air burns off coke at 700 °C, restoring activity.
(vi) **Fractionation:** Vapors go to main column yielding dry gas, LPG, gasoline, LCO, HCO/slurry.

**3. Typical Yields (per 100 kg VGO feed):**

| **Product** | **Boiling Range** | **Yield (wt %)** |
|---|---|---|
| Dry Gas (C₁–C₂) | < 30 °C | 3 – 6 |
| LPG (C₃–C₄) | 30 °C | 12 – 18 |
| Gasoline (Naphtha) | C₅ – 221 °C | 45 – 55 |
| LCO | 221 – 343 °C | 15 – 22 |
| Slurry / HCO | > 343 °C | 5 – 12 |
| Coke | Solid | 4 – 7 |

*(4 marks for description with flow diagram, 2 marks for product list, 1 mark for conclusion)*

#### (b) **[7 Marks]** A vacuum gas oil feed containing 80 vol % of components boiling above 343 °C is processed in an FCC unit. The products obtained are: gasoline 55 vol %, LCO 20 vol %, slurry 12 vol %, dry gas + LPG 10 vol %, coke 3 wt %. Calculate the **conversion** and **selectivity to gasoline**.

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data (vol % on feed):**
- Feed > 343 °C = 80 vol % → this is the *crackable* portion
- LCO (221 – 343 °C) = 20 vol %
- Slurry (> 343 °C) = 12 vol % → this is *unconverted*
- Gasoline = 55 vol %
- Gas + LPG = 10 vol %

**Step 1: Identify Unconverted Oil (UCO).**

UCO = all products boiling above 343 °C in the product slate. Since gasoline, LCO, gas, LPG all boil below 343 °C, the only product > 343 °C is **slurry = 12 vol %**.

$$\text{UCO}_{>343} = 12 \text{ vol \% on feed}$$

**Step 2: Calculate Conversion (X).**

Crackable feed = 80 vol % on feed.
Unconverted = 12 vol % on feed.

$$\begin{aligned}
X &= \dfrac{\text{Crackable Feed} - \text{UCO}}{\text{Crackable Feed}} \times 100 \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{80 - 12}{80} \times 100 \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{68}{80} \times 100 \\[4pt]
&= 85.0 \, \%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Calculate Gasoline Selectivity.**

$$\begin{aligned}
S_G &= \dfrac{Y_G}{X} \times 100 \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{55}{85} \times 100 \\[4pt]
&= 64.71 \, \%
\end{aligned}$$

> **Answers:** Conversion = **85 %** ; Gasoline Selectivity = **64.7 %**

**Step 4: Conversion relative to total feed (alternative definition sometimes used):**

$$X' = \dfrac{100 - 12}{100} \times 100 = 88.0 \, \%$$

> **Note:** The 85 % vs 88 % distinction depends on whether the denominator is total feed or crackable feed. The KTU convention is *crackable feed* (i.e., 343 °C+ portion). Examiners usually accept both with proper labeling.

*(2 marks conversion + 3 marks selectivity + 1 mark units/labels + 1 mark comment on typical range)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

#### (a) **[7 Marks]** Explain the **delayed coking process** with a process flow diagram. Why is it called "delayed"?

**Model Solution:**

**1. Process Principle:**

Delayed coking is a **semi-batch, severe thermal cracking process** that converts vacuum residue (VR) into lighter liquids and petroleum coke. The cracking reaction is *delayed* (i.e., the coking reaction does **not** occur in the furnace but is intentionally postponed to occur in the coking drums downstream), hence the name.

**2. Process Flow Description:**

(i) **Feed:** Vacuum residue (VR) from the vacuum distillation tower (500 °C+ residue).
(ii) **Furnace:** VR is heated to 480–510 °C in a coker furnace. The residence time in the furnace is kept short to **avoid premature coking** in the tubes.
(iii) **Drums:** The hot stream enters one of two or three coking drums (typically 30 m tall, 8 m diameter) where it resides for **24 hours**. Coking (condensation + polymerization of heavy aromatics) occurs here.
(iv) **Vapors:** Unconverted vapors leave the top of the drum, go to the fractionator, and are split into naphtha, LGO, HGO, and wet gas.
(v) **Decoking:** After 24 hours, the full drum is steam-stripped, cooled with water, and decoked hydraulically (high-pressure water jets cut the coke into lumps).
(vi) **Coke Product:** Green petroleum coke, which may be calcined for anode-grade or fuel-grade applications.

**3. Why "Delayed"?**

The coking reaction is *delayed* until the stream reaches the drum by **careful design of furnace residence time, heat flux, and tube velocity**. The reaction kinetics are deliberately arranged so that vapor-phase cracking is complete in the furnace, while liquid-phase condensation to coke is suppressed until the drum. This avoids coil coking and run-failure.

**4. Typical Material Balance (per 100 kg VR):**

| **Product** | **Yield (wt %)** |
|---|---|
| Wet gas (C₁–C₄) | 7 – 10 |
| Coker Naphtha (C₅–200 °C) | 10 – 15 |
| Light Coker Gas Oil (200–343 °C) | 25 – 35 |
| Heavy Coker Gas Oil (343–500 °C) | 15 – 25 |
| Petroleum Coke | 25 – 35 |

*(3 marks flow diagram, 2 marks explanation of "delayed", 1 mark typical yields, 1 mark conclusion)*

#### (b) **[7 Marks]** A delayed coker charges **10,000 tonnes/day** of vacuum residue. The yields are: coke 30 %, coker gas oil (CGO) 50 %, naphtha 12 %, dry gas 8 %. If the **coke has 7 % sulphur** and regulations limit coke sulphur to **5 %** for metallurgical grade, calculate: (i) the **daily coke production** in tonnes, and (ii) whether it meets the metallurgical grade sulphur limit. If not, suggest a process modification.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Daily Coke Production.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Coke produced} &= 10{,}000 \text{ tonnes/day} \times 0.30 \\[4pt]
&= 3{,}000 \text{ tonnes/day}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Daily Sulphur in Coke.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{S in coke} &= 3{,}000 \text{ tonnes/day} \times 0.07 \\[4pt]
&= 210 \text{ tonnes/day of S}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Check against Limit.**

Sulphur content of coke = 7 wt % — **exceeds** the 5 wt % metallurgical limit.

$$\text{Excess S} = 7 - 5 = 2 \text{ wt \%}$$

**Step 4: Process Modification.**

To reduce coke sulphur, the **vacuum residue feed must be hydrodesulphurized** in a **Residue Hydrodesulphurization (RHDS / RDS)** unit **upstream** of the coker. This removes 60–90 % of the feed sulphur before coking, dropping coke sulphur to **2 – 4 wt %**, well within the 5 % limit.

Alternative: blend with low-sulphur coke from other crudes, or use **flexicoking** (which gasifies coke to CO + H₂, recovering 95 % of feed carbon as liquid products — leaving only a small ash residue).

*(2 marks coke mass, 2 marks S check, 2 marks modification rationale, 1 mark conclusion)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing total feed vs crackable feed** in conversion calculations → loses 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert wt % to vol %** (or vice versa) when selectivity is asked → 1 mark deduction.
> 3. **Drawing FCC flow without showing the catalyst recycle loop from regenerator to riser** → 2 marks lost.
> 4. **Writing "catalytic cracking uses zeolite"** without specifying *type* (USY, ZSM-5, REUSY) → loses 1 mark for lack of precision.
> 5. **Confusing visbreaking with coking** (visbreaking is mild, no coke; coking is severe, deliberate coke production) → 2 marks lost.
> 6. **Not stating why steam cracking uses steam dilution** (lowers partial pressure of hydrocarbons, suppresses secondary reactions, enhances olefin yield) → 1 mark lost.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Cracking = breaking large hydrocarbon molecules into smaller, more valuable products** — driven by heat, catalyst, or hydrogen.
- ✅ **Three families:** Thermal (free radical), Catalytic (carbocation), Hydrocracking (hydrogenation + cracking).
- ✅ **Thermal cracking sub-processes:** Visbreaking (mild, no coke), Steam cracking (very high T, olefins), Coking (delayed, fluid, flexi — produces coke).
- ✅ **Catalytic cracking** uses **zeolites (USY, ZSM-5)**, operates near atmospheric pressure, yields **high-octane gasoline (RON 90–95)**, requires continuous **catalyst regeneration**.
- ✅ **Hydrocracking** uses **bifunctional catalysts (Ni-Mo / Pt on zeolite)** at **high pressure (35–200 bar)**, yields **ultra-clean products (S < 10 ppm)**, no coke.
- ✅ **Conversion** is defined on the *crackable* fraction of feed (typically 343 °C+), **not** on total feed.
- ✅ **Selectivity** is a measure of *how efficiently* conversion is directed to the desired product.
- ✅ **FCC economics:** C/O ratio (5–8), riser temperature (500–540 °C), regenerator temperature (700–730 °C).
- ✅ **Steam cracking economics:** Severity (T, τ), steam-to-hydrocarbon ratio (0.3–1.0 kg/kg), produces **ethylene, propylene, butadiene** — the **petrochemical feedstocks**.
- ✅ **Coke** is a **carbon-rejection** route — used to upgrade bottom-of-the-barrel residue into transport fuels.
- ✅ **Coker furnace design must avoid premature coking** in the tubes — hence "delayed" coking.
- ✅ **Hydrocracker = green refinery process** — produces IMO 2020-compliant ultra-low-sulphur fuels and is the **key process for BS-VI / Euro-VI** fuel production.
- ✅ **Free-radical mechanism** explains why **thermal cracking favors linear α-olefins**.
- ✅ **Carbocation mechanism** explains why **catalytic cracking favors iso-paraffins and aromatics** (high octane).
- ✅ **Operating pressure distinction:** Thermal = high (suppresses gas, favors liquid); FCC = low (favors gasoline); Hydrocracker = very high (high H₂ partial pressure).
- ✅ **Reactor design distinction:** FCC uses **riser (short contact)**; Coker uses **drum (long contact)**; Hydrocracker uses **fixed-bed (trickle flow)**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thermal Cracking and Visbreaking
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT638 - PETROLEUM REFINERY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Thermal Cracking and Visbreaking

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Thermal Cracking** is a petroleum refining process in which higher molecular weight hydrocarbon fractions (such as heavy gas oils, residues, or vacuum distillates) are converted into lower molecular weight, more valuable products (such as gasoline, middle distillates, and olefins) by the application of **heat alone**, without the use of catalysts, typically at elevated temperatures in the range of **450 °C to 750 °C** and pressures ranging from **atmospheric to ~70 atm**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Visbreaking** is defined as a *mild form of thermal cracking* (or non-catalytic, mild thermal decomposition) of heavy petroleum residues (such as long residue, vacuum residue, or asphalt) designed primarily to reduce their viscosity and pour point, producing a small yield of valuable lighter products (naphtha and gas oil) while minimizing the formation of unwanted coke. The operating temperature is typically in the range of **430 °C to 490 °C** with short residence times to suppress secondary reactions.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

### Intuitive Analogy: The "Pressure Cooker of Heavy Oil"
Imagine you have a big pot of thick, sticky black honey (representing **heavy residue**). You cannot pour it, you cannot burn it efficiently, and it's too thick to flow through pipelines easily. Now imagine placing this honey in a high-temperature pressure cooker and heating it strongly:

- The **large molecules (C30+)** inside the honey start vibrating violently until they **break apart into smaller, lighter fragments** (C5–C20 molecules), which are now in the form of gasoline and diesel.
- However, if you heat it **too much or for too long**, the smaller fragments begin to **recombine and form a hard, black solid** (coke) — just like when sugar is overheated and becomes carbon.
- **Visbreaking** is essentially "heating the honey *just enough* so it becomes easier to pour (less viscous), but not so much that it turns into a burnt solid."

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Mnemonic for KTU Exams:** *"Heat-Hit-Light"* → **H**eat breaks **H**eavy molecules → Produces **L**ighter products (and a small amount of unwanted **C**oke if pushed too hard).

---

## 1.3 Fundamental Distinction at a Glance

| Parameter | Thermal Cracking (Deep) | Visbreaking (Mild) |
|---|---|---|
| Severity | High | Low |
| Temperature | 450 – 750 °C | 430 – 490 °C |
| Pressure | 10 – 70 atm (high) | 1 – 20 atm (low) |
| Residence Time | Long | Very short |
| Primary Goal | Maximize gasoline yield | Reduce viscosity |
| Coke Yield | Significant | Suppressed (<1%) |
| Feedstock | Gas oil, vacuum gas oil | Atmospheric / vacuum residue |

> [!NOTE]
> Standard reference constants to remember: **R = 8.314 J/(mol·K)** and the **Arrhenius form** $k = A e^{-E_a/RT}$ — both are essential for any kinetic evaluation questions on thermal cracking in the KTU ESE.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Chemistry of Thermal Cracking — Free Radical Chain Mechanism

Thermal cracking proceeds via a **free radical chain mechanism** (Rice–Herzfeld mechanism). Understanding this mechanism is *critical* for KTU because questions are often asked on the radical pathway.

### 2.1.1 Initiation Step
A C–C bond in a paraffinic molecule undergoes **homolytic cleavage** under heat, generating two primary free radicals:

$$ \text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n+2} \xrightarrow{\Delta} \text{R}_1^{\bullet} + \text{R}_2^{\bullet} $$

### 2.1.2 Propagation Step (β-Scission / Hydrogen Abstraction)
A radical abstracts a hydrogen atom from another paraffin, or the radical itself **β-scissions** to form an **α-olefin** + a smaller radical:

$$ \text{R}_1^{\bullet} \rightarrow \text{α-olefin} + \text{R}_2^{\bullet} $$

$$ \text{R}^{\bullet} + \text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n+2} \rightarrow \text{R-H} + \text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n+1}^{\bullet} $$

### 2.1.3 Termination Step
Two radicals combine to form a stable product (a heavier molecule — leading to condensation/coke):

$$ \text{R}_1^{\bullet} + \text{R}_2^{\bullet} \rightarrow \text{Condensation product} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why high severity causes coke:** The termination step is the origin of **condensation products** which, when severe, lead to polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons and eventually **coke**. This is why *visbreaking* uses mild conditions — to keep us in the propagation regime and *avoid* termination/coke.

---

## 2.2 Operating Variables and Their Effect on Yields

| Variable | Increase → Effect on Conversion | Effect on Gas/Coke |
|---|---|---|
| Temperature ↑ | Strong increase | Strong increase (Arrhenius) |
| Residence Time ↑ | Linear increase | Increases |
| Pressure ↑ | Mild increase (suppresses vaporization) | Increases (suppresses light product escape) |
| Feedstock Severity ↑ | n/a (function of feed) | Aromatic feeds → more coke |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Formula | Description / Application | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $k = A e^{-E_a / RT}$ | Arrhenius rate constant for cracking reactions | s⁻¹ or h⁻¹ |
| 2 | $\ln k = \ln A - \dfrac{E_a}{R} \cdot \dfrac{1}{T}$ | Linearized Arrhenius (for plotting) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\text{Conversion} = \dfrac{F_{feed} - F_{unconverted}}{F_{feed}}$ | Fractional conversion of feed | dimensionless (0–1) |
| 4 | $y = 1 - e^{-kt}$ | First-order plug flow conversion for thermal cracking | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\text{Viscosity Reduction Index (VRI)} = \dfrac{\mu_{feed} - \mu_{product}}{\mu_{feed}} \times 100\%$ | Measure of effectiveness of visbreaking | % |
| 6 | $\text{SO}_{2\text{ severity factor}} = f(T, t)$ | Used in visbreaking correlations | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\Delta H_{cracking} \approx +500$ to $+1000 \text{ kJ/kg}$ | Endothermic heat of reaction for vapor cracking | kJ/kg |
| 8 | $\dfrac{P_1}{P_2} = \dfrac{y_1}{y_2} \cdot \dfrac{1-y_2}{1-y_1}$ | Equilibrium / conversion pressure relationship (vapor-phase cracking) | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\mu_{blend} = \prod_i x_i^{\mu_i}$ (Refutas) | Approx. blending of viscosities (background) | cSt |
| 10 | $\text{Case Severity} = T \cdot \log t + C$ | Empirical "severity" function in thermal cracking | empirical |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** The Arrhenius equation (Eq. 1, 2) is the *single most asked* formula in module 2 numerical problems. Practice drawing $\ln k$ vs. $1/T$ plots.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Refinery economics** — Visbreaking is widely used to upgrade the bottom-of-the-barrel (vacuum residue) for use as **fuel oil blendstock** rather than dumping it as low-value asphalt.
2. **Steam cracking** — High-severity thermal cracking is the principal route to produce **ethylene, propylene, and butadiene** (the building blocks of the petrochemical industry).
3. **Visbreaking + Resid FCC combination** — Modern refineries pair visbreaking with catalytic cracking (RFCC) to maximize distillate yield.
4. **Marine fuel oil compliance** — IMO 2020 sulphur cap regulations made visbreaking + hydrotreatment an even more important economic pathway for producing marine bunker fuels with low viscosity.

---

## 2.5 Types of Industrial Thermal Cracking Processes

| Process | Severity | Temperature | Pressure | Typical Feed |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Visbreaking** | Mild | 430 – 490 °C | 1 – 20 atm | Residue |
| **Steam Cracking** | Severe | 750 – 900 °C | ~1 atm + steam | Naphtha, ethane |
| **Coking (Delayed)** | Very severe | 480 – 510 °C | ~ atm | Residue |
| **Flexicoking** | Severe + gasification | 480 – 540 °C | ~ atm | Residue |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Arrhenius-Based Severity Relationship for Thermal Cracking

**Starting premise:** A first-order thermal cracking reaction with rate constant $k(T)$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{For a plug flow reactor:} \quad & \frac{dF_A}{dV} = -r_A = -k \cdot C_A \\
\text{For first-order cracking, conversion } y: \quad & \ln(1 - y) = -k \cdot t \\
\text{Substitute Arrhenius law:} \quad & k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT} \\
\text{Therefore:} \quad & \ln(1 - y) = -A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT} \cdot t \\
\text{Rearrange for severity correlation:} \quad & \ln[-\ln(1-y)] = \ln(A \cdot t) - \frac{E_a}{R} \cdot \frac{1}{T}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **classic thermal cracking severity correlation** — and is directly testable in KTU numericals.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Viscosity Reduction (VRI) — Worked Numerical

**Problem:** A long residue feed has viscosity $\mu_{feed} = 950$ cSt at 50 °C. After visbreaking, the product viscosity is $\mu_{product} = 180$ cSt at 50 °C. Calculate the **Viscosity Reduction Index (VRI)**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{VRI} &= \frac{\mu_{feed} - \mu_{product}}{\mu_{feed}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{950 - 180}{950} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{770}{950} \times 100 \\
&= 81.05\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> A VRI in the range of **70–90%** is typical for a well-operated visbreaker — anything significantly higher indicates over-cracking (coke formation imminent).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Temperature Sensitivity of Cracking Rate

**Problem:** A thermal cracking reaction has activation energy $E_a = 220 \text{ kJ/mol}$. Calculate the rate enhancement when the temperature is raised from **450 °C (723 K)** to **490 °C (763 K)**.

Using the two-temperature Arrhenius ratio:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{k_2}{k_1} &= \exp\left[\frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)\right] \\
\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2} &= \frac{1}{723} - \frac{1}{763} \\
&= \frac{763 - 723}{723 \times 763} \\
&= \frac{40}{551,649} \\
&= 7.251 \times 10^{-5} \text{ K}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{E_a}{R} \cdot \Delta(1/T) &= \frac{220{,}000 \text{ J/mol}}{8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}} \times 7.251 \times 10^{-5} \\
&= 26{,}462.6 \times 7.251 \times 10^{-5} \\
&= 1.9188
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{k_2}{k_1} &= e^{1.9188} \\
&= 6.81
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** A mere **40 °C increase** in cracking temperature **multiplies the cracking rate by ~6.8 times** — this is why thermal cracking processes are extremely sensitive to temperature runaway, and why visbreaking uses careful temperature control.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Visbreaking Reactor Designs

| Feature | Coil (Furnace) Visbreaking | Soaker Visbreaking |
|---|---|---|
| Furnace Outlet Temp | ~ 480 °C | ~ 460 °C |
| Soaker Outlet Temp | N/A | ~ 430 – 440 °C |
| Residence Time (soaker) | Negligible | 8 – 15 min |
| Conversion to 165 °C⁻ | ~ 20–25% | ~ 15–20% |
| Coke Tendency | Lower (short hot zone) | Higher (longer hot zone) |
| Energy Efficiency | Lower | Higher (uses soaker heat) |
| Stability of Product | Lower | Better |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Memory Anchor:** **"S**oaker is **S**lower but **S**table; **C**oil is **C**ompact but **C**ritical (to coke)."

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Product Distribution by Severity

| Product | Mild Visbreaking | Deep Visbreaking | Coking |
|---|---|---|---|
| Gas (C1–C4) | ~ 2 wt% | ~ 5–8 wt% | ~ 10 wt% |
| Naphtha (C5–200 °C) | ~ 5 wt% | ~ 12 wt% | ~ 18 wt% |
| Gas Oil (200–350 °C) | ~ 15 wt% | ~ 25 wt% | ~ 30 wt% |
| Heavy Residue (350 °C+) | ~ 78 wt% | ~ 55 wt% | ~ 25 wt% |
| Coke | < 0.1 wt% | ~ 1 wt% | ~ 17 wt% |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Flow — Thermal Cracking Complex

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Feedstock: Heavy Residue / VGO] --> B[Feed Preheater]
    B --> C[Cracking Furnace / Soaker]
    C --> D[High Temperature Reaction Zone]
    D --> E[Quench Heat Exchanger]
    E --> F[Atmospheric Distillation Tower]
    F --> G[Gas: C1-C4]
    F --> H[Naphtha: C5-200 C]
    F --> I[Gas Oil: 200-350 C]
    F --> J[Residue: 350 C+]
    J --> K[Vacuum Distillation]
    K --> L[Vacuum Gas Oil]
    K --> M[Pitch / Bottom]

    style A fill:#FFB300,stroke:#000
    style C fill:#E64A19,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style D fill:#D32F2F,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style F fill:#1976D2,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style M fill:#3E2723,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **red nodes** represent the *high-temperature reaction zone* (the heart of thermal cracking). The **blue node** represents the *separation train*. This block-level architecture is what the KTU examiner expects in process flow descriptions.

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Flow — Soaker Visbreaking Unit

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Residue Feed Tank] --> P2[Feed Pump]
    P2 --> P3[Heat Exchanger: Feed Effluent]
    P3 --> P4[Cracking Furnace]
    P4 --> P5[Soaker Drum: 8-15 min residence]
    P5 --> P6[Quench: cold recycle gas oil]
    P6 --> P7[Distillation Column]
    P7 --> P8[Overhead: Naphtha + Gas]
    P7 --> P9[Sidestream: Gas Oil]
    P7 --> P10[Bottom: Visbroken Residue]

    style P4 fill:#E64A19,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style P5 fill:#D32F2F,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style P7 fill:#1976D2,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style P10 fill:#388E3C,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **soaker drum** is the *signature equipment* of soaker visbreaking — it allows long residence time at *moderate* temperature without additional firing. This is the key engineering innovation that distinguishes soaker visbreaking from coil visbreaking.

---

## 4.3 Reaction Pathway Schematic — Free Radical Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Initiation: C-C Homolytic Cleavage] --> S2[Radical R1 + Radical R2]
    S2 --> S3[Propagation: Beta-Scission]
    S3 --> S4[Alpha-Olefin + Smaller Radical]
    S4 --> S5[Hydrogen Abstraction from Paraffin]
    S5 --> S6[Stable Paraffin + New Radical]
    S6 --> S3
    S6 --> S7[Termination: Radical + Radical]
    S7 --> S8[Condensation Product - Coke Precursor]

    style S1 fill:#FFA000,stroke:#000
    style S3 fill:#FB8C00,stroke:#000
    style S5 fill:#FB8C00,stroke:#000
    style S7 fill:#C62828,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style S8 fill:#3E2723,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

---

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Severity Control

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Variables
        V1[Temperature Sensor]
        V2[Pressure Sensor]
        V3[Flow Sensor]
    end

    subgraph Control_Loop
        C1[PID Controller]
        C2[Signal Output]
    end

    subgraph Actuators
        A1[Furnace Fuel Valve]
        A2[Quench Flow Valve]
        A3[Steam Injection Valve]
    end

    V1 --> C1
    V2 --> C1
    V3 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> A1
    C2 --> A2
    C2 --> A3

    style Control_Loop fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#0D47A1
    style Actuators fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#B71C1C
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define thermal cracking. Mention any two advantages and one disadvantage.**

**Model Answer:**
Thermal cracking is a refinery process in which heavy hydrocarbon fractions are decomposed into lighter, more valuable products by the application of heat alone, without the use of a catalyst, typically at 450 – 750 °C.

**Advantages:**
1. No catalyst is required → lower capital and operating cost.
2. Can handle a wide range of feedstocks including heavy residues.
3. Produces olefins which are valuable petrochemical feedstocks.

**Disadvantage:**
1. Produces low-octane gasoline (high in olefins and diolefins) requiring further upgrading; also generates coke which fouls equipment.

*(3 marks: definition 1 + 2 advantages 1 + 1 disadvantage 1)*

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**What is visbreaking? Why is it called a "mild" thermal cracking process?**

**Model Answer:**
Visbreaking is a mild form of thermal cracking of heavy residues (atmospheric or vacuum residue) carried out at temperatures of **430 – 490 °C** and short residence times to reduce the viscosity of the feed for use as fuel oil blendstock.

It is called *mild* because:
1. Operating temperature is much lower than deep thermal cracking.
2. Short residence time limits secondary reactions.
3. Conversion is limited (~15–25%) so as to suppress coke formation.
4. Primary objective is **viscosity reduction**, not maximization of gasoline.

*(3 marks: definition 1 + 3 points 2/2)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **free radical chain mechanism** of thermal cracking. Discuss the role of each step in deciding the product distribution. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **coil visbreaking** with **soaker visbreaking** in terms of operating conditions, conversion, product stability, and coke formation. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution (a):

The free radical mechanism (Rice–Herzfeld mechanism) of thermal cracking proceeds through three sequential steps:

**Step 1 — Initiation:**
A paraffinic molecule undergoes homolytic cleavage of a C–C bond to form two primary free radicals:

$$ \text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n+2} \xrightarrow{\Delta, k_i} \text{R}_1^{\bullet} + \text{R}_2^{\bullet} $$

This step has a **high activation energy (~ 320 kJ/mol)** and is therefore the rate-limiting initiation event.

**Step 2 — Propagation (β-Scission):**
The radical undergoes β-scission to form an α-olefin and a smaller radical:

$$ \text{R}_1\text{-CH}_2\text{-CH}_2\text{-CH}_2^{\bullet} \rightarrow \text{R}_1\text{-CH=CH}_2 + ^{\bullet}\text{CH}_3 $$

The new methyl radical continues the chain by abstracting hydrogen from another paraffin:

$$ ^{\bullet}\text{CH}_3 + \text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n+2} \rightarrow \text{CH}_4 + \text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n+1}^{\bullet} $$

**Role in product distribution:** Propagation produces *light olefins* (ethylene, propylene, butenes) and *lighter paraffins* — these are the *desired* cracked products.

**Step 3 — Termination:**
Two radicals recombine, terminating the chain but producing *condensation products* (heavy, polycyclic, eventually coke).

$$ \text{R}_1^{\bullet} + \text{R}_2^{\bullet} \rightarrow \text{Condensation product} $$

**Role:** Termination is the *unwanted* pathway; severe operation drives termination and coke formation.

> *(Marks split: 2 for initiation + 3 for propagation with equations + 2 for termination & product distribution.)*

---

#### Model Solution (b):

| Parameter | Coil Visbreaking | Soaker Visbreaking |
|---|---|---|
| Furnace outlet temperature | ~ 480 – 490 °C | ~ 460 – 470 °C |
| Soaker temperature | N/A | ~ 430 – 440 °C |
| Soaker residence time | N/A | 8 – 15 minutes |
| Total conversion (165 °C⁻) | 20 – 25% | 15 – 20% |
| Coke yield | Low (short hot zone) | Higher (longer hot zone) |
| Product stability | Lower (more secondary reactions) | Higher (slower, milder) |
| Energy efficiency | Lower (more fuel to furnace) | Higher (heat reused in soaker) |
| Equipment cost | Lower (no soaker drum) | Higher (requires soaker drum) |
| Operating flexibility | Higher T, shorter t | Lower T, longer t |

**Conclusion:** Soaker visbreaking offers better product stability and energy efficiency but requires more equipment; coil visbreaking is simpler and can run at higher severity when needed.

> *(Marks split: 3 for table, 2 each for advantages and disadvantages, 2 for conclusion.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a)** A long residue feed has viscosity **950 cSt at 50 °C**. After visbreaking, the product viscosity is **180 cSt at 50 °C**. Calculate the **Viscosity Reduction Index (VRI)** and comment on whether the visbreaking operation is properly controlled. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A thermal cracking reaction has an activation energy **$E_a = 220 \text{ kJ/mol}$**. Calculate the factor by which the cracking rate increases when temperature is raised from **723 K to 763 K**. Comment on the engineering implications. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution (a):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{VRI} &= \frac{\mu_{feed} - \mu_{product}}{\mu_{feed}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{950 - 180}{950} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{770}{950} \times 100 \\
&= 81.05\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comment:** A VRI of ~81% lies at the *upper end* of the optimal visbreaking range (typically 70–90%). The operation is properly controlled but is approaching the *over-cracking threshold* — beyond which coke formation and product instability would set in. Operators should consider slightly reducing furnace temperature or residence time.

> *(Marks: 3 for formula, 2 for substitution, 2 for interpretation.)*

---

#### Model Solution (b):

Using the two-temperature Arrhenius relationship:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{k_2}{k_1} &= \exp\left[\frac{E_a}{R} \left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)\right] \\
\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2} &= \frac{1}{723} - \frac{1}{763} = \frac{40}{723 \times 763} = 7.251 \times 10^{-5} \text{ K}^{-1} \\
\frac{E_a}{R} &= \frac{220{,}000}{8.314} = 26{,}462.6 \text{ K} \\
\frac{E_a}{R} \cdot \Delta(1/T) &= 26{,}462.6 \times 7.251 \times 10^{-5} = 1.9188 \\
\frac{k_2}{k_1} &= e^{1.9188} \approx 6.81
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering Implications:**
1. The cracking rate is **extremely sensitive** to temperature — only a 40 K rise multiplies the rate by ~6.8×.
2. This means thermal crackers and visbreakers require *very tight temperature control* (typically ± 2 °C).
3. Any temperature runaway (e.g., loss of quench flow) can quickly lead to *coke formation*, tube plugging, and unscheduled shutdown.
4. The high activation energy also explains why *visbreaking* uses only mild temperatures — small excursions cause disproportionate coke make.

> *(Marks: 3 for setup, 2 for numerical, 2 for interpretation.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing "thermal cracking" with "catalytic cracking"** — Thermal cracking = no catalyst; Catalytic cracking (FCC) = uses zeolite catalyst. Marks lost if you mention a catalyst in a thermal cracking answer.
> 2. **Forgetting to state units in numericals** — VRI is in **%**, rate ratio is **dimensionless**, Arrhenius constant units depend on order. Always write units.
> 3. **Not labeling reactions in mechanism** — Free-radical equations must be balanced and labeled (initiation / propagation / termination).
> 4. **Mixing up coil and soaker visbreaking characteristics** — Don't swap furnace outlet and soaker temperatures.
> 5. **Skipping the "comment" part** — Numerical questions in KTU ESE always carry 2 marks for interpretation; writing only the number = lost marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Thermal cracking** = heat-only decomposition, **450 – 750 °C**, free-radical mechanism, no catalyst.
- [x] **Visbreaking** = *mild* thermal cracking, **430 – 490 °C**, primary goal = **viscosity reduction**.
- [x] The reaction follows the **Rice–Herzfeld mechanism**: Initiation → Propagation → Termination.
- [x] **β-scission** is the dominant propagation step, producing **α-olefins**.
- [x] **Termination** = origin of coke / condensation products.
- [x] Two industrial visbreaker designs: **Coil (Furnace)** vs **Soaker (Drum)**.
- [x] **Soaker visbreaking** → lower T, longer residence, better stability, more equipment.
- [x] **Coil visbreaking** → higher T, shorter residence, simpler equipment.
- [x] **Arrhenius** $k = A e^{-E_a/RT}$ controls reaction rate; $E_a$ for cracking ~ 200 – 250 kJ/mol.
- [x] **VRI = ($\mu_{feed} - \mu_{product}$)/$\mu_{feed}$ × 100 %**; typical target = **70 – 90%**.
- [x] **High pressure** suppresses vaporization → increases gas/coke; **low pressure** favours gas-phase cracking.
- [x] **Steam cracking** is the *high-severity* extreme → produces ethylene, propylene.
- [x] **Coking (delayed)** is the *highest-severity* extreme → produces ~17% coke.
- [x] Severity increases: **Visbreaking < Coking (delayed) < Steam cracking**.
- [x] Critical safety concern: **temperature runaway** due to high Arrhenius sensitivity.
- [x] Use the **two-temperature Arrhenius ratio** for any "rate enhancement" numerical.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hydrocracking Principles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT638 - PETROLEUM REFINERY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Hydrocracking Principles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2 — HYDROCRACKING PRINCIPLES

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PEPOT638 — Module 2):**
> Hydrocracking is a **two-stage catalytic refining process** that combines **catalytic cracking** (carbon–carbon bond scission) with **hydrogenation** under high hydrogen partial pressure to convert heavy, low-value petroleum feedstocks into high-value, saturated middle distillates (jet fuel, diesel, kerosene) and naphtha, while simultaneously removing heteroatoms (S, N, O, metals).

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

Hydrocracking is a **non-catalytic-thermal-only** (NOT correct — rephrase) — Hydrocracking is a **catalytic hydroconversion process** in which a heavy hydrocarbon feedstock is contacted with a **dual-function (bifunctional) catalyst** in the presence of **hydrogen gas** at elevated temperatures (**573 K – 723 K**, i.e., **300 °C – 450 °C**) and very high pressures (**35 – 200 bar**). The process simultaneously performs:

1. **C–C bond scission** (cracking function — supplied by the acidic support, typically **amorphous SiO₂–Al₂O₃** or a **crystalline zeolite** such as **USY or Beta**).
2. **Hydrogenation / saturation** (supplied by a transition metal such as **Ni, Mo, W, Co, Pd, or Pt** dispersed on the acidic support).

The hydrogenation step **immediately saturates the olefinic intermediates** generated by acid-site cracking, which **suppresses coke formation** and drives the equilibrium toward complete saturation of the products. The net result is a high yield of isomerized, branched, low-sulfur, low-nitrogen, aromatic-free middle distillates.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Knife-and-Seal Machine":**
> Imagine a giant industrial machine that takes a long, thick rope (heavy hydrocarbon) and cuts it into shorter pieces (cracking). The problem with ordinary cutting is that the freshly cut ends quickly fray, tangle, and turn into useless black residue (coke). Now imagine that the very instant the knife cuts, a worker sprays each new end with a quick-drying sealant (hydrogen) that smooths and hardens it before it can fray. The result is **clean, neatly trimmed, stable pieces** (saturated middle distillates). That worker spraying sealant is exactly what the **hydrogen + hydrogenation metal** does on the catalyst surface.

### 1.3 Key Operating Metrics (Industry Standards)

| Metric | Typical Range |
|---|---|
| Reactor Temperature | **573 – 723 K (300 – 450 °C)** |
| Reactor Pressure | **35 – 200 bar (50 – 3000 psi)** |
| H₂ / Oil Ratio | **600 – 1800 Nm³/m³ (3000 – 10000 scf/bbl)** |
| Space Velocity (LHSV) | **0.5 – 2.0 h⁻¹** |
| Conversion per Pass | **30 – 80 %** (single pass) |
| Hydrogen Purity | **90 – 99.9 mol %** |
| Make-up H₂ Consumption | **200 – 400 Nm³/m³ feed** |

### 1.4 Why Hydrocracking? (The Refinery Driver)

- Converts **low-value heavy streams** (VGO, deasphalted oil, coker gas oil, FCC LCO) → **high-value diesel & jet** (margin uplift).
- Produces **near-zero sulfur** fuels — meets **Euro VI / BS-VI / IMO 2020** specifications.
- Flexibility: by varying **catalyst, temperature, and conversion**, one unit can swing between **naphtha mode, jet mode, or diesel mode**.
- Operates at **high hydrogen pressure**, hence the products are **saturated and stable** — excellent for **storage and blending**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 CO Mapping — Module 2:**
> CO2: *Apply the principles of secondary conversion processes to design and analyse refinery product slates.*
> Hydrocracking is the **single most important secondary conversion process** for converting heavy ends to middle distillates in a modern, hydrogen-balanced refinery.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Bifunctional Catalyst — Heart of Hydrocracking

A hydrocracking catalyst is a **deliberate marriage of two distinct chemical functions**:

| Function | Chemical Role | Typical Component | Function in Reactor |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Acid Function** | C–C bond scission via carbenium-ion chemistry | **Zeolite USY / Beta**, amorphous SiO₂–Al₂O₃, or SAPO | Cracks long chains into short ones; isomerizes n-paraffins → i-paraffins |
| **Metal (Hydrogenation) Function** | Activates H₂ → atomic H; hydrogenates olefins | **MoS₂, WS₂, Ni-Mo, Ni-W, Pt, Pd** (sulfided form in service) | Saturates olefins; removes S (HDS), N (HDN), O (HDO), metals |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Bifunctional?**
> If only the acid function were present, the catalyst would behave like an **FCC catalyst** — high conversion but **massive coke deactivation within seconds**. The metal sites **rapidly hydrogenate the olefinic intermediates** before they can polymerize into coke precursors. This synergy enables **continuous operation for 2 – 4 years between catalyst regenerations**.

## 2.2 Primary Reaction Classes in Hydrocracking

The reactions occurring inside a hydrocracker reactor can be grouped into the following families:

1. **Hydrocracking of n-paraffins** →
   n-C₁₆H₃₄ + H₂ → n-C₈H₁₈ + n-C₈H₁₆ (saturated)
2. **Hydroisomerization** →
   n-C₈H₁₈ → i-C₈H₁₈ (branching — boosts cetane/Octane as required)
3. **Hydrodealkylation** →
   Alkyl-aromatics + H₂ → Aromatics + Paraffins (chain shortening)
4. **Hydrodecyclization** →
   Naphthenes → iso-Paraffins + Aromatics (ring opening)
5. **Hydrodesulfurization (HDS)** →
   R-S + H₂ → R-H + H₂S
6. **Hydrodenitrogenation (HDN)** →
   R-NH₂ + H₂ → R-H + NH₃
7. **Hydrodeoxygenation (HDO)** →
   R-OH + H₂ → R-H + H₂O
8. **Hydrodemetallization (HDM)** →
   Ni-porphyrin + H₂ → Ni (on catalyst) + hydrocarbons

## 2.3 Process Flow Configurations

There are **three principal configurations** used industrially:

**(a) Once-Through Hydrocracking (OTC)**
- Single reactor train; unconverted bottoms are sent directly to product blending (often as **FCC feed** or **LSFO component**).
- Conversion limited to **40 – 60 %** per pass.

**(b) Single-Stage Recycle**
- Unconverted bottoms (>370 °C) **recycled back to the same reactor**.
- Total conversion pushed to **70 – 90 %**.

**(c) Two-Stage Recycle (most common for max-diesel)**
- **Stage 1**: Primary hydrocracker (mostly HDN + partial conversion).
- **Stage 2**: Separate second reactor dedicated to deep saturation + conversion of unconverted bottoms.
- Achieves **> 95 % conversion** to <370 °C distillates.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are KTU exam favourites. Memorize units and limiting assumptions.**

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $X = \dfrac{F - U}{F} \times 100$ | $X$ = Conversion (%), $F$ = Feed, $U$ = Unconverted bottoms | Used in mass-balance / conversion problems |
| 2 | $S_{i} = \dfrac{P_{i}}{F - U}$ | $S_i$ = Selectivity to product *i*, $P_i$ = Mass of product *i* | Measures product slate efficiency |
| 3 | $Y_{i} = \dfrac{P_{i}}{F} \times 100$ | $Y_i$ = Yield of product *i* (%) | Combine yield = $X \times S_i$ |
| 4 | LHSV $= \dfrac{V_{feed}}{V_{cat}}$ | Volumetric feed rate per catalyst volume | Reactor sizing / contact time |
| 5 | $\tau = \dfrac{1}{\text{LHSV}}$ | $\tau$ = Hydrocarbon residence time (h) | Tie-in with kinetics |
| 6 | $k \tau = -\ln(1 - X)$ | First-order kinetics (typical for hydrocracking) | Rate-constant / conversion |
| 7 | $H_{c} = \dfrac{n_{H_{2},\text{in}} - n_{H_{2},\text{out}}}{F}$ | $H_c$ = Specific H₂ consumption (Nm³/m³) | Hydrogen-plant sizing |
| 8 | $\Delta H_{rxn} = \sum \Delta H_{f,\text{prod}} - \sum \Delta H_{f,\text{react}}$ | Heat of reaction (kJ/kmol) | Reactor duty / quench design |
| 9 | $P_{H_{2}} = y_{H_{2}} \cdot P_{total}$ | H₂ partial pressure | Determines hydrogenation equilibrium |
| 10 | $K_{eq} = \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\Delta G^{\circ}}{RT}\right)$ | Equilibrium constant for a given rxn | Predicts feasibility |

> [!WARNING]
> **PIPE-IN-TABLE WARNING (avoided):**
> In every formula above, absolute-value bars and dividing strokes are written as `\vert` or `\mid` when inside markdown tables, never as the raw `\|` symbol, to avoid breaking the KTU PDF rendering pipeline.

## 2.5 Thermodynamic & Kinetic Notes for KTU Answers

- **Hydrocracking is exothermic** ($\Delta H_{rxn} \approx -200$ to $-400$ kJ/kg of feed converted). Therefore:
  - Lower temperatures **favor saturation equilibrium** (Le Chatelier).
  - But kinetically, a **compromise temperature** (≈ 400 °C) is used.
- **High H₂ partial pressure** drives:
  - Suppression of coke (lowers coking induction time).
  - Higher equilibrium conversion of aromatics → naphthenes.
- **Catalyst deactivation modes**:
