  1. **Coking** (reversible — burned off in regeneration).
  2. **Metal deposition** (Ni, V from porphyrins — irreversible pore plugging).
  3. **Sintering** of metal sites at T > 450 °C.
  4. **Nitrogen poisoning** — basic N-compounds neutralize acid sites.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — First-Order Hydrocracking Kinetic Equation

The cracking of a hydrocarbon feed is often modeled as a **pseudo-first-order reaction in the liquid hydrocarbon concentration** because hydrogen is in great molar excess.

### Step-by-step derivation:

Let $C$ = concentration of unconverted heavy feedstock at reactor position $z$, and $C_0$ = concentration at inlet.

$$
\frac{dC}{d\tau} = -k \, C
$$

Separating variables:

$$
\int_{C_0}^{C} \frac{dC}{C} = -k \int_{0}^{\tau} d\tau
$$

Integrating:

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{C}{C_0}\right) = -k \, \tau
$$

Exponentiating:

$$
\frac{C}{C_0} = e^{-k\tau}
$$

By definition, fractional conversion is:

$$
X = \frac{C_0 - C}{C_0} = 1 - \frac{C}{C_0}
$$

Substituting:

$$
X = 1 - e^{-k\tau}
$$

Solving for $k\tau$:

$$
k\tau = -\ln(1 - X)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A plot of $-\ln(1-X)$ versus reciprocal LHSV (which is $\tau$) should give a **straight line through the origin** with slope $k$. This is the standard KTU derivation for hydrocracker design.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Conversion and Yield Relationship

Define:
- $F$ = mass flow rate of feed (kg/h)
- $U$ = mass flow rate of unconverted bottoms (kg/h)
- $P_i$ = mass flow rate of product *i* (kg/h)

**Step 1.** Mass balance:

$$
F = U + \sum_{i} P_i
$$

**Step 2.** Conversion:

$$
X = \frac{F - U}{F}
$$

**Step 3.** Selectivity to product *i*:

$$
S_i = \frac{P_i}{F - U}
$$

**Step 4.** Yield of product *i*:

$$
Y_i = \frac{P_i}{F} = \frac{(F - U) S_i}{F} = X \, S_i
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fundamental Identity (memorize for KTU):**
> $$\boxed{Y_i = X \cdot S_i}$$
> This single equation is the **most-tested relation** in Module-2 mass-balance problems.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Hydrogen Consumption Calculation

Hydrogen consumed by the reactor is consumed by four reactions: **HDS, HDN, HDO, and hydrocracking saturation (HC)**.

$$
H_c = H_{HDS} + H_{HDN} + H_{HDO} + H_{HC}
$$

For each heteroatom removal, the stoichiometry is straightforward:

**HDS (per kg S removed):**
$$
\text{R-S} + \text{H}_2 \rightarrow \text{R-H} + \text{H}_2\text{S}
$$

Hydrogen consumed = $1$ mol H₂ per mol S removed.

$$
H_{HDS} = \frac{w_S}{32} \times 22.4 \quad \text{Nm}^3/\text{m}^3 \text{ feed (simplified)}
$$

where $w_S$ = wt% sulfur in feed.

**Numerical Example (KTU-style problem):**
A hydrocracker feed contains **2.5 wt% S**, **0.18 wt% N**, **0.05 wt% O** and achieves **80 % per-pass conversion**. The specific hydrogen consumption is to be estimated.

Assume: 1000 kg feed.
- S removed (90 % efficiency) = $2.5 \times 0.9 = 2.25$ kg S
- N removed (50 % efficiency) = $0.18 \times 0.5 = 0.09$ kg N
- O removed (60 % efficiency) = $0.05 \times 0.6 = 0.03$ kg O
- Hydrocarbon saturation ≈ **1.0 wt% of feed** for an 80 % conversion hydrocracker

H₂ moles consumed (S removal):

$$
n_{H_2,S} = \frac{2250}{32} = 70.31 \text{ mol}
$$

H₂ moles consumed (N removal):

$$
n_{H_2,N} = \frac{90}{14} \times \tfrac{3}{2} = 9.64 \text{ mol} \quad (\text{because HDN consumes 1.5 mol H}_2\text{/mol N})
$$

H₂ moles consumed (O removal):

$$
n_{H_2,O} = \frac{30}{16} = 1.88 \text{ mol}
$$

H₂ consumed for saturation:

$$
n_{H_2,HC} = \frac{10{,}000}{2} = 5000 \text{ mol}
$$

Total H₂ consumed:

$$
n_{H_2,\text{total}} = 70.31 + 9.64 + 1.88 + 5000 = 5081.83 \text{ mol}
$$

At **STP (22.414 Nm³/kmol)**:

$$
V_{H_2} = 5081.83 \times 22.414 \times 10^{-3} = 113.9 \text{ Nm}^3 \text{ per } 1000 \text{ kg feed}
$$

Per m³ feed (assuming feed density 900 kg/m³):

$$
H_c = \frac{113.9}{1.111} \approx 102.5 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{m}^3
$$

This is **lower than typical industrial values (~200–400 Nm³/m³)** because we excluded dissolved/recycle H₂ losses and chemical consumption for olefin/aromatic saturation in the heavy fraction. Industrial estimates must include these.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Adiabatic Temperature Rise Across Reactor

Because hydrocracking is exothermic, temperature rises as reactants flow through the catalyst bed. To prevent runaway, **inter-bed quench** with cold H₂ is used.

Energy balance on reactor (adiabatic, single bed):

$$
\sum F_{i,in} h_{i,in} = \sum F_{i,out} h_{i,out}
$$

If $\Delta T$ is the temperature rise:

$$
\Delta T = \frac{-\Delta H_{rxn} \cdot X}{C_{p,\text{avg}}}
$$

**Typical values:**
- $\Delta H_{rxn} \approx -250$ kJ/kg converted
- $C_{p,\text{avg}} \approx 2.5$ kJ/(kg·K)
- For $X = 0.5$ (50 % conversion): $\Delta T = (250 \times 0.5)/2.5 = 50$ °C

This is why **industrial hydrocrackers limit per-pass conversion to 50–60 %** — to keep the inter-bed quench within manageable limits.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Severity vs Product Slate

| Severity | Temperature | Conversion | Dominant Product | Cetane Number |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Low** | 350 °C | 40 % | Diesel (saturated) | **> 55** (excellent) |
| **Medium** | 400 °C | 70 % | Kero-Jet + Diesel | 45 – 55 |
| **High** | 430 °C | 90 % | Naphtha (isomerized) | 30 – 40 (feed for isomerization/reforming) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hydrocracker Process Flow — Block Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional flow** of a **two-stage recycle hydrocracker**, showing every major block, every gas/liquid recycle stream, and every separation point — exactly as it would appear in a KTU 14-mark answer sketch.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FEEDSTOCK_PREP
        rawFeed[Heavy VGO Feed] --> feedPump[Charge Pump]
        feedPump --> feedHtr[Feed Heater]
    end

    subgraph STAGE1_REACTOR_TRAIN
        feedHtr --> mixTee1[Mix Tee with Recycle H2]
        mixTee1 --> reactor1[Trickle Bed Reactor R1]
        reactor1 --> hotSep[Hot High Pressure Separator]
        hotSep --> coolSep[Cold High Pressure Separator]
        coolSep --> recycleComp[Recycle Gas Compressor]
        recycleComp --> mixTee1
    end

    subgraph STAGE1_DISTILLATION
        hotSep --> fracCol[Product Fractionator]
        fracCol --> naphthaSt[Naphtha 30 to 90 C]
        fracCol --> keroSt[Kerosene 150 to 240 C]
        fracCol --> dieselSt[Diesel 240 to 370 C]
        fracCol --> unconvBottoms[Unconverted Bottoms above 370 C]
    end

    subgraph STAGE2_REACTOR_TRAIN
        unconvBottoms --> mixTee2[Mix Tee with Makeup H2]
        mixTee2 --> reactor2[Second Stage Reactor R2]
        reactor2 --> stage2Sep[Stage 2 Separator]
        stage2Sep --> recycle2[Recycle to R2]
        stage2Sep --> productBlend[Final Product Blending]
    end

    subgraph SUPPORT_UTILS
        makeupH2[Hydrogen Plant Makeup H2] --> mixTee2
        makeupH2 --> mixTee1
        amineT[Amine Treating for H2S Removal] --> recycleComp
    end

    feedPump -.-> |increase pressure to 150 bar| mixTee1
```

**Reading the diagram (KTU-style explanation):**
1. Feed and recycled H₂ are mixed, heated, and enter Reactor **R1** as a **trickle flow** (gas down, liquid down).
2. The reacted effluent passes through **hot** and **cold separators** to recover **recycle hydrogen gas** (which is scrubbed of H₂S in the **amine treater** before compression).
3. The liquid from the cold separator enters the **fractionator** that splits the products into **naphtha, kerosene, diesel, and unconverted bottoms (>370 °C)**.
4. Unconverted bottoms are sent to **Reactor R2** along with **fresh makeup H₂** for deep conversion.
5. The Stage-2 separator sends its heavy recycle back to R2 (total recycle scheme) and its distillate streams to **product blending**.

## 4.2 Bifunctional Catalyst — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    pellet[Hydrocracker Catalyst Pellet - cylindrical extrudate] --> outerShell[Outer macroporous shell - gamma alumina binder]
    outerShell --> mesoPore[Mesopore network 4 to 30 nm]
    outerShell --> zeoliteSite[Zeolite Y or USY crystals - acid function]
    zeoliteSite --> bronstedSite[Bronsted acid sites - Si OH Al]
    zeoliteSite --> lewisSite[Lewis acid sites - extra framework Al]
    outerShell --> metalSite[Metal sulfide nanoparticles MoS2 or NiWS2]
    metalSite --> edgeSite[Edge and corner sites of MoS2 crystallites - hydrogen activation]
    metalSite --> promoterSite[Promoter atoms Ni or Co at MoS2 edges]
    pellet --> poreMouth[Pore mouth controls diffusion of heavy molecules]

    classDef acid fill:#e6f3ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef metal fill:#fff2e6,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef support fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class bronstedSite,lewisSite acid
    class edgeSite,promoterSite metal
    class zeoliteSite,outerShell,mesoPore support
```

## 4.3 Hydrocracking Reaction Network — Functional Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nParaffin[Normal Paraffin C16] --> acidCrack[Acid Site C C Scission]
    iParaffin[Isoparaffin] --> acidCrack
    naphthene[Naphthene Ring] --> ringOpen[Ring Opening on Acid Site]
    aromatic[Aromatic Ring] --> metalSat[Metal Site Hydrogenation]
    metalSat --> naphthene2[Partially Saturated Naphthene]
    naphthene2 --> ringOpen
    acidCrack --> olefin[Olefin Intermediate]
    olefin --> metalSat2[Metal Site Saturation]
    metalSat2 --> iParaffinOut[Saturated Branched Product]
    ringOpen --> iParaffinOut

    feedS[Sulfur Compounds] --> HDS[HDS Reaction]
    feedN[Nitrogen Compounds] --> HDN[HDN Reaction]
    feedO[Oxygen Compounds] --> HDO[HDO Reaction]

    HDS --> h2sOut[H2S Removed]
    HDN --> nh3Out[NH3 Removed]
    HDO --> h2oOut[H2O Removed]

    classDef rxn fill:#fff8dc,stroke:#8b7500,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#d4f1d4,stroke:#228b22,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class acidCrack,ringOpen,metalSat,metalSat2,HDS,HDN,HDO rxn
    class h2sOut,nh3Out,h2oOut,iParaffinOut out
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**What is hydrocracking? Why is hydrogen required in the process?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Hydrocracking is a catalytic refining process in which heavy hydrocarbon feedstocks such as vacuum gas oil (VGO) and deasphalted oil are converted into lighter, saturated middle distillates (diesel, jet fuel, kerosene) by **simultaneous catalytic cracking and hydrogenation** at high pressure (**35–200 bar**) and temperature (**300–450 °C**).

Hydrogen is required to:
1. **Saturate olefinic intermediates** formed during acid-site cracking, thereby **suppressing coke formation** and prolonging catalyst life.
2. **Remove heteroatoms** (S as H₂S, N as NH₃, O as H₂O) through HDS, HDN, and HDO reactions.
3. **Maintain catalyst activity** by shifting the equilibrium towards saturated products.

*(1 mark definition + 1 mark operating conditions + 1 mark three roles of H₂.)*

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**List the two main functions of a hydrocracking catalyst and give one example material for each function.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A hydrocracking catalyst is **bifunctional**:
1. **Acid function** — provides C–C bond scission via carbenium-ion chemistry. *Example: crystalline zeolite USY or amorphous SiO₂–Al₂O₃.* (1.5 marks)
2. **Hydrogenation (metal) function** — activates molecular H₂ and saturates olefins/aromatics. *Example: sulfided Ni-Mo, Ni-W, or Pt-Pd on the acidic support.* (1.5 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, describe the **two-stage recycle hydrocracking process**. Explain the role of the **hot high-pressure separator**, the **cold high-pressure separator**, and the **amine treater**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A hydrocracker is fed **10,000 kg/h** of VGO containing **2.2 wt% S, 0.15 wt% N, 0.04 wt% O**. The unit achieves **88 % per-pass conversion**, with heteroatom removal efficiencies of **92 % S, 55 % N, 65 % O**. The hydrocarbon-saturation hydrogen demand equals **1.4 wt% of feed**. Estimate the **specific hydrogen consumption** in **Nm³ per m³ of feed** (feed density = **880 kg/m³**).

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Block Diagram (2 marks):**
Draw (or describe) the standard two-stage recycle flow: **Feed → Heater → R1 → Hot Separator → Cold Separator → Recycle Compressor → Amine Treater → back to R1;** **Cold-Sep liquid → Fractionator → Naphtha / Kero / Diesel / Unconverted Bottoms;** **Bottoms + Makeup H₂ → R2 → Stage-2 Separator → final blending.**

**Hot High-Pressure Separator (HHPS) (1.5 marks):**
Operates at **reactor temperature (~400 °C) and reactor pressure (~150 bar)**. Its function is to separate the **bulk of the recycle hydrogen gas** from the **unconverted hydrocarbon liquid** while most of the H₂ is still dissolved in the liquid. This minimizes the volume of liquid going to the cold separator.

**Cold High-Pressure Separator (CHPS) (1.5 marks):**
Operates at **~50 °C, same high pressure**. At this temperature, almost all remaining dissolved H₂ flashes out. The separated gas joins the HHPS overhead gas as the **recycle hydrogen stream**. The liquid leaves for the fractionator.

**Amine Treater (2 marks):**
Recycle H₂ contains **H₂S** from the HDS reaction and **NH₃** from the HDN reaction. Both are **catalyst poisons** (NH₃ neutralizes acid sites; H₂S can redistribute metal sites). The amine treater selectively absorbs **H₂S** using **alkanolamine (MEA, DEA, or MDEA)**, returning **ultra-clean H₂ (> 95 mol %)** back to the reactor.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:**
- Feed rate $F = 10{,}000$ kg/h
- Density $\rho = 880$ kg/m³ → Volumetric feed = $10{,}000/880 = 11.36$ m³/h
- Sulfur 2.2 wt%, removed 92 % → S removed = $2.2 \times 0.92 = 2.024$ wt% = **202.4 kg/h**
- Nitrogen 0.15 wt%, removed 55 % → N removed = $0.15 \times 0.55 = 0.0825$ wt% = **8.25 kg/h**
- Oxygen 0.04 wt%, removed 65 % → O removed = $0.04 \times 0.65 = 0.026$ wt% = **2.6 kg/h**
- Hydrocarbon saturation = 1.4 wt% of feed → **140 kg/h of H₂ equivalent consumption**

**Stoichiometric hydrogen demands:**

For **HDS**: 1 mol H₂ per mol S

$$
n_{H_2,S} = \frac{202.4}{32} \times 1000 = 6325 \text{ mol/h}
$$

For **HDN**: 1.5 mol H₂ per mol N (NH₃ carries 1.5 H₂)

$$
n_{H_2,N} = \frac{8.25}{14} \times 1000 \times 1.5 = 884 \text{ mol/h}
$$

For **HDO**: 1 mol H₂ per mol O

$$
n_{H_2,O} = \frac{2.6}{16} \times 1000 = 162.5 \text{ mol/h}
$$

For **HC saturation**: 140 kg/h H₂

$$
n_{H_2,HC} = \frac{140{,}000}{2} = 70{,}000 \text{ mol/h}
$$

**Total H₂ consumed:**

$$
n_{H_2,\text{total}} = 6325 + 884 + 162.5 + 70{,}000 = 77{,}371.5 \text{ mol/h}
$$

**At STP (22.414 Nm³/kmol):**

$$
V_{H_2} = 77{,}371.5 \times 22.414 \times 10^{-3} = 1734.2 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{h}
$$

**Specific hydrogen consumption:**

$$
H_c = \frac{1734.2}{11.36} = \boxed{152.7 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{m}^3 \text{ feed}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the 1.5 multiplier for HDN** is the #1 error — students lose **1 full mark**.
> 2. **Not converting kg/h → mol/h via atomic mass (S = 32, N = 14, O = 16, H₂ = 2)** loses another mark.
> 3. **Forgetting to divide by volumetric feed flow** at the end will leave the answer in Nm³/h instead of Nm³/m³ — another mark lost.
> 4. Always state **assumptions** (1.5 H₂/N, 1 H₂/S, 1 H₂/O) at the start of the solution.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **bifunctional mechanism** of hydrocracking. Describe how the **acid site** and the **metal site** cooperate to give a saturated, isomerized product. Use a **schematic reaction pathway** for the conversion of **n-hexadecane (n-C₁₆H₃₄)** to a mixture of **iso-octane (i-C₈H₁₈)** products.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A first-order hydrocracking reactor operates with LHSV = **1.2 h⁻¹** and achieves **65 % conversion**. If the same feed is processed in a reactor with the same catalyst mass but **double the volumetric feed rate**, calculate the **new conversion**. Assume first-order kinetics and **constant hydrogen partial pressure**.

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Bifunctional mechanism (3 marks):**
1. **Step 1 — Dehydrogenation on metal site:**
   n-C₁₆H₃₄ → n-C₁₆H₃₂ (olefin) + H₂. The metal (Pt, Pd, or sulfided Ni-Mo) **activates H₂ and abstractively dehydrogenates** the paraffin.
2. **Step 2 — Protonation on acid site:**
   The olefin is **protonated by a Bronsted acid site** of the zeolite to form a **carbenium ion**, e.g., C₁₆H₃₃⁺.
3. **Step 3 — Isomerization + β-scission on acid site:**
   The carbenium ion undergoes **methyl shift** to form a branched isomer, then **β-scission** to yield two shorter carbenium ions (e.g., C₈H₁₇⁺ + C₈H₁₆).
4. **Step 4 — Hydrogenation on metal site:**
   The short carbenium ion is **deprotonated → olefin → saturated by metal site** with atomic H to give i-C₈H₁₈.

**Schematic Pathway (2 marks):**

$$
\text{n-C}_{16}\text{H}_{34} \xrightarrow{\text{Metal}} \text{n-C}_{16}\text{H}_{32} \xrightarrow{\text{Acid, H}^+} \text{C}_{16}\text{H}_{33}^+ \xrightarrow{\text{Isomerize}} \text{i-C}_{16}\text{H}_{33}^+ \xrightarrow{\beta\text{-scission}} \text{i-C}_{8}\text{H}_{17}^+ + \text{C}_{8}\text{H}_{16}
$$

$$
\text{i-C}_{8}\text{H}_{17}^+ \xrightarrow{\text{Deprotonation}} \text{i-C}_{8}\text{H}_{16} \xrightarrow{\text{Metal, H}} \text{i-C}_{8}\text{H}_{18}
$$

**Why cooperation matters (2 marks):**
Without the metal step, the olefin polymerizes → coke. Without the acid step, no chain scission occurs. The **synergy yields isomerized, saturated, stable products** — exactly the specification for modern diesel and jet fuel.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given (first run):** LHSV₁ = 1.2 h⁻¹, $X_1$ = 0.65
**Second run:** Same catalyst mass, double volumetric feed rate → LHSV₂ = 2 × 1.2 = 2.4 h⁻¹

**First-order kinetics (2 marks):**

$$
k\tau_1 = -\ln(1 - X_1)
$$

Since $\tau_1 = 1/\text{LHSV}_1 = 1/1.2 = 0.8333$ h:

$$
k(0.8333) = -\ln(1 - 0.65) = -\ln(0.35) = 1.0498
$$

$$
k = \frac{1.0498}{0.8333} = 1.2598 \text{ h}^{-1}
$$

**New conversion (3 marks):**

New residence time $\tau_2 = 1/\text{LHSV}_2 = 1/2.4 = 0.4167$ h:

$$
k\tau_2 = 1.2598 \times 0.4167 = 0.5250
$$

$$
X_2 = 1 - e^{-0.5250} = 1 - 0.5916 = 0.4084 \approx \boxed{40.8 \%}
$$

**Conclusion (2 marks):** Doubling the feed rate **halves the contact time**, which — because of the logarithmic relationship — reduces conversion only modestly (from 65 % → 41 %), not linearly. This illustrates the inherent **robustness of first-order processes against throughput variation**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> 1. Students often write "$X_2 = X_1 / 2 = 32.5\%$" — this is **wrong** because the relationship is logarithmic, not linear. This will cost **2 marks**.
> 2. Failing to compute $k$ explicitly from the first condition costs another mark.
> 3. Skipping the final conversion formula application loses **1 mark**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hydrocracking — One-Page Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- **Definition:** Catalytic cracking + hydrogenation at **35–200 bar** and **300–450 °C** to make saturated middle distillates.
- **Feedstocks:** VGO, deasphalted oil, coker gas oil, FCC LCO.
- **Products:** Naphtha, kerosene, jet (ATF), diesel, with **< 10 ppm S** and **high cetane**.
- **Catalyst — Bifunctional:**
  - Acid site → zeolite USY / Beta / amorphous SiO₂-Al₂O₃ (cracking).
  - Metal site → sulfided Ni-Mo / Ni-W / Pt-Pd (hydrogenation).
- **Reactor types:** Trickle-bed fixed bed (most common), ebullated-bed (for heavy metal-laden feeds), slurry reactor.
- **Configurations:** Once-through, single-stage recycle, two-stage recycle.
- **Reactions:** HDS, HDN, HDO, HDM, hydrocracking, hydroisomerization, hydrodealkylation, ring-opening.
- **Key Formulas (must-memorize):**
  - $X = (F - U)/F$
  - $Y_i = X \cdot S_i$
  - $X = 1 - e^{-k\tau}$
  - $k\tau = -\ln(1 - X)$
  - $H_c$ via stoichiometric HDS, HDN, HDO (+ HC saturation).
  - $\Delta T = -\Delta H_{rxn} X / C_p$.
- **Stoichiometric H₂ demand:** 1 mol H₂ per S, 1.5 mol H₂ per N, 1 mol H₂ per O atom removed.
- **Hydrogen consumption (industrial):** **200 – 400 Nm³/m³** feed.
- **Thermodynamics:** **Exothermic**; lower T favored for saturation but kinetics need **~400 °C**.
- **Severity knob:** T ↑ → more cracking → more naphtha, less diesel.
- **Deactivation:** Coking (regenerable), metal deposition (irreversible), N-poisoning, sintering.
- **Hydrogen partial pressure $P_{H_2} = y_{H_2} P$** controls saturation equilibrium.
- **CO Mapping:** Module-2 questions test **CO2** — *Apply conversion-process principles to design product slates*.
- **Common mistakes:**
  - Treating conversion-yield as linear (it is logarithmic).
  - Forgetting 1.5 H₂ multiplier in HDN.
  - Confusing HDN (1.5 H₂) with HDS (1 H₂).
  - Mis-stating catalyst as "thermal" — it is **catalytic**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🛢️ SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Hydrocracking** is a two-stage, high-pressure catalytic refining process that combines **catalytic cracking** (thermolytic cleavage of carbon–carbon bonds) with **catalytic hydrogenation** in a hydrogen-rich atmosphere. It is used to convert heavy, low-value feedstocks (vacuum gas oil, deasphalted oil, atmospheric residue, and coker gas oils) into high-value, lower-boiling products such as **naphtha, jet fuel, and diesel**, while simultaneously removing heteroatoms (S, N, O, metals).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Tone):**
> *"Hydrocracking is a hydrogen-addition process used to convert heavy hydrocarbon fractions into lighter, more valuable products. The reactions proceed via a bifunctional (dual-site) catalytic mechanism involving both acidic (cracking) sites and metallic (hydrogenation) sites, typically operated at **70–200 bar** and **350–450 °C**."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Molecular Saw + Welding Torch"

Imagine a long, dirty, rusty metal chain (the heavy hydrocarbon molecule) lying in a junkyard:

- The **chain itself** has weak joints (C–C bonds) that need to be cut into smaller, useful pieces.
- The **rust** (sulfur, nitrogen, oxygen, metals) must be removed to make the chain clean and strong.
- A **saw** represents the **acidic catalyst site** (like zeolite), which *cuts* the chain into fragments.
- A **welding torch** represents the **metallic hydrogenation site** (like Ni-Mo, Pt-Pd, or Ni-W sulfides), which *repairs broken ends* by adding hydrogen so they don't recombine into sludge (coke).

> **Result:** A handful of clean, sturdy, short metal pieces (light, saturated, high-quality fuels) instead of a useless, rusty long chain.

| Process Step | Real-World Tool | Catalyst Component |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Cutting long chain | Saw | **Acidic site** (Zeolite USY, SiO₂-Al₂O₃) |
| Welding ends with H₂ | Torch | **Metallic site** (Ni-Mo, Pt, Pd, W) |
| Rust removal | Sandblaster | **Metal site** (HDS, HDN reactions) |

## 1.3 Why Hydrocracking? — The Refinery's "Heavy Lifter"

- **Feedstock flexibility:** Processes anything from heavy VGO to deasphalted oil.
- **Product slate tunability:** Operator can swing yield between **naphtha (~30–80%)**, **jet/kerosene (~30–60%)**, or **diesel (~50–80%)** simply by changing temperature, pressure, and catalyst.
- **Near-zero sulfur, nitrogen, aromatics** in products (compliant with Euro-VI / BS-VI norms).
- **High cetane number** diesel output (CN = 55–65+), critical for modern engines.
- **Low olefin & coke yield** due to abundant hydrogen.

> [!NOTE]
> Standard Metrics Used in KTU Problems:
> - **Operating Pressure:** **70–200 bar (typically 100–150 bar)**
> - **Operating Temperature:** **350–450 °C**
> - **H₂/HC ratio (molar):** **3–8 Nm³ H₂ per m³ feed**
> - **LHSV (Liquid Hourly Space Velocity):** **0.5–2.0 h⁻¹**
> - **Conversion per pass:** **40–80%**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ⚙️ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Hydrocracking Reactions

The reactions occurring inside a hydrocracker can be classified into **two broad families**: *Primary reactions* (responsible for the core conversion) and *Secondary reactions* (responsible for product upgrading and heteroatom removal).

### 2.1.1 Primary Reactions

**(A) Cracking of Higher Hydrocarbons (C–C Bond Cleavage)**

This is the dominant reaction. Paraffins, naphthenes, and alkyl-aromatics undergo β-scission on acidic sites:

$$ \text{C}_{n}\text{H}_{2n+2} \xrightarrow{\text{Acid Site, H}_2} \text{C}_{m}\text{H}_{2m+2} + \text{C}_{(n-m)}\text{H}_{2(n-m)} $$

**(B) Hydrogenation of Cracked Fragments**

The carbenium ions formed on acid sites are immediately quenched (hydrogenated) on metal sites:

$$ \text{R}-\text{CH}=\text{CH}_2 + \text{H}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Metal Site}} \text{R}-\text{CH}_2-\text{CH}_3 $$

### 2.1.2 Secondary Reactions (Satellite Reactions)

| # | Reaction | Purpose | Site |
|:-:|:---------|:--------|:----:|
| 1 | **Hydrodesulfurization (HDS)** | Remove S → H₂S | Metal |
| 2 | **Hydrodenitrogenation (HDN)** | Remove N → NH₃ | Metal + Acid |
| 3 | **Hydrodeoxygenation (HDO)** | Remove O → H₂O | Metal |
| 4 | **Hydrodemetallization (HDM)** | Remove Ni, V → deposited on catalyst | Metal |
| 5 | **Isomerization** | n-paraffin → i-paraffin (↑octane, ↓cloud point) | Acid |
| 6 | **Cyclization / Ring Opening** | Naphthenes → paraffins (cetane boost) | Acid + Metal |
| 7 | **Dealkylation** | Alkyl-aromatic → lighter aromatic + paraffin | Acid |
| 8 | **Saturation of Aromatics** | Polyaromatic → monoaromatic → naphthene | Metal |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Insight:** The combination of **hydrogenation + cracking** distinguishes hydrocracking from fluid catalytic cracking (FCC). FCC uses *no external H₂*, produces *olefins + coke*, and operates at *lower pressure*. Hydrocracking uses *external H₂*, produces *saturated products*, and gives *higher diesel selectivity*.

## 2.2 Bifunctional (Dual-Site) Catalytic Mechanism

The hallmark of hydrocracking is the **dual-function catalyst**. The two functions must be **balanced** — too much acidity → coking; too much metal → poor cracking.

```
Step 1: ADSORPTION — Feed molecule adsorbs on metal site → loses H → forms olefin
Step 2: MIGRATION — Olefin migrates to adjacent acid site
Step 3: PROTONATION — Acid site donates H⁺ → forms carbenium ion
Step 4: β-SCISSION — Carbenium ion cracks into smaller carbenium + olefin
Step 5: HYDRIDE TRANSFER — New carbenium gets H⁻ from alkane
Step 6: HYDROGENATION — Fragment returns to metal site → picks up H → saturated paraffin
Step 7: DESORPTION — Final paraffin desorbs
```

## 2.3 Reaction Network for Different Feedstock Families

| Feedstock Family | Primary Reaction | Dominant Product |
|:-----------------|:-----------------|:-----------------|
| **n-Paraffins** | β-scission + isomerization | Iso-paraffins (naphtha/diesel) |
| **Naphthenes** | Ring opening + dealkylation | Branched paraffins (high cetane) |
| **Aromatics** | Saturation → ring opening | Naphthenes + paraffins |
| **Resins & Asphaltenes** | HDM, HDN, HDS, dealkylation | Light oils + metal sulfides (on cat.) |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Formulas for KTU Board Examination**

| # | Formula / Quantity | Expression | Typical Units |
|:-:|:-------------------|:-----------|:--------------|
| 1 | **Conversion (X)** | $X = \dfrac{F_{\text{in}} - F_{\text{unconverted}}}{F_{\text{in}}} \times 100$ | % |
| 2 | **LHSV** | $\text{LHSV} = \dfrac{V_{\text{feed/hr}}}{V_{\text{catalyst}}}$ | h⁻¹ |
| 3 | **Space–Time Yield (STY)** | $\text{STY} = \dfrac{\text{kg product}}{\text{kg cat.} \cdot \text{hr}}$ | h⁻¹ |
| 4 | **H₂ Consumption** | $W_{\text{H}_2} = \dfrac{n_{\text{H}_2,\text{in}} - n_{\text{H}_2,\text{out}}}{F_{\text{feed}}}$ | Nm³/m³ or scf/bbl |
| 5 | **Selectivity (Sᵢ)** | $S_i = \dfrac{Y_i}{X}$ (mass or mol basis) | – |
| 6 | **Overall H₂ for HDS** | $\text{RSH} + \text{H}_2 \to \text{RH} + \text{H}_2\text{S}$ | Stoichiometric |
| 7 | **Overall H₂ for HDN** | $\text{R-NH}_2 + \text{H}_2 \to \text{RH} + \text{NH}_3$ | Stoichiometric |
| 8 | **Aromatic Saturation** | $\text{Ar} + 3\text{H}_2 \to \text{Naphthene}$ | 3 mol H₂ / ring |
| 9 | **Overall Cracking Stoichiometry** | $\text{C}_{20}\text{H}_{42} + \text{H}_2 \to 2\,\text{C}_{10}\text{H}_{22}$ | – |
| 10 | **Diesel Cetane Boost via Saturation** | $\text{Aromatic (CN}\approx 0\text{)} \xrightarrow{+3\text{H}_2} \text{Naphthene (CN}\approx 40\text{)}$ | – |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Reaction analysis feeds directly into **reactor design (PFR vs. trickle-bed)**, **catalyst selection (Ni-Mo vs. Ni-W)**, and **H₂ compressor sizing** in process flow sheets. KTU problems often couple reactions with material balance over the reactor.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🔬 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivations

### 3.1.1 Stoichiometric Hydrogen Consumption for a Mixed Feed

**Problem Setup:** A hydrocracker processes 1000 kg/hr of feed containing 2 wt% sulfur and 15 wt% poly-aromatics. Calculate the **minimum stoichiometric H₂ requirement** in Nm³/hr.

**Step 1 — HDS reaction stoichiometry:**

$$
\text{R-S-R'} + \text{H}_2 \longrightarrow \text{RH} + \text{R'H} + \text{H}_2\text{S}
$$

Per mole of S (atomic mass 32), 1 mole of H₂ is consumed.

**Step 2 — Mass of sulfur in feed per hour:**

$$
m_S = 0.02 \times 1000 = 20 \text{ kg/hr}
$$

**Step 3 — Moles of S per hour:**

$$
n_S = \frac{20{,}000 \text{ g/hr}}{32 \text{ g/mol}} = 625 \text{ mol/hr}
$$

**Step 4 — Moles of H₂ for HDS:**

$$
n_{\text{H}_2}^{\text{HDS}} = 625 \text{ mol/hr} = 0.625 \text{ kmol/hr}
$$

**Step 5 — Aromatic saturation stoichiometry (3 H₂ per aromatic ring):**

$$
\text{Ar} + 3\text{H}_2 \longrightarrow \text{Naphthene}
$$

**Step 6 — Average molar mass of feed ≈ 350 g/mol** (typical VGO). Mass of aromatics:

$$
m_{\text{Ar}} = 0.15 \times 1000 = 150 \text{ kg/hr}
$$

$$
n_{\text{Ar}} = \frac{150{,}000}{350} = 428.57 \text{ mol/hr}
$$

**Step 7 — H₂ for ring saturation:**

$$
n_{\text{H}_2}^{\text{Ar}} = 3 \times 428.57 = 1285.71 \text{ mol/hr} = 1.286 \text{ kmol/hr}
$$

**Step 8 — Cracking contribution** (assume 10 wt% conversion, average C–C bond → 1 H₂ per bond):

$$
m_{\text{cracked}} = 100 \text{ kg/hr}, \quad n = \frac{100{,}000}{350} = 285.7 \text{ mol bonds}
$$

$$
n_{\text{H}_2}^{\text{crack}} \approx 0.286 \text{ kmol/hr}
$$

**Step 9 — Total H₂ moles:**

$$
n_{\text{H}_2}^{\text{total}} = 0.625 + 1.286 + 0.286 = 2.197 \text{ kmol/hr}
$$

**Step 10 — Convert to Nm³/hr** (1 kmol ideal gas = 22.414 Nm³ at STP):

$$
V_{\text{H}_2} = 2.197 \times 22.414 = 49.25 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{hr}
$$

> **Final Answer:** ≈ **49.3 Nm³ H₂/hr** (stoichiometric; actual operation uses **3–5× excess** = 150–250 Nm³/hr).

### 3.1.2 Conversion, Selectivity & Yield Relationship

For a single-pass hydrocracker:

$$
X = \frac{F_{\text{feed,in}} - F_{\text{unconv,out}}}{F_{\text{feed,in}}}
$$

$$
Y_i = \frac{F_i}{F_{\text{feed,in}}}
$$

$$
S_i = \frac{Y_i}{X}
$$

**Identity (must hold for mass balance):**

$$
\sum_{i} Y_i + Y_{\text{unconverted}} = 1.0
$$

### 3.1.3 Mass Balance Across Hydrocracker Reactor

**Inlet:** Feed (F) + H₂ (H_in)  
**Outlet:** Products (P) + Unconverted (U) + H₂S + NH₃ + H₂O + H₂ (H_out)

$$
F + H_{\text{in}} = P + U + H_{\text{out}} + H_2S + NH_3 + H_2O
$$

**Carbon balance:**

$$
\sum_i F_i \cdot w_{C,i}\Big|_{\text{in}} = \sum_j F_j \cdot w_{C,j}\Big|_{\text{out}}
$$

where $w_{C}$ is the weight fraction of carbon in stream *i*.

### 3.1.4 LHSV Derivation

LHSV is *not* a derivation but a *definition* tied to reactor design:

$$
\text{LHSV} = \frac{\dot{V}_{\text{liq feed}}}{V_{\text{catalyst bed}}}
$$

A **lower LHSV** = *longer residence time* = *higher conversion* but *risk of coking*.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Operating Variables

| Variable | ↑ Effect on Conversion | ↑ Effect on Product Quality | Trade-off / Risk |
|:---------|:---------------------:|:--------------------------:|:-----------------|
| **Temperature** | ↑↑ | ↓ (overcracking) | Coke, gas make |
| **Pressure (H₂)** | ↑↑ | ↑↑ (aromatic sat., HDN) | CapEx, H₂ cost |
| **LHSV** | ↓↓ | ↓ | Throughput ↓ |
| **H₂/HC ratio** | ↑ | ↑ | Compressor duty |
| **Acidity of catalyst** | ↑ | ↑ (isomerization) | Coking ↑ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of Thumb from Industrial Practice:** Every **+10 °C** in reactor WABT (Weighted Average Bed Temperature) increases conversion by **5–8%**, but cuts catalyst cycle life by ~30%. Hydrocrackers typically operate at **WABT = 380–410 °C**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Bifunctional Reaction Pathway (Mermaid Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Heavy Feedstock C20 plus paraffins aromatics resins"] --> B["Metal Site Adsorption and Dehydrogenation"]
    B --> C["Olefin Intermediate Migrates to Acid Site"]
    C --> D["Protonation on Brønsted Acid Site"]
    D --> E["Carbenium Ion Formation"]
    E --> F["Beta Scission Cracking"]
    F --> G["Smaller Carbenium Ion and Olefin"]
    G --> H["Hydride Transfer from Alkane"]
    H --> I["Fragment Returns to Metal Site"]
    I --> J["Hydrogenation to Saturated Paraffin"]
    J --> K["Desorption of Light Saturated Product"]
    K --> L["Lighter Products Naphtha Jet Diesel"]
    A --> M["Parallel Pathway HDN HDS HDM"]
    M --> N["Heteroatom Removal H2S NH3 H2O"]
    N --> L
```

## 4.2 Reaction Network on Dual-Function Catalyst

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph METALSITE["METAL HYDROGENATION FUNCTION Ni Mo Pt W"]
        M1["Dehydrogenation of Paraffin to Olefin"]
        M2["Hydrogenation of Cracked Fragments"]
        M3["HDS HDN HDO HDM Saturation of Aromatics"]
    end
    subgraph ACIDSITE["ACIDIC CRACKING FUNCTION Zeolite USY SiO2 Al2O3"]
        A1["Protonation of Olefin to Carbenium"]
        A2["Beta Scission C C Bond Cleavage"]
        A3["Isomerization Skeletal Rearrangement"]
        A4["Cyclization and Dealkylation"]
    end
    FEED["VGO or DAO Feed"] --> M1
    M1 --> A1
    A1 --> A2
    A2 --> A3
    A3 --> A4
    A4 --> M2
    M2 --> PRODUCT["Saturated Light Products"]
    FEED --> M3
    M3 --> CLEAN["Clean Heteroatom Free Streams"]
```

## 4.3 Process Block Diagram of Two-Stage Hydrocracker

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H2MAKE["H2 Plant Reformer"] --> COMP["H2 Compressor"]
    COMP --> HEATER["Feed Heater"]
    FEED["Fresh Feed plus Recycle"] --> HEATER
    HEATER --> R1["Stage 1 Reactor Hydrotreating HDN HDS HDM"]
    R1 --> SEP1["HP Separator Gas Liquid"]
    SEP1 --> R2["Stage 2 Reactor Hydrocracking Bifunctional Catalyst"]
    R2 --> SEP2["LP Separator"]
    SEP2 --> FRAC["Product Fractionator"]
    FRAC --> NAPH["Naphtha"]
    FRAC --> JET["Jet Kerosene"]
    FRAC --> DSL["Diesel"]
    FRAC --> BTMS["Heavy Bottoms Recycle to R2"]
    SEP1 --> RECYCLE["Recycle Gas Scrub H2S"]
    RECYCLE --> COMP
```

```mermaid

    L["Low Severity 380 C 100 bar"] --> M["Mild Severity 400 C 130 bar"]
    M --> H["High Severity 420 C 160 bar"]

```
