<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 🅰️ Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Differentiate between catalytic cracking and hydrocracking. List three key advantages of hydrocracking over FCC.**

**Model Answer (KTU Key Style):**

| Feature | Catalytic Cracking (FCC) | Hydrocracking |
|:--------|:------------------------:|:-------------:|
| External H₂ | Not used | **Used (70–200 bar)** |
| Products | Olefins, gasoline (high octane) | **Saturated, diesel/jet (high cetane)** |
| Coke yield | High (5–8%) | **Low (<1%)** |
| Sulfur removal | Poor | **Excellent (HDS >99%)** |
| Catalyst | Zeolite Y | **Bifunctional (Metal + Acid)** |
| Pressure | 1–3 bar | **70–200 bar** |

**Three advantages of hydrocracking:**
1. **Feedstock flexibility** — handles heavy VGO, DAO, coker GO.
2. **Product slate flexibility** — same unit can produce naphtha / jet / diesel.
3. **High-quality, low-S, low-N products** meeting BS-VI norms.

*(3 marks — Full credit for tabular comparison + 3 valid points)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**What is a bifunctional catalyst in hydrocracking? Name the two functions and their roles.**

**Model Answer:**

A **bifunctional (dual-site) catalyst** performs two distinct catalytic roles on the same pellet:

1. **Metallic function (hydrogenation/dehydrogenation):**
   - Active metals: **Ni, Mo, W (as sulfides); Pt, Pd**.
   - Role: Dehydrogenate feed → olefins; hydrogenate cracked fragments → saturates; perform HDS, HDN, HDM.

2. **Acidic function (cracking/isomerization):**
   - Active component: **Zeolite USY, SiO₂-Al₂O₃, amorphous silica-alumina**.
   - Role: Protonate olefins → carbenium ions → β-scission → smaller fragments; skeletal isomerization.

> **The two functions must be balanced** — excessive acidity → coking; excessive metal → poor cracking.

*(3 marks — 1 mark for definition, 1 for each function, bonus for balance concept)*

---

## 5.2 🅱️ Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Set 1**
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat flow diagram, explain the **bifunctional reaction mechanism** of hydrocracking. Discuss the role of metal and acid sites in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A hydrocracker feed contains **2.5 wt% sulfur** and **18 wt% aromatics**. Feed rate = **1500 kg/hr**. Calculate the **stoichiometric hydrogen consumption** in Nm³/hr, assuming all S is removed as H₂S, all aromatics are saturated to naphthenes, and average molar mass of feed = **380 g/mol**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### 🔑 Model Solution — Part (a)

**The bifunctional mechanism of hydrocracking** proceeds through a sequence of well-defined steps, alternately using metal and acid sites:

1. **Adsorption & Dehydrogenation (Metal Site):** The paraffin molecule adsorbs on a metal site (e.g., Ni-Mo-S) and loses hydrogen atoms to form an **α-olefin intermediate**.
   $$ \text{C}_{n}\text{H}_{2n+2} \xrightarrow{\text{Metal}} \text{C}_{n}\text{H}_{2n} + \text{H}_2 $$
2. **Migration:** The olefin desorbs from the metal site and migrates to a nearby **acid site** (e.g., Brønsted acid site of USY zeolite).
3. **Protonation (Acid Site):** The acid site donates a proton to the olefin, forming a **carbenium ion (carbocation)**.
   $$ \text{R-CH=CH}_2 + \text{H}^+ \longrightarrow \text{R-}\overset{+}{\text{CH}}\text{-CH}_3 $$
4. **β-Scission (Acid Site):** The carbenium ion undergoes **β-bond cleavage**, breaking a C–C bond two carbons away from the positive charge, yielding a smaller carbenium and an olefin.
5. **Hydride Transfer (Acid Site):** The new carbenium ion abstracts a hydride (H⁻) from a feed alkane, producing a **branched paraffin** and a **new carbenium** (chain carrier).
6. **Hydrogenation (Metal Site):** The cracked fragments return to the metal site, pick up H atoms, and desorb as **saturated paraffins**.

**Roles Recap:**
- **Metal site:** Activates H₂, performs hydrogenation, HDS, HDN, HDM, prevents coke.
- **Acid site:** Performs protonation, β-scission, isomerization, ring opening.

*(7 marks: 2 for diagram, 3 for steps, 2 for role analysis)*

#### 🔑 Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
- Feed rate = 1500 kg/hr
- S content = 2.5 wt% → 37.5 kg S/hr
- Aromatics = 18 wt% → 270 kg/hr
- Avg. molar mass = 380 g/mol
- Reactions: HDS (1 H₂/S) + Aromatic Saturation (3 H₂/ring)

**Step 1 — HDS hydrogen requirement:**

$$
n_{\text{H}_2}^{\text{HDS}} = \frac{37{,}500 \text{ g/hr}}{32 \text{ g/mol}} = 1171.875 \text{ mol/hr} = 1.172 \text{ kmol/hr}
$$

**Step 2 — Aromatic saturation hydrogen requirement:**

$$
n_{\text{Ar}} = \frac{270{,}000 \text{ g/hr}}{380 \text{ g/mol}} = 710.53 \text{ mol/hr}
$$

$$
n_{\text{H}_2}^{\text{Ar}} = 3 \times 710.53 = 2131.58 \text{ mol/hr} = 2.132 \text{ kmol/hr}
$$

**Step 3 — Total stoichiometric H₂:**

$$
n_{\text{H}_2}^{\text{total}} = 1.172 + 2.132 = 3.304 \text{ kmol/hr}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to Nm³/hr:**

$$
V_{\text{H}_2} = 3.304 \times 22.414 = \boxed{74.06 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{hr}}
$$

*(7 marks: 1 given-data, 2 HDS, 2 aromatic, 1 unit conversion, 1 final answer)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Set 2 (Internal Choice Alternative)**

**(a)** Classify the **reactions occurring in a hydrocracker** into primary and secondary categories. Give at least **two examples of each** with balanced chemical equations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A two-stage hydrocracker has a feed rate of **100 m³/hr** with LHSV = **1.2 h⁻¹**. The conversion per pass is **65%**, and the product slate is **40% diesel, 30% naphtha, 20% jet, 10% unconverted (recycled)**. Calculate the **catalyst volume required** and the **overall conversion on fresh feed basis** if recycle ratio = 1.5. **(7 marks)**

---

#### 🔑 Model Solution — Part (a)

**Primary Reactions** (responsible for molecular weight reduction):

1. **Cracking of Paraffins:**
   $$ \text{C}_{16}\text{H}_{34} \longrightarrow \text{C}_{8}\text{H}_{18} + \text{C}_{8}\text{H}_{16} \xrightarrow{+\text{H}_2} 2\,\text{C}_{8}\text{H}_{18} $$
2. **Ring Opening of Naphthenes:**
   $$ \text{C}_{10}\text{H}_{18}\,(\text{decalin}) + \text{H}_2 \longrightarrow 2\,\text{C}_{5}\text{H}_{12}\,(\text{i-pentane}) $$

**Secondary Reactions** (heteroatom removal + product upgrading):

3. **Hydrodesulfurization (HDS):**
   $$ \text{R-S-R'} + \text{H}_2 \longrightarrow \text{RH} + \text{R'H} + \text{H}_2\text{S} $$
4. **Hydrodenitrogenation (HDN):**
   $$ \text{R-NH}_2 + \text{H}_2 \longrightarrow \text{RH} + \text{NH}_3 $$
5. **Aromatic Saturation:**
   $$ \text{C}_6\text{H}_6 + 3\text{H}_2 \longrightarrow \text{C}_6\text{H}_{12} $$
6. **Isomerization:**
   $$ \text{n-C}_7\text{H}_{16} \longrightarrow \text{i-C}_7\text{H}_{16} $$

*(7 marks: 2 for classification, 3 for equations, 2 for relevance)*

#### 🔑 Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Catalyst volume from LHSV:**

$$
V_{\text{cat}} = \frac{\dot{V}_{\text{feed}}}{\text{LHSV}} = \frac{100}{1.2} = \boxed{83.33 \text{ m}^3}
$$

**Step 2 — Per-pass conversion X = 65%.**  
Unconverted per pass = 35%.  
With recycle ratio R = 1.5 (recycle:fresh feed):

**Overall conversion** for a single-pass-with-recycle system:

$$
X_{\text{overall}} = \frac{X_{\text{per pass}}}{1 - (1 - X_{\text{per pass}})(1 - R/(1+R))}
$$

Alternative (simpler standard form):

$$
X_{\text{overall}} = \frac{X}{1 - (1 - X)\,y_{\text{recycle}}}
$$

where $y_{\text{recycle}}$ is the fraction of unconverted material not bled.

For recycle ratio = 1.5: recycle flow = 1.5 F, fresh = F, total reactor feed = 2.5 F. Unconverted from reactor = 0.35 × 2.5F = 0.875F. Recycle = 0.875F (assume 100% recycled).

Single-pass conversion on total feed = 65%.  
Conversion on **fresh feed basis**:

$$
X_{\text{fresh}} = \frac{0.65 \times 2.5F}{F} = 1.625 \text{ or 100\% (clipped at 100%)}
$$

In practice, with all unconverted material recycled indefinitely:

$$
X_{\text{overall, fresh}} \to 100\%
$$

**Practically, overall conversion on fresh feed basis = 95–100%** (assuming no bottoms purge or purge is negligible).

*(7 marks: 2 for catalyst vol, 3 for material balance, 2 for overall conversion interpretation)*

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in 'Reactions in Hydrocracking' Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing HDS/HDN stoichiometry:** Many students write `R-S + H₂ → RH + H₂S` (correct) but forget the **1:1 H₂ per S-atom** ratio. For thiophene-type ring-S, **2 H₂** may be needed — read the molecule carefully.
> 2. **Wrong units in H₂ consumption:** Converting *kg* to *Nm³* via molar volume (22.414) — examiners *will* check the unit step.
> 3. **Skipping the bifunctional role:** A "mechanism" answer that only describes acid-site cracking (like FCC) gets only partial credit. **Always mention the metal site.**
> 4. **Neglecting recycle:** KTU problems often involve recycle streams. Many students compute per-pass conversion but **forget to convert to fresh-feed basis** — a guaranteed 2-mark loss.
> 5. **No equation for aromatic saturation:** `Ar + 3H₂ → Naphthene` is the **most-skipped** equation. **Memorize the 3 H₂ per ring.**

---

## 5.4 🎯 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- ✅ **Hydrocracking = Cracking + Hydrogenation** (bifunctional catalyst, high H₂ pressure)
- ✅ **Feedstocks:** VGO, DAO, coker GO, atmospheric residue
- ✅ **Products:** Naphtha, jet, diesel (low S, low N, high cetane)
- ✅ **Operating window:** **70–200 bar**, **350–450 °C**, **LHSV 0.5–2 h⁻¹**
- ✅ **Catalyst:** Metal (Ni-Mo, Ni-W, Pt, Pd) + Acid (USY zeolite, SiO₂-Al₂O₃)
- ✅ **Two functions balanced:** Metal → H₂ activation; Acid → cracking
- ✅ **Primary reaction:** β-scission on acid site
- ✅ **Secondary reactions:** HDS, HDN, HDO, HDM, isomerization, aromatic saturation
- ✅ **HDS stoichiometry:** 1 H₂ per S atom → H₂S
- ✅ **Aromatic saturation:** 3 H₂ per aromatic ring → naphthene
- ✅ **Diesel cetane boost:** Aromatic (CN~0) → Naphthene (CN~40) → Paraffin (CN~65+)
- ✅ **Two-stage design:** Stage 1 = Hydrotreating (HDN, HDS); Stage 2 = Hydrocracking
- ✅ **Pressure ↑ → Conversion ↑, Quality ↑, CapEx ↑**
- ✅ **Temperature ↑ → Conversion ↑, but catalyst life ↓, gas make ↑**
- ✅ **H₂/HC ratio:** Stoichiometric × (3–5) in practice
- ✅ **Conversion definitions:** Per-pass vs. Overall (fresh-feed basis) — **don't confuse!**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Catalyst
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT638 - PETROLEUM REFINERY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Catalyst

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **catalyst** is a substance that **alters the rate of a chemical reaction** without being consumed in the overall process, by providing an alternative reaction pathway with a lower **activation energy** ($E_a$). In the context of **Petroleum Refinery Engineering**, catalysts are solid (heterogeneous) or liquid (homogeneous) materials that selectively promote specific hydrocarbon transformations such as **cracking, isomerization, alkylation, reforming, and hydrotreating** to convert low-value heavy feedstocks into high-value products like gasoline, diesel, and jet fuel.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A catalyst is a substance which changes the rate of reaction but is regenerated at the end of the reaction. In petroleum refining, **heterogeneous solid catalysts** (pellets, extrudates, or fluidizable powders) are predominantly used in fixed-bed, moving-bed, or fluidized-bed reactors.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a busy mountain pass (the reaction pathway) where cars (reactant molecules) want to cross from one valley (reactants) to another (products). Normally, the cars must climb over a steep, high mountain (high activation energy). Now, imagine a civil engineer builds a **tunnel through the mountain** (the catalyst surface). The cars can now pass more easily and quickly, but the tunnel itself is not destroyed or consumed by the traffic — it just facilitates the journey. If the tunnel gets jammed or blocked (catalyst deactivation by coking), traffic slows down, and the engineer must clean it (catalyst regeneration).

### 1.3 Standard Catalyst Metrics in Refining

The performance of a refinery catalyst is quantified using three core metrics:

| Metric | Symbol | Unit | Definition |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Activity** | $A$ | dimensionless / mol·g⁻¹·s⁻¹ | Rate at which catalyst converts feed to products |
| **Selectivity** | $S$ | % | Fraction of feed converted to a *desired* product |
| **Stability** | — | hours / days | Ability to retain activity/selectivity over time |

> [!NOTE]
> **Catalyst Turnover Number (TON):** The number of reactant molecules converted per active site before deactivation. A high TON indicates a robust, long-lived catalyst. A related metric, **Turnover Frequency (TOF)**, measured in **s⁻¹**, expresses conversions per active site per unit time.

### 1.4 The Why of Catalysis in Refining

Without catalysts, the high activation energies for breaking strong **C–C bonds** (~347 kJ/mol) and rearranging hydrocarbons would require extreme temperatures (>800 °C), which:
- Cause excessive **coke formation** and gas yield
- Produce low-quality, olefinic gasoline with poor octane
- Increase energy cost and equipment metallurgy burden

Catalysts allow these reactions at **400–550 °C** with controlled product distribution.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Catalysts Used in Refineries

#### 2.1.1 By Phase
- **Homogeneous Catalysts:** Same phase as reactants (e.g., HF or H₂SO₄ in alkylation units).
- **Heterogeneous Catalysts:** Different phase; most refinery catalysts are solids contacting gaseous/liquid hydrocarbons. Examples: **zeolites, silica-alumina, platinum on alumina, Ni-Mo, Co-Mo on Al₂O₃**.

#### 2.1.2 By Function (Refinery-Specific)
| Function | Process | Typical Catalyst |
|---|---|---|
| **Cracking (acidic)** | FCC, Hydrocracking | Zeolite Y (USY), ZSM-5, silica-alumina |
| **Hydrogenation/Dehydrogenation** | Reforming, Hydrotreating | Pt, Pt-Re, Pt-Sn on Al₂O₃; Ni-Mo, Co-Mo sulfides |
| **Isomerization** | C5/C6 isomerization | Pt on chlorided alumina, zeolite-based |
| **Alkylation** | C4 alkylation | Liquid HF, H₂SO₄ (homogeneous) |

### 2.2 Required Physical and Chemical Properties

For a refinery catalyst to be industrially viable, it must possess:
1. **High Surface Area** — Typically **100–800 m²/g** (measured by BET nitrogen adsorption at 77 K).
2. **Optimal Pore Structure** — Micropores (<2 nm), mesopores (2–50 nm), macropores (>50 nm). FCC catalysts use a hierarchical pore network for diffusion of heavy molecules.
3. **Thermal and Hydrothermal Stability** — Must withstand steam at 700+ °C during regeneration.
4. **Mechanical Strength** — Resistance to attrition in fluidized beds (FCC catalyst attrition index < 5 wt%/hr is industry standard).
5. **Acidity (Bronsted and Lewis)** — Required for cracking and isomerization via carbocation chemistry.

### 2.3 Acidic Catalysis: The Heart of Refining

Most refinery catalysts function through **acid sites**:
- **Bronsted acid sites:** Donate a proton (H⁺) to the hydrocarbon, forming a **carbocation (carbonium ion)**. Found in zeolites as bridging Si–O(H)–Al groups.
- **Lewis acid sites:** Accept an electron pair; common in alumina and extra-framework aluminum.

The classical **Haag–Dessau mechanism** of monomolecular cracking and the classical **β-scission mechanism** of bimolecular cracking both proceed through these carbocations.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **KTU exam-ready**. Master these for Part A and Part B numericals.

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Arrhenius with catalyst effect | $k = A \exp(-E_a / RT)$ | s⁻¹ | Catalyst lowers $E_a$ |
| 2 | Rate enhancement by catalyst | $\frac{k_{cat}}{k_{uncat}} = \exp\!\left(\frac{E_{a,uncat}-E_{a,cat}}{RT}\right)$ | dimensionless | Shows why catalyst works |
| 3 | BET Surface Area | $\frac{P}{V(P_0-P)} = \frac{1}{V_m C} + \frac{(C-1)P}{V_m C P_0}$ | m²/g | Catalyst characterization |
| 4 | Turnover Frequency (TOF) | $TOF = \frac{\text{moles converted}}{\text{moles active sites} \cdot t}$ | s⁻¹ | Catalyst activity metric |
| 5 | Selectivity (two products) | $S_i = \frac{r_i}{r_1+r_2}$ | fraction/% | FCC gasoline vs. gas selectivity |
| 6 | Catalyst Effectiveness Factor | $\eta = \frac{\tanh(\phi)}{\phi}$ | dimensionless | Internal diffusion limitation |
| 7 | Thiele Modulus | $\phi = L \sqrt{\frac{k}{D_e}}$ | dimensionless | First-order reaction |
| 8 | Catalyst Life | $t_{life} \propto \frac{\text{coke capacity}}{\text{coking rate}}$ | hours | Time on stream |
| 9 | Conversion | $X = \frac{F_{in}-F_{out}}{F_{in}}$ | fraction/% | Reactor performance |
| 10 | Yield | $Y_i = \frac{F_i^{out}}{F_{feed}^{in}}$ | fraction/% | Product distribution |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall:** When writing the rate enhancement formula, use **positive exponent** $(E_{a,uncat} - E_{a,cat})$ since the uncatalyzed barrier is **larger** than the catalyzed one. The ratio $k_{cat}/k_{uncat}$ is always **greater than 1**.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In an industrial **FCC unit**, the catalyst circulates continuously between the **riser reactor** (where it picks up carbonaceous deposits called **coke**) and the **regenerator** (where it is burned clean with air at ~700 °C). This single concept — the **catalyst regeneration loop** — is the foundation of modern fluid catalytic cracking, enabling 100,000+ barrels/day processing capacity. Similar continuous-regeneration concepts appear in **CCR (Continuous Catalyst Regeneration) reformers** using Pt-Sn/Al₂O₃ catalyst beads.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Why a Catalyst Increases Reaction Rate

Starting from the fundamental **Arrhenius equation** for both uncatalyzed and catalyzed pathways:

$$k_{uncat} = A_{uncat} \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_{a,uncat}}{RT}\right)$$

$$k_{cat} = A_{cat} \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_{a,cat}}{RT}\right)$$

The catalyst provides a **new pathway** with activation energy $E_{a,cat} < E_{a,uncat}$, while the pre-exponential factor $A$ is assumed approximately equal. Taking the ratio:

$$\frac{k_{cat}}{k_{uncat}} = \frac{A_{cat}}{A_{uncat}} \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{E_{a,uncat}-E_{a,cat}}{RT}\right)$$

**Step-by-step simplification:**

- **Step 1:** Assume $A_{cat} \approx A_{uncat}$ (valid when the catalyst provides a chemically similar transition state).
- **Step 2:** Cancel the pre-exponential factors:

$$\frac{k_{cat}}{k_{uncat}} \approx \exp\!\left(\frac{E_{a,uncat}-E_{a,cat}}{RT}\right)$$

- **Step 3:** Since $E_{a,uncat} > E_{a,cat}$, the exponent is **positive**, so the ratio is **greater than 1**.
- **Step 4:** Let $\Delta E_a = E_{a,uncat} - E_{a,cat}$, then:

$$\frac{k_{cat}}{k_{uncat}} = \exp\!\left(\frac{\Delta E_a}{RT}\right)$$

**Numerical Example (KTU Typical):**
A catalytic cracking reaction has $E_{a,uncat}=200$ kJ/mol and $E_{a,cat}=120$ kJ/mol at $T = 773$ K (500 °C).

Calculate $\Delta E_a$:

$$\Delta E_a = 200 - 120 = 80 \text{ kJ/mol} = 80{,}000 \text{ J/mol}$$

Now compute the rate ratio:

$$\frac{k_{cat}}{k_{uncat}} = \exp\!\left(\frac{80{,}000}{8.314 \cdot 773}\right) = \exp(12.45)$$

$$\frac{k_{cat}}{k_{uncat}} = 2.56 \times 10^{5}$$

This means the catalyzed reaction is **~256,000 times faster** than the uncatalyzed one — a dramatic enhancement that justifies the entire FCC industry.

### 3.2 Derivation: Catalyst Effectiveness Factor (Internal Diffusion)

In a porous catalyst pellet, the reactant must diffuse through the pore network to reach internal active sites. The **Thiele modulus** $\phi$ quantifies the competition between reaction and diffusion.

For a **first-order irreversible reaction** $A \to B$ in a spherical pellet of radius $R$:

$$\phi = R\sqrt{\frac{k}{D_e}}$$

where:
- $k$ = first-order rate constant (s⁻¹)
- $D_e$ = effective diffusivity of reactant in pellet (m²/s)

The **internal effectiveness factor** $\eta$ is:

$$\eta = \frac{\text{actual rate with diffusion}}{\text{rate at surface conditions}} = \frac{3}{\phi}\!\left[\frac{1}{\tanh(\phi)} - \frac{1}{\phi}\right]$$

**Limiting cases:**
- If $\phi \ll 1$ (slow reaction, fast diffusion): $\eta \to 1$. No diffusion limitation; the entire pellet is utilized.
- If $\phi \gg 1$ (fast reaction, slow diffusion): $\eta \to \frac{3}{\phi}$. Severe diffusion limitation; only the outer shell reacts.

**Step-by-step numerical example (KTU typical problem):**
Given: spherical catalyst pellet radius $R = 2$ mm $= 2 \times 10^{-3}$ m, $k = 5$ s⁻¹, $D_e = 1 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s.

$$\phi = (2 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot \sqrt{\frac{5}{1 \times 10^{-5}}}$$

$$= (2 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot \sqrt{5 \times 10^{5}} = (2 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot 707.1 = 1.414$$

Since $\phi = 1.414$ is **moderately greater than 1**, diffusion limitations begin to be significant. Calculate $\eta$:

$$\eta = \frac{3}{1.414}\!\left[\frac{1}{\tanh(1.414)} - \frac{1}{1.414}\right]$$

Using $\tanh(1.414) \approx 0.888$:

$$\eta = 2.121 \cdot \left[1.126 - 0.707\right] = 2.121 \cdot 0.419 = 0.889$$

So only **~89% of the catalyst volume is effectively utilized**. The KTU examiner will expect you to comment: *"The pellet is mildly diffusion-limited; a smaller pellet radius or a more porous structure would increase $\eta$."*

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Major Refinery Catalysts

| Property | FCC Catalyst (Zeolite Y) | Reforming Catalyst (Pt/Al₂O₃) | Hydrotreating (Co-Mo/Al₂O₃) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Active Component** | USY Zeolite + Matrix + Binder | Pt (0.3–0.6 wt%) + Cl on γ-Al₂O₃ | CoO (3 wt%) + MoO₃ (15 wt%) on Al₂O₃ |
| **Function** | Acidic cracking | Metallic dehydrogenation + acidic isomerization | Hydrodesulfurization, hydrodenitrogenation |
| **Reaction Type** | C–C bond scission via carbocation | C–H bond activation | C–S, C–N bond hydrogenolysis |
| **Operating Temp (°C)** | 480–540 | 480–525 | 300–400 |
| **Operating Pressure** | 1–3 atm (riser top) | 5–35 atm | 30–150 atm |
| **Regeneration** | Continuous (regenerator) | Continuous (CCR) or cyclic | In-situ via swing reactor |
| **Deactivation** | Coke, thermal dealumination | Coke, metal sintering | Coking, metal (V, Ni) deposition |
| **Promoter Role** | ZSM-5 additive boosts gasoline octene | Re or Sn improves metal dispersion | Ni promotes HDA, Co promotes HDS |
| **Surface Area (m²/g)** | 200–600 | 180–250 | 150–300 |

### 3.4 Deactivation Kinetics

Catalyst deactivation is modeled using a **time-on-stream deactivation function**:

$$\frac{da}{dt} = -k_d \cdot a^n$$

where $a$ = activity, $k_d$ = deactivation constant, $n$ = deactivation order (often 1 for coking).

For **first-order deactivation** ($n=1$):

$$a(t) = a_0 \exp(-k_d t)$$

This simple exponential decay model is widely used in refinery reactor design to predict catalyst cycle length.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Refinery Catalyst Classification Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Refinery Catalysts] --> B[Heterogeneous Solid]
    A --> C[Homogeneous Liquid]
    B --> D[Acid Function Catalysts]
    B --> E[Metal Function Catalysts]
    B --> F[Bifunctional Catalysts]
    D --> D1[Zeolites - USY]
    D --> D2[Silica Alumina]
    D --> D3[Chlorided Alumina]
    E --> E1[Pt on Alumina]
    E --> E2[Ni on Alumina]
    E --> E3[Pd on Alumina]
    F --> F1[Pt Re on Alumina - Reforming]
    F --> F2[Ni Mo on Alumina - Hydrocracking]
    F --> F3[Co Mo on Alumina - Hydrotreating]
    C --> C1[HF Catalyst - Alkylation]
    C --> C2[H2SO4 Catalyst - Alkylation]
    D1 --> G1[FCC Process]
    D1 --> G2[Hydrocracking]
    F1 --> G3[Catalytic Reforming]
    F2 --> G2
    F3 --> G4[Hydrotreating]
```

### 4.2 FCC Catalyst Structure Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Particle[FCC Catalyst Particle]
        A1[Matrix 50 percent] --> A2[Attrition resistance and macropore diffusion]
        B1[Zeolite USY 30 percent] --> B2[Active cracking sites and shape selectivity]
        C1[Clay 15 percent] --> C2[Binder and heat capacity]
        D1[Binder 5 percent] --> D2[Mechanical strength and pellet integrity]
    end
    Particle --> E[Pore Network]
    E --> E1[Micropores - 0.5 to 1 nm - active site location]
    E --> E2[Mesopores - 2 to 50 nm - diffusion paths]
    E --> E3[Macropores - greater than 50 nm - bulk transport]
```

### 4.3 Catalyst Deactivation and Regeneration Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fresh Catalyst] --> B[Riser Reactor - Cracking]
    B --> C[Coked Catalyst]
    C --> D[Stripper - Steam Stripping]
    D --> E[Regenerator - Air Combustion at 700 C]
    E --> F[Hot Regenerated Catalyst]
    F --> B
    F --> G[Side Stream Withdrawal - Equilibrium Catalyst]
    G --> H[Fresh Catalyst Make-up]
    H --> A
    E --> I[Flue Gas - CO2, CO, SOx, NOx]
    style B fill:#ffe4b5
    style E fill:#ffb6c1
    style C fill:#d3d3d3
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Refinery Catalytic Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Feed[Feedstock Preparation]
        F1[Hydrocarbon Feed] --> F2[Desalter and Distillation]
    end
    subgraph Reactor[Reactor System]
        F2 --> R1[Preheater]
        R1 --> R2[Fixed Bed or Fluid Bed Reactor]
        R2 --> R3[Product Separator]
    end
    subgraph CatalystLoop[Catalyst Management]
        C1[Catalyst Hopper] --> C2[Reactor Loading or Circulation]
        C2 --> C3[Spent Catalyst]
        C3 --> C4[Regenerator]
        C4 --> C1
    end
    subgraph Output[Product Recovery]
        R3 --> O1[Distillation Column]
        O1 --> O2[Gasoline]
        O1 --> O3[Diesel]
        O1 --> O4[Heavy Bottoms Recycle]
    end
    C2 -.feeds.-> R2
    C3 -.returns.-> R2
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagrams above follow KTU 2024 preferred schematic conventions. The **FCC catalyst particle** consists of four main components, each with a specific engineering purpose. The **deactivation-regeneration loop** is a closed material cycle, fundamental to continuous catalytic processes.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define the term "catalyst." Differentiate between heterogeneous and homogeneous catalysts with one refinery example each.*

**Model Answer:**
A **catalyst** is a substance that increases the rate of a chemical reaction by lowering the activation energy, without being consumed in the overall stoichiometry. It is regenerated at the end of the reaction cycle.

| Feature | Heterogeneous | Homogeneous |
|---|---|---|
| Phase | Different from reactants (usually solid catalyst, gaseous/liquid feed) | Same phase as reactants (e.g., liquid in liquid) |
| Separation | Easy (filtration, settling) | Difficult (requires distillation/washing) |
| Refinery Example | Zeolite Y in **Fluid Catalytic Cracking (FCC)** | **HF (hydrofluoric acid)** in alkylation unit |

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for differentiation with example)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*List any three desirable properties of a catalyst used in fluid catalytic cracking (FCC).*

**Model Answer:**
1. **High activity and selectivity** toward gasoline-range hydrocarbons (C5–C12) over light gases.
2. **High thermal and hydrothermal stability** to withstand 700 °C regeneration in the presence of steam.
3. **Good attrition resistance** to survive the violent fluidization in the regenerator and riser; typical attrition index < 5 wt%/hr.
4. *(Bonus)* Hierarchical pore structure for diffusion of bulky VGO molecules.

*(3 marks: 1 mark per property with brief justification)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module 2 Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks**

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**
**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the **composition and structure of a typical FCC catalyst particle**. Discuss the role of each component. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **mechanism of catalytic cracking** over a solid acid catalyst, highlighting the role of Bronsted and Lewis acid sites. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) FCC Catalyst Composition (7 marks):**

A typical FCC equilibrium catalyst (E-cat) is composed of:

| Component | wt% | Role |
|---|---|---|
| **Zeolite (USY)** | 15–40 | Active cracking site; provides Bronsted acidity and shape selectivity |
| **Matrix (active alumina/silica)** | 25–50 | Pre-cracking of large molecules, mesopore diffusion, attrition resistance |
| **Clay (kaolin)** | 20–40 | Heat sink, binder, controls bulk density |
| **Binder (silica sol/alumina sol)** | 5–15 | Mechanical strength, holds particle together |

Additional additives: **ZSM-5** (5–10% to boost propylene/butylene), SOx/NOx traps, **CO oxidation promoter** (Pt, 0.5–2 ppm).

**Structure:** The particle is an **80 µm spray-dried microsphere** with a hierarchical pore network — macropores (50–1000 nm) for bulk diffusion, mesopores (2–50 nm) for matrix pre-cracking, and micropores (<2 nm) inside zeolite crystals where the active acid sites reside.

*(Mark distribution: 2 for neat sketch of particle, 3 for composition table, 2 for hierarchical pore explanation)*

**(b) Catalytic Cracking Mechanism (7 marks):**

The mechanism follows the **Haag–Dessau pathway** on Bronsted acid sites:

**Step 1 — Protonation:** A surface Bronsted acid site (Si–O(H)–Al) donates a proton to an alkene or a saturated hydrocarbon.

$$R\text{–}CH=CH_2 + H^+ \rightarrow R\text{–}CH^+ \text{–}CH_3 \text{ (secondary carbocation)}$$

**Step 2 — β-Scission:** The carbocation cleaves at the β-position to give a smaller alkene and a new carbocation.

$$R\text{–}CH^+ \text{–}CH_2\text{–}CH_2\text{–}R' \rightarrow R\text{–}CH=CH_2 + ^+CH_2\text{–}R'$$

**Step 3 — Hydride/alkyl shift:** The new carbocation rearranges via hydride shift to a more stable form.

**Step 4 — Termination:** Proton return to the surface releases an alkene and regenerates the acid site.

**Role of acid sites:**
- **Bronsted acids** initiate the reaction by protonation — dominant in zeolites.
- **Lewis acids** abstract hydride to form carbocations, also creating extra-framework Al species.

*(Mark distribution: 2 for mechanism steps with equations, 2 for Haag–Dessau, 2 for acid site roles, 1 for conclusion)*

---

#### **Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks**

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]**
**(a)** Derive the **rate enhancement expression** for a catalyst and show that a 10% reduction in activation energy can lead to a several-fold increase in reaction rate at typical FCC operating temperatures. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is the **Thiele modulus and effectiveness factor**? For a first-order reaction in a spherical catalyst pellet with $R = 1$ mm, $k = 10$ s⁻¹, $D_e = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s, calculate $\phi$ and $\eta$. Comment on the result. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Rate Enhancement Derivation (7 marks):**

Write the Arrhenius equation for both pathways:

$$k_{uncat} = A \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_{a,uncat}}{RT}\right), \quad k_{cat} = A \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_{a,cat}}{RT}\right)$$

Taking the ratio with the assumption that the pre-exponential factor is unchanged:

$$\frac{k_{cat}}{k_{uncat}} = \exp\!\left(\frac{E_{a,uncat} - E_{a,cat}}{RT}\right) = \exp\!\left(\frac{\Delta E_a}{RT}\right)$$

**Numerical evaluation at FCC conditions:** $T = 510$ °C $= 783$ K. Suppose $E_{a,uncat} = 180$ kJ/mol, $E_{a,cat} = 162$ kJ/mol, giving $\Delta E_a = 18$ kJ/mol.

$$\frac{k_{cat}}{k_{uncat}} = \exp\!\left(\frac{18{,}000}{8.314 \cdot 783}\right) = \exp(2.766) \approx 15.9$$

A **10% reduction** in $E_a$ (from 180 to 162 kJ/mol) gives an **~16-fold** rate enhancement — a powerful demonstration of catalytic action.

*(Mark distribution: 3 for derivation, 2 for substitution, 1 for conclusion, 1 for interpretation)*

**(b) Thiele Modulus and Effectiveness Factor (7 marks):**

**Definitions:**
- **Thiele modulus $\phi$** is a dimensionless number that compares the rate of reaction to the rate of diffusion inside a porous catalyst pellet.
- **Effectiveness factor $\eta$** is the ratio of the actual reaction rate (with internal diffusion) to the rate that would be obtained if the entire pellet were at the surface concentration.

**Formulas:**

$$\phi = R \sqrt{\frac{k}{D_e}}, \qquad \eta = \frac{3}{\phi}\!\left[\frac{1}{\tanh(\phi)} - \frac{1}{\phi}\right]$$

**Calculation:** $R = 1$ mm $= 1 \times 10^{-3}$ m, $k = 10$ s⁻¹, $D_e = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s.

$$\phi = (1 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot \sqrt{\frac{10}{5 \times 10^{-6}}} = (1 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot \sqrt{2 \times 10^{6}} = (1 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot 1414.2 = 1.414$$

Since $\phi = 1.414$, the system is **moderately diffusion-limited**. Compute $\eta$:

$$\eta = \frac{3}{1.414}\!\left[\frac{1}{\tanh(1.414)} - \frac{1}{1.414}\right]$$

Using $\tanh(1.414) \approx 0.888$:

$$\eta = 2.121 \cdot (1.126 - 0.707) = 2.121 \cdot 0.419 = 0.889$$

**Comment:** The effectiveness factor $\eta = 0.889$ means only **~89% of the catalyst volume is utilized effectively**. The remaining 11% is wasted due to internal diffusion limitations. The KTU examiner expects the student to recommend using a **smaller pellet size, higher porosity, or higher $D_e$** to bring $\eta$ closer to 1.

*(Mark distribution: 2 for definitions, 2 for formulas, 2 for calculation, 1 for comment)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the sign in the rate ratio:** Students often write $\exp(-\Delta E_a / RT)$, which is **< 1** — physically meaningless. Always $\exp(+\Delta E_a / RT)$ with $\Delta E_a = E_{a,uncat} - E_{a,cat}$.
> 2. **Mixing up $R$ (radius) with $R$ (gas constant):** Use clear notation — $R_p$ for pellet radius, $\mathcal{R}$ for gas constant.
> 3. **Confusing Bronsted and Lewis acid sites:** Bronsted = proton donor; Lewis = electron-pair acceptor. Examiners will deduct 1 mark per swap.
> 4. **Skipping units in the Thiele calculation:** Always show that $\phi$ is dimensionless before plugging numbers.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] A **catalyst** lowers $E_a$ and is regenerated at the end of the reaction; it is not consumed.
- [x] **Refinery catalysts are predominantly heterogeneous solids:** zeolites (acidic), Pt/Pd/Ni metals (hydrogenation), and bifunctional combinations.
- [x] **FCC catalyst** is a **80 µm spray-dried microsphere** of zeolite (USY) + matrix + clay + binder; ZSM-5 additive boosts olefins.
- [x] **Catalytic cracking** proceeds through **carbocations** on Bronsted acid sites (Haag–Dessau mechanism).
- [x] **Rate enhancement:** $\frac{k_{cat}}{k_{uncat}} = \exp(\Delta E_a / RT)$ — a 10% reduction in $E_a$ gives ~16× rate at 783 K.
- [x] **Thiele modulus** $\phi = R\sqrt{k/D_e}$; **Effectiveness factor** $\eta$ measures internal diffusion limitation.
- [x] **$\eta \to 1$** for small $\phi$ (reaction-limited); **$\eta \to 3/\phi$** for large $\phi$ (diffusion-limited).
- [x] **Deactivation** is mainly by **coking, sintering, and metal poisoning**; **regeneration** burns off coke in air at ~700 °C.
- [x] **Key catalyst properties:** high surface area (100–800 m²/g), optimal pore structure, thermal stability, mechanical strength, acidity.
- [x] **Selectivity** is more important than activity in refining — engineers prefer a catalyst that gives more gasoline and less dry gas.
- [x] **Continuous catalyst regeneration (CCR)** is used in modern FCC and reforming units for high uptime.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hydrocracking Reaction Conditions, Different Feed Stocks
Products Yields
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT638 - PETROLEUM REFINERY ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Products Yields # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Product Yield** in petroleum refinery engineering refers to the quantitative measure (by volume or weight) of refined products obtained from a unit quantity (typically one barrel or one tonne) of crude oil feed, through primary and secondary refining processes. It is the cornerstone metric used by refinery economists, planners, and process engineers to evaluate refinery performance, optimize process configuration, and forecast profitability. In the KTU 2024 syllabus framework, product yield is formally defined as: $$\text{Yield} = \frac{\text{Quantity of Specific Product Obtained}}{\text{Quantity of Crude Oil Processed}} \times 100\%$$ The yield can be expressed on: - **Volume basis (vol %)** — most common in refinery planning - **Weight basis (wt %)** — used in material balance calculations - **Energy basis** — for evaluating thermal efficiency > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The Module 2 focus on *Products Yields* requires students to understand the typical product slate from crude, the effect of crude composition (paraffinic, naphthenic, aromatic) on yield distribution, and the material balance governing primary distillation columns (CDU/VDU) and secondary conversion units. --- ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy Imagine a giant industrial **"fruit juice factory"** where the raw input is a truckload of mixed fruits (the crude oil). The factory has several sorting belts: - The **lightest, sweetest juices** come out first (LPG and Naphtha). - The **mid-weight pulps** follow (Gasoline, Kerosene, Diesel). - The **heavy syrups and residue** settle at the bottom (Fuel Oil, Bitumen). If you put in **100 fruits**, you won't necessarily get 100 juices — some water evaporates, some pulp is converted into different products through additional cooking/cracking stages, and a tiny bit is lost as waste. **Product Yield is simply the recipe card** that tells the factory manager: *"For every 100 kg of fruit input, expect X kg of apple juice, Y kg of orange concentrate, Z kg of pulp..."* In a real refinery, the "recipe card" changes depending on: 1. **Type of crude** (sweet vs sour, light vs heavy, paraffinic vs naphthenic) 2. **Refinery configuration** (simple hydroskimming vs complex cracking refinery) 3. **Market demand** (winter → more kerosene; summer → more gasoline) --- ## 1.3 Standard Reference Metrics The following constants and metrics are universally accepted in KTU board evaluations: | Metric | Standard Value | |---|---| | 1 Barrel (bbl) | **0.158987 m³** or **158.987 litres** | | 1 Barrel of water | **42 US gallons** | | 1 Tonne (metric) | **1000 kg** | | 1 Barrel → Tonne factor | **1 bbl × SG ÷ 0.159** = tonnes (approx.) | | Global average crude density | **SG ≈ 0.85 to 0.95** | | API range of typical crude | **22° API (heavy) to 38° API (light)** | > [!NOTE] > **Key Conversion Insight:** A common KTU question asks to convert between barrels and tonnes. Always remember: **Mass = Volume × Density**, and the density must be derived from API gravity using the formula in Section 2. --- ## 1.4 Typical Refinery Product Slate (Reference Data) A standard hydroskimming refinery processing a **light sweet crude (≈35° API)** typically yields: | Product Fraction | Boiling Range (°C) | Approx. Vol % Yield | |---|---|---| | Liquefied Petroleum Gas (LPG) | < 40 | **2 – 4 %** | | Light Naphtha | 40 – 90 | **8 – 12 %** | | Heavy Naphtha | 90 – 190 | **12 – 16 %** | | Kerosene / ATF | 150 – 250 | **10 – 14 %** | | Diesel / Gas Oil | 250 – 370 | **20 – 28 %** | | Atmospheric Residue (Long Residue) | > 350 | **30 – 45 %** | | Vacuum Gas Oil (from VDU) | 350 – 550 | **20 – 25 %** of feed | | Vacuum Residue (Short Residue) | > 550 | **15 – 20 %** of feed | > [!IMPORTANT] > The **Atmospheric Residue (AR)** is the primary feedstock for secondary conversion units (FCC, Hydrocracker, Visbreaker), where it is *cracked* into lighter, higher-value products — thereby **increasing the overall gasoline + diesel yield** at the expense of residual fuel oil. --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — The "Why" Behind Product Yields The product yield distribution of a crude oil is governed by three fundamental physico-chemical realities: ### A. Compositional Reality (Molecular Make-up) Crude oil is not a uniform liquid. It is a complex mixture of hydrocarbons whose **carbon number distribution** dictates yield: - **C₁ to C₄** → Gaseous at ambient conditions → **LPG** - **C₅ to C₁₀** → Light liquids → **Naphtha/Gasoline** - **C₁₁ to C₁₈** → Mid-distillates → **Kerosene/Diesel** - **C₁₉ to C₄₀** → Heavy liquids → **Lubricants, Fuel Oil** - **C₄₀⁺** → Solid/semi-solid → **Bitumen, Coke** ### B. Separation Reality (Boiling Point Differentiation) Atmospheric distillation exploits the **true boiling point (TBP)** curve of crude. Each "cut" is taken between two TBP temperatures, and the **yield is the integral under the TBP curve** between those temperatures. ### C. Conversion Reality (Secondary Processing) For a simple refinery, yield = function of TBP curve only. For a complex refinery, **cracking reactions** (thermal, catalytic, hydro) convert heavier molecules into lighter ones, **shifting the yield distribution toward gasoline and middle distillates**. --- ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table is the **exam-day survival kit**. Memorize these before attempting any numerical under the topic "Products Yields." | # | Formula / Equation | Symbolic Form | Units / Boundary Conditions | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | API Gravity from Specific Gravity | $$\,^{\circ}\text{API} = \dfrac{141.5}{SG} - 131.5\,$$ | SG measured at 15.6 °C (60 °F) | | 2 | Specific Gravity from API | $$SG = \dfrac{141.5}{^{\circ}\text{API} + 131.5}$$ | Valid for $^{o}API \geq 0$ | | 3 | Volumetric Yield | $$Y_v = \dfrac{V_{product}}{V_{feed}} \times 100$$ | % (dimensionless) | | 4 | Weight Yield | $$Y_w = \dfrac{W_{product}}{W_{feed}} \times 100$$ | % (dimensionless) | | 5 | Volume-Weight Conversion | $$W = V \times \rho = V \times SG \times \rho_{water}$$ | $\rho_{water} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$ | | 6 | Barrel to Tonne | $$m \text{ (tonnes)} = V \text{ (bbl)} \times SG \times 0.1590$$ | 1 bbl = 0.1590 m³ | | 7 | Tonne to Barrel | $$V \text{ (bbl)} = \dfrac{m \text{ (tonnes)}}{SG \times 0.1590}$$ | Reverse of above | | 8 | Conversion Yield (Cracking) | $$Y_{conv} = \dfrac{V_{gasoline + diesel \text{ produced}}}{V_{feed \text{ to reactor}}} \times 100$$ | Always $> 100\%$ when feeds crack into lighter products | | 9 | Overall Material Balance | $$\sum W_{in} = \sum W_{out} + W_{loss}$$ | Closed system, steady state | | 10 | Feed-Product Density Relation | $$Y_w = Y_v \times \dfrac{SG_{product}}{SG_{feed}}$$ | Mass ↔ Volume yield conversion | | 11 | Conversion in Conversion Unit | $$\text{Conversion (\%)} = \dfrac{F_{feed} - F_{unconverted}}{F_{feed}} \times 100$$ | FCC, Hydrocracker definition | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Reminder:** When a refinery problem gives **API gravity of feed and product**, you MUST first convert both to **specific gravity**, perform calculations, then convert back if required. Skipping this step leads to direct mark deduction. --- ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility Product yield analysis is the **economic engine** of a refinery. It directly determines: 1. **Profitability** — Gasoline and diesel are premium products; fuel oil is cheap. A refinery that can shift yield toward middle distillates earns more. 2. **Crude Selection** — Buyers compute "Netback Value" = (Σ yieldᵢ × priceᵢ) − crude cost. Higher yields of valuable fractions = higher netback. 3. **Refinery Configuration Planning** — A deep conversion refinery (with FCC + Hydrocracker) maximizes gasoline yield; a simple topping refinery maximizes residue yield. 4. **Carbon Accounting & Energy Yield** — Each kg of product carries an energy content (LHV), and yield × LHV = refinery thermal output. 5. **Process Optimization** — Operators tune cut points (e.g., TBP end-point of kerosene) to shift yield between adjacent fractions based on demand. > [!NOTE] > In KTU board papers, the examiner frequently tests the **conversion of volume % to weight % using the density relationship** — this single calculation appears in nearly every Module 2 past paper. --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Numerical — Comprehensive Yield Computation > [!IMPORTANT] > This is a **model KTU-style 14-mark problem** solved in complete detail. Read each step; do not skip. ### **Problem Statement:** A refinery processes **10,000 barrels per day** of a crude oil with an API gravity of **32°**. The volumetric yields from atmospheric distillation are as follows: | Fraction | Vol % Yield | |---|---| | LPG | 3 % | | Naphtha | 14 % | | Kerosene | 12 % | | Diesel | 24 % | | Atmospheric Residue (AR) | 47 % | The API gravities of the products are: LPG = 110°, Naphtha = 65°, Kerosene = 48°, Diesel = 32°, AR = 16°. **Calculate:** 1. The mass flow rate (tonnes/day) of the crude feed. 2. The **weight % yield** of each product. 3. The **mass flow rate** (tonnes/day) of each product. --- ### **Step 1: Convert API Gravity of Feed to Specific Gravity** Using the standard API formula: $$SG_{feed} = \frac{141.5}{^{\circ}\text{API} + 131.5}$$ Substituting $^{o}API = 32$: $$SG_{feed} = \frac{141.5}{32 + 131.5} = \frac{141.5}{163.5}$$ $$SG_{feed} = 0.8654$$ > **Explanation:** The specific gravity is dimensionless and represents the ratio of crude density to water density at standard conditions. Since the API value is moderate (32°), the crude is classified as **"light crude"** (boundary at 31.1° API). --- ### **Step 2: Compute Mass Flow Rate of Crude Feed** First, convert the daily volume from barrels to cubic metres: $$V_{feed} = 10{,}000 \text{ bbl/day} \times 0.158987 \text{ m}^3/\text{bbl}$$ $$V_{feed} = 1589.87 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$$ Now apply the mass-volume-density relationship: $$m_{feed} = V_{feed} \times \rho_{feed} = V_{feed} \times SG_{feed} \times \rho_{water}$$ $$m_{feed} = 1589.87 \text{ m}^3/\text{day} \times 0.8654 \times 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$$ $$m_{feed} = 1{,}375{,}910 \text{ kg/day} = 1375.91 \text{ tonnes/day}$$ > **Explanation:** This is the total mass of crude processed per day — the **denominator** for all subsequent weight-based yield calculations. --- ### **Step 3: Compute Volumetric Flow Rate of Each Product** For each fraction, multiply the daily feed volume by the vol % yield: $$V_{product} = V_{feed} \times \frac{\text{Vol \%}}{100}$$ For LPG: $$V_{LPG} = 1589.87 \times 0.03 = 47.696 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$$ For Naphtha: $$V_{Naphtha} = 1589.87 \times 0.14 = 222.582 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$$ For Kerosene: $$V_{Kero} = 1589.87 \times 0.12 = 190.784 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$$ For Diesel: $$V_{Diesel} = 1589.87 \times 0.24 = 381.569 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$$ For AR: $$V_{AR} = 1589.87 \times 0.47 = 747.239 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$$ > **Verification:** Sum = 47.696 + 222.582 + 190.784 + 381.569 + 747.239 = **1589.870 m³/day** ✓ Matches feed volume (Material Balance satisfied). --- ### **Step 4: Convert API of Each Product to Specific Gravity** Applying the same API formula for each product: | Product | °API | Calculation | SG | |---|---|---|---| | LPG | 110 | 141.5 / (110 + 131.5) = 141.5 / 241.5 | **0.5859** | | Naphtha | 65 | 141.5 / (65 + 131.5) = 141.5 / 196.5 | **0.7201** | | Kerosene | 48 | 141.5 / (48 + 131.5) = 141.5 / 179.5 | **0.7883** | | Diesel | 32 | 141.5 / (32 + 131.5) = 141.5 / 163.5 | **0.8654** | | AR | 16 | 141.5 / (16 + 131.5) = 141.5 / 147.5 | **0.9593** | > **Note:** LPG with 110° API has the lowest density (it's mostly gaseous at ambient). AR with 16° API is barely lighter than water (it's almost bituminous). --- ### **Step 5: Compute Mass Flow Rate of Each Product** Using $m = V \times SG \times \rho_{water}$ for each product (where $\rho_{water} = 1000$ kg/m³, so $m$ in kg/day): $$m_{LPG} = 47.696 \times 0.5859 \times 1000 = 27{,}946 \text{ kg/day} = 27.95 \text{ t/day}$$ $$m_{Naphtha} = 222.582 \times 0.7201 \times 1000 = 160{,}275 \text{ kg/day} = 160.28 \text{ t/day}$$ $$m_{Kero} = 190.784 \times 0.7883 \times 1000 = 150{,}395 \text{ kg/day} = 150.40 \text{ t/day}$$ $$m_{Diesel} = 381.569 \times 0.8654 \times 1000 = 330{,}209 \text{ kg/day} = 330.21 \text{ t/day}$$ $$m_{AR} = 747.239 \times 0.9593 \times 1000 = 716{,}818 \text{ kg/day} = 716.82 \text{ t/day}$$ --- ### **Step 6: Compute Weight % Yield of Each Product** $$Y_w = \frac{m_{product}}{m_{feed}} \times 100$$ For LPG: $$Y_{w,LPG} = \frac{27.95}{1375.91} \times 100 = 2.031\%$$ For Naphtha: $$Y_{w,Naphtha} = \frac{160.28}{1375.91} \times 100 = 11.649\%$$ For Kerosene: $$Y_{w,Kero} = \frac{150.40}{1375.91} \times 100 = 10.931\%$$ For Diesel: $$Y_{w,Diesel} = \frac{330.21}{1375.91} \times 100 = 24.001\%$$ For AR: $$Y_{w,AR} = \frac{716.82}{1375.91} \times 100 = 52.101\%$$ **Sum check:** 2.031 + 11.649 + 10.931 + 24.001 + 52.101 = **100.713 %** > **Note:** The small deviation from 100% is due to rounding in SG values. In a KTU paper, write: *"Approximately 100%; minor deviation due to rounding."* --- ### **Step 7: Analytical Comparison Table** | Product | Vol % Yield | Wt % Yield | Mass (t/day) | |---|---|---|---| | LPG | 3.00 | **2.03** | 27.95 | | Naphtha | 14.00 | **11.65** | 160.28 | | Kerosene | 12.00 | **10.93** | 150.40 | | Diesel | 24.00 | **24.00** | 330.21 | | AR | 47.00 | **52.10** | 716.82 | | **TOTAL** | **100.00** | **100.71** | **1385.66** | --- ### **Step 8: Engineering Insight from the Numerical** > [!NOTE] > **Observation:** Notice that the **lighter products (LPG, Naphtha) have LOWER wt % yield than their vol %**, while **AR has a HIGHER wt % yield than vol %**. This is because lighter products occupy MORE volume per unit mass. This is the classic "volume contracts when converted from light to heavy" effect that refinery engineers exploit when negotiating product pricing. --- ## 3.2 Conversion Process Yield — FCC Example A typical **Fluid Catalytic Cracking (FCC) unit** receives **100 m³/hr** of vacuum gas oil (VGO) feed (SG = 0.92) and produces: | Product | Vol % of Feed | |---|---| | Dry Gas (C₁–C₂) | 4 % | | LPG (C₃–C₄) | 16 % | | Gasoline | 55 % | | LCO (Light Cycle Oil) | 18 % | | Clarified Oil (Heavy Cycle Oil) | 7 % | Compute the **combined gasoline + LCO yield on weight basis** if Gasoline SG = 0.74 and LCO SG = 0.94. **Mass of feed:** $$m_{feed} = 100 \times 0.92 \times 1000 = 92{,}000 \text{ kg/hr}$$ **Mass of gasoline:** $$m_{gasoline} = 100 \times 0.55 \times 0.74 \times 1000 = 40{,}700 \text{ kg/hr}$$ **Mass of LCO:** $$m_{LCO} = 100 \times 0.18 \times 0.94 \times 1000 = 16{,}920 \text{ kg/hr}$$ **Combined wt % yield (Gasoline + LCO):** $$Y_{w,combined} = \frac{40{,}700 + 16{,}920}{92{,}000} \times 100$$ $$Y_{w,combined} = \frac{57{,}620}{92{,}000} \times 100 = 62.63\%$$ > **Engineering takeaway:** FCC achieves a **62.6 wt % yield** of valuable transportation fuels (gasoline + diesel blending pool) from a heavy feed — this is why FCC is the **backbone of modern refineries**. --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Conceptual Block Flow — Refinery Product Yield Architecture > [!NOTE] > The following Mermaid diagram is a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** mapping the input-output transformations in a refinery's yield chain — physically equivalent to drawing the unit-interconnection block diagram required in KTU Module 2 answers. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[CRUDE OIL FEED
10,000 bbl/day, 32 API] --> B[ATMOSPHERIC
DISTILLATION UNIT] B --> B1[LPG - 3% vol] B --> B2[NAPHTHA - 14% vol] B --> B3[KEROSENE - 12% vol] B --> B4[DIESEL - 24% vol] B --> B5[ATMOSPHERIC RESIDUE
AR - 47% vol] B5 --> C[VACUUM
DISTILLATION UNIT] C --> C1[VACUUM GAS OIL
VGO - 25% of feed] C --> C2[VACUUM RESIDUE
VR - 22% of feed] C1 --> D[FLUID CATALYTIC
CRACKER FCC] D --> D1[DRY GAS - 4%] D --> D2[LPG - 16%] D --> D3[GASOLINE - 55%] D --> D4[LCO DIESEL POOL - 18%] D --> D5[CLARIFIED OIL - 7%] C2 --> E[VISBREAKER OR
COKER UNIT] E --> E1[NAPHTHA - 8%] E --> E2[DIESEL - 12%] E --> E3[FUEL OIL - 70%] E --> E4[COKE - 10%] B2 --> F[CATALYTIC
REFORMER] F --> F1[REFORMATE - 85%] F --> F2[HYDROGEN RICH GAS - 15%] B3 --> G[KEROSENE
TREATMENT] G --> G1[ATF JET FUEL] B4 --> H[DIESEL
HYDROTREATER] H --> H1[ULSD EURO VI
DIESEL] D3 --> I[GASOLINE
POOL BLENDING] F1 --> I I --> I1[MARKET GRADE
GASOLINE] D4 --> H H1 --> J[DIESEL
POOL BLENDING] E2 --> J J --> J1[MARKET GRADE
DIESEL] ``` --- ## 4.2 Yield Conversion Logic Flowchart This diagram summarizes the **vol % → wt % conversion algorithm** that every KTU examiner expects students to demonstrate. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[START: Crude Oil Feed] --> B[Volume Flow Rate V bbl/day] B --> C[Convert bbl to m3
V x 0.158987] C --> D[Get Feed API gravity] D --> E[Compute Feed SG
SG = 141.5 / API + 131.5] E --> F[Compute Feed Mass
m = V x SG x 1000] F --> G[For each Product i] G --> H[Vol of Product i
Vi = V x Vol%] H --> I[Get Product i API] I --> J[Compute Product i SG] J --> K[Compute Mass of Product i
mi = Vi x SGi x 1000] K --> L[Compute Wt % Yield
Ywi = mi / mfeed x 100] L --> M{All Products
Processed?} M -->|No| G M -->|Yes| N[Tabulate Wt % Yields
Verify Sum approx 100%] N --> O[END: Yield Report] ``` --- ## 4.3 Refinery Complexity vs Yield Spectrum ```mermaid flowchart LR A[SIMPLE TOPPING
REFINERY] -->|Low complexity| A1[Residue: 50%+
Gasoline: 15-20%] B[HYDROSKIMMING
REFINERY] -->|Medium| B1[Residue: 30-40%
Gasoline: 25-35%] C[CONVERSION
REFINERY] -->|High| C1[Residue: 10-15%
Gasoline: 45-55%] D[DEEP CONVERSION
REFINERY] -->|Very High| D1[Residue: <5%
Gasoline: 60-70%
Coke/Petchem] A1 -.-> B B1 -.-> C C1 -.-> D ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Insight:** As refinery complexity increases (via FCC, Hydrocracker, Coker addition), the **residue yield drops dramatically** while **gasoline yield rises**. This is the *quantitative essence* of product yield optimization that KTU examiners test. --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- ### **Question A1:** [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Define the term "Product Yield" as used in petroleum refinery engineering. Differentiate between volumetric yield and weight yield.** **Model Answer:** Product yield is the ratio of the quantity of a specific refined product obtained to the quantity of crude oil processed, expressed as a percentage. $$\text{Yield (\%)} = \frac{\text{Quantity of Product}}{\text{Quantity of Crude Feed}} \times 100$$ | Basis | Definition | Typical Use | |---|---|---| | **Volumetric Yield (vol %)** | Ratio of product volume to feed volume | Refinery planning, product pricing | | **Weight Yield (wt %)** | Ratio of product mass to feed mass | Material balance, process accounting | The two are interconvertible using: $$Y_w = Y_v \times \frac{SG_{product}}{SG_{feed}}$$ --- ### **Question A2:** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **A crude oil has an API gravity of 28°. Calculate its specific gravity. Comment on whether this crude is "light" or "heavy."** **Model Answer:** $$SG = \frac{141.5}{28 + 131.5} = \frac{141.5}{159.5} = 0.8871$$ Since the API gravity (28°) is **below 31.1°**, the crude is classified as **"Heavy Crude."** Heavy crudes typically yield **more residue and less gasoline/diesel**, requiring more secondary conversion processing. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > As per the **KTU 2024 Scheme End Semester Evaluation (ESE)** pattern, every Module 2 question offers an internal choice. Both **Question A** and **Question B** below carry equal marks; examiners typically use them as paired alternatives. --- ### **PART B — Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]** A refinery processes **15,000 bbl/day** of crude oil with API = 34°. The volumetric product distribution is: | Product | Vol % Yield | Product °API | |---|---|---| | LPG | 4 | 108 | | Naphtha | 16 | 62 | | Kerosene | 13 | 46 | | Diesel | 27 | 33 | | Atmospheric Residue | 40 | 14 | **(a)** Calculate the mass flow rate (tonnes/day) of the crude feed. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Determine the **weight % yield** and **mass flow rate (tonnes/day)** of each product. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Solution to Question A:** ##### **Part (a) — Mass Flow Rate of Crude Feed** Step 1: Convert API to SG: $$SG_{feed} = \frac{141.5}{34 + 131.5} = \frac{141.5}{165.5} = 0.8550$$ Step 2: Convert bbl/day to m³/day: $$V_{feed} = 15{,}000 \times 0.158987 = 2384.81 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$$ Step 3: Compute mass: $$m_{feed} = 2384.81 \times 0.8550 \times 1000 = 2{,}038{,}989 \text{ kg/day}$$ $$\boxed{m_{feed} \approx 2039 \text{ tonnes/day}}$$ --- ##### **Part (b) — Weight % Yield and Mass of Each Product** Compute SG of each product first: | Product | °API | SG | |---|---|---| | LPG | 108 | 141.5/239.5 = 0.5908 | | Naphtha | 62 | 141.5/193.5 = 0.7312 | | Kerosene | 46 | 141.5/177.5 = 0.7972 | | Diesel | 33 | 141.5/164.5 = 0.8602 | | AR | 14 | 141.5/145.5 = 0.9725 | Compute volume of each product: $$V_{LPG} = 2384.81 \times 0.04 = 95.39 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$$ $$V_{Naphtha} = 2384.81 \times 0.16 = 381.57 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$$ $$V_{Kero} = 2384.81 \times 0.13 = 310.03 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$$ $$V_{Diesel} = 2384.81 \times 0.27 = 643.90 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$$ $$V_{AR} = 2384.81 \times 0.40 = 953.92 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$$ Compute mass of each product: $$m_{LPG} = 95.39 \times 0.5908 \times 1000 = 56{,}356 \text{ kg/day} = 56.36 \text{ t/day}$$ $$m_{Naphtha} = 381.57 \times 0.7312 \times 1000 = 278{,}996 \text{ kg/day} = 279.00 \text{ t/day}$$ $$m_{Kero} = 310.03 \times 0.7972 \times 1000 = 247{,}156 \text{ kg/day} = 247.16 \text{ t/day}$$ $$m_{Diesel} = 643.90 \times 0.8602 \times 1000 = 553{,}902 \text{ kg/day} = 553.90 \text{ t/day}$$ $$m_{AR} = 953.92 \times 0.9725 \times 1000 = 927{,}687 \text{ kg/day} = 927.69 \text{ t/day}$$ Compute weight % yields: | Product | Mass (t/day) | Wt % Yield | |---|---|---| | LPG | 56.36 | **2.76 %** | | Naphtha | 279.00 | **13.68 %** | | Kerosene | 247.16 | **12.12 %** | | Diesel | 553.90 | **27.17 %** | | AR | 927.69 | **45.51 %** | | **Total** | **2064.11** | **~101.24 %** | > **Note:** Sum exceeds 100% slightly due to rounding in SG; document this in the answer. --- ### **PART B — Question B (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]** A **Fluid Catalytic Cracking (FCC) unit** processes **500 m³/hr** of vacuum gas oil (VGO) feed having SG = 0.91 and °API = 24. The unit produces: | Product | Vol % of Feed | Product SG | |---|---|---| | Dry Gas (C₁–C₂) | 5 | 0.0008 (gas at STP) | | LPG (C₃–C₄) | 18 | 0.55 | | Gasoline | 52 | 0.74 | | LCO (Diesel blend) | 17 | 0.94 | | Clarified Slurry Oil | 8 | 1.05 | **(a)** Calculate the **mass flow rate (kg/hr)** of the feed and of each product. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Determine the **overall conversion** of the FCC unit and the **wt % yield of total liquid fuel products (gasoline + LCO + LPG)**. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Solution to Question B:** ##### **Part (a) — Mass Flow Rates** Mass of feed: $$m_{feed} = 500 \times 0.91 \times 1000 = 455{,}000 \text{ kg/hr}$$ Volumes of each product: | Product | Volume (m³/hr) | |---|---| | Dry Gas | 500 × 0.05 = 25.00 | | LPG | 500 × 0.18 = 90.00 | | Gasoline | 500 × 0.52 = 260.00 | | LCO | 500 × 0.17 = 85.00 | | Slurry Oil | 500 × 0.08 = 40.00 | Mass of each product: $$m_{DryGas} = 25.00 \times 0.0008 \times 1000 = 20 \text{ kg/hr}$$ $$m_{LPG} = 90.00 \times 0.55 \times 1000 = 49{,}500 \text{ kg/hr}$$ $$m_{Gasoline} = 260.00 \times 0.74 \times 1000 = 192{,}400 \text{ kg/hr}$$ $$m_{LCO} = 85.00 \times 0.94 \times 1000 = 79{,}900 \text{ kg/hr}$$ $$m_{Slurry} = 40.00 \times 1.05 \times 1000 = 42{,}000 \text{ kg/hr}$$ **Material balance check:** $$\text{Total out} = 20 + 49{,}500 + 192{,}400 + 79{,}900 + 42{,}000 = 363{,}820 \text{ kg/hr}$$ > The discrepancy from feed (455,000 kg/hr) represents **unaccounted losses/coke/catalyst carryover**, typical for FCC units. **Acknowledge this in the answer.** --- ##### **Part (b) — Conversion & Liquid Fuel Yield** For FCC, **conversion** is defined as the fraction of feed converted to products lighter than the feedstock (typically all products except slurry oil): $$\text{Conversion} = \frac{m_{feed} - m_{slurry}}{m_{feed}} \times 100$$ $$\text{Conversion} = \frac{455{,}000 - 42{,}000}{455{,}000} \times 100 = 90.77\%$$ Liquid fuel yield (Gasoline + LCO + LPG): $$\sum m_{liquid fuel} = 192{,}400 + 79{,}900 + 49{,}500 = 321{,}800 \text{ kg/hr}$$ $$Y_{w,liquid} = \frac{321{,}800}{455{,}000} \times 100 = 70.73\%$$ --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls > [!WARNING] > **Where KTU Students Typically Lose Marks on "Products Yields" Questions:** > > 1. **Forgetting the unit conversion factor (1 bbl = 0.158987 m³).** Many students assume 1 bbl = 1 m³ or use wrong factors like 0.159 → 0.16. > 2. **Mixing up the API formula direction.** Some students write $SG = 141.5 \times API - 131.5$ (wrong). The correct form is the **reciprocal**: $SG = 141.5/(API+131.5)$. > 3. **Not converting API of EACH product to SG individually.** A common error is to use the *feed* SG for products, which gives wrong mass values. > 4. **Failing to verify the material balance.** Always sum your final product masses — it should approximately equal feed mass (within rounding). Examiners reward students who mention this check. > 5. **Confusing volume yield with conversion.** In FCC, "conversion" is feed-cracked-to-light-products, NOT a yield. Yield is a per-product metric. > 6. **Not stating units clearly.** Always write "tonnes/day," "kg/hr," etc. Examiners deduct 0.5 mark per missing unit. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Products Yields (Module 2)** - ☐ **Product Yield** = (Product Quantity / Feed Quantity) × 100, expressed in **vol %** or **wt %** - ☐ **API → SG**: $SG = 141.5 / (°API + 131.5)$; **SG → API**: $°API = (141.5/SG) - 131.5$ - ☐ **1 bbl = 0.158987 m³ = 42 US gallons**; **1 tonne = 1000 kg** - ☐ **Mass = Volume × SG × ρ_water**, where ρ_water = 1000 kg/m³ - ☐ **Wt % = Vol % × (SG_product / SG_feed)** — the canonical interconversion formula - ☐ **Material Balance:** Σ Feed = Σ Products + Losses (always verify) - ☐ **Light crude** (°API > 31.1) → more gasoline/diesel yield; **Heavy crude** (°API < 22) → more residue - ☐ **FCC, Hydrocracker, Coker** are conversion units that **shift yield toward gasoline/diesel** at the expense of residue - ☐ **Conversion in FCC** = (Feed − Slurry/Clarified Oil) / Feed × 100 - ☐ **Sum of wt % yields ≈ 100%** (with rounding tolerance) - ☐ In KTU board papers, **always tabulate results** with units — partial marks are awarded even if the final answer is wrong - ☐ **Draw a flow diagram** wherever possible (mass balance block diagram) — examiners award 1–2 marks for neat sketches ---

topics.title: Qualities and Recent Development, Catalytic Cracking
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT638 - PETROLEUM REFINERY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Qualities and Recent Development, Catalytic Cracking

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Definition of Cracking (Refresher)

**Cracking** is the thermal or catalytic decomposition of high-molecular-weight hydrocarbon fractions (long-chain heavy hydrocarbons) into lower-molecular-weight, more valuable products such as gasoline, diesel, LPG, and olefins. It is the single most important secondary refining process.

### 1.2 Definition of Catalytic Cracking

**Catalytic Cracking** is a refinery conversion process in which heavy hydrocarbon feedstocks (e.g., Vacuum Gas Oil - VGO, Atmospheric Residue, Heavy Vacuum Gas Oil) are broken down in the presence of a **solid acidic catalyst** (typically a **zeolite-based catalyst**, such as Zeolite Y or ZSM-5 dispersed in a silica-alumina matrix) at moderate temperatures (**480 °C – 540 °C**) and near-atmospheric pressure, employing a **bifunctional (acidic + hydrogen-transfer) catalytic mechanism** to yield high-octane gasoline, middle distillates, and light olefins.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** Unlike thermal cracking (free-radical mechanism, high temperature 800–900 °C), catalytic cracking proceeds via a **carbocation (carbonium ion) mechanism**, which suppresses coke formation, gives more branched isomers, and produces gasoline with a higher **Research Octane Number (RON = 88–95)**.

### 1.3 Qualities — Meaning in KTU Context

The phrase "**Qualities of Cracking**" refers to two interrelated concepts:
1. **Qualities of the Feedstock** (properties like API gravity, CCR, sulfur, metals, viscosity, nitrogen content) that determine whether a given stream is suitable for catalytic cracking.
2. **Qualities of the Products** (octane number, cetane number, sulfur level, olefinicity, aromaticity) obtained from catalytic cracking.

### 1.4 Recent Developments in Catalytic Cracking

Recent (post-2010) developments are driven by:
- Processing **heavier, dirtier feeds** (residue, deasphalted oil, shale oil, biomass-derived oils).
- Demand for **propylene and light olefins** (DCC, PetroFCC, Maxofin, HS-FCC processes).
- **Environmental regulations** on sulfur in gasoline (**Bharat Stage VI / Euro VI**: S < 10 ppm).
- **Catalyst innovations**: matrix technology, hierarchical zeolites, low-carbon catalysts, ZSM-5 additives for propylene.

### 1.5 Conceptual Analogy — "Molecular Tailoring Shop"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy — The Molecular Tailor:** Imagine the catalyst as a highly skilled tailor who can take a long, oversized fabric (heavy hydrocarbon, C30+) and, using only scissors (acidic sites) and a measuring tape (pore architecture), cut it into smaller, well-fitted pieces (gasoline, diesel, LPG) **without** burning or destroying the material. Recent developments (new fabrics = shale/resid feeds; new styles = propylene-rich) have required the tailor to upgrade his tools — this is what modern **FCC catalyst R&D** has been doing.

### 1.6 Standard Metrics at a Glance

| Metric | Typical Value (FCC) |
|---|---|
| Reactor Temperature | **480 – 540 °C** |
| Reactor Pressure | **1 – 3 atm (gauge)** |
| Catalyst-to-Oil Ratio (C/O) | **5 – 10** |
| WHSV | **8 – 30 hr⁻¹** |
| Conversion (per pass) | **65 – 80 %** |
| Gasoline Yield | **45 – 55 vol%** |
| Coke on Catalyst | **0.5 – 1.5 wt%** |
| RON of Naphtha | **88 – 95** |

## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Chemistry of Catalytic Cracking — The **Carbocation (Carbonium Ion) Mechanism**

Catalytic cracking is governed by the **Bronsted and Lewis acid sites** on the zeolite catalyst. The elementary steps are:

1. **Initiation:** A hydrocarbon is protonated by a Bronsted acid site to form a **carbonium ion (R–CH₂⁺)**. For paraffins, this is the rate-determining step.
2. **β-Scission:** The carbonium ion cleaves at the β-carbon position, producing a smaller carbenium ion and an **α-olefin**.
3. **Isomerization:** Carbenium ions rearrange (hydride shift, methyl shift) to form **branched isomers** — the reason FCC gasoline has high octane.
4. **Hydrogen Transfer:** A carbenium ion abstracts a hydride from a donor (e.g., naphthene) yielding an **aromatic** and a **paraffin**.
5. **Cyclization / Condensation:** Aromatics + olefins → polynuclear aromatics → **coke (precursor)**.
6. **Termination:** Carbenium ion loses a proton back to the catalyst, returning the acid site.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this matter for KTU?** Examiners frequently ask: *"Why is FCC gasoline more branched and higher in octane than thermally cracked gasoline?"* The correct answer is the **isomerization step** above (and the suppression of free-radical termination).

### 2.2 Catalyst — Anatomy of an FCC Catalyst

A modern FCC catalyst is a fine powder (average particle size **60 – 80 μm**, Geldart Group A) with three engineered components:

- **Zeolite active component** (Y-type faujasite, USY, or ZSM-5) — provides the strong acid sites responsible for primary cracking. ZSM-5 additive boosts **propylene yield** by 3–6 wt%.
- **Active matrix** (alumina, silica-alumina) — pre-cracks bulky resins/asphaltenes (which cannot enter the zeolite micropores).
- **Binder + Filler** (kaolin clay) — provides mechanical strength and heat capacity for circulation.

### 2.3 Qualities of a Good Catalytic Cracking Feed

A good FCC feed should have:
- **API gravity** > 22 (lighter is better).
- **Conradson Carbon Residue (CCR)** < 5 wt% (less coke).
- **Ni + V metals** < 5 ppm (poison zeolite acid sites → dehydrogenation → excess H₂ + coke).
- **Nitrogen (basic N)** < 0.2 wt% (neutralizes acid sites).
- **Sulfur** as low as practical (corrosion, SOₓ in regenerator off-gas).
- **Asphaltene** content moderate (otherwise coke yield explodes).

### 2.4 Qualities of FCC Products

| Product | Distillate Range | Key Quality |
|---|---|---|
| Dry Gas (C1–C2) | – | Off-gas, but rich in ethylene |
| LPG (C3–C4) | – | High olefinicity (40–60% olefins) → alkylation / petrochemical feedstock |
| FCC Naphtha | C5 – 220 °C | **RON 88–95**, high aromatics + olefins |
| LCO (Light Cycle Oil) | 220 – 360 °C | Low cetane (~20), used as **diesel blendstock or FCC feedstock** |
| HCO / Decant Oil (Slurry) | 360 °C+ | High aromaticity → **carbon black feedstock** or recycle |
| Coke | – | Deposited on catalyst, burned in regenerator |

### 2.5 Recent Developments in Catalytic Cracking — Detailed

| Development | Key Innovation | Typical Benefit |
|---|---|---|
| **Residue FCC (RFCC)** | Feed injection with better atomization, two-stage riser, high-temp regeneration | Processes atmospheric / vacuum residue |
| **ZSM-5 additive** | Shape-selective additive (10–25 wt% of catalyst inventory) | + 3 – 6 % propylene yield |
| **MSCC (Micro-Sphere Short-Contact)** | Short-contact (0.1–0.5 s) riser with downflow reactor | Higher gasoline + lower coke |
| **DCC (Deep Catalytic Cracking)** | Higher severity, steam dilution, 520–580 °C | Maximizes **propylene** (≥ 20 wt%) |
| **PetroFCC / Maxofin** | Modified riser, ZSM-5 + high C/O, 530–540 °C | Propylene + ethylene for petrochemicals |
| **HS-FCC (High-Severity)** | High C/O, high regeneration T (~750 °C), short contact | High yield of gasoline + olefins |
| **Bio-FCC** | Co-processing vegetable oils / pyrolysis oil | Renewable carbon in gasoline |
| **Catalyst deSOx additives** | MgO / spinel additives | Captures SOₓ in regenerator → reduces emissions |
| **Hierarchical / nano-zeolites** | Meso + micro pores | Cracks heavy resin molecules; less diffusion limitation |

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master the following equations — these are the most tested in KTU PEPOT638.**

| # | Formula / Definition | Symbol Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{Conversion} = \dfrac{F - U}{F} \times 100$ | F = fresh feed, U = unconverted feed (bbl/day or kg/h) | **%** |
| 2 | $\text{Yield}_{i} = \dfrac{\text{Mass (or vol) of product } i}{\text{Mass (or vol) of feed}} \times 100$ | Product yield | **wt% / vol%** |
| 3 | $\text{Selectivity}_{i} = \dfrac{Y_i}{Conversion} \times 100$ | – | **%** |
| 4 | $\text{WHSV} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{\text{feed}}}{W_{\text{cat}}}$ | Weight hourly space velocity | **hr⁻¹** |
| 5 | $\text{C/O ratio} = \dfrac{\text{Catalyst circulation rate}}{\text{Oil feed rate}}$ | – | **dimensionless** |
| 6 | $\text{Carbon on regenerated catalyst} = \dfrac{C_{\text{sp}}}{1 + \Delta C}$ | Csp = carbon on spent catalyst | **wt%** |
| 7 | $\Delta T_{\text{riser}} = T_{\text{out}} - T_{\text{in}}$ | Riser temperature profile | **°C** |
| 8 | $\text{Coke yield} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{\text{coke}}}{\dot{m}_{\text{feed}}} \times 100$ | Coke deposition | **wt%** |
| 9 | $\text{Heat of reaction} \approx 350 \text{ kJ/kg converted}$ (FCC, average) | – | **kJ/kg** |
| 10 | $\text{Catalyst activity} \propto \dfrac{\text{Conversion}}{C_{\text{sp}}^{n}}$ | n ≈ 0.5 – 1 | – |
| 11 | $\text{Hydrogen balance:} \; H_{F} = H_{P} + H_{\text{gas}} + H_{\text{coke}}$ | Atomic H balance | **wt%** |
| 12 | $\text{API gravity} = \dfrac{141.5}{\rho_{60^{\circ}\text{F}}} - 131.5$ | Density @ 15.6 °C | **°API** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Tip:** In KTU, $\vert x \vert$ in tables is written using the LaTeX commands `\vert` or `\mid`, not the keyboard pipe `|`, to keep the table clean.

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Refinery Planning:** Conversion units (FCC, hydrocracker) determine gasoline-to-distillate ratio.
- **Petrochemical Integration:** FCC LPG (with ZSM-5) feeds alkylation and MTBE units → high-octane blending pool.
- **Emissions Control:** Regenerator flue gas SOₓ, NOₓ, and particulate emissions must meet **MOEF&CC norms**.
- **Economics:** Every 1 % rise in gasoline yield can shift refinery margin by **$0.3–0.8/barrel**.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Analytical Problems

### 3.1 Derivation — Conversion and Yield from Plant Data

**Problem Statement (Worked Example — KTU Style):**

An FCC unit processes **20,000 kg/h** of feed. The product slate per hour is:

- Dry gas: **1,200 kg**
- LPG: **3,400 kg**
- Naphtha: **9,800 kg**
- LCO: **3,200 kg**
- Decant oil (HCO): **1,600 kg**
- Coke: **800 kg**

Calculate the **(a) Conversion**, **(b) Naphtha yield**, and **(c) Overall material balance check**.

**Step 1 — Define Conversion (assume 220 °C⁺ = unconverted, i.e., LCO + HCO + coke is "unconverted"):**

For FCC, conversion is typically defined as the fraction of feed converted to gasoline + lighter products (i.e., everything lighter than LCO). The KTU standard definition is:

$$
\text{Conversion} = \frac{\text{Feed} - \text{LCO} - \text{HCO} - \text{Coke}}{\text{Feed}} \times 100
$$

> Many textbooks also exclude coke (treat coke as a catalyst-side product, not a feed-side one). KTU accepts either, but you **must** state your assumption.

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Conversion} &= \frac{20{,}000 - 3{,}200 - 1{,}600 - 800}{20{,}000} \times 100 \\[4pt]
&= \frac{14{,}400}{20{,}000} \times 100 \\[4pt]
&= 72.0 \; \%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Naphtha yield:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y_{\text{naphtha}} &= \frac{9{,}800}{20{,}000} \times 100 = 49.0 \; \text{wt}\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Material balance check:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum \text{Products} &= 1{,}200 + 3{,}400 + 9{,}800 + 3{,}200 + 1{,}600 + 800 \\
&= 20{,}000 \text{ kg} \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

The closure is exact — the problem is internally consistent.

---

### 3.2 Derivation — Carbon on Regenerated Catalyst

**Problem Statement:**

Spent catalyst leaves the stripper with **0.95 wt% C**. The catalyst-to-oil ratio is **6.0**, coke yield is **4.0 wt% on feed**, and feed rate is **50,000 kg/h**. Find the **carbon on regenerated catalyst (CRC)**.

**Step 1 — Catalyst circulation rate:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_{\text{cat}} = (\text{C/O}) \times \dot{m}_{\text{feed}} = 6.0 \times 50{,}000 = 300{,}000 \; \text{kg/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Coke deposited per hour (carbon basis):**

Assuming coke is 100 % carbon (standard FCC assumption for back-of-envelope):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_{\text{coke}} = 0.040 \times 50{,}000 = 2{,}000 \; \text{kg/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Carbon picked up per kg of catalyst:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta C = \frac{2{,}000}{300{,}000} = 0.00667 \text{ kg C / kg cat} = 0.667 \; \text{wt}\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Carbon on regenerated catalyst:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CRC} = \frac{C_{\text{sp}}}{1 + \Delta C / 100} \approx \frac{0.95 - 0.667}{1} = 0.283 \; \text{wt}\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note:** The exact formula used in industry is
> $$\text{CRC} = \frac{C_{\text{sp}} \times 100 - \text{Coke yield} \times 100 / (\text{C/O})}{100 - \text{Coke yield} / (\text{C/O})}$$
> but the simplified form $\text{CRC} \approx C_{\text{sp}} - \Delta C$ is **always** accepted in ESE if the numerical effect is < 1 %.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison: Thermal vs. Catalytic Cracking

> [!NOTE]
> This comparison is a **favourite KTU 14-mark question** (it appears almost every academic year in some form).

| Property | **Thermal Cracking** | **Catalytic Cracking (FCC)** |
|---|---|---|
| Temperature (°C) | 800 – 900 | 480 – 540 |
| Pressure | 10 – 70 atm | 1 – 3 atm (gauge) |
| Mechanism | Free radical | Carbocation (ionic) |
| Catalyst | None | Zeolite (Y, USY, ZSM-5) |
| Product distribution | Gasoline + heavy cycle oil, more olefins | High-octane gasoline, LPG rich in C3=, C4= |
| Octane of gasoline | RON 60 – 70 | RON 88 – 95 |
| Coke yield | Low (0.1 wt%) | Higher (3 – 6 wt% on feed) |
| H₂ make (in dry gas) | Low | Moderate (with high metal feed → high) |
| Investment | Lower | Higher (complex regenerator, catalyst) |
| Operating cost | High (heat) | Moderate (catalyst make-up) |
| Feed flexibility | Moderate | Excellent (with residue upgrading) |
