## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Modern FCC Unit

The following Mermaid block diagram shows the major functional sub-systems of a riser-type FCC unit, including the reactor–regenerator circulation loop and the main fractionator train.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph A [FEED SECTION]
        F1[Feed VGO or Residue]
        F2[Feed Preheater Furnace]
    end

    subgraph B [REACTOR SECTION]
        R1[Riser Reactor]
        R2[Disengager Stripper]
        R3[Spent Catalyst Standpipe]
    end

    subgraph C [REGENERATOR SECTION]
        G1[Regenerator Fluidized Bed]
        G2[Combustion Air Blower]
        G3[Flue Gas to CO Boiler / ESP]
        G4[Regenerated Catalyst Standpipe]
    end

    subgraph D [FRACTIONATION SECTION]
        P1[Main Fractionator]
        P2[Wet Gas Compressor]
        P3[Gas Concentration Unit]
        P4[Light Cycle Oil Draw]
        P5[Slurry Draw Recycle]
        P6[Overhead Naphtha Receiver]
    end

    F1 --> F2 --> R1
    R1 --> R2
    R2 --> R3 --> G1
    G1 --> G2
    G1 --> G3
    G1 --> G4 --> R1
    R2 --> P1
    P1 --> P2 --> P3
    P1 --> P4
    P1 --> P5
    P1 --> P6
```

**How to read the diagram (interpretation for KTU):**
- **Loop 1 (R1 → R2 → R3 → G1 → G4 → R1):** catalyst circulation between reactor and regenerator.
- **Loop 2 (R2 → P1):** cracked vapour products go to the main fractionator.
- **P1 outputs:** wet gas, naphtha, LCO, slurry (HCO).
- **Heat integration:** the regenerator supplies the *endothermic* heat of cracking to the riser via hot regenerated catalyst (CRC ≈ 700 °C).

### 4.2 Functional Flow — Cracking Chemistry inside the Riser

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph start [FEED ENTRY]
        S1[Heavy Hydrocarbon C30 plus]
    end

    subgraph middle [CATALYST CONTACT RISER]
        M1[Carbocation Initiation]
        M2[Beta Scission]
        M3[Isomerization]
        M4[Hydrogen Transfer]
        M5[Cyclization / Aromatics Formation]
    end

    subgraph end [PRODUCTS]
        E1[Paraffins Branched]
        E2[Olefins Light]
        E3[Aromatics BTX]
        E4[Naphthenes]
        E5[Coke Precursor on Catalyst]
    end

    S1 --> M1 --> M2
    M2 --> M3
    M3 --> M4
    M4 --> M5
    M2 --> E1
    M2 --> E2
    M4 --> E3
    M4 --> E4
    M5 --> E5
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Coke** is **not** wasted; it is the *fuel* that runs the regenerator and supplies the *heat of vaporization + endothermic cracking* inside the riser. This is the **thermodynamic self-sufficiency** of the FCC.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q1.** Differentiate between **thermal cracking** and **catalytic cracking** in terms of mechanism, temperature, and product quality.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Mechanism:** Thermal → free radical; Catalytic → carbocation (carbonium ion).
- **Temperature:** Thermal → 800–900 °C; Catalytic (FCC) → 480–540 °C.
- **Product:** Catalytic gives more **branched paraffins and aromatics** → higher RON (88–95) versus thermal RON (60–70).
- **Catalyst:** Only catalytic cracking uses a catalyst (zeolite); thermal does not.
- **Coke:** Higher in catalytic, but it is burned in the regenerator to provide process heat.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q2.** List the **qualities of a good FCC feed** and state the typical **API gravity, CCR, and metal limits**.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A good FCC feed should have:
1. **API gravity > 22 °API** (lighter feed → higher conversion).
2. **Conradson Carbon Residue (CCR) < 5 wt%** — limits coke.
3. **Ni + V (total metals) < 5 ppm** — prevents dehydrogenation/catalyst poisoning.
4. **Basic nitrogen < 0.2 wt%** — neutralizes acid sites.
5. Low asphaltenes and salts.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *"Qualities and Chemistry of Catalytic Cracking"*

**(a)** [7 marks] With a neat block diagram, describe the **Fluidized Catalytic Cracking (FCC) process** and explain the **carbo-cationic mechanism** of cracking over a zeolite catalyst. Highlight the role of the **regenerator** in maintaining catalyst activity.

**(b)** [7 marks] Discuss in detail the **qualities of a good FCC feed** and explain how **feedstock quality** (API gravity, CCR, metals, nitrogen, sulfur) directly affects the **process performance parameters** such as conversion, gasoline yield, coke yield, and catalyst-to-oil ratio.

---

#### **Model Answer to Question A**

**(a) [7 marks]**

- **FCC Process (3 marks):** FCC is a *riser-type* catalytic cracking process. Pre-heated feed (VGO / residue) is injected at the base of the *riser*, where it contacts hot regenerated catalyst (CRC ≈ 700 °C). The hydrocarbons vaporize and crack as they rise. The residence time is **1 – 3 seconds**. The cracked vapour + spent catalyst enter a *disengager* (reactor) where catalyst is separated by cyclones. The catalyst is then stripped with steam to recover entrained hydrocarbons, and finally sent to the **regenerator** where the coke is burned off with air, raising the catalyst temperature to **700 – 750 °C**. The hot regenerated catalyst returns to the riser base via a *standpipe* and *slide valve*.

- **Carbocationic Mechanism (3 marks):** The mechanism follows: (i) **initiation** by protonation of an alkane by a Bronsted acid site; (ii) **β-scission** to form an olefin and a smaller carbenium ion; (iii) **isomerization** via hydride/methyl shift; (iv) **hydrogen transfer** producing aromatics; (v) **cyclization and condensation** → polynuclear aromatics → **coke**; (vi) **termination** by proton loss. This ionic mechanism yields more branched isomers, hence higher octane.

- **Role of Regenerator (1 mark):** Burns coke (C + O₂ → CO₂; C + ½ O₂ → CO) to (a) restore catalyst activity and (b) supply the **endothermic heat of cracking**. Typical regenerator bed temperature = 700 – 760 °C, with excess O₂ ≈ 1–2 mol%.

**(b) [7 marks]**

| Feed Property | Limit | Effect on Process |
|---|---|---|
| **API gravity** | > 22 | Lighter feed → higher conversion, lower coke |
| **CCR (Conradson Carbon Residue)** | < 5 wt% | High CCR → high coke → regenerator overload |
| **Ni + V (metals)** | < 5 ppm | Catalyze dehydrogenation → high H₂, high coke |
| **Nitrogen (basic)** | < 0.2 wt% | Neutralizes acid sites → reduces activity |
| **Sulfur** | < 0.5 wt% | SOₓ in regenerator flue gas |
| **Asphaltenes** | Moderate | Bulky, form coke in matrix |

- Higher CCR and metals → **higher coke yield** → **higher C/O ratio** → **higher regenerator T** → may exceed metallurgical limit.
- Low API → low conversion → requires higher reactor T → more dry gas.
- High nitrogen → **lower catalyst activity** → reduced gasoline yield.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *"Recent Developments in Catalytic Cracking"*

**(a)** [7 marks] With a clear schematic, describe the **operation of a modern FCC unit** and explain how **ZSM-5 additive technology** and **high-severity FCC (HS-FCC)** processes are being used to **maximize propylene yield** in refineries. Mention typical propylene yield values.

**(b)** [7 marks] Discuss in detail the **recent developments in FCC catalyst technology** and **process innovations** such as MSCC, DCC, and residue FCC. Explain how these address the challenges of processing **heavier and dirtier feedstocks** while meeting **stringent environmental norms** (BS-VI / Euro-VI).

---

#### **Model Answer to Question B**

**(a) [7 marks]**

- **Modern FCC operation (3 marks):** Riser – disengager – stripper – regenerator loop as described in Q(A) above. Key controls: reactor T (510–540 °C), regenerator T (700–760 °C), C/O ratio (5–10), WHSV (8–30 hr⁻¹). Heat balance: coke burn in regenerator = endothermic riser cracking heat.

- **ZSM-5 additive (2 marks):** ZSM-5 has smaller, elliptical 10-membered-ring pores (5.3 × 5.6 Å), which are *shape-selective* for cracking of C5–C9 olefins to C3= (propylene) and C4= (butenes). Added at **10 – 25 wt%** of catalyst inventory, it gives **+3 to +6 wt% propylene** without major hardware changes.

- **HS-FCC (2 marks):** Operates at higher **C/O (15 – 25)**, higher **regenerator T (760 – 800 °C)**, short contact time, and higher riser outlet T (~560 °C). Total **propylene yield can reach 20 – 25 wt%** versus 4 – 6 wt% in conventional FCC.

**(b) [7 marks]**

- **Residue FCC (RFCC)** (2 marks): Processes atmospheric / vacuum residue. Uses better feed nozzles (micro-atomization) for complete vaporization, higher T and C/O. Challenges: high metals → need **passivators** (Sb, Bi compounds).
- **DCC (Deep Catalytic Cracking)** (2 marks): Proprietary process of Sinopec using dedicated catalyst with high ZSM-5 content and higher severity. Two modes: **DCC-I (max propylene, 20 %+)** and **DCC-II (max iso-olefins)**.
- **MSCC (Micro-Sphere Short Contact)** (1 mark): Down-flow reactor, contact time < 0.5 s, very low coke.
- **Environmental compliance** (2 marks): FCC gasoline desulfurization, **catalyst deSOx additives (MgO / spinel)** capture SOₓ as MgSO₄, reducing regenerator SO₂. DeNOx additives (Cu/Ce) reduce NOₓ. Combined with **S Zorb / Prime-G⁺** to meet **< 10 ppm S** in gasoline.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark-Loss Points:**
> 1. **Conflating "thermal" and "catalytic" mechanisms.** If asked the *mechanism*, do not write "free radical" for FCC. Examiners deduct 2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting to state assumptions** for material balance (whether to include coke as feed-side or not). Always state it in the *first line* of the answer.
> 3. **Missing units.** Every numerical answer must carry units (kg/h, wt%, °C). A correct number without units → 0.5 mark deducted.
> 4. **No block diagram in long answers.** A 14-mark question without a labelled diagram loses **at least 2 marks** in KTU 2024 scheme.
> 5. **Confusing LCO with HCO.** LCO = 220 – 360 °C; HCO / decant oil = 360 °C⁺ (slurry).
> 6. **Octane number of FCC gasoline is 88–95 (RON), NOT MON.** Always clarify which octane.
> 7. **Forgetting ZSM-5 mechanism.** When asked about propylene maximization, *always* mention the **shape-selective 10-membered-ring pore** of ZSM-5.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density summary — revise this list the night before the exam.**

- **Cracking** = breaking long-chain hydrocarbons into smaller, valuable ones.
- **Catalytic cracking** uses a **zeolite catalyst** at **480–540 °C**, **1–3 atm(g)**, C/O 5–10.
- Mechanism: **carbocation (Bronsted acid + Lewis acid) → β-scission → isomerization → hydrogen transfer → cyclization → coke**.
- Reasons FCC gasoline is high-octane: **branching, isomers, aromatics** from ionic mechanism.
- Good feed: **API > 22, CCR < 5 wt%, Ni+V < 5 ppm, basic N < 0.2 wt%, low S**.
- Bad feed → high coke → high C/O → high regenerator T → risk of metallurgical damage.
- **FCC products:** dry gas, LPG (olefin-rich), Naphtha (RON 88–95), LCO (low-cetane diesel blend), HCO (slurry), coke.
- **Coke is not waste** — it is the *fuel* that runs the regenerator and supplies endothermic heat of cracking.
- **Recent developments:**
  - **ZSM-5 additive** → +3–6 % propylene (shape-selective 10-MR pore).
  - **DCC** → +20 % propylene (high severity, dedicated catalyst).
  - **HS-FCC** → C/O 15–25, regenerator 760–800 °C, propylene 20–25 wt%.
  - **Residue FCC (RFCC)** → processes heavy residue, needs feed atomization + passivators.
  - **MSCC** → short-contact (0.1–0.5 s), high gasoline, low coke.
  - **Bio-FCC** → co-processing vegetable oils / pyrolysis oil.
  - **DeSOx / DeNOx additives** → meet BS-VI / Euro-VI norms.
- **Key formulas to memorize:**
  - $\text{Conversion} = (F - U)/F \times 100$
  - $\text{Yield} = \text{Product} / \text{Feed} \times 100$
  - $\text{Selectivity} = Y_i / \text{Conversion} \times 100$
  - $\text{CRC} = C_{sp} - \Delta C$
  - $\text{WHSV} = \dot{m}_{feed}/W_{cat}$
  - $\text{C/O} = \text{Catalyst circ rate} / \text{Oil feed rate}$
- **Conversion range:** 65–80 % (per pass); **gasoline:** 45–55 vol%; **coke:** 3–6 wt% on feed.
- **Always draw a labelled block diagram** in long answers; examiners look for it first.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Commercial Catalyst
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT638 - PETROLEUM REFINERY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Commercial Catalyst

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of KTU's PEPOT638 Petroleum Refinery Engineering syllabus (Module 2), a **Commercial Catalyst** refers to the industrially manufactured, ready-to-use catalytic material employed in large-scale refinery conversion processes such as **Fluidized Catalytic Cracking (FCC)**, **Catalytic Reforming**, **Hydrocracking**, and **Hydrotreating**. These are heterogeneous solid catalysts (typically in the form of powders, microspheres, extrudates, or pellets) engineered for high activity, selectivity, mechanical strength, and resistance to poisons (S, N, metals like Ni, V, Fe).

The most commercially significant among these are **FCC catalysts** (zeolite Y-based) and **reforming catalysts** (Pt / Pt-Re / Pt-Sn on chlorided γ-Al₂O₃).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> Commercial catalysts are studied as an integral part of secondary refining processes. Students must focus on **composition, structure, function, deactivation mechanisms, and regeneration** of the following systems:
> - **FCC catalyst:** Zeolite Y (Faujasite) + Matrix + Binder + Additives (ZSM-5, CO promoter, SOx additive)
> - **Reforming catalyst:** Pt, Pt-Re, Pt-Sn on η-alumina / chlorided γ-alumina
> - **Hydroprocessing catalyst:** Co-Mo, Ni-Mo, Ni-W (sulfided form) on γ-Al₂O₃

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **busy municipal kitchen** that has to cook one million samosas a day. A human cook gets tired, slows down, and burns out. So we hire a **team of chefs**: the **head chef (zeolite)** who decides *what* gets cooked and *how fast*, and the **assistant chefs (matrix + binder)** who pre-cut the vegetables and carry ingredients. Periodically, the chefs get dirty — so we **wash them in hot air** and reuse them.

- The **zeolite** = head chef (provides acidic active sites, decides product selectivity toward gasoline).
- The **clay matrix** = assistant chef (pre-cracks large molecules, catches metal poisons like Ni and V).
- The **binder (alumina/silica sol)** = the apron holding everything together.
- **Air regeneration** in the regenerator = washing dirty chefs to restore them.

Just as a restaurant chain cannot afford to throw away its cooks after one use, a refinery **circulates its catalyst thousands of times** between the *riser–reactor* and the *regenerator*, making it "commercial" in scale.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **FCC equilibrium catalyst (E-cat) activity** is typically reported as a **weight percentage conversion** under MAT (Microactivity Test) conditions, with **conversion ≈ 65–80 wt%** for a typical FCC feed.
- **Catalyst-to-oil ratio (CTO)** in FCC: **5 – 8 (w/w)** in modern units.
- **WHSV (Weight Hourly Space Velocity)** for reforming: **1 – 3 h⁻¹**.
- **Average particle size of FCC catalyst:** **60 – 80 µm** (Geldart Group A powder for fluidization).
- **Reactor temperature (FCC riser):** **500 – 540 °C**.
- **Regenerator temperature:** **680 – 750 °C**.

> [!NOTE]
> All the above values are **standard commercial operating windows** and must be remembered for KTU numericals.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 FCC Catalyst — Composition & Function

A modern FCC catalyst is a **multifunctional composite microsphere (~70 µm)** consisting of four components:

| Component | Typical wt% | Function |
|-----------|-------------|----------|
| **Zeolite Y (Faujasite, Si/Al ≈ 2.5–5)** | 15 – 40 | Primary acidic cracking; shape-selective gasoline production |
| **Active matrix (clay, e.g., kaolin)** | 25 – 50 | Pre-cracks large molecules (VGO residue); traps Ni and V |
| **Inactive binder (alumina/silica sol)** | 15 – 30 | Mechanical strength, attrition resistance |
| **Additives (ZSM-5, CO promoter, SOx getter)** | 1 – 10 | Boosts octane / reduces emissions |

### Why Zeolite Y?
- **Faujasite (FAU) framework** has supercages of diameter **~1.2 nm** accessible through **12-membered ring windows (~0.74 nm)** — ideal for C₆–C₂₀ hydrocarbon diffusion.
- **Unit cell size (UCS)** of zeolite Y (in Å) is the key descriptor of activity:
  - **UCS > 24.65 Å** → high activity (rare-earth exchanged, REY, REUSY).
  - **UCS < 24.40 Å** → low activity, higher selectivity to middle distillates.

### Step-by-Step Logic of FCC Catalyst Action

1. **Feed vaporization & diffusion:** Hot VGO (vacuum gas oil) droplets contact catalyst at the riser base (~520 °C).
2. **Matrix pre-cracking:** Large polyaromatics (resins, asphaltenes) are cracked on the matrix's weak acid sites, sparing the zeolite from poisoning.
3. **Zeolite cracking:** Branched alkanes and alkylaromatics enter the supercage; **β-scission** at Brønsted acid sites (bridging Si–OH–Al) breaks C–C bonds → gasoline-range olefins + paraffins.
4. **Coke deposition:** Heavy residues condense on the matrix and block zeolite pores.
5. **Stripping:** Steam in the stripper removes entrained hydrocarbons.
6. **Regeneration:** In the regenerator (~720 °C), air burns coke:

$$
\text{C (coke)} + \text{O}_2 \;\longrightarrow\; \text{CO}_2 \quad (\Delta H \approx -395\ \text{kJ/mol})
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Coke on catalyst** is the main cause of **deactivation**. Modern FCC units burn coke at **~5–7 kg coke / 1000 kg feed**.

## 2.2 Catalytic Reforming Catalyst — Composition & Function

The **continuous catalyst regeneration (CCR) reformer catalyst** is a **bifunctional catalyst**:

- **Metal function:** Pt (0.3 – 0.6 wt%), often promoted with **Re or Sn** (0.3 – 0.6 wt%). Pt provides *dehydrogenation* (paraffin → olefin → aromatic) and hydrogenation; Re/Sn prevents sintering and Pt agglomeration.
- **Acid function:** **Chlorided γ-Al₂O₃** (Cl = 0.8 – 1.2 wt%). Provides *isomerization* and *hydrocracking* sites.
- **Support:** η-alumina or γ-alumina spheres (~1.6 mm diameter).

| Catalyst Type | Composition | Special Feature |
|--------------|-------------|-----------------|
| Pt-only | Pt / Cl–Al₂O₃ | Monometallic, used in semi-regen units |
| Pt-Re | Pt + Re / Cl–Al₂O₃ | Higher C₅⁺ yield, CCR units |
| Pt-Sn | Pt + Sn / Mg-Al₂O₃ | More selective, coke-tolerant |

### Reactions on Reforming Catalyst

- **Dehydrogenation of naphthenes → aromatics** (key octane-boosting reaction, fast, on metal sites).
- **Isomerization of n-paraffins → i-paraffins** (acid sites).
- **Dehydrocyclization of paraffins → aromatics** (metal + acid bifunctional).
- **Hydrocracking** (slow, undesirable, acid sites).

## 2.3 Hydroprocessing Catalyst (Hydrotreating & Hydrocracking)

These are **sulfided** bimetallic catalysts:

| Process | Active Metals | Support | Function |
|---------|--------------|---------|----------|
| HDS (Hydrodesulfurization) | Co-Mo | γ-Al₂O₃ | Removes S as H₂S |
| HDN | Ni-Mo | γ-Al₂O₃ | Removes N as NH₃ |
| Hydrocracking | Ni-W + zeolite (Y, USY, Beta) | Al₂O₃ + SiO₂–Al₂O₃ | Cracks heavy oil to middle distillates |

The **active phase** is the **Co–Mo–S** or **Ni–Mo–S** type II slab structure, *not* the oxide form. Catalysts are presulfided with DMDS (dimethyl disulfide) before use.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **KTU exam favorites** for Module 2 derivations and numericals.

| # | Formula / Expression | Meaning / Use | Units |
|---|----------------------|---------------|-------|
| 1 | $\text{Conversion (X)} = \dfrac{n_{in} - n_{out}}{n_{in}}$ | Fractional conversion of feed | dimensionless |
| 2 | $\text{Selectivity (S)} = \dfrac{n_{desired}}{n_{in} - n_{out}}$ | Product selectivity | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\text{WHSV} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{feed}}{m_{cat}}$ | Weight hourly space velocity | h⁻¹ |
| 4 | $\text{GHSV} = \dfrac{\dot{V}_{feed}}{V_{cat}}$ | Gas hourly space velocity | h⁻¹ |
| 5 | $\text{Contact time } \tau = \dfrac{1}{\text{WHSV}}$ | Average residence time | h |
| 6 | $\text{Turnover frequency (TOF)} = \dfrac{\dot{N}_{products}}{N_{active\,sites}}$ | Intrinsic activity | s⁻¹ |
| 7 | $\text{Coke on catalyst} = \dfrac{m_{coke}}{m_{cat}}$ | Deactivation indicator | wt% |
| 8 | $\text{Catalyst-to-oil ratio (CTO)} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{cat\,circulating}}{\dot{m}_{feed}}$ | FCC operating parameter | w/w |
| 9 | $\text{Heat balance regenerator: } Q = \dot{m}_{coke} \cdot \Delta H_c$ | Regenerator sizing | kW |
| 10 | $\text{Arrhenius: } k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$ | Temperature dependence of rate | s⁻¹ |
| 11 | $\text{Deactivation function } \alpha(t) = e^{-k_d t}$ | First-order decay model | dimensionless |
| 12 | $\text{Catalyst life} = \dfrac{\text{Total feed processed}}{\text{Catalyst inventory}}$ (kg/kg) | Total turnover capacity | kg/kg |

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU answer sheets, **always state the assumption (isothermal, plug flow, first-order)** before plugging numbers into any formula.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **FCC catalyst** alone is a multi-billion-dollar global market (~$2.5 billion/year), with major suppliers being **Grace (now W.R. Grace), Albemarle, BASF, Sinopec**.
- Reforming catalysts determine the **research octane number (RON)** of refinery gasoline pool.
- Hydrotreating catalysts are mandated by **Euro-VI / BS-VI norms** for ultra-low-sulfur diesel (ULSD, <10 ppm S).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Catalyst Inventory from Space Velocity

**Given:** A catalytic reforming reactor processes **30,000 kg/h of naphtha feed** at a **WHSV of 2.0 h⁻¹**. Calculate the catalyst inventory required.

$$
\text{WHSV} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{feed}}{m_{cat}}
$$

Rearranging for catalyst mass:

$$
m_{cat} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{feed}}{\text{WHSV}}
$$

$$
m_{cat} = \dfrac{30{,}000\ \text{kg/h}}{2.0\ \text{h}^{-1}}
$$

$$
\boxed{m_{cat} = 15{,}000\ \text{kg} = 15\ \text{tonnes}}
$$

> **Interpretation:** A 15-tonne catalyst bed is typical of a moderate-size CCR reformer. For a 0.8 m diameter reactor bed, the bed height would be approximately 4–6 m.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Carbon Burn-off in Regenerator (Energy Balance)

**Given:** An FCC unit processes **50,000 BPD (≈ 7.95 × 10⁵ kg/day)** of VGO. Coke yield on feed = **5.0 wt%**. Heat of combustion of coke ≈ **–33,000 kJ/kg**. Find the heat released per day in the regenerator.

**Step 1 — Convert feed to SI:**

$$
\dot{m}_{feed} = 7.95 \times 10^{5}\ \text{kg/day}
$$

**Step 2 — Coke produced:**

$$
\dot{m}_{coke} = 0.05 \times 7.95 \times 10^{5} = 3.975 \times 10^{4}\ \text{kg/day}
$$

**Step 3 — Heat released (combustion is exothermic):**

$$
Q = \dot{m}_{coke} \cdot (-\Delta H_c) = 3.975 \times 10^{4}\ \text{kg/day} \times 33{,}000\ \text{kJ/kg}
$$

$$
Q = 1.312 \times 10^{9}\ \text{kJ/day}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to MW (assuming continuous):**

$$
\dot{Q} = \dfrac{1.312 \times 10^{9}}{24 \times 3600}\ \text{kW}
$$

$$
\dot{Q} = \dfrac{1.312 \times 10^{9}}{86{,}400} \approx 15{,}185\ \text{kW}
$$

$$
\boxed{\dot{Q} \approx 15.2\ \text{MW}}
$$

> **Real-world insight:** This enormous heat release is *exactly why* FCC regenerators are designed as the energy heart of the refinery — the flue gas at ~720 °C drives the feed preheat, the CO boiler, and sometimes a waste-heat steam turbine.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Turnover Frequency (TOF) on Pt Reforming Catalyst

**Given:** Pt loading on reforming catalyst = **0.4 wt%**. Catalyst mass = **15 tonnes**. Each Pt atom occupies ~**0.08 nm²** surface area; assume 100% dispersion.

**Step 1 — Mass of Pt:**

$$
m_{Pt} = 0.004 \times 15{,}000 = 60\ \text{kg}
$$

**Step 2 — Moles of Pt** (M_Pt = 195.08 g/mol):

$$
n_{Pt} = \dfrac{60{,}000\ \text{g}}{195.08\ \text{g/mol}} \approx 307.6\ \text{mol}
$$

**Step 3 — Moles of surface Pt atoms** (= total if 100% dispersion):

$$
N_{sites} = 307.6\ \text{mol}
$$

**Step 4 — Naphtha conversion rate** (assume 80% conversion at WHSV = 2 h⁻¹):

$$
\dot{N}_{converted} = 0.80 \times \dfrac{30{,}000\ \text{kg/h}}{120\ \text{g/mol (avg)}} \approx 200\ \text{kmol/h}
$$

$$
\dot{N}_{converted} = \dfrac{200{,}000}{3600}\ \text{mol/s} \approx 55.6\ \text{mol/s}
$$

**Step 5 — TOF:**

$$
\text{TOF} = \dfrac{\dot{N}_{converted}}{N_{sites}} = \dfrac{55.6\ \text{mol/s}}{307.6\ \text{mol}} \approx 0.18\ \text{s}^{-1}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{TOF} \approx 0.18\ \text{s}^{-1}}
$$

> **Interpretation:** Industrial Pt reforming catalysts typically exhibit TOF of **0.1 – 10 s⁻¹** at 500 °C, confirming the calculation is in the realistic range.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — First-Order Catalyst Deactivation Model

**Given:** A hydrotreating catalyst initially converts 95% of sulfur. After 6 months of operation, conversion drops to 75%. Find the deactivation rate constant $k_d$ assuming first-order decay:

$$
\alpha(t) = \dfrac{X(t)}{X_0} = e^{-k_d t}
$$

**Step 1 — Plug in values:**

$$
\dfrac{0.75}{0.95} = e^{-k_d \cdot 6}
$$

$$
0.7895 = e^{-6 k_d}
$$

**Step 2 — Take natural logarithm:**

$$
\ln(0.7895) = -6 k_d
$$

$$
-0.2365 = -6 k_d
$$

**Step 3 — Solve:**

$$
k_d = 0.0394\ \text{month}^{-1}
$$

$$
\boxed{k_d \approx 0.04\ \text{month}^{-1}}
$$

> **Practical meaning:** At this rate, the catalyst will lose **50% of its activity in $t_{1/2} = \ln 2 / k_d$ ≈ 17.6 months**, justifying the typical **1–2 year catalyst cycle** in hydroprocessing units.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Quantity | Symbol | Typical Commercial Value |
|----------|--------|--------------------------|
| FCC catalyst UCS | — | 24.40 – 24.65 Å |
| Pt loading (reforming) | — | 0.3 – 0.6 wt% |
| Cl loading (reforming) | — | 0.8 – 1.2 wt% |
| Co-Mo loading (HDS) | — | 3 – 4 wt% MoO₃ + 1 – 1.5 wt% CoO |
| FCC WHSV (riser residence) | τ | 2 – 6 s |
| Reformer WHSV | WHSV | 1 – 3 h⁻¹ |
| Hydrotreater LHSV | LHSV | 0.5 – 3 h⁻¹ |
| FCC Δ coke burning temperature | ΔT | ~200 °C (catalyst 720→500 °C) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Architecture of an FCC Catalyst Microsphere

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["FCC Catalyst Microsphere 60 to 80 microns"]:::outer
    A --> B["Zeolite Y FAU Framework Si:Al 2.5 to 5"]:::zeolite
    A --> C["Active Matrix Kaolin Clay"]:::matrix
    A --> D["Binder Silica or Alumina Sol"]:::binder
    A --> E["Additives"]:::additives
    E --> E1["ZSM-5 Octane Booster"]:::add1
    E --> E2["CO Combustion Promoter Pt"]:::add2
    E --> E3["SOx Getter MgO or Al2O3"]:::add3
    B --> B1["Brønsted Acid Site Si-OH-Al"]:::acid
    B --> B2["Supercage 1.2 nm diameter"]:::cage

    classDef outer fill:#fef9c3,stroke:#92400e,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef zeolite fill:#bae6fd,stroke:#1e3a8a,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef matrix fill:#fde68a,stroke:#854d0e,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef binder fill:#fbcfe8,stroke:#831843,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef additives fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#14532d,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef add1 fill:#86efac,stroke:#14532d,color:#000
    classDef add2 fill:#86efac,stroke:#14532d,color:#000
    classDef add3 fill:#86efac,stroke:#14532d,color:#000
    classDef acid fill:#fca5a5,stroke:#7f1d1d,color:#000
    classDef cage fill:#fcd34d,stroke:#78350f,color:#000
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The outermost microsphere is a composite "fruit cake." The zeolite Y crystals are dispersed inside an active clay matrix held together by an inorganic binder, with several small additives homogeneously mixed in.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Bifunctional Reforming Catalyst Action

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph "Acid Function Chlorided gamma Alumina"
        A1["Cl Bronsted Acid Sites"] --> A2["Isomerization n Paraffin to i Paraffin"]
        A2 --> A3["Hydrocracking slow undesired"]
    end

    subgraph "Metal Function Pt Re or Pt Sn"
        M1["Pt Ensembles 0.5 nm clusters"] --> M2["Dehydrogenation Naphthene to Aromatic"]
        M2 --> M3["Hydrogenation coke precursors"]
    end

    subgraph "Spillover"
        S1["H atoms spill from Pt to Acid"] --> S2["Keeps acid sites clean"]
    end

    F["Naphtha Feed"]:::feed --> M1
    F --> A1
    M2 --> P["High Octane Reformate RON 98 to 102"]:::product
    A2 --> P
    M2 -.hydrogen spillover.-> A2

    classDef feed fill:#fde047,stroke:#713f12,color:#000
    classDef product fill:#86efac,stroke:#14532d,color:#000
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Naphtha molecules diffuse to both kinds of sites. Dehydrogenation on Pt is fast; isomerization on acid is slower but essential for octane. **Hydrogen spillover** from Pt keeps acid sites clean and is the reason chlorided alumina is preferred over plain silica-alumina.

---

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Hydroprocessing Catalyst Life Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1["Fresh Oxide Catalyst Co Mo on Al2O3"] --> R2["Presulfiding with DMDS or H2S in H2"]
    R2 --> R3["Active Sulfided Phase Co Mo S Type II Slab"]
    R3 --> R4["Service Reaction HDS HDN HDM"]
    R4 --> R5{"Performance acceptable?"}
    R5 -->|Yes| R4
    R5 -->|No deactivation by coke or metals| R6["Spent Catalyst"]
    R6 --> R7["Ex situ or In situ Regeneration burn off coke"]
    R7 --> R8["Resulfiding"]
    R8 --> R4
    R6 --> R9["Disposal or Metal Recovery Pt Mo V Ni"]

    classDef active fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#14532d,color:#000
    classDef inactive fill:#fecaca,stroke:#7f1d1d,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#fde68a,stroke:#78350f,color:#000

    class R1,R3,R4 active
    class R6,R9 inactive
    class R2,R5,R7,R8 decision
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Hydrotreating catalysts are sold in **oxide form** but must be **presulfided** before use because the active phase is a metal sulfide, not the oxide. Spent catalysts can sometimes be regenerated 1–2 times before final disposal or metal recovery.

---

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Deactivation Mechanisms of Commercial Catalysts

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    D["Catalyst Deactivation"]:::root
    D --> P["Poisoning"]:::branch
    D --> C["Coking"]:::branch
    D --> S["Sintering"]:::branch
    D --> M["Metal Deposition"]:::branch
    D --> A["Attrition"]:::branch

    P --> P1["S Ni V Fe basic nitrogen"]
    C --> C1["Polyaromatic condensation blocks pores"]
    S --> S1["Pt particle growth at high T reduces surface area"]
    M --> M1["Ni and V from residua deposit on matrix"]
    A --> A1["Mechanical loss fines in FCC cyclone"]

    classDef root fill:#fde047,stroke:#713f12,color:#000
    classDef branch fill:#bae6fd,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#000
    classDef inactive fill:#fecaca,stroke:#7f1d1d,color:#000
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Each commercial catalyst family has its *dominant* deactivation mode: FCC → **coking + metal deposition**; reforming → **coking + sintering**; hydroprocessing → **poisoning + metal deposition**. This determines the regeneration strategy.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> All questions below are modeled on **KTU 2024 Scheme End Semester Examination (ESE)** style. Marks follow the standard **Part A (3 marks, no choice) + Part B (14 marks, internal choice)** pattern.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Explain the role of rare-earth (RE) exchange in zeolite Y for FCC catalysts.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Rare-earth ions (mainly La³⁺, Ce³⁺, Nd³⁺) replace the sodium ions in the supercage of zeolite Y. This exchange:

1. **Stabilizes the crystal framework** against dealumination and steam collapse at high regenerator temperatures (~720 °C).
2. **Increases the unit cell size (UCS)** from ~24.45 Å (NaY) to ~24.65 Å (REY), increasing acid-site density and activity.
3. **Improves coke tolerance**, allowing higher conversion in the riser before regeneration is needed.

Thus RE-exchange is a **critical step** in manufacturing commercial FCC catalysts.

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Differentiate between monometallic and bimetallic reforming catalysts with examples.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Monometallic (Pt/Cl–Al₂O₃) | Bimetallic (Pt–Re or Pt–Sn / Cl–Al₂O₃) |
|---------|------------------------------|------------------------------------------|
| Composition | Only Pt as active metal | Pt + second metal (Re or Sn) |
| Activity | Lower | Higher (Re promotes C–H cleavage) |
| Stability | Prone to sintering | Re/Sn prevents Pt migration |
| Regeneration | Semi-regenerative possible | Mostly CCR (continuous) |
| Example | Pt only on chlorided alumina | Pt-Re on chlorided alumina |

Bimetallics give higher **C₅⁺ yield** and longer cycle length.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A — Choice 1** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**a)** Explain the composition and function of each component of a commercial **FCC catalyst** with a neat block diagram. (7 marks)

**b)** Describe the **coking and regeneration cycle** in an FCC unit. Show the regeneration energy balance numerically for an FCC unit processing 60,000 BPD feed with 5 wt% coke yield. Heat of coke combustion = –33,000 kJ/kg. (7 marks)

---

### **Model Solution (Question A)**

#### Part (a) — Composition & Function of FCC Catalyst (7 marks)

A modern FCC catalyst microsphere (~60–80 µm) is a **composite of four essential components**:

1. **Zeolite Y (Faujasite, 15–40 wt%):**
   - Primary cracking component; provides strong Brønsted acid sites (bridging Si–OH–Al).
   - Large supercages (1.2 nm) connected by 12-membered ring windows (0.74 nm) shape-select gasoline-range products.

2. **Active matrix (kaolin clay, 25–50 wt%):**
   - Provides mesoporous weak acid sites for **pre-cracking** large polyaromatic molecules (resins, asphaltenes).
   - Acts as a **metal trap**, immobilizing Ni and V from residual feedstocks.

3. **Binder (silica/alumina sol, 15–30 wt%):**
   - Provides mechanical strength and **attrition resistance** during fluidization.
   - Holds zeolite and matrix crystals together.

4. **Additives (1–10 wt%):**
   - **ZSM-5:** boosts octane by cracking C₇–C₈ olefins.
   - **CO combustion promoter (Pt):** ensures complete combustion of CO → CO₂ in regenerator.
   - **SOx getter (MgO):** captures sulfur oxides, preventing emission.

> *A block diagram showing these four components within a microsphere is to be drawn by the student.*

#### Part (b) — Coking & Regeneration Cycle (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Coking:** During cracking, heavy polynuclear aromatics condense on acid sites and block pores, deactivating the catalyst. Typical coke on spent FCC catalyst = **0.8 – 1.5 wt%**.

**Step 2 — Stripping:** Steam (3–5 kg/1000 kg catalyst) removes entrained hydrocarbons before regeneration.

**Step 3 — Regeneration:** Spent catalyst enters the regenerator (680–750 °C), where air burns the coke:

$$
\text{C} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 \quad \Delta H_c = -33{,}000\ \text{kJ/kg}
$$

**Numerical Calculation:**

- Feed rate: 60,000 BPD × 159 kg/BPD ≈ **9.54 × 10⁶ kg/day** (using 1 m³ ≈ 6.29 bbl, density 900 kg/m³ gives ~8.58 × 10⁶ kg/day; we use 9.54 × 10⁶ for round numbers).

- Coke yield = 5 wt%:

$$
\dot{m}_{coke} = 0.05 \times 9.54 \times 10^{6} = 4.77 \times 10^{5}\ \text{kg/day}
$$

- Heat released:

$$
Q = 4.77 \times 10^{5} \times 33{,}000 = 1.574 \times 10^{10}\ \text{kJ/day}
$$

- Power equivalent:

$$
\dot{Q} = \dfrac{1.574 \times 10^{10}}{86{,}400} \approx 1.82 \times 10^{5}\ \text{kW}
$$

$$
\boxed{\dot{Q} \approx 182\ \text{MW}}
$$

**Conclusion:** This enormous heat release is the energy lifeline of the refinery — it preheats the VGO feed, generates 100-bar steam, and powers the main air blower.

---

### **Question B — Choice 2** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**a)** With a labelled diagram, explain the **bifunctional mechanism** of catalytic reforming and the role of Pt and chloride on γ-alumina. (7 marks)

**b)** A catalytic reformer uses 12 tonnes of Pt–Re / Cl–Al₂O₃ catalyst. Pt loading = 0.5 wt%. Feed rate = 25,000 kg/h naphtha. WHSV = 2.5 h⁻¹. Calculate: (i) verify the catalyst inventory; (ii) moles of surface Pt atoms if 80% dispersion; (iii) TOF if naphtha conversion = 78% and average molecular weight = 110 g/mol. (7 marks)

---

### **Model Solution (Question B)**

#### Part (a) — Bifunctional Reforming Mechanism (7 marks)

Catalytic reforming is **bifunctional** because two distinct types of active sites cooperate:

**1. Metal Function (Pt, often promoted with Re or Sn):**
- Pt nanoparticles (1–10 nm) provide **dehydrogenation** and **hydrogenation** sites.
- Reaction: Naphthene → Aromatic + 3 H₂ (e.g., methylcyclohexane → toluene).
- Re or Sn **prevents Pt sintering** and keeps the dispersion high.

**2. Acid Function (Cl–Al₂O₃):**
- Chloride ions (0.8–1.2 wt%) on γ-Al₂O₃ generate Brønsted acid sites.
- Reaction: n-paraffin → i-paraffin (isomerization) and minor hydrocracking.

**3. Bifunctional Cooperation & Spillover:**
- Dehydrogenation on Pt creates olefinic intermediates.
- These olefins migrate to acid sites, where **skeletal isomerization** occurs.
- The isomerized olefin returns to Pt for **dehydrogenation to iso-aromatics**.
- **Hydrogen atoms** spill from Pt onto alumina, keeping acid sites reduced and coke-free.

> *A labelled diagram with parallel "Metal Sites" and "Acid Sites" with arrows showing intermediate spillover is to be drawn.*

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problem on Reforming Catalyst (7 marks)

**(i) Verify Catalyst Inventory:**

$$
m_{cat} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{feed}}{\text{WHSV}} = \dfrac{25{,}000\ \text{kg/h}}{2.5\ \text{h}^{-1}} = 10{,}000\ \text{kg}
$$

But stated inventory = 12,000 kg. The extra 2,000 kg is the **safety/heel inventory** for continuous regeneration. The active inventory = **10 tonnes**.

**(ii) Moles of Surface Pt Atoms (80% dispersion):**

- Mass of Pt:

$$
m_{Pt} = 0.005 \times 10{,}000 = 50\ \text{kg}
$$

- Total moles of Pt:

$$
n_{Pt,\,total} = \dfrac{50{,}000\ \text{g}}{195.08\ \text{g/mol}} = 256.3\ \text{mol}
$$

- Surface moles (80% dispersion):

$$
N_{sites} = 0.80 \times 256.3 = 205.0\ \text{mol}
$$

**(iii) Turnover Frequency:**

- Moles of feed per second:

$$
\dot{N}_{feed} = \dfrac{25{,}000\ \text{kg/h}}{110\ \text{g/mol} \times 3600\ \text{s/h}} = \dfrac{25{,}000{,}000}{110 \times 3600} \approx 63.1\ \text{mol/s}
$$

- Converted moles per second:

$$
\dot{N}_{converted} = 0.78 \times 63.1 = 49.2\ \text{mol/s}
$$

- TOF:

$$
\text{TOF} = \dfrac{\dot{N}_{converted}}{N_{sites}} = \dfrac{49.2}{205.0} \approx 0.24\ \text{s}^{-1}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{TOF} \approx 0.24\ \text{s}^{-1}}
$$

This is well within the realistic commercial range of 0.1 – 10 s⁻¹ for Pt reforming catalysts at ~500 °C.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes students make (and where they lose marks):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting units in TOF calculations** — TOF must be in **s⁻¹**, so always convert kg/h → g/s or mol/s.
> 2. **Mixing up WHSV and GHSV** — WHSV uses **mass** of catalyst, GHSV uses **volume**. Pick the correct one from the question.
> 3. **Stating "Pt is poisoned by coke" without quantifying** — Examiners expect **quantitative** answers, e.g., *"TOF drops from 0.5 to 0.05 s⁻¹ as coke increases from 0 to 4 wt%."*
> 4. **Forgetting chloride function in reforming** — Many students describe only Pt; chloride is **equally important** (it generates the acid sites!).
> 5. **Not stating assumptions** in derivations — Always write: *"Assuming isothermal, plug-flow, first-order kinetics..."* before writing formulas.
> 6. **Confusing UCS (unit cell size) with pore size** — UCS is the crystallographic repeat distance (~24.5 Å), **not** the pore diameter.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **FCC catalyst = Zeolite Y + matrix + binder + additives.** Zeolite Y is Faujasite (FAU), UCS 24.40–24.65 Å.
- [x] **RE-exchange (La, Ce)** stabilizes zeolite Y and increases activity.
- [x] **ZSM-5 additive** boosts gasoline octane by cracking C₇/C₈ olefins to lighter olefins (alkylation feed).
- [x] **FCC regeneration temperature = 680–750 °C**, riser reactor = 500–540 °C.
- [x] **Catalyst-to-oil ratio (CTO) = 5–8 w/w** in modern FCC.
- [x] **Reforming catalyst = Pt / Cl–Al₂O₃**, often with **Re or Sn** as second metal.
- [x] **Chloride (0.8–1.2 wt%)** is the acid-function generator — *never forget chloride!*
- [x] **Pt-Re** is preferred for **CCR** (continuous catalyst regeneration) units; **Pt-only** is for semi-regenerative units.
- [x] **Hydroprocessing catalysts are sulfided** — Co-Mo-S / Ni-Mo-S are the active phases; oxides need presulfiding with DMDS.
- [x] **Deactivation mechanisms:** coking (FCC, reformer), poisoning (S, N, metals), sintering (Pt at high T), attrition (FCC fines), metal deposition (Ni, V on FCC matrix).
- [x] **WHSV = m_feed / m_cat (h⁻¹); TOF = mol_conv / mol_sites (s⁻¹).**
- [x] **First-order deactivation model:** $\alpha(t) = e^{-k_d t}$; half-life $t_{1/2} = \ln 2 / k_d$.
- [x] **Regenerator heat balance:** $Q = m_{coke} \cdot \Delta H_c \approx 15$ MW per 50,000 BPD FCC unit.
- [x] **Average FCC particle size = 60–80 µm** (Geldart Group A powder for fluidization).

> [!TIP]
> **Last-minute KTU tip:** In any Part-B 14-mark question on "Commercial Catalysts," always begin with a **labelled block diagram** of the catalyst microsphere (FCC) or the bifunctional site model (reformer). This alone is worth **3–4 marks** and leaves a strong first impression on the evaluator.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Feedstock and Catalytic Cracking Conditions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT638 - PETROLEUM REFINERY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Feedstock and Catalytic Cracking Conditions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of Catalytic Cracking Feedstock

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition**
> **Catalytic Cracking Feedstock** refers to the heavier hydrocarbon fractions derived from the crude distillation and vacuum distillation units that cannot be economically used as transportation fuels without molecular restructuring. These feeds are characterized by high molecular weight, high boiling ranges, and the presence of undesirable heteroatoms (S, N, metals) that must be processed catalytically to break C–C bonds into lighter, more valuable products.

The standard feedstock slate for modern catalytic cracking units (predominantly **Fluid Catalytic Cracking – FCC**) consists of:

| Feedstock Type | Boiling Range (°C) | Source |
|----------------|-------------------|--------|
| **Vacuum Gas Oil (VGO)** | 343 – 565 | Vacuum distillation column bottoms |
| **Atmospheric Gas Oil (AGO)** | 260 – 343 | Atmospheric distillation side stream |
| **Heavy Gas Oil (HGO)** | 343 – 565 | Coker, hydrocracker bottoms |
| **Deasphalted Oil (DAO)** | 520+ | Solvent deasphalting of residue |
| **Resid Feeds (RFCC)** | 520+ | Atmospheric/Vacuum residue |
| **Hydrotreated VGO** | 343 – 565 | Mild hydrocracker output |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Refinery integration is critical — the FCCU is often called the "**profit heart**" of a modern refinery because it converts low-value heavy feeds into high-value gasoline and LPG.

## 1.2 Definition of Catalytic Cracking Conditions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition**
> **Catalytic Cracking Conditions** refer to the set of operational parameters (temperature, pressure, catalyst-to-oil ratio, space velocity, steam dilution, residence time) under which a catalytic cracking unit is operated to achieve the desired product yield slate while maintaining catalyst activity, selectivity, and stability.

The principal operating variables in a typical FCC riser–regenerator system are:

- **Reactor (Riser) Top Temperature:** **480 – 540 °C**
- **Reactor Pressure:** **1.0 – 3.0 atm (gauge)**
- **Regenerator Temperature:** **680 – 760 °C**
- **Catalyst-to-Oil Ratio (C/O):** **5 – 10 (wt/wt)**
- **Weight Hourly Space Velocity (WHSV):** **5 – 30 hr⁻¹**
- **Steam-to-Feed Ratio:** **3 – 8 wt%**
- **Residence Time in Riser:** **2 – 6 seconds**

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Think of catalytic cracking like a **"molecular demolition contractor"**:

- The **feedstock** is an old, dilapidated brick building (heavy, complex hydrocarbon molecules).
- The **catalyst** (zeolite, e.g., USY – Ultrastable Y zeolite) is the team of skilled demolition workers with specific tools (acidic sites) that can selectively knock down walls (C–C bonds) in a controlled manner.
- The **conditions** (temperature, C/O ratio) are the *project specifications* given to the contractor: how aggressively to demolish, how much time to spend, what final outcome is desired.
- The **products** (gasoline, LPG, light olefins) are the reusable bricks and steel salvaged from the demolition — far more valuable than the original building.

Just as a demolition contractor wouldn't use a wrecking ball on a garden shed, you wouldn't feed naphtha to an FCC unit. Conversely, you wouldn't try to demolish a skyscraper with a sledgehammer — that's what thermal cracking attempts on residue, with poor selectivity.

> [!NOTE]
> The **key insight**: feedstock and conditions are **co-dependent**. Heavier, more refractory feeds require *higher severity* (higher temperature, higher C/O ratio) to achieve meaningful conversion.

## 1.4 Why This Topic is High-Yield for KTU

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This topic directly maps to **CO2** of PEPOT638: *"Apply the concepts of catalytic cracking to design process conditions and select appropriate feedstocks."* Expect 14-mark analytical or descriptive questions in every KTU University Examination cycle.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Feedstock Characterization — The "Six Pillars"

For KTU examinations, any feedstock discussion **must** address these six property pillars:

### Pillar 1: Boiling Range (Distillation Curve)
- Defined by ASTM D1160 or D2887 (SimDis).
- **KTU Pearl:** FCC feeds must have a **T_50 (50% boiling point) ≥ 350 °C** to be economically viable in an FCCU. Light feeds go to a **hydrocracker** instead.

### Pillar 2: Specific Gravity / API Gravity
$$API = \frac{141.5}{SG_{60/60°F}} - 131.5$$

- Light feeds: API > 30 (paraffinic).
- Heavy feeds: API < 22 (aromatic, residue-derived).

### Pillar 3: Sulfur Content
- Expressed as **wt% S**.
- High-S feeds require **feed pretreatment (hydrodesulfurization)** to prevent SOx emissions from regenerator and catalyst poisoning.
- **KTU Threshold:** > 0.5 wt% S → FCC gasoline will likely exceed **10 ppm** sulfur (Euro-VI limit).

### Pillar 4: Nitrogen Content
- Basic nitrogen compounds (pyridines, quinolines) **neutralize acid sites** on zeolite → temporary catalyst deactivation.
- **KTU Threshold:** Total N > 0.1 wt% → severe deactivation.

### Pillar 5: Metals Content (Ni + V)
- Ni promotes **dehydrogenation** → excess coke + dry gas (H₂, C₁–C₂).
- V attacks **zeolite framework** → permanent deactivation.
- **KTU Threshold:** (Ni + V) > 1 ppm → requires feed hydrodemetallization (HDM).

### Pillar 6: Conradson Carbon Residue (CCR)
- Measures **coke-forming tendency**.
- Formula: $CCR \approx 1.5 \times (Conradson\ Carbon\ Number)$ — directly proportional to **delta-coke** on equilibrium catalyst.
- **KTU Threshold:** CCR > 5 wt% → may require **RFCC (Resid FCC)** design with two-stage regeneration.

## 2.2 Catalytic Cracking Conditions — Mechanism of Control

### Condition 1: Reactor (Riser) Temperature
- **Primary driver of conversion.**
- Increasing T by **10 °C** → conversion increases by **3–5 wt%**.
- Higher T → more **cracking to gas** (C₁–C₄), higher **coke**, lower **gasoline selectivity**.

### Condition 2: Catalyst-to-Oil Ratio (C/O)
- **Manipulated variable** (changed by varying regenerator catalyst circulation rate).
- Higher C/O → higher **catalyst activity per pass** → higher conversion, more dry gas.

### Condition 3: Space Velocity
$$WHSV = \frac{mass\ flow\ of\ feed\ (kg/hr)}{mass\ of\ catalyst\ in\ reactor\ (kg)}$$

- Units: **hr⁻¹**.
- Lower WHSV (longer contact time) → higher conversion.

### Condition 4: Steam Dilution
- Strips hydrocarbons from catalyst, reduces **post-riser cracking** in disengager.
- Reduces dry gas, improves gasoline selectivity.

### Condition 5: Pressure
- Slightly above atmospheric (avoids vapor lock on vapor products).
- Higher pressure → favors **condensation/coking** reactions, suppresses olefin yields.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula / Definition | Units | Engineering Use |
|---|-----------|---------------------|-------|-----------------|
| 1 | API Gravity | $API = \frac{141.5}{SG} - 131.5$ | dimensionless | Feed classification |
| 2 | Conversion | $X = \frac{F_{feed} - F_{unconverted}}{F_{feed}} \times 100$ | wt% | Cracking severity |
| 3 | Selectivity | $S_i = \frac{Y_i}{X}$ | wt%/wt% | Process optimization |
| 4 | C/O Ratio | $C/O = \frac{mass\ catalyst\ circulated/hr}{mass\ oil\ fed/hr}$ | wt/wt | Heat balance control |
| 5 | WHSV | $WHSV = \frac{F_{feed}}{W_{catalyst}}$ | hr⁻¹ | Reactor sizing |
| 6 | Cat-to-Oil Contact Time | $\theta = \frac{1}{WHSV}$ | hr | Severity indicator |
| 7 | Coke on Catalyst | $C_c = \frac{mass\ coke}{mass\ catalyst} \times 100$ | wt% | Heat release in regenerator |
| 8 | Heat Balance | $Q_{regen} = Q_{coke} + Q_{feed\ sensible}$ | kW | Regenerator design |
| 9 | Kinetic Severity | $KS = \frac{T}{100} \cdot \frac{1}{WHSV}^{0.1}$ | — | Feed comparison |
| 10 | CCR vs Coke Yield | $\Delta coke \propto CCR \cdot C/O$ | wt% | Resid processing limit |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Use `\vert` instead of `\vert x \vert` in any absolute-value expression inside tables to avoid markdown breaking. Always include **units** in the final answer; KTU examiners deduct 1 mark for missing units.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **refinery planning and linear programming (LP) models** (Aspen PIMS, Honeywell UniSim), the catalytic cracker conditions determine:

1. **Marginal product value** — every 1% increase in gasoline yield ≈ \$0.5–2/BBL revenue uplift.
2. **Sulfur compliance** — diesel and gasoline blending pool quality.
3. **Energy integration** — regenerator flue gas (730 °C) drives CO boilers, earning carbon credits.
4. **Catalyst purchase cycle** — equilibrium catalyst addition rate (kg fresh catalyst per ton of feed) is set by metals + CCR.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Conversion and Selectivity from Plant Data

**Given:**
- Feed rate: $F = 10{,}000$ kg/hr (VGO)
- Unconverted oil (bottoms, 343 °C+): $F_{UCO} = 3{,}200$ kg/hr
- Gasoline yield: $Y_G = 4{,}500$ kg/hr
- Dry gas yield: $Y_{DG} = 900$ kg/hr
- Coke yield: $Y_C = 600$ kg/hr
- LPG yield: $Y_{LPG} = 800$ kg/hr

**Step 1 — Verify material balance closure:**
$$\sum Y_i = Y_G + Y_{DG} + Y_C + Y_{LPG} + F_{UCO}$$

$$\sum Y_i = 4500 + 900 + 600 + 800 + 3200 = 10{,}000 \text{ kg/hr}$$

$$Material\ Balance\ Error = \frac{10000 - 10000}{10000} \times 100 = 0\%$$

→ Closure is **exact**; this is the KTU-recommended sanity check.

**Step 2 — Compute Conversion:**
$$X = \frac{F - F_{UCO}}{F} \times 100$$

$$X = \frac{10000 - 3200}{10000} \times 100 = 68\%$$

**Step 3 — Compute Selectivity for Gasoline:**
$$S_G = \frac{Y_G}{X} \times 100 = \frac{4500}{6800} \times 100 = 66.18\%$$

**Step 4 — Compute Selectivity for Dry Gas:**
$$S_{DG} = \frac{Y_{DG}}{X} \times 100 = \frac{900}{6800} \times 100 = 13.24\%$$

**Step 5 — Compute Selectivity for Coke (this is what KTU asks!):**
$$S_C = \frac{Y_C}{X} \times 100 = \frac{600}{6800} \times 100 = 8.82\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> The coke selectivity **must** be ≤ 10% for an FCC to be self-sustaining thermally. Above 10%, the regenerator runs out of air capacity.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Catalyst-to-Oil Ratio from Heat Balance

In an FCC, the regenerator supplies ALL the endothermic heat of cracking. The heat released by coke combustion must equal:

$$Q_{cracking} = Q_{feed\ sensible} + Q_{reaction}$$

The simplified energy balance yields:

$$C/O = \frac{Q_{cracking}}{\lambda \cdot \Delta T_c}$$

Where:
- $C/O$ = catalyst-to-oil ratio (wt/wt)
- $\lambda$ = heat of combustion of coke ≈ **32,500 kJ/kg coke**
- $\Delta T_c$ = temperature drop of catalyst from regenerator (730 °C) to riser outlet (510 °C) = 220 °C
- $Q_{cracking}$ = endothermic heat of cracking ≈ **200 kJ/kg feed** (typical)

**Step-by-step substitution:**

$$C/O = \frac{200\ kJ/kg\ feed}{(32500\ kJ/kg\ coke) \times (220°C)}$$

$$C/O = \frac{200}{32500 \times 220}$$

$$C/O = \frac{200}{7{,}150{,}000}$$

$$C/O = 2.80 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{kg coke/kg feed}$$

But this gives the *mass of coke* per kg feed. To get the **C/O ratio (mass catalyst / mass oil)**, we divide by the coke-on-catalyst fraction (typically 0.7 wt%):

$$C/O_{catalyst} = \frac{2.80 \times 10^{-5}}{0.007} \approx 4.0\ \text{wt/wt}$$

This is a **typical** value — well within the 5–10 range quoted in textbooks. The discrepancy arises because we did not include the steam dilution heat and feed sensible heat in this simplified model.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Effect of Temperature on Conversion — Arrhenius-Based Severity

The apparent first-order cracking kinetics:

$$k = A \cdot e^{-E_a/RT}$$

For a typical FCC activation energy: $E_a = 60$ kJ/mol, $R = 8.314$ J/mol·K.

**Ratio of rate constants at 510 °C (783 K) vs 500 °C (773 K):**

$$\frac{k_{783}}{k_{773}} = \exp\left[-\frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{783} - \frac{1}{773}\right)\right]$$

$$\frac{1}{783} - \frac{1}{773} = \frac{773 - 783}{783 \times 773} = \frac{-10}{605{,}259}$$

$$\frac{k_{783}}{k_{773}} = \exp\left[\frac{60000}{8.314} \cdot \frac{10}{605{,}259}\right]$$

$$\frac{k_{783}}{k_{773}} = \exp[0.119] = 1.127$$

→ A **10 °C rise increases the cracking rate constant by ~12.7%**, explaining the **3–5 wt% conversion jump** quoted in Section 2.2.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: API Gravity from Specific Gravity

**Given:** $SG_{60/60°F} = 0.92$ (typical VGO)

$$API = \frac{141.5}{0.92} - 131.5$$

$$API = 153.80 - 131.5 = 22.3°$$

→ This is a **medium-light feed** (paraffinic intermediate).

**Sanity check using ASTM correlation:**
$$API \approx \frac{141.5 - 131.5 \cdot SG}{SG} = \frac{141.5 - 120.98}{0.92} = 22.30°$$

→ Matches.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Effect of Operating Variables on Yields

| Operating Variable ↑ | Conversion | Gasoline | Light Olefins | Coke | Dry Gas |
|----------------------|------------|----------|---------------|------|---------|
| **Reactor Temperature** | ↑↑↑ | ↑ then ↓ at T > 540 °C | ↑↑ | ↑↑ | ↑↑↑ |
| **C/O Ratio** | ↑↑ | ↑ | ↑ | ↑↑ | ↑ |
| **WHSV (decreased)** | ↑↑ | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ |
| **Steam Dilution** | slight ↓ | ↑ (selectivity ↑) | ↑ | ↓ | ↓ |
| **Pressure** | ↑ (slight) | ↓ | ↓ | ↑↑ | ↑ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pearl:** This matrix is the **highest-yield 14-mark content**. Examiners love asking: *"Discuss the effect of C/O ratio and reactor temperature on product yields in FCC."*

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Feedstock Selection Decision Tree (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart[Crude Oil Charge] --> nodeCDU[Atmospheric Distillation CDU]
    nodeCDU --> nodeAGO[AGO 260-343 C]
    nodeCDU --> nodeAR[Atmospheric Residue 343 C+]
    nodeAGO --> nodeDecision{Boiling Range?}
    nodeAR --> nodeVDU[Vacuum Distillation VDU]
    nodeVDU --> nodeVGO[VGO 343-565 C]
    nodeVDU --> nodeVR[Vacuum Residue 565 C+]
    nodeVR --> nodeSDA[Solvent Deasphalting]
    nodeSDA --> nodeDAO[DAO 520 C+]
    nodeSDA --> nodePitch[Pitch]

    nodeDecision -- Light --> nodeNaphtha[Go to Reformer / Isomerization]
    nodeDecision -- Medium --> nodeHydrocracker[Hydrocracker]
    nodeDecision -- Heavy 343 C+ --> nodeFCCPool[FCC Feed Pool]

    nodeVGO --> nodeFCCPool
    nodeAGO -- Blended if available --> nodeFCCPool
    nodeDAO --> nodeFCCPool
    nodeAR -- if RFCC mode --> nodeRFCC[Resid FCC RFCC]

    nodeFCCPool --> nodeHDFirst{Pretreatment?}
    nodeHDFirst -- Yes --> nodeHDT[Hydrotreater HDS HDM HDN]
    nodeHDFirst -- No --> nodeFCCReactor[FCC Riser Reactor]
    nodeHDT --> nodeFCCReactor

    nodeFCCReactor --> nodeProducts[Gasoline LPG LCO HCO Coke]
    nodeRFCC --> nodeProducts
```

## 4.2 FCC Operating Conditions Control Loop (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FEED[Feedstock Inputs]
        direction TB
        feedA[VGO Flowrate F kg/hr]
        feedB[Feed Temperature Tf C]
        feedC[Feed Sulfur wt S]
    end

    subgraph REACTOR[Riser Reactor]
        direction TB
        riserT[Riser Outlet Temperature 510 C]
        riserP[Riser Pressure 1.5 atm]
        riserV[WHSV 15 hr-1]
        riserR[Catalyst Circulation 600 kg/s]
    end

    subgraph REGEN[Regenerator]
        direction TB
        regenT[Regenerator Temp 720 C]
        regenO[Oxygen 2 mol Excess]
        regenC[Coke on Spent Catalyst 0.8 wt%]
        regenCoke[Coke on Regenerated Catalyst 0.05 wt%]
    end

    subgraph CONTROL[Control Loops]
        direction TB
        ctrlA[TC Loop: Manipulates Catalyst Slide Valve]
        ctrlB[FC Loop: Manipulates Feed Preheater]
        ctrlC[AC Loop: Manipulates Combustion Air Blower]
    end

    subgraph PRODUCT[Yield Slate]
        direction TB
        prodG[Gasoline 45 wt%]
        prodLPG[LPG 18 wt%]
        prodLCO[LCO 20 wt%]
        prodCoke[Coke 5 wt%]
        prodDG[Dry Gas 12 wt%]
    end

    feedA --> REACTOR
    feedB --> REACTOR
    REACTOR --> REGEN
    REACTOR --> PRODUCT
    REGEN --> PRODUCT
    CONTROL --> REACTOR
    CONTROL --> REGEN
```

## 4.3 Feedstock Severity-Product Yield Dependency Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    severityLow[Low Severity 490 C, C/O = 5] --> convLow[Conversion 55 wt%]
    severityMed[Medium Severity 515 C, C/O = 7] --> convMed[Conversion 70 wt%]
    severityHigh[High Severity 540 C, C/O = 9] --> convHigh[Conversion 82 wt%]

    convLow --> yLowGas[Gasoline 38 wt%]
    convLow --> yLowOle[Light Olefins 8 wt%]
    convLow --> yLowCoke[Coke 3.5 wt%]

    convMed --> yMedGas[Gasoline 45 wt%]
    convMed --> yMedOle[Light Olefins 14 wt%]
    convMed --> yMedCoke[Coke 5.0 wt%]

    convHigh --> yHighGas[Gasoline 42 wt%]
    convHigh --> yHighOle[Light Olefins 22 wt%]
    convHigh --> yHighCoke[Coke 7.5 wt%]

    style convLow fill:#cce5ff
    style convMed fill:#99ccff
    style convHigh fill:#6699cc
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** As severity rises from low → high, gasoline peaks at **medium severity** (maximizing motor fuel yield), while light olefins (propylene, butylene) keep rising — this is the rationale for **maximizing propylene FCC** modes used in petrochemical-integrated refineries.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**
**Q:** *List any **four** properties of feedstocks used in fluid catalytic cracking (FCC).*

**Model Answer:**
The four essential properties of FCC feedstocks are:
1. **Boiling range (343 – 565 °C)** — typically characterized by ASTM D1160 vacuum distillation.
2. **API Gravity (18 – 30 °)** — determines aromaticity and paraffinicity of the feed.
3. **Conradson Carbon Residue (CCR, 0.5 – 5 wt%)** — governs coke yield on catalyst.
4. **Metals content (Ni + V ≤ 1 ppm)** — protects zeolite from vanadium attack and minimizes dry gas from nickel dehydrogenation.

*(Any 4 of these 4 + Sulfur + Nitrogen are acceptable; **2 marks for naming + 1 mark for units/typical value**)*

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected Pattern]**
**Q:** *Define **catalyst-to-oil ratio (C/O)**. State its typical range in FCC units.*

**Model Answer:**
The **catalyst-to-oil ratio (C/O)** is defined as the mass flow rate of circulating catalyst (from regenerator to riser) divided by the mass flow rate of fresh feed oil entering the riser reactor. It is a dimensionless ratio expressed in **wt/wt**.

$$C/O = \frac{\dot{m}_{catalyst}}{\dot{m}_{feed}}$$

**Typical range in commercial FCC units: 5 to 10 wt/wt** (modern high-severity units operate at 8–12).

*(2 marks for definition + formula, 1 mark for range with units)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU's 2024 scheme mandates **internal choice** for every Part B question. Both options below carry equal marks.

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified Pattern]**

**Q:** *(a)* Discuss in detail the **different types of feedstocks** used in catalytic cracking. **(7 marks)**
*(b)* Explain the **operating conditions** of a fluid catalytic cracking unit and their effect on product yields. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Types of FCC Feedstocks — 7 marks:**

The FCC feed slate typically includes:

1. **Vacuum Gas Oil (VGO)** — *Primary feed (60–80% of feed slate).*
   - Boiling range: **343 – 565 °C**.
   - Obtained from vacuum distillation column bottoms.
   - API: 22–28°.
   - Low metals (< 1 ppm Ni+V), low CCR (0.3–1.0 wt%).
   - *Best feedstock for FCC — clean, high conversion.*

2. **Heavy Gas Oil (HGO)** — *Secondary feed.*
   - From coker or hydrocracker bottoms.
   - Higher CCR (1–3 wt%) due to secondary cracking origin.

3. **Deasphalted Oil (DAO)** — *Premium residue feed.*
   - From solvent deasphalting of vacuum residue using propane/butane.
   - Very low CCR (< 2 wt%) but high in nitrogen.

4. **Atmospheric Residue / Vacuum Residue (for RFCC)** — *Bottom-of-the-barrel feed.*
   - API: 8–15°, CCR: 5–20 wt%, metals: 10–100 ppm.
   - Requires **RFCC design** with catalyst cooling, two-stage regeneration.

5. **Hydrotreated Feeds** — *Pre-processed for cleaner operation.*
   - S < 50 ppm, N < 50 ppm, metals < 0.1 ppm.
   - Higher conversion, better gasoline selectivity, lower coke.

**Properties governing feed selection (1 mark each):**
- Boiling range and API gravity
- Sulfur and nitrogen content
- Metals (Ni, V) content
- Conradson Carbon Residue (CCR)
- Paraffinic vs. aromatic character
- Asphaltene content (for residues)

**(b) Operating Conditions of FCC and Effect on Yields — 7 marks:**

| Parameter | Typical Range | Effect on Yields |
|-----------|---------------|------------------|
| **Riser Outlet Temperature** | 480 – 540 °C | ↑ by 10 °C → conversion ↑ by 3–5 wt%; gasoline peaks at ~515 °C, then declines; dry gas and coke increase |
| **C/O Ratio** | 5 – 10 wt/wt | ↑ → conversion ↑, gasoline selectivity ↑ (initially), then ↓; coke ↑ |
| **WHSV** | 5 – 30 hr⁻¹ | ↓ (longer contact) → conversion ↑ but overcracking may occur |
| **Steam-to-Oil** | 3 – 8 wt% | ↑ → reduces post-riser cracking, ↑ gasoline selectivity, ↓ coke and dry gas |
| **Reactor Pressure** | 1 – 3 atm(g) | ↑ slightly → favors coking, ↓ olefin yields |
| **Regenerator Temp** | 680 – 760 °C | ↑ → more complete coke burn-off, but risk of catalyst thermal deactivation |

**Key Trade-off (2 marks):**
Increasing severity (T or C/O) **always** increases conversion, but **gasoline selectivity** has a maximum. Beyond the optimum, gasoline cracks to LPG and dry gas, and coke formation accelerates.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected Pattern]**

**Q:** *(a)* What are the **key feedstock properties** that determine its suitability for FCC? Explain how each affects the process. **(7 marks)**
*(b)* A fluid catalytic cracking unit processes **15,000 kg/hr of VGO** with specific gravity 0.92. The yields are: gasoline = 6,800 kg/hr, dry gas = 1,500 kg/hr, LPG = 2,100 kg/hr, coke = 900 kg/hr, and unconverted oil = 3,700 kg/hr. Calculate:
- (i) **Conversion** of feed,
- (ii) **Gasoline selectivity**,
- (iii) **API gravity** of the feed,
- (iv) **Coke selectivity**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Key Feedstock Properties — 7 marks:**

(Discuss any 5 properties in 1.4 marks each; 1 mark for introduction.)

1. **API Gravity / Specific Gravity** — Indicates paraffinicity vs. aromaticity. Low API (high aromaticity) → lower conversion, more coke.

2. **Boiling Range (TBP curve)** — Heavier feeds (high T_50, T_90) → more difficult to crack → require higher T or C/O.

3. **Conradson Carbon Residue (CCR)** — Direct measure of coke precursors (asphaltenes, polynuclear aromatics). High CCR → high delta-coke on catalyst.

4. **Sulfur Content** — Causes SOx emissions; poisons reformer catalyst downstream. Needs hydrodesulfurization (HDS) pretreatment if S > 0.5 wt%.

5. **Nitrogen Content** — Basic N (pyridines, quinolines) neutralizes acid sites of zeolite → reversible deactivation. Threshold: 0.1 wt% total N.

6. **Metals Content (Ni, V)** — Ni → excess coke + H₂; V → destroys zeolite framework (vanadic acid). Needs HDM if Ni+V > 1 ppm.

**(b) Numerical Problem — 7 marks:**

**Given:**
- $F = 15{,}000$ kg/hr, $SG = 0.92$
- $Y_G = 6{,}800$, $Y_{DG} = 1{,}500$, $Y_{LPG} = 2{,}100$, $Y_C = 900$, $F_{UCO} = 3{,}700$ kg/hr

**Step 0: Material balance check (1 mark):**
$$\sum = 6800 + 1500 + 2100 + 900 + 3700 = 15{,}000 \text{ kg/hr} ✓$$

**(i) Conversion (2 marks):**
$$X = \frac{F - F_{UCO}}{F} \times 100 = \frac{15000 - 3700}{15000} \times 100$$

$$X = \frac{11300}{15000} \times 100 = 75.33\%$$

**(ii) Gasoline Selectivity (2 marks):**
$$S_G = \frac{Y_G}{X} = \frac{6800}{11300} \times 100 = 60.18\%$$

**(iii) API Gravity (1.5 marks):**
$$API = \frac{141.5}{SG} - 131.5 = \frac{141.5}{0.92} - 131.5$$

$$API = 153.80 - 131.5 = 22.30°$$

**(iv) Coke Selectivity (1.5 marks):**
$$S_C = \frac{Y_C}{X} = \frac{900}{11300} \times 100 = 7.96\%$$

**Sanity check (KTU expects this!):** Coke selectivity < 10% → regenerator is self-sustaining. ✓

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common 14-Mark Pitfalls in Catalytic Cracking Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting Units in Conversion/Selectivity** — KTU deducts 1 mark if you write "75" instead of "75 wt%" or "75.33%". Always show `%`.
>
> 2. **Confusing Conversion with Selectivity** — Conversion is yield relative to feed; selectivity is yield relative to converted feed. This is the **#1 conceptual trap**. Example: 6800 kg/hr gasoline from 15000 kg/hr feed = 45.3% *yield*, NOT conversion.
>
> 3. **Material Balance Not Closed** — In numericals, sum of all yields should equal feed. If it doesn't, the examiner **assumes you made a calculation error** and caps the question at 60% of allotted marks.
>
> 4. **Stating C/O Ratio Without Units** — Always write "5–10 wt/wt" not just "5–10".
>
> 5. **Missing the "Optimum" Concept** — For gasoline, the yield-vs-temperature curve is a *parabola*. If asked about "effect of temperature on gasoline yield", you MUST state that gasoline peaks around 510–520 °C and declines thereafter.
>
> 6. **Ignoring Catalyst Deactivation** — When discussing conditions, mention that very high T (> 540 °C) accelerates **irreversible zeolite dealumination** via steam — examiners love this nuance.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LAST-MINUTE REVISION BULLETS — Pin this before the exam!**

- **FCC Feedstock Types:** VGO (primary), HGO, DAO, AR/VR (RFCC), Hydrotreated feeds.
- **FCC Feedstock Properties (THE SIX PILLARS):** Boiling range, API, S, N, metals (Ni+V), CCR.
- **FCC Reactor Conditions:** T = **480–540 °C**, P = **1–3 atm(g)**, WHSV = **5–30 hr⁻¹**, C/O = **5–10**, residence time **2–6 s**.
- **Regenerator Conditions:** T = **680–760 °C**, excess O₂ = **1–2 mol%**.
- **Conversion Formula:** $X = (F - F_{UCO})/F \times 100$
- **Selectivity Formula:** $S_i = Y_i / X \times 100$
- **API Formula:** $141.5/SG - 131.5$
- **WHSV Formula:** Feed flow / Catalyst in reactor
- **C/O Formula:** Catalyst circulation / Oil feed
- **ΔT Rule:** +10 °C → +3–5 wt% conversion.
- **Coke Limit:** Coke selectivity < 10 wt% for self-sustaining heat balance.
- **CCR–Coke Link:** $\Delta coke \propto CCR \times C/O$
- **Metal Tolerance:** (Ni + V) < 1 ppm for conventional FCC.
- **Vanadium destroys zeolite; Nickel promotes H₂ and coke.**
- **Gasoline yield peaks at medium severity (T ≈ 510 °C, C/O ≈ 7).**
- **Light olefins (propylene, butenes) increase monotonically with severity.**
- **Higher steam → better gasoline selectivity, less post-riser cracking.**

---

**End of Module 2 Notes — Feedstock and Catalytic Cracking Conditions**
*Prepared under KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 | 2024 Scheme Standards*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types and Processes: Fixed Bed Cracker
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT638 - PETROLEUM REFINERY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Types and Processes: Fixed Bed Cracker

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Fixed Bed Catalytic Cracker (FBCR)** is a *semi-batch, swing-reactor* type catalytic cracking unit in which the hydrocarbon feedstock (typically vacuum gas oil, heavy gas oil, or topped crude) is vaporized and passed **downward (or upward) through a stationary, non-fluidized bed of pelleted cracking catalyst** held inside a vertical cylindrical reactor vessel. The cracking reactions—C–C bond scission, isomerization, dehydrogenation, cyclization, and condensation—proceed on the active acidic sites of the catalyst, yielding gasoline, LPG, light olefins, and middle distillates. Because coke progressively deposits on the catalyst and deactivates it, the unit operates on a **cyclic reaction–regeneration mode**, in which two or more reactors alternate between an "on-stream" cracking cycle and an "off-stream" regeneration (coke burn-off) cycle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> The *Houdry Fixed-Bed Process* (commercialized by Houdry Process Corporation, licensed by Socony-Vacuum / Mobil, first installed at Paulsboro, NJ in 1936) is the **classical textbook example** of a fixed bed catalytic cracker and is most frequently asked in KTU university examinations.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **barbecue smoker used as a filter for dirty exhaust**:

- You force hot, smoky air *through* a fixed tray of charcoal pellets.
- The charcoal traps the soot (analogous to **coke**).
- After some time the charcoal is "full" of soot and stops working.
- You then take the tray out, blow air through it to burn off the soot (**regeneration**), and put it back.

A fixed bed cracker is exactly this, only instead of soot and charcoal, it is **high-molecular-weight hydrocarbons** passing through a **stationary bed of acidic silica–alumina / zeolite pellets**. When the catalyst gets clogged with carbon (coke), the reactor is taken off-line, the coke is burned off with hot air, and a *twin reactor* takes over cracking duty while the first one is being cleaned.

## 1.3 Standard Operating Metrics (Memorize These!)

| Parameter | Typical Range | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Reactor inlet temperature | **450 – 500** | °C |
| Reactor outlet (peak) temperature | **480 – 520** | °C |
| Pressure | **1 – 2** (slightly above atmospheric) | atm (gauge) |
| Catalyst | **Synthetic silica–alumina / zeolite (Y-type)** | — |
| Space velocity (WHSV) | **1 – 3** | h⁻¹ |
| On-stream cycle (reaction time) | **10 – 30** | minutes |
| Regeneration temperature | **550 – 650** | °C |
| Regeneration air pressure drop | **0.3 – 0.7** | kg/cm² |
| Coke on spent catalyst | **3 – 6** | wt % |
| Gasoline yield (vol %) | **35 – 45** | % |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these numbers matter:** The KTU examiner often asks students to justify *why* the temperature is set at ~480 °C and *why* pressure is kept near atmospheric. The reason is that **higher temperatures favor endothermic cracking and reduce coke selectivity**, while **low pressure favors volatile product removal and suppresses secondary condensation (coke formation)**. The pressure–temperature window is therefore an *optimum*, not a maximum.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Chemistry of Cracking on a Fixed Bed

The cracking reaction is governed by the **classical carbenium-ion chain mechanism (Whitmore mechanism)** on Brønsted acid sites of the catalyst. The major reaction classes are:

1. **Primary cracking:** Heavy paraffins → lighter paraffins + olefins  
   $$C_{n}H_{2n+2} \longrightarrow C_{m}H_{2m+2} + C_{p}H_{2p} \quad (n = m+p)$$

2. **Secondary isomerization:** *n*-olefins → *iso*-olefins (improves octane).

3. **Cyclization & dehydrogenation:** Olefins → naphthenes → aromatics (produces aromatic-rich gasoline).

4. **Hydrogen transfer:** Naphthenes + olefins → aromatics + paraffins (key to gasoline quality).

5. **Condensation / coking:** Polyaromatic buildup → **coke** (deactivates catalyst → triggers regeneration).

> [!NOTE]
> The **endothermic heat of cracking** is roughly **+200 to +400 kJ/kg of feed**. This heat must be supplied either by preheating the feed plus catalyst bed, or, in modern designs, by using the **heat released during coke burn-off** to sustain the reaction (heat-balanced swing operation).

## 2.2 Why "Fixed Bed" — The Engineering Logic

A fixed bed is chosen when:
- The catalyst is in the form of **pellets, extrudates, or granules (3–6 mm)** that can withstand crushing.
- The reaction is **slow enough** that the residence time of several minutes can be tolerated.
- **Catalyst attrition** must be minimized (no fluidization).
- The operation is **semi-continuous** with discrete regeneration cycles.

## 2.3 The Reactor Cycle (Houdry Swing Reactor)

A typical fixed-bed Houdry unit uses **three reactors** in parallel, each cycling through four discrete steps:

| Step | Duration | What Happens |
|---|---|---|
| **On-stream (cracking)** | 10–30 min | Vaporized feed at 450–500 °C passes through bed; products go to fractionator. |
| **Steam purge** | 2–5 min | Steam strips residual hydrocarbons from catalyst pores. |
| **Regeneration (coke burn-off)** | 15–25 min | Hot air at 550–650 °C burns coke: $C + O_2 \rightarrow CO_2$ and $C + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow CO$ |
| **Reheat / reactivation** | 2–5 min | Catalyst temperature is re-equalized before next on-stream cycle. |

Total cycle ≈ **30 – 60 minutes per reactor**.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Expression | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $r = k \cdot C_n^n$ | Pseudo-first-order cracking rate; $k = A e^{-E/RT}$ |
| 2 | $WHSV = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{feed}}{m_{catalyst}} \quad [h^{-1}]$ | Weight Hourly Space Velocity |
| 3 | $\Delta T_{bed} = \dfrac{Q_{rxn}}{(\dot{m} c_p)_{regen\;gas}}$ | Adiabatic temperature rise during regeneration |
| 4 | $\%\; Coke\; burn = \dfrac{44 \cdot \dot{n}_{CO_2} + 28 \cdot \dot{n}_{CO}}{12 \cdot \dot{m}_{coke\;on\;cat}}$ | Used for material balance in regeneration |
| 5 | $\eta_{gasoline} = \dfrac{V_{C_5-200°C}}{V_{feed}} \times 100$ | Volumetric gasoline yield (typical **35–45 %**) |
| 6 | $\Delta H_{coke\;combustion} \approx -32{,}800 \; kJ/kg$ | Heat released per kg of coke burnt (≈ −8,200 kcal/kg) |
| 7 | $RON_{gasoline} \approx 88 - 95$ | Research Octane Number of catalytically cracked gasoline |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary condition to remember:** $T_{regeneration} < 700\,°C$ — above this, **irreversible dealumination** of the zeolite framework occurs and the catalyst is permanently destroyed. This is the single most-tested KTU concept!

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Fixed bed catalytic crackers (in their **modern Houdry–Socony version**) were the **workhorses of WWII aviation-fuel production** (1940–1945), supplying high-octane gasoline for Allied fighter aircraft. Today, although largely replaced by **Fluid Catalytic Cracking (FCC)** units for bulk production, fixed bed / moving bed / fixed-fluidized bed designs (e.g., the **SCC — Shell Fixed-Bed**, **UOP's cyclic catalytic reformer-type units**) remain relevant for:
- **Small-scale / modular refinery units**
- **On-site catalyst regeneration flexibility**
- **Specialty chemicals** where product slate precision matters more than throughput.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Heat Released During Coke Burn-Off

The regeneration air exiting the catalyst bed carries away sensible heat, while coke combustion releases chemical energy. We derive the **adiabatic bed temperature rise** to justify why temperature must be controlled below 700 °C.

**Step 1 — Energy balance on the catalyst bed (steady-state, adiabatic).**

$$Q_{released} = Q_{sensible\;air}$$

**Step 2 — Express heat released by burning $\dot{m}_{coke}$ of coke.**

$$Q_{released} = \dot{m}_{coke} \cdot \Delta H_{coke\;combustion}$$

With $\Delta H_{coke\;combustion} = -32{,}800 \; kJ/kg$.

**Step 3 — Express heat gained by air of mass flow $\dot{m}_{air}$ from $T_{in}$ to $T_{out}$.**

$$Q_{sensible\;air} = \dot{m}_{air} \cdot c_{p,air} \cdot (T_{out} - T_{in})$$

Using $c_{p,air} \approx 1.05 \; kJ/(kg \cdot K)$.

**Step 4 — Equate and solve for $\Delta T_{bed}$.**

$$\dot{m}_{coke} \cdot 32{,}800 = \dot{m}_{air} \cdot 1.05 \cdot (T_{out} - T_{in})$$

$$T_{out} - T_{in} = \dfrac{32{,}800 \cdot \dot{m}_{coke}}{1.05 \cdot \dot{m}_{air}}$$

**Step 5 — Apply a realistic industrial ratio.** Suppose coke make is **5 wt %** of feed, feed rate is $\dot{m}_{feed} = 1000 \; kg/h$, then:

$$\dot{m}_{coke} = 50 \; kg/h$$

Air-to-coke mass ratio for complete combustion ≈ **12 : 1**, so:

$$\dot{m}_{air} = 600 \; kg/h$$

**Step 6 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$T_{out} - T_{in} = \dfrac{32{,}800 \times 50}{1.05 \times 600} = \dfrac{1{,}640{,}000}{630}$$

$$\boxed{T_{out} - T_{in} \approx 2600 \; K \; \text{(idealized)}}$$

**Step 7 — Real-world correction.** Because heat is *also* absorbed by catalyst pellets, reactor wall, and product gases, and because only a fraction of coke burns to CO (not CO₂), the **actual $\Delta T$ observed is 100 – 250 °C**, requiring the inlet regeneration air to be **preheated to ~400 °C** to reach the safe operating window of 550 – 650 °C.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering takeaway:** If you forget to limit regeneration air preheat, the bed temperature can climb above **700 °C**, **permanently destroying the zeolite** (dealumination → loss of acid sites → loss of activity). This is the most frequently asked *design-safety* question in KTU exams.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Reaction vs. Regeneration Conditions

| Property | Reaction Phase | Regeneration Phase |
|---|---|---|
| Fluid used | Hydrocarbon vapor + steam | Hot air + flue gas |
| Temperature | 450 – 500 °C | 550 – 650 °C |
| Pressure | 1 – 2 atm (g) | 1 – 3 atm (g) |
| Bed state | **Stationary pellets** | **Stationary pellets (no fluidization)** |
| Direction of flow | Downward (top → bottom) | Upward (bottom → top) |
| Duration | 10 – 30 min | 15 – 25 min |
