| Net heat effect | **Endothermic** (+200 to +400 kJ/kg) | **Exothermic** (−32,800 kJ/kg coke) |
| Product of interest | Gasoline, LPG, gas oil | Flue gas (CO₂ + CO) |
| Coke level (start → end) | 0.5 % → 5 % | 5 % → 0.1 % |

## 3.3 Mass Balance Snapshot (KTU Favourite)

For **1000 kg feed of vacuum gas oil (VGO)** processed in a fixed-bed cracker:

- Gasoline yield: **≈ 420 kg** (≈ 45 vol %)
- Light cycle oil (LCO): **≈ 200 kg**
- Heavy cycle oil (HCO): **≈ 100 kg**
- LPG (C₃ + C₄): **≈ 80 kg**
- Dry gas (C₁ + C₂ + H₂): **≈ 60 kg**
- **Coke deposited on catalyst: ≈ 50 kg**  
- Loss / water: **≈ 90 kg**

Check: $420 + 200 + 100 + 80 + 60 + 50 + 90 = 1000 \; kg$ ✓

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow: Houdry Fixed-Bed Cracker — Reactor Cycle and Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Feedstock VGO + recycle HCO] --> A2[Fired Heater 350 to 420 C vaporizer]
    A2 --> A3{Stream Distributor Manifold}
    A3 --> R1[Reactor 1 ON STREAM cracking 450 to 500 C 10 to 30 min]
    A3 --> R2[Reactor 2 ON STREAM cracking 450 to 500 C 10 to 30 min]
    A3 --> R3[Reactor 3 ON STREAM cracking 450 to 500 C 10 to 30 min]
    R1 --> B1[Reactor 1 STEAM PURGE 2 to 5 min]
    B1 --> C1[Reactor 1 REGENERATION coke burn off 550 to 650 C 15 to 25 min]
    C1 --> D1[Reactor 1 REHEAT ready for next cycle]
    R2 --> B2[Reactor 2 STEAM PURGE]
    B2 --> C2[Reactor 2 REGENERATION]
    C2 --> D2[Reactor 2 REHEAT]
    R3 --> B3[Reactor 3 STEAM PURGE]
    B3 --> C3[Reactor 3 REGENERATION]
    C3 --> D3[Reactor 3 REHEAT]
    R1 --> E1[Product Vapor to Main Fractionator]
    R2 --> E1
    R3 --> E1
    E1 --> E2[Gasoline C5 to 200 C]
    E1 --> E3[LCO Light Cycle Oil]
    E1 --> E4[HCO Heavy Cycle Oil recycle]
    E1 --> E5[LPG and Dry Gas to Gas Concentration]
    C1 --> F1[Flue Gas CO2 plus CO to Atmosphere via Stack]
    C2 --> F1
    C3 --> F1
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Functional Architecture of a Single Swing Reactor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Inlet Header vapor feed] --> B[Top Distributor Tray]
    B --> C[Catalyst Bed Zone 1 top section 3 m of pellets]
    C --> D[Catalyst Bed Zone 2 middle section 3 m of pellets]
    D --> E[Catalyst Bed Zone 3 bottom section 3 m of pellets]
    E --> F[Collector Hopper and Outlet to Fractionator]
    G[Air Blower preheated air 400 C] --> H[Regeneration Inlet Plenum]
    H --> I[Check Valve prevents backmixing of feed and air]
    I --> E
    E --> J[Cyclone Separator removes fines from flue gas]
    J --> K[Stack to Atmosphere]
    F --> L[Quench Zone rapid cooling to stop secondary reactions]
    L --> M[Main Fractionator]
```

## 4.3 Reading the Diagrams

- The **three reactors** rotate through the four steps so that **at any instant one reactor is on-stream**, one is being purged, and one is being regenerated — giving *continuous* overall throughput despite *batch* operation per reactor.
- The **catalyst bed height** is segmented into multiple zones (3 m each) to keep pressure drop below ≈ 0.5 kg/cm² per reactor; otherwise pellets would crush.
- The **cyclone at the outlet** captures entrained catalyst fines — vital because in fixed beds there is no continuous catalyst withdrawal, so fines accumulation is a *failure mode* KTU examiners love to ask about.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU image-question pattern:** A labelled sketch of the Houdry reactor with four zones (distributor, catalyst bed, collector, regenerator dome) is a **2-mark** mandatory sketch question in every KTU Dec/Dec 2022 / May 2023 supplementary exam paper. Practice drawing it from memory!

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

### Q1. Define "Fixed Bed Catalytic Cracking". Mention any two characteristic features. `[3 marks]`

**Model Answer:**
Catalytic cracking carried out in a reactor containing a *stationary, non-fluidized* bed of pelleted catalyst through which vaporized feedstock passes is called **Fixed Bed Catalytic Cracking**.  
*Characteristic features:*
1. The catalyst remains immobile inside the reactor; feedstock flows through it.
2. Reaction and regeneration occur in the *same vessel* on a time-cycled basis (swing-reactor operation).

### Q2. Why is the regeneration temperature in a fixed-bed cracker restricted to ≤ 700 °C? `[3 marks]`

**Model Answer:**
Above **700 °C**, the zeolite (Y-type faujasite) component of the catalyst undergoes irreversible **dealumination** — aluminium atoms are expelled from the crystalline framework, destroying the Brønsted acid sites that drive cracking. The catalyst loses activity permanently and must be replaced. Hence regeneration is held at **550–650 °C**, with strict temperature alarms.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Internal Choice — 14 Marks)

### ⭐ Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam — May 2023 model paper]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **Houdry fixed-bed catalytic cracking process**. Label the major equipment and state typical operating conditions. **\[7 marks\]**

**(b)** Discuss the **reaction and regeneration cycles** of the fixed-bed cracker. Why is regeneration necessary, and how is the heat released during regeneration utilized? **\[7 marks\]**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The Houdry fixed-bed catalytic cracker consists of three vertical cylindrical reactors, each packed with pelleted silica–alumina or Y-zeolite catalyst (3–6 mm pellets, bed height ≈ 9–12 m). Vaporized feedstock (VGO + recycle HCO) preheated in a fired heater to **400–420 °C** is distributed by a manifold to one reactor. The bed is maintained at **450–500 °C**, slightly above atmospheric pressure (**1–2 atm gauge**). Cracking occurs as vapor flows downward; products leave the bottom through a collector and enter a main fractionator, where they are separated into dry gas, LPG, gasoline, LCO and HCO.  

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Reaction cycle (10–30 min):** Cracking proceeds via the carbenium-ion mechanism on the catalyst's acid sites. The reaction is mildly endothermic (+200 to +400 kJ/kg of feed). Coke progressively deposits, reducing activity.  

**Regeneration cycle (15–25 min):** Once coke reaches 3–6 wt %, feed is cut off and the bed is purged with steam to remove hydrocarbons. Preheated air (~400 °C) is then admitted; coke burns:  

$$C + O_2 \rightarrow CO_2 \quad \Delta H \approx -32{,}800 \; kJ/kg$$

The released heat (a) raises the catalyst temperature from ~500 °C to ~620 °C, (b) preheats the incoming feed-air stream via internal heat exchange, and (c) generates high-pressure steam in waste-heat boilers. Regeneration **restores catalyst activity** and the reactor returns to the on-stream cycle. **Necessity:** Without regeneration, coke would choke active sites, pressure drop would rise, and conversion would collapse.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students *describe the Houdry process without naming it*. Always mention "**Houdry**" and give the **operating temperature range explicitly** in °C. Examiners explicitly award 1 mark for naming the process and 1 mark for quoting the **450–500 °C** window. Skipping either loses you 2 marks.

---

### ⭐ Question B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 supplementary paper]`

**(a)** Compare **fixed-bed catalytic cracking** with **fluidized-bed catalytic cracking (FCC)** under the headings: (i) catalyst state, (ii) regenerator design, (iii) gasoline yield, (iv) operating flexibility, and (v) typical unit capacity. **\[7 marks\]**

**(b)** A fixed-bed cracker processes **1000 kg/h of VGO** with **5 wt % coke make** on catalyst. The regeneration air is supplied at a **12 : 1 mass ratio (air : coke)**. Calculate the **theoretical adiabatic temperature rise** of the catalyst bed. Take $c_{p,air} = 1.05 \; kJ/(kg \cdot K)$ and $\Delta H_{coke} = -32{,}800 \; kJ/kg$. State any **two assumptions**. **\[7 marks\]**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — Comparison Table

| S.No. | Feature | Fixed Bed | Fluidized Bed (FCC) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Catalyst state | Stationary pellets | Continuously circulating fine powder |
| 2 | Regenerator design | Same reactor used (swing mode) | Separate regenerator vessel with air grid |
| 3 | Gasoline yield (vol %) | 35–45 | 50–65 (higher) |
| 4 | Operating flexibility | Low (fixed cycles) | High (variable catalyst circulation) |
| 5 | Unit capacity (typical) | 500 – 5,000 TPD | 10,000 – 100,000 TPD |
| 6 | Catalyst replacement | Once every 1–3 years | Continuous makeup + withdrawal |

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — Numerical

**Given:**  
- $\dot{m}_{feed} = 1000 \; kg/h$  
- Coke make = 5 wt % ⇒ $\dot{m}_{coke} = 50 \; kg/h$  
- Air-to-coke mass ratio = 12 : 1 ⇒ $\dot{m}_{air} = 600 \; kg/h$  
- $\Delta H_{coke} = 32{,}800 \; kJ/kg$  
- $c_{p,air} = 1.05 \; kJ/(kg \cdot K)$

**Step 1 — Heat released by combustion.**  

$$Q_{rel} = \dot{m}_{coke} \times \Delta H = 50 \times 32{,}800 = 1{,}640{,}000 \; kJ/h$$

**Step 2 — Sensible heat gained by air.**  

$$Q_{air} = \dot{m}_{air} \times c_p \times \Delta T = 600 \times 1.05 \times \Delta T = 630 \; \Delta T$$

**Step 3 — Equate $Q_{rel} = Q_{air}$ (adiabatic assumption).**  

$$630 \; \Delta T = 1{,}640{,}000$$

$$\Delta T = \dfrac{1{,}640{,}000}{630}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta T \approx 2603 \; K}$$

**Step 4 — Apply realistic correction factor.**  
In practice, only a fraction of coke burns (≈ 70 %), and heat is absorbed by catalyst pellets and walls. The **observed bed temperature rise is 100 – 250 °C**, requiring **inlet air preheat to ~400 °C** to maintain bed at 550–650 °C.

**Assumptions:**  
1. **Adiabatic** reactor walls (no heat loss).  
2. **Complete combustion** of coke to CO₂ (no CO formation).  
3. Constant $c_p$ for air over the temperature range.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Numerical answers are graded on **units + correct setup + assumption statement**. If you write the final number but skip the **two assumptions** in part (b), you lose **2 marks** outright. *Always* write assumptions even if you think they are "obvious".

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- ✅ Fixed bed catalytic cracking = **stationary pellet bed** + **swing reaction/regeneration cycle**.
- ✅ Industrial prototype = **Houdry process** (1936, Paulsboro, USA).
- ✅ Operating window: **T = 450–500 °C**, **P ≈ 1–2 atm (g)**.
- ✅ Catalyst: **silica–alumina** (early) → **Y-zeolite** (modern); pellet size 3–6 mm.
- ✅ Gasoline yield: **35–45 vol %**, RON ≈ **88–95**.
- ✅ Regeneration: **air at 550–650 °C**; never exceed **700 °C** (zeolite destruction).
- ✅ Heat of coke combustion ≈ **−32,800 kJ/kg** — drives bed temperature rise.
- ✅ Cycle sequence: **ON-STREAM → STEAM PURGE → REGENERATION → REHEAT**.
- ✅ Multiple reactors (typically 3) operate in parallel so that overall throughput is *continuous*.
- ✅ Fixed bed largely **superseded by FCC** for bulk refining; still used in **small/modular units**.
- ✅ Always quote the **Houdry name** + **temperature range** + **regeneration limit** in KTU answers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Mantra for this Topic:**
> *"Houdry, three reactors, 480 °C cracking, 620 °C regeneration, never cross 700 °C — that's the whole answer."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fluid Catalytic Cracking (FCC)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT638 - PETROLEUM REFINERY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fluid Catalytic Cracking (FCC)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fluid Catalytic Cracking (FCC)** is a continuous, high-temperature, heterogeneous catalytic conversion process used in petroleum refineries to break down high-molecular-weight hydrocarbon fractions (typically vacuum gas oil, VGO, and heavier residues) into more valuable, lower-molecular-weight products such as gasoline, diesel, LPG, and light olefins. The "fluid" in FCC refers to the fact that the solid catalyst behaves as a pseudo-fluid when aerated with vapor or steam, enabling continuous circulation between the **riser reactor** (where cracking occurs) and the **regenerator** (where coke is burned off).

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, FCC is classified as a **secondary conversion process** that upgrades the bottom-of-the-barrel streams into transportation fuels with significantly higher economic value.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Factory Conveyor Belt" Analogy:**
> Imagine a factory where you have a giant rock (heavy oil) that you want to break into smaller, useful pebbles (gasoline, diesel). Instead of using a hammer (thermal cracking — which is violent and uncontrolled), you use a special chemical "sharpener" (catalyst) that slides along a conveyor belt. The rock passes over the sharpener at high speed (riser reactor, residence time 2-4 seconds), gets sliced into uniform pebbles, and exits. The sharpener gets dull because it gets coated with black grime (coke). So the conveyor belt carries the dull sharpener to a furnace (regenerator) where the grime is burned off, making it sharp again — ready to slice more rocks. This is exactly how FCC works.

## 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU Board-Standard)

| Term | Definition | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Riser** | Vertical pipe where feed + catalyst mix and crack | Reaction zone, residence time ~2-4 s |
| **Regenerator** | Fluidized bed where coke is burned in air | Restores catalyst activity |
| **Catalyst-to-Oil Ratio (CTO)** | Mass flow rate of catalyst / mass flow rate of feed | Typically 5-8 in modern units |
| **WHSV** | Weight Hourly Space Velocity (h⁻¹) | Defines throughput per catalyst mass |
| **Coke** | Carbonaceous deposit on catalyst | Deactivates catalyst, must be burned |
| **Yields** | Mass % of products (gasoline, LCO, slurry, gas, coke) | Determines economics |

> [!TIP]
> **Remember:** FCC is a **catalytic** process, not thermal. The presence of an acidic solid catalyst (zeolite) lowers the activation energy dramatically and allows operation at **lower temperatures (480-540 °C)** compared to thermal cracking (800-900 °C).

## 1.4 Why FCC Exists in a Refinery

Modern crude oil yields only **15-25% gasoline** by simple distillation. World demand for gasoline, however, is enormous. FCC is the **workhorse conversion unit** that converts the **~50% vacuum residue + VGO fraction** of crude into high-octane gasoline. A modern FCC unit processes **20,000 to 100,000 barrels per day** of feed.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Chemistry of Catalytic Cracking

The cracking mechanism follows the **classical carbenium ion (carbocation) theory** on the acidic Brønsted sites of the zeolite catalyst.

### 2.1.1 The β-scission mechanism (step-by-step)

1. **Initiation:** A hydrocarbon (R-H) is protonated by a Brønsted acid site on the zeolite, forming a **carbenium ion** (R⁺).

   $$R-H + H^+(zeolite) \rightarrow R^+ + H_2(zeolite)$$

2. **Isomerization:** The carbenium ion rearranges to a more stable form (secondary → tertiary) via hydride shift.

3. **β-scission (the main cracking step):** The C–C bond **two carbons away from the positive carbon** breaks, releasing an **olefin** and a **smaller carbenium ion**.

   $$C_{12}H_{26}^+ \rightarrow C_5H_{11}^+ + C_7H_{14}(olefin)$$

4. **Termination:** The carbenium ion either loses a proton (forming another olefin) or abstracts a hydride from a fresh feed molecule, propagating the chain.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Olefins Form:** Because the mechanism proceeds through carbenium ions, FCC products are **rich in branched paraffins and olefins** — both of which contribute to high **Research Octane Number (RON = 90-95)**. This is why FCC gasoline is the **highest-octane gasoline blending stock** in a refinery.

## 2.2 The FCC Catalyst

A modern FCC catalyst is a finely divided powder (avg. particle size **60-80 µm**, Geldart Group A powder) with the following architecture:

| Layer | Component | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Core** | Kaolin clay matrix | Provides heat capacity, attrition resistance |
| **Binder** | Silica / Alumina gel | Holds the particle together |
| **Active component** | **Zeolite Y (Faujasite)** | Provides acidic sites for cracking (main activity) |
| **Additives** | ZSM-5 | Boosts LPG / propylene yield |
| **Additives** | SOx / NOx traps | Environmental compliance |
| **Additives** | CO promoter (Pt) | Complete CO → CO₂ in regenerator |

> [!NOTE]
> The zeolite Y supercage has a pore opening of **~7.4 Å**. This shape-selectivity rejects large polynuclear aromatics from entering the active sites, which is why FCC is selective for cracking long-chain aliphatics rather than resids directly (though residue FCC uses matrix acidity).

## 2.3 Process Flow Description

A typical FCC unit has **three main sections**:

### Section A — Riser-Reactor Section
- Preheated feed (**315-400 °C**) is injected at the base of the riser through **feed nozzles**.
- Hot regenerated catalyst (**650-730 °C**) from the regenerator enters the riser bottom and mixes with feed.
- Feed vaporizes instantaneously on contact with hot catalyst (heat balance).
- Cracking proceeds as the catalyst-oil mixture travels **up the riser in 2-4 seconds**.
- At the riser top, products + spent catalyst are quickly separated in a **cyclone / disengager** to **terminate overcracking**.

### Section B — Regenerator Section
- Spent catalyst (laden with **0.8-1.2 wt% coke**) flows down a **stripper** where steam strips out residual hydrocarbons.
- The stripped catalyst enters the regenerator, a **fluidized bed** operating at **680-730 °C** with **5-10 mol% O₂ in air**.
- Coke is burned: 
  $$C_{coke} + O_2 \rightarrow CO_2 \quad (\Delta H \approx -32{,}800\ \text{kJ/kg coke})$$
- The exothermic heat raises catalyst temperature back to ~720 °C, providing all the heat required for feed vaporization and endothermic cracking reactions in the riser.

### Section C — Fractionation Section
- Cracked vapors enter a **main fractionator** (similar to a crude distillation tower) where they are separated by boiling point:
  - **Wet Gas** (C1–C4) → goes to gas concentration unit (LPG recovery)
  - **Gasoline** (C5 – 221 °C) → Naphtha blending
  - **LCO** (Light Cycle Oil, 221-343 °C) → Diesel blending (poor cetane, needs upgrading)
  - **HCO / Decant Oil / Slurry** (>343 °C) → Fuel oil or feedstock to coker

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations for ESE problems.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Y_i = \dfrac{m_i}{\sum m_{products}} \times 100$ | Product yield (mass basis) | wt% |
| 2 | $CTO = \dfrac{F_{catalyst}}{F_{feed}}$ | Catalyst-to-oil ratio | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\eta = \dfrac{m_{gasoline}}{m_{feed\ converted}} \times 100$ | Gasoline selectivity | wt% |
| 4 | $\Delta T_{reg} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{coke} \cdot \Delta H_{comb}}{F_{cat} \cdot C_{p,cat}}$ | Regenerator temperature rise | °C |
| 5 | $C/O = \dfrac{12 \cdot W_{C,spent}}{M_{feed}}$ | Carbon-to-oil ratio for heat balance | — |
| 6 | $WHSV = \dfrac{F_{feed}}{W_{catalyst\ in\ bed}}$ | Weight Hourly Space Velocity | h⁻¹ |
| 7 | $X = \dfrac{F_{in} - F_{out}}{F_{in}} \times 100$ | Conversion of feed (typically defined as 221 °C⁻ conversion) | wt% |
| 8 | $\Delta H_{cracking,net} = \sum Y_i \Delta H_{f,i} - \Delta H_{f,feed}$ | Heat of cracking (negative = endothermic) | kJ/kg |
| 9 | $E_{reg} = \dot{m}_{coke} \cdot \Delta H_{comb,coke}$ | Energy released in regenerator | kW |
| 10 | $T_{mix} = \dfrac{F_c C_{pc} T_c + F_o C_{po} T_o}{F_c C_{pc} + F_o C_{po}}$ | Catalyst-oil mix temperature | °C |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Refinery Economics:** FCC is the **highest profit-margin unit** in a refinery, converting cheap heavy feeds into high-value gasoline + petrochemical feedstocks (propylene via ZSM-5 additive).
- **Petrochemical Integration:** Modern FCC units with **ZSM-5 additive** can produce **6-9 wt% propylene**, directly feeding polypropylene plants.
- **Emissions Control:** Modern FCC regenerators use **CO promoters, SOx transfer additives, and NSCR (Non-Selective Catalytic Reduction)** to meet environmental norms.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Problem 1 — Material Balance & Yield Calculation (Type: 10-Mark Analytical)

### Problem Statement
An FCC unit processes **50,000 kg/h** of vacuum gas oil (VGO) feed. The product distribution (by mass) is:
- Dry gas (C1–C2): 3.0 wt%
- LPG (C3–C4): 12.0 wt%
- Gasoline (C5–221 °C): 48.0 wt%
- LCO (221–343 °C): 18.0 wt%
- HCO / Slurry (>343 °C): 12.0 wt%
- Coke: 7.0 wt%

Calculate:
(a) The mass flow rate of each product in kg/h.
(b) The **conversion** (defined as feed converted to products boiling below 221 °C).
(c) The **gasoline selectivity** based on total feed.

### Step-by-Step Model Solution

**(a) Mass flow rate of each product**

The total mass balance must close. Verify: 
$$3.0 + 12.0 + 48.0 + 18.0 + 12.0 + 7.0 = 100.0\ \text{wt\%} \quad \checkmark$$

For each product, multiply feed flow rate by the yield fraction:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_{dry\ gas} &= 50{,}000 \times 0.030 = 1{,}500\ \text{kg/h} \\[4pt]
\dot{m}_{LPG} &= 50{,}000 \times 0.120 = 6{,}000\ \text{kg/h} \\[4pt]
\dot{m}_{gasoline} &= 50{,}000 \times 0.480 = 24{,}000\ \text{kg/h} \\[4pt]
\dot{m}_{LCO} &= 50{,}000 \times 0.180 = 9{,}000\ \text{kg/h} \\[4pt]
\dot{m}_{HCO} &= 50{,}000 \times 0.120 = 6{,}000\ \text{kg/h} \\[4pt]
\dot{m}_{coke} &= 50{,}000 \times 0.070 = 3{,}500\ \text{kg/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification (mass balance closure):**
$$\sum \dot{m}_{products} = 1500 + 6000 + 24000 + 9000 + 6000 + 3500 = 50{,}000\ \text{kg/h} \quad \checkmark$$

**(b) Conversion (221 °C⁻ cut point)**

Conversion is the fraction of feed converted to products boiling **below 221 °C**. In FCC convention, this includes dry gas + LPG + gasoline.

$$
\begin{aligned}
X &= \frac{F_{in} - F_{unconverted\ (>221°C)}}{F_{in}} \times 100 \\[4pt]
  &= \frac{\dot{m}_{dry\ gas} + \dot{m}_{LPG} + \dot{m}_{gasoline}}{F_{feed}} \times 100 \\[4pt]
  &= \frac{1500 + 6000 + 24000}{50000} \times 100 \\[4pt]
  &= \frac{31{,}500}{50{,}000} \times 100 \\[4pt]
  &= 63.0\ \text{wt\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(c) Gasoline selectivity (based on total feed)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{gasoline} &= \frac{\dot{m}_{gasoline}}{F_{feed}} \times 100 \\[4pt]
                &= \frac{24{,}000}{50{,}000} \times 100 \\[4pt]
                &= 48.0\ \text{wt\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Alternatively, "selectivity based on converted feed"** is sometimes defined as:
> $$\eta'_{gasoline} = \frac{\dot{m}_{gasoline}}{F_{feed} \cdot X} \times 100 = \frac{24{,}000}{50{,}000 \times 0.63} \times 100 = 76.2\%$$
> KTU boards accept both interpretations; **state your definition clearly**.

## 3.2 Problem 2 — Regenerator Heat Balance (Type: 14-Mark Derivation)

### Problem Statement
An FCC regenerator burns coke from spent catalyst. Given:
- Catalyst circulation rate: $F_{cat} = 600{,}000\ \text{kg/h}$
- Coke on spent catalyst: $W_C = 0.85\ \text{wt\%}$ (of catalyst mass)
- Spent catalyst enters regenerator at $T_{c,in} = 510\ \degree C$
- Specific heat of catalyst: $C_{p,cat} = 1.10\ \text{kJ/(kg·K)}$
- Air enters at $T_{air,in} = 200\ \degree C$, $C_{p,air} = 1.05\ \text{kJ/(kg·K)}$
- Air mass flow rate: $F_{air} = 75{,}000\ \text{kg/h}$
- Flue gas exits at $T_{flue,out} = 720\ \degree C$, $C_{p,flue} = 1.10\ \text{kJ/(kg·K)}$
- Coke heat of combustion: $\Delta H_{comb} = -32{,}800\ \text{kJ/kg coke}$ (exothermic)
- Assume coke is pure carbon, and combustion is complete: $C + O_2 \rightarrow CO_2$

Calculate:
(a) Mass of coke burned per hour.
(b) Heat released by coke combustion per hour.
(c) Regenerated catalyst temperature, $T_{cat,out}$ (assuming no heat losses).

### Solution

**(a) Mass of coke burned per hour**

The mass of coke entering with the catalyst per hour:
$$
\dot{m}_{coke} = F_{cat} \times W_C = 600{,}000 \times 0.0085 = 5{,}100\ \text{kg coke/h}
$$

In steady state, all coke entering is burned (complete combustion), so the mass burned per hour = **5,100 kg/h**.

**(b) Heat released by coke combustion per hour**

$$
Q_{comb} = \dot{m}_{coke} \times \vert \Delta H_{comb} \vert = 5100 \times 32{,}800
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{comb} &= 5{,}100 \times 32{,}800 \\[4pt]
        &= 167{,}280{,}000\ \text{kJ/h} \\[4pt]
        &= 1.673 \times 10^8\ \text{kJ/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(c) Regenerated catalyst temperature (Energy balance on regenerator)**

Energy balance on the regenerator (steady state, no heat loss):
$$Q_{comb} + Q_{cat,in} + Q_{air,in} = Q_{cat,out} + Q_{flue,out}$$

Computing each enthalpy term:
$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{cat,in} &= F_{cat} \cdot C_{p,cat} \cdot T_{c,in} = 600{,}000 \times 1.10 \times 510 = 3.366 \times 10^8\ \text{kJ/h} \\[4pt]
Q_{air,in} &= F_{air} \cdot C_{p,air} \cdot T_{air,in} = 75{,}000 \times 1.05 \times 200 = 1.575 \times 10^7\ \text{kJ/h} \\[4pt]
Q_{flue,out} &= F_{flue} \cdot C_{p,flue} \cdot T_{flue,out}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Air mass balance:** Air in ≈ Flue gas out (since O₂ consumed equals mass of O in CO₂ formed from 5,100 kg C).

Mass of O₂ consumed = $5{,}100 \times \frac{32}{12} = 13{,}600\ \text{kg/h}$.

So flue gas mass flow: $F_{flue} \approx 75{,}000 - 13{,}600 = 61{,}400\ \text{kg/h}$ (approximately, with nitrogen dominating).

$$
Q_{flue,out} = 61{,}400 \times 1.10 \times 720 = 4.864 \times 10^7\ \text{kJ/h}
$$

Now solve for $T_{cat,out}$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{cat,out} &= Q_{comb} + Q_{cat,in} + Q_{air,in} - Q_{flue,out} \\[4pt]
1.673 \times 10^8 &+ 3.366 \times 10^8 + 1.575 \times 10^7 - 4.864 \times 10^7 \\[4pt]
&= (1.673 + 3.366 + 0.1575 - 0.4864) \times 10^8 \\[4pt]
&= 4.710 \times 10^8\ \text{kJ/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
T_{cat,out} = \frac{Q_{cat,out}}{F_{cat} \cdot C_{p,cat}} = \frac{4.710 \times 10^8}{600{,}000 \times 1.10}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{cat,out} &= \frac{4.710 \times 10^8}{6.60 \times 10^5} \\[4pt]
            &= 713.6\ \degree C
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineer's Insight:** This $T_{cat,out} \approx 714\ \degree C$ is exactly the **hot regenerated catalyst temperature** that flows back to the riser to provide the heat for endothermic cracking. This is the heart of FCC's autothermal design — **no external furnace fuel is needed** for cracking heat.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Operating Variables

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is a high-yield KTU board topic. Memorize the trends.**

| Operating Variable ↑ | Conversion | Gasoline Yield | Coke Yield | RON |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Reactor Temperature ↑** | ↑↑ | ↑ (then ↓ due to overcracking) | ↑↑ | ↑ |
| **CTO Ratio ↑** | ↑ | ↑ (peaks then drops) | ↑↑ | Slight ↑ |
| **WHSV ↓ (more contact time)** | ↑ | ↓ (overcracking) | ↑ | ↓ |
| **Feed Heavier ↑** | — | ↓ | ↑↑↑ | ↑ |
| **ZSM-5 Additive ↑** | — | ↓ | — | ↑↑ (more iso-olefins) |
| **Catalyst Activity ↑** | ↑ | ↑ | — | ↑ |

**Rule of thumb:** Increasing reactor temperature by **5 °C ≈ 1% more conversion**, but **also ~0.5% more coke**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Flow Diagram — Modern FCC Unit (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    VGOFeed[Vacuum Gas Oil Feed, 315 to 400 C] --> FeedNozzle[Feed Injection Nozzles, Riser Base]
    HCR[Hot Regenerated Catalyst, 700 to 730 C] --> FeedNozzle
    FeedNozzle --> Riser[Riser Reactor, Residence 2 to 4 s, 510 to 540 C]
    Riser --> Disengager[Cyclone Disengager, Quick Separation to Stop Cracking]
    Disengager --> Vapors[Cracked Vapors to Main Fractionator]
    Disengager --> SpentCat[Spent Catalyst with 0.8 to 1.2 wt% Coke]
    SpentCat --> Stripper[Steam Stripper, 510 C, Removes Residual HC]
    Stripper --> Regen[Fluidized Bed Regenerator, 680 to 730 C]
    RegAir[Combustion Air, 5 to 10 percent O2] --> Regen
    Regen --> FlueStack[Flue Gas with CO2 to Stack, with ESP and FGD]
    Regen --> HCR
    Vapors --> Frac[Main Fractionator, Distillation Column]
    Frac --> WetGas[Wet Gas, C1 to C4, to Gas Plant]
    Frac --> Gasoline[Gasoline, C5 to 221 C, RON 90 to 95]
    Frac --> LCO[Light Cycle Oil, 221 to 343 C, to Diesel Pool]
    Frac --> Slurry[Decant Oil / Slurry, above 343 C, to Fuel Oil or Coker]
    Regen --> CatCooler[External Catalyst Cooler, Optional, for Heat Removal]
    CatCooler --> HCR
    styleRiser fill=#ffd166 stroke:#333 stroke-width:2px
    styleRegen fill:#ef476f stroke:#333 stroke-width:2px color:#fff
    styleFrac fill:#06d6a0 stroke:#333 stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this:** The catalyst loop is shown in red. The hydrocarbon path is shown in green/yellow. Note the **closed loop** between Riser → Stripper → Regenerator → Riser — this is the heart of FCC's continuous operation.

## 4.2 Riser-Reactor Internal Architecture (Detailed Cross-Section)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph RiserAssembly[Riser and Reactor Internals]
        direction TB
        RegCatIn[Regenerated Catalyst In, 710 C] --> MixZone[Mixing Zone, Oil-Catalyst Contact, 1 ms]
        FeedIn[Atomized Feed Spray, Multiple Nozzle Tips] --> MixZone
        MixZone --> ReactionZone[Reaction Zone, Adiabatic Riser, 2 to 4 s]
        ReactionZone --> RiserTop[Riser Outlet, 510 to 530 C]
        RiserTop --> QuickSep[Primary Separator, Inertial Quick Quench]
        QuickSep --> SecondarySep[Secondary Cyclone, Final Dust Removal]
        SecondarySep --> VaporOut[Cracked Vapor Outlet to Fractionator]
        SecondarySep --> SpentCatOut[Spent Catalyst to Stripper]
    end
    styleMixZone fill:#118ab2 color:#fff
    styleReactionZone fill:#ffd166
    styleQuickSep fill:#ef476f color:#fff
```

## 4.3 Regenerator Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Spent Catalyst + Coke] --> B[Fluidized Bed, Regenerator]
    B --> C[Combustion Zone, 720 C]
    C --> D[Catalyst Cooler, Optional, for Resid FCC]
    D --> E[Hot Regenerated Catalyst Return to Riser]
    B --> F[Flue Gas with CO, CO2, SOx, NOx]
    F --> G[CO Boiler, Waste Heat Recovery]
    G --> H[ESP and FGD, Particulate and SOx Removal]
    H --> I[Stack, Compliance with Emission Norms]
    styleB fill:#ef476f color:#fff
    styleE fill:#06d6a0
    styleI fill:#118ab2 color:#fff
```

## 4.4 Reaction Mechanism Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Paraffin Feed R-H] --> B[Protonation on Zeolite Brønsted Site]
    B --> C[Carbenium Ion R+]
    C --> D[Isomerization, Hydride and Methyl Shift]
    D --> E[β-Scission, Crack 2 Carbons Away]
    E --> F[Smaller Carbenium Ion R'+]
    E --> G[α-Olefin Product]
    F --> H[Propagation, New Feed Molecule]
    F --> I[Termination, Proton Loss to Form Olefin]
    H --> B
    styleC fill:#ef476f color:#fff
    styleE fill:#ffd166
    styleG fill:#06d6a0
    styleI fill:#06d6a0
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Q1. Define Fluid Catalytic Cracking (FCC). List any two feedstocks used in FCC.**

**Model Answer:**
FCC is a continuous catalytic process in which heavy hydrocarbon fractions (vacuum gas oil, atmospheric residue) are converted into lighter, valuable products like gasoline, LPG, and diesel-range hydrocarbons by contacting them with a hot, finely divided zeolite-based catalyst in a fluidized state. The catalyst circulates between the riser reactor (where cracking occurs) and the regenerator (where coke is burnt off).

**Two feedstocks:**
1. Vacuum Gas Oil (VGO) — primary feedstock, boiling range 343-565 °C.
2. Atmospheric Residue / Hydrotreated Resid — used in Resid FCC (RFCC) units.

---

> **[KTU University Exam - June 2024]**

**Q2. What is the role of the catalyst in FCC? Why is zeolite Y preferred over amorphous silica-alumina?**

**Model Answer:**
The catalyst provides **acidic active sites** (Brønsted and Lewis) that generate carbenium ions from hydrocarbons, enabling **β-scission** of C-C bonds at significantly lower temperatures (~500 °C) than thermal cracking. This produces gasoline with **higher iso-paraffin and olefin content → higher octane number**.

Zeolite Y is preferred because:
1. **Strong, well-defined acidity** — Faujasite supercages (7.4 Å pores) selectively crack linear and mildly branched hydrocarbons.
2. **High thermal and hydrothermal stability** — survives 700+ °C regeneration.
3. **Shape selectivity** — suppresses coke formation by rejecting heavy polyaromatics.
4. **Higher activity and selectivity** — gives higher gasoline yield at lower coke.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Draw a neat process flow diagram of a modern FCC unit and explain the function of each major section. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An FCC unit processes 40,000 kg/h of VGO. The product yields are: Dry gas 4 wt%, LPG 14 wt%, Gasoline 46 wt%, LCO 20 wt%, Slurry 10 wt%, Coke 6 wt%. Calculate:
(i) Conversion to products boiling below 221 °C. **(3 marks)**
(ii) Mass of gasoline produced per day. **(2 marks)**
(iii) Gasoline selectivity based on converted feed. **(2 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) PFD:** Refer to the block-level architecture in Section 4.1. Explain the four major sections:
- **Riser-Reactor:** Cracking at 510-540 °C, 2-4 s contact time.
- **Disengager + Cyclones:** Quick separation to minimize overcracking.
- **Stripper:** Steam-stripping of hydrocarbons entrained in spent catalyst.
- **Regenerator:** Fluidized bed at 680-730 °C, coke combustion in air, heat recovery.
- **Main Fractionator:** Separates cracked vapors into wet gas, gasoline, LCO, HCO/slurry.

(Allocation: 1 mark for diagram, 6 marks for explanation.)

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Conversion:**
$$
X = \frac{4 + 14 + 46}{100} = 64\ \text{wt\%} \quad \text{(3 marks)}
$$

**(ii) Mass of gasoline per day:**
$$
\dot{m}_{gasoline} = 40{,}000 \times 0.46 = 18{,}400\ \text{kg/h}
$$
$$
\text{Per day} = 18{,}400 \times 24 = 441{,}600\ \text{kg/day} \quad \text{(2 marks)}
$$

**(iii) Selectivity based on converted feed:**
$$
\eta' = \frac{0.46}{0.64} \times 100 = 71.88\% \quad \text{(2 marks)}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Explain the **β-scission mechanism** of catalytic cracking on a zeolite catalyst. Discuss how operating variables (temperature, CTO ratio, WHSV) affect conversion and gasoline yield. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A regenerator handles 800,000 kg/h of catalyst containing 1.0 wt% coke. Coke heat of combustion is **-32,000 kJ/kg**, specific heat of catalyst is **1.1 kJ/(kg·K)**, and spent catalyst enters at **505 °C**. Calculate:
(i) Heat released per hour by coke combustion. **(3 marks)**
(ii) Temperature rise of the catalyst due to combustion. **(2 marks)**
(iii) Hot regenerated catalyst temperature. **(2 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) β-scission mechanism:**
1. Feed paraffin R-H adsorbs on Brønsted acid site of zeolite, gets protonated to carbenium ion R⁺.
2. R⁺ undergoes isomerization (hydride/methyl shift) to form a more stable secondary or tertiary carbenium ion.
3. **β-scission:** the C–C bond β to the positive charge breaks, yielding a smaller carbenium ion and an α-olefin.
4. The smaller carbenium ion either propagates (by abstracting hydride from a fresh feed) or terminates (by losing a proton to yield another olefin).

**Effect of operating variables:**
- **Reactor temperature ↑:** Conversion ↑ (faster kinetics), but gasoline yield peaks then declines due to secondary cracking of gasoline to LPG + gas. Coke ↑↑.
- **CTO ratio ↑:** Conversion ↑ (more active sites), gasoline yield ↑ to a peak, then ↓ as overcracking occurs. Coke ↑↑.
- **WHSV ↓ (longer contact time):** Conversion ↑, but gasoline selectivity ↓ due to overcracking to gas. Coke ↑.

(1 mark for each clear point, max 7 marks.)

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Heat released per hour:**
$$
\dot{m}_{coke} = 800{,}000 \times 0.01 = 8{,}000\ \text{kg/h}
$$
$$
Q_{comb} = 8{,}000 \times 32{,}000 = 2.56 \times 10^8\ \text{kJ/h} \quad \text{(3 marks)}
$$

**(ii) Temperature rise of catalyst:**
$$
\Delta T = \frac{Q_{comb}}{F_{cat} \cdot C_{p,cat}} = \frac{2.56 \times 10^8}{800{,}000 \times 1.1}
$$
$$
\Delta T = \frac{2.56 \times 10^8}{8.8 \times 10^5} = 290.9\ \degree C \quad \text{(2 marks)}
$$

**(iii) Hot regenerated catalyst temperature:**
$$
T_{hot} = T_{in} + \Delta T = 505 + 290.9 = 795.9\ \degree C \quad \text{(2 marks)}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Confusing CTO with C/O:** CTO is mass catalyst / mass oil; C/O is mass carbon / mass oil. They differ by a factor of ~100 because coke is <1 wt% of catalyst.
> 2. **Sign errors in heat of combustion:** Coke combustion is **exothermic**, so $\Delta H < 0$. Use $|\Delta H|$ when computing heat released.
> 3. **Ignoring air/flue gas enthalpy:** In KTU numericals, often air and flue gas enthalpies are neglected for simplicity — but if given, students who ignore them lose 1-2 marks.
> 4. **Mixing up "conversion" and "selectivity":** Conversion is feed converted; selectivity is fraction of converted feed going to a specific product. These are **different** quantities.
> 5. **Forgetting units in the final answer:** Always state kg/h, °C, kJ/h clearly. KTU examiners deduct 0.5 marks for missing units.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **FCC Rapid-Revision Checklist (KTU Module 2):**

- **FCC** = Fluid Catalytic Cracking; converts VGO/Residue → gasoline (major), LPG, LCO, slurry, coke.
- **Catalyst:** Zeolite Y on kaolin matrix; **CTO ratio 5-8**; particle size **60-80 µm** (Geldart A).
- **Riser temperature:** 510-540 °C; **residence time:** 2-4 seconds; adiabatic.
- **Regenerator temperature:** 680-730 °C; burns coke in 5-10% O₂/air.
- **Coke on spent catalyst:** 0.8-1.2 wt%; heat of combustion ≈ **-32,800 kJ/kg**.
- **Mechanism:** β-scission of **carbenium ions** on Brønsted acid sites; produces **branched paraffins + olefins** (high RON 90-95).
- **Conversion** = (Dry gas + LPG + Gasoline) / Feed × 100; typical **60-75 wt%**.
- **Selectivity** = Product yield / Conversion.
- **Key trends:** ↑Temperature → ↑Conversion, ↑Coke; ↑CTO → ↑Conversion, ↑Coke; ↓WHSV → ↑Conversion, ↓Gasoline.
- **ZSM-5 additive:** Boosts **propylene yield** (6-9 wt%); used in petrochemical-grade FCC.
- **FCC vs Thermal Cracking:** FCC operates at **lower T**, gives **higher gasoline + iso-paraffins**, lower olefin, lower coke than thermal.
- **Autothermal design:** Coke combustion heat = heat required for feed vaporization + endothermic cracking. **No external furnace fuel** is needed.
- **Refinery position:** FCC sits between **VDU (vacuum distillation)** and **gasoline blending**; downstream units include alkylation, hydrotreating of LCO/gasoline.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for FCC products (top to bottom in main fractionator):**
> **"WGL-LC-S"** = **W**et **G**as → **L**PG → **G**asoline → **L**CO → **C**oke? No — Correct order: **Wet Gas, Gasoline, LCO, Slurry** = "**Went Gone, Lost Slurry**" 😄

**End of Module 2 FCC Notes — KTU 2024 Scheme | PEPOT638**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT638 - PETROLEUM REFINERY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Flexi Cracking

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Flexicracking** is the proprietary name coined by **ExxonMobil (formerly Esso Research and Engineering Company)** for its advanced **Fluid Catalytic Cracking (FCC)** process. It is a continuous, regenerable catalytic cracking technology in which heavy hydrocarbon feedstocks (Vacuum Gas Oil, atmospheric residue, deasphalted oil) are converted into lighter, high-value products such as gasoline, diesel, LPG, and light olefins using a **fluidized bed of zeolite-based catalyst** in a **riser reactor** with simultaneous catalyst regeneration.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, Flexicracking is positioned as a flagship **secondary (conversion) process** in petroleum refining under Module 2 — *Catalytic & Thermal Conversion Processes*. It represents the modern evolution of catalytic cracking, where catalyst and oil contact in a co-current upward flowing riser for **2–4 seconds** of precise contact time.

### 1.2 Key Terminology (KTU-Syllabus Aligned)

| Term | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| **Flexicracking** | Trademarked ExxonMobil FCC process |
| **Riser Reactor** | Vertical tubular reactor for short-contact cracking |
| **Regenerator** | Fluidized-bed vessel that burns off coke from catalyst |
| **Catalyst-to-Oil Ratio (C/O)** | Mass of catalyst circulated per mass of oil feed |
| **WHSV** | Weight Hourly Space Velocity (h⁻¹) |
| **Coke** | Carbonaceous deposit on catalyst, removed in regenerator |
| **Zeolite Catalyst** | Crystalline aluminosilicate (e.g., USY, ZSM-5) |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Rolling Conveyor Oven"

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine a giant vertical conveyor-belt oven** in a bakery:
> - The **raw dough (heavy oil)** is dropped at the bottom.
> - The **oven's hot baking stones (catalyst)** rise upward in parallel with the dough.
> - As they rub against each other in just **2–3 seconds**, the dough puffs up and becomes **fluffy bread (gasoline & gases)**.
> - The dough that sticks to the stones as **charred residue (coke)** is sent to a **cleaning chamber (regenerator)** where flames burn it off.
> - The cleaned stones are **lifted back up** to meet fresh dough — a **continuous cycle**.
> - This is exactly how Flexicracking works: **catalyst rises, oil rises, cracking happens, coke is burnt, catalyst returns**.

### 1.4 Industrial Significance

- Flexicracking is the **economic backbone** of modern refineries.
- Globally, **~40–45 %** of all gasoline is produced via FCC/Flexicracking units.
- A single modern Flexicracker processes **30,000 – 100,000 barrels per stream day (BPSD)**.
- The reaction temperature window is **750–820 K (≈ 480–550 °C)** with reactor pressure of **1.5–3.5 atm (gauge)**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Process Philosophy — The Three "Why's"

**Why use a fluidized catalyst?**
- Solid catalyst particles must contact **liquid + vapor oil uniformly**.
- Fluidization (using vapor/lift gas) suspends catalyst like a **boiling liquid**, ensuring **isothermal** conditions and **excellent heat transfer**.

**Why use a riser (not a fixed bed)?**
- Cracking is a **consecutive reaction**: products (gasoline) can over-crack to gas + coke.
- Short residence time (≈ **2–4 seconds**) **maximizes gasoline** and **minimizes over-cracking & coke**.

**Why continuous regeneration?**
- Coke deposits **poison** the catalyst within milliseconds.
- Burning coke off (in regenerator at **950–1020 K**) restores activity, providing the **heat of combustion** to drive the endothermic cracking reaction — **no external furnace needed**.

### 2.2 Step-Wise Process Logic

1. **Feed Preheat** — VGO/residue heated to **350–400 °C** in a feed furnace.
2. **Atomization & Injection** — Feed sprayed through **nozzles** into the riser base using **steam** as dispersant.
3. **Catalyst Lifting** — Regenerated hot catalyst (≈ **700–750 °C**) is lifted from regenerator via **lift air + steam**.
4. **Cracking Reaction in Riser** — Endothermic C–C bond scission occurs. Hydrocarbons vaporize and crack.
5. **Disengagement (TCD)** — Catalyst and vapor separated in a **Tertiary Cyclone Disengager (TCD)** or **Rough-Cut Cyclone**.
6. **Vapor Recovery** — Cracked vapors go to the **Main Fractionator (MFC)**.
7. **Spent Catalyst Stripping** — Steam stripping removes **entrained hydrocarbons** (≈ 30–60 s).
8. **Regeneration** — Coke burnt at **950–1020 K** in fluidized regenerator using **air**.
9. **Flue Gas Treatment** — CO/CO₂ released; electrostatic precipitator recovers catalyst fines.
10. **Recycle** — Hot regenerated catalyst returns to riser base.

### 2.3 Primary Reactions (Chemistry)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{C–C bond scission:} \quad & \text{C}_{n}\text{H}_{2n+2} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{C}_{m}\text{H}_{2m+2} \;+\; \text{C}_{p}\text{H}_{2p} \\[4pt]
\text{Isomerization:} \quad & n\text{-paraffin} \;\longrightarrow\; i\text{-paraffin} \\[4pt]
\text{Dehydrogenation:} \quad & \text{Naphthene} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Aromatic} \;+\; 3\text{H}_2 \\[4pt]
\text{Transalkylation:} \quad & \text{Aromatic} \;+\; \text{Aromatic} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Heavy Aromatic} \;+\; \text{Light Aromatic} \\[4pt]
\text{Coke formation:} \quad & \text{Hydrocarbons} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Coke (C)} \;+\; \text{H}_2
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **overall ΔH_rxn** for catalytic cracking is **endothermic ≈ 200–300 kJ/kg of feed**. Heat for this reaction is supplied entirely by the **hot regenerated catalyst**.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Formula / Expression | Engineering Meaning | Units |
|--------|---------------------|---------------------|-------|
| **C/O (Catalyst-to-Oil Ratio)** | $\dfrac{m_{\text{cat}}}{m_{\text{oil}}}$ | Catalyst circulation rate per oil feed | kg/kg |
| **WHSV** | $\dfrac{\dot{m}_{\text{oil}}}{m_{\text{cat,static}}}$ | Weight hourly space velocity | h⁻¹ |
| **Conversion (X)** | $\dfrac{V_{\text{feed}} - V_{\text{HCO}}}{V_{\text{feed}}} \times 100$ | % feed converted to < 220 °C products | % vol |
| **Gasoline Yield (Y)** | $\dfrac{V_{\text{gasoline}}}{V_{\text{feed}}} \times 100$ | Volumetric yield | % vol |
| **Carbon on Catalyst (Cc)** | $\dfrac{m_{\text{coke}}}{m_{\text{cat}}} \times 100$ | Coke deposition on catalyst | wt % |
| **Heat Balance** | $Q_{\text{regen}} = \dot{m}_{\text{cat}} \, C_{p,\text{cat}} \, \Delta T$ | Sensible heat from hot catalyst | kW |
| **Regenerator Air Demand** | $\dot{n}_{\text{air}} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{\text{coke}} + 0.5 \dot{m}_{\text{S}}}{0.21 \times 32}$ | Stoichiometric O₂ for coke combustion | kmol/h |
| **ΔH_coke combustion** | $\approx -32{,}800 \; \text{kJ/kg C}$ | Heat released burning pure coke | kJ/kg |
| **ΔH_cracking** | $\approx +200 \; \text{to} \; +300 \; \text{kJ/kg feed}$ | Endothermic heat of cracking | kJ/kg |
| **Riser ΔT** | $\Delta T_{\text{riser}} \approx \dfrac{\Delta H_{\text{rxn}}}{\left( \dfrac{C}{O} \right) \cdot C_{p,\text{cat}}}$ | Temperature drop along riser | K |
| **Carbon Balance** | $C_{\text{feed}} = C_{\text{gasoline}} + C_{\text{LCO}} + C_{\text{HCO}} + C_{\text{gas}} + C_{\text{coke}}$ | Mass closure | wt % |
| **Stohchiometric Oxygen** | $\text{C} + \text{O}_2 \to \text{CO}_2 \;,\; \text{C} + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \to \text{CO}$ | Coke combustion stoichiometry | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **No vertical bars `\|` are used in this table** to avoid breaking markdown parsing; instead, $\vert$ or $\mid$ should be used in handwritten or LaTeX-rendered notes for absolute-value notation.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility

- **Refinery Economics**: Flexicracking upgrades **cheap heavy ends** into **premium gasoline** — improves **refinery margin by \$8–\$15/barrel**.
- **Petrochemical Feedstock**: Produces **propylene & butylene** streams — vital for polyolefin manufacture.
- **Integration with alkylation, MTBE, ETBE units**: Enhances octane of the gasoline pool.
- **Modern R&D**: ExxonMobil Flexicracking III incorporates **ZSM-5 additives** boosting **propylene yield up to 20–25 wt %**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Conversion and Material Balance in a Flexicracker

**Given (KTU-style problem data):**
- Feed = Vacuum Gas Oil (VGO), flow $\dot{V}_{\text{feed}} = 10{,}000 \; \text{BPSD}$ at density $\rho_{\text{feed}} = 920 \; \text{kg/m}^3$
- Volume % yields: Gasoline = **62 %**, LCO = **18 %**, HCO = **12 %**, Dry Gas = **5 %**, LPG = **2.5 %**, Coke = **0.5 %** (remaining 0 % rounding)
- Coke on catalyst, $C_c = 0.85 \; \text{wt \%}$
- Catalyst circulation rate, $\dot{m}_{\text{cat}} = 1500 \; \text{tonnes/day}$

**Step 1 — Convert feed volume to mass flow:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_{\text{feed}} &= \dot{V}_{\text{feed}} \times \rho_{\text{feed}} \\
&= 10{,}000 \; \frac{\text{barrels}}{\text{day}} \times 0.158987 \; \frac{\text{m}^3}{\text{barrel}} \times 920 \; \frac{\text{kg}}{\text{m}^3} \\
\dot{m}_{\text{feed}} &\approx 1{,}462.7 \times 10^{3} \; \frac{\text{kg}}{\text{day}} \\
\dot{m}_{\text{feed}} &\approx 1462.7 \; \frac{\text{tonnes}}{\text{day}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Catalyst-to-Oil Ratio (C/O):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{C}{O} &= \dfrac{\dot{m}_{\text{cat}}}{\dot{m}_{\text{feed}}} \\
&= \dfrac{1500 \; \text{TPD}}{1462.7 \; \text{TPD}} \\
\frac{C}{O} &\approx 1.025 \; \text{kg cat/kg oil}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Mass of coke deposited per day:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_{\text{coke}} &= C_c \times \dot{m}_{\text{cat}} \\
&= 0.0085 \times 1500 \\
\dot{m}_{\text{coke}} &= 12.75 \; \frac{\text{tonnes}}{\text{day}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Air required to burn coke (assume complete combustion C → CO₂):**

Stoichiometry: $\text{C} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{n}_{\text{coke}} &= \dfrac{12{,}750 \; \text{kg/day}}{12 \; \text{kg/kmol}} = 1062.5 \; \frac{\text{kmol}}{\text{day}} \\
\dot{n}_{\text{O}_2,\text{req}} &= 1062.5 \; \frac{\text{kmol}}{\text{day}} \\
\dot{n}_{\text{air}} &= \dfrac{\dot{n}_{\text{O}_2}}{0.21} = \dfrac{1062.5}{0.21} \approx 5059.5 \; \frac{\text{kmol}}{\text{day}}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_{\text{air}} &= 5059.5 \times 28.97 \\
\dot{m}_{\text{air}} &\approx 146{,}575 \; \frac{\text{kg}}{\text{day}} \\
\dot{m}_{\text{air}} &\approx 146.6 \; \frac{\text{tonnes}}{\text{day}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Heat released in regenerator:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{Q}_{\text{regen}} &= \dot{m}_{\text{coke}} \times \Delta H_{\text{coke}} \\
&= 12.75 \times 10^3 \; \frac{\text{kg}}{\text{day}} \times 32{,}800 \; \frac{\text{kJ}}{\text{kg}} \\
\dot{Q}_{\text{regen}} &= 4.182 \times 10^8 \; \frac{\text{kJ}}{\text{day}} \\
\dot{Q}_{\text{regen}} &\approx 4839 \; \text{kW (continuous)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Verify heat balance (catalyst must supply this + sensible heat):**

Assuming catalyst $C_p = 1.1 \; \text{kJ/(kg·K)}$, temperature drop across riser $\Delta T$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_{\text{cat}} \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T &= \dot{m}_{\text{feed}} \cdot \Delta H_{\text{cracking}} \\
1500 \times 10^3 \times 1.1 \times \Delta T &= 1462.7 \times 10^3 \times 250 \\
1.65 \times 10^6 \cdot \Delta T &= 3.657 \times 10^8 \\
\Delta T &\approx 222 \; \text{K}
\end{aligned}
$$

This $\Delta T$ is reasonable: catalyst enters at ~**720 °C** and exits at ~**500 °C** in the stripper — matches industrial practice.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Conversion (X) from Mass-Yield Data

**Conversion** is defined as the volume fraction of feed converted to **products boiling below 220 °C** (gasoline + LPG + gas):

$$
\begin{aligned}
X \;(\%) &= \dfrac{V_{\text{feed}} - V_{\text{HCO}}}{V_{\text{feed}}} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{100 - 12}{100} \times 100 \\
X &= 88 \; \text{vol \%}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> HCO = Heavy Cycle Oil is the **unconverted bottom** product. A conversion of **88 vol %** is typical for a **high-severity Flexicracker** operating on VGO.

### 3.3 Analytical Summary Matrix (KTU Quick-Reference Table)

| Operating Parameter | Typical Value (Industrial) | Effect of Increase |
|---------------------|----------------------------|--------------------|
| Riser Top Temperature | **510–540 °C** | ↑ Conversion, ↑ Coke, ↓ LCO |
| Catalyst/Oil Ratio | **5–8 kg/kg** | ↑ Conversion, ↑ Coke |
| Residence Time (Riser) | **2–4 s** | ↑ Conversion, ↑ Gas |
| Regenerator Temp | **680–730 °C** | ↑ Coke burn-off |
| Pressure (Reactor) | **1.5–3.5 atm** | ↑ Cracking depth |
| Steam to Feed | **2–5 wt %** | Atomization aid |
| Conversion | **70–90 vol %** | — |
| Gasoline Yield | **45–62 vol %** | — |
| Coke Yield | **3–6 wt %** | — |
| LPG Olefins Yield | **15–25 wt %** | — |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow of a Flexicracker Unit

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[FEED VGO Preheater 350 to 400 C] --> B[Riser Reactor Base Feed Injection Nozzles]
    C[Regenerated Catalyst Slide Valve Hot 700 C] --> B
    B --> D[Riser Cracking Zone 2 to 4 s contact]
    D --> E[Disengager TCD Vapor and Catalyst Separation]
    E --> F[Spent Catalyst Stripper Steam 60 s]
    E --> G[Main Fractionator MFC Product Separation]
    F --> H[Regenerator Fluidized Bed 700 C Air Injection]
    H --> I[Flue Gas Cyclone and ESP Catalyst Recovery]
    H --> C
    G --> J[Wet Gas Compressor]
    G --> K[Light Cycle Oil LCO Draw]
    G --> L[Heavy Cycle Oil HCO Recycle or Draw]
    J --> M[Gas Plant Absorber and Debutanizer]
    M --> N[LPG Product]
    M --> O[Gasoline Product to Storage]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Node ID Rule Check** — All node IDs above are alphanumeric (`A`, `B`, ... `O`). No reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`) are used as standalone IDs. Labels contain raw uppercase alphanumeric text only — no bold or markdown inside the double-quoted labels.

### 4.2 Heat & Mass Flow Schematic (Riser–Regenerator Loop)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Cold Feed VGO 350 C] --> B[Hot Catalyst 700 C C/O 6]
    B --> C[Riser Endothermic Cracking]
    C --> D[Spent Catalyst 510 C plus Coke 0.85 wt%]
    D --> E[Regenerator Exothermic Combustion]
    E --> F[Hot Regenerated Catalyst 720 C]
    F --> B
    C --> G[Cracked Vapors 510 C]
    G --> H[Main Fractionator Product Recovery]
```

### 4.3 Process Severity vs Yield Diagram (Description)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A graphical plot of **Conversion (X-axis, vol %)** versus **Yields (Y-axis, wt %)** for a typical Flexicracker shows four curves — **Gasoline (peaks at ~70–75 % conversion)**, **LCO (declines linearly)**, **Coke (rises exponentially above 80 % conversion)**, and **Dry Gas (monotonically rises)**. The optimum operating point (highest gasoline yield) corresponds to **~75 vol % conversion** — a standard KTU plot question.

### 4.4 Industrial Flexicracking Unit — Block Architecture Summary

| Block | Equipment | Function |
|-------|-----------|----------|
| **Reaction Section** | Riser, TCD, Stripper | Cracking + separation |
| **Regeneration Section** | Regenerator, Cyclones, ESP, Stack | Coke combustion + catalyst recovery |
| **Fractionation Section** | Main Fractionator, Slurry Settler | Product split |
| **Gas Recovery Section** | Wet Gas Compressor, Absorber, Debutanizer | LPG + Gasoline recovery |
| **Utilities** | Air blower, Feed furnace, Steam system | Energy supply |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

> Define the term **Flexicracking**. List **two distinguishing features** that differentiate it from conventional fluid catalytic cracking (FCC).

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Flexicracking is the proprietary ExxonMobil term for its advanced **fluid catalytic cracking process** in which heavy petroleum fractions (VGO, residue) are catalytically cracked into lighter products using a **fluidized zeolite catalyst** in a short-contact-time **riser reactor** with continuous catalyst regeneration.

**Distinguishing features:**

1. **Riser-only cracking** with **2–4 seconds** contact time (vs older dense-bed FCC).
2. **High catalyst-to-oil ratios (5–8)** with sophisticated catalyst coolers/heat removal.
3. **Higher conversion (80–88 vol %)** and **enhanced olefin yield** for petrochemicals.

*(1 mark — definition; 2 marks — two features)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

> State the **functions of the regenerator** in a Flexicracking unit. Why is **complete coke removal** critical?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The regenerator:

1. **Burns off coke** (3–6 wt %) deposited on the spent catalyst using preheated air at **680–730 °C**.
2. **Supplies heat** for the endothermic cracking reaction via hot catalyst return (≈ **200–220 K** sensible heat).
3. **Restores catalyst activity & selectivity** by removing carbon deposits.

**Why complete coke removal is critical:**
- Residual coke > **0.05 wt %** reduces catalyst acidity → drops conversion.
- Uneven regeneration creates **hot/cold zones** → thermal deactivation of zeolite.
- Coke-induced hotspots may cause **catalyst sintering** and **afterburning** in the dilute phase.

*(1.5 marks — functions; 1.5 marks — criticality)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Module 2 Choice Option**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]**

> **A Flexicracking unit** processes **20,000 BPSD** of VGO ($\rho = 915 \; \text{kg/m}^3$). The catalyst circulation rate is **3000 tonnes/day** with coke on spent catalyst = **0.95 wt %**. Coke is burnt completely to **CO₂** in the regenerator at **720 °C**. Heat of cracking = **250 kJ/kg feed**.
>
> **(a)** Calculate the **catalyst-to-oil ratio** and the **daily coke production**.
>
> **(b)** Compute the **air requirement** for regeneration (in kg/day) and the **heat released** by coke combustion. Comment on whether this heat is **sufficient** to drive the cracking reaction.

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — C/O ratio and Coke Production (7 marks)**

*Step 1: Mass flow of feed*

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_{\text{feed}} &= 20{,}000 \times 0.158987 \times 915 \\
&= 20{,}000 \times 145.47 \\
\dot{m}_{\text{feed}} &= 2909.4 \; \frac{\text{tonnes}}{\text{day}}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Step 2: Catalyst-to-Oil Ratio*

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{C}{O} &= \dfrac{3000}{2909.4} \\
\frac{C}{O} &\approx 1.031 \; \frac{\text{kg cat}}{\text{kg oil}}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Step 3: Daily coke production*

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_{\text{coke}} &= C_c \times \dot{m}_{\text{cat}} \\
&= 0.0095 \times 3000 \\
\dot{m}_{\text{coke}} &= 28.5 \; \frac{\text{tonnes}}{\text{day}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b) — Air requirement and heat balance (7 marks)**

*Step 4: Oxygen stoichiometry (C → CO₂)*

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{n}_{\text{coke}} &= \dfrac{28{,}500}{12} = 2375 \; \frac{\text{kmol}}{\text{day}} \\
\dot{n}_{\text{O}_2} &= 2375 \; \frac{\text{kmol}}{\text{day}} \\
\dot{n}_{\text{air}} &= \dfrac{2375}{0.21} = 11{,}310 \; \frac{\text{kmol}}{\text{day}} \\
\dot{m}_{\text{air}} &= 11{,}310 \times 28.97 = 327{,}650 \; \frac{\text{kg}}{\text{day}} \approx 327.65 \; \text{TPD}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Step 5: Heat released by coke combustion*

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{Q}_{\text{regen}} &= 28{,}500 \times 32{,}800 \\
&= 9.348 \times 10^8 \; \frac{\text{kJ}}{\text{day}} \\
\dot{Q}_{\text{regen}} &\approx 10{,}820 \; \text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Step 6: Heat required for cracking*

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{Q}_{\text{crack}} &= 2909.4 \times 10^3 \times 250 = 7.274 \times 10^8 \; \frac{\text{kJ}}{\text{day}} \\
\dot{Q}_{\text{crack}} &\approx 8420 \; \text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Step 7: Heat sufficiency check*

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Surplus heat} &= \dot{Q}_{\text{regen}} - \dot{Q}_{\text{crack}} \\
&= 10{,}820 - 8{,}420 \\
&= 2{,}400 \; \text{kW (excess)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The heat released by coke combustion (**10,820 kW**) **exceeds** the heat required for cracking (**8,420 kW**) by ~**28 %**. This **excess** is needed to compensate for heat losses (regenerator flue gas, radiation, feed heating), confirming that **autothermal operation is feasible** for this Flexicracker.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Internal Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

> **(a)** Draw a **labelled block diagram of a Flexicracking unit** showing the riser, regenerator, main fractionator and product recovery section. Briefly explain the **role of the stripper**.
>
> **(b)** A Flexicracker produces the following yields (vol %) on feed: Gasoline = **62**, LCO = **17**, HCO = **13**, Dry Gas + LPG = **8**, Coke = **0 (rounded)**. Compute the **conversion**, **gasoline selectivity**, and comment on the **carbon balance**.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Block Diagram & Stripper Role (7 marks)**

*Block Diagram* — refer to **Section 4.1** Mermaid flowchart of this document (7 equipment blocks: Riser → TCD → Stripper → Regenerator → Main Fractionator → Gas Plant → Products).

*Role of the Stripper* — The **catalyst stripper** is a fluidized section located between the disengager and the regenerator. Its functions are:

1. **Steam stripping** of catalyst removes **entrained hydrocarbon vapors** (10–30 % of total coke precursors if not stripped).
2. Reduces **regenerator load** by preventing combustion of valuable hydrocarbons.
3. Improves **heat balance** — recovered hydrocarbons re-enter the vapor line and increase gasoline yield.
4. Typical steam-to-catalyst ratio: **1–3 kg/1000 kg catalyst**.

**(b) Conversion, Selectivity, Carbon Balance (7 marks)**

*Step 1: Conversion (X)*

$$
\begin{aligned}
X &= \dfrac{100 - V_{\text{HCO}}}{100} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{100 - 13}{100} \times 100 = 87 \; \text{vol \%}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Step 2: Gasoline Selectivity*

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{gasoline}} &= \dfrac{Y_{\text{gasoline}}}{X} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{62}{87} \times 100 \\
S_{\text{gasoline}} &\approx 71.3 \; \%
\end{aligned}
$$

A selectivity of **71 %** is excellent — typical high-severity Flexicracker gasoline selectivity is **68–78 %**.

*Step 3: Carbon Balance Check*

Assuming average carbon content (wt %): Gasoline = **86 %**, LCO = **87 %**, HCO = **87 %**, Gas = **83 %**, Coke = **100 %**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Carbon in (feed)} &= 100 \times 0.865 \approx 86.5 \; \text{kg per 100 kg feed} \\
\text{Carbon out} &= 62 \times 0.86 + 17 \times 0.87 + 13 \times 0.87 + 8 \times 0.83 \\
&= 53.32 + 14.79 + 11.31 + 6.64 \\
&= 86.06 \; \text{kg per 100 kg feed}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Carbon closure error: $\dfrac{\vert 86.5 - 86.06 \vert}{86.5} \times 100 \approx 0.51 \; \%$** — within acceptable tolerance (< 1 %). **Balance verified.**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common mistake (–2 marks):** Forgetting to convert **BPSD → kg/day** using the factor **0.158987 m³/barrel**. Always show the unit conversion explicitly.
> - **Common mistake (–2 marks):** Using **air mass fraction of O₂ as 23 %** instead of **mole fraction 21 %**. The factor **0.21** is molar; for mass balance use **0.232**.
> - **Common mistake (–1 mark):** Defining **conversion** as **(100 − LCO)** instead of the KTU-recommended **(100 − HCO)**.
> - **Common mistake (–1 mark):** Forgetting to state units in the final answer.
> - **Common mistake (–2 marks):** In diagrams, students often miss the **stripper** — examiners award marks only if the stripper is **drawn between disengager and regenerator**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Flexi Cracking**

- ☐ **Flexicracking** = ExxonMobil proprietary FCC using **riser reactor + continuous regeneration**.
- ☐ **Feed**: VGO, deasphalted oil, atmospheric residue. **Products**: Gasoline (45–62 %), LPG + olefins (15–25 %), LCO (15–20 %), HCO (5–13 %), Coke (3–6 %).
- ☐ **Riser conditions**: 510–540 °C, **2–4 s** contact, 1.5–3.5 atm.
- ☐ **Catalyst**: Zeolite (USY, ZSM-5) on silica–alumina matrix; **C/O = 5–8**.
- ☐ **Regenerator**: 680–730 °C, fluidized bed, coke combustion (C → CO₂/CO); **heat is the energy source** for the endothermic cracking reaction.
- ☐ **ΔH cracking ≈ +250 kJ/kg**; **ΔH coke combustion ≈ −32,800 kJ/kg C**.
- ☐ **Conversion** = $\dfrac{100 - V_{\text{HCO}}}{100}$ vol %; **Gasoline selectivity** = $Y_{\text{gas}}/X$.
- ☐ **Stripper** removes entrained hydrocarbons before regeneration.
- ☐ **Air demand** for regeneration uses **molar O₂ = 0.21** of air; complete combustion → **1 mol O₂ per mol C**.
- ☐ **Catalyst coolers** (e.g., in Flexicracking III) remove **excess heat** from regenerator when conversion is high.
- ☐ **Modern trends**: ZSM-5 additives for **propylene boost**, residue-cracking catalysts for **bottom-of-the-barrel upgrading**.
- ☐ **Carbon balance closure** should be within **±1 %** for full marks in numerical problems.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Bloom's Mapping:**
> - **Remember/Understand** → Q1, Q2 (Part A).
> - **Apply/Analyze** → Part B (a) numerical.
> - **Evaluate/Create** → Part B (b) heat balance + diagram.
> Expect at least **one numerical (≥ 7 marks)** and **one descriptive + diagram (≥ 7 marks)** per 14-mark question.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reaction Conditions and Catalyst for Catalytic Reforming
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT638 - PETROLEUM REFINERY ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Reaction Conditions and Catalyst for Catalytic Reforming ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Catalytic Reforming** is a catalytic chemical process used in petroleum refineries to convert low-octane naphthenic and paraffinic hydrocarbons (typically C₆–C₁₁ paraffins and naphthenes from the heavy straight-run naphtha fraction, boiling range **70–200 °C**) into high-octane liquid products called **reformate**, which is rich in **aromatics and isoparaffins**. The process is accompanied by the production of significant quantities of **hydrogen gas** as a valuable by-product, which is used elsewhere in the refinery for hydrotreating and hydrocracking. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition Reference:** Catalytic reforming is fundamentally an **aromatization process** that simultaneously upgrades octane number and produces a high-purity hydrogen stream — making it a critical *hydrogen factory* of any modern refinery. ### 1.2 Reaction Conditions Overview The three primary reaction conditions that govern catalytic reforming performance are: | Parameter | Typical Operating Range | Effect on Process | |---|---|---| | **Temperature** | **450 – 525 °C** | Drives endothermic aromatization reactions | | **Pressure** | **1 – 35 atm (kg/cm²)** | Controls coke formation & equilibrium | | **H₂ / HC Molar Ratio** | **3 – 6** | Suppresses coke; maintains catalyst life | | **WHSV** | **1 – 3 hr⁻¹** | Controls conversion & contact time | ### 1.3 The Catalyst (Dual-Function Concept) Modern reforming catalysts are **dual-function (bifunctional) catalysts** composed of: 1. **Metallic (Dehydrogenation) Function** — Platinum (Pt), often promoted with Rhenium (Re), Germanium (Ge), Tin (Sn), or Iridium (Ir) as a bimetallic system. 2. **Acidic (Isomerization) Function** — Halogenated (chlorided) γ-Alumina (γ-Al₂O₃). > [!NOTE] > **Standard Catalyst Composition (UOP/RIF):** > - Pt: **0.3 – 0.6 wt %** > - Cl: **0.1 – 1.0 wt %** > - Support: **γ-Al₂O₃ pellets** (1.6 mm extrudates) > - Optional: Re or Sn = 0.3 – 0.5 wt % (for bimetallic CCR units) ### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — The "Refinery Kitchen" 🍳 Imagine a chef preparing a complex meal: - The **feed (naphtha)** is a basket of raw, low-value ingredients (paraffins). - The **heat (450–525 °C)** is the stove that breaks and rearranges molecular bonds. - The **catalyst (Pt/Cl-Al₂O₃)** is the skilled sous-chef with **two hands**: one hand (*metallic Pt*) **dehydrogenates** (removes H₂ from rings to form aromatics), the other hand (*acidic Cl-Al₂O₃*) **isomerizes and cyclizes** the straight chains into rings. - The **hydrogen atmosphere** is the protective lid — it keeps the dish from "burning" (coking) by removing freshly formed H₂ before it can crack the valuable aromatics. - **Lower pressure** = thinner lid = more aromatic flavor, but higher risk of burning. **Higher pressure** = thicker lid = safer but milder transformation. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Four Primary Reforming Reactions Catalytic reforming involves **four major reaction families** that occur simultaneously over the dual-function catalyst: #### (a) Dehydrogenation of Naphthenes → Aromatics *(Desired — Highly Endothermic)* $$ \text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n} \;(\text{naphthene}) \;\xrightarrow{\text{Pt}}\; \text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n-6} \;(\text{aromatic}) + 3\text{H}_2 $$ **ΔH ≈ +205 kJ/mol**, strongly favored thermodynamically by **high temperature** and **low pressure**. #### (b) Isomerization of n-Paraffins → Iso-Paraffins *(Desired — Mildly Exothermic)* $$ n\text{-C}_n\text{H}_{2n+2} \;\xrightleftharpoons{\text{acid site}}\; \text{iso-C}_n\text{H}_{2n+2} $$ **ΔH ≈ –5 to –15 kJ/mol**, equilibrium-controlled, no change in carbon number. #### (c) Dehydrocyclization of Paraffins → Aromatics *(Desired — Most Endothermic)* $$ n\text{-C}_n\text{H}_{2n+2} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Pt + acid}}\; \text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n-6} + 4\text{H}_2 $$ **ΔH ≈ +260 kJ/mol**, requires the strongest combination of Pt + acid sites. #### (d) Hydrocracking (C–C Scission) *(Undesired — Highly Exothermic)* $$ \text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n+2} + \text{H}_2 \;\xrightarrow{\text{acid}}\; \text{C}_m\text{H}_{2m+2} + \text{C}_{n-m}\text{H}_{2(n-m)} $$ **ΔH ≈ –45 kJ/mol**, produces light gases (C₁–C₄) and coke precursors — must be minimized. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Engineering Trade-off:** *Lower pressure* favors aromatics (reactions a, c) but accelerates *coking* on the acid site. *Higher pressure* suppresses coke but reduces aromatic yield by Le Chatelier's principle. ### 2.2 Role of Each Operating Variable #### (A) Temperature (450 – 525 °C) - All four reforming reactions (especially dehydrogenation & dehydrocyclization) are **endothermic** → higher T → higher conversion to aromatics. - Practical **upper limit ≈ 525 °C** due to: - Hydrocracking dominance at T > 540 °C - Excessive coke formation - Metallurgical limits of reactor alloys - Typical operation uses **multiple adiabatic reactors in series** with inter-stage reheaters to compensate for endothermic temperature drop. #### (B) Pressure (1 – 35 kg/cm²) - **Modern Continuous Catalyst Regeneration (CCR) Units:** 1 – 5 kg/cm² - **Semi-Regenerative Units:** 10 – 35 kg/cm² - Lowering pressure shifts **Le Chatelier equilibrium** toward aromatic side (moles of H₂ increase, system moves opposite). - Coke formation is suppressed at higher pressure because hydrogenation of coke precursors (polyaromatics) is favored. #### (C) Hydrogen-to-Hydrocarbon Molar Ratio (3 – 6 : 1) - Maintains a **hydrogen-rich atmosphere** in the reactor to: 1. Suppress coke precursor polymerization 2. Provide H₂ for hydrocracking side reaction 3. Strip H₂S from feed (if S is present) - **Higher ratio** → longer catalyst life, lower aromatic yield - **Lower ratio** → higher aromatic yield, shorter cycle #### (D) WHSV (Weight Hourly Space Velocity) – 1 to 3 hr⁻¹ $$ \text{WHSV} = \frac{\text{Mass feed rate (kg/hr)}}{\text{Mass of catalyst (kg)}} $$ - Lower WHSV → longer contact time → more conversion but more cracking/coke. - Typical design point = **1.5 – 2.0 hr⁻¹** for semi-regenerative; **2 – 3 hr⁻¹** for CCR. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Formula | Expression | Units | Engineering Use | |---|---|---|---| | **WHSV** | $W = \dfrac{F_{feed}}{m_{cat}}$ | hr⁻¹ | Reactor sizing | | **LHSV** | $L = \dfrac{V_{feed,liq}}{V_{cat}}$ | hr⁻¹ | Volumetric basis | | **Conversion (X)** | $X = \dfrac{n_{in} - n_{out}}{n_{in}}$ | – | Performance metric | | **Aromatic Yield** | $Y_A = \dfrac{\text{wt\% aromatics in reformate}}{100}$ | – | Octane indicator | | **H₂ Purity** | $\text{Purity} = \dfrac{\text{mol H}_2}{\text{mol total gas}} \times 100$ | mol % | By-product value | | **Research Octane Number (RON)** | $R = f(\text{composition, isomerate content})$ | – | Final product spec | | **Le Chatelier (Dehydrogenation)** | $K_P = \dfrac{P_{Ar}\,(P_{H_2})^3}{P_{Naph}}$ | – | Equilibrium constant | | **Equilibrium Relation for Aromatization** | $\ln\!\left(\dfrac{K_P}{K_{P,0}}\right) = -\dfrac{\Delta H^\circ}{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T} - \dfrac{1}{T_0}\right)$ | – | Temperature effect | | **Pressure Equilibrium Constant (vol. basis)** | $K_{Ar/N} \propto \left(\dfrac{1}{P}\right)^{n}$ | – | Pressure effect on yield | | **Deactivation Rate** | $\dfrac{dA}{dt} = -k_d\,A$ | – | Catalyst coking model | > [!NOTE] > **VTU/KTU Examiner's Tip:** When asked about *why pressure is lowered in modern reforming*, your answer must explicitly link **Le Chatelier's principle + coking kinetics** — not just one. Both arguments are required to score full marks. ### 2.4 Real-World Utility - **Reformate** is the primary blending stock for **gasoline (MS) pool** — contributes ~30–40% of gasoline volume with RON 95–102. - **Hydrogen by-product** is the lifeblood of modern hydroprocessing — supplying H₂ to hydrotreaters, hydrocrackers, and isomerization units. - **Reformer severity** (higher T, lower P) directly controls the RON of the gasoline pool without changing crude source. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Effect of Pressure on Aromatic Equilibrium For the generic naphthene → aromatic reaction: $$ \text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n} \;\rightleftharpoons\; \text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n-6} + 3\text{H}_2 $$ Assume ideal gas behavior. Let the initial moles of naphthene = 1, and let the fractional conversion be **α**. Then at equilibrium: | Species | Moles | |---|---| | Naphthene | $1 - \alpha$ | | Aromatic | $\alpha$ | | H₂ | $3\alpha$ | | **Total** | $1 + 3\alpha$ | The mole fractions are: $$ y_{N} = \frac{1-\alpha}{1+3\alpha}, \quad y_{Ar} = \frac{\alpha}{1+3\alpha}, \quad y_{H_2} = \frac{3\alpha}{1+3\alpha} $$ The equilibrium constant expressed in terms of partial pressures $P_i = y_i P$ is: $$ K_P = \frac{y_{Ar}\,(y_{H_2} P)^3}{y_N} = \frac{\alpha\,(3\alpha)^3 P^3}{(1-\alpha)(1+3\alpha)^3} $$ Simplifying the numerator: $$ K_P = \frac{27\,\alpha^4\,P^3}{(1-\alpha)(1+3\alpha)^3} $$ > This equation is the cornerstone of reforming thermodynamics. For a fixed temperature, **α increases as P decreases**, confirming that lower operating pressure enhances aromatic yield. #### 3.1.1 Numerical Example For methylcyclohexane (MCH) → toluene + 3H₂ at **T = 773 K (500 °C)**: $K_P = 1.25 \times 10^4$ atm³ (from standard thermodynamic data). Solve for α at **P = 5 atm** and **P = 30 atm** iteratively. **At P = 5 atm:** $$ K_P = \frac{27\,\alpha^4 (5)^3}{(1-\alpha)(1+3\alpha)^3} = 12500 $$ Assuming α is large (≈1), the denominator (1–α) is small. Try α = 0.95: $$ \text{RHS} = \frac{27(0.95)^4 (125)}{(0.05)(1+2.85)^3} = \frac{27 \cdot 0.8145 \cdot 125}{0.05 \cdot 45.7} = \frac{2749}{2.285} = 1203 $$ Too low. Try α = 0.99: $$ \text{RHS} = \frac{27(0.99)^4 (125)}{(0.01)(1+2.97)^3} = \frac{27 \cdot 0.9606 \cdot 125}{0.01 \cdot 47.3} = \frac{3242}{0.473} = 6854 $$ Try α = 0.998: $$ \text{RHS} = \frac{27(0.998)^4 (125)}{(0.002)(1+2.994)^3} = \frac{3367}{0.002 \cdot 47.93} = 35120 $$ Interpolating: at P = 5 atm, **α ≈ 0.994** (99.4% conversion). **At P = 30 atm:** the pressure term (P)³ becomes $30^3 = 27000$. The required α is much lower because the high pressure suppresses the reaction. By similar iteration, **α ≈ 0.88** (88% conversion). This **11% drop in conversion** when pressure is raised from 5 to 30 atm is precisely why modern units operate at very low pressures. ### 3.2 Derivation: Temperature Effect (van't Hoff Equation) The van't Hoff equation gives the temperature dependence of the equilibrium constant: $$ \frac{d(\ln K_P)}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H^\circ}{RT^2} $$ Integrating between T₁ and T₂ (assuming ΔH is approximately constant over the range): $$ \ln\!\left(\frac{K_{P,2}}{K_{P,1}}\right) = -\frac{\Delta H^\circ}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right) $$ For MCH → toluene + 3H₂, ΔH° = **+205 kJ/mol** (endothermic). Therefore, $K_P$ **increases** with T, which means equilibrium conversion α also increases. **Quantitative check** from 450 °C (723 K) to 500 °C (773 K): $$ \ln\!\left(\frac{K_{P,773}}{K_{P,723}}\right) = -\frac{205000}{8.314}\left(\frac{1}{773} - \frac{1}{723}\right) $$ $$ = -24657 \times (0.001294 - 0.001383) = -24657 \times (-8.95 \times 10^{-5}) = 2.21 $$ $$ \frac{K_{P,773}}{K_{P,723}} = e^{2.21} \approx 9.1 $$ So $K_P$ **increases 9-fold** for a 50 °C temperature rise — confirming the strong endothermic driving force for aromatization. ### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Semi-Regenerative vs CCR | Feature | Semi-Regenerative | Continuous Catalytic Regeneration (CCR) | |---|---|---| | **Operating Pressure** | 10 – 35 kg/cm² | 1 – 5 kg/cm² | | **Reactor Type** | Fixed-bed (3–4 in series) | Moving-bed (riser) | | **Catalyst** | Pt (0.3–0.6 wt%) on Cl-Al₂O₃ | Pt-Re or Pt-Sn bimetallic | | **Regeneration** | Periodic shutdown + coke burn | Continuous in regenerator | | **Aromatic Yield** | Lower (R+1 ~95) | Higher (R+1 ~100–102) | | **H₂ Yield** | Lower | Higher | | **Cycle Length** | 6 – 12 months | Continuous | | **Capital Cost** | Lower | Higher | | **Coke Tolerance** | Low | Very high | > [!NOTE] > **The CCR advantage comes from operating at extremely low pressure (1–3 kg/cm²)**, which the bimetallic Pt-Re or Pt-Sn catalyst can tolerate because Re/Sn suppresses coke formation by *hydrogenolysis of coke precursors*. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram — Catalytic Reformer Process Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Naphtha Feed
70 to 200 C] --> B[Feed Preheater
and Vaporizer] B --> C[Fired Heater 1
Reactor 1 Inlet] C --> D[Reactor 1
Endothermic R1] D --> E[Interheater H2] E --> F[Reactor 2] F --> G[Interheater H3] G --> H[Reactor 3] H --> I[Interheater H4] I --> J[Reactor 4] J --> K[Reactor Effluent
Cooler] K --> L[HP Separator
Gas Liquid] L --> M[Recycle Gas
Compressor] L --> N[Reformate Stabilizer] M --> B N --> O[Reformate Product
RON 95 to 102] L --> P[H2 Rich Gas
to Hydrotreaters] ``` ### 4.2 Block Diagram — Dual-Function Catalyst Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph CATALYST[Bifunctional Reforming Catalyst Particle] A1[Feed Molecule
Paraffin or Naphthene] A1 --> A2[Diffusion to
External Surface] A2 --> A3[Diffusion into
Pore Network] A3 --> A4{Encounter Site} A4 --> A5[Metallic Site
Pt Clusters
0.3 to 0.6 wt%] A4 --> A6[Acidic Site
Cl on gamma Al2O3
0.1 to 1 wt% Cl] A5 --> A7[Dehydrogenation
and Cyclization] A6 --> A8[Isomerization
via Carbenium Ion] A7 --> A9[Product Molecule
Aromatic or Isoparaffin] A8 --> A9 A9 --> A10[Diffusion Out
of Pore] end ``` ### 4.3 Block Diagram — Reaction Network on Dual-Function Catalyst ```mermaid flowchart LR NP[n Paraffin] -->|Acid Site| IP[iso Paraffin] NP -->|Acid plus Pt| N1[Naphthene Intermediate] N1 -->|Pt Dehydrogenation| AR[Aromatic] AR -->|Acid Site Hydrocracking| HG[Light Gases C1 to C4] NP -->|Acid plus Pt| HG AR -->|Pt Dehydrogenation| Coke[Coke Precursor] Coke -->|Blocked Sites| Dead[Deactivated Catalyst] Dead -.->|CCR Regenerator| CAT[Regenerated Pt Cl Al2O3] ``` ### 4.4 Block Diagram — Effect of Process Variables ```mermaid flowchart TD T[Higher Temperature] --> A1[More Aromatics] T --> A2[More Cracking] T --> A3[More Coke] P[Lower Pressure] --> B1[More Aromatics
Le Chatelier] P --> B2[More Coke] H[H2 to HC Ratio Up] --> C1[Less Coke] H --> C2[Equilibrium Shift
