Toward Naphthene] H --> C3[More Hydrocracking] W[WHSV Down] --> D1[More Conversion] W --> D2[More Cracking and Coke] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Insight from the Diagrams:** The dual-function catalyst forces the feed to "hop" between metal sites (for dehydrogenation) and acid sites (for skeletal rearrangement). The **spacing between the two site types** is critical — too close, and cracking dominates; too far, and the bifunctional synergy is lost. Re and Sn promoters fine-tune this *inter-site distance* by selectively blocking the strongest acid sites that cause overcracking. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 PART A Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *"What is a dual-function (bifunctional) catalyst in catalytic reforming? List the two active functions and identify the metal and acid components."* **(3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** A dual-function catalyst performs two distinct catalytic roles simultaneously: 1. **Metallic (Dehydrogenation) Function** — Provided by finely dispersed **Platinum (Pt)** particles (0.3–0.6 wt%) supported on the carrier. Pt catalyzes *dehydrogenation* of naphthenes to aromatics and *dehydrocyclization* of paraffins. 2. **Acidic (Isomerization) Function** — Provided by **chloride (Cl⁻)-impregnated γ-alumina (γ-Al₂O₃)**. The chloride (0.1–1.0 wt%) creates Lewis and Brønsted acid sites that catalyze *skeletal isomerization* of paraffins via carbenium ion intermediates and promote cyclization. The two functions work *in tandem*: paraffins are first isomerized on acid sites, then cyclized and dehydrogenated on Pt sites to yield aromatics. The relative strength of the two functions must be balanced to maximize aromatics and minimize hydrocracking and coking. > [!NOTE] > Modern catalysts are often **bimetallic** (e.g., Pt-Re, Pt-Sn), where the second metal improves catalyst life and selectivity by suppressing coke and stabilizing Pt dispersion. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** *"Explain why catalytic reforming is operated at low hydrogen-to-hydrocarbon ratio and low pressure in modern units."* **(3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **Low Pressure (1–5 kg/cm² in CCR units):** - The aromatization reaction (naphthene → aromatic + 3H₂) is endothermic and produces more moles of gas; by **Le Chatelier's principle**, lowering the partial pressure of H₂ shifts the equilibrium toward the aromatic side, increasing aromatic yield. - Modern bimetallic Pt-Re or Pt-Sn catalysts can tolerate the higher coking tendency at low pressure, allowing continuous regeneration (CCR). **Low H₂/HC Ratio (3–6 : 1):** - Hydrogen is necessary to suppress coke precursor polymerization and to keep the metal/acid sites clean. - However, an excessively high H₂ ratio pushes the equilibrium *back* toward naphthenes and increases hydrocracking — wasting valuable feed to light gases. - The modern optimum is a *minimum* H₂/HC ratio (typically 3:1) that still provides adequate coking suppression, combined with continuous catalyst regeneration to remove the small amount of coke that does form. --- ### 5.2 PART B Questions (Module Internal Choice — 14 Marks Each) --- #### **QUESTION A — Choice Option 1** *(14 Marks)* **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 | Module 3]** **(a)** *Discuss in detail the effect of the four major operating variables — temperature, pressure, H₂/HC ratio, and space velocity — on the performance of a catalytic reforming unit.* **(7 Marks)** **(b)** *With the help of a neat sketch, describe the composition and structure of a modern bimetallic reforming catalyst. Explain the role of each component.* **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Effect of Operating Variables — 7 Marks** **(i) Temperature (450–525 °C):** *(2 marks)* - Higher temperature increases the rate of all four reforming reactions but particularly favors the *endothermic* dehydrogenation and dehydrocyclization reactions (van't Hoff analysis shows K_P increases ~9× per 50 °C rise for MCH → toluene). - Higher T also accelerates *undesired* hydrocracking and coke formation; hence a practical upper limit of ~525 °C is observed. - Because the overall reaction is strongly endothermic, multiple adiabatic reactors in series with inter-stage reheaters are used to maintain temperature in the optimum window. **(ii) Pressure (1–35 kg/cm²):** *(2 marks)* - The aromatization reactions produce H₂ (Δn > 0); lowering the system pressure shifts equilibrium toward aromatics per Le Chatelier's principle. - Quantitative analysis shows conversion of methylcyclohexane drops from ~99% at 5 atm to ~88% at 30 atm at 500 °C. - However, low pressure increases coke formation. The compromise between yield and coking is the central design challenge — solved by bimetallic catalysts and CCR. **(iii) H₂/HC Molar Ratio (3–6 : 1):** *(1.5 marks)* - Sufficient H₂ partial pressure is needed to suppress coke by hydrogenating polycyclic coke precursors before they graphitize. - Excess H₂ reduces equilibrium conversion (back-shift reaction), so the modern trend is the *minimum* ratio consistent with acceptable coking. **(iv) WHSV (1–3 hr⁻¹):** *(1.5 marks)* - WHSV is the inverse of contact time. Lower WHSV (longer contact) → higher conversion but more cracking and coking. - Typical optimum ≈ 1.5–2 hr⁻¹ for fixed-bed, 2–3 hr⁻¹ for CCR units. **(b) Bimetallic Reforming Catalyst — 7 Marks** *Refer to Section 4.2 diagram for the schematic.* A modern bimetallic reforming catalyst consists of the following components (composition by mass): | Component | Composition | Function | |---|---|---| | **γ-Al₂O₃ Support** | > 95% | High surface area (200 m²/g), porous pellet carrier | | **Platinum (Pt)** | 0.3 – 0.6 wt% | Metallic dehydrogenation function | | **Rhenium (Re) or Tin (Sn)** | 0.3 – 0.5 wt% | Promotes Pt dispersion, suppresses coke | | **Chloride (Cl)** | 0.1 – 1.0 wt% | Creates acidic sites on alumina surface | **Roles of each component:** - **γ-Al₂O₃ Support:** Provides high surface area, mechanical strength, and thermal stability. Its Lewis acid sites are *activated* by chloride addition. - **Platinum (Pt):** Catalyzes dehydrogenation of naphthenes (very fast, equilibrium-limited) and provides the metal function for dehydrocyclization. Pt must be finely dispersed (1–10 nm clusters); large Pt crystals cause excessive hydrocracking. - **Rhenium (Re):** Strongly interacts with Pt to form Pt-Re clusters that: 1. Prevent Pt sintering at high regeneration temperatures (up to 500 °C). 2. Hydrogenolyze coke precursors back to volatile products, dramatically lowering net coke deposition. - **Tin (Sn):** An alternative to Re; Sn preferentially blocks the strongest acid sites (which cause overcracking) and reduces metal–acid site interaction, improving selectivity. - **Chloride (Cl):** Maintains the *acidic function*. Without Cl, γ-alumina has only weak Lewis acidity. Cl is mobile and can be lost during operation; reformer feed is therefore often *chlorided* with organic chlorides (e.g., CCl₄) to maintain Cl inventory. --- #### **QUESTION B — Choice Option 2** *(14 Marks)* **[KTU University Exam – June 2023 | Module 3]** **(a)** *List the four main chemical reactions occurring in catalytic reforming. For each, state the heat of reaction (endothermic or exothermic) and identify whether the reaction is desired or undesired.* **(7 Marks)** **(b)** *Explain the role of hydrogen in catalytic reforming. Why is a hydrogen-rich atmosphere maintained in the reactors? Discuss the consequences of operating with insufficient hydrogen partial pressure.* **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Four Main Reforming Reactions — 7 Marks** | # | Reaction | ΔH° (approx.) | Desired? | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Dehydrogenation of naphthenes → aromatics | +205 kJ/mol (endothermic) | **Yes — Primary** | | 2 | Isomerization of n-paraffins → iso-paraffins | –5 to –15 kJ/mol (mildly exothermic) | **Yes — Secondary** | | 3 | Dehydrocyclization of paraffins → aromatics | +260 kJ/mol (highly endothermic) | **Yes — Important** | | 4 | Hydrocracking of paraffins → light gases | –45 kJ/mol (exothermic) | **No — Undesired** | **Detailed discussion:** 1. **Naphthene Dehydrogenation (Primary desired):** Fastest of all reforming reactions. Example: methylcyclohexane → toluene + 3H₂. Highly endothermic, equilibrium-limited; maximized by high T, low P, H₂ removal. **(1.75 marks)** 2. **Paraffin Isomerization (Desired):** Mildly exothermic; occurs on acid sites via carbenium ions. No change in carbon number or H₂ balance. Increases RON because iso-paraffins have higher octane than n-paraffins. **(1.75 marks)** 3. **Paraffin Dehydrocyclization (Desired, hardest):** Straight-chain paraffin → 5- or 6-membered naphthene → aromatic. Requires both metal and acid sites; slowest of the desired reactions. Most endothermic — strongly favored by high T. **(1.75 marks)** 4. **Hydrocracking (Undesired):** Acid-site-catalyzed C–C scission with H₂ consumption. Produces light gases (C₁–C₄) that lower reformate yield. Also the *initiator* of coke formation. **(1.75 marks)** **(b) Role of Hydrogen — 7 Marks** Hydrogen plays **three critical roles** in catalytic reforming: **1. Suppression of Coke Formation (3 marks):** - Coke forms by polymerization and dehydrogenation of polycyclic aromatic precursors on the catalyst surface. - A high H₂ partial pressure *hydrogenates* these precursors back to volatile species before they can graphitize, dramatically extending catalyst life. - Without sufficient H₂, the catalyst deactivates within hours due to pore blockage. **2. Stripping of Catalyst Poisons (2 marks):** - Sulfur, nitrogen, and oxygen compounds in the feed would otherwise poison the Pt sites. - The H₂ atmosphere converts organic S to H₂S, organic N to NH₃ — both of which are stripped from the recycle gas stream. - Therefore, the reformer effluent gas is rich in H₂S, requiring amine scrubbing before recycling. **3. Provision of Reactant for Hydrocracking (2 marks):** - Although hydrocracking is undesired, the small amount that occurs requires H₂ to proceed. - The H₂/HC ratio (3–6 : 1) is therefore a *balance*: too low → coke; too high → excessive cracking & lower aromatic equilibrium yield. **Consequences of Insufficient H₂:** - Rapid catalyst deactivation (coking) - Lower reformate yield - Exothermic runaway due to coke-induced hot spots - Loss of Pt metal dispersion due to localized heating > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall:** > A frequent mistake students make is stating that "hydrogen reacts with the feed" or "hydrogen increases the octane number." These are **wrong**. Hydrogen does not react *with* naphtha to form aromatics — it is a *product* of the aromatization reaction. The role of H₂ is **environmental** (suppressing coke, stripping poisons) — not chemical conversion. Examiners specifically deduct marks for this conceptual confusion. Always phrase your answer in terms of *partial pressure effects, coking kinetics, and thermodynamic suppression of polymerization*. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **📋 Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3: Reaction Conditions & Catalyst** **Core Definitions:** - Catalytic reforming = **aromatization of naphthenes and paraffins** to produce high-octane reformate + H₂. - Feed = **straight-run heavy naphtha** (70–200 °C, C₆–C₁₁). - Reformate RON = **95–102**. **Reaction Conditions — Must Remember the Ranges:** - **Temperature:** 450 – 525 °C (typical 500 °C) - **Pressure:** 1 – 35 kg/cm² (1–5 for CCR, 10–35 for semi-regen) - **H₂/HC molar ratio:** 3 – 6 : 1 - **WHSV:** 1 – 3 hr⁻¹ **Catalyst — Must Know the Composition:** - **Pt** (0.3–0.6 wt%) — metallic dehydrogenation - **Cl** (0.1–1.0 wt%) — acidic function - **γ-Al₂O₃** — high surface area support - **Bimetallic promoters:** Re or Sn (0.3–0.5 wt%) - **Bifunctionality** = Pt does dehydrogenation, Cl-Al₂O₃ does isomerization/cyclization **Four Key Reactions — Heat Effects:** - Naphthene → Aromatic: **+205 kJ/mol** (endothermic, desired) - Paraffin → Iso-paraffin: **–5 to –15 kJ/mol** (mildly exothermic, desired) - Paraffin → Aromatic (dehydrocyclization): **+260 kJ/mol** (highly endothermic, desired) - Hydrocracking: **–45 kJ/mol** (exothermic, undesired) **Trade-offs to Always Mention:** - ↓ Pressure → ↑ Aromatic yield but ↑ Coking - ↑ Temperature → ↑ Aromatic yield but ↑ Cracking - ↑ H₂/HC ratio → ↓ Coking but ↓ Aromatic equilibrium - ↓ WHSV → ↑ Conversion but ↑ Coke **High-Yield Derivations to Practice:** - Le Chatelier pressure effect: $K_P = \dfrac{27\alpha^4 P^3}{(1-\alpha)(1+3\alpha)^3}$ - van't Hoff temperature effect: $\ln\!\left(\dfrac{K_{P,2}}{K_{P,1}}\right) = -\dfrac{\Delta H^\circ}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T_2} - \dfrac{1}{T_1}\right)$ **Reactor Configuration:** - 3–4 **adiabatic fixed-bed reactors** in series (semi-regenerative) **or** - **Moving-bed CCR** with continuous regeneration **Examiner's High-Marks Triggers:** - Use the phrase *"bifunctional catalyst"* (not "dual catalyst"). - Always link *pressure reduction* to **both** Le Chatelier and coking kinetics. - Always mention **bimetallic Pt-Re or Pt-Sn** when asked about modern catalysts. - Use the term **"hydrocarbon to hydrogen recycle gas ratio"** correctly (not "hydrogen content"). - Mention **inter-stage reheaters** when discussing reactor configuration. > [!TIP] > **Mnemonic for the Four Reactions:** **"N-I-P-C"** → **N**aphthene dehydrogenation, **I**somerization, **P**araffin dehydrocyclization, **C**racking. Remember: the *desired* three start with N, I, P and the *undesired* one is C (Cracking).

topics.title: Platforming
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT638 - PETROLEUM REFINERY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Platforming

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Platforming** is a proprietary **catalytic reforming process** developed and licensed by **UOP (Universal Oil Products)** in **1949**. It is a continuous catalytic process that converts low-octane heavy naphtha (C₆–C₁₂ hydrocarbons) into high-octane gasoline blending stock (reformate), aromatics (BTX — Benzene, Toluene, Xylene), and large quantities of **hydrogen** by passing the vaporized feed over a fixed bed of platinum (Pt) or platinum-rhenium (Pt-Re) catalyst supported on chlorided alumina at elevated temperatures and moderate pressures in the presence of recycled hydrogen.

The name **"Platforming"** is a portmanteau of **"Platinum"** and **"Reforming"**, directly indicating the use of a **platinum-based catalyst** for the molecular rearrangement (reformation) of hydrocarbon molecules.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Molecular Makeover Salon"**
> Imagine a salon where low-quality hair (straight, dull paraffins and naphthenes) walks in and walks out as beautiful, curly, shiny hair (aromatic rings with high octane). The "stylists" are tiny **platinum atoms** scattered on a sponge-like alumina surface. The salon needs:
> - **High heat** (≈ 500 °C) to break and rearrange molecular bonds,
> - **Hydrogen gas (H₂)** floating around the room to prevent the hair from being totally destroyed (it suppresses carbon lay-down/coking on the stylist's hands),
> - **Specific pressure** to keep the process economical.
>
> The "scissors" cut straight chains, "curlers" loop them into rings, and "hairdryers" (dehydrogenation) remove hydrogen to form those rings. The result: a premium, high-octane product ready for the gasoline market.

## 1.3 Key Process Metrics and Physical Anchors

| Metric | Typical Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Operating Temperature** | **450 – 520 °C** (840 – 970 °F) | Provides activation energy for endothermic reforming reactions |
| **Reactor Pressure** | **3.5 – 35 atm** (modern: low pressure ≈ 3.5 atm) | Low pressure favors aromatics (Le Chatelier's principle) |
| **Hydrogen / Hydrocarbon Molar Ratio** | **3 : 1 to 6 : 1** | Suppresses coke, extends catalyst life |
| **WHSV (Weight Hourly Space Velocity)** | **1.0 – 2.5 h⁻¹** | Controls feed contact time |
| **Research Octane Number (RON)** | Feed ≈ 40–60 → Reformate ≈ **95–102** | Primary quality indicator |
| **Hydrogen Purity** | **75 – 92 mol %** (dry gas) | By-product used in hydrodesulfurization units |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – Module 3):** Platforming is a *core* topic. Students must master: (i) the four major reaction classes, (ii) the role of platinum and bimetallic catalysts, (iii) the multi-reactor design with interstage heaters, and (iv) the effect of low-pressure operation on aromatics yield.

## 1.4 Historical & Industrial Context

- **Pre-Platforming era**: Thermal reforming (c. 1930s) used high temperature and pressure but produced low-octane, unstable gasoline with poor yields.
- **1949 — UOP Platforming**: First commercial catalytic reformer using **monometallic Pt/Al₂O₃**.
- **1960s — Rhenium promotion**: Introduction of **Pt-Re bimetallic catalyst** allowed operation at lower pressure with longer cycle lengths.
- **1970s — Continuous Catalyst Regeneration (CCR) Platforming** introduced for ultra-low pressure operation and high aromatics yield.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Major Reaction Classes in Platforming

The complex chemistry of catalytic reforming is conventionally grouped into four reaction families. The first two are **highly endothermic and favored at low pressure / high temperature**, while the fourth is **exothermic and parasitic**.

### Reaction 1: Dehydrogenation of Naphthenes → Aromatics (Endothermic, Fastest)

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12} \text{ (cyclohexane)} &\longrightarrow \text{C}_6\text{H}_6 \text{ (benzene)} + 3\text{H}_2 \quad \Delta H_{r} \approx +205 \text{ kJ/mol} \\
\text{C}_7\text{H}_{14} \text{ (methylcyclohexane)} &\longrightarrow \text{C}_7\text{H}_8 \text{ (toluene)} + 3\text{H}_2 \\
\text{C}_8\text{H}_{16} \text{ (dimethylcyclohexane)} &\longrightarrow \text{C}_8\text{H}_{10} \text{ (xylene)} + 3\text{H}_2
\end{aligned}$$

- **Why important?** Contributes the **largest single yield of aromatics**.
- **Kinetics:** Approximate first-order; very fast; reaches near-equilibrium in the first reactor.
- **KTU Logic:** Net H₂ producer.

### Reaction 2: Isomerization of n-Paraffins → iso-Paraffins (Mildly Exothermic)

$$n\text{-C}_5\text{H}_{12} \rightleftharpoons iso\text{-C}_5\text{H}_{12} \quad \Delta H_{r} \approx -7 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

$$n\text{-C}_6\text{H}_{14} \rightleftharpoons 2,2\text{-dimethylbutane} \quad (\text{RON jump from } 25 \to 92)$$

- **Why important?** Boosts octane **without producing extra aromatics** — useful for low-aromatic gasoline (e.g., California-grade reformate).
- **Catalytic site:** Acidic chlorided alumina (the Cl functionality).

### Reaction 3: Dehydrocyclization of Paraffins → Aromatics (Endothermic, Slowest)

$$n\text{-C}_6\text{H}_{14} \longrightarrow \text{C}_6\text{H}_6 + 4\text{H}_2 \quad \Delta H_{r} \approx +260 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

$$n\text{-C}_7\text{H}_{16} \longrightarrow \text{C}_7\text{H}_8 + 4\text{H}_2$$

- **Why important?** Converts the **least reactive paraffins** into aromatics, dramatically boosting both octane and aromatic yield.
- **Difficulty:** Slowest; requires longer residence time → last reactor in the train.

### Reaction 4: Hydrocracking (Exothermic, Undesirable)

$$\text{C}_8\text{H}_{18} + \text{H}_2 \longrightarrow 2\text{ C}_4\text{H}_{10} \quad \Delta H_{r} \approx -160 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

- **Why undesirable?** Destroys potential reformate yield, produces light gases (C₁–C₄).
- **Suppression:** Adequate H₂ partial pressure, controlled acid function of catalyst.

## 2.2 The Bifunctional Catalyst — Two Active Sites in One Bead

> [!NOTE]
> Platforming catalyst is a **bifunctional** material. Each pellet performs two distinct chemistries simultaneously.

| Function | Active Site | Reactions Handled | Role |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Metallic (dehydrogenation)** | Pt⁰ (or Pt-Re) nanoclusters, 0.5–1.5 nm | Dehydrogenation of naphthenes; dehydrocyclization | Releases H₂, forms C=C and aromatic rings |
| **Acidic (isomerization)** | Cl⁻-doped γ-Al₂O₃ (Lewis + Brønsted sites) | Skeletal isomerization of paraffins and naphthenes | Rearranges carbon skeleton |

The **dual-site mechanism** requires the reactant to migrate: an olefin is dehydrogenated on Pt, isomerized on acid, re-hydrogenated on Pt.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are *examinable* in **Part A (3 marks)** numerical sub-parts and in the **aromatics yield / H₂ balance** sub-questions of **Part B (14 marks)**.

| # | Formula / Relation | Description / Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Y_{\text{arom}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{arom,out}} - m_{\text{arom,in}}}{m_{\text{feed}}} \times 100\%$ | Aromatics yield on feed (wt%) |
| 2 | $\text{Conversion}_{\text{napht}} = \dfrac{n_{\text{napht,in}} - n_{\text{napht,out}}}{n_{\text{napht,in}}} \times 100\%$ | Naphthene conversion (target: > 95%) |
| 3 | $\text{Conv.}_{P \to A} = \dfrac{n_{P,\text{in}} - n_{P,\text{out}} - n_{A,\text{from naph}}}{n_{P,\text{in}}} \times 100\%$ | Paraffin dehydrocyclization conversion |
| 4 | $\text{WHSV} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{\text{feed}}}{m_{\text{cat}}} \;\; [\text{h}^{-1}]$ | Weight Hourly Space Velocity |
| 5 | $\text{LHSV} = \dfrac{V_{\text{feed (liq, h}^{-1})}}{V_{\text{cat}}}$ | Liquid Hourly Space Velocity |
| 6 | $\dfrac{\text{H}_2}{\text{HC}} = \dfrac{\dot{n}_{\text{H}_2,\text{recycle}}}{\dot{n}_{\text{feed,HC}}}$ | Molar H₂-to-hydrocarbon recycle ratio |
| 7 | $\text{Le Chatelier (dehydrogenation):} \;\; \downarrow P \Rightarrow \uparrow \text{ aromatics yield}$ | Lower pressure drives equilibrium toward aromatics + H₂ |
| 8 | $\text{Equilibrium constant: } K_p = \dfrac{p_A \cdot p_{\text{H}_2}^3}{p_N}$ for cyclohexane → benzene | Used in thermodynamic limit calculations |
| 9 | $\text{Octane Barrel} = \text{RON} \times \text{Volume (bbl/day)}$ | Measure of reformate commercial value |
| 10 | $\eta_{\text{H}_2} = \dfrac{\dot{n}_{\text{H}_2,\text{net produced}}}{\dot{m}_{\text{feed}}} \;\; [\text{SCF/bbl or Nm}^3/\text{m}^3]$ | Hydrogen production efficiency |
| 11 | $\Delta T_{\text{reactor}} = \dfrac{-\Delta H_r \cdot \xi}{F_{\text{feed}} \cdot C_p}$ | Adiabatic temperature drop used to size interstage heaters |
| 12 | $\text{Carbon balance:} \;\; \sum_i n_i^{\text{in}} = \sum_i n_i^{\text{out}}$ | Stoichiometric verification of H₂ and aromatics |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Do not confuse the **dehydrogenation of naphthenes** (Reaction 1, produces 3 H₂) with **dehydrocyclization of paraffins** (Reaction 3, produces 4 H₂). This single H₂ stoichiometric difference is a common 1-mark trap in Part A.

## 2.4 Why Low Pressure? — Thermodynamic Justification

For the net dehydrogenation to aromatics:

$$ \text{Naphthene (g)} \rightleftharpoons \text{Aromatic (g)} + 3 \text{H}_2 \text{ (g)} $$

By **Le Chatelier's principle**, since Δn_gas = +3, a reduction in total pressure shifts the equilibrium **rightward**, increasing aromatics yield. This is why modern **CCR (Continuous Catalyst Regeneration) Platforming** operates at **≈ 3.5 atm (≈ 50 psig)** compared to the original 35 atm units.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Trade-off:** Lower pressure increases aromatics yield **and** hydrogen yield, *but* also accelerates **coking** (carbon deposition on catalyst), shortening cycle length. This trade-off is resolved by:
> (a) Using **Pt-Re bimetallic catalyst** (coke tolerant), and
> (b) Adopting **CCR** where coke is continuously burned off in a separate regenerator.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Problems & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Problem 1 — Material Balance on a Platforming Reactor

> **Problem:** A Platforming unit processes **10,000 kg/h** of naphtha feed containing 35 wt% naphthenes and 65 wt% paraffins. The reformate contains 12 wt% naphthenes, 28 wt% paraffins, and 60 wt% aromatics. Calculate the **(a) naphthene conversion**, **(b) aromatics yield on feed**, and **(c) the approximate net H₂ produced (kg/h)** assuming the dominant reaction is cyclohexane → benzene. (Molar masses: cyclohexane = 84, benzene = 48, H₂ = 2.)

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the input streams on a 10,000 kg/h feed basis.**

- Naphthenes in feed: 0.35 × 10,000 = 3,500 kg/h
- Paraffins in feed: 0.65 × 10,000 = 6,500 kg/h

**Step 2 — Determine the output (reformate) composition.**

For a 100 kg basis reformate: 12 kg naphthenes, 28 kg paraffins, 60 kg aromatics.

Let total reformate flow = M kg/h. Mass balance (assuming negligible C₅⁻ loss for this part):

$$3{,}500 + 6{,}500 = (0.12 + 0.28 + 0.60) \cdot M \cdot \frac{\text{feed basis}}{\text{reformate basis}}$$

Wait — we must allow for H₂ leaving the liquid. So a portion of feed (≈ 3–5 wt%) leaves as H₂ + light gas. For mass balance we use:

$$\text{Feed} = \text{Reformate} + \text{H}_2 + \text{Light ends}$$

Assuming a typical 3% gas yield: Reformate ≈ 9,700 kg/h. Then:

- Naphthenes out: 0.12 × 9,700 = 1,164 kg/h
- Paraffins out: 0.28 × 9,700 = 2,716 kg/h
- Aromatics out: 0.60 × 9,700 = 5,820 kg/h

**Step 3 — Compute naphthene conversion.**

$$\begin{aligned}
X_{\text{napht}} &= \dfrac{3{,}500 - 1{,}164}{3{,}500} \times 100\% \\
&= \dfrac{2{,}336}{3{,}500} \times 100\% \\
&\approx 66.74\%
\end{aligned}$$

> **Answer (a):** Naphthene conversion ≈ **66.7 %**.

**Step 4 — Compute aromatics yield on feed.**

$$\begin{aligned}
Y_{\text{arom}} &= \dfrac{5{,}820 - 0}{10{,}000} \times 100\% \\
&= 58.2\%
\end{aligned}$$

> **Answer (b):** Aromatics yield ≈ **58.2 wt% on feed**.

**Step 5 — Compute H₂ produced (from naphthene dehydrogenation only).**

Naphthenes converted = 2,336 kg/h. Assume all converted naphthenes are C₆ (M = 84). Moles of naphthene reacted:

$$n_{\text{napht}} = \dfrac{2{,}336}{84} = 27.81 \text{ kmol/h}$$

Each mole of cyclohexane dehydrogenates to benzene + 3 H₂. So:

$$n_{\text{H}_2} = 3 \times 27.81 = 83.43 \text{ kmol/h}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{H}_2} &= 83.43 \times 2 \\
&= 166.86 \text{ kg/h} \\
&\approx 167 \text{ kg/h}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Answer (c):** H₂ produced ≈ **167 kg/h** from naphthene dehydrogenation alone (real plants produce 250–400 kg/h when paraffin dehydrocyclization is also included).

---

## 3.2 Worked Problem 2 — Adiabatic Temperature Drop Across First Reactor

> **Problem:** In the first Platforming reactor, **90% of cyclohexane** in the feed is converted to benzene. Feed enters at **510 °C**. Compute the **adiabatic temperature drop** in the first reactor if the inlet mass flow is 10,000 kg/h with 35% naphthenes (all C₆). Use $\Delta H_r = +205$ kJ/mol for cyclohexane → benzene, and assume average $C_p$ of the reactor contents ≈ **3.0 kJ/(kg·K)**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Calculate mass of cyclohexane reacted.**

$$m_{\text{reacted}} = 0.35 \times 10{,}000 \times 0.90 = 3{,}150 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to moles.**

$$n = \dfrac{3{,}150}{84} = 37.5 \text{ kmol/h}$$

**Step 3 — Compute total enthalpy absorbed by the endothermic reaction.**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{abs}} &= n \cdot \Delta H_r \\
&= 37.5 \times 10^3 \text{ mol/h} \times 205 \text{ kJ/mol} \\
&= 7.6875 \times 10^6 \text{ kJ/h} \\
&\approx 2{,}135.4 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the energy balance to find ΔT.**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{abs}} &= \dot{m}_{\text{total}} \cdot C_p \cdot (-\Delta T) \\
2{,}135.4 \text{ kW} &= \dfrac{10{,}000 \text{ kg/h}}{3{,}600} \times 3.0 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)} \cdot (-\Delta T) \\
2{,}135.4 &= 8.333 \times 3.0 \cdot (-\Delta T) \\
2{,}135.4 &= 25.0 \cdot (-\Delta T) \\
\Delta T &\approx -85.4 \text{ °C}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Answer:** Temperature drops by **≈ 85 °C** across the first reactor — this is why **interstage heaters** are mandatory between reactors. Without them, the reactor effluent would fall below the activation threshold of subsequent (slower) reactions.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's trap:** A common student error is to forget to divide by 3,600 to convert kg/h to kg/s, or to use the wrong sign for the temperature drop. The reactor *cools*, not heats, because dehydrogenation is **endothermic**.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Process Variants

| Feature | Conventional Platforming | **CCR Platforming** (Modern) |
|---|---|---|
| Pressure | 15–35 atm | **3.5 – 5 atm** |
| Catalyst | Pt/Al₂O₃ | **Pt-Sn / Pt-Re on Al₂O₃** |
| Cycle Length | 6–12 months (then shutdown) | **Continuous (1–7 day catalyst residence)** |
| Aromatics Yield | Moderate | **High (+5–10 wt%)** |
| H₂ Yield | Moderate | **High** |
| RON Capability | 95–98 | **98–102** |
| Capital Cost | Lower | Higher (regenerator + catalyst circulation) |
| Best Suited For | Small / older refineries | **Large integrated refineries** |

> [!TIP]
> For KTU 2024 short-answer questions, you may be asked: *"Why is CCR preferred over fixed-bed reforming?"* — The concise answer is: CCR permits **continuous low-pressure operation**, maximizing **aromatics yield** and **H₂ production** while **eliminating reactor shutdowns** for catalyst regeneration.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Flow Diagram — Conventional Fixed-Bed Platforming Unit (3 Reactors)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    FeedN["Naphtha Feed"] --> H1["Feed Heater F-1"]
    H1 --> R1["Reactor 1 Naphthene Dehydrogenation 510 C"]
    R1 --> IH1["Interstage Heater H-2"]
    IH1 --> R2["Reactor 2 Naphthene Dehydrogenation plus Isomerization"]
    R2 --> IH2["Interstage Heater H-3"]
    IH2 --> R3["Reactor 3 Dehydrocyclization of Paraffins"]
    R3 --> CL["Cooler E-1"]
    CL --> SEP["HP Separator V-1"]
    SEP -- "Liquid Reformate" --> STA["Stabilizer Column"]
    SEP -- "H2 rich gas" --> COMP["Recycle Compressor K-1"]
    COMP -- "Recycle H2" --> H1
    STA -- "C1 to C4 LPG" --> LPG["LPG Product"]
    STA -- "C5 plus Reformate" --> REFP["High Octane Reformate 95 to 102 RON"]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The feed mixes with **recycled H₂** at heater **F-1**, where it is vaporized and brought to ≈ 510 °C.
- Three **adiabatic fixed-bed reactors (R-1, R-2, R-3)** operate in series.
- **Interstage heaters H-2 and H-3** restore the temperature lost due to endothermic reactions.
- Effluent is cooled, separated into **recycle H₂** (returned via compressor K-1) and **liquid reformate**.
- The reformate is stabilized (light ends stripped off) to yield finished gasoline blendstock.

## 4.2 Reactor Train Temperature Profile — Visualizing the Need for Interstage Heating

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph T_profile["Temperature Profile Across Reactor Train"]
        T1["Inlet 510 C"] --> R1T["Outlet 425 C Drop 85 C"]
        R1T --> IH1T["Heater raises to 510 C"]
        IH1T --> R2T["Outlet 470 C Drop 40 C"]
        R2T --> IH2T["Heater raises to 510 C"]
        IH2T --> R3T["Outlet 490 C Drop 20 C"]
    end
```

> **Interpretation:** Notice the **progressively smaller temperature drop** in each successive reactor. This is because the highly endothermic, fast **naphthene dehydrogenation** (Reaction 1) dominates in **Reactor 1**; by **Reactor 3**, most naphthenes are depleted, leaving the slower, less endothermic dehydrocyclization. This dictates the **decreasing catalyst volume** in R-1 > R-2 > R-3.

## 4.3 Block Architecture of a CCR (Continuous Catalyst Regeneration) Platforming Unit

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph REACT["REACTION SECTION"]
        A1["Feed Mix with Recycle H2"] --> A2["Charge Heater"]
        A2 --> A3["Reactor 1 Radial Flow"]
        A3 --> A4["Reactor 2"]
        A4 --> A5["Reactor 3"]
        A5 --> A6["Reactor 4"]
    end
    A6 --> A7["Reactor Effluent Cooler"]
    A7 --> A8["HP Separator"]
    A8 -- "Net H2 Product" --> A9["H2 Purification"]
    A8 -- "Liquid" --> A10["Stabilizer"]
    A10 -- "Reformate" --> A11["Reformate Storage"]
    A10 -- "LPG" --> A12["LPG Pool"]
    A3 -. "Spent Catalyst" .-> A13["Catalift Loop to Regenerator"]
    A13 --> A14["CCR Regenerator Coke Burn Off"]
    A14 -. "Regenerated Catalyst" .-> A1
```

> **Key Innovation — The Catalyst Loop:** In CCR Platforming, spent catalyst flows **continuously** from the last reactor to a separate **regenerator** (where coke is burned off in air), and is then returned to the first reactor. This eliminates the need for periodic shutdowns and allows sustained operation at very low pressure (3.5 atm) without coking concerns.

## 4.4 Schematic — Bifunctional Catalyst Mechanism (Pt + Acid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CAT["Pt Cl Al2O3 Bifunctional Catalyst Pellet"]
        M1["Pt Site Dehydrogenation"] 
        A1["Acid Site Cl doped Alumina Isomerization"]
    end
    S1["n Paraffin Adsorbs on Pt"] --> M1
    M1 --> S2["Olefin Intermediate Migrates to Acid Site"]
    S2 --> A1
    A1 --> S3["Branched Olefin Returns to Pt"]
    S3 --> M1
    M1 --> S4["iso Paraffin Desorbs"]
```

> **Why both functions matter:** Pure Pt (without Cl) cannot isomerize; pure acid (without Pt) cannot dehydrogenate. The synergy is the heart of the Platforming chemistry.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> **Define Platforming. What is the role of platinum in the catalyst?**

**Model Answer (≈ 60 words):**
Platforming is a UOP catalytic reforming process that converts low-octane naphtha into high-octane reformate, aromatics, and hydrogen over a Pt (or Pt-Re) chlorided alumina catalyst at 450–520 °C and 1–35 atm. The **metallic Pt function** catalyzes **dehydrogenation** of naphthenes to aromatics and **dehydrocyclization** of paraffins, while the **acidic chlorided alumina** catalyzes **skeletal isomerization**. The two functions act **synergistically** on a single bifunctional pellet.

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
> **List the four major reactions occurring in a Platforming reactor. Which one is most undesirable and why?**

**Model Answer (≈ 60 words):**
1. **Dehydrogenation** of naphthenes → aromatics (endothermic, fast).
2. **Isomerization** of n-paraffins → iso-paraffins (mildly exothermic).
3. **Dehydrocyclization** of paraffins → aromatics (endothermic, slow).
4. **Hydrocracking** of heavy hydrocarbons → light gases (exothermic).

**Hydrocracking** is the most **undesirable** reaction because it **destroys potential reformate yield**, overproduces low-value C₁–C₄ gases, and is **exothermic**, causing local hot spots that accelerate catalyst coking.

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Full ESE Pattern, with Internal Choice)

> ### **Question A — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3]**
> **(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat flow diagram, describe the **process flow of a conventional fixed-bed Platforming unit** with three reactors in series. Explain the role of **interstage heaters**.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** A Platforming unit processes **12,000 kg/h** of naphtha feed (40 wt% naphthenes, 60 wt% paraffins). The reformate contains 10 wt% naphthenes, 30 wt% paraffins, and 60 wt% aromatics on a **9,500 kg/h** reformate basis. Calculate the **(i)** Naphthene conversion, **(ii)** Aromatics yield on feed, and **(iii)** Net H₂ produced (in kg/h) assuming naphthene dehydrogenation is dominant (M_naphthene ≈ 86, M_aromatic ≈ 50, M_H₂ = 2).

### Model Solution — Question A

**(a) Process Flow and Interstage Heaters (7 marks):**

A conventional Platforming unit consists of:

1. **Feed mixing & preheating:** Naphtha feed mixes with **recycled H₂** (H₂/HC ≈ 4:1 molar) at the inlet of a fired charge heater (**F-1**), which vaporizes the feed and brings it to ≈ **510 °C**.

2. **Three fixed-bed reactors in series (R-1, R-2, R-3):**
   - **R-1:** Dominated by **fast endothermic naphthene dehydrogenation**; largest catalyst volume; largest temperature drop.
   - **R-2:** Mix of dehydrogenation + isomerization; medium catalyst volume.
   - **R-3:** Dominated by **slow dehydrocyclization of paraffins**; smallest catalyst volume; smallest temperature drop.

3. **Interstage heaters (H-2, H-3):** Placed between consecutive reactors, they **reheat the partially reacted effluent** back to ≈ 510 °C. This is essential because the endothermic reactions drop the gas temperature (≈ 85 °C in R-1) below the activation threshold of subsequent reactions. **Without interstage heaters, the reactor train would "quench" and aromatics yield would collapse.**

4. **Product recovery:** Effluent is cooled (**E-1**), flashed in a **high-pressure separator (V-1)**. The **vapor overhead** is mostly H₂, compressed (**K-1**), with a portion purged and the rest recycled. The **liquid bottoms** go to a **stabilizer column** where dissolved light gases (C₁–C₄) are stripped off as LPG, leaving finished **reformate** (RON 95–102).

(Mark breakdown: 3 marks for flow diagram description, 2 marks for interstage heater explanation, 2 marks for product recovery section.)

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Inlet streams on 12,000 kg/h feed basis:**
- Naphthenes in: 0.40 × 12,000 = **4,800 kg/h**
- Paraffins in: 0.60 × 12,000 = **7,200 kg/h**

**Step 2 — Outlet streams on 9,500 kg/h reformate basis:**
- Naphthenes out: 0.10 × 9,500 = 950 kg/h
- Paraffins out: 0.30 × 9,500 = 2,850 kg/h
- Aromatics out: 0.60 × 9,500 = 5,700 kg/h

**Step 3 — (i) Naphthene conversion:**

$$X_{\text{napht}} = \dfrac{4{,}800 - 950}{4{,}800} \times 100\% = \dfrac{3{,}850}{4{,}800} \times 100\% \approx \mathbf{80.2\%}$$

**Step 4 — (ii) Aromatics yield on feed:**

$$Y_{\text{arom}} = \dfrac{5{,}700 - 0}{12{,}000} \times 100\% = \mathbf{47.5 \text{ wt\%}}$$

**Step 5 — (iii) Net H₂ produced:**

Naphthenes converted = 3,850 kg/h. Moles of naphthene reacted:

$$n = \dfrac{3{,}850}{86} = 44.77 \text{ kmol/h}$$

For cyclohexane → benzene + 3 H₂:

$$n_{\text{H}_2} = 3 \times 44.77 = 134.30 \text{ kmol/h}$$

$$m_{\text{H}_2} = 134.30 \times 2 = \mathbf{268.6 \text{ kg/h}}$$

> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> > [!WARNING]
> > Many students incorrectly **assume feed aromatics = 0** without checking the problem. Always re-read the given composition — here aromatics yield is calculated **on a no-aromatics-in-feed basis only because the problem implicitly assumes so**. Also, do not forget to multiply by **3** (not 4) for the H₂ stoichiometry of naphthene dehydrogenation.

---

> ### **Question B — [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 3]**
> **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of a **bifunctional catalyst** in Platforming. Discuss the role of **platinum** and the **acidic chlorided alumina** support. How does the addition of **rhenium** improve the catalyst performance?
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** Compare **conventional fixed-bed Platforming** with **CCR (Continuous Catalyst Regeneration) Platforming** in terms of **(i)** operating pressure, **(ii)** catalyst type, **(iii)** cycle length, **(iv)** aromatics yield, and **(v)** RON capability. Why is low-pressure operation preferred in modern reforming?

### Model Solution — Question B

**(a) Bifunctional Catalyst and Rhenium Promotion (7 marks):**

The Platforming catalyst is **bifunctional**, containing two distinct catalytic sites on a single pellet:

1. **Metallic function — Platinum (Pt):** Pt is dispersed as 1–2 nm crystallites on the alumina support. It catalyzes:
   - **Dehydrogenation of naphthenes** to aromatics (the fastest reforming reaction).
   - **Dehydrocyclization** of paraffins (slow, requires multiple Pt sites in proximity).
   - Hydrogenation/dehydrogenation of olefin intermediates.
   The Pt operates via the **Horiuti–Polanyi mechanism** with chemisorbed α-β di-hydrogen.

2. **Acidic function — Chlorided γ-Al₂O₃:** Chloride ions (≈ 0.5–1.0 wt% Cl) generate **Brønsted and Lewis acid sites** on the alumina surface. These catalyze:
   - **Skeletal isomerization** of paraffins and alkyl-naphthenes.
   - Ring isomerization (e.g., methylcyclopentane → cyclohexane).
   Without the acid function, the catalyst would be a pure dehydrogenation catalyst (poor octane uplift).

3. **Synergy — "Tight Couple" Mechanism:** A typical paraffin diffuses to a Pt site, is dehydrogenated to an olefin, migrates to an adjacent acid site, isomerizes, returns to a Pt site, and is rehydrogenated as an iso-paraffin. The two functions are spatially separated on the **nanometer scale** — hence the name **bifunctional**.

4. **Role of Rhenium (Re):** Adding Re (typically 0.3 wt% Re to 0.3 wt% Pt) as a **bimetallic alloy** dramatically improves performance by:
   - **Suppressing coke formation** on Pt, especially at low pressure. Re acts as a "coke sink," spreading coke deposits over a larger surface and delaying deactivation.
   - **Allowing lower-pressure operation** (down to 8–15 atm for fixed bed) with stable performance.
   - **Increasing cycle length** from 6 months (Pt-only) to 12–24 months (Pt-Re).
   - The Pt-Re alloy is more resistant to **sulfur poisoning** and **sintering**.

(Mark breakdown: 2 marks each for Pt and Cl functions, 2 marks for synergy, 1 mark for Re benefits.)

**(b) Comparison: Conventional vs CCR Platforming (7 marks):**

| S.No. | Parameter | Conventional (Fixed-Bed) | CCR (Continuous Catalyst Regeneration) |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) | Operating Pressure | 15 – 35 atm | **3.5 – 5 atm** |
| (ii) | Catalyst | Pt / Al₂O₃ or Pt-Re / Al₂O₃ | **Pt-Sn / Al₂O₃** (regeneration-tolerant) |
| (iii) | Cycle Length | 6 – 12 months (shutdown needed) | **Continuous (catalyst regenerated in situ)** |
| (iv) | Aromatics Yield | Moderate (≈ 50–60 wt%) | **High (≈ 60–70 wt%)** |
| (v) | RON Capability | 95 – 98 | **98 – 102+** |

**Why is low-pressure operation preferred?**

By **Le Chatelier's principle**, the dehydrogenation equilibrium

$$\text{Naphthene} \rightleftharpoons \text{Aromatic} + 3\text{H}_2 \quad (\Delta n_{\text{gas}} = +3)$$

is **shifted toward products** (aromatics + H₂) when total pressure is reduced. Lower pressure therefore:

1. **Increases equilibrium aromatics concentration**, raising reformate octane.
2. **Increases H₂ yield**, valuable for hydrotreating/hydrocracking.
3. **Reduces gas compression costs** for the recycle loop.

The penalty — accelerated coking — is mitigated by **bimetallic catalysts** (Pt-Sn) and **continuous regeneration (CCR)**.

(Mark breakdown: 3 marks for the table, 2 marks for Le Chatelier, 2 marks for coke mitigation.)

> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> > [!WARNING]
> > A common mistake is to confuse **Pt-Re** (used in fixed-bed) with **Pt-Sn** (used in CCR). They are **not interchangeable**. Pt-Re is for **semi-regenerative** fixed-bed; Pt-Sn is for **continuous** regeneration because Sn stabilizes the metal against the high-temperature oxidation cycle in the regenerator. Also, do **not** state "low pressure reduces coking" — it **increases** coking; you must state the **mitigation** to justify why low pressure is still preferred.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Platforming (KTU Module 3):**

- ✅ **Definition:** Platforming = **Pt-catalyzed** reforming of naphtha to reformate + aromatics + H₂ (UOP, 1949).
- ✅ **Catalyst:** Bifunctional — **Pt** (metallic, dehydrogenation) + **chlorided Al₂O₃** (acidic, isomerization).
- ✅ **Modern Bimetallics:** **Pt-Re** (fixed-bed), **Pt-Sn** (CCR).
- ✅ **Four Reactions:**
   1. Naphthene → Aromatic + 3 H₂ (endothermic, fast, R-1).
   2. n-Paraffin ⇌ iso-Paraffin (mildly exothermic, R-2).
   3. n-Paraffin → Aromatic + 4 H₂ (endothermic, slow, R-3).
   4. Hydrocracking → light gases (exothermic, **undesirable**).
- ✅ **Operating Conditions:** T = 450–520 °C; P = 3.5–35 atm; H₂/HC = 3:1 to 6:1; WHSV = 1–2.5 h⁻¹.
- ✅ **Three Reactors in Series** with **interstage heaters** (mandatory — restores temperature lost to endothermic reactions).
- ✅ **Catalyst Volume Distribution:** R-1 > R-2 > R-3 (since reactions get progressively slower and less endothermic).
- ✅ **Lower Pressure → Higher Aromatics** (Le Chatelier: Δn_gas = +3 favors products at low P).
- ✅ **CCR (Continuous Catalyst Regeneration)** enables **low-pressure** operation with no shutdowns.
- ✅ **Hydrogen Yield:** 600–1,200 SCF/bbl of feed (≈ 100–200 Nm³/m³) — a critical by-product for hydrotreating.
- ✅ **RON of Reformate:** **95–102** (vs. feed 40–60); high aromatics + iso-paraffin content drive octane.
- ✅ **Master Formulae:** WHSV = ṁ_feed / m_cat; Conversion = (in − out) / in × 100%; Adiabatic ΔT = −ΔH_r·ξ / (F·C_p).
- ✅ **Typical Numerical Trap:** Forgetting to multiply by **3** (not 4) for H₂ stoichiometry of naphthene dehydrogenation.

> **End of Module 3 — Platforming Notes.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hurdy Forming
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT638 - PETROLEUM REFINERY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Hydroforming (Thermal Reforming Process)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Hydroforming** is a *non-catalytic thermal reforming process* used in petroleum refineries to upgrade low-octane naphthenic and paraffinic feedstocks into high-octane aromatic-rich gasoline blendstocks. The process is carried out at elevated temperatures (**510 °C – 565 °C**) and moderate pressures (**15 – 35 atm**) in the presence of a substantial quantity of **hydrogen-rich recycle gas**, without employing any solid catalyst.

The chemical transformation fundamentally involves:
- **Dehydrogenation** of naphthenes → aromatics
- **Cyclization** of paraffins → naphthenes → aromatics
- **Hydrocracking** of high-molecular-weight hydrocarbons to lower boiling fractions
- Suppression of coke formation via hydrogen partial pressure

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEPOT638, Module 3):**
> Hydroforming is classified under **Thermal Reforming Processes**, distinct from **Catalytic Reforming (Platforming, Houdriforming)**. Students must clearly differentiate between catalytic and non-catalytic reforming routes.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Pressure Cooker Bakery"**
>
> Imagine you have a bowl of plain dough (low-octane heavy naphtha). You want to bake aromatic pastries (high-octane reformate). If you simply bake in an open oven, the dough burns and turns into black, bitter charcoal (coke).
>
> Now, suppose you seal the dough inside a pressure cooker and pump in a steady stream of fresh air (hydrogen-rich gas). The trapped atmosphere prevents burning while the high heat transforms the dough into fluffy, golden pastries. The fresh airflow continuously sweeps away any charred bits before they ruin the batch.
>
> **Hydroforming works exactly like this:** a hydrogen-rich atmosphere inside a heated, pressurized reactor "bakes" heavy naphtha into aromatic-rich gasoline without depositing destructive coke.

## 1.3 Standard Process Metrics (Key Engineering Parameters)

| Parameter | Typical Range | Engineering Significance |
|-----------|---------------|--------------------------|
| Reaction Temperature | **510 – 565 °C** | Drives endothermic dehydrogenation |
| Reactor Pressure | **15 – 35 atm** | Maintains hydrogen partial pressure |
| H₂ / Hydrocarbon Mole Ratio | **2 : 1 to 5 : 1** | Suppresses coke; dilutes feed |
| Space Velocity (LHSV) | **0.5 – 2.0 h⁻¹** | Controls contact time |
| Per-pass Conversion | **50 – 70 %** | Single-pass efficiency |
| Net Octane Number Gain | **+15 to +25 (RON)** | Primary economic driver |
| H₂ Purity in Recycle Gas | **70 – 85 vol %** | Recirculated from separator |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Constants Used:**
> - Universal Gas Constant: $R = 8.314 \, \text{J} \cdot \text{mol}^{-1} \cdot \text{K}^{-1}$
> - Standard Temperature & Pressure: $T_{std} = 273.15 \, \text{K}$, $P_{std} = 1 \, \text{atm} = 101.325 \, \text{kPa}$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Chemistry of Hydroforming Reactions

The overall chemistry is governed by four dominant reaction families:

### A. Dehydrogenation of Naphthenes → Aromatics (Strongly Endothermic)

$$\text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n} \xrightarrow{\Delta} \text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n-6} + 3\text{H}_2$$

- **Example:** Methylcyclohexane $\rightarrow$ Toluene $+ 3\text{H}_2$
- Heat of reaction: $\Delta H \approx +205 \, \text{kJ/mol}$
- This reaction is the **principal octane-boosting pathway**.

### B. Dehydrocyclization of Paraffins → Aromatics

$$\text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n+2} \xrightarrow{\Delta} \text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n-6} + 4\text{H}_2$$

- **Example:** n-Heptane $\rightarrow$ Toluene $+ 4\text{H}_2$
- Strongly endothermic ($\Delta H \approx +260 \, \text{kJ/mol}$).

### C. Hydrocracking of Heavy Hydrocarbons

$$\text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n+2} + \text{H}_2 \rightarrow \text{C}_m\text{H}_{2m+2} + \text{C}_{n-m}\text{H}_{2(n-m)}$$

- Moderately exothermic; produces valuable LPG and lighter gases.

### D. Isomerization of n-Paraffins → iso-Paraffins

Mildly exothermic; boosts octane without altering carbon number.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Process Flow Logic

1. **Feedstock Pre-heat:** Straight-run heavy naphtha (typically **100 °C – 200 °C** boiling range) is preheated against reactor effluent in a feed-effluent heat exchanger.
2. **Furnace Heating:** Mixed with hot recycle gas, the stream enters a fired tubular heater raising the temperature to **525 °C – 565 °C**.
3. **Reactor Section (Soaking Drum):** The hot mixture flows through an adiabatic soaking drum where endothermic reactions absorb heat. To maintain temperature, **inter-stage reheat furnaces** are placed between successive reactor drums (typically **2 – 3 reactors in series**).
4. **Effluent Cooling:** Reactor outlet exchanges heat with incoming feed, then enters a **product cooler**.
5. **High-Pressure Separator:** Condensed liquid (reformate) separates from hydrogen-rich vapor. The vapor is compressed and recycled; a small purge removes light ends and inert gases.
6. **Stabilization Column:** Reformate is debutanized to remove dissolved light gases, yielding the final **hydroformate** product with octane number typically **RON 78 – 88**.

## 2.3 Why Hydrogen Atmosphere is Critical — The Core "Why"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Rationale for H₂ Injection:**
>
> 1. **Thermodynamic Shift:** High H₂ partial pressure shifts dehydrogenation equilibria toward aromatic formation (Le Chatelier's principle).
> 2. **Coke Suppression:** Hydrogen reacts with coke precursors, gasifying them into CH₄ and lighter products:
> $$\text{C (coke)} + 2\text{H}_2 \rightarrow \text{CH}_4$$
> 3. **Dilution Effect:** Inert H₂ reduces partial pressure of hydrocarbons, lowering residence time and cracking severity.
> 4. **Heat Sink:** H₂ has high heat capacity, helping to moderate the strong endothermic temperature drop.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Expression | Variables & Units | Application |
|---------|------------|-------------------|-------------|
| Le Chatelier — Dehydrogenation Equilibrium | $K_p = \dfrac{P_{Aro} \cdot P_{H_2}^3}{P_{Naph}}$ | $P$ = partial pressure (atm) | Predicts aromatics yield |
| Hydrogen-to-Oil Ratio | $\text{HOR} = \dfrac{V_{H_2}}{V_{HC}}$ | $V$ = volumetric flow (Nm³/h) | Process control |
| Space Velocity | $\text{LHSV} = \dfrac{F_{feed}}{V_{cat/void}}$ | $h^{-1}$ | Reactor sizing |
| Heat Duty of Reactor | $Q = \dot{n}_{HC} \cdot \sum y_i \cdot \Delta H_{rxn,i}$ | $\dot{n}$ = molar flow (kmol/h), $\Delta H$ in kJ/kmol | Furnace sizing |
| Recycle Ratio | $R = \dfrac{F_{recycle}}{F_{fresh\,feed}}$ | Dimensionless | Gas circulation |
| Conversion | $X = \dfrac{n_{in} - n_{out}}{n_{in}}$ | Dimensionless (0–1) | Reactor performance |
| Octane Gain | $\Delta \text{RON} = \text{RON}_{product} - \text{RON}_{feed}$ | Unitless number | Economics |
| Ideal Gas Law (Separator Design) | $PV = nRT$ | $P$ in kPa, $V$ in m³, $n$ in kmol | Separator volume |
| Arrhenius Rate Constant | $k = A \cdot \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{RT}\right)$ | $A$ = pre-exponential, $E_a$ in J/mol | Reaction kinetics |
| Coke Gasification Equilibrium | $\text{C} + 2\text{H}_2 \rightleftharpoons \text{CH}_4$ | — | Coke suppression |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** Hydroforming was historically important during **World War II** (1940s) when aviation gasoline demanded very high octane numbers (≥ 100 RON). Today, it has largely been superseded by **Catalytic Reforming** (Platforming) due to better selectivity, longer run lengths, and higher aromatics yields. However, hydroforming remains an excellent pedagogical case study for understanding *thermal reforming* fundamentals.

## 2.5 Comparison: Hydroforming vs. Catalytic Reforming

| Feature | Hydroforming | Catalytic Reforming |
|---------|--------------|---------------------|
| Catalyst | None | Pt / Pt-Re on Al₂O₃ + Cl |
| Temperature | 510 – 565 °C | 480 – 525 °C |
| Pressure | 15 – 35 atm | 3 – 35 atm |
| Yield of Aromatics | Moderate (~ 30 – 40 %) | High (~ 45 – 55 %) |
| Run Length | Continuous (no catalyst) | 6 – 24 months |
| Hydrogen Purity | Lower | Higher (85 – 92 %) |
| Modern Relevance | Largely obsolete | Dominant process |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Equilibrium Conversion of Methylcyclohexane → Toluene

We derive the equilibrium per-pass conversion to evaluate reactor performance.

### Step 1: Write the Stoichiometry

$$\text{Methylcyclohexane (M)} \rightleftharpoons \text{Toluene (T)} + 3\text{H}_2$$

### Step 2: Define Extent of Reaction

Let $x$ = moles of M reacted at equilibrium. Initial moles (basis: 1 mol M):
- $n_M = 1 - x$
- $n_T = x$
- $n_{H_2} = 3x$

Total moles at equilibrium: $n_{total} = (1 - x) + x + 3x = 1 + 3x$

### Step 3: Express Partial Pressures

If total pressure is $P$, partial pressures are:

$$P_M = \dfrac{(1-x)}{(1+3x)} \cdot P$$

$$P_T = \dfrac{x}{(1+3x)} \cdot P$$

$$P_{H_2} = \dfrac{3x}{(1+3x)} \cdot P$$

### Step 4: Write the Equilibrium Constant Expression

$$K_p = \dfrac{P_T \cdot P_{H_2}^3}{P_M}$$

Substituting the partial pressures:

$$K_p = \dfrac{\left[\dfrac{x}{(1+3x)} P\right] \cdot \left[\dfrac{3x}{(1+3x)} P\right]^3}{\left[\dfrac{(1-x)}{(1+3x)} P\right]}$$

### Step 5: Simplify

$$K_p = \dfrac{x \cdot (3x)^3 \cdot P^3}{(1+3x)^3 \cdot (1+3x)} \cdot \dfrac{(1+3x)}{(1-x) \cdot P}$$

$$K_p = \dfrac{27 \, x^4 \, P^3}{(1+3x)^3 \, (1-x)}$$

### Step 6: Solve Numerically at T = 825 K, P = 20 atm

At $T = 825 \, \text{K}$, thermodynamic tables give $\log_{10}(K_p) \approx 3.42$, so:

$$K_p \approx 2630 \, \text{atm}^3$$

Substituting $P = 20$ atm:

$$2630 = \dfrac{27 \, x^4 \, (20)^3}{(1+3x)^3 (1-x)} = \dfrac{216000 \, x^4}{(1+3x)^3 (1-x)}$$

$$\dfrac{27 \, x^4}{(1+3x)^3 (1-x)} = 0.01218$$

Iterative solution yields $x \approx 0.86$.

### Step 7: Equilibrium Conversion

$$\boxed{X_{eq} = \dfrac{x}{1} \times 100\% = 86\%}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic Explanation:**
> Each step applies **Raoult's Law** for ideal gas partial pressures, **Le Chatelier's principle** for Kp dependence, and **iterative numerical methods** (Newton-Raphson) for nonlinear equilibrium equations. The high conversion confirms that **thermodynamics strongly favors aromatics formation at low H₂ partial pressure**.

## 3.2 Derivation: Hydrogen Recycle Compressor Power

### Step 1: Define Required Recycle Flow

Recycle molar flow $n_{recycle}$ is fixed by the hydrogen-to-oil ratio (HOR):

$$n_{recycle} = \text{HOR} \times n_{feed}$$

For HOR = 4, $n_{feed} = 1000$ kmol/h:

$$n_{recycle} = 4 \times 1000 = 4000 \, \text{kmol/h}$$

### Step 2: Compressor Inlet Conditions

Separator temperature $T_1 = 313 \, \text{K}$, pressure $P_1 = 17$ atm. Outlet pressure $P_2 = 22$ atm (to overcome reactor pressure drop).

### Step 3: Apply Isentropic Compression Work

$$W_s = \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} R T_1 \left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1\right]$$

For diatomic H₂-rich gas, $\gamma \approx 1.40$:

$$\dfrac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma} = \dfrac{0.40}{1.40} = 0.2857$$

Pressure ratio:

$$\dfrac{P_2}{P_1} = \dfrac{22}{17} = 1.294$$

Isentropic exponent term:

$$(1.294)^{0.2857} \approx 1.077$$

### Step 4: Compute Specific Work per kmol

$$W_s = \dfrac{1.40}{0.40} \times 8.314 \times 313 \times (1.077 - 1)$$

$$W_s = 3.5 \times 8.314 \times 313 \times 0.077$$

$$W_s \approx 700 \, \text{kJ/kmol}$$

### Step 5: Total Compressor Power (Assuming 75% Isentropic Efficiency)

$$W_{actual} = \dfrac{W_s}{\eta_s} = \dfrac{700}{0.75} \approx 933 \, \text{kJ/kmol}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{W}_{total} = 933 \times 4000 = 3.73 \times 10^6 \, \text{kJ/h} \approx 1037 \, \text{kW}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic Explanation:**
> The isentropic work equation is derived from the **first law of thermodynamics** applied to an ideal-gas compressor with adiabatic and reversible (isentropic) assumptions. Dividing by isentropic efficiency $\eta_s$ accounts for real-world irreversibilities (friction, heat losses). This power requirement is a **major operating cost** of hydroforming and motivates the move toward lower-pressure catalytic reforming.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Operating Variables

| Variable | Increase Effect on Aromatics Yield | Increase Effect on Coke | KTU Exam Tip |
|----------|-----------------------------------|------------------------|---------------|
| Temperature ↑ | ↑ (endothermic rxns favored) | ↑ (faster cracking) | Always specify °C, not K |
| Pressure ↑ | ↓ (Le Chatelier — H₂ suppression) | ↓ (hydrogenation of precursors) | Total vs. partial pressure |
| H₂ / HC Ratio ↑ | ↓ (shifts equilibrium left) | ↓↓ (strong suppression) | Common 7-mark question |
| Space Velocity ↑ | ↓ (shorter residence) | ↓ | Lower conversion |
| Feed Naphthene Content ↑ | ↑↑ | — | Best feed for hydroforming |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hydroforming Process Block Diagram (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[feedNapht["FRESH HEAVY NAPHTHA FEED"]"]
    B[heatExch["FEED EFFLUENT HEAT EXCHANGER"]"]
    C[furnace1["REHEAT FURNACE F1 (525 TO 565 C)"]"]
    D[reactor1["REACTOR DRUM 1 SOAKING DRUM"]"]
    E[furnace2["INTERSTAGE REHEAT FURNACE F2"]"]
    F[reactor2["REACTOR DRUM 2 SOAKING DRUM"]"]
    G[prodCool["PRODUCT COOLER"]"]
    H[hpSep["HIGH PRESSURE SEPARATOR"]"]
    I[recycleComp["RECYCLE HYDROGEN COMPRESSOR"]"]
    J[lowSep["LOW PRESSURE SEPARATOR"]"]
    K[stabCol["STABILIZATION COLUMN DEBUTANIZER"]"]
    L[reformate["FINAL HYDROFORMATE PRODUCT RON 78 TO 88"]"]
    M[purge["PURGE GAS LIGHT ENDS AND INERTS"]"]
    N[lpgOut["LPG AND LIGHT NAPHTHA SIDESTREAM"]"]

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
    E --> F
    F --> G
    G --> H
    H --> B
    H --> I
    I --> C
    H --> J
    J --> K
    K --> L
    J --> M
    K --> N

    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style L fill:#fff4e1
    style M fill:#ffe1e1
    style N fill:#e1ffe1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Guide:**
> - **Blue nodes** = fresh feed entry.
> - **Orange node** = primary high-value product (reformate).
> - **Red node** = off-gas / purge stream.
> - **Green node** = valuable by-product (LPG).
> - Recycle loop (B → C → D → E → F → G → H → B) shows how reactor effluent preheats incoming feed — a classic **pinch-analysis energy integration**.

## 4.2 Detailed Reactor Section Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1[hotFeed["HOT FEED PLUS RECYCLE H2"]"]
    P2[drum1["SOAKING DRUM 1 ADIABATIC"]"]
    P3[coil1["REHEAT COIL FURNACE F1"]"]
    P4[drum2["SOAKING DRUM 2 ADIABATIC"]"]
    P5[coil2["REHEAT COIL FURNACE F2"]"]
    P6[drum3["SOAKING DRUM 3 ADIABATIC"]"]
    P7[outlet["REACTOR EFFLUENT TO COOLER"]"]
    P8[thermo1["THERMOCOUPLE T1"]"]
    P9[thermo2["THERMOCOUPLE T2"]"]
    P10[thermo3["THERMOCOUPLE T3"]"]
    P11[sample1["ONLINE OCTANE ANALYZER"]"]

    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> P4
    P4 --> P5
    P5 --> P6
    P6 --> P7

    P2 -.monitor.-> P8
    P4 -.monitor.-> P9
    P6 -.monitor.-> P10
    P7 -.monitor.-> P11

    style P2 fill:#ffeaa7
    style P4 fill:#ffeaa7
    style P6 fill:#ffeaa7
    style P3 fill:#fab1a0
    style P5 fill:#fab1a0
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Multiple Soaking Drums?**
> Hydroforming reactions are **strongly endothermic** ($\Delta H \approx +200$ to +260 kJ/mol). A single reactor would experience a temperature drop exceeding **80 – 100 °C**, which would quench aromatics formation. By splitting into **multiple adiabatic reactors with interstage reheating**, the temperature profile is kept closer to optimum, dramatically improving aromatics yield.

## 4.3 Material Balance — Block-Level Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q1["FRESH FEED F = 1000 kg/h"]
    Q2["RECYCLE HYDROGEN R kg/h"]
    Q3["MIXED REACTOR FEED"]
    Q4["REACTOR PRODUCT VAPOR PLUS LIQUID"]
    Q5["REFORMATE PRODUCT P kg/h"]
    Q6["SEPARATOR OFF GAS G kg/h"]
    Q7["PURGE STREAM W kg/h"]

    Q1 --> Q3
    Q2 --> Q3
    Q3 --> Q4
    Q4 --> Q5
    Q4 --> Q6
    Q6 --> Q2
    Q6 --> Q7

    style Q5 fill:#fff4e1
    style Q7 fill:#ffe1e1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mass Balance Equation (overall plant):**
> $$F = P + W$$
> **Component balance on hydrogen:**
> $$R_{in} + H_{2,produced} = R_{out} + H_{2,dissolved} + H_{2,purge}$$
> **Overall balance on carbon:** All carbon entering in feed F must exit in reformate P or purge W.
