topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT638 - PETROLEUM REFINERY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Feedstock Selection and Effect of Steam, Coking Methods of Petroleum

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Feedstock Selection in Petroleum Refining

### Formal Academic Definition
**Feedstock Selection** is the systematic engineering process of evaluating crude oil fractions or derived streams based on physical, chemical, and economic properties to determine their suitability as input materials (charge stocks) for specific downstream refinery processes such as catalytic cracking, hydrocracking, coking, reforming, and hydrotreating.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Feedstock selection is the *primary design variable* in any refinery configuration. The same crude oil can yield drastically different product slates depending on which process unit it is routed to.

### Key Properties Governing Feedstock Selection
The following properties act as **decision filters** for routing a refinery stream to a specific process:

| Property | Symbol / Unit | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **API Gravity** | °API | Indicates lightness/heaviness; higher API = lighter crude |
| **Sulfur Content** | wt% | Determines environmental compliance and corrosion |
| **Conradson Carbon Residue** | CCR (wt%) | Predicts coke yield in thermal processes |
| **Metal Content** | Ni + V (ppm) | Catalyst poison in catalytic processes |
| **Nitrogen Content** | ppm | Catalyst poison; affects hydroprocessing severity |
| **Asphaltene Content** | wt% | Causes fouling and unstable emulsions |
| **Naphthenes/Aromatics ratio** | — | Governs reformer feed quality |
| **Paraffin content** | wt% | Affects wax/coke balance |
| **Salt Content** | ptb (lb/1000 bbl) | Causes corrosion in distillation overheads |

---

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
> [!NOTE]
> **The Restaurant Kitchen Analogy:** Think of a refinery as a large restaurant kitchen. The *crude oil* is the raw grocery haul, and the *process units* (crackers, cokers, reformers) are the cooking stations (grill, fryer, oven). Just as a chef would not throw a delicate fish fillet into a deep fryer or use a cheap cut of beef for sashimi, a refiner must *match the feedstock to the right cooking process*. A heavy, metal-laden vacuum residue must go to a **coker** (slow thermal "roasting" to break it down), while a clean, paraffinic naphtha must go to a **reformer** (gentle catalytic "baking" to upgrade octane). Mismatching leads to **coked catalyst, fouled tubes, and ruined product** — exactly as a fish fillet dropped into a deep fryer disintegrates.

### Physical Constants & Standard Metrics
- **Standard API Gravity Reference:** 141.5 / (Specific Gravity at 15.6°C) – 131.5
- **Crude Classification:** Light (API > 31.1), Medium (22.3–31.1), Heavy (< 22.3)
- **Sour vs Sweet Crude:** Sour if S > 0.5 wt%; Sweet if S ≤ 0.5 wt%
- **1 barrel = 0.158987 m³ = 42 US gallons**
- **Steam to Oil Ratio (SOR):** 0.3 – 1.0 (typical in steam crackers)

---

## 1.2 Effect of Steam in Petroleum Processes

### Formal Academic Definition
**Steam injection** in petroleum processes serves as a *diluent, heat carrier, and reaction moderator*. In steam cracking, steam lowers the partial pressure of hydrocarbons, suppresses secondary bimolecular reactions (coking), and shifts the product slate toward lighter olefins.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Concept:** Steam does **not** participate chemically in most cracking/coking reactions (it is largely **inert** at < 850°C). Its role is **physical** — partial pressure reduction and coking suppression.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
> [!NOTE]
> **The Pressure Cooker Analogy:** Imagine boiling vegetables in a covered vs uncovered pot. The uncovered (low pressure) pot produces more aroma and lighter vapors but burns faster, while the covered (high pressure) pot keeps more moisture. Steam in a cracker acts like lifting the lid — it *reduces partial pressure*, encouraging hydrocarbon molecules to break into smaller, lighter pieces (olefins) rather than recombining into heavy tars and coke. Steam is the **"anti-coke police officer"** standing in the reactor, preventing reactive fragments from meeting and forming solid carbon.

### Quantitative Metrics
- **Steam-to-Oil ratio (SOR):** typically **0.3 – 0.8 kg steam/kg hydrocarbon** for naphtha crackers
- **Dilution steam partial pressure reduction:** from ~101 kPa (no steam) to ~25–50 kPa
- **Coking suppression factor:** up to **60–80%** reduction in coke deposition at optimal SOR

---

## 1.3 Coking Methods of Petroleum

### Formal Academic Definition
**Coking** is a *severe thermal cracking process* (operating at 480–520°C and near-atmospheric pressure) that converts heavy, low-value residual feedstocks (vacuum residue, cracked residue, tar sands bitumen) into lighter gaseous and liquid products, leaving behind **petroleum coke** as a solid byproduct.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Mandate:** Students must be able to *compare and contrast* the three principal coking technologies: **Delayed Coking, Fluid Coking, and Flexicoking**, including feedstock, yield structure, operating conditions, and economic trade-offs.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
> [!NOTE]
> **The Barbecue vs Industrial Furnace Analogy:** 
> - **Delayed Coking** = a giant batch-style barbecue (coke forms in drums over 16–24 hours, then is cut out hydraulically). 
> - **Fluid Coking** = a continuous fluidized-bed fryer (small coke particles circulate like grains of sand in hot air, constantly removing themselves). 
> - **Flexicoking** = the same fluidized fryer, but with a *gasifier* attached that *burns the coke in situ* to make H₂/CO syngas, eliminating the solid coke product entirely.

### Industrial Importance
- **Coke yield** from vacuum residue: typically **15–35 wt%** (delayed) and **20–30 wt%** (fluid)
- **Liquid yield (Coker Gas Oil):** typically **55–70 wt%**
- **Global petroleum coke production:** > 150 million tonnes/year (2024 estimate)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Feedstock Selection — The Decision Hierarchy

### Step-by-Step Decision Logic

1. **Step 1 — Identify the Process Target.** Define what product is desired (gasoline, diesel, olefins, lube base oil, coke). Each process has a *preferred carbon number range* and *impurity tolerance*.
2. **Step 2 — Apply Compositional Filters.** 
   - For **catalytic cracking (FCC)**: prefer feed with low CCR (< 8 wt%), low metals (Ni+V < 30 ppm), moderate sulfur.
   - For **hydrocracking**: tolerant of higher metals and CCR; requires H₂ availability.
   - For **coking**: any residue, regardless of metal/CCR — this is the *most tolerant* process.
   - For **steam reforming (naphtha → aromatics)**: requires low-sulfur, low-nitrogen, paraffinic-naphthenic feed.
3. **Step 3 — Apply Economic Filters.** Hydrogen cost, catalyst replacement, energy intensity, product price differentials.
4. **Step 4 — Apply Logistics/Regulatory Filters.** Pipeline compatibility, TAN (Total Acid Number), salt content, storage stability.

### Core 'Why' Behind Feedstock Selection
> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why":** Heavy feeds (vacuum residue, deasphalted oil) cannot be catalytically processed directly because their high metal content (Ni, V) and asphaltenes *poison and foul* the catalyst within hours. Therefore, they are routed to **coking** (tolerates any feed) or **hydrocracking** (requires expensive H₂ to remove S/N/metals). Light feeds (naphtha) are routed to **steam crackers** (for olefins) or **catalytic reformers** (for aromatics/octane).

### Feedstock-to-Process Routing Matrix

| Feedstock | Boiling Range (°C) | Best Suited Process | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Naphtha** (C5–C11) | 30–200 | Steam Cracking / Catalytic Reforming | High paraffin → high olefin / aromatic yield |
| **Kerosene/ATF** | 150–250 | Hydrotreating | Sulfur removal, smoke point improvement |
| **Diesel / LGO** | 250–380 | Hydrodesulfurization, FCC (as blend) | Cetane/quality upgrade |
| **Vacuum Gas Oil (VGO)** | 380–570 | FCC, Hydrocracking | Good conversion, moderate metals |
| **Vacuum Residue (VR)** | 570+ | Delayed Coking, Fluid Coking, Flexicoking, Resid FCC | Only cokers tolerate metals/CCR |

---

## 2.2 Effect of Steam — Theoretical Framework

### Physical Role of Steam
1. **Partial Pressure Dilution:** Steam reduces hydrocarbon partial pressure according to Dalton's Law.
2. **Coking Suppression:** Lower hydrocarbon partial pressure means fewer bimolecular collisions between reactive radicals, inhibiting secondary polymerization → less coke.
3. **Heat Carrier:** Steam carries latent heat into the reactor coils, smoothing the temperature profile.
4. **Reactor Wall Protection:** Steam preferentially reacts with trace metal surfaces, reducing catalytic coking on tube walls.
5. **Velocity Enhancement:** Higher mass velocity improves heat transfer coefficient.

### Core 'Why' Behind Steam Injection
> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why":** Hydrocarbon cracking is a *first-order unimolecular* reaction. Secondary reactions (radical recombination, condensation) are *second-order bimolecular*. By halving hydrocarbon partial pressure, the rate of cracking is halved, but the rate of bimolecular coke-formation is **quartered** (∝ P²). The net effect is **more olefins, less coke**.

### Steam Cracking Yield Impact

| SOR (kg/kg) | Ethylene Yield (wt%) | Coke Formation (relative) |
|---|---|---|
| 0.0 (no steam) | ~22 | 100% (baseline) |
| 0.3 | ~28 | ~50% |
| 0.5 | ~32 | ~25% |
| 0.8 | ~35 | ~10% |
| 1.0 | ~36 | ~5% |

---

## 2.3 Coking Process Theory

### Thermal Cracking Chemistry
At 480–520°C, large hydrocarbon molecules undergo **free-radical chain scission**:
- **Initiation:** C–C bond homolysis → alkyl radicals
- **Propagation:** β-scission → smaller radicals + olefins
- **Termination:** Radical recombination / disproportionation
- **Condensation:** Aromatic cores fuse → asphaltenes → mesophase → **petroleum coke**

### The 'Why' of Coke Formation
> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why":** Aromatic molecules, especially polyaromatics, become increasingly **thermodynamically stable** as they grow larger. Beyond ~7 fused rings, they are insoluble (asphaltenes) and physically precipitate as a separate liquid phase (mesophase). On prolonged heating, this mesophase **dehydrogenates and graphitizes** into petroleum coke. The coker's job is to *drive the reaction to completion* so this solid is cleanly separated, rather than fouling downstream equipment.

---

## KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $°API = \dfrac{141.5}{SG_{15.6°C}} - 131.5$ | API gravity from specific gravity | dimensionless |
| 2 | $P_{HC} = y_{HC} \cdot P_{total}$ | Hydrocarbon partial pressure via Dalton's Law | kPa |
| 3 | $r_{coke} \propto P_{HC}^{\,2}$ | Bimolecular coke formation rate | mol/(L·s) |
| 4 | $r_{crack} \propto P_{HC}^{\,1}$ | First-order cracking rate | mol/(L·s) |
| 5 | $SOR = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{steam}}{\dot{m}_{hydrocarbon}}$ | Steam-to-oil ratio (mass basis) | kg/kg |
| 6 | $Y_{coke} \approx 0.55 \cdot CCR_{feed} + k$ | Coke yield correlation (Speight) | wt% |
| 7 | $Y_{CGO} = 100 - Y_{coke} - Y_{gas} - Y_{losses}$ | Mass balance for coker yields | wt% |
| 8 | $T_{coke} = 480 - 520$ | Coking drum operating temperature | °C |
| 9 | $T_{steam\,crack} = 750 - 900$ | Steam cracker coil outlet temperature | °C |
| 10 | $P_{steam\,crack} = 100 - 200$ | Steam cracker coil outlet pressure | kPa |
| 11 | $CCR = \dfrac{m_{residue}}{m_{feed}} \times 100$ | Conradson Carbon Residue definition | wt% |
| 12 | $\text{Decoking Time (Delayed)} = 16 - 24$ | Hydraulic decoking cycle | hours |
| 13 | $\text{Fluid Coker Bed T} = 510 - 570$ | Fluid coker reactor bed temperature | °C |
| 14 | $H/C_{\text{atomic}}$ in coke $\approx 0.05 - 0.4$ | Coke maturity indicator (lower = more graphitic) | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Avoidance:** In the coke yield correlation, the constant *k* depends on the coker type and feedstock type. For **delayed coking of vacuum residue**, k is typically 8–12 wt%. Students who use a single value without specifying the coker type lose marks.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Effect of Steam on Hydrocarbon Partial Pressure

### Problem Statement
A steam cracker operates at a total pressure of **175 kPa** with a steam-to-oil ratio (SOR) of **0.6 (mass basis)**. The hydrocarbon feedstock is n-hexane (MW = 86 g/mol) and steam is at MW = 18 g/mol. Calculate the partial pressure of the hydrocarbon in the reactor coil.

### Step 1 — Convert SOR to Mole Ratio
For 1 kg of hydrocarbon, steam mass = 0.6 kg.
- Moles of hydrocarbon: $n_{HC} = \dfrac{1000\,g}{86\,g/mol} = 11.628\,mol$
- Moles of steam: $n_{steam} = \dfrac{600\,g}{18\,g/mol} = 33.333\,mol$

### Step 2 — Compute Mole Fractions
Total moles = $11.628 + 33.333 = 44.961$ mol

$$y_{HC} = \frac{11.628}{44.961} = 0.2586$$

$$y_{steam} = \frac{33.333}{44.961} = 0.7414$$

### Step 3 — Apply Dalton's Law
$$P_{HC} = y_{HC} \cdot P_{total} = 0.2586 \times 175\,kPa$$

$$\boxed{P_{HC} \approx 45.3\,kPa}$$

### Step 4 — Compare to No-Steam Case
Without steam: $P_{HC} = 175$ kPa. **Partial pressure reduction = 74%**. This is why olefin yield and run-length improve dramatically.

### Step 5 — Compare to Lower SOR (0.3)
If SOR = 0.3:
- $n_{HC} = 11.628$ mol; $n_{steam} = 16.667$ mol
- Total = 28.295 mol
- $y_{HC} = 0.411$; $P_{HC} = 0.411 \times 175 = 71.9$ kPa

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight for KTU Exam:** Doubling SOR from 0.3 to 0.6 reduces $P_{HC}$ by ~37%. The relationship is **non-linear** because of the mole-fraction denominator.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Coke Yield Prediction Using Speight's Correlation

### Problem Statement
A vacuum residue with **CCR = 22 wt%** is fed to a **delayed coker**. Using Speight's correlation, estimate the petroleum coke yield. Assume the correlation constant k = 10 wt% for delayed coking of this residue type.

### Step 1 — Recall Correlation
$$Y_{coke} = 0.55 \cdot CCR_{feed} + k$$

### Step 2 — Substitute Values
$$Y_{coke} = 0.55 \times 22 + 10$$

$$\boxed{Y_{coke} = 22.1\,wt\%}$$

### Step 3 — Cross-Check via Mass Balance
If typical gas yield = 10 wt% and losses = 2 wt%:
$$Y_{CGO} = 100 - 22.1 - 10 - 2 = 65.9\,wt\%$$
This is consistent with industry data (60–70% CGO from VR delayed coking).

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Decoking Cycle Time and Throughput

### Problem Statement
A delayed coker has **2 drums**, each with capacity 1500 m³. The coke porosity is **0.40** and bulk density of coke in drum is **900 kg/m³**. The feed is the same vacuum residue (CCR = 22 wt%). Coke yield = 22.1 wt%. Feed rate = 80,000 kg/hr. Determine the cycle time and daily throughput of coke.

### Step 1 — Coke Mass per Drum
$$V_{coke} = V_{drum} \times (1 - \phi) = 1500 \times (1 - 0.40) = 900\,m^3$$
$$m_{coke} = 900 \times 900 = 810{,}000\,kg = 810\,tonnes$$

### Step 2 — Feed Required per Drum
$$m_{feed} = \frac{m_{coke}}{Y_{coke}/100} = \frac{810{,}000}{0.221} = 3{,}665{,}158\,kg$$

### Step 3 — Cycle Time
$$t_{cycle} = \frac{3{,}665{,}158}{80{,}000} \approx 45.8\,hours$$

This exceeds the typical 16–24 hour cycle — *this design is oversized.* A real refinery uses multiple parallel drums (4–6) to maintain continuous operation.

### Step 4 — Daily Coke Throughput (with 6 drums, 24-hr staggered cycle)
- Each drum produces 810 tonnes in 24 hours → 33.75 tonnes/hr
- Total → $6 \times 33.75 = 202.5$ tonnes/hr ≈ **4,860 tonnes/day**

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Coking Methods Comparison

| Parameter | Delayed Coking | Fluid Coking | Flexicoking |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Reactor type** | Semi-batch drum | Continuous fluidized bed | Continuous fluidized bed + gasifier |
| **Operating T (°C)** | 480–520 | 510–570 | 510–570 (reactor); 800–1000 (gasifier) |
| **Operating P** | Near atmospheric | Near atmospheric | Slightly above atmospheric |
| **Coke residence time** | 16–24 hr | 30 min – 1 hr | < 1 hr (recycled) |
| **Feedstock flexibility** | Very high | High | High |
| **Coke yield (wt%)** | 25–35 | 15–25 | ~3–5 (most burned in gasifier) |
| **Liquid yield (CGO)** | 55–65 | 65–75 | 65–75 |
| **Gas yield (wt%)** | 5–10 | 8–12 | 12–18 (more due to gasification) |
| **By-product** | Pet coke (sold) | Pet coke (sold or burned) | Low-BTU fuel gas (H₂+CO) |
| **Coke handling** | Hydraulic decoking (high pressure water) | Continuous withdrawal | None — consumed in gasifier |
| **Capital cost** | Low | High | Very High |
| **Operating cost** | Low (manual decoking) | Moderate (fluidization air) | High (air, HP steam) |
| **CO₂ footprint** | Moderate (sold coke offset) | Moderate | Higher (gasifier emits CO₂) |
| **Suitable scale** | Small to very large | Medium to large | Large only (>50,000 bbl/day) |
| **Coke form** | Shot/lump coke (anode grade or fuel grade) | Granular powder | None (consumed) |

---

## 3.5 Detailed Mass Balance Worked Example (Delayed Coker)

### Problem Statement
A delayed coker processes **100,000 kg/hr** of vacuum residue with CCR = 18 wt%. The following product yields are observed:

- Coke: 25.0 wt%
- Coker Naphtha (C5–200°C): 15.0 wt%
- Coker Gas Oil (200–370°C): 50.0 wt%
- Heavy Coker Gas Oil (370–565°C): 5.0 wt%
- Dry Gas (C1–C4): 5.0 wt%

### Step 1 — Mass Balance Check
$$15.0 + 50.0 + 5.0 + 5.0 + 25.0 = 100.0\,wt\%$$
Mass balance is **closed** ✓

### Step 2 — Compute Hourly Flows

| Product | Mass Flow (kg/hr) | Daily (tonnes/day) |
|---|---|---|
| Coker Naphtha | 15,000 | 360 |
| Coker Gas Oil | 50,000 | 1,200 |
| Heavy CGO | 5,000 | 120 |
| Dry Gas | 5,000 | 120 |
| Petroleum Coke | 25,000 | 600 |
| **TOTAL** | **100,000** | **2,400** |

### Step 3 — Compare Coke Yield to Speight Correlation
Predicted: $Y_{coke} = 0.55 \times 18 + 10 = 19.9\,wt\%$
Actual: 25.0 wt% — **25% higher than predicted**, indicating a heavier-than-typical residue (likely high asphaltene content).

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
*List the four most important properties that govern feedstock selection for a fluid catalytic cracking (FCC) unit, and briefly justify each.*

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):
1. **Conradson Carbon Residue (CCR):** Should be < 8 wt%; higher values cause excessive coke on catalyst, deactivating it rapidly and causing regenerator hot spots. *(1 mark)*
2. **Metal Content (Ni + V):** Should be < 30 ppm; nickel promotes dehydrogenation to coke, vanadium destroys zeolite structure. *(1 mark)*
3. **Sulfur Content:** Determines whether the unit needs feed hydrodesulfurization (HDS) upstream; high S causes SOx emissions from regenerator. *(0.5 mark)*
4. **Nitrogen Content:** Basic nitrogen compounds (e.g., quinoline) neutralize acid sites of FCC catalyst, reducing conversion. *(0.5 mark)*

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
*Explain why steam is injected in a steam cracking furnace. State the typical steam-to-oil ratio (SOR) range.*

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):
- **Function of steam:** Steam acts as an **inert diluent** that reduces the partial pressure of hydrocarbons in the cracking coil. This is the *primary* effect. *(1 mark)*
- **Consequence:** Lower hydrocarbon partial pressure suppresses **secondary bimolecular reactions** (radical recombination and condensation), which are responsible for coke formation. Steam also serves as a **heat carrier** and protects the tube walls from catalytic coking. *(1.5 marks)*
- **Typical SOR range:** **0.3 – 0.8 kg steam/kg hydrocarbon** (naphtha crackers). *(0.5 mark)*

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **INTERNAL CHOICE: Students must answer EITHER Question A OR Question B.**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**a)** Discuss the criteria for **feedstock selection** for (i) catalytic reforming, (ii) hydrocracking, and (iii) delayed coking. Bring out the contrasting nature of these three feed specifications. *(7 marks)*

**b)** With a neat process flow diagram, describe the working of the **delayed coking process**. Discuss the role of steam in enhancing the operation. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A (a):

| Process | Preferred Feed | Key Selection Criteria |
|---|---|---|
| **Catalytic Reforming** | Heavy Naphtha (C7–C11, 90–190°C) | Naphthenes + paraffins preferred; low sulfur (< 5 ppm) to avoid catalyst poisoning (Pt-Re or Pt-Sn); high N+A (≥ 50%) for good aromatics yield; low olefins |
| **Hydrocracking** | VGO, coker gas oil, deasphalted oil | Tolerates moderate CCR (< 20 wt%), moderate metals (< 100 ppm total); requires H₂ availability; high N acceptable (gets converted to NH₃) |
| **Delayed Coking** | Vacuum residue, atmospheric residue, tar sands bitumen | **Most tolerant** — accepts ANY heavy feed regardless of CCR, metals, asphaltenes, TAN; economics improve with heavier feeds |

**Contrast:** Reforming demands *the cleanest, lightest* feed; hydrocracking tolerates *moderate impurities*; delayed coking tolerates *any impurity* but produces *solid waste* (coke). This is the essence of the **refinery feedstock hierarchy**. *(Rest 1 mark for flow & conclusion)*

#### Model Solution to Q-A (b):

**Delayed Coking Process — Description (5 marks):**
- Heavy residue feed is preheated in a **furnace** to ~350°C (below coking temperature) and then routed to one of two **coking drums** operating at 480–520°C and near-atmospheric pressure.
- Residence time in the drum is **16–24 hours**. Cracking occurs in the liquid phase; lighter products leave as vapors.
- The vapors go to the **coker fractionator**, separating into wet gas, coker naphtha, light coker gas oil, and heavy coker gas oil.
- Once a drum is full of coke, feed is switched to the second drum, and the first is **hydraulically decoked** using high-pressure water jets (350 bar).
- Petroleum coke is cut, removed, and stored as **anode-grade** (for aluminum smelting) or **fuel-grade** coke.

**Role of Steam (2 marks):**
- Steam is **not directly injected into the drum** in conventional delayed coking — the steam in the question may be referring to the steam used in the **fractionator overhead** to suppress corrosion and in the **decoking water** to cool and cut coke.
- In contrast, **fluid coking and flexicoking** use **fluidization steam** to keep coke particles suspended, and **gasification steam/air** to partially consume coke in flexicoking.
- Steam's general role: **partial pressure reduction, coke suppression, heat carrier, wall protection** (as in steam crackers).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Alternative]

**a)** Explain the principle of **fluid coking** and **flexicoking** with suitable diagrams. Compare their yield structures. *(7 marks)*

**b)** A vacuum residue (CCR = 20 wt%, feed rate = 50,000 kg/hr) is processed in a **delayed coker**. Using Speight's correlation, estimate the coke yield. If the coker produces 12 wt% gas and 2 wt% losses, calculate the coker gas oil (CGO) yield. If the CGO has a density of 0.92 g/cm³, find the **volumetric flow rate of CGO in m³/day**. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B (a):

**Fluid Coking (3.5 marks):**
- Continuous thermal cracking process using a **fluidized bed** of coke particles.
- Preheated vacuum residue is sprayed onto a hot (~520°C) fluidized coke bed in the **reactor**.
- Coke formed coats the seed particles; a portion is circulated to the **heater** (a separate fluidized bed at 600°C) where partial combustion with air supplies heat.
- Net coke is withdrawn as a granular product.
- **Steam** is used as the fluidizing medium in both reactor and heater (small quantities, ~2–5% of feed).

**Flexicoking (2.5 marks):**
- An extension of fluid coking; the **heater is replaced by a gasifier**.
- Coke is reacted with air and steam at 800–1000°C:
$$C + O_2 \rightarrow CO_2$$
$$C + H_2O \rightarrow CO + H_2$$
$$C + CO_2 \rightarrow 2CO$$
- The resulting **low-BTU fuel gas** (H₂ + CO) is recycled to the refinery furnace.
- **No solid coke product** — all coke is gasified to syngas.

**Yield Comparison (1 mark):**

| Yield (wt%) | Fluid Coking | Flexicoking |
|---|---|---|
| Liquid (CGO) | 65–75 | 65–75 |
| Coke (solid) | 15–25 | 0 (consumed) |
| Gas | 8–12 | 15–20 (includes syngas) |

#### Model Solution to Q-B (b):

**Step 1 — Coke Yield from Speight Correlation**
For delayed coking of VR, take k = 10 wt%:
$$Y_{coke} = 0.55 \times 20 + 10 = 21.0\,wt\%$$

**Step 2 — Mass Balance for CGO**
$$Y_{CGO} = 100 - Y_{coke} - Y_{gas} - Y_{losses}$$
$$Y_{CGO} = 100 - 21.0 - 12 - 2 = 65.0\,wt\%$$

**Step 3 — Hourly CGO Mass Flow**
$$\dot{m}_{CGO} = 0.65 \times 50{,}000 = 32{,}500\,kg/hr$$

**Step 4 — Convert to Volumetric Flow Rate**
Using density ρ = 0.92 g/cm³ = 920 kg/m³:
$$\dot{V}_{CGO} = \frac{32{,}500}{920} = 35.33\,m^3/hr$$
$$\dot{V}_{CGO} = 35.33 \times 24 = \boxed{847.8\,m^3/day}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Coke yield correlation constant (k)** is process-specific. Using k = 5 or 15 will give a different answer; KTU evaluators check whether you *mentioned the assumption* and used a *justified* value.
> 2. **Density unit conversion** is a classic error zone: 0.92 g/cm³ = 920 kg/m³ — students often miss this and get volumetric flow wrong by a factor of 1000.
> 3. **In flexicoking diagrams**, students often confuse the *heater* (in fluid coking) with the *gasifier* (in flexicoking). They are functionally different units.
> 4. **Steam is not a reactant in coking** — it is a fluidization/heat carrier. Examiners deduct marks for treating it as a chemical participant.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION CHECKLIST — KTU PEPOT638 Module 3**

### Feedstock Selection
- ✅ **API gravity** is the master indicator of crude lightness/heaviness.
- ✅ **CCR** predicts coke-forming tendency — the single most important property for coking feed selection.
- ✅ **Ni + V metals** poison FCC and hydrocracking catalysts; the **only** tolerant process is **coking**.
- ✅ **Sulfur**: sweet (≤0.5%) vs sour (>0.5%) determines hydrotreating severity and product specs (Euro VI: S < 10 ppm diesel).
- ✅ **Naphtha routes**: clean paraffinic → steam cracker (olefins) or reformer (aromatics).
- ✅ **Residue routes**: heavy, dirty → coker (delayed / fluid / flexi).

### Effect of Steam
- ✅ Steam is **inert** at cracker conditions; it does **not** react chemically.
- ✅ Primary role: **partial pressure dilution** → suppresses bimolecular coking.
- ✅ Secondary roles: heat carrier, tube wall protection, velocity enhancement.
- ✅ Typical SOR = **0.3 – 0.8 kg/kg** in ethylene crackers.
- ✅ **Doubling SOR** reduces hydrocarbon partial pressure by ~37% (non-linear).
- ✅ In cokers, steam is a **fluidization medium** (fluid/flexi cokers) and **overhead corrosion suppressor** (fractionator).

### Coking Methods
- ✅ **Delayed coking** = batch-style drum coking, **16–24 hr cycle**, hydraulic decoking; produces **anode-grade coke**; lowest CAPEX.
- ✅ **Fluid coking** = continuous fluidized bed, **520–570°C**; produces **granular coke**; higher CAPEX, higher liquid yield.
- ✅ **Flexicoking** = fluid coking + integrated **gasifier** (800–1000°C); produces **low-BTU syngas**; **no solid coke output**; highest CAPEX.
- ✅ Typical coker yields: coke 20–35%, CGO 55–70%, gas 5–15%, losses 1–3%.
- ✅ Coking temperature range: **480–520°C** (delayed), **510–570°C** (fluid/flexi reactor).
- ✅ **Coke is a value-added byproduct** in delayed/fluid coking but a **waste-to-energy stream** in flexicoking.
- ✅ **Speight correlation** for coke yield: $Y_{coke} = 0.55 \cdot CCR + k$, with k depending on coker type.

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT638 - PETROLEUM REFINERY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Coke Production

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Petroleum Coke** is a solid, carbonaceous residue produced by the thermal decomposition and polymerization of heavy petroleum fractions—predominantly vacuum residue, decant oil, tar sand bitumen, or coal-tar pitch—under conditions of high temperature (typically **480 °C – 520 °C**) and moderate pressure (**1 atm – 7 atm**), in a process collectively termed **Coking**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PEPOT638 – Module 3):**
> "Coke production refers to the thermal conversion of low-value, high-asphaltene, high-metal residual oils (vacuum residue, deasphalted pitch, slurry oil) into valuable lighter distillates (naphtha, diesel, gas oil) and a solid carbonaceous by-product known as petroleum coke, which is subsequently classified, calcined, and marketed."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of petroleum coke as the **"charcoal of the oil industry"**:

- When you burn a piece of wood in a fireplace with **limited oxygen**, the volatile gases, moisture, and tars escape as flame/smoke, and what remains is a black, porous **charcoal**—rich in fixed carbon.
- In petroleum refining, we don't "burn" the residue. Instead, we heat heavy residual oil in a **sealed reactor** to a temperature so high that the long hydrocarbon chains **crack (break)** into smaller, valuable gas and liquid products, while the aromatic, asphaltenic, and metallic components **polymerize and condense** into a solid, coal-like carbon mass—**petroleum coke**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy Summary:**
> | Fireplace (Charcoal) | Petroleum Refinery (Coke) |
> |---|---|
> | Wood (heavy biomass) | Vacuum residue (heavy oil) |
> | Heat + limited O₂ | Heat + no O₂ (inert/inert-like atmosphere) |
> | Smoke + flame (volatiles) | LPG, naphtha, diesel, gas oil |
> | Charcoal remains | Petroleum coke remains |
> | Used as fuel | Used as fuel / electrode / anode |

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Conventions

The following **bolded** engineering metrics and standards govern coke specifications worldwide:

- **API Gravity of Feed:** Typically **< 8 °API** (heavy residue) for sponge/shot coke; up to **15 °API** for needle coke feedstocks.
- **Conradson Carbon Residue (CCR):** **5 – 40 wt%** (higher CCR → higher coke yield).
- **Sulfur Content:** **0.5 – 7.0 wt%** in green coke; can exceed **8 wt%** in high-sulfur feeds.
- **Vanadium + Nickel (V+Ni) content:** **50 – 1500 ppm** in green coke.
- **Volatile Matter (VM):** **8 – 14 wt%** in green coke; reduced to **< 0.5 wt%** after calcination.
- **Fixed Carbon:** **80 – 95 wt%** in calcined coke.
- **Hardgrove Grindability Index (HGI):** **35 – 100** (lower = harder coke).
- **Real density:** **1.3 – 1.4 g/cm³** (green); **2.0 – 2.13 g/cm³** (calcined needle coke).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Exam Note:** Whenever a numerical problem on coke is given, students must explicitly **quote operating temperature (~500 °C), operating pressure (delayed = ~4 atm, fluid = ~1 atm), and feed CCR value** because these three parameters together govern **coke yield, structure (sponge/shot/needle), and product slate**.

## 1.4 Why Coke Production? (Refinery Economics)

Modern refineries are forced to convert the **bottom-of-the-barrel** vacuum residue (VR) into value-added products because:
1. **Fuel Oil demand is declining** due to IMO 2020 sulphur regulations (sulphur cap **0.5 %** in marine fuels).
2. **Aluminum, steel, and graphite industries** consume calcined petroleum coke (CPC) as the principal raw material for anodes.
3. Coking has the **highest liquid yield** (≈ 70 – 80 wt%) among residue conversion processes (vs. visbreaking at 15 – 25 wt%).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Chemistry of Coking (Reaction Mechanism)

Coking is fundamentally a **free-radical, high-temperature, non-catalytic** process occurring in **three overlapping stages**:

### Stage 1: Vaporization & Mild Cracking (300 °C – 450 °C)
- Light fractions and saturates vaporize.
- Long-chain paraffins and naphthenes undergo **thermal cracking** (C–C bond scission) producing lighter hydrocarbons + free radicals.
- **Reaction class:** Homolytic bond cleavage.

### Stage 2: Aromatization & Condensation (450 °C – 500 °C)
- Naphthenes dehydrogenate to aromatics.
- Aromatic rings **condense** into polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAH): naphthalene → phenanthrene → pyrene → coronene.
- **Reaction class:** Diels–Alder condensation + dehydrogenation.

### Stage 3: Polymerization & Coke Formation (480 °C – 520 °C)
- PAHs polymerize into **mesophase spheres** (liquid crystalline domains).
- Mesophase spheres coalesce and solidify into **green coke**.
- Asphaltenes, resins, and metallic porphyrins (V, Ni) are **trapped** in the coke matrix.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Net Chemical Change:**
> Vacuum Residue (H/C ≈ 1.5) → Coke (H/C ≈ 0.5) + Liquids (H/C ≈ 1.8) + Gases (H/C ≈ 2.5)
> The **hydrogen balance** is maintained by releasing H₂ and light hydrocarbon gases.

## 2.2 Coking Process Classification

The two most commercially dominant processes are:

| Process | Reactor Type | Temperature | Pressure | Coke Morphology |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Delayed Coking** | Semi-batch (drum) | 480 – 520 °C | 1 – 7 atm (gauge) | Sponge / Shot / Needle |
| **Fluid Coking** | Continuous fluidized bed | 510 – 565 °C | ~1 atm (gauge) | Granular (≈ 0.1 – 0.4 mm) |
| **Flexicoking** | Fluidized bed + gasifier | 510 – 565 °C | ~1 atm | Granular (low-sulphur) |

## 2.3 Delayed Coking Process (Detailed)

The term **"delayed"** signifies that the actual coking reaction is *delayed* until the heated feedstock enters the **coke drum**, allowing the furnace tubes to remain clean of coke.

**Step-by-step process flow:**
1. **Feed (Vacuum Residue)** is preheated in the convection section of the fractionator.
2. It enters the **furnace (Heater)** where it is rapidly heated to **495 – 505 °C** (residence time ≈ 2 minutes in tubes).
3. The hot feed is routed (via a switching valve) into the **bottom of a coke drum** (typically 2 drums operated in parallel: one filling, one decoking).
4. Inside the drum at low velocity, residence time = **16 – 24 hours**, the coking reactions proceed to completion.
5. Vapors exit the top of the drum, return to the **fractionator**, and are separated into **off-gas (C1–C4), naphtha (C5–200 °C), light gas oil (LGO, 200–350 °C), heavy gas oil (HGO, 350–500 °C)**. The HGO is recycled to extinction (used as feedstock for hydrocracking or returned to the coker).
6. The drum is **steam-stripped** to recover light hydrocarbons.
7. Coke is **cut out** by high-pressure water jets (10,000 – 15,000 kPa).
8. **Decoking** cycle ≈ 16 – 24 hours.

## 2.4 Fluid Coking & Flexicoking (Concise)

- **Fluid Coking:** Residue is sprayed onto a hot (≈ 530 °C) fluidized bed of coke particles. Coke is deposited on particles, which are withdrawn, and a portion is burned in a **heater vessel** to supply the heat of reaction (continuous heat integration).
- **Flexicoking:** Adds a **third fluidized gasifier vessel** where ~ 60 % of the coke is gasified with steam + air/O₂, producing **low-BTU fuel gas** (replacing H₂ from external reformers). Net coke yield is only **1 – 2 wt%** of feed.

## 2.5 Coke Morphology (Structure of Green Coke)

| Type | Appearance | Feeds | Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Sponge Coke** | Porous, spongy, uniform | Low-CCR (< 15 %), low-metal VR | Fuel, calcined for general anodes |
| **Shot Coke** | Spherical, fused "BBs" (5 – 30 mm) | High-asphaltene, high-CCR VR | Fuel-grade, cement kilns |
| **Needle Coke** | Acicular, needle-like, low CTE | Decant oil, coal-tar pitch, hydrotreated VR | Graphite electrodes for steel/EAF |
| **Honeycomb / Transition** | Intermediate morphology | Mixed feedstock | Marginal anode grade |

> [!NOTE]
> **Determining factor for morphology:** The **feed CCR** and the **aromaticity** of the feed. Needles form from highly aromatic, low-sulphur, low-CCR feeds; shot forms from highly paraffinic or high-CCR feeds.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units | Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Y_{coke} = \dfrac{\text{CCR} \cdot 1.6}{100} \approx 1.6 \cdot \text{CCR}$ | Yield of coke from Conradson Carbon Residue | wt% | Empirical; valid for delayed coking of VR |
| 2 | $Y_{coke} = (1.05 \text{ to } 2.0) \cdot \text{CCR}$ | Process-dependent factor | wt% | Delayed ≈ 1.6, Fluid ≈ 1.05, Flexi ≈ 0.1 |
| 3 | $\text{Coke Yield (wt\%)} = \dfrac{M_c}{M_f} \times 100$ | Coke mass / Feed mass | wt% | Material balance |
| 4 | $F = G + L + C$ | Feed = Gas + Liquid + Coke (mass balance) | kg/h | Closed system |
| 5 | $F \cdot h_F = G \cdot h_G + L \cdot h_L + C \cdot h_C$ | Energy balance | kW | Adiabatic assumed |
| 6 | $\text{H/C atomic ratio} = \dfrac{\%H/1}{\%C/12}$ | Aromaticity index | — | Lower = more aromatic |
| 7 | $VM_{\text{calcined}} \approx 0.5\% \text{ (max)}$ | Specification for anode-grade calcined coke | wt% | Rotary kiln / rotary hearth calciner at 1200 – 1400 °C |
| 8 | $\text{CO}_2 \text{ reactivity} < 10\% \text{ loss}$ | ASTM D8138 specification | wt% | For anode-grade coke |
| 9 | $\text{Pore volume} = \dfrac{1}{\rho_{\text{bulk}}} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_{\text{real}}}$ | Open porosity of coke | cm³/g | Green coke |
| 10 | $HGI = 13 + \frac{66}{m^{0.185}}$ | Hardgrove Index, $m$ = screen oversize | — | Lower = harder |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aluminum industry (Hall-Héroult process):** Calcined petroleum coke is mixed with coal-tar pitch to form the **carbon anode** (consumes ≈ 0.42 t CPC per t Al).
- **Steel industry (EAF, UHP):** Ultra-high-power graphite electrodes made from **needle coke** are used in electric arc furnaces.
- **Cement & power industries:** Fuel-grade shot/sponge coke is co-fired in cement kilns and CFBC boilers.
- **TiO₂ (chloride process):** Coke is the reducing agent + carbon source.
- **Specialty:** Nuclear graphite, lithium-ion battery anodes (with further purification).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Perspective:** Every modern integrated refinery that processes sour heavy crude (e.g., Kuwait, Arab Heavy, Maya) MUST have a coker; otherwise, the bottom of the barrel becomes a stranded, unsellable product under IMO 2020.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Coke Yield from Conradson Carbon Residue (CCR)

The relationship between the laboratory CCR test and the actual coke yield of a delayed coker is empirical, derived from correlations on operating units:

**Laboratory CCR (Conradson Carbon Residue, ASTM D189):**
This test measures the residue left after evaporation and pyrolysis of a petroleum sample under specified conditions. The residue is reported in **wt%** and represents the **coke-forming tendency** of the feedstock.

**Empirical correlation (Maxwell and Olson, 1960s; modernized by Watson):**
Delayed cokers, because of partial secondary cracking of volatiles, produce **1.4 – 1.8 times** the laboratory CCR. Fluid cokers (shorter residence time, higher temperature) produce **0.95 – 1.15 times** the CCR.

**General correlation:**

$$
Y_{coke} = K \cdot \text{CCR}
$$

where the multiplier $K$ depends on the coking process:

$$
K = \begin{cases}
1.6 & \text{(Delayed Coking)} \\
1.05 & \text{(Fluid Coking)} \\
0.05 \text{ to } 0.15 & \text{(Flexicoking, net yield after gasification)}
\end{cases}
$$

### Worked Example 1 (3-mark / Part A standard):

> **Question:** A vacuum residue feed has CCR = 22 wt%. Estimate the coke yield from a **delayed coker**.

**Solution:**

$$
Y_{coke} = 1.6 \times \text{CCR} = 1.6 \times 22\,\text{wt\%}
$$

$$
Y_{coke} = 35.2\,\text{wt\%}
$$

**Answer:** Approximately **35 wt%** of feed becomes petroleum coke.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Five-Component Material Balance Across a Delayed Coker

Let:
- $F$ = feed rate (kg/h)
- $G$ = off-gas rate (C1–C4) (kg/h)
- $N$ = naphtha (C5 – 200 °C) (kg/h)
- $L$ = light gas oil (200 – 350 °C) (kg/h)
- $H$ = heavy gas oil (350 – 500 °C) (kg/h)
- $C$ = green coke (kg/h)

**Mass balance (steady state):**

$$
F = G + N + L + H + C
$$

**Coke yield:**

$$
Y_{coke} = \frac{C}{F} \times 100
$$

**Sum of liquid yields (distillates):**

$$
Y_{liquid} = \frac{N + L + H}{F} \times 100
$$

**Gas yield:**

$$
Y_{gas} = \frac{G}{F} \times 100
$$

**Closure check:**

$$
Y_{coke} + Y_{liquid} + Y_{gas} = 100\%
$$

### Worked Example 2 (Full 14-mark Part B style):

> **Question:** A delayed coker unit processes **10,000 kg/h** of vacuum residue (CCR = 18 wt%, S = 4.5 wt%). The unit produces:
> - Off-gas: 950 kg/h
> - Naphtha: 2,400 kg/h
> - Light gas oil: 2,800 kg/h
> - Heavy gas oil: 1,800 kg/h
> - Green coke: 2,050 kg/h
>
> Calculate:
> (a) The coke yield (wt%) and the **multiplier K** (i.e., Y_coke/CCR).
> (b) The total liquid distillate yield.
> (c) Verify the mass balance closure.
> (d) The sulphur content of the green coke if 35 % of feed sulphur is distributed to coke.

**Solution:**

#### Part (a): Coke yield and K-factor

$$
Y_{coke} = \frac{C}{F} \times 100 = \frac{2050}{10000} \times 100 = 20.5\,\text{wt\%}
$$

$$
K = \frac{Y_{coke}}{\text{CCR}} = \frac{20.5}{18} = 1.139
$$

> This is lower than the typical $K = 1.6$, indicating a **low-residue design coker** (modern high-conversion unit) or that some heavy gas oil is being recycled.

#### Part (b): Total liquid distillate yield

$$
Y_{liquid} = \frac{N + L + H}{F} \times 100 = \frac{2400 + 2800 + 1800}{10000} \times 100
$$

$$
Y_{liquid} = \frac{7000}{10000} \times 100 = 70.0\,\text{wt\%}
$$

#### Part (c): Mass balance closure

$$
Y_{gas} = \frac{G}{F} \times 100 = \frac{950}{10000} \times 100 = 9.5\,\text{wt\%}
$$

$$
Y_{coke} + Y_{liquid} + Y_{gas} = 20.5 + 70.0 + 9.5 = 100.0\,\text{wt\%}
$$

**Balanced.** ✓

#### Part (d): Sulphur content of green coke

Sulphur entering the coke (mass basis):

$$
S_{coke} = 0.35 \times S_{feed} = 0.35 \times (0.045 \times 10000) = 0.35 \times 450 = 157.5\,\text{kg/h}
$$

Sulphur content in coke (wt% of coke stream):

$$
\%S_{coke} = \frac{157.5}{2050} \times 100 = 7.68\,\text{wt\%}
$$

**Result:** Green coke has **7.68 wt% sulphur**, classifying it as **high-sulphur fuel-grade coke** (suitable for cement kilns, **NOT** for anode-grade aluminum smelting).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Insight:** KTU examiners often give a feed CCR and ask the student to back-calculate **typical product slate** (gas 8–12 %, liquid 65–75 %, coke 18–30 %). Memorize:
> - Total liquid ≈ **70 wt%**
> - Coke ≈ **1.6 × CCR**
> - Off-gas ≈ **10 wt%**

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Calcined Coke Mass Balance

**Definition of Calcination:** Heating green coke to **1200 – 1400 °C** to drive off residual volatiles and moisture, increasing real density to **2.0 – 2.13 g/cm³** and electrical conductivity.

Let:
- $M_g$ = mass of green coke (kg)
- $M_c$ = mass of calcined coke (kg)
- $VM_g$ = volatile matter in green coke (wt%)
- $M_g$ in green coke (wt%)

**Mass loss during calcination:**

$$
M_c = M_g \left(1 - \frac{VM_g + M_g}{100}\right) = M_g \left(1 - \frac{VM_g}{100} - \frac{M_g}{100}\right)
$$

In a **simplified** form (assuming moisture is small):

$$
M_c \approx M_g \left(1 - \frac{VM_g}{100}\right)
$$

### Worked Example 3:

> **Question:** 10,000 kg of green coke contains 11 wt% volatile matter and 1 wt% moisture. The calciner produces calcined coke with VM ≤ 0.5 wt% and no moisture. Calculate the calcined coke yield and the volumetric loss in the kiln.

**Solution:**

Volatiles + moisture lost:

$$
\text{Loss} = (0.11 + 0.01) \times 10000 = 1200\,\text{kg}
$$

Calcined coke:

$$
M_c = 10000 - 1200 = 8800\,\text{kg}
$$

Calcined coke yield:

$$
Y_{calcined} = \frac{8800}{10000} \times 100 = 88\,\text{wt\%}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU Answer:** Calcined coke yield from green coke ≈ **84 – 88 wt%**, corresponding to **0.86 – 0.88 t calcined coke per t green coke**.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Coke Drum Cycle Time

For a delayed coker with two drums operating in **semi-batch** mode:

Total cycle time:

$$
t_{cycle} = t_{fill} + t_{coke} + t_{steam-strip} + t_{cool} + t_{decoke} + t_{warm-up}
$$

Typical values:

$$
t_{cycle} = 1 + 18 + 2 + 4 + 4 + 3 = 32\,\text{h}
$$

With two drums staggered, **one drum is always filling while the other is being decoked**. The unit operates continuously with a **throughput per day** of:

$$
\text{Throughput (t/day)} = \frac{V_{drum} \times \rho_{coke} \times 24}{t_{cycle}}
$$

where $V_{drum}$ = drum volume (m³) and $\rho_{coke} \approx 0.8$ t/m³ (in-place green coke density).

### Worked Example 4:

> **Question:** A delayed coker has a drum diameter of 8 m and straight height of 26 m. In-place coke density = 0.8 t/m³. Cycle time = 36 h. Calculate coke production rate (t/day).

**Solution:**

Drum volume (cylindrical):

$$
V_{drum} = \pi r^2 h = \pi \times 4^2 \times 26 = \pi \times 16 \times 26 = 416\pi \approx 1307\,\text{m}^3
$$

Coke mass per drum:

$$
m_{coke} = V_{drum} \times \rho_{coke} = 1307 \times 0.8 = 1045.6\,\text{t}
$$

Production rate (one drum fills in cycle, two drums alternate):

$$
\dot{m} = \frac{m_{coke}}{t_{cycle}} \times 24 = \frac{1045.6}{36} \times 24 = 697.1\,\text{t/day}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Always state assumptions (in-place density 0.8 t/m³, cycle 30–40 h). Examiners deduct 1–2 marks if these are not explicitly mentioned.

---

## 3.5 Summary of Key Numerical Anchors (For Rapid Recall)

$$
\boxed{Y_{coke} \approx 1.6 \cdot \text{CCR} \quad ; \quad Y_{liquid} \approx 70\% \quad ; \quad Y_{gas} \approx 10\% \quad ; \quad Y_{calcined} \approx 87\%}
$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Flow Diagram — Delayed Coking Unit

The following Mermaid diagram shows the **functional architecture** of a Delayed Coking Unit (DCU), including the two-drum operation, fractionator, recycle loop, and decoking water jet system.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Vacuum Residue Feed Tank] --> B[Feed Pump P101]
    B --> C[Fractionator Bottom Preheater]
    C --> D[Heater F101 - 500 C]
    D --> E[Switching Valve SV01]
    E --> F1[Coke Drum 1 - Filling]
    E --> F2[Coke Drum 2 - Decoking]
    F1 --> G1[Top Vapor Line to Fractionator]
    F2 --> G2[Steam Strip Line]
    F1 --> H1[High Pressure Water Jet - 15000 kPa]
    F2 --> H2[Coke Pit and Crusher]
    G1 --> I[Main Fractionator]
    I --> J1[Off Gas C1-C4]
    I --> J2[Naphtha 35-200 C]
    I --> J3[Light Gas Oil 200-350 C]
    I --> J4[Heavy Gas Oil 350-500 C]
    I --> J5[Recycle HGO back to Heater]
    F2 --> K[Coke Conveyor to Calciner]
    K --> L[Rotary Kiln Calciner 1300 C]
    L --> M[Calcined Petroleum Coke Silos]
    H1 --> N[Decoking Water Settling Tank]
    N --> O[Clarified Water Recycle to P101]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Guide for KTU Students:**
> - **A → B → C → D:** Liquid residue is pressurized and progressively heated.
> - **D → E → F1/F2:** The hot, partially cracked feed alternates between two coke drums.
> - **F1 → I:** Vapors return to the fractionator for separation.
> - **F2 → H1 → N:** Coke removal and water recovery.
> - **K → L → M:** Green coke is upgraded to calcined coke for industrial use.

## 4.2 Process Flow Topology — Fluid Coking vs. Flexicoking Comparison

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph FC[FLUID COKING UNIT]
        F1[Fresh Feed VR] --> R1[Reactor 530 C]
        R1 --> V1[Vapors to Fractionator]
        R1 --> C1[Coke Particles to Heater]
        C1 --> H1[Heater Vessel 600 C]
        H1 --> FT1[Flue Gas to Stack]
        H1 --> R1
    end
    subgraph FX[FLEXICOKING UNIT]
        F2[Fresh Feed VR] --> R2[Reactor 530 C]
        R2 --> V2[Vapors to Fractionator]
        R2 --> C2[Coke Particles to Heater]
        C2 --> H2[Heater Vessel 600 C]
        H2 --> G2[Gasifier 950 C Steam+O2]
        G2 --> LB[Low BTU Fuel Gas]
        G2 --> R2
    end
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree — Coke Morphology Selection

```mermaid
graph TD
    S[Start: Choose Coker Type] --> Q1{Feed CCR?}
    Q1 -->|CCR less than 8 low aromatic| N1[Decant oil / Coal tar pitch]
    Q1 -->|CCR 8 to 20 aromatic| N2[Vacuum Residue]
    Q1 -->|CCR greater than 20 high asphaltene| N3[Heavy Residue]
    N1 --> P1[Needle Coke]
    N2 --> P2[Sponge Coke]
    N3 --> P3[Shot Coke]
    P1 --> A1[Graphite Electrodes / Nuclear Graphite]
    P2 --> A2[Anode Grade CPC / Fuel]
    P3 --> A3[Cement Kiln / Power Plant]
```

## 4.4 Material Balance Sankey-Style Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    F[Feed VR 10000 kg/h] --> G[Off Gas 950 kg/h 9.5 percent]
    F --> N[Naphtha 2400 kg/h 24 percent]
    F --> L[LGO 2800 kg/h 28 percent]
    F --> H[HGO 1800 kg/h 18 percent]
    F --> C[Green Coke 2050 kg/h 20.5 percent]
    C --> CV[Volatiles 12 percent lost]
    C --> CC[Calcined Coke 1804 kg/h]
    CC --> AN[Anode Grade for Aluminum]
    CC --> FU[Fuel Grade for Cement]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Tip:** KTU board questions often ask for a **labeled block diagram** of a delayed coker. The minimum required labeled blocks are: **Heater → Coke Drum → Fractionator → Decoking system → Calciner (optional)**. Missing any of these = 2 mark deduction.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: Define petroleum coke. List the four major morphological types of petroleum coke with one industrial application of each.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Petroleum coke is a solid, black, carbonaceous residue produced by the **thermal cracking and condensation** of heavy petroleum residues (vacuum residue, decant oil, or tar pitch) at temperatures of **480 – 520 °C** in a coking process.

**Morphological Types and Applications:**

1. **Sponge Coke** — Used as **calcined petroleum coke (CPC)** for **carbon anodes in aluminum smelting** (Hall-Héroult process).
2. **Shot Coke** — Used as **fuel-grade coke** in **cement kilns and power plants** because of its high heat value and easy handling.
3. **Needle Coke** — Used as a precursor for **graphite electrodes in electric arc furnaces (EAF) for steel-making**.
4. **Honeycomb Coke** — Used as **general-purpose fuel** and as filler in the carbon industry.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Students often confuse the morphology of coke with the type of coker (delayed/fluid/flexi). The morphology (sponge/shot/needle) is a property of the **coke product**, not the reactor. Examiners deduct 1 mark if this distinction is blurred.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: What is the role of the recycle HGO stream in a delayed coker? Why is delayed coking called "delayed"?**

**Model Answer:**

**Role of Recycle HGO:**
- The heavy gas oil (HGO, 350 – 500 °C) fraction from the fractionator is **recycled back to the coker heater** to increase conversion of heavy liquids to lighter products.
- The recycle ratio is defined as $\text{RR} = \dfrac{\text{Recycle HGO}}{\text{Fresh Feed}}$, typically **0.05 – 0.5** (low RR) or **1.0 – 1.4** (high RR for full conversion).
- Higher RR → lower coke yield, higher gas + naphtha yield, but higher furnace duty.

**Why "Delayed":**
- The coking reactions are **delayed (retarded)** until the heated feedstock exits the furnace and enters the **coke drum**.
- This design allows the **furnace tubes to remain relatively coke-free** (residence time in tubes ≈ 1 – 2 min, below the induction period of mesophase formation).
- The actual coke formation occurs slowly (16 – 24 h residence) in the **drum**, not in the heater.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students wrongly explain "delayed" as "delayed coke removal" or "delayed decoking." The correct technical meaning is **delayed coking reaction (delayed mesophase formation until after the heater)**. Examiners specifically test this in 3-mark definition questions.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (Module 3 Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Each Module in the End-Semester Examination (ESE) carries a **14-mark question** with **internal choice** (i.e., the student answers EITHER Choice A OR Choice B). Both questions are designed to be of equal difficulty, mapped to CO3 and Bloom's Levels L3 (Apply) and L4 (Analyze).

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]`

**Q: With the help of a neat labeled process flow diagram, describe the **Delayed Coking Process** for petroleum residue. Discuss:**
- **(a)** The operating conditions, feed characteristics, and product yields in a delayed coker. **(7 marks)**
- **(b)** A vacuum residue feed of **12,000 kg/h** with **CCR = 20 wt%** is processed in a delayed coker. Estimate the **coke yield**, **liquid distillate yield**, and **gas yield**. Also calculate the **mass of calcined coke produced per day** if the calciner yield is 87 wt% of green coke. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Answer — Part (a): 7 marks**

**Process Description:**

The delayed coking process converts heavy residual oils into gaseous and liquid products plus petroleum coke in **semi-batch operation** using a switching drum system.

**Process Flow (refer to Section 4.1 diagram):**

1. **Vacuum residue feed** is pumped from storage, preheated against hot fractionator bottoms, and enters the **convection section** of the **coker heater (furnace)**.
2. The feed is rapidly heated to **495 – 505 °C** in the radiant tubes of the heater (residence time ≈ 1 – 2 min). Coking reactions are deliberately suppressed in the tubes.
3. The hot feed exits the heater and is routed via a **four-way switching valve** to the bottom of **Coke Drum 1** (operating), while **Coke Drum 2** is being decoked.
4. Inside the drum, the residence time is **16 – 24 hours** at low velocity, allowing the coking reactions to proceed to completion. Vapors rise, are quenched in the fractionator overhead, and are separated into **off-gas, naphtha, LGO, HGO**.
5. The HGO is **partially recycled** to the heater; the rest is sent to hydrocracker/other units.
6. After the drum is full of solid coke, it is **steam-stripped** (steam removes residual hydrocarbons from the coke pores).
7. The drum is **water-cooled**, then **deheaded**, and coke is **cut out** using a **high-pressure water jet (10,000 – 15,000 kPa)**.
8. The green coke is conveyed to a **crusher and calciner** (rotary kiln, 1200 – 1400 °C) to produce **calcined petroleum coke (CPC)**.

**Operating Conditions:**

| Parameter | Typical Value |
|---|---|
| Drum outlet temperature | **480 – 520 °C** |
| Drum pressure | **1 – 7 atm (gauge)** |
| Residence time in drum | **16 – 24 h** |
| Heater outlet temperature | **495 – 505 °C** |
| Recycle ratio | **0.05 – 1.4** |
| Cycle time | **24 – 48 h** |

**Feed Characteristics:** API 5 – 15, CCR 10 – 30 wt%, S 1 – 6 wt%, V+Ni 100 – 1000 ppm.

**Product Yields (typical):**

- **Off-gas (C1–C4):** 7 – 12 wt%
- **Naphtha (C5–200 °C):** 15 – 25 wt%
- **Light gas oil (200 – 350 °C):** 25 – 35 wt%
- **Heavy gas oil (350 – 500 °C):** 10 – 20 wt% (partially recycled)
- **Green coke:** 20 – 35 wt%

#### **Model Answer — Part (b): 7 marks**

**Given:**
- Feed rate, $F = 12{,}000$ kg/h
- CCR = 20 wt%
- Coker type: Delayed (multiplier $K = 1.6$)

**Step 1: Coke yield**

$$
Y_{coke} = 1.6 \times \text{CCR} = 1.6 \times 20 = 32\,\text{wt\%}
$$

Coke mass rate:

$$
\dot{m}_{coke} = 0.32 \times 12{,}000 = 3840\,\text{kg/h}
$$

**Step 2: Liquid distillate yield**

Using standard delayed coker product slate:

$$
Y_{liquid} \approx 70\,\text{wt\%} = 0.70 \times 12{,}000 = 8400\,\text{kg/h}
$$

**Step 3: Gas yield**

$$
Y_{gas} = 100 - Y_{coke} - Y_{liquid} = 100 - 32 - 70 = -2
$$

This indicates an **error in the typical 70 % liquid assumption**; with CCR = 20 wt% the liquid yield is closer to **58 – 60 %**. A more accurate method is:

**Alternative — Empirical Distribution:**

For a modern DCU with high recycle, the typical distribution is:

$$
Y_{gas} = 8\,\text{wt\%} \quad ; \quad Y_{liquid} = 60\,\text{wt\%} \quad ; \quad Y_{coke} = 32\,\text{wt\%}
$$

(verifying: 8 + 60 + 32 = 100 ✓)

Liquid mass rate:

$$
\dot{m}_{liquid} = 0.60 \times 12{,}000 = 7200\,\text{kg/h}
$$

Gas mass rate:

$$
\dot{m}_{gas} = 0.08 \times 12{,}000 = 960\,\text{kg/h}
$$

**Step 4: Mass of calcined coke per day**

Green coke per day:

$$
M_{green} = 3840 \times 24 = 92{,}160\,\text{kg/day} = 92.16\,\text{t/day}
$$

Calcined coke per day (87 wt% yield):

$$
M_{calcined} = 0.87 \times 92{,}160 = 80{,}179.2\,\text{kg/day} \approx 80.18\,\text{t/day}
$$

**Final Answers:**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Coke yield | **32 wt%** |
| Liquid distillate yield | **60 wt%** |
| Off-gas yield | **8 wt%** |
| Green coke rate | **3840 kg/h (92.16 t/day)** |
| Calcined coke rate | **80.18 t/day** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning (4 marks lost on this type of question):**
> 1. Using $K = 1.6$ for **fluid coker** (should be 1.05) → −1 mark.
> 2. Reporting calcined coke as **equal to green coke** (forgetting 12 – 16 % mass loss) → −1 mark.
> 3. Forgetting to multiply coke rate by 24 h when reporting **daily tonnage** → −1 mark.
> 4. Not specifying the **drum cycle / semi-batch nature** in the process description → −1 mark.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`** (ALTERNATIVE CHOICE)

**Q: Discuss the following with respect to petroleum coke production:**
- **(a)** Compare **Fluid Coking and Flexicoking** processes with respect to feed, products, operating conditions, and net coke yield. Explain why Flexicoking produces virtually no net coke. **(7 marks)**
- **(b)** A needle coke plant processes **5000 kg/h** of decant oil feedstock. The coke yield is **18 wt%**. The calciner operates at **1300 °C** with **green coke VM = 10 wt%** and **moisture = 0.5 wt%**. Calculate (i) green coke rate per day, (ii) calcined needle coke rate per day, (iii) the real density of the calcined coke assuming true density of graphite (2.26 g/cm³) and an apparent porosity of 21 %. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Answer — Part (a): 7 marks**

| Aspect | Fluid Coking | Flexicoking |
|---|---|---|
| **Inventor / Year** | Exxon, 1954 | Exxon, 1976 |
| **Reactor** | Fluidized bed of coke particles | Fluidized bed + gasifier |
| **Feed** | Vacuum residue, atmospheric residue | Vacuum residue, heavy oils |
| **Temperature** | 510 – 565 °C (reactor), 600 °C (heater) | 510 – 565 °C (reactor), 815 – 980 °C (gasifier) |
| **Pressure** | ~ 1 atm (gauge) | ~ 1 atm (gauge) |
| **Heat source** | Burning a portion of coke in heater vessel with air | Burning a portion of coke in gasifier with steam + O₂/air |
| **Products** | Gases, LPG, naphtha, gas oil, fluid coke | Gases, LPG, naphtha, gas oil, **low-BTU fuel gas**, **very little net coke** |
| **Net Coke Yield** | 1.05 × CCR (~ 95 % of CCR) | **0.05 – 0.15 × CCR** (5 – 15 % of CCR) |
| **Coke morphology** | Granular, 0.1 – 0.4 mm | Granular, 0.1 – 0.4 mm |
| **Sulphur in coke** | Same as feed (~ 5 – 7 wt%) | **Low (1 – 3 wt%)** because most S is gasified to H₂S and recovered |

**Why Flexicoking Produces Almost No Net Coke:**

In Flexicoking, the coke particles are circulated through **three vessels**:
1. **Reactor** (coking at 530 °C): fresh feed coats hot coke particles and cracks to vapors.
2. **Heater** (600 °C): partial combustion of coke with air supplies process heat.
3. **Gasifier** (815 – 980 °C): remaining coke reacts with **steam + O₂**:

$$
\text{C} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 \quad ; \quad \text{C} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CO} + \text{H}_2
$$

Approximately **60 – 95 %** of the coke is **gasified** to CO + CO₂ + H₂, leaving only a **small purge stream** of coke for sale. Hence, net coke yield is only **1 – 5 wt%** of feed.

The **low-BTU fuel gas** produced (mainly CO + H₂ + CH₄) is piped to the refinery's fuel gas header to fire heaters and boilers, replacing expensive natural gas.

#### **Model Answer — Part (b): 7 marks**

**Given:**
- Feed rate $= 5000$ kg/h (decant oil)
- Coke yield $Y_{coke} = 18$ wt%
- VM in green coke = 10 wt%
- Moisture in green coke = 0.5 wt%
- Calcination temperature = 1300 °C

**Part (i): Green coke rate per day**

Coke rate (hourly):

$$
\dot{m}_{green} = 0.18 \times 5000 = 900\,\text{kg/h}
$$

Daily green coke:

$$
M_{green,daily} = 900 \times 24 = 21{,}600\,\text{kg/day} = 21.6\,\text{t/day}
$$

**Part (ii): Calcined needle coke rate per day**

Total loss during calcination (VM + moisture):

$$
\text{Loss} = 0.10 + 0.005 = 0.105 = 10.5\,\text{wt\%}
$$

Calcined coke yield (per kg green):

$$
Y_{calcined} = 1 - 0.105 = 0.895
$$

Calcined coke per day:

$$
M_{calcined,daily} = 0.895 \times 21{,}600 = 19{,}332\,\text{kg/day} \approx 19.33\,\text{t/day}
$$

**Part (iii): Real density of calcined coke**

Apparent (bulk) density of calcined needle coke is **typically 1.65 – 1.75 g/cm³**, while real (He-pycnometer) density is **2.05 – 2.13 g/cm³** for needle coke.

The relationship between **real density ($\rho_{real}$)**, **apparent density ($\rho_{app}$)**, and **porosity ($P$)** is:

$$
P = 1 - \frac{\rho_{app}}{\rho_{real}}
$$

Solving for apparent density (real density of graphite = 2.26 g/cm³, porosity = 0.21):

$$
\rho_{app} = \rho_{real} \times (1 - P) = 2.26 \times (1 - 0.21) = 2.26 \times 0.79 = 1.785\,\text{g/cm}^3
$$

**Final Answers:**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Green coke rate | **900 kg/h (21.6 t/day)** |
| Calcined needle coke rate | **19.33 t/day** |
| Apparent (bulk) density of calcined coke | **1.785 g/cm³** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Errors in Density Questions:**
> 1. Confusing **real density** (He-pycnometer, ~ 2.0 – 2.13 g/cm³ for calcined needle coke) with **bulk (apparent) density** (~ 1.6 – 1.8 g/cm³) → −2 marks.
> 2. Forgetting to apply **porosity formula** $P = 1 - \rho_{app}/\rho_{real}$ → −1 mark.
> 3. Reporting calcined coke yield as **100 %** of green coke (ignoring VM + moisture loss) → −1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — COKE PRODUCTION (Module 3)**

### A. Conceptual Recap
- [x] Petroleum coke is a **solid, carbonaceous residue** from thermal coking of VR / decant oil at **480 – 520 °C**.
- [x] Three commercial processes: **Delayed, Fluid, Flexi** (in order of coke yield: Delayed > Fluid > Flexi).
- [x] The term **"delayed"** refers to **delayed mesophase formation** (coking occurs in drum, not in heater).
- [x] **Coke morphology** = feed property (CCR, aromaticity, S, metals) — not reactor type.
- [x] **Needle coke** requires **highly aromatic, low-S, low-metal** feedstock (decant oil / coal-tar pitch).
- [x] **Shot coke** forms from **high-CCR, high-asphaltene, paraffinic** feeds.
- [x] **Calcination** removes VM + moisture at **1200 – 1400 °C**, increasing real density from 1.3 to 2.0+ g/cm³.

### B. Formula Recap (Memorize)
- [x] $Y_{coke} = K \cdot \text{CCR}$, with $K = 1.6$ (delayed), $1.05$ (fluid), $0.05$ (flexi).
- [x] $F = G + L + C$ (mass balance closure).
- [x] $Y_{calcined} = 1 - (VM + M_g)/100 \approx 0.85 - 0.88$.
- [x] Recycle Ratio $\text{RR} = \dfrac{\text{Recycle HGO}}{\text{Fresh Feed}}$, typically 0.05 – 1.4.
- [x] Coke drum cycle ≈ 24 – 48 h, two-drum operation, throughput $\propto V_{drum} \rho_{coke} / t_{cycle}$.

### C. Key Operating Parameters
- [x] **Temperature:** 480 – 520 °C (delayed) | 510 – 565 °C (fluid/flexi).
- [x] **Pressure:** 1 – 7 atm (delayed) | ~ 1 atm (fluid/flexi).
- [x] **Residence time in drum:** 16 – 24 h.
- [x] **Heater outlet T:** 495 – 505 °C.
- [x] **Coke drum cycle:** 24 – 48 h.
- [x] **Calcination T:** 1200 – 1400 °C.

### D. Industrial Applications
- [x] **Aluminum smelting** (Hall-Héroult) → Sponge / CPC → **Carbon anodes**.
- [x] **Steel EAF** → Needle coke → **UHP graphite electrodes**.
- [x] **Cement kilns, power plants** → Shot / sponge coke → **Fuel-grade**.
- [x] **TiO₂ chloride process** → Coke as **reductant + carbon source**.

### E. Common Board-Question Traps
- [x] Confusing "delayed coking" with "delayed decoking."
- [x] Using $K = 1.6$ for fluid coker (wrong — use 1.05).
- [x] Reporting calcined coke yield as 100 % of green coke (wrong — subtract VM + moisture).
- [x] Forgetting **sulphur partition** (35 – 50 % of feed S goes to coke in delayed coker).
- [x] Confusing **real density (He-pycnometer)** with **bulk (tap) density** in numerical problems.

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 3 – Coke Production Notes. Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme Course Outcomes CO3 (Apply) and CO4 (Analyze).**
