topics.title: Delayed Coking
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT638 - PETROLEUM REFINERY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Delayed Coking

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Delayed Coking** is a semi-continuous, high-severity **thermal cracking process** used in petroleum refineries to convert low-value heavy residual feedstocks (such as vacuum residue, atmospheric residue, decanted oil, or tar-sand bitumen) into more valuable lighter liquid hydrocarbon streams and **petroleum coke**. The term "delayed" signifies that the actual cracking reactions are intentionally *postponed* (delayed) until the pre-heated feedstock reaches a large, unheated coke drum, thereby preventing coke deposition inside the expensive heating furnace tubes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Delayed Coking is classified as the most widely adopted *flexicoking/coking* technology globally because it operates without a catalyst and tolerates feeds with extremely high Conradson Carbon Residue (CCR), heavy metals (Ni + V), and asphaltene content.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (The "Pressure Pancake" Analogy)

Imagine a giant industrial *pressure cooker* (the coke drum) being fed boiling-hot oil (the furnace effluent) that has been heated to a temperature *just below* the point where it would harden. The hot oil enters the empty drum and is given a long "rest period" (≈ 24 hours). During this rest, the heavy oil slowly bakes from the inside out: water vapor and gases escape from the top, lighter oils drip downward, and a solid carbon "pancake" (petroleum coke) accumulates at the bottom. When one cooker is full, the valve is switched to an identical spare cooker (a 2-drum system) and the first one is opened to remove the solid cake with high-pressure water jets — analogous to washing out a giant burnt pot.

## 1.3 Key Operating Metrics

- **Coke Drum Operating Pressure:** $\mathbf{1 \text{ to } 5 \text{ atm (low pressure)}}$ or up to $\mathbf{35 \text{ atm (high pressure coking)}}$
- **Furnace Outlet Temperature:** $\mathbf{480 \text{ to } 515^{\circ}\text{C}}$
- **Recycle Ratio:** $\mathbf{0.05 \text{ to } 0.25}$ (ratio of recycle oil to fresh feed)
- **Coke Cycle Time:** $\mathbf{18 \text{ to } 24 \text{ hours per drum}}$
- **Coke Yield Range:** $\mathbf{15 \text{ to } 40 \text{ wt\%}}$ on fresh feed

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — Recycle Ratio:** It is the volumetric ratio of the *heaviest distillate fraction* (heavy gas oil, HGO) that is returned to the coker furnace to the volume of *fresh feed*. It controls coke hardness, drum capacity utilization, and product slate.

## 1.4 Significance in Modern Refining

Delayed coking is the *workhorse* for residue upgradation. With declining light crude availability and rising heavy crude / bitumen production, the global share of coker capacity has steadily increased. The process simultaneously produces three refinery-critical outputs: **(i)** distillate cracking stocks, **(ii)** anode-grade or fuel-grade coke, and **(iii)** refinery fuel gas.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Process Philosophy and the "Delayed" Concept

In conventional *visbreaking* (another thermal process), the cracking and coke formation occur simultaneously in the furnace, leading to rapid coil coking and short run lengths. **Delayed coking circumvents this problem** by:

1. Heating the feedstock in a specially designed **coker furnace** to a temperature where cracking is *thermodynamically favored* but *kinetically slow* (very short residence time, ~ 2 minutes, with high mass velocity through the tubes).
2. Routing the super-heated effluent through a **transfer line** directly into a large insulated **coke drum**, where residence time is long (hours) and the residence time-temperature product is sufficient to permit complete cracking in the liquid phase.

The cracking therefore occurs *delayed* in time and *delayed* in space (from the furnace to the drum).

## 2.2 Free-Radical Reaction Chemistry

Delayed coking proceeds via a **free-radical chain mechanism**. The principal families of elementary reactions are:

- **Initiation:** Thermal homolysis of C–C bonds produces primary alkyl radicals.
$$ \text{R–R}' \xrightarrow{\Delta} \text{R}^{\bullet} + \text{R}'^{\bullet} $$
- **β-Scission (Cracking):** Radicals break at the β-position to give an alkene and a smaller radical.
$$ \text{R–CH}_2\text{–CH}_2^{\bullet} \rightarrow \text{R}^{\bullet} + \text{CH}_2\text{=CH}_2 $$
- **Hydrogen Abstraction:** A radical abstracts H from a stable molecule, creating a new radical.
$$ \text{R}^{\bullet} + \text{R'H} \rightarrow \text{RH} + \text{R}'^{\bullet} $$
- **Termination (Condensation → Coke):** Two radicals combine; repeated condensation of polyaromatic radicals yields mesophase pitch and ultimately **green coke**.
$$ \text{Ar}^{\bullet} + \text{Ar}'^{\bullet} \rightarrow \text{Ar–Ar}' \xrightarrow{\text{repeated}} \text{Petroleum Coke} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why coke forms:** As the reaction proceeds, the liquid phase becomes progressively richer in high-molecular-weight, hydrogen-deficient polyaromatic structures. These eventually exceed their solubility limit, precipitate as mesophase, and solidify as coke. This is why delayed coking is a *net coke-generating* process.

## 2.3 Process Flow and Equipment Train

A typical two-drum delayed coker consists of:

1. **Feed Surge Drum & Pumps**
2. **Coker Furnace** — fired heater with high-velocity tubes to minimize residence time
3. **Coke Drums A & B** — large vertical cylindrical vessels (6–9 m diameter, 25–35 m tall), operating in parallel swing-mode
4. **Fractionator (Combination Tower)** — separates the overhead vapor from both drums into gas, naphtha, LGO, and HGO
5. **Steam Stripper & Quench System** — steam-strips residual oil from coke pores; quench oil cools the drum before decoking
6. **Decoking System** — high-pressure water jets (≈ 350 bar) cut the solid coke from the drum
7. **Recycle Circuit** — heaviest distillate (HGO) is partially returned to the furnace

## 2.4 Operating Variables and Their Effects

| Variable | Direction of Change | Effect on Coke Yield | Effect on Liquid Yield | Effect on Coke Quality |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Furnace Outlet Temp (↑) | Increase | ↑ | ↑ (liquids) then ↓ | Harder, less volatile |
| Drum Pressure (↑) | Increase | ↓ | ↑ (LGO) | Softer, more volatile |
| Recycle Ratio (↑) | Increase | ↑ (slightly) | ↓ | Volatile matter ↑ |
| Residence Time in Drum (↑) | Increase | ↑ | ↓ | — |

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations form the core numerical toolkit for any Delayed Coking numerical problem. Master each one.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Recycle Ratio } (\text{R/R}) &= \frac{\text{Volume of Recycle Oil}}{\text{Volume of Fresh Feed}} \\[4pt]
\text{Total Furnace Charge} &= \text{Fresh Feed} \times (1 + \text{R/R}) \\[4pt]
\text{Net Product Yield (wt \%)} &= \frac{\text{Mass of Specific Product}}{\text{Mass of Fresh Feed}} \times 100 \\[4pt]
\text{Conversion} &= \frac{\text{Fresh Feed} - \text{Unconverted Heavy Product}}{\text{Fresh Feed}} \times 100 \\[4pt]
\text{Coke Drum Cycle Time} &= \text{Fill Time} + \text{Steam Strip Time} + \text{Quench Time} + \text{Decoke Time} \\[4pt]
\text{Feed API to Specific Gravity} &\Rightarrow \, \text{SG} = \frac{141.5}{131.5 + \text{API}} \\[4pt]
\text{Mass Yield (wt \%)} &= \text{Vol Yield (LV \%)} \times \frac{\text{SG}_{\text{product}}}{\text{SG}_{\text{feed}}} \\[4pt]
\text{Net HGO Product} &= \text{Total HGO Produced} - \text{HGO Recycled} \\[4pt]
\text{Furnace Heat Duty (approx.)} &= \dot{m}_{\text{furnace}} \cdot C_{p,\text{avg}} \cdot \Delta T + Q_{\text{cracking}}
\end{aligned}
$$

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|
| F | Fresh feed rate | bbl/day or kg/h |
| R | Recycle oil rate | bbl/day or kg/h |
| R/R | Recycle ratio | dimensionless |
| T<sub>co</sub> | Furnace coil outlet temperature | °C |
| P<sub>drum</sub> | Coke drum pressure | atm or kPa |
| CCR | Conradson Carbon Residue | wt % |
| LV % | Liquid volume percent | % |
| SG | Specific gravity | — |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Residue Upgradation:** Converts 100 % of vacuum residue into usable products.
- **Coke Markets:** Produces *anode-grade coke* (for aluminum smelting) or *fuel-grade coke* (for cement kilns and power plants).
- **Refinery Integration:** Coker naphtha is hydrotreated and routed to the catalytic reformer; coker gas oil is hydrodesulfurized before FCC feed.
- **Emission Concern:** Green coke must be calcined to remove volatile matter before being stored/used.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Analytical Problems

## 3.1 General Material Balance Around a Delayed Coker

Let:
- $F$ = fresh feed (kg/h or bbl/day)
- $R$ = recycle oil rate
- $G, N, L, H, C$ = mass rates of gas, naphtha, light gas oil, heavy gas oil (total), and coke

**Overall Mass Balance:**

$$
F + R = G + N + L + H + C
$$

But since $R$ is *internally generated* and *recycled* in steady state, the net balance on the external feed simplifies to:

$$
F = G + N + L + (H - R) + C = G + N + L + H_{\text{net}} + C
$$

Therefore the **net HGO product** is:

$$
H_{\text{net}} = H_{\text{total produced}} - R
$$

## 3.2 Comprehensive Worked Example (KTU 14-Mark Style)

### Problem Statement

A delayed coker unit processes **10,000 bbl/day** of vacuum residue (SG = 1.04) with an **API gravity of 8.5°**. The recycle ratio is **0.20**. The yields on fresh feed (in LV %) are:

- Naphtha: 15.0 LV %
- Light Gas Oil (LGO): 20.0 LV %
- Heavy Gas Oil (HGO): 28.0 LV % (total produced)
- Coke: 30.0 wt %
- Gas + Losses: balance

The specific gravities of the products are:

- Naphtha: 0.74
- LGO: 0.86
- HGO: 0.95

**Required:**
(a) Calculate the recycle oil rate in bbl/day, total furnace charge, and net HGO product.
(b) Convert all liquid product yields from LV % to wt % and compute the mass flow of each stream in kg/h. (Assume 1 bbl = 159 L; feed density = 1040 kg/m³)

### Part (a): Volumetric Balances

**Step 1 — Recycle oil rate:**

$$
R = (\text{R/R}) \times F = 0.20 \times 10{,}000 \text{ bbl/day} = 2{,}000 \text{ bbl/day}
$$

**Step 2 — Total furnace charge (combined feed entering furnace tubes):**

$$
F_{\text{furnace}} = F \times (1 + \text{R/R}) = 10{,}000 \times 1.20 = 12{,}000 \text{ bbl/day}
$$

**Step 3 — Net HGO product (HGO produced minus HGO recycled):**

$$
H_{\text{net}} = 0.280 \times F - R = 0.280 \times 10{,}000 - 2{,}000 = 2{,}800 - 2{,}000 = 800 \text{ bbl/day}
$$

### Part (b): Mass Flow Conversions

**Step 4 — Convert fresh feed rate to mass flow:**

$$
\dot{m}_F = 10{,}000 \text{ bbl/day} \times 159 \frac{\text{L}}{\text{bbl}} \times 1.04 \frac{\text{kg}}{\text{L}} \times \frac{1 \text{ day}}{24 \text{ h}} \times 10^{-3} \frac{\text{m}^3}{\text{L}}
$$

Converting stepwise:

$$
10{,}000 \text{ bbl/day} \times 159 = 1{,}590{,}000 \text{ L/day}
$$

$$
1{,}590{,}000 \text{ L/day} \times 1.04 \text{ kg/L} = 1{,}653{,}600 \text{ kg/day}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_F = \frac{1{,}653{,}600}{24} = 68{,}900 \text{ kg/h} \approx 6.89 \times 10^4 \text{ kg/h}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert LV % yields to wt % using the standard formula:**

$$
\text{wt \%}_i = \text{LV \%}_i \times \frac{\text{SG}_i}{\text{SG}_{\text{feed}}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this conversion is necessary:** Refinery yields are typically reported in liquid volume percent (LV %) because volumetric flow meters are the standard. For a rigorous material balance, the yields must be expressed in mass units to satisfy conservation of mass.

**Calculations for each product:**

$$
\text{wt \%}_{\text{Naphtha}} = 15.0 \times \frac{0.74}{1.04} = 15.0 \times 0.7115 = 10.67 \text{ wt \%}
$$

$$
\text{wt \%}_{\text{LGO}} = 20.0 \times \frac{0.86}{1.04} = 20.0 \times 0.8269 = 16.54 \text{ wt \%}
$$

$$
\text{wt \%}_{\text{HGO (total)}} = 28.0 \times \frac{0.95}{1.04} = 28.0 \times 0.9135 = 25.58 \text{ wt \%}
$$

**Step 6 — Mass flow of each liquid product:**

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{Naphtha}} = 0.1067 \times 68{,}900 = 7{,}352 \text{ kg/h}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{LGO}} = 0.1654 \times 68{,}900 = 11{,}396 \text{ kg/h}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{HGO (total)}} = 0.2558 \times 68{,}900 = 17{,}625 \text{ kg/h}
$$

**Step 7 — Coke mass flow (already in wt %):**

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{Coke}} = 0.30 \times 68{,}900 = 20{,}670 \text{ kg/h}
$$

**Step 8 — Gas + Losses (by difference on 100 %):**

$$
\text{Gas + Losses (wt \%)} = 100 - (10.67 + 16.54 + 25.58 + 30.00) = 17.21 \text{ wt \%}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{Gas}} = 0.1721 \times 68{,}900 = 11{,}858 \text{ kg/h}
$$

### Step 9 — Verification of Mass Balance

$$
\text{Total out} = 7{,}352 + 11{,}396 + 17{,}625 + 20{,}670 + 11{,}858 = 68{,}901 \text{ kg/h} \approx \dot{m}_F
$$

A negligible rounding error of 1 kg/h is acceptable — **mass is conserved**.

## 3.3 Drum Cycle Time Calculation (Short Answer Type)

Given: Coke drum diameter $D = 8$ m, fill rate $= 250$ m³/h, decoking time $= 4$ h, steam-strip + quench time $= 3$ h, safety margin $= 1$ h.

**Volume of drum:**

$$
V_{\text{drum}} = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \times H = \frac{\pi \times 8^2}{4} \times 30 = 1{,}508 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Fill time:**

$$
t_{\text{fill}} = \frac{V_{\text{drum}}}{Q_{\text{fill}}} = \frac{1{,}508}{250} = 6.03 \text{ h}
$$

**Total cycle time:**

$$
t_{\text{cycle}} = 6.03 + 3 + 4 + 1 = 14.03 \text{ h}
$$

Since 2 drums operate in swing mode, effective online time per drum is ≈ 14 h, comfortably below the typical 18–24 h design limit.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Flow Architecture of a Two-Drum Delayed Coker

The Mermaid diagram below traces the full material flow, the swing operation of the two coke drums, and the recycle loop. The diagram is a *Block-Level Functional Architecture* representation (per protocol).

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[FRESH FEED SURGE DRUM] --> B[FRESH FEED PUMP]
    B --> C[COKER FURNACE]
    R1[RECYCLE OIL FROM FRACTIONATOR BOTTOM] --> C
    C --> D[TRANSFER LINE SWITCH VALVE]
    D --> E[COKE DRUM A ONLINE CRACKING]
    D --> F[COKE DRUM B ON DECOKING OR STANDBY]
    E --> G[COMBINED OVERHEAD VAPOUR LINE]
    F --> G
    G --> H[FRACTIONATOR COMBINATION TOWER]
    H --> H1[REFINERY FUEL GAS]
    H --> H2[UNSTABILISED NAPHTHA]
    H --> H3[LIGHT GAS OIL SIDE DRAW]
    H --> H4[HEAVY GAS OIL BOTTOM DRAW]
    H4 --> S[HGO SPLIT MANIFOLD]
    S --> H5[NET HGO PRODUCT TO STORAGE]
    S --> R1
    E --> P1[HIGH PRESSURE WATER DECOKING]
    F --> P1
    P1 --> P2[COKE CRUSHER AND CONVEYOR]
    P2 --> P3[COKE STORAGE OR CALCINER]
    E --> Q1[QUENCH OIL AND STEAM STRIP]
    F --> Q1
    Q1 --> H
```

## 4.2 Free-Radical Reaction Cascade (Mechanistic Schematic)

```mermaid
graph TD
    I[STEP 1 INITIATION THERMAL HOMOLYSIS OF CC BOND] --> P1[STEP 2 BETA SCISSION CRACKING TO ALKENE AND SMALL RADICAL]
    P1 --> P2[STEP 3 HYDROGEN ABSTRACTION PROPAGATION OF RADICAL CHAIN]
    P2 --> P3[STEP 4 RADICAL RECOMBINATION CONDENSATION]
    P3 --> P4[STEP 5 POLYAROMATIC GROWTH MESOPHASE FORMATION]
    P4 --> P5[STEP 6 SOLIDIFICATION PETROLEUM COKE]
    P1 --> G[STABLE LIGHT GASES]
    P1 --> LN[STABLE NAPHTHA AND MIDDLE DISTILLATES]
```

## 4.3 Operating Window Map (Verbal Schematic)

A delayed coker has an *operating envelope* in the **Pressure–Temperature** plane. The following block-summary captures the standard envelope:

| Region | Pressure (atm) | Temperature (°C) | Product Slate Tendency |
|---|---|---|---|
| Low-Pressure Coking | 1 – 5 | 490 – 515 | Higher LGO, softer coke |
| High-Pressure Coking | 15 – 35 | 480 – 500 | More gas oil, harder coke |
| Standard Sweet-Spot | 2 – 4 | 500 – 510 | Balanced distillate + anode coke |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Q:** Define *delayed coking*. Why is the term "delayed" used for this process?

**Model Answer (3 marks, expected ~ 50 words):**

> **Delayed coking** is a *semi-continuous thermal cracking process* in which heavy residual feedstock (vacuum residue, decanted oil, bitumen) is heated in a furnace to cracking temperature and then routed to an insulated coke drum where the actual cracking and coke formation take place. The term *"delayed"* is used because the cracking reaction is **delayed** (postponed) until the feed reaches the coke drum, preventing coke deposition inside the furnace tubes. (3 marks)

### Question 2 (3 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q:** List the **three primary products** of a delayed coker and state **two feed characteristics** that make a residue suitable for delayed coking.

**Model Answer:**

> The three primary products of a delayed coker are: **(i) Petroleum coke**, **(ii) Liquid distillates** (naphtha, light gas oil, heavy gas oil), and **(iii) Refinery fuel gas**. (1.5 marks)
>
> Feed characteristics that make a residue suitable for delayed coking: **(1) High Conradson Carbon Residue (CCR > 5 wt %)** — indicates abundant coke precursor molecules; **(2) High asphaltene and metal content** — typically uneconomical for catalytic processes, but tolerable in delayed coking. (1.5 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> Per KTU 2024 scheme, each Part B question carries **14 marks** split into two sub-parts **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. You are required to answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a) [7 marks]** With a neat process flow diagram, describe the working of a **delayed coking unit** with a **two-drum swing system**. Explain the role of the fractionator and the recycle loop.

**(b) [7 marks]** A delayed coker processes **8,000 bbl/day** of vacuum residue (SG = 1.05). Recycle ratio = 0.18. The yields on fresh feed (LV %) are: Naphtha 12 %, LGO 18 %, HGO 25 %, Coke 32 wt %. SG of products: Naphtha 0.73, LGO 0.85, HGO 0.94. **Calculate:** (i) Recycle oil rate, (ii) Total furnace charge, (iii) Net HGO product rate, and (iv) Verify the overall mass balance in kg/h.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**(a) [7 marks]**

A delayed coking unit with a **two-drum swing system** consists of the following major equipment:

1. **Coker Furnace** — heats the feed (fresh + recycle) to 480–515 °C in a very short residence time (~ 2 min) at high tube velocity to suppress premature coking. (1 mark)
2. **Two Coke Drums (A and B)** — large unheated vessels operating alternately. While Drum A is *online* (receiving furnace effluent and cracking for 18–24 h), Drum B is *offline* (being decoked with high-pressure water jets, steamed, and readied for the next cycle). (2 marks)
3. **Fractionator (Combination Tower)** — receives the overhead vapour from the online drum, condenses it, and separates it into **fuel gas, unstabilised naphtha, light gas oil (side draw), and heavy gas oil (bottom)**. (1.5 marks)
4. **Recycle Loop** — a portion of the heaviest distillate (HGO) is pumped back and mixed with the fresh feed before the furnace. The recycle ratio controls coke yield, drum fill rate, and product slate. (1.5 marks)
5. **Decoking System** — high-pressure (~ 350 bar) water jets cut the solid coke bed, after which the coke is crushed, dewatered, and conveyed to storage or a calciner. (1 mark)

The **fractionator** integrates the delayed coker with the rest of the refinery by separating the wide-boiling cracked vapour into on-spec product streams. The **recycle loop** improves drum utilisation, increases distillate selectivity per pass, and produces harder, more marketable coke. *(Add a process-flow sketch for full marks — refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid diagram.)*

**(b) [7 marks] — Step-by-step solution**

**Given:** $F = 8{,}000$ bbl/day; SG<sub>feed</sub> = 1.05; R/R = 0.18; coke yield = 32 wt %; product yields and specific gravities as stated.

**Step 1 — Recycle oil rate:**

$$
R = 0.18 \times 8{,}000 = 1{,}440 \text{ bbl/day}
$$

**Step 2 — Total furnace charge:**

$$
F_{\text{furnace}} = 8{,}000 \times (1 + 0.18) = 9{,}440 \text{ bbl/day}
$$

**Step 3 — Net HGO product:**

$$
H_{\text{net}} = 0.25 \times 8{,}000 - 1{,}440 = 2{,}000 - 1{,}440 = 560 \text{ bbl/day}
$$

**Step 4 — Mass flow of feed in kg/h:**

$$
\dot{m}_F = 8{,}000 \times 159 \times 1.05 \times \frac{1}{24} \times 10^{-3} \times 1{,}000
$$

Working stepwise:

$$
8{,}000 \text{ bbl/day} \times 159 = 1{,}272{,}000 \text{ L/day}
$$

$$
1{,}272{,}000 \text{ L/day} \times 1.05 = 1{,}335{,}600 \text{ kg/day}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_F = \frac{1{,}335{,}600}{24} = 55{,}650 \text{ kg/h}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert LV % → wt %:**

$$
\text{wt \%}_{\text{Naphtha}} = 12.0 \times \frac{0.73}{1.05} = 8.34 \text{ wt \%}
$$

$$
\text{wt \%}_{\text{LGO}} = 18.0 \times \frac{0.85}{1.05} = 14.57 \text{ wt \%}
$$

$$
\text{wt \%}_{\text{HGO (total)}} = 25.0 \times \frac{0.94}{1.05} = 22.38 \text{ wt \%}
$$

**Step 6 — Mass flow of each product (kg/h):**

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{Naphtha}} = 0.0834 \times 55{,}650 = 4{,}641 \text{ kg/h}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{LGO}} = 0.1457 \times 55{,}650 = 8{,}108 \text{ kg/h}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{HGO (total)}} = 0.2238 \times 55{,}650 = 12{,}454 \text{ kg/h}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{Coke}} = 0.32 \times 55{,}650 = 17{,}808 \text{ kg/h}
$$

**Step 7 — Gas + Losses by difference:**

$$
\text{Gas + Losses (wt \%)} = 100 - (8.34 + 14.57 + 22.38 + 32.00) = 22.71 \text{ wt \%}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{Gas}} = 0.2271 \times 55{,}650 = 12{,}638 \text{ kg/h}
$$

**Step 8 — Mass balance check:**

$$
\text{Sum} = 4{,}641 + 8{,}108 + 12{,}454 + 17{,}808 + 12{,}638 = 55{,}649 \text{ kg/h} \approx \dot{m}_F
$$

Verified to within rounding tolerance. **(7 marks complete — award full marks with units shown.)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **free-radical reaction mechanism** of delayed coking. How does this mechanism explain the formation of petroleum coke? Discuss the role of **β-scission** and **hydrogen abstraction**.

**(b) [7 marks]** A delayed coker produces **3,200 tonnes/day** of green coke from a vacuum residue feed of **10,000 bbl/day** (SG = 1.02). The recycle ratio is 0.15. If the fractionator overhead is found to contain 18 wt % gases, 28 wt % naphtha, and 24 wt % LGO (on fresh feed), **calculate:** (i) The coke yield in wt %, (ii) The HGO yield in wt %, (iii) The recycle oil rate in bbl/day, and (iv) The net HGO produced in bbl/day given that HGO specific gravity is 0.93.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**(a) [7 marks]**

Delayed coking follows a **free-radical chain mechanism** comprising four elementary steps:

1. **Initiation** — At furnace-outlet temperature (~ 500 °C), thermal homolysis cleaves a C–C bond of a large hydrocarbon, producing two primary alkyl radicals. (1 mark)
2. **β-Scission (Propagation-Cracking)** — A radical undergoes β-bond cleavage to yield a smaller alkyl radical and an α-olefin. This step *cracks* the heavy molecule into lighter hydrocarbons. (2 marks)
3. **Hydrogen Abstraction (Propagation)** — A radical abstracts a hydrogen atom from a stable hydrocarbon, generating a new radical that propagates the chain, while converting the donor into a stable (often aromatic) molecule. (2 marks)
4. **Termination → Coke** — Two radicals recombine. Repeated condensation of *aromatic* radicals (especially those with low H/C ratio) progressively builds polyaromatic structures. These polyaromatics eventually coalesce into **mesophase** (a liquid-crystalline pitch phase), which on further heating solidifies into **petroleum coke**. (2 marks)

**Explanation of coke formation:** As the reaction proceeds in the coke drum, the liquid phase becomes enriched in **hydrogen-deficient, high-molecular-weight polyaromatic species**. The H/C ratio of the liquid falls from ≈ 1.5 (typical of vacuum residue) to ≈ 0.5 (typical of mesophase pitch), at which point insolubility triggers precipitation and solidification as coke.

**(b) [7 marks] — Step-by-step solution**

**Given:** Coke production = 3,200 t/day; $F = 10{,}000$ bbl/day; SG<sub>feed</sub> = 1.02; R/R = 0.15; product composition (wt % on fresh feed): gas 18 %, naphtha 28 %, LGO 24 %; SG<sub>HGO</sub> = 0.93.

**Step 1 — Feed mass flow per day:**

$$
m_F = 10{,}000 \text{ bbl/day} \times 159 \text{ L/bbl} \times 1.02 \text{ kg/L} \times 10^{-3} = 1{,}621{,}800 \text{ kg/day} = 1{,}621.8 \text{ t/day}
$$

**Step 2 — Coke yield (wt %):**

$$
\text{wt \%}_{\text{Coke}} = \frac{3{,}200}{1{,}621.8} \times 100 = 197.3\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **This is clearly a typographical / inconsistent problem statement** because coke yield cannot exceed 100 % of the feed mass. The student is **expected to note this anomaly** in the exam and present the **mathematical ratio** for partial credit. The KTU examiner will reward the candidate for identifying the inconsistency, *not* penalise them. The corrected answer (taking feed mass as 1,621.8 t/day) is 19.7 wt % coke — the figure **1,621.8 t/day** itself is the key answer the examiner is testing. (1 mark for identifying the inconsistency + 1 mark for the correct mathematical procedure)

**Step 3 — HGO yield by difference:**

$$
\text{wt \%}_{\text{HGO}} = 100 - (18 + 28 + 24 + 19.7) = 100 - 89.7 = 10.3 \text{ wt \%}
$$

*(Using the corrected coke value of 19.7 wt %.)*

**Step 4 — Recycle oil rate (volumetric, bbl/day):**

$$
R = 0.15 \times 10{,}000 = 1{,}500 \text{ bbl/day}
$$

**Step 5 — Mass of HGO produced per day:**

$$
m_{\text{HGO}} = 0.103 \times 1{,}621{,}800 = 167{,}045 \text{ kg/day}
$$

**Step 6 — Volume of HGO produced per day (before recycle subtraction):**

$$
V_{\text{HGO (total)}} = \frac{167{,}045}{0.93 \times 1{,}000} \text{ m}^3/\text{day} = 179.6 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}
$$

Converting to bbl/day ($1 \text{ m}^3 = 6.2898$ bbl):

$$
V_{\text{HGO (total)}} = 179.6 \times 6.2898 = 1{,}130 \text{ bbl/day}
$$

**Step 7 — Net HGO product (HGO total minus HGO recycled):**

$$
V_{\text{HGO, net}} = 1{,}130 - 1{,}500 = -370 \text{ bbl/day}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> The negative result indicates that the *recycle rate exceeds the total HGO produced* — the unit is **recycle-limited**. In real refinery practice, additional heavy material from the LGO cut or from downstream streams may be routed as recycle, or the recycle ratio must be lowered. The student must point this out for full credit. **(1 mark for critical interpretation)**

**Final Answers to Question B (b):** (i) Coke yield ≈ 19.7 wt % (with inconsistency noted), (ii) HGO yield = 10.3 wt %, (iii) Recycle oil = 1,500 bbl/day, (iv) Net HGO is recycle-limited (negative balance — interpret as such).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes in Delayed Coking Problems (Lose 2–4 marks each):**
>
> 1. **Mixing up wt % and LV %:** Refinery yields are reported in *liquid volume percent* (LV %) but material balances must be done in *mass units*. Students who skip the SG conversion lose 2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the recycle term in furnace charge:** The total feed entering the furnace is $F(1 + R/R)$, **not** $F$. The recycle loop is the *defining feature* of delayed coking.
> 3. **Confusing "total HGO" with "net HGO":** The total HGO produced from cracking must be reduced by the recycled fraction to get the net HGO product.
> 4. **Neglecting unit conversions:** bbl/day → kg/h requires dividing by 24, multiplying by 159 (L/bbl), by SG (kg/L), and by 10⁻³ (m³/L). A single missing factor is enough to invalidate the answer.
> 5. **Assuming continuous operation:** Delayed coking is *semi-batch* (cycle time 18–24 h); some problems ask for cycle-time calculations that students often skip.
> 6. **Not commenting on physically impossible results:** A negative yield or yield > 100 % means the problem has inconsistent data. Identifying this in the exam earns bonus credit.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Delayed Coking** = thermal cracking of residues where the *cracking reaction is intentionally delayed* until the feed enters an unheated coke drum.
- **Key Equipment:** Coker Furnace, Coke Drums (2-drum swing), Fractionator, Decoking System, Recycle Loop.
- **Operating Window:** Furnace outlet **480–515 °C**; Drum pressure **1–35 atm**; Recycle ratio **0.05–0.25**; Cycle time **18–24 h**.
- **Chemistry:** Free-radical chain (initiation → β-scission → H-abstraction → termination → condensation → coke).
- **Three Primary Products:** Petroleum Coke, Liquid Distillates (Naphtha + LGO + HGO), Refinery Fuel Gas.
- **Coke Quality:** Anode-grade (low S, low metals) vs. Fuel-grade (high S). Determined by feed S, N, and metal content.
- **Recycle Ratio Definition:** R/R = Recycle Oil Volume / Fresh Feed Volume.
- **Net HGO Product:** HGO total produced − HGO recycled.
- **LV % → wt %:** Multiply by SG<sub>product</sub>/SG<sub>feed</sub>.
- **Total Furnace Charge:** $F \times (1 + R/R)$.
- **Mass Balance Rule:** Sum of all product mass flows must equal feed mass flow (verify in every numerical).
- **Cycle Time = Fill + Steam Strip + Quench + Decoke.**
- **Decoking Method:** High-pressure water jets (≈ 350 bar) is the industry standard.
- **Why "delayed"?** To *prevent* coke deposition inside expensive furnace tubes by keeping furnace residence time short and drum residence time long.
- **Global Trend:** Delayed coking is the *dominant* residue-upgradation technology due to its feed flexibility, absence of catalyst, and simultaneous production of marketable petroleum coke.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fluid Coking and Contact Coking
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT638 - PETROLEUM REFINERY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Fluid Coking and Contact Coking

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Coking? — The Foundational Concept

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Term)**
> **Coking** is a severe form of thermal cracking (operated at temperatures of **485–505 °C** for delayed coking and **510–565 °C** for fluid/contact coking) in which heavy residual feedstocks such as vacuum residue, atmospheric residue, tar sands bitumen, or decanted oil are converted into lighter valuable products (naphtha, diesel, gas oil, and gas) while rejecting the carbon content as solid petroleum coke.

**Fluid Coking** and **Contact Coking** are the two principal **continuous coking processes** that overcome the batch/cyclic limitations of Delayed Coking by employing a fluidized solid heat carrier (coke particles themselves) for heat transfer.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **popcorn machine**:
- You put in raw corn (heavy oil) and it comes out as fluffy popcorn (light hydrocarbons) with the un-popped kernels (coke) staying behind.
- In **Fluid Coking**, tiny hot sand-like coke particles are continuously circulated like a "thermal elevator" — they pick up oil in one chamber, deliver heat instantly, get regenerated (burned clean) in another chamber, and loop back. It's a 24/7 conveyor belt of heat.
- In **Contact Coking**, the same principle applies but the coke is allowed to build up in large pellets ("coke balls") on a moving grate — like cookies baking on a moving oven belt rather than free-flowing sand.

### 1.3 Key Distinguishing Features at a Glance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Distinction (High-Yield for KTU)**
> - **Fluid Coking →** uses **finely divided coke particles (60–150 µm)** fluidized by steam/air. Coke is produced as a **by-product** sold to fuel/electrode markets.
> - **Contact Coking →** uses **larger coke pellets (3–10 mm)** that grow on a moving grate. The **coke itself is the primary economic product** (e.g., electrode-grade coke).

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Operating Metrics

| Parameter | Fluid Coking | Contact Coking |
|---|---|---|
| Reactor temperature | **510–540 °C** | **510–565 °C** |
| Pressure | **Slightly above atmospheric (≈ 0.3–0.7 kg/cm²g)** | **Near atmospheric** |
| Heat carrier | Fluidized coke powder | Coke pellets on rotating grate |
| Coke residence time | **15–30 minutes** | **Hours (longer)** |
| Coke yield | **15–25 wt%** (lower) | **25–35 wt%** (higher) |
| Primary product | **Gaseous/liquid hydrocarbons** | **Electrode-grade coke** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Thermodynamic & Kinetic Foundation

Both fluid coking and contact coking operate on the **free-radical thermal cracking mechanism**. The governing kinetic expression is the standard first-order Arrhenius rate equation:

$$\begin{aligned}
k &= A \cdot e^{\frac{-E_a}{RT}}
\end{aligned}$$

where:
- $k$ = reaction rate constant (s⁻¹)
- $A$ = pre-exponential factor (s⁻¹)
- $E_a$ = activation energy (typically **40–60 kcal/mol** for C–C bond cleavage)
- $R$ = universal gas constant = **1.987 cal/(mol·K)**
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)

### 2.2 The Two-Stage Coking Process Architecture

All continuous coking processes share a common architecture: **Reaction → Solid/Gas Separation → Solid Regeneration**.

**Step 1 — Coking (Endothermic Reaction in Reactor)**
- Heavy feed is atomized/sprayed onto hot circulating coke particles.
- Heat transfer is **instantaneous** (heat transfer coefficient ≈ **200–400 W/m²·K** for fluidized bed).
- Free radical chain mechanism: **Initiation → Propagation → Termination**.

**Step 2 — Coke Laydown**
- Heavy molecules condense and dehydrogenate, depositing **carbon-rich solid** (≈ **85–92 wt% C**, **3–5 wt% H**, balance S, N, metals).

**Step 3 — Regeneration (Exothermic in Burner/Heater)**
- Partial combustion of deposited coke:
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{C} + \text{O}_2 &\rightarrow \text{CO}_2 \quad (\Delta H = -94.05 \text{ kcal/mol})
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{C} + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 &\rightarrow \text{CO} \quad (\Delta H = -26.42 \text{ kcal/mol})
\end{aligned}$$

The combustion enthalpy provides the heat required for the next cycle.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Quick Reference

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formula Cheat Sheet — Must Memorize for KTU Board Exam**

| Equation | Formula | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Conversion** | $X = \frac{(W_0 - W)}{W_0}$ | $W_0$ = feed mass, $W$ = unconverted residue | Degree of cracking achieved |
| **Coke Yield** | $Y_c = \frac{W_{\text{coke}}}{W_{\text{feed}}} \times 100\%$ | wt% | Solid product yield |
| **Liquid Yield** | $Y_L = \frac{W_{\text{liquid}}}{W_{\text{feed}}} \times 100\%$ | wt% | Naphtha + Gas Oil produced |
| **Heat Balance (Reactor)** | $Q_{\text{sens}} + Q_{\text{lat}} + Q_{\text{rxn}} = 0$ | $Q$ in kcal/hr | Energy conservation in reactor |
| **Air/Fuel Ratio (Burner)** | $AFR = \frac{W_{\text{air}}}{W_{\text{coke burned}}}$ | dimensionless | Combustion stoichiometry |
| **Arrhenius Rate** | $k = A \cdot e^{-E_a/RT}$ | $k$ in s⁻¹ | Kinetic analysis |
| **Space Velocity** | $S_v = \frac{F_{\text{feed}}}{V_{\text{reactor}}}$ | hr⁻¹ | Reactor sizing |
| **Coke-on-catalyst (inventory)** | $C_c = \frac{W_{\text{coke in bed}}}{W_{\text{bed solids}}}$ | wt% | Fluidization quality check |
| **Fluidization velocity** | $U_{mf}$ from Ergun equation | m/s | Minimum fluidization check |
| **Heat carrier circulation rate** | $G_s = \frac{Q_{\text{required}}}{C_p \cdot \Delta T}$ | kg/hr | Solid flow rate needed |

> [!WARNING]
> **Do NOT use `|` symbol inside markdown table formulas** — use `\vert` or `\mid` instead. Already implemented above.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Fluid Coking** is industrially deployed at **ExxonMobil's Baytown (USA), Suncor (Canada), Syncrude (Canada)** to process **Athabasca bitumen** into synthetic crude.
2. **Contact Coking (LR/UCC Process — currently obsolete)** was historically used to produce **premium electrode-grade coke** for aluminum smelting anodes.
3. Modern variants like **Flexicoking (ExxonMobil)** integrate fluid coking with a **coke gasifier** — eliminating net coke yield entirely, converting all carbon to **fuel gas (CO + H₂)**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Reactor Heat Balance for Fluid Coking

The reactor (coker) is an **endothermic** vessel. Heat must be supplied by the hot recirculating coke. Deriving the **required solids circulation rate**:

**Given:**
- Feed flow rate: $F$ = 100,000 kg/hr of vacuum residue
- Feed specific heat: $C_{p,f}$ = **0.6 kcal/(kg·°C)**
- Feed inlet temperature: $T_{\text{in}}$ = **300 °C**
- Reactor temperature: $T_R$ = **525 °C**
- Coke specific heat: $C_{p,c}$ = **0.30 kcal/(kg·°C)**
- Coke inlet temperature (from burner): $T_{\text{coke,in}}$ = **620 °C**
- Coke outlet temperature: $T_{\text{coke,out}}$ = **525 °C**
- Heat of cracking (endothermic): $\Delta H_{\text{rxn}}$ = **+50 kcal/kg of feed**
- Heat losses: assume **3%** of total load

**Step 1 — Calculate sensible heat required to heat feed from 300 °C to 525 °C.**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{sens,feed}} &= F \cdot C_{p,f} \cdot (T_R - T_{\text{in}}) \\
&= 100{,}000 \cdot 0.6 \cdot (525 - 300) \\
&= 100{,}000 \cdot 0.6 \cdot 225 \\
&= 13{,}500{,}000 \text{ kcal/hr}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Add endothermic heat of reaction.**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{rxn}} &= F \cdot \Delta H_{\text{rxn}} \\
&= 100{,}000 \cdot 50 \\
&= 5{,}000{,}000 \text{ kcal/hr}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Total heat demand before losses.**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{total}} &= Q_{\text{sens,feed}} + Q_{\text{rxn}} \\
&= 13{,}500{,}000 + 5{,}000{,}000 \\
&= 18{,}500{,}000 \text{ kcal/hr}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Add 3% heat losses.**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{design}} &= 1.03 \cdot 18{,}500{,}000 \\
&= 19{,}055{,}000 \text{ kcal/hr}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Calculate heat supplied per kg of circulating coke.**

$$\begin{aligned}
q_{\text{coke}} &= C_{p,c} \cdot (T_{\text{coke,in}} - T_{\text{coke,out}}) \\
&= 0.30 \cdot (620 - 525) \\
&= 0.30 \cdot 95 \\
&= 28.5 \text{ kcal/kg coke}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Calculate required coke circulation rate.**

$$\begin{aligned}
G_s &= \frac{Q_{\text{design}}}{q_{\text{coke}}} \\
&= \frac{19{,}055{,}000}{28.5} \\
&= 668{,}596 \text{ kg/hr} \\
&\approx 6.69 \times 10^5 \text{ kg/hr}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The solids-to-oil mass ratio is **$G_s/F \approx 6.7$**, meaning roughly **6.7 kg of hot coke circulates per kg of feed**. This huge ratio confirms why fluid coking needs a **large, robust burner vessel** to regenerate coke continuously.

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Fluid vs. Contact Coking

| Feature | Fluid Coking | Contact Coking |
|---|---|---|
| **Developer / Year** | Exxon Research, 1954 | Universal Oil Products (UOP), 1955 |
| **Heat carrier** | Coke powder (60–150 µm) | Coke pellets (3–10 mm) on rotating grate |
| **Reactor type** | Fluidized bed (bubbling) | Horizontal moving-grate kiln |
| **Coke removal** | Pneumatic lift, elutriation | Mechanical scraping |
| **Main product** | Liquid hydrocarbons (gas oil + naphtha) | Premium electrode coke |
| **Coke yield** | 15–25 wt% | 25–35 wt% |
| **Liquid yield** | 70–80 wt% | 55–65 wt% |
| **Gas yield** | 5–10 wt% | 5–10 wt% |
| **Cycle type** | Fully continuous | Fully continuous |
| **Heat recovery** | Excellent (regenerator + waste heat boiler) | Moderate |
| **Capital cost** | Higher | Comparable |
| **Operating complexity** | Moderate (fluidization control) | High (mechanical grate) |
| **Current status** | Widely used (ExxonMobil, Suncor) | Mostly obsolete, replaced by delayed coker for coke |

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Product Yield Analysis (Typical Vacuum Residue Feed)

For a **Middle Eastern vacuum residue** (Conradson Carbon Residue ≈ **18 wt%**):

**Step 1 — Total mass balance closure.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Feed} = \text{Gas} + \text{Naphtha} + \text{Gas Oil} + \text{Coke}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Plug in representative yields.**

$$\begin{aligned}
100 &= 8 + 18 + 49 + 25 \quad \text{(wt%, Fluid Coking)} \\
100 &= 7 + 15 + 43 + 35 \quad \text{(wt\%, Contact Coking)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Verify carbon rejection efficiency.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Carbon Rejection} &= \frac{\text{C in coke}}{\text{C in feed}} \times 100\%
\end{aligned}$$

With feed CCR = 18% and coke = 85% C, fluid coking rejects approximately **118%** (excess hydrogen in feed gives higher coke carbon than CCR).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**Q: Differentiate between Fluid Coking and Delayed Coking with respect to heat transfer mechanism and product slate.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
In **Delayed Coking**, heat transfer is **indirect** — the feed is heated in a furnace and the coking reaction is delayed until it reaches the drum where sensible heat drives the endothermic cracking. In **Fluid Coking**, heat transfer is **direct** — hot fluidized coke particles come into intimate contact with atomized feed, providing extremely rapid heat transfer (fluidized bed heat transfer coefficients 200–400 W/m²·K vs. 50–100 W/m²·K in delayed coker drums).

**Product Slate Differences:**
- Delayed Coker: 30–35% coke, 25–30% gas oil, 15% naphtha, 5–8% gas.
- Fluid Coker: 15–25% coke, 45–55% gas oil, 15–20% naphtha, 8–10% gas.

Fluid coking produces **less coke and more liquid products** due to higher severity and shorter residence time.

---

### Question A2
**Q: Why is the coke circulation rate in fluid coking typically 5–8 times the feed rate? Explain with energy balance reasoning.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
The high circulation ratio arises because:
1. **Low heat capacity of coke** (≈ 0.30 kcal/kg·°C) compared to the **large endothermic heat demand** of cracking (~50 kcal/kg feed).
2. **Limited temperature difference** (ΔT ≈ 95–120 °C) between burner outlet and reactor, constrained by coke ignition temperature and reactor coking kinetics.
3. Energy balance: $G_s = Q_{\text{design}} / (C_{p,c} \cdot \Delta T)$, with $Q_{\text{design}}$ ≈ 200–250 kcal/kg feed, gives $G_s/F \approx 5–8$.

This high ratio ensures **uniform fluidization** and prevents feed quenching of the bed.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Fluid Coking

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **Fluid Coking process** and explain the role of the burner/regenerator. Discuss operating conditions and material flows in the reactor and burner. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A vacuum residue feed (CCR = 20 wt%, Sulfur = 3.5 wt%) is processed at 5,00,000 kg/hr in a fluid coker operating at 525 °C. Calculate: **(i)** heat duty required for feed vaporization and cracking; **(ii)** required hot coke circulation rate assuming coke enters at 620 °C and leaves at 525 °C; **(iii)** air required in burner for partial combustion if 80% of coke is burned. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Fluid Coking Process Description

**Process Description:**
Fluid Coking uses a **fluidized bed of coke particles (60–150 µm)** as the heat transfer medium. It consists of two main vessels:

**1. Reactor (Coker Vessel):**
- Operates at **510–540 °C** and slightly above atmospheric pressure (**0.3–0.7 kg/cm²g**).
- Pre-heated heavy feed is injected through **atomizing nozzles** into the fluidized bed.
- Steam is introduced at the bottom to fluidize the coke bed.
- Vapor residence time: **5–15 seconds**.
- Solid residence time: **15–30 minutes**.
- Vapors leave the top, pass through cyclones (to remove entrained fines), and enter the **fractionator**.

**2. Burner/Regenerator:**
- Spent coke from the reactor is transported to the burner via a **steam-lift pipe**.
- Air is introduced at the bottom, fluidizing and partially combusting the coke at **600–650 °C**.
- 80–85% of coke is burned; remainder leaves as **product coke**.
- Hot regenerated coke flows back to the reactor through a **standpipe** (gravity-driven).

**Role of Burner:**
- Provides **heat for the endothermic cracking reaction**.
- Removes deposited coke to **maintain bed inventory and activity**.
- Supplies hot regenerated coke (~620 °C) for fluidization and heat carrier.

**Key Operating Parameters:**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Reactor T | 525 °C |
| Burner T | 620 °C |
| Solids-to-oil ratio | 6–8 |
| Steam fluidization | 0.3–0.6 m/s superficial velocity |

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Calculation

**Given:**
- Feed rate $F$ = 5,00,000 kg/hr
- $C_{p,\text{feed}}$ = 0.65 kcal/(kg·°C) (typical vacuum residue)
- Feed inlet T = 300 °C
- Reactor T = 525 °C
- $C_{p,\text{coke}}$ = 0.30 kcal/(kg·°C)
- Hot coke T = 620 °C, reactor T = 525 °C
- $\Delta H_{\text{cracking}}$ = 50 kcal/kg
- Coke yield assumed = 20 wt% = 1,00,000 kg/hr
- 80% of coke burned = 80,000 kg/hr

**(i) Heat Duty for Vaporization + Cracking:**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{sensible}} &= F \cdot C_{p,\text{feed}} \cdot (T_R - T_{\text{in}}) \\
&= 5{,}00{,}000 \cdot 0.65 \cdot (525 - 300) \\
&= 5{,}00{,}000 \cdot 0.65 \cdot 225 \\
&= 7.3125 \times 10^7 \text{ kcal/hr}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{reaction}} &= F \cdot \Delta H_{\text{cracking}} \\
&= 5{,}00{,}000 \cdot 50 \\
&= 2.5 \times 10^7 \text{ kcal/hr}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{total}} &= 7.3125 \times 10^7 + 2.5 \times 10^7 \\
&= 9.8125 \times 10^7 \text{ kcal/hr}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{Q_{\text{total}} \approx 9.81 \times 10^7 \text{ kcal/hr} \approx 98.1 \text{ GCal/hr}}$$

**(ii) Hot Coke Circulation Rate:**

$$\begin{aligned}
G_s &= \frac{Q_{\text{total}}}{C_{p,\text{coke}} \cdot (T_{\text{coke,in}} - T_{\text{coke,out}})} \\
&= \frac{9.8125 \times 10^7}{0.30 \cdot (620 - 525)} \\
&= \frac{9.8125 \times 10^7}{0.30 \cdot 95} \\
&= \frac{9.8125 \times 10^7}{28.5} \\
&= 3.44 \times 10^6 \text{ kg/hr}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{G_s \approx 3.44 \times 10^6 \text{ kg/hr}, \quad \text{ratio } G_s/F \approx 6.88}$$

**(iii) Air Required in Burner:**

Coke is approximately 90% carbon on burned basis. For 80,000 kg/hr carbon burned:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{C} + \text{O}_2 &\rightarrow \text{CO}_2 \\
\text{Molar C burned} &= \frac{80{,}000}{12} = 6666.7 \text{ kgmol/hr} \\
\text{Molar O}_2 \text{ required} &= 6666.7 \text{ kgmol/hr} \\
\text{Mass O}_2 &= 6666.7 \cdot 32 = 2.13 \times 10^5 \text{ kg/hr} \\
\text{Mass Air (23% O}_2\text{)} &= \frac{2.13 \times 10^5}{0.23} = 9.27 \times 10^5 \text{ kg/hr}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Air required} \approx 9.27 \times 10^5 \text{ kg/hr} \approx 927 \text{ tonnes/hr}}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Contact Coking

**(a)** Describe the **Contact Coking (LR/UCC) process** with a process flow diagram. Discuss the formation and growth of coke pellets on the moving grate. Compare its merits and demerits versus Fluid Coking. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **chemistry and reaction mechanism** of coking, including free radical pathways, secondary reactions, and how temperature affects product distribution. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — Contact Coking Process

**Process Description (LR/UCC Process):**
Contact Coking uses a **horizontal, rotating grate** similar to a sintering machine or Dwight-Lloyd type pelletizing machine.

**Key Equipment:**
1. **Rotating grate conveyor** — a continuous metal belt with perforations through which air can be drawn.
2. **Coking section** — feed is sprayed onto the hot bed at one end; as the grate moves, the bed depth increases due to coke deposition.
3. **Calcining section** — at the discharge end, the temperature rises to **700–800 °C** to remove residual volatile matter.

**Stages of Coke Pellet Formation:**
1. **Seed formation:** Fine coke particles (seed) are initially charged on the grate.
2. **Coating:** Atomized liquid feed coats the hot seed particles.
3. **Polymerization/dehydrogenation:** Heavy molecules crack and deposit carbon-rich layers.
4. **Growth:** Pellets grow from initial size of **1–2 mm to 8–10 mm** as the grate traverses the length.

**Operating Conditions:**
- Coking zone: **510–540 °C**
- Calcining zone: **700–800 °C**
- Total residence time on grate: **1–2 hours**

**Comparison: Contact vs. Fluid Coking**

| Aspect | Contact Coking | Fluid Coking |
|---|---|---|
| Coke form | Large pellets (3–10 mm) | Fine powder (60–150 µm) |
| Product focus | Premium electrode coke | Maximum liquid yield |
| Coke yield | High (25–35%) | Lower (15–25%) |
| Heat transfer | Moderate (less intimate) | Excellent (fluidized) |
| Mechanical complexity | High (rotating grate) | Lower (no moving bed) |
| Current status | Mostly obsolete | Active worldwide |

---

#### Part (b) — Chemistry & Reaction Mechanism

**Free Radical Chain Mechanism:**

**Initiation Step:**
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{R–R}' &\xrightarrow{\Delta} \text{R}^{\bullet} + \text{R}'^{\bullet}
\end{aligned}$$

The C–C bond undergoes homolytic cleavage at high temperature, generating two hydrocarbon free radicals.

**Propagation Steps:**
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{R}^{\bullet} + \text{RH} &\rightarrow \text{RH} + \text{R}^{\bullet} \quad \text{(H-abstraction)} \\
\text{R}^{\bullet} &\rightarrow \text{alkene} + \text{R}'^{\bullet} \quad \text{(}\beta\text{-scission)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Termination Steps:**
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{R}^{\bullet} + \text{R}'^{\bullet} &\rightarrow \text{R–R}' \quad \text{(combination)} \\
\text{R}^{\bullet} + \text{R}'^{\bullet} &\rightarrow \text{alkene} + \text{RH} \quad \text{(disproportionation)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Coke Formation (Polymerization → Condensation):**
- Heavy radicals recombine → **asphaltenes** → **pre-coke** → **petroleum coke**.
- Dehydrogenation dominates: $\text{CH}_x \rightarrow \text{C} + \tfrac{x}{2}\text{H}_2$

**Effect of Temperature on Product Distribution:**

| Temperature | Dominant Products |
|---|---|
| 450 °C | Mild cracking — gas oil dominant |
| 500 °C | Moderate — naphtha + gas oil |
| 550 °C | Severe — gas + naphtha + coke |
| 600 °C+ | Excessive coke and gas |

Higher temperatures favor **secondary cracking** of primary products, increasing gas yield and coke deposition.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing coke circulation with feed rate:** Students often write "the circulation rate equals the feed rate." WRONG — it is **6–8× the feed rate** due to low $C_p$ of coke.
> 2. **Forgetting sensible heat of feed:** Many calculate only reaction enthalpy; the **sensible heat from 300 → 525 °C is typically 60–70% of total duty** — examiner expects both terms.
> 3. **Using fluid coking coke (fine powder) when explaining contact coking:** Always specify particle size differences clearly.
> 4. **Ignoring heat losses:** Design calculations must include **2–5% heat loss margin** for realistic answers.
> 5. **Not drawing cyclones:** Reactor overhead cyclones are **mandatory** in fluid coking flow diagrams — omission costs 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Must Memorize Before KTU Exam**

- ☐ **Fluid Coking:** Continuous fluidized bed process using **60–150 µm coke particles**.
- ☐ **Contact Coking:** Continuous moving-grate process producing **3–10 mm coke pellets**.
- ☐ **Both** are **continuous** (vs. batch delayed coking).
- ☐ **Reactor temperature:** **510–540 °C** (fluid) / **510–565 °C** (contact).
- ☐ **Burner/Regenerator temperature:** **620–650 °C**.
- ☐ **Coke circulation rate is 6–8× feed rate** — critical numerical insight.
- ☐ **Heat of cracking ≈ 50 kcal/kg feed** (endothermic).
- ☐ **Heat of coke combustion (C → CO₂):** **-94.05 kcal/mol**.
- ☐ **Coke composition:** ~85–92% C, 3–5% H, balance S + metals.
- ☐ **Free radical mechanism:** Initiation → Propagation → Termination.
- ☐ **Fluid Coking advantage:** Maximum liquid yield, lower coke yield.
- ☐ **Contact Coking advantage:** Premium electrode-grade coke as primary product.
- ☐ **Flexicoking:** Modern variant — fully gasifies coke to eliminate net solid product.
- ☐ **Industrial users:** ExxonMobil, Suncor, Syncrude (Canada).
- ☐ **Cyclones** mandatory above reactor to prevent fines loss.
- ☐ **Atomization** of feed critical for intimate heat carrier contact.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Chemical Structure of Asphalt
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT638 - PETROLEUM REFINERY ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Chemical Structure of Asphalt ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Asphalt** is defined by KTU (PEPOT638) as a **dark brown to black, highly viscous, semi-solid or solid** mixture of high molecular weight hydrocarbons and their non-metallic derivatives (heteroatom-containing species), obtained as the **non-volatile residue** from the vacuum distillation of petroleum crude oil. Its chemical structure is best described as a **colloidal dispersion** of high-molecular-weight polyaromatic *asphaltene micelles* dispersed in a lower-molecular-weight *maltene* (oily) continuous phase. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Module 4:** The chemical structure of asphalt is studied in terms of (a) **Elemental composition** (C, H, S, N, O), (b) **Chemical class composition** (SARA fractionation: Saturates, Aromatics, Resins, Asphaltenes), and (c) the **Colloidal micellar model** proposed by Nellensteyn and later refined by Pfeiffer & Saal. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Dirty Ocean" Model Imagine a **muddy ocean frozen in time**: - The **water** represents the oily, light **maltene phase** (saturates + aromatics) — it is fluid and surrounds everything. - The **clumps of mud suspended in the water** represent the **asphaltene micelles** — heavy, sticky polyaromatic blobs. - The **surfactant (soap) layer** wrapping around each mud clump represents the **resins** — polar molecules that act as a bridge, keeping the mud (asphaltenes) from clumping together and sinking (precipitating). - If the soap (resins) is destroyed (e.g., by oxidation or aging), the mud clumps fall out — this is exactly what happens when asphalt hardens and cracks on a road. This "frozen muddy ocean" is the **colloidal nature of asphalt** — the foundation of all its engineering behavior. ### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics > [!NOTE] > - **Elemental Carbon (C):** 80 – 88 % by weight > - **Elemental Hydrogen (H):** 8 – 12 % by weight > - **Sulfur (S):** 0.5 – 7 % by weight (heteroatom) > - **Nitrogen (N):** 0.5 – 2 % by weight (heteroatom) > - **Oxygen (O):** 0.5 – 3 % by weight (heteroatom) > - **Trace metals (V, Ni, Fe):** 10 – 1000 ppm > - **Average Molecular Weight:** 600 – 1500 g/mol (whole asphalt); asphaltene sub-fraction can reach **10,000 g/mol** > - **Atomic H/C Ratio:** 1.4 – 1.7 (an indicator of aromaticity — lower ratio = more aromatic) > - **Density at 15.6 °C:** 1.01 – 1.04 g/cm³ ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Hierarchical Levels of Asphalt Chemical Structure The chemical structure of asphalt is best understood at **three hierarchical levels**: - **Level 1 — Elemental Composition:** Simply the wt% of C, H, S, N, O. It does NOT tell us the molecular architecture, but the **H/C atomic ratio** is a powerful indicator of aromaticity. - **Level 2 — Chemical Class (SARA) Composition:** Asphalt is separated by sequential solvent precipitation/adsorption into four operational fractions: - **Saturates (S):** Non-polar aliphatic + naphthenic hydrocarbons. Soluble in n-heptane, but NOT adsorbed on alumina/clay. Lowest polarity, lowest MW (~500 g/mol). - **Aromatics (A):** Mixed aromatic-naphthenic compounds with some heteroatoms. Soluble in n-heptane and adsorbed on alumina. Acts as the **solvent/dispersant** for asphaltenes. - **Resins (R):** Highly polar, heteroatom-rich, dark-colored solids. Adsorbed on clay/alumina but eluted with pyridine. They **peptize** the asphaltene micelles (like soap). - **Asphaltenes (As):** Insoluble in n-heptane, soluble in toluene. Highest MW, highest polarity, most aromatic. Form the **micelle cores**. - **Level 3 — Molecular Architecture:** Within asphaltenes, the accepted model is the **"Yen–Dickie" or "Modified Yen Model"** — a polyaromatic hydrocarbon (PAH) "island" core (5–10 fused rings) with pendant aliphatic side chains and trapped porphyrin/metalloporphyrin complexes (Ni, V). ### 2.2 Why the Colloidal Model Matters — The 'Why' Behind Each Fraction - **Why is asphalt colloidal, not a true solution?** Because asphaltene molecules are so large and polar that they cannot dissolve molecularly in the maltene oil — they aggregate into **micelles** (~3–5 nm in diameter) stabilized by resin layers. - **Why are resins called the "stabilizer"?** Resins have an intermediate polarity and intermediate aromaticity. Their polar heads (containing N, S, O) anchor onto the polar asphaltene surface, while their non-polar aliphatic tails dissolve into the maltene oil — a classic **surfactant structure**. - **Why is n-heptane used to define asphaltenes?** n-Heptane is a strong non-solvent (low refractive index, low solubility parameter ~7.4 cal⁰·⁵/cm¹·⁵) that precipitates the most polar, highest-MW species. n-Pentane gives a slightly different (larger) asphaltene fraction — KTU 2024 specifies **n-heptane asphaltenes**. ### 2.3 The Sol/Gel Rheological Classification (Pfeiffer & Saal) Based on SARA ratios, asphalts are classified into three structural types: - **Sol-type:** Low asphaltene, high aromatics + resins. Asphaltenes are **fully peptized**, nearly Newtonian flow. Lower viscosity. - **Gel-type:** High asphaltene, low resins. Asphaltenes form a **connected network**, non-Newtonian (pseudo-plastic), high elastic recovery. Common in air-blown (oxidized) asphalts. - **Sol–gel type:** Intermediate. The typical paving-grade asphalt. ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units / Typical Range | Engineering Use | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Atomic H/C Ratio** | $H/C = \dfrac{w_H / 1.008}{w_C / 12.011}$ | 1.4 – 1.7 (dimensionless) | Lower value → higher aromaticity | | 2 | **Colloidal Instability Index (CII)** | $CII = \dfrac{\text{Saturates} + \text{Asphaltenes}}{\text{Aromatics} + \text{Resins}}$ | 0.3 (good) – 1.2 (poor) | Predicts aging susceptibility | | 3 | **Gaestel Index (Nc)** | $N_c = \dfrac{\text{Asphaltenes} + \text{Saturates}}{\text{Aromatics} + \text{Resins}}$ | 0.5 – 1.2 | Similar to CII — same formula | | 4 | **Aromaticity Factor (fa)** | $f_a = \dfrac{C_{ar}}{C_{ar} + C_{al}}$ | 0.20 – 0.45 | Fraction of carbon in aromatic rings | | 5 | **SARA Mass Balance** | $S + A + R + As = 100$ | wt% | Closure check on SARA analysis | | 6 | **Average Molecular Weight (colligative)** | $\overline{M}_n = \dfrac{\sum N_i M_i}{\sum N_i}$ | 600 – 1500 g/mol | VPO / osmometry data reduction | | 7 | **Asphaltene Number (Ra)** | $R_a = \dfrac{w_S + w_N + w_O}{w_C}$ | 0.01 – 0.05 | Heteroatom density indicator | | 8 | **Density-API Conversion** | ${}^{\circ}\text{API} = \dfrac{141.5}{\rho_{60^{\circ}F}} - 131.5$ | 5 – 15 (asphalt range) | Crude source classification | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pitfall Note:** The **Colloidal Instability Index (CII)** and the **Gaestel Index (Nc) are mathematically identical** in the KTU textbook. Do not present them as different indices. The CII is used to predict whether an asphalt binder will remain stable (low CII) or become gel-like and crack-prone (high CII) upon aging. ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Pavement Engineering:** SARA composition directly controls PG (Performance Grade) binder classification in the **Superpave** system (AASHTO M320). - **Aging Prediction:** CII predicts oxidative hardening; H/C ratio decreases upon aging as aliphatic chains oxidize to carbonyls. - **Refinery Blending:** Vacuum residue from different crudes is blended to target SARA ratios for specific asphalt grades (e.g., 30/40, 60/70 penetration grade). - **Heavy Oil Upgrading:** Asphaltenes are the main culprits in **coke formation** during coking and **catalyst deactivation** in hydrocracking. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation — Atomic H/C Ratio and Aromaticity from Elemental Analysis A KTU 2024 Module 4 numerical problem typically gives the elemental wt% of an asphalt sample and asks the student to compute the H/C atomic ratio, the aromaticity factor, and the asphaltene number. **Given (sample problem — KTU pattern):** - $w_C = 84.2\ \%$ - $w_H = 10.5\ \%$ - $w_S = 3.8\ \%$ - $w_N = 0.9\ \%$ - $w_O = 0.6\ \%$ **Step 1 — Normalize and check mass balance.** Total heteroatoms + C + H must sum to 100 %. First, verify: $$ w_C + w_H + w_S + w_N + w_O = 84.2 + 10.5 + 3.8 + 0.9 + 0.6 = 100.0\ \% $$ The mass balance closes. (In real samples, the balance is often < 100% due to trace metals; the difference is treated as "trace metals" and ignored in H/C calculations.) **Step 2 — Convert weight % to atomic proportions (moles per 100 g).** Atomic weights: C = 12.011, H = 1.008, S = 32.06, N = 14.007, O = 15.999. $$ n_C = \frac{w_C}{M_C} = \frac{84.2}{12.011} = 7.011\ \text{mol per 100 g} $$ $$ n_H = \frac{w_H}{M_H} = \frac{10.5}{1.008} = 10.417\ \text{mol per 100 g} $$ $$ n_S = \frac{w_S}{M_S} = \frac{3.8}{32.06} = 0.1185\ \text{mol per 100 g} $$ $$ n_N = \frac{w_N}{M_N} = \frac{0.9}{14.007} = 0.0643\ \text{mol per 100 g} $$ $$ n_O = \frac{w_O}{M_O} = \frac{0.6}{15.999} = 0.0375\ \text{mol per 100 g} $$ **Text row:** We divide the weight of each element (in grams per 100 g of asphalt) by its atomic mass to obtain the relative molar amounts. This conversion is the **gateway** between mass-based analytical data and molecular stoichiometry. **Step 3 — Compute the atomic H/C ratio.** $$ \frac{H}{C} = \frac{n_H}{n_C} = \frac{10.417}{7.011} = 1.486 $$ **Text row:** The atomic H/C ratio of 1.486 lies within the standard asphalt window of 1.4 – 1.7. A value closer to 1.4 indicates a more aromatic, heavier asphalt; a value closer to 1.7 indicates a more aliphatic, paraffinic asphalt (e.g., waxy crude residue). **Step 4 — Estimate the aromaticity factor (fa) using the empirical Williams correlation.** For a petroleum-derived asphalt, an empirical correlation (used in KTU reference problems) is: $$ f_a \approx 1.13 - 0.54 \left(\frac{H}{C}\right) $$ Substituting: $$ f_a \approx 1.13 - 0.54 \times 1.486 = 1.13 - 0.8024 = 0.328 $$ **Text row:** This means about **32.8 %** of the carbon atoms in the asphalt are part of aromatic ring structures, and the remaining 67.2 % are in aliphatic (open-chain or naphthenic) environments. This is consistent with a moderately aromatic paving-grade asphalt. **Step 5 — Compute the Asphaltene Number (Ra).** $$ R_a = \frac{w_S + w_N + w_O}{w_C} = \frac{3.8 + 0.9 + 0.6}{84.2} = \frac{5.3}{84.2} = 0.0629 $$ **Text row:** The asphaltene number of 0.063 represents the mass of heteroatoms per gram of carbon — a measure of the polarity and reactivity of the asphalt. Higher Ra → higher polarity → more aging-prone. ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparing the Four SARA Fractions | Property | Saturates (S) | Aromatics (A) | Resins (R) | Asphaltenes (As) | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Polarity** | Non-polar | Low-polar | Polar | Highly polar | | **Color** | Colorless / pale yellow | Yellow – brown | Dark brown – black | Black | | **H/C Ratio** | ~1.9 – 2.0 | ~1.5 – 1.7 | ~1.3 – 1.5 | ~1.1 – 1.3 | | **Avg. MW (g/mol)** | ~500 | ~600 – 800 | ~800 – 1500 | 1500 – 10,000 | | **Solubility in n-C7H16** | Soluble | Soluble | Soluble | **Insoluble** | | **Solubility in toluene** | Soluble | Soluble | Soluble | Soluble | | **Role in asphalt** | Softener / viscosity reducer | Maltene solvent for As | Peptizing agent | Micelle core / "skeleton" | | **Wt% in paving asphalt** | 5 – 15 | 30 – 45 | 15 – 25 | 10 – 25 | ### 3.3 Worked Example — Computing the Colloidal Instability Index **Given:** A vacuum residue has the following SARA composition: S = 12 %, A = 38 %, R = 20 %, As = 30 %. **Step 1 — Verify mass balance.** $$ S + A + R + As = 12 + 38 + 20 + 30 = 100\ \% \quad \checkmark $$ **Step 2 — Apply the CII formula.** $$ \text{CII} = \frac{S + As}{A + R} = \frac{12 + 30}{38 + 20} = \frac{42}{58} = 0.724 $$ **Step 3 — Interpret the result.** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{CII} &< 0.7 \quad \Rightarrow \text{Stable, sol-type asphalt} \\ 0.7 \le \text{CII} &\le 1.0 \quad \Rightarrow \text{Sol-gel transition zone} \\ \text{CII} &> 1.0 \quad \Rightarrow \text{Gel-type, unstable, aging-prone} \end{aligned} $$ A CII of **0.724** places this asphalt in the **sol–gel transition zone** — typical of paving-grade asphalt 60/70. Upon oxidative aging, the aromatics and resins will convert to heavier polar species, pushing CII above 1.0 and causing the binder to become gel-like and crack-susceptible. ### 3.4 Step-by-Step Logic — How the Colloidal Micelle Forms - **Step 1:** Asphaltene molecules (large polyaromatic sheets) attract each other via $\pi$-$\pi$ stacking → they self-aggregate into a **nanoscale cluster (micelle core)** of ~3–5 nm. - **Step 2:** Resin molecules (intermediate polarity) **physically adsorb** onto the asphaltene surface, with their polar heads (–OH, –NH, =S) anchored to the asphaltene and their aliphatic tails extending into the oil. - **Step 3:** The maltene phase (saturates + aromatics) forms the **continuous oil matrix** in which the resin-coated asphaltene micelles are dispersed. - **Step 4:** If the system is heated, the resins partially desorb, allowing micelles to grow and viscosity to drop (the basis of **viscosity reduction for hot-mix asphalt**). - **Step 5:** On cooling, the resins re-adsorb and the structure re-establishes — a **reversible sol–gel transition** that is the physical basis of asphalt's thermoplastic behavior. ## 4. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *Define the term "asphaltene" in the context of petroleum refinery engineering. Why is n-heptane preferred over n-pentane as the precipitating solvent in the KTU/IP-143 method?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** > [!NOTE] > **Asphaltenes** are defined as the **high-molecular-weight, highly polar, polyaromatic hydrocarbon fraction of asphalt that is insoluble in n-heptane (n-C7H16) but soluble in toluene** (typically at 35 – 60 °C). They form the micelle cores of the colloidal asphalt structure and contain most of the heteroatoms (S, N, O) and trace metals (Ni, V) of the parent crude. n-Heptane is preferred over n-pentane because: 1. **Lower solubility parameter** (7.4 vs 7.05 cal⁰·⁵/cm¹·⁵ for n-pentane) and stronger precipitating power → gives a **sharper, more reproducible cut**. 2. n-Pentane co-precipitates some **resins**, leading to an **over-estimation** of the asphaltene content. 3. n-Heptane yields a fraction that **better correlates with refinery process behavior** (coking, hydrocracking) and is the **IP-143 / ASTM D2007 standard** adopted in the KTU syllabus. *(1 mark: definition; 1 mark: definition of n-heptane insolubility; 1 mark: reason for n-heptane preference.)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** *With the help of a neat sketch, describe the "Pfeiffer–Saal colloidal model" of asphalt. Name the four SARA fractions and identify which one acts as the "peptizing agent."* **Model Answer (3 marks):** > [!NOTE] > The **Pfeiffer–Saal colloidal model** (1936, refined 1940) describes asphalt as a **colloidal dispersion** of **asphaltene micelles** dispersed in a **maltene oil matrix**, with **resins** adsorbed on the asphaltene surface acting as a **peptizing (stabilizing) agent** — analogous to a soap micelle in water. The four SARA fractions are: 1. **Saturates** — non-polar aliphatic oils (continuous phase component). 2. **Aromatics** — semi-polar maltene oils (solvent for asphaltenes). 3. **Resins** — polar peptizing agents (stabilize micelles). 4. **Asphaltenes** — high-MW polyaromatic micelle cores. **The "peptizing agent" is the Resin fraction (R).** *(1 mark: model description + sketch mention; 1 mark: four SARA fractions; 1 mark: identification of resins as peptizing agent.)* --- ### Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice) #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *(Comprehensive Analytical)* **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Paper, Module 4]** > **(a)** The elemental composition of a vacuum residue (asphalt feedstock) is: C = 83.5 %, H = 10.8 %, S = 4.2 %, N = 1.0 %, O = 0.5 %. Calculate the: > (i) atomic H/C ratio, > (ii) aromaticity factor (using $f_a = 1.13 - 0.54 (H/C)$), and > (iii) asphaltene number Ra. > Comment on the **aromatic character** of the residue. **[7 marks]** > **(b)** The SARA composition of a paving-grade asphalt is: S = 10 %, A = 42 %, R = 23 %, As = 25 %. > (i) Compute the **Colloidal Instability Index (CII)**. > (ii) Classify the asphalt as **Sol, Sol–Gel, or Gel** type and justify. > (iii) Predict how the CII will change after **20 years of service** on a Kerala NH-66 pavement. Justify your answer in terms of SARA transformations. **[7 marks]** --- **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]:** **Step 1 — Compute atomic moles (per 100 g).** $$ n_C = \frac{83.5}{12.011} = 6.953\ \text{mol} $$ $$ n_H = \frac{10.8}{1.008} = 10.714\ \text{mol} $$ $$ n_S = \frac{4.2}{32.06} = 0.1310\ \text{mol} $$ $$ n_N = \frac{1.0}{14.007} = 0.0714\ \text{mol} $$ $$ n_O = \frac{0.5}{15.999} = 0.0313\ \text{mol} $$ **Step 2 — Atomic H/C ratio.** $$ \frac{H}{C} = \frac{10.714}{6.953} = \mathbf{1.541} $$ **Step 3 — Aromaticity factor.** $$ f_a = 1.13 - 0.54 \times 1.541 = 1.13 - 0.832 = \mathbf{0.298} $$ **Step 4 — Asphaltene number.** $$ R_a = \frac{4.2 + 1.0 + 0.5}{83.5} = \frac{5.7}{83.5} = \mathbf{0.0683} $$ **Step 5 — Comment on aromatic character.** > [!IMPORTANT] > The H/C ratio of **1.541** is in the middle of the asphalt range (1.4 – 1.7), and $f_a = 0.298$ indicates that about **29.8 %** of carbon atoms are in aromatic rings. This is consistent with a **moderately aromatic paving-grade asphalt** with substantial aliphatic content (good low-temperature flexibility). The Ra value of 0.0683 indicates a moderate heteroatom content, slightly above average — meaning the asphalt has a **moderate polarity** and will age at an average rate under Kerala's tropical coastal climate. --- **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]:** **Step 1 — Verify SARA balance.** $$ S + A + R + As = 10 + 42 + 23 + 25 = 100\ \% \quad \checkmark $$ **Step 2 — Compute the Colloidal Instability Index.** $$ \text{CII} = \frac{S + As}{A + R} = \frac{10 + 25}{42 + 23} = \frac{35}{65} = \mathbf{0.538} $$ **Step 3 — Classification.** A CII of **0.538** lies in the **0.4 – 0.9** band → this is a **Sol–Gel type** asphalt, typical of **60/70 penetration grade** used in Indian/Kerala highway paving. It is mildly non-Newtonian, with a balanced ratio of peptizing resins to asphaltenes. **Step 4 — Predicted change after 20 years of service.** During 20 years of service on a Kerala NH-66 pavement (high solar radiation, ~32 °C average, monsoon humidity, oxidative aging): - **Aromatics (A) → Resins (R)** oxidation (mild, slow) - **Resins (R) → Asphaltenes (As)** oxidation (the dominant aging pathway) - **Saturates (S)** remain nearly constant (non-reactive) Therefore, after 20 years: A ↓, R ↓, As ↑↑, S ≈ const. The numerator (S + As) **increases** sharply because As grows from 25 % to perhaps 35 – 40 %. The denominator (A + R) **decreases** because both aromatics and resins are consumed. $$ \text{CII}_{aged} = \frac{10 + 38}{32 + 18} = \frac{48}{50} = \mathbf{0.96} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > The CII **rises from 0.538 to ~0.96** — pushing the asphalt from Sol–Gel to the **Gel-type boundary (CII > 0.9)**. The aged binder becomes **highly non-Newtonian, brittle, and crack-prone** — explaining the typical **thermal cracking, raveling, and ruts-with-cracks** failure mode seen on aged Kerala highway surfaces. This is the **engineering rationale** for re-surfacing and re-cycling of aged asphalt pavements. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *(Alternative — Conceptual + Design-Oriented)* **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question Paper, Module 4]** > **(a)** With the help of a labelled block diagram, describe the **Yen–Dickie (Modified Yen) model** of the asphaltene molecule. Identify the role of (i) the polyaromatic core, (ii) the aliphatic side chains, and (iii) the trapped metalloporphyrins. **[7 marks]** > **(b)** A refinery produces three vacuum residues A, B, and C with the following SARA compositions (wt%): > > | Fraction | A | B | C | > |---|---|---|---| > | Saturates | 8 | 14 | 22 | > | Aromatics | 45 | 38 | 28 | > | Resins | 25 | 18 | 15 | > | Asphaltenes | 22 | 30 | 35 | > > (i) Compute the CII of each residue. > (ii) Rank them in order of **paving-grade suitability** and **aging resistance**. > (iii) Suggest a **blending strategy** to obtain a Sol–Gel type 60/70 grade asphalt (target CII ≈ 0.55). **[7 marks]** --- **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]:** > [!NOTE] > The **Modified Yen Model (Dickie & Yen, 1967)** is the molecular-level picture of an asphaltene particle, complementary to the colloidal Pfeiffer–Saal model. It depicts the asphaltene as a layered architecture: **Block Diagram (textual):** ``` +---------------------------------------------------+ | ALIPHATIC SIDE CHAINS (C10 – C30) | ← "hairy" outer layer | ├─── n-alkyl chains | | ├─── naphthenic rings | | └─── small branched alkanes | +---------------------------------------------------+ | RESIN-LIKE INTERMEDIATE LAYER | ← transitional polarity | (heteroatom-rich: S, N, O functional groups) | +---------------------------------------------------+ | POLYAROMATIC (PAH) CORE | ← "island" of 5–10 fused rings | ├─── peri-condensed aromatic sheets (C40 – C200) | | └─── METALLOPORPHYRINS (Ni, V) trapped inside | +---------------------------------------------------+ ``` **Roles:** 1. **Polyaromatic core** — Provides the high electron density and π-π stacking that drives asphaltene self-aggregation. It is the **rigid, planar "island"** that defines the asphaltene's insolubility and high MW. 2. **Aliphatic side chains** — Provide **steric stabilization** and partial solubility in the maltene oil. They also lower the bulk viscosity of the asphalt. 3. **Metalloporphyrins (Ni, V)** — Trace metals from the original crude are **chelated inside the porphyrin ring** and trapped within the PAH core. They are **catalytic centers for oxidative aging** — a key reason why high-Vanadium crudes (e.g., Venezuelan, Middle East) age faster. *(2 marks: labelled block diagram; 2 marks: PAH core role; 2 marks: aliphatic chains role; 1 mark: metalloporphyrin role.)* --- **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]:** **Step 1 — Compute CII for each residue.** **Residue A:** $$ \text{CII}_A = \frac{8 + 22}{45 + 25} = \frac{30}{70} = \mathbf{0.429} $$ **Residue B:** $$ \text{CII}_B = \frac{14 + 30}{38 + 18} = \frac{44}{56} = \mathbf{0.786} $$ **Residue C:** $$ \text{CII}_C = \frac{22 + 35}{28 + 15} = \frac{57}{43} = \mathbf{1.326} $$ **Step 2 — Rank by paving-grade suitability (lower CII = better):** $$ A \;(0.429) \;>\; B \;(0.786) \;>\; C \;(1.326) \quad \text{(paving suitability)} $$ **Step 3 — Rank by aging resistance (lower CII = better aging resistance):** Same order: A is the **most aging-resistant** (low asphaltene, high aromatics + resins), C is the **least aging-resistant** (Gel-type, prone to oxidative hardening). **Step 4 — Blending strategy to obtain Sol–Gel 60/70 (target CII ≈ 0.55).** We need a blend with: - CII between 0.4 and 0.9 (Sol–Gel) - Target CII ≈ 0.55 A linear blending of **A (CII = 0.429)** and **B (CII = 0.786)** is most appropriate. Let $x$ = mass fraction of B in the blend: $$ \text{CII}_{blend} = x \cdot 0.786 + (1 - x) \cdot 0.429 = 0.55 $$ $$ 0.786x + 0.429 - 0.429x = 0.55 $$ $$ 0.357 x = 0.121 $$ $$ x = \frac{0.121}{0.357} = \mathbf{0.339} $$ **Blending recipe:** Mix **33.9 % B + 66.1 % A** to obtain a Sol–Gel 60/70 grade asphalt with CII ≈ 0.55. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Do NOT blend residue C (CII = 1.326) into a paving-grade binder** — it would push the CII above 1.0 and produce a Gel-type, crack-prone binder. Residue C is more suitable for **air-blowing to produce oxidized roofing-grade asphalt** or for **coker feed to FCC/coker units**. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Confusing CII and Gaestel Index:** They are the same formula — do not present them as different. **(–1 mark)** > 2. **Wrong solvent in SARA definition:** Students often write "insoluble in n-pentane" — KTU Module 4 specifies **n-heptane (IP-143)**. **(–1 mark)** > 3. **Forgetting to verify SARA mass balance** = 100 % in numerical problems. **(–0.5 mark)** > 4. **Calling resins "polar" without specifying** that they are the **peptizing agent** (surfactant) — this is a frequent half-mark loss. **(–0.5 mark)** > 5. **Skipping units** in H/C calculation or stating H/C as a "ratio" without confirming it is **dimensionless**. **(–0.5 mark)** > 6. **In the Yen–Dickie model, students often forget to mention metalloporphyrins** — the trapped Ni and V are critical for the catalytic aging mechanism. **(–1 mark)** --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Asphalt = colloidal dispersion** of asphaltene micelles in maltene oil, stabilized by resin surfactant layers (Pfeiffer–Saal model). - **SARA = Saturates, Aromatics, Resins, Asphaltenes** — the four operational chemical classes obtained by IP-143 / ASTM D2007 sequential fractionation. - **Asphaltenes** = insoluble in **n-heptane**, soluble in toluene; highest MW, highest polarity, highest aromaticity, contain most heteroatoms and trace metals. - **Resins** = the **peptizing agent** (surfactant), bridging polar asphaltenes and non-polar maltenes. - **Aromatics** = the maltene solvent; disperses asphaltene micelles. - **Saturates** = non-polar oil; softener and viscosity reducer. - **Atomic H/C ratio (1.4 – 1.7)** is the master indicator of aromaticity — lower H/C = more aromatic. - **Aromaticity factor** $f_a = 1.13 - 0.54 (H/C)$ — Williams empirical correlation. - **Asphaltene number** $R_a = (w_S + w_N + w_O) / w_C$ — heteroatom density per gram of carbon. - **CII = Gaestel Index = (S + As) / (A + R)** — master stability index; 0.4–0.9 is the Sol–Gel paving-grade sweet spot. - **Yen–Dickie (Modified Yen) model** of asphaltene = PAH core + heteroatom intermediate layer + aliphatic side chains + trapped **Ni/V metalloporphyrins**. - **Sol-type** (CII < 0.4): Newtonian, low-viscosity, high aromaticity. - **Gel-type** (CII > 0.9): highly non-Newtonian, brittle, aging-prone. - **Sol–Gel-type** (0.4 ≤ CII ≤ 0.9): paving-grade 60/70 — the KTU Module 4 target. - **Aging pathway** in service: Aromatics → Resins → Asphaltenes (oxidation-driven); CII rises with age. - **Kerala-specific relevance:** Tropical climate, monsoon humidity, and high solar radiation accelerate aging → SARA-based CII is the **engineering predictor of pavement life** on NH-66 and other Kerala highways. - **Refinery application:** SARA-guided blending of vacuum residues to target paving-grade specifications (IS 73, ASTM D946, AASHTO M320 Superpave).
