topics.title: Action of Heat on Asphalt
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT638 - PETROLEUM REFINERY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Action of Heat on Asphalt

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Asphalt (Bitumen)** is a dark brown to black, thermoplastic, viscoelastic, cementitious material composed primarily of **high molecular weight hydrocarbons** (asphaltenes, resins, and oils) obtained as the **non-volatile residue** from the vacuum distillation of crude oil or by oxidation of vacuum residue.

**Action of Heat on Asphalt** refers to the **physical and chemical transformations** that asphalt undergoes when subjected to elevated temperatures during refining, blending, storage, transport, application (paving), and service. These transformations include:

1. **Physical changes** — softening, melting, flow, viscosity reduction, and phase transition.
2. **Chemical changes** — volatilization of light ends, oxidation, polymerization, and cracking (pyrolysis).
3. **Property changes** — hardening, aging, change in penetration, ductility, softening point, and durability.

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight: The action of heat is studied through standardized tests such as the **Thin Film Oven Test (TFOT)**, **Rolling Thin Film Oven Test (RTFOT)**, **Flash Point Test**, and **Fire Point Test** — all of which simulate the real-world thermal stress on asphalt.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Imagine a **stick of butter** left on a hot pan:

- At **low heat (~40 °C)** — it softens and starts to spread (softening / viscosity drop).
- At **moderate heat (~100–150 °C)** — it melts and flows freely like a thick liquid (liquefaction).
- At **higher heat (~160–200 °C)** — it begins to **smoke** (volatilization of light ends), and if air is present, it turns **darker and starts to smell burnt** (oxidation & polymerization).
- At **very high heat (>350 °C)** — it **cracks**, releasing gases and forming a hard black solid residue called **coke** (thermal cracking).

Asphalt behaves **exactly the same way**, but at much higher temperatures because it is far heavier and more complex chemically. The pan of butter is asphalt; the stove is the refinery heater or the hot road surface in tropical Kerala summers.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics & Constants

| Parameter | Value / Range | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Flash Point (asphalt) | **≥ 230 °C (open cup)** | Safety limit for handling |
| Softening Point | **35 – 95 °C** (grade dependent) | Application temperature guide |
| Mixing temperature | **150 – 175 °C** | Hot mix asphalt (HMA) |
| Compaction temperature | **120 – 150 °C** | Road laying |
| Cracking onset | **> 350 °C** | Pyrolysis begins |
| Coking temperature | **> 450 °C** | Solid carbon residue forms |
| TFOT test temperature | **163 °C** | Standard aging simulation |
| TFOT test duration | **5 hours** | Aging duration |

### 1.4 Classification of Heat-Induced Effects

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Three Principal Effects of Heat on Asphalt:
>
> 1. **Physical (Reversible)** — Softening, melting, viscosity change.
> 2. **Chemical (Irreversible)** — Oxidation, polymerization, cracking.
> 3. **Aging / Hardening (Irreversible)** — Loss of volatile fractions + oxidation = brittleness over time.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Stage-Wise Action of Heat on Asphalt

When asphalt is progressively heated, it passes through **four distinct thermal regimes**:

#### Stage 1 — Softening Zone (Ambient to 100 °C)
- Asphalt is a **rigid, brittle solid** at ambient temperature.
- Heat causes **gradual softening** with no chemical change.
- **Sulfur** present in asphalt may already begin very slow cross-linking.
- The **Rheology** changes from elastic to viscoelastic.

#### Stage 2 — Flow / Melting Zone (100 – 175 °C)
- Asphalt becomes a **viscous Newtonian liquid**.
- **Viscosity drops sharply** (often by orders of magnitude).
- Safe handling temperature range for **hot mix asphalt (HMA)**.
- Negligible chemical change; loss of moisture and trace volatiles.

#### Stage 3 — Aging / Oxidation Zone (175 – 300 °C)
- Significant **oxidation** by atmospheric oxygen begins.
- Formation of **sulfoxides, ketones, carboxylic acids, and anhydrides**.
- **Polymerization / condensation** of asphaltenes — molecular weight increases.
- **Loss on Heating (LOH)** test becomes significant.
- Asphalt **hardens**: penetration ↓, softening point ↑, ductility ↓.

#### Stage 4 — Cracking / Pyrolysis Zone (> 300 °C)
- **Thermal cracking** of large hydrocarbon molecules.
- Yields: **fuel gas, naphtha, gas oil, and petroleum coke**.
- This is the basis of **visbreaking** and **coking** (delayed coker) processes.
- Irreversible — original asphalt cannot be recovered.

### 2.2 Physical vs Chemical Changes — Tabular Summary

| Aspect | Physical Change | Chemical Change |
|---|---|---|
| Temperature | 30 – 175 °C | > 175 °C |
| Reversibility | Reversible (on cooling) | Irreversible |
| Dominant Mechanism | Phase change (molecular mobility ↑) | Oxidation, polymerization, cracking |
| Property Trend | Viscosity ↓, penetration ↑ (temporary) | Penetration ↓, softening point ↑, brittleness ↑ |
| Tested By | Penetration, viscosity, ring-and-ball | TFOT, RTFOT, flash & fire point |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following formulas form the **high-yield numerical backbone** for KTU exam problems on this topic.

| # | Formula / Expression | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{LOH} = \dfrac{m_1 - m_2}{m_1} \times 100$ | Loss on Heating (mass loss) | **%** |
| 2 | $\text{RVP} = \dfrac{m_1 - m_2}{m_1} \times 100$ | Retained Penetration after TFOT | **%** |
| 3 | $\text{PI} = \dfrac{20 - 500A}{1 + 50A}$ | Penetration Index (Pfeiffer & Van Doormaal) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\log(\log \eta) = n - m \cdot \log T$ | Refutas equation (viscosity–temperature) | °R or K |
| 5 | $T_{R\&B} = \dfrac{80.6 - 20 \cdot \log P}{1 + \log P}$ | Softening point from penetration (approx.) | °C |
| 6 | $\eta = A \cdot e^{E_a / RT}$ | Arrhenius viscosity relation | Pa·s |
| 7 | $k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$ | Arrhenius rate of oxidation | s⁻¹ |
| 8 | $\dfrac{P_{\text{after}}}{P_{\text{before}}} \times 100 = \text{Residual Penetration}$ | Ductility retention | **%** |

Where:
- $m_1$ = initial mass of asphalt sample
- $m_2$ = mass after heating
- $P$ = penetration value (in 0.1 mm)
- $\eta$ = dynamic viscosity
- $E_a$ = activation energy
- $T$ = absolute temperature
- $R$ = **8.314 J/(mol·K)** — universal gas constant
- $A, n, m$ = empirical constants

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is this topic critical for engineers?**
>
> - **Highway engineers** must know the compaction window (120–150 °C) for durable pavements.
> - **Refinery engineers** control visbreaker and coker heater outlet temperatures (typically **430–470 °C**) to convert vacuum residue into lighter products.
> - **Storage & transport safety** is dictated by flash point and fire point.
> - **Aging prediction** of asphalt in service (e.g., a 5-year-old Kerala NH pavement) uses TFOT/RTFOT data.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Loss on Heating (LOH)

**Given:** A sample of asphalt weighing $m_1$ grams is placed in a **thin film oven** at **163 °C for 5 hours**. After cooling, the mass is $m_2$ grams.

**To Find:** The percentage loss of mass (Loss on Heating, LOH).

**Derivation:**

Let the initial mass be $m_1$. During heating, the asphalt loses:
- Light hydrocarbon fractions (volatilization).
- Trapped moisture (steam evaporation).
- Small molecules from mild thermal cracking.

The mass lost is:
$$\Delta m = m_1 - m_2$$

Expressed as a percentage of original mass:
$$\text{LOH} = \dfrac{\Delta m}{m_1} \times 100 = \dfrac{m_1 - m_2}{m_1} \times 100$$

**Acceptance Criterion (IS 73 / ASTM D6):** LOH should **not exceed 1.0 %** for paving-grade asphalt.

### 3.2 Derivation: Retained Penetration After TFOT

The penetration test gives the **hardness** of asphalt in units of 0.1 mm (decimillimetre).

Let:
- $P_b$ = penetration **before** TFOT
- $P_a$ = penetration **after** TFOT

As asphalt ages, hardness increases → penetration decreases:
$$P_a < P_b$$

Retained penetration (a measure of aging resistance):
$$\text{Retained Penetration} = \dfrac{P_a}{P_b} \times 100 \;\; (\%)$$

A higher retained penetration indicates **better aging resistance**.

### 3.3 Derivation: Viscosity–Temperature Relationship (Arrhenius Form)

The viscosity of asphalt follows an Arrhenius-type temperature dependence:

$$\eta(T) = A \cdot \exp\!\left(\dfrac{E_a}{RT}\right)$$

**Step 1** — Take natural logarithm:
$$\ln \eta = \ln A + \dfrac{E_a}{R} \cdot \dfrac{1}{T}$$

**Step 2** — Rearrange into linear form $y = mx + c$:
$$\underbrace{\ln \eta}_{y} = \underbrace{\dfrac{E_a}{R}}_{m} \cdot \underbrace{\dfrac{1}{T}}_{x} + \underbrace{\ln A}_{c}$$

**Step 3** — Slope of $\ln \eta$ vs $1/T$ plot gives activation energy:
$$E_a = R \cdot (\text{slope})$$

**Engineering Meaning:** A **higher $E_a$** means viscosity is **more temperature-sensitive** (typical of harder grades). A **lower $E_a$** means better temperature susceptibility.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem (KTU Style):**
A 50.00 g sample of paving-grade asphalt is heated in a Thin Film Oven at 163 °C for 5 hours. The mass after the test is 49.62 g. The penetration values are **before** = 70 (0.1 mm) and **after** = 49 (0.1 mm). Calculate (i) Loss on Heating, (ii) Retained Penetration.

**Solution:**

**(i) Loss on Heating:**
$$\text{LOH} = \dfrac{m_1 - m_2}{m_1} \times 100$$
$$\text{LOH} = \dfrac{50.00 - 49.62}{50.00} \times 100 = \dfrac{0.38}{50.00} \times 100$$
$$\boxed{\text{LOH} = 0.76 \; \%}$$

Since 0.76 % < 1.0 % (IS 73 limit), the sample **passes** the LOH test.

**(ii) Retained Penetration:**
$$\text{Retained Penetration} = \dfrac{P_a}{P_b} \times 100 = \dfrac{49}{70} \times 100$$
$$\boxed{\text{Retained Penetration} = 70.0 \; \%}$$

A retained penetration ≥ 55 % is considered acceptable for paving asphalt.

### 3.5 Comprehensive Analytical Matrix — Tests vs Heat Effects

| Test Performed | Temperature | Time | Property Measured | Indicates |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Penetration Test | **25 °C** | 5 s | Depth (0.1 mm) | Consistency / hardness |
| Ring & Ball Softening | Rising 5 °C/min | — | Softening point (°C) | Flow tendency |
| Flash Point (Cleveland) | Rising | — | Flash (°C) | Volatility / safety |
| Fire Point (Cleveland) | Rising | — | Fire (°C) | Combustion safety |
| TFOT | **163 °C** | 5 h | LOH, residual pen | Heat & air aging |
| RTFOT | **163 °C** | 75 min | LOH, residual pen, ductility | Heat & air aging (modified) |
| Ductility Test | **27 °C** | — | Stretch length (cm) | Tensile property |
| Viscosity (Rotational) | 60 / 135 / 150 °C | — | Viscosity (Pa·s) | Flow behaviour |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram 1 — Thermal Regime Flowchart of Asphalt Heating

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Solid Asphalt at 25 deg C] --> B[Stage 1 Softening 30 to 100 deg C]
    B --> C[Stage 2 Melting Flow 100 to 175 deg C]
    C --> D[Stage 3 Aging Oxidation 175 to 300 deg C]
    D --> E[Stage 4 Cracking Pyrolysis above 300 deg C]
    B --> F[Physical change Reversible]
    C --> F
    D --> G[Chemical change Irreversible]
    E --> G
    D --> H[TFOT Test Window 163 deg C]
    E --> I[Coking Process above 450 deg C]
    E --> J[Cracking Process 350 to 450 deg C]
```

### 4.2 Diagram 2 — Functional Architecture of Heat-Action Test Apparatus

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Input
        A[Asphalt Sample m1]
        B[Heating Source 163 deg C]
    end
    subgraph Process
        C[Thin Film Oven]
        D[Aged Asphalt m2]
    end
    subgraph Output
        E[Mass Measurement]
        F[Penetration Test]
        G[Ductility Test]
        H[Softening Point]
    end
    A --> C
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
    D --> F
    D --> G
    D --> H
    E --> I[LOH percent]
    F --> J[Retained Penetration]
    G --> K[Residual Ductility]
    H --> L[Softening Point Shift]
```

### 4.3 Diagram 3 — Property Change Trend with Increasing Temperature

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T1[Temperature 25 to 175 deg C] --> P1[Viscosity DECREASES]
    T1 --> P2[Penetration INCREASES temporarily]
    T1 --> P3[Softening REVERSIBLE]
    T2[Temperature 175 to 300 deg C] --> P4[Oxidation begins]
    T2 --> P5[Penetration DECREASES permanently]
    T2 --> P6[Brittleness INCREASES]
    T2 --> P7[Softening point INCREASES]
    T3[Temperature above 300 deg C] --> P8[Cracking yields lighter products]
    T3 --> P9[Coke residue forms]
    T3 --> P10[Original asphalt destroyed]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is meant by "action of heat on asphalt"? Mention the principal physical and chemical changes that occur.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **action of heat on asphalt** refers to the combined physical and chemical transformations that occur when asphalt is subjected to elevated temperatures during refining, blending, storage, and use.

**Physical changes** (reversible, 30–175 °C):
- Gradual softening and melting.
- Viscosity drops sharply with temperature.
- Change of phase from rigid solid to viscous liquid.

**Chemical changes** (irreversible, >175 °C):
- Oxidation — formation of carbonyl, sulfoxide groups.
- Loss of volatile light fractions (volatilization).
- Polymerization / condensation of asphaltenes.
- Thermal cracking at > 350 °C producing lighter products and coke.

> **Marking Scheme:** 1 mark definition + 1 mark physical + 1 mark chemical.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Define Loss on Heating (LOH) test. State its significance and the IS acceptance limit.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition:** Loss on Heating (LOH) is the **percentage loss in mass** of an asphalt sample when heated in a **thin film oven at 163 °C for 5 hours**, expressed as:

$$\text{LOH} = \dfrac{m_1 - m_2}{m_1} \times 100 \; (\%)$$

**Significance:**
- Simulates **aging** of asphalt during hot mixing and service.
- Indicates the **volatility** and **thermal stability** of the binder.
- A high LOH indicates excessive loss of light fractions → hardening → cracking of pavement in service.

**IS 73 Acceptance Limit:** **LOH shall not exceed 1.0 %** by mass for paving-grade asphalt.

> **Marking Scheme:** 1 mark definition + 1 mark significance + 1 mark limit.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT** confuse TFOT and RTFOT test conditions (RTFOT uses 75 minutes with rotation, TFOT uses 5 h static).
> - **Always** state the **numerical acceptance criteria** (LOH ≤ 1.0 %, retained pen ≥ 55 %) — leaving them out costs 1–2 marks.
> - **Show the formula before substitution** — never write just numbers.
> - **Distinguish clearly** between *physical* (reversible) and *chemical* (irreversible) changes — examiners explicitly test this.
> - **Units** in final answer are mandatory — examiners deduct 0.5 marks for missing units.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the action of heat on asphalt with reference to the various temperature ranges and the corresponding physical and chemical changes that take place. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Thin Film Oven Test (TFOT)**. How is **Loss on Heating (LOH)** calculated? An asphalt sample weighing **52.40 g** is heated in a TFOT. After the test, the mass is **51.85 g**. The penetration **before** heating is **75** (0.1 mm) and **after** heating is **50** (0.1 mm). Calculate the LOH and retained penetration, and comment on the result. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Question A**

**(a) Action of Heat on Asphalt (7 marks):**

When asphalt is heated, it passes through **four distinct thermal stages**:

**Stage 1 — Softening (30 – 100 °C):**
- Asphalt is a hard, brittle solid at ambient temperature.
- Heat imparts molecular mobility; it gradually softens.
- **Reversible** — on cooling, it regains hardness.
- Rheology transitions from elastic to viscoelastic.

**Stage 2 — Flow / Melting (100 – 175 °C):**
- Asphalt becomes a **viscous Newtonian liquid**.
- Viscosity drops dramatically (often by 3–4 orders of magnitude).
- Hot Mix Asphalt (HMA) is mixed and laid in this range.
- Negligible chemical change; only moisture and trace volatiles are lost.

**Stage 3 — Aging / Oxidation (175 – 300 °C):**
- **Oxidation by atmospheric O₂** becomes significant.
- Formation of polar oxygen-containing groups (sulfoxides, ketones, carboxylic acids).
- **Polymerization / condensation** of asphaltenes → molecular weight rises.
- Asphalt **hardens irreversibly** — penetration ↓, softening point ↑, ductility ↓.
- This stage is simulated by the **TFOT / RTFOT** tests.

**Stage 4 — Cracking / Pyrolysis (> 300 °C):**
- **Thermal cracking** breaks large hydrocarbon molecules into smaller ones.
- Products: **fuel gas, naphtha, gas oil, coke**.
- Forms the basis of **visbreaking (430–470 °C)** and **delayed coking (480–510 °C)**.
- **Original asphalt is destroyed** — cannot be recovered.

**Summary table:**

| Stage | Temperature | Type of Change | Reversibility |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 30 – 100 °C | Softening | Reversible |
| 2 | 100 – 175 °C | Flow / Melting | Reversible |
| 3 | 175 – 300 °C | Aging / Oxidation | Irreversible |
| 4 | > 300 °C | Cracking / Coking | Irreversible |

> **Mark Split:** 1 mark intro + 2 marks stages 1–2 + 2 marks stages 3–4 + 1 mark table + 1 mark conclusion.

---

**(b) TFOT, LOH & Numerical (7 marks):**

**Thin Film Oven Test (TFOT):**

- **Standard:** IS 9382 / ASTM D1754.
- **Sample:** 50 g of asphalt spread in a shallow dish as a **thin film (~3.2 mm thick)**.
- **Conditions:** Oven at **163 °C for 5 hours** in air.
- **Purpose:** Simulates **short-term aging** of asphalt during hot mixing and laying.

**Formula for LOH:**
$$\text{LOH} = \dfrac{m_1 - m_2}{m_1} \times 100 \; (\%)$$

**Given:**
- $m_1 = 52.40 \text{ g}$
- $m_2 = 51.85 \text{ g}$
- $P_b = 75$ (before)
- $P_a = 50$ (after)

**Calculation 1 — LOH:**
$$\text{LOH} = \dfrac{52.40 - 51.85}{52.40} \times 100 = \dfrac{0.55}{52.40} \times 100$$
$$\boxed{\text{LOH} = 1.05 \; \%}$$

**Calculation 2 — Retained Penetration:**
$$\text{Retained Penetration} = \dfrac{P_a}{P_b} \times 100 = \dfrac{50}{75} \times 100$$
$$\boxed{\text{Retained Penetration} = 66.67 \; \%}$$

**Comments:**
- LOH = 1.05 % **slightly exceeds** the IS 73 limit of 1.0 % → asphalt has **higher-than-acceptable volatility**, may harden excessively in service.
- Retained penetration = 66.67 % is **acceptable** (≥ 55 %) → reasonable aging resistance.

> **Mark Split:** 2 marks TFOT explanation + 1 mark formula + 2 marks calculations + 2 marks comment.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a)** Define **flash point** and **fire point**. Explain how the **Cleveland Open Cup (COC) apparatus** is used to determine them. Discuss their significance in asphalt handling safety. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is the **Ring and Ball Softening Point test**? Describe the procedure and state the approximate softening point range for paving-grade asphalt. The penetration of an asphalt sample is **65 (0.1 mm) at 25 °C**. Estimate its softening point using the empirical relation. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Question B**

**(a) Flash & Fire Point — COC Method (7 marks):**

**Flash Point:**
The **lowest temperature** at which the **vapour of asphalt, when mixed with air, ignites momentarily** when a flame is passed across its surface.

**Fire Point:**
The **lowest temperature** at which the **vapour burns continuously for at least 5 seconds** when a flame is applied.

> Fire point > Flash point (typically 5–30 °C higher).

**Cleveland Open Cup (COC) Apparatus — Procedure:**
1. The asphalt sample is filled in an **open brass cup** up to a marked level.
2. The cup is heated at a **rate of 5 °C per minute**.
3. A small **test flame** is passed across the surface of the sample at every **2 °C rise**.
4. The temperature at which a **flash appears** is recorded as the **Flash Point**.
5. Heating continues; the temperature at which the sample **burns for ≥ 5 s** is the **Fire Point**.

**Significance in Asphalt Safety:**
- Asphalt is handled, transported and stored as hot liquid (150–200 °C).
- Flash point indicates the **fire hazard temperature** — for paving asphalt it is typically **≥ 230 °C**.
- Ensures that **storage tanks and bitumen tankers** can be heated safely without fire risk.
- Critical for insurance, transport regulations (IMO), and refinery HSE norms.

> **Mark Split:** 1 mark definitions + 3 marks COC procedure + 2 marks significance + 1 mark conclusion.

---

**(b) Ring & Ball Softening Point (7 marks):**

**Definition:**
The **Ring and Ball (R&B) softening point** is the temperature at which the asphalt sample, held in a horizontal **brass ring**, deforms enough to **allow a steel ball (3.5 g) to sink through it by 25 mm** under controlled heating.

**Procedure:**
1. Two horizontal brass rings are filled with asphalt.
2. A steel ball (3.5 g) is placed on each asphalt disc.
3. The assembly is heated in a **liquid bath** (water up to 80 °C, then glycerol) at a rate of **5 °C per minute**.
4. The temperature at which the asphalt sags and touches the **lower plate (25 mm below)** is recorded.

**Approximate Range for Paving-Grade Asphalt:**

| Grade | Softening Point (°C) |
|---|---|
| VG-10 | 40 – 50 |
| VG-20 | 45 – 55 |
| VG-30 | 47 – 60 |
| VG-40 | 50 – 65 |

**Empirical Estimate from Penetration:**

Given penetration $P = 65$ (0.1 mm) at 25 °C, an approximate relation (Hveem–Pfeiffer type):

$$T_{R\&B} \approx \dfrac{80.6 - 20 \log P}{1 + \log P} \;\; (\text{°C})$$

Calculate $\log P = \log_{10} 65 = 1.8129$:

$$T_{R\&B} = \dfrac{80.6 - 20 (1.8129)}{1 + 1.8129} = \dfrac{80.6 - 36.258}{2.8129} = \dfrac{44.342}{2.8129}$$

$$\boxed{T_{R\&B} \approx 15.76 \; \text{°C (approx. using this rough relation)}}$$

> **Note:** This empirical relation is approximate; in practice, the R&B test is performed directly. Students should remember that for VG-grade asphalt, $T_{R\&B}$ lies between **40–65 °C**.

> **Mark Split:** 2 marks definition + 2 marks procedure + 1 mark range + 2 marks calculation.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Action of Heat on Asphalt**

- **Asphalt** = heaviest non-volatile residue from vacuum distillation of crude oil.
- **Three effects** of heat: **physical (reversible)** + **chemical (irreversible)** + **aging**.
- **Four thermal stages:** Softening (30–100 °C) → Flow (100–175 °C) → Aging/Oxidation (175–300 °C) → Cracking/Coking (> 300 °C).
- **TFOT:** **163 °C, 5 hours, static thin film** — simulates hot-mix aging.
- **RTFOT:** **163 °C, 75 minutes, rotating bottles** — faster version, used widely today.
- **LOH Formula:** $\text{LOH} = \dfrac{m_1 - m_2}{m_1} \times 100$ **≤ 1.0 %** (IS 73).
- **Retained Penetration Formula:** $\dfrac{P_a}{P_b} \times 100$ **≥ 55 %** acceptable.
- **Flash Point (COC):** Asphalt **≥ 230 °C**; fire point is **5–30 °C higher**.
- **Ring & Ball Softening Point:** **40–65 °C** for paving grades.
- **Cracking** begins **> 350 °C** → basis of **visbreaking and delayed coking**.
- **Viscosity** of asphalt follows **Arrhenius-type** temperature dependence: $\eta = A \exp(E_a / RT)$.
- **Activation energy $E_a$** = $R \times$ slope of $\ln \eta$ vs $1/T$ plot.
- **Numerical must-know:** Always show **formula → substitution → final answer with units**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Asphalts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT638 - PETROLEUM REFINERY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Types of Asphalts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4: TYPES OF ASPHALTS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Asphalt? — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Asphalt** is defined as a dark brown to black, highly viscous, semi-solid or solid, thermoplastic, adhesive and waterproofing material composed principally of high molecular weight hydrocarbons (asphaltenes, resins, and oils) obtained as the non-volatile residue from the vacuum distillation of petroleum crude oil, or as a naturally occurring substance in geological formations.

In KTU 2024 scheme terminology, asphalt is classified as the heaviest, highest-boiling residue of petroleum refining, with a specific gravity generally between **0.95 and 1.10**, softening above **50 °C**, and being completely soluble in carbon disulfide (CS₂).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Black Honey" Analogy:** Imagine refining sugar syrup to make honey — the longer you cook it and the more water you evaporate, the thicker, darker, and stickier it gets. Asphalt is essentially the "cooked-down" black honey of the petroleum world. Crude oil is boiled in tall distillation columns, and asphalt is the heavy, sticky residue that refuses to evaporate even under vacuum. Different "cooking recipes" (air-blowing, blending with solvents, emulsifying with water) produce different types of asphalts — like how the same honey base can be used in candy, cough syrup, or a glaze.

The classification of asphalts is important because **one crude source cannot serve all applications**. The same heavy residue that paves a highway would be unsuitable for roofing shingles, and vice-versa. Hence, refiners manipulate chemistry, viscosity, and consistency to produce application-specific grades.

### 1.3 Why "Types" Matter in Petroleum Refinery Engineering

Asphalt is the **largest volume by-product of a refinery** in terms of residue yield. Selecting the right type determines:
- Pavement durability under traffic and climate stress
- Adhesion to mineral aggregates
- Resistance to aging, oxidation, and water stripping
- Process economics of the refinery (whether to sell as residue, blend, or further process)

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Test Methods Governing Asphalt Properties (IS / ASTM references used in KTU 2024):**
> - Penetration → **IS 1203 / ASTM D5**
> - Softening Point (Ring & Ball) → **IS 1205 / ASTM D36**
> - Ductility → **IS 1208 / ASTM D113**
> - Viscosity (Absolute / Kinematic) → **ASTM D2171 / D2170**
> - Flash Point (Cleveland Open Cup) → **IS 1448 / ASTM D92**
> - Solubility in Trichloroethylene → **ASTM D2042**
> - Specific Gravity → **IS 1202 / ASTM D70**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Master Classification of Asphalts

The complete taxonomy of asphalts recognized under the KTU 2024 syllabus is shown below as a logical hierarchy:

### A. Natural Asphalts
Formed by prolonged natural weathering of crude oil seeps, leaving behind oxidized heavy fractions.
- **Lake Asphalt** (e.g., Trinidad Lake Asphalt) — natural deposit with ~40% bitumen + mineral matter + water
- **Rock Asphalt** — limestone or sandstone naturally impregnated with bitumen (e.g., Kentucky Rock Asphalt)
- **Gilsonite** — a high-purity (≥98% bitumen), glassy, brittle natural asphalt found in veins

### B. Petroleum Asphalts (Manufactured)
Obtained during refining of crude oil.

#### B1. Residual (Straight-Run) Asphalts
- Produced as the bottom product of **vacuum distillation tower**
- Properties depend on crude source and cut-point
- Used as base stock for further processing

#### B2. Blown (Air-Rectified / Oxidized) Asphalts
- Manufactured by passing air through hot residual asphalt (200–300 °C) under controlled conditions
- Air oxidizes the maltene fraction → converts oils to resins, resins to asphaltenes
- Result: **higher softening point, lower penetration, greater age-hardening resistance**
- Used in roofing, waterproofing, pipe coatings, and industrial applications

> [!NOTE]
> **Process Chemistry of Air Blowing:**
> 2 R-H + O₂ → 2 R-OH (alcohol/ketone intermediates) → further oxidation → polymeric asphaltenes

#### B3. Cracked (Visbroken) Asphalts
- Obtained as residue from **thermal cracking** or **visbreaking** units
- Contain cracked products (olefins) which make them susceptible to oxidation
- Generally harder and more brittle than straight-run

#### B4. Sludge Asphalts
- By-product from acid treatment (H₂SO₄) of lube oil distillates
- Contain emulsified acid sludge, low-grade, used in dust laying or fuel blending

#### B5. Cut-Back Asphalts
- Blends of asphalt cement with **petroleum solvents (diluents)** to reduce viscosity for easy application
- Solvent evaporates after application, leaving behind asphalt binder

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Sub-Classes of Cut-Back Asphalts (High-Yield for KTU):**
>
> | Type | Diluent Used | Evaporation Rate | Typical Application |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | **RC — Rapid Curing** | Gasoline / Naphtha | Very fast | Surface dressing, tack coats in cold weather |
> | **MC — Medium Curing** | Kerosene | Moderate | Prime coats, patch repair, road mix |
> | **SC — Slow Curing** | Diesel / Heavy gas oil | Very slow | Dust palliatives, road mix in hot climates |

#### B6. Emulsified Asphalts
- Suspension of fine asphalt droplets (1–10 µm) in water, stabilized by an **emulsifier (surfactant)**
- Three types based on electric charge on droplets:
  - **Anionic** — negatively charged (alkaline emulsifier: fatty acids, sulfonate soaps)
  - **Cationic** — positively charged (acidic emulsifier: amine salts) — preferred for siliceous aggregates
  - **Non-ionic** — no charge (used in specialized applications)
- Break by **flocculation / coalescence** as water evaporates on the road

#### B7. Asphalt Mastics
- Mixture of asphalt (~15–20%), mineral filler (limestone dust), and graded aggregate
- Impermeable, used in bridge decks, hydraulic structures, and damp-proof courses

#### B8. Modified Asphalts
- Polymer-modified bitumen (PMB) using **SBS (Styrene-Butadiene-Styrene)**, **EVA, SBR**
- Improves elasticity, rutting resistance, and fatigue life
- High-temperature performance grade (e.g., PG 76-22) bitumens

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations, conversions, and numerical constants are repeatedly tested in KTU university examinations. Master them thoroughly.

| # | Property / Formula | Equation | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Penetration Index (PI) — Pfeiffer's | $PI = \dfrac{20 - 500A}{1 + 50A}$ , where $A = \dfrac{\log 800 - \log P_{25}}{T_{R\&B} - 25}$ | Dimensionless | $P_{25}$ = penetration at 25 °C, $T_{R\&B}$ = softening point in °C |
| 2 | Mixing Temperature (Bitumen–Aggregate) | $T_{mix} = (0.65)\,T_{R\&B} + (0.35)\,T_{pen\,max}$ | °C | Where $T_{pen\,max}$ is temp at which penetration ≥ 100 |
| 3 | Compaction Temperature | $T_{compaction} = (0.45)\,T_{R\&B} + (0.55)\,T_{pen\,max}$ | °C | Used in mix-design |
| 4 | Viscosity–Temperature Susceptibility (VTS) | $\log \log(\eta + 0.7) = A + B \cdot \log T$ | — | $B$ = VTS slope; lower $\vert B \vert$ = better temperature stability |
| 5 | Specific Gravity of Bitumen Mix | $G_{mm} = \dfrac{100}{\dfrac{P_s}{G_{se}} + \dfrac{P_b}{G_b}}$ | Dimensionless | $P_s$ = % aggregate, $P_b$ = % binder |
| 6 | Solubility Test Acceptance | Solubility in CS₂ / TCE ≥ **99.0%** by mass | wt % | IS / ASTM specification limit |
| 7 | Penetration Grade Number | Penetration at 25 °C, 100 g, 5 s (in 0.1 mm) | dmm | e.g., VG-30, 60/70 grade |
| 8 | Softening Point Range | **50 – 100 °C** for paving grades | °C | Higher for oxidized/industrial grades |
| 9 | Flash Point Minimum | ≥ **220 °C** (Cleveland Open Cup) | °C | Safety / storage criterion |
| 10 | Ductility at 25 °C | ≥ **75 cm** (for paving grade) | cm | Indicates cohesion and tensile elongation |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Highway & Airport Runway Construction:** Paving-grade cut-back or emulsified asphalt is mixed with aggregates to produce hot-mix asphalt (HMA). Penetration grade **30/40, 60/70, 80/100** are the workhorses.
- **Roofing and Damp-Proofing:** Blown (oxidized) asphalt is preferred because of its high softening point and non-tracking behavior under solar load.
- **Pipeline Coatings:** Asphalt mastic or enamel is applied on buried pipelines to prevent corrosion, often reinforced with fiberglass tissue.
- **Hydraulic Works:** Asphaltic concrete linings of canals and dams exploit its impermeability.
- **Emulsion Use in Cold Mix:** Allows paving in cold/wet weather without heating — significant energy saving and reduced emissions.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Pfeiffer's Penetration Index (PI)

The Penetration Index is the single most important derived parameter in KTU exam problems on asphalt type identification. We derive it from first principles.

### Step 1 — Empirical Penetration–Temperature Relation
For a given asphalt, the logarithm of penetration varies approximately linearly with temperature. The ASTM/IP convention fixes two reference points:
- At $T = 25\,°C$, penetration $= P_{25}$ (typically the grade number × 100, e.g., 60/70 → 65)
- At $T = T_{R\&B}$ (softening point), penetration $= 800$ (Ring & Ball convention)

So the linear relationship in semi-log space gives:

$$
\log P = \log P_{25} - A\,(T - 25)
$$

where $A$ is the temperature susceptibility slope.

### Step 2 — Evaluate A Using the Softening Point
At $T = T_{R\&B}$, $P = 800$:

$$
\log 800 = \log P_{25} - A\,(T_{R\&B} - 25)
$$

Solving for $A$:

$$
A = \frac{\log P_{25} - \log 800}{25 - T_{R\&B}} = \frac{\log 800 - \log P_{25}}{T_{R\&B} - 25}
$$

Which simplifies to:

$$
A = \frac{\log(800/P_{25})}{T_{R\&B} - 25}
$$

### Step 3 — Define the Penetration Index (Pfeiffer)
Pfeiffer & Van Doormaal (1936) defined PI such that a **conventional paving bitumen has PI = 0** (West Venezuelan crude baseline). They empirically obtained:

$$
PI = \frac{20 - 500\,A}{1 + 50\,A}
$$

This is a dimensionless, signed integer (or near-integer) quantity. The reasoning: when $A$ is very small (low temperature susceptibility), $PI \approx +20$ (very good). When $A \approx 0.04$ (typical for paving grade), $PI \approx 0$. When $A \to 0.06$, $PI \to -2$ (very susceptible, e.g., blown asphalt).

### Step 4 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style Problem)

**Problem:** A paving-grade asphalt shows penetration $P_{25} = 70$ dmm and softening point $T_{R\&B} = 49\,°C$. Determine its PI and classify the temperature susceptibility.

**Step 4a — Compute A**

$$
A = \frac{\log(800/70)}{49 - 25} = \frac{\log(11.4286)}{24} = \frac{1.0578}{24} = 0.04407
$$

**Step 4b — Compute PI**

$$
PI = \frac{20 - 500(0.04407)}{1 + 50(0.04407)} = \frac{20 - 22.035}{1 + 2.2035} = \frac{-2.035}{3.2035} = -0.635
$$

**Step 4c — Classify**

Since $PI \approx -0.6$ (lies between −1 and +1), this is a **conventional paving-grade bitumen** with normal temperature susceptibility, suitable for tropical / sub-tropical road construction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Classification Reference for KTU 2024:**
> - $PI \geq +2$ → **High-grade (low susceptibility)** — modified asphalts
> - $0 \le PI < +2$ → **Paving grade (normal)**
> - $-2 \le PI < 0$ → **Blown / oxidized (high susceptibility)**
> - $PI < -2$ → **Cracked or highly aged**

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Mix Design Temperatures (Mixing & Compaction)

McLeod's equations (used in Marshall Mix design) relate mixing and compaction temperatures to binder rheology.

### Step 1 — Reference Viscosity Windows
From SHRP/Superpave specifications and historical Marshall practice:
- Mixing viscosity range: $\eta_{mix} = 170 \pm 20$ cSt (kinematic)
- Compaction viscosity range: $\eta_{comp} = 280 \pm 30$ cSt

### Step 2 — Viscosity–Temperature Equation (Walther / ASTM D2493)
The ASTM D2493 equation for binder viscosity is:

$$
\log \log(\eta + 0.7) = A_1 - B_1 \log T
$$

where $T$ is in **Rankine (°R)** for cSt inputs. Two known viscosity points (e.g., from rotational viscometer at 135 °C and 165 °C) yield $A_1$ and $B_1$. Then we solve for $T_{mix}$ and $T_{compaction}$.

### Step 3 — Approximate Closed-Form (McLeod)

$$
T_{mix} = 0.65 \cdot T_{R\&B} + 0.35 \cdot T_{pen\,max}
$$

$$
T_{compaction} = 0.45 \cdot T_{R\&B} + 0.55 \cdot T_{pen\,max}
$$

where $T_{pen\,max}$ is the temperature at which penetration reaches **100 dmm** (read from penetration vs. temperature log-linear plot). This serves as a proxy for $\eta = 170$ cSt.

**Worked Example:**
Given $T_{R\&B} = 49\,°C$ and $T_{pen\,max} = 110\,°C$:

$$
T_{mix} = 0.65(49) + 0.35(110) = 31.85 + 38.50 = 70.35\,°C
$$

$$
T_{compaction} = 0.45(49) + 0.55(110) = 22.05 + 60.50 = 82.55\,°C
$$

These are the laboratory mixing and compaction temperatures reported in KTU practical / theory answers.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Asphalt Types

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This matrix is exam-critical.** KTU Part-B questions frequently ask: *"Compare blown asphalt with cut-back asphalt"* or *"Differentiate RC, MC, and SC grades"*.

| Property | Residual (Straight-Run) | Blown (Oxidized) | Cracked (Visbroken) | Cut-Back (RC/MC/SC) | Emulsified | Natural (Gilsonite) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Source | Vacuum tower bottom | Vacuum residue + air at 200–300 °C | Thermal cracker residue | Asphalt cement + solvent | Asphalt + water + emulsifier | Mines (Uinta Basin) |
| Penetration | High (60–300) | Low (10–40) | Very low (5–30) | High (liquid) | Liquid | Very low (0–5) |
| Softening Point (°C) | 35–70 | 80–130 | 70–110 | — (liquid) | — (liquid) | >150 |
| Temperature Susceptibility | Moderate | High | Very high | Depends on cut | Depends on grade | Very low |
| Ductility | High | Low | Low | Medium | Medium | Brittle |
| Adhesion to Aggregate | Good | Moderate | Poor | Excellent (RC) | Excellent (cationic) | Brittle film |
| Aging Resistance | Moderate | Excellent | Poor | Solvent evaporates | Breaks on contact | Excellent |
| Main Application | Paving base | Roofing, pipe coating | Rarely used alone | Surface dressing, tack coat | Cold-mix paving, fog seal | Specialty paints, inks |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Classification Tree — All Asphalt Types

The following Mermaid diagram presents the entire taxonomy of asphalts as a hierarchical flow. The architecture is rendered as a block-level functional flow to ensure clean compilation across all Mermaid versions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[ASPHALT MASTER CLASSIFICATION]
    A0 --> B1[NATURAL ASPHALT]
    A0 --> B2[PETROLEUM ASPHALT]

    B1 --> C1A[Lake Asphalt e.g. Trinidad]
    B1 --> C1B[Rock Asphalt e.g. Kentucky]
    B1 --> C1C[Gilsonite high purity vein asphalt]

    B2 --> C2A[Residual Straight Run Vacuum bottom]
    B2 --> C2B[Blown Air Rectified 200 to 300 C]
    B2 --> C2C[Cracked Visbroken residue]
    B2 --> C2D[Sludge Acid treatment by product]
    B2 --> C2E[Cut Back Asphalt]
    B2 --> C2F[Emulsified Asphalt]
    B2 --> C2G[Asphalt Mastic]
    B2 --> C2H[Modified Asphalt PMB]

    C2E --> D1E1[RC Rapid Curing Gasoline cut]
    C2E --> D1E2[MC Medium Curing Kerosene cut]
    C2E --> D1E3[SC Slow Curing Diesel cut]

    C2F --> D1F1[Anionic Negatively charged]
    C2F --> D1F2[Cationic Positively charged]
    C2F --> D1F3[Non Ionic No charge]

    C2H --> D1H1[SBS Modified]
    C2H --> D1H2[EVA Modified]
    C2H --> D1H3[SBR Modified]
```

## 4.2 Functional Flow of Cut-Back Asphalt Manufacturing

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F0[Vacuum Residue from Distillation]
    F0 --> F1[Heat to 150 to 180 C]
    F1 --> F2[Select Diluent Type]
    F2 --> F3{RC MC or SC}
    F3 --> F3A[Add Naphtha 0.5 to 4 percent]
    F3 --> F3B[Add Kerosene 8 to 15 percent]
    F3 --> F3C[Add Diesel 15 to 30 percent]
    F3A --> F4[Blending Tank with Agitator]
    F3B --> F4
    F3C --> F4
    F4 --> F5[Storage at 60 to 100 C]
    F5 --> F6[Quality Tests Penetration Viscosity Flash]
    F6 --> F7[Road Application Evaporation of Solvent Leaves Binder]
```

## 4.3 Asphalt Emulsion Process Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    G0[Asphalt Cement 60 to 70 grade]
    G0 --> G1[Heated to 120 to 140 C]
    G1 --> G2[Colloid Mill High Shear Mixer]
    G2 --> G3[Soap Solution Water plus Emulsifier 60 to 80 C]
    G3 --> G2
    G2 --> G4[Emulsion 60 to 70 percent Asphalt plus 30 to 40 percent Water]
    G4 --> G5[Cooling to 30 to 60 C]
    G5 --> G6{Type Selection}
    G6 --> G6A[Anionic Emulsion for Limestone]
    G6 --> G6B[Cationic Emulsion for Granite and Silica]
    G6 --> G6C[Non Ionic for Specialty]
    G6A --> G7[Storage Tank Ambient]
    G6B --> G7
    G6C --> G7
    G7 --> G8[Application by Spray or Mix]
    G8 --> G9[Break and Set on Aggregate Evaporation plus Flocculation]
```

## 4.4 Comparison Block — Blown Asphalt vs. Straight-Run Asphalt

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    H0[Compare Two Asphalt Types]
    H0 --> H1[Straight Run Residual]
    H0 --> H2[Blown Oxidized]

    H1 --> H1A[Source Vacuum Tower Bottom]
    H1 --> H1B[Softening 35 to 70 C]
    H1 --> H1C[Penetration 60 to 300 dmm]
    H1 --> H1D[Application Paving]
    H1 --> H1E[Aging Susceptibility Moderate]

    H2 --> H2A[Source Vacuum Residue plus Air Blowing at 200 to 300 C]
    H2 --> H2B[Softening 80 to 130 C]
    H2 --> H2C[Penetration 10 to 40 dmm]
    H2 --> H2D[Application Roofing and Pipe Coating]
    H2 --> H2E[Aging Susceptibility Low Excellent]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART-A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**

**Q:** Differentiate between **RC, MC, and SC** cut-back asphalts in terms of the diluent used and rate of curing. Give one typical application of each.

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Cut-back asphalts are classified based on the volatility of the petroleum diluent blended with asphalt cement:**
>
> - **RC (Rapid Curing):** Blended with **gasoline or naphtha** (very volatile). Sets within minutes. Used in **surface dressing and tack coats** where rapid opening to traffic is needed.
> - **MC (Medium Curing):** Blended with **kerosene** (intermediate volatility). Sets in hours. Used in **prime coats and patch repair works**.
> - **SC (Slow Curing):** Blended with **diesel oil or heavy gas oil** (low volatility). Sets slowly over days. Used in **road mix in hot climates and dust palliatives**.

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**Q:** What is **Gilsonite**? Why is it considered a special natural asphalt? State any two industrial applications.

**Model Answer:**

Gilsonite is a naturally occurring, **high-purity (98–99.5% bitumen)** asphalt found in vein deposits, primarily in the Uinta Basin of Utah, USA. It is considered special because of:
- Its exceptionally **high softening point (>150 °C)**
- **Very low penetration (0–5 dmm)** — nearly brittle
- **High asphaltene content (>70%)** with negligible mineral matter
- High resistance to acids and alkalis

**Two applications:** (1) **Specialty paints and varnishes** (carbon black dispersant), (2) **Oil-well drilling mud additive** to control fluid loss.

---

## PART-B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – July 2024 Model Question]**

**Q (a) [7 Marks]:** Explain with a neat process flow diagram the **manufacture of air-blown (oxidized) asphalt** from vacuum residue. Discuss the chemistry of air-blowing and how it alters the physical properties of the base asphalt.

**Q (b) [7 Marks]:** A paving-grade asphalt shows penetration $P_{25} = 65$ dmm and softening point (R&B) $T_{R\&B} = 51\,°C$. Determine its **Penetration Index (PI)** and comment on the temperature susceptibility. Also estimate **mixing and compaction temperatures** assuming $T_{pen\,max} = 115\,°C$.

---

#### Model Solution — Q (a)

**Process Steps:**

1. **Feed:** Hot vacuum residue (VBR) at 200–220 °C enters a vertical cylindrical **blowing tower** (also called "oxidizer" or "converter"), typically 2.5–3.5 m diameter and 6–10 m height, fitted with internal sparger rings at the bottom for air distribution.
2. **Air Injection:** Compressed air is blown through the sparger at 50–200 Nm³/h per ton of charge. The temperature is held in the range 200–300 °C using either internal heating coils or external hot-oil jackets.
3. **Residence Time:** Reaction continues for 4–12 hours. Foam generated by vaporization of light fractions rises; the level is controlled by an overflow weir.
4. **Fumes Treatment:** The overhead vapors pass through a **fume scrubber** (oil- or water-based) to remove condensable hydrocarbons and reduce air pollution.
5. **Product:** Oxidized asphalt is withdrawn from the bottom, cooled in a **blending bay**, and stored.

**Chemistry of Air Blowing:**

At 200–300 °C, free-radical reactions dominate:

$$
\text{Maltene (oils)} \xrightarrow{+O_2} \text{Resins} \xrightarrow{+O_2} \text{Asphaltenes}
$$

Concurrently, dehydrogenation and condensation produce higher molecular weight polar species, increasing the **asphaltene-to-maltene ratio**. Oxygen is incorporated as **carbonyl (C=O), hydroxyl (–OH), and sulfoxide (S=O)** groups, increasing polarity and stiffness.

**Property Changes (High-Yield):**

| Property | Straight-Run → Blown |
|---|---|
| Softening Point | 50 °C → 95 °C |
| Penetration | 70 dmm → 20 dmm |
| Ductility | 100 cm → 5 cm |
| Temperature Susceptibility | Low → High |
| Adhesion to aggregates | Good → Reduced |
| Aging resistance | Moderate → Excellent |

**Diagram:** Refer to SECTION 4.4 for the comparison block, and the Mermaid functional flows above.

---

#### Model Solution — Q (b)

**Step 1 — Compute A (slope):**

$$
A = \frac{\log(800/65)}{T_{R\&B} - 25} = \frac{\log(12.3077)}{51 - 25} = \frac{1.0902}{26} = 0.04193
$$

**Step 2 — Compute PI:**

$$
PI = \frac{20 - 500(0.04193)}{1 + 50(0.04193)} = \frac{20 - 20.965}{1 + 2.0965} = \frac{-0.965}{3.0965} = -0.312
$$

**Step 3 — Comment:** $PI \approx -0.3$ is within the **normal paving-grade range (−1 to +1)**. The asphalt exhibits **moderate temperature susceptibility** and is suitable for **sub-tropical road construction**.

**Step 4 — Mixing Temperature:**

$$
T_{mix} = 0.65 \times 51 + 0.35 \times 115 = 33.15 + 40.25 = 73.40\,°C
$$

**Step 5 — Compaction Temperature:**

$$
T_{compaction} = 0.45 \times 51 + 0.55 \times 115 = 22.95 + 63.25 = 86.20\,°C
$$

**Final Answer:** $PI = -0.31$ (normal paving grade); $T_{mix} = 73.4\,°C$, $T_{compaction} = 86.2\,°C$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model Question]**

**Q (a) [7 Marks]:** Classify asphalts in detail with a neat hierarchical diagram. Explain the difference between **anionic, cationic, and non-ionic emulsified asphalts**, highlighting which type is preferred for siliceous aggregates and why.

**Q (b) [7 Marks]:** With a neat process flow diagram, describe the **manufacture of cationic bitumen emulsion**. State the role of emulsifier and the significance of pH.

---

#### Model Solution — Q (a) — Classification

The full hierarchical classification of asphalts is presented in SECTION 4.1 (Mermaid Master Classification Tree). Refer to it directly in the answer sheet.

**Emulsion Sub-Classes:**

| Type | Emulsifier Charge | Aggregate Surface | Aggregate Charge | Adhesion Mechanism |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Anionic** | Negative | Limestone, dolomite (basic) | Positive | Chemical bond via Ca²⁺ bridge |
| **Cationic** | Positive | Granite, quartzite, sandstone (acidic / siliceous) | Negative | Electrostatic attraction |
| **Non-ionic** | None | Specialized substrates | None | Mechanical / surfactant adhesion |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cationic emulsions are preferred for siliceous aggregates** because siliceous (SiO₂-rich) aggregates carry a **negative surface charge** in water. Positively charged cationic droplets (≈ +30 to +60 mV zeta potential) are electrostatically attracted to the negative aggregate, ensuring rapid "break" and tenacious adhesion — even on damp, dusty surfaces. Anionic emulsions would repel siliceous aggregates and fail to bond.

---

#### Model Solution — Q (b) — Process Flow

1. **Two Streams Enter the Colloid Mill:**
   - **Asphalt phase:** Asphalt cement (typically 60/70 grade) heated to **120–140 °C** in a storage tank and metered by a gear pump.
   - **Soap phase:** Water (60–80 °C) + cationic emulsifier (fatty amine, e.g., tallow diamine, 0.5–1.5%) + HCl to adjust pH to **2–5 (acidic)**. The acidic pH protonates the amine to R-NH₃⁺ (positively charged head).

2. **High-Shear Mixing:** Both streams are fed simultaneously into a **colloid mill** (rotor–stator gap ≈ 0.2–0.5 mm) at high RPM (3000–6000). The intense shear breaks the bulk asphalt into droplets of **1–10 µm diameter**, instantly coated with emulsifier molecules that orient with their cationic head pointing into the water phase.

3. **Cooling:** The hot emulsion exits the mill and is cooled in a heat exchanger to 30–60 °C to prevent water evaporation and viscosity drift.

4. **Storage:** Cationic emulsion is stored in mild steel tanks with gentle agitation. It should not be stored in direct sunlight or allowed to freeze (freezing breaks the emulsion).

5. **Quality Control:** Tests include — **Saybolt Furol viscosity (50 °C), residue by distillation (≥57%), particle charge test (positive), sieve test (<0.1% retained on 850 µm), storage stability (24 h, <1%).**

**Role of Emulsifier:**
- Reduces **interfacial tension** between asphalt and water (from ~30 mN/m to ~5 mN/m)
- Forms a **mechanical + electrical barrier** preventing immediate coalescence of droplets
- Provides the **electrostatic charge** that determines break behavior on aggregate

**Significance of pH:**
- Cationic emulsions require **acidic pH (2–5)** to keep the amine in protonated (R-NH₃⁺) form
- If pH rises above 7 (alkaline), amine deprotonates → charge lost → emulsion collapses
- Anionic emulsions require **alkaline pH (9–12)** for fatty-acid emulsifiers to ionize (R-COO⁻)

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in KTU 2024 Valuation:**
> 1. **Confusing Penetration Index (PI) classification range** — Students often state "PI > 0 means paving grade" but fail to quote the actual bounds (normal: −1 to +1, blown: −2 to 0). Examiner deducts 1 mark for vague wording.
> 2. **Mixing up RC/MC/SC diluents** — A common error is writing "RC = kerosene." It is **naphtha/gasoline**. Memorize the volatility sequence: **RC > MC > SC**.
> 3. **Omitting the chemistry of air-blowing** — In Q(a) on blown asphalt, merely stating "asphalt is heated with air" is incomplete. KTU examiners expect the **maltene → resin → asphaltene progression** and the **incorporation of oxygen functional groups** (carbonyl, hydroxyl).
> 4. **Neglecting pH in emulsion answers** — A full-marks answer must explicitly state the **pH range (acidic 2–5 for cationic, alkaline 9–12 for anionic)** and the reason (protonation / ionization of the surfactant).
> 5. **Not converting units correctly in PI calculation** — Penetration is in dmm (0.1 mm), but $P_{25}$ is used **dimensionlessly** in the log ratio. Do not mistakenly convert to mm and lose 1 mark on numerical accuracy.
> 6. **Forgetting to mention softening-point convention P = 800 at $T_{R\&B}$** — This is the anchor point of the PI derivation; examiners often award 2 marks purely for stating this convention.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Types of Asphalts**
>
> - **Asphalt definition:** Heavy, dark, viscous, thermoplastic residue; specific gravity 0.95–1.10; soluble in CS₂.
> - **Two main families:** **Natural** (Lake, Rock, Gilsonite) vs. **Petroleum/Manufactured**.
> - **Petroleum sub-classes:** Residual, Blown, Cracked, Sludge, Cut-back, Emulsified, Mastic, Modified.
> - **Cut-back triad (RC/MC/SC):** Naphtha, Kerosene, Diesel — decreasing volatility, increasing diluent %.
> - **Emulsion triad:** Anionic (negative), Cationic (positive, acidic pH), Non-ionic. **Cationic = siliceous aggregates.**
> - **Blown asphalt chemistry:** Maltene → Resin → Asphaltene; O₂ incorporated as –C=O, –OH, S=O groups.
> - **Penetration Index (PI) formula:** $PI = (20 - 500A)/(1 + 50A)$ where $A = \log(800/P_{25})/(T_{R\&B} - 25)$.
> - **PI classification:** +2 to +5 = modified, 0 to +2 = paving, −2 to 0 = blown, < −2 = cracked/aged.
> - **Mixing temperature:** $T_{mix} = 0.65\,T_{R\&B} + 0.35\,T_{pen\,max}$.
> - **Compaction temperature:** $T_{comp} = 0.45\,T_{R\&B} + 0.55\,T_{pen\,max}$.
> - **Key specification limits:** Penetration grade 30/40, 60/70, 80/100; Softening point 35–100 °C (paving); Flash point ≥ 220 °C; Solubility ≥ 99%; Ductility ≥ 75 cm.
> - **Pfeiffer convention anchor:** Penetration = 800 dmm at the Ring & Ball softening point (this is the universal reference).
> - **Application matrix:** Paving → cut-back/emulsion; Roofing → blown; Pipelines → mastic/enamel; Specialty → Gilsonite.
> - **Emulsion pH ranges:** Cationic (2–5, acidic), Anionic (9–12, alkaline) — never invert these in the exam.
> - **Most-tested topics (high yield):** PI calculation, cut-back classification, blown-asphalt chemistry, emulsion mechanism on siliceous aggregates.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Air Blowing of Bitumen and Upgradation of Heavy Crudes
Specialty Products: Industrial Grease - Manufacture of Calcium Grease
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT638 - PETROLEUM REFINERY ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Specialty Products: Industrial Grease - Manufacture of Calcium Grease # 🛢️ SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is an Industrial Lubricating Grease? An **Industrial Lubricating Grease** is a **semisolid to solid** dispersion of a **thickener** in a **liquid lubricant** (base oil), which may also contain additives that impart special properties. Unlike liquid oils, greases are designed to **stay in place**, provide **sealing action**, and lubricate mechanisms operating under conditions where liquid lubricants would leak, drip, or be thrown off. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition (Strict):** > Grease is a *structurally stable colloidal suspension* of a solid phase (thickener — typically a metallic soap) in a liquid phase (lubricating base oil). The thickener forms a three-dimensional fibrous matrix that immobilizes the oil via **capillary forces** and **van der Waals interactions**, preventing the oil from flowing under gravity. ### The Three Pillars of Grease Composition | Component | Function | Typical % (by wt.) | Example | |---|---|---|---| | **Base Oil** | The actual lubricant (80–95%) | 65 – 95 % | Mineral oil (Group I/II), Synthetic (PAO, Ester) | | **Thickener** | Provides the structural matrix that holds the oil | 5 – 25 % | Calcium, Lithium, Sodium, Aluminum, Polyurea soaps; Non-soaps (clay, silica) | | **Additives** | Impart performance enhancements | 0 – 10 % | Anti-oxidants, Anti-wear (ZDDP), Rust inhibitors, EP additives, Tackifiers | > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** A grease is essentially a **sponge** — the thickener is the *sponge* that holds the *oil* (which does the real lubricating work) and releases it gradually under shear stress at the lubricating point. --- ## 1.2 What is Calcium Grease? **Calcium grease** (also called "lime grease" or "cup grease") is a class of industrial lubricating grease in which the **thickener is a calcium soap** — typically **calcium stearate, calcium palmitate, or calcium 12-hydroxystearate** — formed by the in-situ saponification of animal/vegetable fats with **calcium hydroxide (slaked lime, Ca(OH)₂)**. ### Real-World Analogy: The "Soap in Oil Sponge" Analogy Imagine pouring cooking oil onto a kitchen sponge — the oil drips out. But if you take a **bar of calcium soap** (chemically similar to household soap made with hard water) and *shred it into microscopic fibers* and mix it with oil, the fibers form a tangled 3D mesh that **traps** the oil like a sponge. The resulting product is a soft, buttery substance that **does not flow** at room temperature, but **releases oil** when pressed or sheared — exactly like a grease. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Two Sub-Classes of Calcium Grease (KTU High-Yield):** > 1. **Anhydrous Calcium Grease (Regular / Lime Grease):** Uses **calcium stearate/palmitate** soap. Operating range: **−10 °C to +60 °C**. *No water required in the formulation* — but historically "cup grease" used ~0.5–1.5% water as a stabilizer. > 2. **Hydrated Calcium Grease (Modern / Smooth Texture):** Uses **calcium 12-hydroxystearate** soap with bound water of hydration. Operating range: **−20 °C to +93 °C**. The water molecule is *chemically* part of the soap crystal. --- ## 1.3 Advantages & Limitations ### ✅ Advantages - **Excellent water resistance** — best among conventional soap greases - **Good rust protection** — natural corrosion inhibition - **Simple and cheap raw materials** — fats, lime, mineral oil - **Smooth texture and good pumpability** - **Reversible (shear-sensitive)** — re-absorbs oil after stress removal ### ❌ Limitations - **Low dropping point** (≤ 100 °C for regular; ≤ 95 °C for hydrated) — **NOT suitable for high-temperature service** - **Limited mechanical stability** compared to lithium grease - **Anhydrous form** tends to harden and lose oil on standing - **Slow cooling required** → long batch cycle times # ⚙️ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Chemistry of Soap Formation (Saponification) The heart of grease manufacture is the **saponification reaction** — the formation of a metal soap by reacting a fatty acid (or its triglyceride — fat) with an alkali/metal hydroxide. ### 2.1.1 From Fatty Acid (Direct Neutralization) The general neutralization of a long-chain fatty acid with calcium hydroxide: $$ \text{2 RCOOH} + \text{Ca(OH)}_2 \longrightarrow (\text{RCOO})_2 \text{Ca} + \text{2 H}_2\text{O} $$ Where: - **RCOOH** = fatty acid (stearic, palmitic, oleic, or 12-hydroxystearic acid) - **R** = C₁₇H₃₅, C₁₅H₃₁, C₁₇H₃₃, etc. - **Ca(OH)₂** = slaked lime (the alkali) - **(RCOO)₂Ca** = calcium soap (the thickener) - **H₂O** = by-product water ### 2.1.2 From Natural Fats (Indirect Saponification) Natural fats are **triglycerides** (glyceryl esters of fatty acids). Reaction with calcium hydroxide: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{(RCOO)}_3 \text{C}_3\text{H}_5 &+ \text{3 Ca(OH)}_2 \\ &\longrightarrow \text{3 (RCOO)}_2 \text{Ca} + \text{C}_3\text{H}_5(\text{OH)}_3 \end{aligned} $$ Where **C₃H₅(OH)₃** is **glycerol** (a by-product, often retained as a "sweat inhibitor" or removed). > [!NOTE] > **Why 12-Hydroxystearic Acid?** > Modern hydrated calcium greases use **12-hydroxystearic acid** (derived from hydrogenated castor oil, *ricinoleic acid*) because the **–OH group on the 12th carbon** hydrogen-bonds with water molecules, allowing the soap to **crystallize in a stable hydrated form** that gives much higher drop point (~95 °C) and better mechanical stability. ### 2.1.3 Stoichiometry of Fat Saponification — the **Saponification Value (SV)** The **Saponification Value** is defined as the number of **milligrams of KOH** (or its equivalent) required to saponify **1 gram of fat**. For calcium hydroxide (Ca(OH)₂ = MW 74.10, KOH = MW 56.11), the equivalent mass of Ca(OH)₂ per gram of fat is: $$ \text{Ca(OH)}_2 \text{ required (g)} = \frac{\text{SV} \times 74.10}{56.11 \times 1000} = \frac{\text{SV} \times 0.00132}{1} $$ But stoichiometrically, for a triglyceride of average molecular weight **M_fat**: $$ \text{Moles of fat} = \frac{\text{Mass of fat}}{\text{M}_fat} $$ $$ \text{Moles of Ca(OH)}_2 \text{ needed} = 3 \times \text{moles of fat} $$ $$ \text{Mass of Ca(OH)}_2 = 3 \times \frac{\text{Mass of fat}}{\text{M}_fat} \times 74.10 \quad \text{(g)} $$ ### 2.1.4 Saponification Number (Koettstorfer Number) | Property | Formula / Expression | Units | |---|---|---| | **Saponification Value (SV)** | $\text{SV} = \frac{V \times N \times 56.11}{W}$ | mg KOH / g | | **Acid Value (AV)** | $\text{AV} = \frac{V \times N \times 56.11}{W}$ | mg KOH / g | | **% Free Fatty Acid (as oleic)** | $\% \text{FFA} = \frac{\text{AV} \times 0.503}{1}$ | % | > *Where V = mL of KOH used, N = normality of KOH, W = weight of sample (g), 56.11 = MW of KOH, 0.503 = equivalent factor for oleic acid.* --- ## 2.2 Physico-Chemical Properties of Greases (KTU Exam-Favorite) | Property | Definition / Method | Significance | Typical Value (Calcium Grease) | |---|---|---|---| | **Dropping Point** (IP 132 / ASTM D566) | The temperature at which a grease passes from semisolid to liquid state under standardized heating | Indicates **upper temperature limit** | **85 – 95 °C** (hydrated); 75 – 100 °C (anhydrous) | | **Cone Penetration** (ASTM D217) | Depth (in 0.1 mm units) a standard cone sinks into grease in 5 sec at 25 °C | Measures **consistency/softness** | Worked penetration 220 – 340 (NLGI grade 1–3) | | **NLGI Grade** | Empirical number from worked penetration | **Consistency classification** | 00 (softest) – 6 (hardest) | | **Oil Separation** (ASTM D1742) | % of oil that separates from grease under pressure | **Storage stability** | < 5 % (good) | | **Oxidation Stability** (ASTM D942) | Pressure drop (psi) after 100 h @ 99 °C under O₂ | **Service life** | < 5 psi in 100 h | | **Water Washout** (ASTM D1264) | % grease washed out by water at 79 °C | **Water resistance** | < 10 % (excellent for Ca) | | **Roll Stability** (ASTM D1831) | Penetration change after 2 h roll | **Mechanical stability** | Δ < 30 dmm (good) | ### NLGI Consistency Classification (Must Memorize for KTU) | NLGI Grade | Worked Penetration (0.1 mm) | Consistency | |---|---|---| | 000 | 445 – 475 | Very fluid (semi-fluid) | | 00 | 400 – 430 | Fluid | | 0 | 355 – 385 | Very soft | | 1 | 310 – 340 | Soft | | 2 | 265 – 295 | **Soft-to-medium (most common)** | | 3 | 220 – 250 | Medium | | 4 | 175 – 205 | Hard | | 5 | 130 – 160 | Very hard | | 6 | 85 – 115 | Block grease | --- ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) | # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Expression | Engineering Use | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Saponification reaction (fat) | $(\text{RCOO})_3\text{C}_3\text{H}_5 + 3\,\text{Ca(OH)}_2 \rightarrow 3(\text{RCOO})_2\text{Ca} + \text{C}_3\text{H}_5(\text{OH})_3$ | Calculate Ca(OH)₂ requirement | | 2 | Saponification reaction (fatty acid) | $2\,\text{RCOOH} + \text{Ca(OH)}_2 \rightarrow (\text{RCOO})_2\text{Ca} + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$ | Calculate Ca(OH)₂ requirement | | 3 | Mass of Ca(OH)₂ from fat | $m_{Ca(OH)_2} = 3 \times \dfrac{m_{fat}}{MW_{fat}} \times 74.10$ | Reactor design / dosing | | 4 | Mass of soap formed | $m_{soap} = \dfrac{m_{fat}}{MW_{fat}} \times MW_{soap}$ | Yield calculation | | 5 | Soap concentration in grease | $\%\,\text{Soap} = \dfrac{m_{soap}}{m_{soap} + m_{oil}} \times 100$ | Thickener ratio | | 6 | Worked penetration (after 60 strokes) | Read directly in 0.1 mm | NLGI grading | | 7 | NLGI Worked Penetration | Empirical grade table | Consistency check | | 8 | Dropping Point | Temperature at which first drop falls (°C) | Service temperature limit | | 9 | Saponification Value | $\text{SV} = \dfrac{56.11 \times V_{KOH} \times N_{KOH}}{W_{sample}}$ (mg KOH / g) | Fat characterization | | 10 | Ca(OH)₂ dose (from SV) | $m_{Ca(OH)_2} = \dfrac{\text{SV} \times 74.10 \times m_{fat}}{56.11 \times 1000}$ | Practical recipe calculation | | 11 | Yield of soap | $\eta_{soap} = \dfrac{\text{actual mass of soap}}{\text{theoretical mass of soap}} \times 100\,(\%)$ | Process efficiency | | 12 | Water content (Karl Fischer) | $\%\,H_2O = \dfrac{V_{KF} \times \rho_{KF}}{m_{sample}} \times 100$ | Quality control | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Utility:** > - **Reactor sizing** in batch kettle manufacture uses kinetics of saponification (second-order, ~30–60 min at 80–100 °C). > - **Cooling rate** (~1 °C/min) controls soap fiber length, which determines **mechanical stability** — a critical design parameter. > - **Saponification value** is the single most important feedstock specification in grease plants. --- ## 2.4 Why Calcium Grease Specifically? **Why not Na or Li?** The KTU examiner often asks this: | Soap | Drop Point (°C) | Water Resistance | Cost | Common Use | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Calcium** | 85 – 95 | **Excellent** | **Lowest** | Chassis, agricultural, marine | | Sodium | 150 – 180 | Poor (washout) | Low | Wheel bearings, hot applications | | Lithium | 180 – 220 | Good | Moderate | Multi-purpose (dominant) | | Aluminum | 100 – 110 | Excellent | Moderate | Polymers, food-grade | | Complex Calcium (sulfate/carbonate) | 260+ | Excellent | High | High-temperature + water | Calcium grease's **water resistance and cheapness** make it the grease of choice for **wet, low-temperature, low-speed applications** — chassis, bearings of agricultural equipment, marine deck machinery, water pumps, etc. # 📐 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Complete Stoichiometric Derivation — Calcium Soap from Natural Fat ### Problem Setup A grease manufacturer uses **tallow (chiefly glyceryl tristearate, MW ≈ 890 g/mol)** as the starting fat. We need to manufacture **1000 kg of calcium soap, (C₁₇H₃₅COO)₂Ca**, using Ca(OH)₂ (MW = 74.10 g/mol). **Given:** - Molecular weight of glyceryl tristearate (tristearin), C₅₇H₁₁₀O₆ ≈ **890 g/mol** - Molecular weight of calcium stearate (soap), C₃₆H₇₀O₄Ca ≈ **607 g/mol** - Molecular weight of Ca(OH)₂ = 74.10 g/mol - Molecular weight of glycerol, C₃H₅(OH)₃ = 92.09 g/mol ### Step 1 — Write the Balanced Reaction $$ (\text{C}_{17}\text{H}_{35}\text{COO})_3 \text{C}_3\text{H}_5 + 3\,\text{Ca(OH)}_2 \rightarrow 3\,(\text{C}_{17}\text{H}_{35}\text{COO})_2\text{Ca} + 2\,\text{C}_3\text{H}_5(\text{OH})_3 $$ **Logic:** 1 molecule of triglyceride (3 fatty acid chains) reacts with 3 molecules of Ca(OH)₂ to give 3 molecules of calcium stearate (since each Ca²⁺ holds 2 fatty acid chains) and 1 molecule of glycerol is released per 3 soap molecules. *Corrected:* 1 triglyceride → 3 Ca²⁺ are needed to displace the 3 fatty acid chains → 3 / 2 = 1.5 soap molecules per triglyceride (since each soap has 2 fatty acid chains). **Corrected balanced equation:** $$ \begin{aligned} 2\,(\text{C}_{17}\text{H}_{35}\text{COO})_3\text{C}_3\text{H}_5 &+ 3\,\text{Ca(OH)}_2 \\ &\longrightarrow 3\,(\text{C}_{17}\text{H}_{35}\text{COO})_2\text{Ca} + 2\,\text{C}_3\text{H}_5(\text{OH})_3 \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 2 — Identify Molar Ratios From the balanced equation: - 2 mol triglyceride : 3 mol Ca(OH)₂ : 3 mol soap : 2 mol glycerol ### Step 3 — Moles of Soap Required Target soap mass = 1000 kg = 1,000,000 g $$ n_{soap} = \frac{1{,}000{,}000 \text{ g}}{607 \text{ g/mol}} = 1647.45 \text{ mol of soap} $$ ### Step 4 — Moles of Ca(OH)₂ Required (Stoichiometric) From ratio: 3 mol Ca(OH)₂ : 3 mol soap → 1 : 1 $$ n_{Ca(OH)_2} = 1647.45 \text{ mol} $$ ### Step 5 — Mass of Ca(OH)₂ Required (Stoichiometric) $$ m_{Ca(OH)_2}^{stoich} = 1647.45 \times 74.10 = 122{,}076 \text{ g} = 122.08 \text{ kg} $$ ### Step 6 — Mass of Fat Required (Stoichiometric) From ratio: 2 mol fat : 3 mol soap $$ n_{fat} = \frac{2}{3} \times 1647.45 = 1098.30 \text{ mol} $$ $$ m_{fat}^{stoich} = 1098.30 \times 890 = 977{,}487 \text{ g} \approx 977.49 \text{ kg} $$ ### Step 7 — Mass of Glycerol By-Product $$ n_{glycerol} = \frac{2}{3} \times 1647.45 = 1098.30 \text{ mol} $$ $$ m_{glycerol} = 1098.30 \times 92.09 = 101{,}143 \text{ g} = 101.14 \text{ kg} $$ ### Step 8 — Practical Recipe with 10 % Excess Lime Industrial practice always uses a small **excess of Ca(OH)₂** (typically 5–15 %) to ensure **complete saponification** of all fat (since unreacted fat = soft, oily, unstable grease). $$ m_{Ca(OH)_2}^{actual} = 122.08 \times 1.10 = 134.29 \text{ kg} $$ ### Step 9 — Mass Balance for Total Batch A finished grease typically contains **10 % soap** (by weight) in oil. For 1000 kg soap → total grease = 10,000 kg | Stream | Mass (kg) | % of Total | |---|---|---| | Tallow (fat) | 977.49 | 9.77 % | | Ca(OH)₂ | 134.29 | 1.34 % | | Soap produced | 1000.00 | 10.00 % | | Glycerol released | 101.14 | 1.01 % | | Base oil (added) | 9000.00 | 90.00 % | | Additives (typical) | 50.00 | 0.50 % | | **TOTAL Grease** | **10,000.00** | **100.00 %** | ### Step 10 — % Theoretical Yield Verification $$ \eta = \frac{\text{actual soap}}{\text{theoretical soap}} \times 100 = \frac{1000}{1000} \times 100 = 100\,\% $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Examiner Tip:** Always show the **molar balance** explicitly — the most common mistake in such derivation problems is using **3 soap molecules per 1 triglyceride** (which is **wrong**, because each Ca²⁺ uses only 2 fatty acid chains; therefore 1 triglyceride gives **1.5** soap molecules, or 2 triglyceride gives **3** soap molecules). # 🏭 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Process Block Diagram — Batch Manufacture of Calcium Grease ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Base Oil Storage Tank] -->|Charged to Kettle| K1[Open Kettle Reactor
with Steam Coil and
Mechanical Agitator] B[Fat / Tallow Melting Tank] -->|Molten Fat| K1 C[Ca(OH)2 Slurry Tank] -->|Lime Slurry via Metering Pump| K1 D[Additives Storage] -->|Added at Finishing| K1 K1 -->|Saponification
100 to 130 °C
90 to 120 min| S1[Saponification Stage] S1 -->|Dehydration
120 to 150 °C
Vacuum optional| S2[Dehydration and
High-Temp Cook Stage] S2 -->|Cooling
1 °C per min| S3[Cooling and
Crystallization Stage] S3 -->|Milling via Colloid Mill or
Homogenizer| S4[Finishing Mill] S4 -->|De-aeration under Vacuum| S5[De-aerator] S5 -->|Filtering through
mesh strainer| S6[Filter] S6 --> P1[Pumping to Packaging] P1 --> P2[Cartridge, Drum, Pail
Packaging Lines] K1 -.->|Glycol or Steam
Heating Jacket| E1[Heat Exchanger] K1 -.->|Water Jacket
Cooling Coils| E2[Heat Exchanger] V1[Vent Condenser
Water Vapour] -.->|Recovers Water| S2 ``` --- ## 4.2 Detailed Process Flow Diagram — Modern Hydrated Calcium Grease (12-Hydroxy Soap) ```mermaid flowchart TD O1[Mineral Base Oil
Charged First] --> R1 F1[12-Hydroxystearic Acid
Molten, at 80 °C] --> R1 L1[Ca OH 2 Slurry
15 to 20 percent in Water] --> R1 R1[Stratco Contact Reactor
or Open Kettle
Saponification 100 to 110 °C] --> R2 R2[Dehydration Stage
150 to 160 °C under Vacuum
Water removed] --> R3 R3[Base Oil Quench Oil
Added for Rapid Cooling] --> R4 R4[Cooling Loop
1 to 2 °C per min to 80 °C] --> R5 R5[Additive Addition Tank
Antioxidant AW EP Tackifier] --> R6 R6[Homogenizer Colloid Mill] --> R7 R7[De-aeration Vacuum Chamber] --> R8 R8[Final Filter 100 mesh] --> R9 R9[Packaging Drum Filling] R1 -.->|Steam 200 kPa| UT[Utility System] R1 -.->|Mechanical Agitator
Paddle or Impeller Type| M1[Drive Motor] V1[Vacuum Pump] -.-> R2 V1 -.-> R7 X1[Water and Glycerol
Condensate] -.->|From R2| R2 ``` --- ## 4.3 Manufacturing Equipment Hierarchy (KTU High-Yield Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Grease Plant Equipment Hierarchy] --> B[Feed Preparation] A --> C[Reaction Kettle] A --> D[Post-Reaction Processing] A --> E[Finishing and Quality] A --> F[Utilities and Packaging] B --> B1[Fat Melters] B --> B2[Base Oil Storage] B --> B3[Lime Slaker
CaO to Ca OH 2] B --> B4[Additive Blending Tank] C --> C1[Open Kettle with
Paddle Agitator] C --> C2[Stratco Continuous
Contactor Reactor] C --> C3[Pressure Saponifier
5 to 10 bar] C --> C4[Vacuum Dehydrator] D --> D1[Sweat Cooling Pan] D --> D2[Scraper Cooler
Votator Type] D --> D3[Chiller and Crystallizer] E --> E1[Colloid Mill] E --> E2[Homogenizer] E --> E3[De-aerator Vacuum] E --> E4[Online Penetrometer] E --> E5[Filter Press] F --> F1[Steam Boiler] F --> F2[Compressed Air] F --> F3[Cooling Water] F --> F4[Packaging Lines] F --> F5[Laboratory QC] ``` --- ## 4.4 Operational Sequence — Time-Temperature-Composition Profile ```mermaid flowchart TD P0[Time 0 min: Charge 50 percent Base Oil + Molten Fat] --> P1 P1[Time 30 min: Begin Saponification
Add Ca OH 2 Slowly
T = 95 to 105 °C] --> P2 P2[Time 90 min: Hold for Complete Reaction
pH 8 to 9
Test with Phenolphthalein] --> P3 P3[Time 120 min: Heat to 130 to 150 °C
Dehydration Stage
Optionally apply Vacuum] --> P4 P4[Time 180 min: Add Remaining Cold Base Oil
Quench to 100 °C
Initiate Slow Cooling] --> P5 P5[Time 240 min: Cool to 80 °C
1 °C per min
Soap Fibers Crystallize] --> P6 P6[Time 270 min: Add Additives
Antioxidant ZDDP
EP Tackifier] --> P7 P7[Time 300 min: Mill and Homogenize] --> P8 P8[Time 320 min: De-aerate under Vacuum
20 mbar] --> P9 P9[Time 350 min: Filter and Pump to Filling] --> P10[Finished Grease Dispatch] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Visualization Tip for Exam:** When asked to "draw the manufacturing flow diagram", always include **(a) feed streams, (b) reaction stage, (c) cooling stage, (d) finishing mill, (e) packaging** — the KTU examiner marks these five sub-stages consistently. # 🎯 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer) --- ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Define grease. What are the three essential components of an industrial lubricating grease?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **lubricating grease** is a *semisolid to solid colloidal dispersion* of a thickener in a liquid lubricant (base oil), optionally containing performance additives. The three essential components are: 1. **Base Oil** (65–95 %): the liquid lubricant, typically mineral oil (Groups I/II/III) or synthetics (PAO, ester) — responsible for actual lubricity. 2. **Thickener** (5–25 %): a solid phase (most commonly a metal soap such as calcium stearate, lithium 12-hydroxystearate) that forms a 3D fibrous matrix holding the oil in place. 3. **Additives** (0–10 %): chemicals that enhance oxidation stability, anti-wear, extreme pressure, rust inhibition, water resistance, and tackiness. > **Marking Scheme:** Definition (1 m), Base oil (1 m), Thickener + Additives (1 m). --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **Differentiate between "hydrated" and "anhydrous" calcium grease.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Feature | Hydrated Calcium Grease | Anhydrous (Regular) Calcium Grease | |---|---|---| | **Soap used** | Calcium 12-hydroxystearate | Calcium stearate / palmitate | | **Water** | Essential — water is part of the crystal structure | No water required | | **Dropping point** | 85 – 95 °C | 70 – 100 °C | | **Temperature range** | −20 °C to +93 °C | −10 °C to +60 °C | | **Mechanical stability** | Superior | Inferior | | **Texture** | Smooth, buttery | Stringy, may harden on storage | > **Marking Scheme:** 1.5 m for each column, or 1 m for definition + 2 m for any three valid differences. --- ## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) --- ### **🔷 QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]** **Q.A.** (a) **Describe the chemistry of saponification for the manufacture of calcium grease. Derive the stoichiometric requirement of calcium hydroxide needed to saponify 800 kg of tallow (tristearin, MW = 890 g/mol). Use a 10 % excess of Ca(OH)₂.** (7 marks) (b) **Explain the step-by-step batch manufacturing process of hydrated calcium grease in a kettle reactor with a neat flow diagram.** (7 marks) --- #### **Model Solution:** **(a) Chemistry & Stoichiometry (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Reaction:** $$ 2\,(\text{C}_{17}\text{H}_{35}\text{COO})_3\text{C}_3\text{H}_5 + 3\,\text{Ca(OH)}_2 \rightarrow 3\,(\text{C}_{17}\text{H}_{35}\text{COO})_2\text{Ca} + 2\,\text{C}_3\text{H}_5(\text{OH})_3 $$ **Step 2 — Moles of tristearin:** $$ n_{fat} = \frac{800 \times 1000}{890} = 898.88 \text{ mol} $$ **Step 3 — Moles of Ca(OH)₂ required (stoichiometric):** From equation: 2 mol fat : 3 mol Ca(OH)₂ $$ n_{Ca(OH)_2} = \frac{3}{2} \times 898.88 = 1348.31 \text{ mol} $$ **Step 4 — Mass of Ca(OH)₂ (stoichiometric):** $$ m_{Ca(OH)_2}^{stoich} = 1348.31 \times 74.10 = 99{,}910 \text{ g} = 99.91 \text{ kg} $$ **Step 5 — With 10 % excess:** $$ m_{Ca(OH)_2}^{actual} = 99.91 \times 1.10 = 109.90 \text{ kg} $$ **Step 6 — Verification — Mass of soap produced:** $$ n_{soap} = \frac{3}{2} \times 898.88 = 1348.31 \text{ mol} $$ $$ m_{soap} = 1348.31 \times 607 = 818{,}425 \text{ g} \approx 818.43 \text{ kg} $$ **Answer: ~109.9 kg of Ca(OH)₂** is required to saponify 800 kg of tallow (with 10 % excess), producing ~818.4 kg of calcium stearate soap and ~82.79 kg of glycerol. > **Mark distribution:** Reaction (1 m), molar calculation (2 m), mass balance (2 m), excess calculation (1 m), final answer with units (1 m). --- **(b) Step-by-Step Batch Process of Hydrated Calcium Grease (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Feed Preparation:** - Molten **12-hydroxystearic acid** is prepared in the fat-melting tank at ~80 °C. - **Slaked lime slurry** (Ca(OH)₂ in water, ~15–20 % concentration) is prepared in the slaker. - ~50 % of the total **base oil** is pre-heated to 60 °C in the kettle. **Step 2 — Saponification:** - The fat is charged into the open kettle (a vertical cylindrical vessel, 5–25 m³, with steam heating coil and paddle agitator). - Lime slurry is added slowly with agitation at **100–110 °C** for 1.5–2 hours. - Saponification is **exothermic**; temperature is controlled at ~105 °C. - A **saponification test** (sample dissolves clearly in hot water/petroleum ether) confirms completion. **Step 3 — Dehydration and High-Temperature Cook:** - The soap mass is heated to **150–160 °C**, often under **vacuum (50–100 mbar)**, to remove free and combined water. - This step ensures the soap is "dry" and uniform before crystallization. **Step 4 — Cooling and Crystallization (Critical Stage):** - The remaining **cold base oil** is added as a quench to bring the temperature to **100 °C**. - Slow, controlled cooling at **1–2 °C/min** down to **80 °C** allows the soap fibers to crystallize into a uniform 3D matrix. - Cooling rate determines **fiber length** → directly affects **mechanical stability** and **oil bleeding** properties. **Step 5 — Additive Addition:** - At **80 °C**, additives (antioxidant, anti-wear, EP, tackifier, rust inhibitor) are blended in. **Step 6 — Finishing:** - The grease is passed through a **colloid mill** or **homogenizer** to break large agglomerates and ensure uniformity. - **De-aeration** under vacuum (~20 mbar) removes entrained air that would cause **oxidation and pumping problems**. **Step 7 — Filtering and Packaging:** - The grease is filtered through a 100-mesh strainer and pumped to cartridge/drum filling lines. **Process Flow Diagram (Schematic):** ``` Base Oil + Molten 12-HSA → [Kettle: Saponify 100 °C] → [Heat to 150 °C, Vacuum Dehydration] → [Quench + Slow Cool 1 °C/min] → [Additive Addition] → [Mill] → [De-aerate] → [Filter] → [Pack] ``` > **Mark distribution:** Feed prep (1 m), saponification (1.5 m), dehydration (1 m), cooling/crystallization (1.5 m), additive + finishing (1 m), diagram (1 m). --- ### **🔷 QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]** **Q.B.** (a) **List the key physico-chemical properties of an industrial grease and explain the significance of "Dropping Point" and "Worked Cone Penetration" with the NLGI grade classification.** (7 marks) (b) **Compare calcium grease with lithium and sodium greases.
