Justify the use of calcium grease in marine and agricultural applications.** (7 marks) --- #### **Model Solution:** **(a) Physico-Chemical Properties (7 marks):** **1. Dropping Point (ASTM D566 / D2265):** The **dropping point** is the temperature at which a grease loses its structural integrity and transitions from semisolid to a completely liquid state — detected as the **first drop of grease falling** from a standard cup suspended in a heated oil bath. It defines the **upper temperature service limit** of the grease. For **hydrated calcium grease**, it is **85–95 °C**; for **lithium**, 180–220 °C; for **sodium**, 150–180 °C. *Significance:* A grease must always be used **at least 20 °C below** its dropping point to maintain the soap matrix and avoid oil leakage. **2. Worked Cone Penetration (ASTM D217):** A standard **cone** of specified mass is allowed to sink freely into a grease sample at **25 °C for 5 seconds**. The depth of penetration, measured in **0.1 mm units** (called a *decimillimetre, dmm*), is the worked penetration. The grease is first **worked** in a standard worker (60 double strokes) to determine the *worked penetration*, which represents the **in-service consistency**. **3. NLGI Grade Classification:** | NLGI Grade | Worked Penetration (dmm) | Application | |---|---|---| | 000 | 445 – 475 | Centralized lubrication systems | | 00 | 400 – 430 | Gearbox grease, semi-fluid | | 0 | 355 – 385 | Open gears | | 1 | 310 – 340 | Low-temperature bearings | | **2** | **265 – 295** | **Multi-purpose (most common)** | | 3 | 220 – 250 | Wheel bearings, motors | | 4 – 6 | 175 – 115 | Block greases, very high load | **4. Other Important Properties (Brief):** - **Oil Separation (ASTM D1742):** indicates storage stability - **Oxidation Stability (ASTM D942):** pressure drop under O₂ at 99 °C - **Water Washout (ASTM D1264):** loss of grease under water splash - **Roll Stability (ASTM D1831):** penetration change after 2 h rolling — measures mechanical stability > **Mark distribution:** Dropping point — definition + significance (1.5 m); Penetration — definition + procedure (1.5 m); NLGI table (2 m); Other properties (1 m); Conclusion (1 m). --- **(b) Comparison of Calcium, Lithium & Sodium Greases (7 marks):** | Property | Calcium Grease | Lithium Grease | Sodium Grease | |---|---|---|---| | **Soap** | Ca-stearate / 12-OH stearate | Li-12-hydroxystearate | Na-stearate | | **Dropping point** | 85 – 95 °C | 180 – 220 °C | 150 – 180 °C | | **Max service temp** | ~ 90 °C | ~ 150 °C | ~ 120 °C | | **Water resistance** | **Excellent** (best) | Good | Poor (washes out) | | **Mechanical stability** | Moderate | **Excellent** | Moderate | | **Cost** | **Lowest** | Moderate | Low | | **Texture** | Smooth (hydrated) | Smooth, glossy | Stringy, fibrous | | **Polar activation** | Yes (high) | No | No | | **Additive response** | Limited | Good | Limited | **Justification for Marine and Agricultural Use:** 1. **Marine Applications (deck machinery, winches, stern tubes, rudder bearings):** - **Constant exposure to salt water and humidity** → calcium grease's **superior water resistance** is unmatched. - Sodium grease would wash out in a few hours; lithium would survive but is more expensive. - The **rust-inhibiting nature** of calcium soap protects ferrous components. - Operating temperatures in marine environment rarely exceed 80 °C → calcium grease's 90 °C ceiling is sufficient. 2. **Agricultural Applications (tractor chassis, plough bearings, harvesters, irrigation pumps):** - Equipment is exposed to **mud, water, dust, and crop residue** → need for **water resistance and sealing** — calcium's strengths. - **Low to moderate speeds and loads** are typical → don't need the high drop point of lithium. - **Frequent re-greasing** is accepted in agriculture → longer life of lithium is not utilized. - **Lowest cost** makes it economical for **high-volume, frequent re-lubrication** intervals. > **Mark distribution:** Comparison table (3 m); Marine justification (2 m); Agricultural justification (2 m). --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **⚠️ Where Students Lose Marks — Common Pitfalls** > 1. **Wrong stoichiometric ratio (1 mark lost):** The biggest trap is writing *"1 triglyceride → 3 soap molecules."* It is actually **1 triglyceride → 1.5 soap molecules** (since Ca²⁺ needs 2 fatty acid chains). Always write the balanced equation as **2 fat : 3 Ca(OH)₂ : 3 soap : 2 glycerol**. > 2. **Confusing dropping point with flash point (1 mark lost):** Dropping point = soap matrix melting, **NOT** oil vaporization. Flash point relates to oil, not grease. > 3. **Skipping the slow cooling step (1–2 marks lost):** Many students describe saponification but forget that **controlled cooling is the most critical step** for grease texture. > 4. **Wrong NLGI grade vs penetration (1 mark lost):** Worked penetration 265–295 dmm = **NLGI Grade 2**, *not* Grade 1 or 3. Memorize the boundaries. > 5. **Ignoring the role of additives (0.5–1 mark lost):** Always mention at least *one* additive (antioxidant, anti-wear, EP, tackifier) and its function. > 6. **Confusing "cup grease" terminology:** Historically refers to **anhydrous Ca grease with 1–2 % water as stabilizer** — not a separate product. > 7. **Forgetting to show units and convert kg ↔ g** — this is an automatic 0.5 m deduction in KTU valuation. --- ## 5.4 📝 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > ### ⚡ High-Yield Revision — Calcium Grease Manufacture - ✅ **Grease = Base Oil (65–95 %) + Thickener (5–25 %) + Additives (0–10 %)**. - ✅ **Calcium grease thickener = calcium soap** of long-chain fatty acids (stearate, palmitate, 12-hydroxystearate). - ✅ **Saponification reaction:** Fat + Ca(OH)₂ → Ca-soap + Glycerol (and for fatty acid → Ca-soap + Water). - ✅ **Stoichiometric ratio (memorize):** 2 mol tristearin : 3 mol Ca(OH)₂ : 3 mol Ca-stearate : 2 mol glycerol. - ✅ **12-hydroxystearic acid** is the modern preferred fatty acid — gives higher drop point via hydrogen-bonded water of hydration. - ✅ **Two types:** *Anhydrous* (no water, lower T) vs *Hydrated* (12-OH stearate, drop point ~ 95 °C). - ✅ **Manufacturing steps in order:** Feed prep → Saponification (100 °C) → Dehydration (150 °C) → Quench & slow cool (1 °C/min) → Additive addition → Milling → De-aeration → Filtering → Packaging. - ✅ **Equipment:** Open kettle (batch), Stratco contactor (continuous), colloid mill, scraper cooler (Votator), vacuum de-aerator. - ✅ **Dropping point = upper T limit**; use grease at *T < drop point − 20 °C*. - ✅ **Worked penetration (ASTM D217) → NLGI grade**; Grade 2 (265–295 dmm) is the *most common multi-purpose grade*. - ✅ **Calcium grease strengths:** cheapest, best water resistance, good rust protection. - ✅ **Calcium grease weakness:** low drop point (≤ 95 °C) — *NOT* for high-temperature use. - ✅ **Use calcium grease in:** marine, agricultural, chassis, water pumps, low-speed wet bearings. - ✅ **Don't use calcium grease in:** wheel bearings at high T, electric motor bearings, high-speed machinery → choose **lithium** or **complex soap**. - ✅ **Practical recipe rule of thumb:** 10 % excess Ca(OH)₂ ensures complete saponification. - ✅ **Glycerol is a by-product** — sometimes retained as a "sweat inhibitor" (prevents oil separation). > **🎯 Examiner's Quick-Fire Question Pattern for Module 4:** > *"Explain the manufacture of [X] grease with a flow diagram."* → Always draw the **same 5-stage flow** (Feed → React → Dehydrate → Cool → Finish) but customize for the soap type.

topics.title: Liquid Paraffin and Petroleum Jellies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT638 - PETROLEUM REFINERY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Liquid Paraffin and Petroleum Jellies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Liquid Paraffin (White Mineral Oil)

**Academic Definition:**
Liquid paraffin (also known as **white mineral oil**, **white oil**, or **paraffinum liquidum**) is a highly refined, colorless, odorless, and tasteless petroleum-derived hydrocarbon mixture composed predominantly of saturated aliphatic (paraffinic) and alicyclic (naphthenic) hydrocarbons, typically in the carbon range **C15 to C40**, possessing viscosities between approximately **3.5 cSt to 75 cSt at 40 °C** and being virtually free of aromatics, sulfur, and nitrogen compounds.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Liquid paraffins are classified by pharmacopoeial standards into two main grades:
> - **Light Liquid Paraffin (LLP):** Low viscosity (~3–5 cSt at 40 °C), used in pharmaceuticals and food-grade applications.
> - **Heavy Liquid Paraffin (HLP):** Higher viscosity (~10–70 cSt at 40 °C), used in cosmetics, lubrication, and industrial applications.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine the crude oil barrel as a layered cake. The "cake" sits on top as bitumen and waxes. Liquid paraffin is like the **purified middle layer** of that cake — the part that has been washed, filtered, and bleached so thoroughly that it becomes as clear as spring water and harmless enough to be used in baby lotions, food machinery lubricants, and even laxatives. It is, in essence, "refined crude oil at its cleanest."

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Test Metrics for Liquid Paraffin:**
> - **Saybolt Color:** +30 (min) — "water white"
> - **UV Absorbance (260–350 nm):** Pharmacopoeia-controlled
> - **Carbonizable Substances Test:** USP/IP/BP standard
> - **Kinematic Viscosity:** 3.5–75 cSt at 40 °C
> - **Flash Point (COC):** ≥ 150 °C (for industrial grades)
> - **Pour Point:** – 6 °C to – 20 °C depending on grade

### 1.2 Petroleum Jelly (Petrolatum)

**Academic Definition:**
Petroleum jelly, also called **petrolatum** or **Vaseline™** (a trademarked brand name), is a semi-solid, translucent, gel-like hydrocarbon mixture obtained as a by-product during the dewaxing of lubricating oil fractions, composed of a microcrystalline wax matrix (≈10–20 % w/w) dispersed in heavy liquid hydrocarbons (≈80–90 % w/w), with a melting point typically in the range of **38 °C to 60 °C**, and exhibiting thixotropic, occlusive, and water-repellent properties.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of petroleum jelly as a **"sponge of oil trapped inside a wax cage."** The microcrystalline wax forms a delicate, three-dimensional lattice that physically traps the heavy liquid hydrocarbons. When you rub it on your skin, the warmth of the body slightly melts the wax lattice, releasing the oil — which is why it feels slippery, occlusive, and moisturizing. The wax network is what makes it hold its shape in the jar at room temperature, yet flow under mechanical stress.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Test Metrics for Petroleum Jelly:**
> - **Melting Point (Drop Point / Ring & Ball):** 38–60 °C
> - **Cone Penetration (25 °C, 100 g, 5 s):** 100–300 dmm (decimillimeters)
> - **Color (Lovibond):** ≤ 1.5 Y (yellow tint)
> - **UV Absorbance (USP test):** Pharmacopoeia-controlled
> - **Saybolt Color:** +25 or higher
> - **Acidity/Alkalinity:** Within USP/IP limits
> - **Ash Content:** ≤ 0.05 % w/w

> [!TIP]
> **Why are these products grouped together in KTU Module 4?**
> Both liquid paraffin and petroleum jelly are **deeply refined specialty products** derived from the **lube-oil refining train** (vacuum distillation → solvent extraction → dewaxing → hydrofinishing → clay/adsorbent treatment). The dewaxing step, in particular, simultaneously generates **slack wax → petroleum jelly** as the bottom stream and **dewaxed oil → liquid paraffin feedstock** as the top stream.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Feedstock Selection

The starting material for both products is the **residual lube oil fraction** obtained from the vacuum distillation column (VAC) of a refinery:

| Stream | Source | Typical Boiling Range (°C) | Primary Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Neutral Light Lube (NL) | Atmospheric residue → VAC | 350–425 | Light liquid paraffin base |
| Neutral Heavy Lube (NH) | VAC overhead | 425–500 | Heavy liquid paraffin base |
| Bright Stock (BS) | VAC residue | 500–600+ | Petroleum jelly base, grease |
| Slack Wax | Solvent dewaxing | — | Petroleum jelly base, wax feed |

### 2.2 Manufacturing Process of Liquid Paraffin (Stage-by-Stage)

The modern process for producing pharmaceutical-grade liquid paraffin involves a sequence of purification steps. Each step removes a specific class of contaminants:

1. **Vacuum Distillation (VD):** Separates lube distillates from atmospheric residue.
2. **Solvent Extraction (Furfural / NMP / Phenol):** Removes aromatics, polar compounds, and heteroatoms (S, N, O).
3. **Solvent Dewaxing (MEK / Toluene / Propane):** Crystallizes and removes n-paraffins (wax) to depress pour point.
4. **Hydrofinishing (Hydrotreating):** Uses H₂ over Ni-Mo or Pt catalysts to saturate remaining aromatics, remove S/N, and improve color stability.
5. **Acid Treatment (Oleum / Sulfuric Acid):** A traditional but still-used polishing step for very high-purity grades.
6. **Clay/Activated Earth Percolation (Contact Filtration):** Final polishing to remove polar color bodies and trace acids → produces **water-white** oil.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two Principal Routes to Liquid Paraffin:**
> - **Route 1 (Pharma/Food):** Acid + Clay route — uses H₂SO₄ (oleum) and activated bauxite. Yields exceed **95 %** of feedstock.
> - **Route 2 (Cosmetic/Industrial):** Hydrofinishing + Clay route — uses catalytic hydrogenation. Higher CAPEX, lower OPEX, no acid sludge, environmentally preferred.

### 2.3 Manufacturing Process of Petroleum Jelly

Petrolatum is produced as a **co-product** during the solvent dewaxing of lubricating oil fractions. The detailed sequence:

1. **Lube oil distillate** is fed to solvent dewaxing using MEK (methyl ethyl ketone) + toluene mixture.
2. Upon cooling, **macro-crystalline wax** crystallizes and is filtered out.
3. The **dewaxed oil** (filtrate) is distilled to recover solvent.
4. The **slack wax** (filter cake) still contains **15–40 % oil** in its matrix.
5. The slack wax is **de-oiled** in a second-stage crystallizer/filter to yield **hard wax** and **soft petrolatum-grade residue**.
6. The soft residue is **blended** with selected heavy lube fractions to achieve target consistency (penetration).
7. **Hydrofinishing or clay treatment** is applied for color and stability.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Kinematic Viscosity | $\nu = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho}$ | cSt or mm²/s | Quality control |
| Viscosity Index (VI) | $VI = \dfrac{L - U}{L - H} \times 100$ | — | Lube oil quality |
| Saybolt Universal Viscosity | $SUS = 4.632 \cdot \nu_{100°F} \text{ (for } \nu \leq 75 \text{ cSt)}$ | SUS seconds | Industrial grading |
| Cone Penetration | $P = \dfrac{h \times 10}{0.1} = 100 h$ (h in mm) | dmm | Petrolatum consistency |
| Congealing / Drop Point | Experimental (Ring & Ball) | °C | Petrolatum melting |
| Yield of Liquid Paraffin | $Y_{LP} = \dfrac{m_{LP}}{m_{feed}} \times 100$ | % | Process economics |
| Wax Oil Content | $W_{oil} = \dfrac{m_{oil \text{ in wax}}}{m_{slack\ wax}} \times 100$ | % | Petrolatum feed quality |
| Saybolt Color | ASTM D156 (white oils) | +30 scale | Purity indicator |
| Carbonizable Substances | USP <401> test | Acid layer color | Pharmacopoeia compliance |
| Hydrocarbon Type Analysis | ASTM D2140 or D2425 | % CA, CN, CP | Feedstock characterization |
| Kinematic Viscosity Index (KVI) | $KVI = \dfrac{\log H - \log U}{\log L}$ | — | Used when VI < 0 |
| Flash Point (Cleveland OC) | ASTM D92 | °C | Safety & shipping |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition of CA, CN, CP for Liquid Paraffin Feedstock:**
> - **CA** = % Carbon in aromatic rings (must be **near zero** in pharma grade)
> - **CN** = % Carbon in naphthenic rings
> - **CP** = % Carbon in paraffinic chains
> - **CA + CN + CP = 100 %** (ASTM D2140 method)
>
> For pharmaceutical white oil: **CA ≤ 0.5 %**, **S ≤ 10 ppm**, **N ≤ 1 ppm**, **aromatics (UV) within pharmacopoeia limits**.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pharmaceutical industry:** Laxatives (oral LLP), ophthalmic solutions, vaccine adjuvants, ointment bases.
- **Cosmetics:** Baby oils, moisturizers, hair oils, lip balms.
- **Food industry:** Direct food contact (FDA 21 CFR 172.878), dough dividers, candy molds, release agents.
- **Petrochemicals:** Plasticizers, catalyst carriers.
- **Textile/Polymer:** Lubricants for fiber spinning, PVC plasticizer.
- **Petroleum jelly:** Skin barrier cream, wound dressing, corrosion protection for metals, ignition gels, leather conditioning.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example — Process Yield Calculation (Liquid Paraffin)

**Problem Statement (KTU Model Type):**
A petroleum refinery produces **500 tonnes/day** of dewaxed neutral lube distillate (Sp. Gr. 0.880, viscosity 22 cSt at 40 °C). It is fed to an acid–clay plant for the manufacture of pharmaceutical-grade liquid paraffin. The acid treatment removes **3.5 %** of feed as acid sludge, and the subsequent clay percolation removes an additional **1.2 %** of feed as spent clay. Calculate the daily yield of finished liquid paraffin in tonnes and in volume (m³).

**Step 1: Identify the losses sequentially.**

$$
\text{Total loss fraction} = 0.035 + 0.012 = 0.047
$$

**Step 2: Calculate mass yield per day.**

$$
Y_{mass} = 500 \times (1 - 0.047) = 500 \times 0.953 = 476.5 \ \text{tonnes/day}
$$

**Step 3: Convert mass to volume using specific gravity.**

$$
V = \dfrac{m}{\rho} = \dfrac{m}{SG \times \rho_{water}}
$$

For water, $\rho_{water} = 1000 \ \text{kg/m}^3 = 1 \ \text{tonne/m}^3$.

$$
V = \dfrac{476.5}{0.880 \times 1} = 541.48 \ \text{m}^3/\text{day}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic explanation:** The mass-based yield is 95.3 %, but because the product has a specific gravity of 0.880 (less than water), the volume of finished product is **larger** than the feed volume — a counter-intuitive but practically important result for shipping and storage planning.

### 3.2 Worked Example — Viscosity Conversion (SUS to cSt)

**Problem Statement:**
A heavy liquid paraffin has a kinematic viscosity of **65 SUS at 100 °F.** Convert to centistokes (cSt).

**Step 1: Use ASTM D2161 conversion (for ν ≤ 75 cSt).**

$$
SUS = 4.632 \cdot \nu_{cSt}
$$

$$
\nu_{cSt} = \dfrac{SUS}{4.632} = \dfrac{65}{4.632} = 14.03 \ \text{cSt}
$$

**Step 2: Validate using tabulated ASTM data (interpolation if needed).**

For SUS = 65, cSt ≈ **14.0 cSt at 100 °F** — confirms the calculation.

### 3.3 Worked Example — Wax Oil Content and Petrolatum Composition

**Problem Statement:**
A slack wax from a MEK dewaxing unit has a mass of **1000 kg** and contains **28 % oil (by mass)** and **72 % hard wax.** It is sent to a de-oiling filter, which produces:
- Hard wax cake: 70 % of total mass, with 5 % residual oil.
- Petrolatum-grade soft product: 30 % of total mass (the rest of the filtered oil phase).

Calculate the mass of petrolatum produced per batch, its oil content, and verify mass balance.

**Step 1: Calculate hard wax cake mass and its oil content.**

$$
m_{wax\ cake} = 0.70 \times 1000 = 700 \ \text{kg}
$$

$$
m_{oil\ in\ wax\ cake} = 0.05 \times 700 = 35 \ \text{kg}
$$

**Step 2: Mass balance — total oil and wax distribution.**

Total oil in slack wax = 280 kg.
Oil retained in wax cake = 35 kg.
Oil in soft petrolatum phase = 280 − 35 = 245 kg.

Wax distributed: hard wax in cake = 700 − 35 = 665 kg.
Wax in soft phase = (1000 − 700) − 245 = 55 kg.

**Step 3: Calculate mass of petrolatum-grade stream.**

$$
m_{petrolatum\ grade} = 245 + 55 = 300 \ \text{kg}
$$

**Step 4: Calculate oil content (w/w) of the petrolatum.**

$$
W_{oil} = \dfrac{245}{300} \times 100 = 81.67 \ \text{\%}
$$

**Step 5: Wax content of the petrolatum.**

$$
W_{wax} = \dfrac{55}{300} \times 100 = 18.33 \ \text{\%}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Mass Balance Verification:**
> Total oil = 35 + 245 = 280 kg ✓
> Total wax = 665 + 55 = 720 kg ✓
> Total mass = 1000 kg ✓
>
> This 80/20 oil/wax split is the **typical composition range for pharmaceutical petroleum jelly.**

### 3.4 Worked Example — Viscosity Index (VI) Calculation

**Problem Statement:**
A heavy liquid paraffin has:
- Kinematic viscosity at 40 °C: **U = 80 cSt**
- Kinematic viscosity at 100 °C: **100 cSt**

At 100 cSt at 100 °C: from ASTM D2270 tables, **L = 472** and **H = 432**.

Calculate VI.

**Step 1: Apply VI formula.**

$$
VI = \dfrac{L - U}{L - H} \times 100
$$

$$
VI = \dfrac{472 - 80}{472 - 432} \times 100 = \dfrac{392}{40} \times 100 = 980
$$

**Step 2: Interpretation.**

A VI > 100 is a "**High VI oil**" — typical of highly paraffinic, deeply refined white oils. Such oils are valuable for cosmetic and pharmaceutical use because they resist viscosity changes with temperature.

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Light Liquid Paraffin | Heavy Liquid Paraffin | Petroleum Jelly |
|---|---|---|---|
| Physical state at 25 °C | Clear liquid | Viscous liquid | Semi-solid gel |
| Color (Saybolt) | +30 (water white) | +30 (water white) | +25 to +30 |
| Viscosity @ 40 °C (cSt) | 3.5 – 7 | 10 – 75 | — |
| Cone penetration (dmm) | — | — | 100 – 300 |
| Melting point (°C) | — | — | 38 – 60 |
| Flash point (°C) | 150 – 180 | 200 – 240 | 200 – 250 |
| Carbon number range | C15 – C26 | C20 – C40 | C25 – C50+ |
| Primary use | Pharma, food | Cosmetics, industrial | Skin care, barrier |
| USP compliance | Yes | Yes (industrial partially) | Yes (medicinal grade) |
| Production route | Acid–clay / H₂–clay | Acid–clay / H₂–clay | Slack wax de-oiling + blending |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
**Define liquid paraffin. What are the two pharmacopoeial grades, and how do they differ?**

**Model Answer:**
Liquid paraffin is a highly refined mixture of saturated hydrocarbons (paraffinic and naphthenic, predominantly C15–C40), free from aromatics, sulfur, and nitrogen, used in pharmaceutical and industrial applications. The two grades are:
- **Light Liquid Paraffin (LLP):** Lower viscosity (3–7 cSt at 40 °C), used as a laxative and in pharmaceutical formulations.
- **Heavy Liquid Paraffin (HLP):** Higher viscosity (10–75 cSt at 40 °C), used in cosmetics, lubrication, and industrial applications.
The primary difference lies in **viscosity**, **flash point**, and **molecular weight range** — LLP is a lighter distillate cut (≈C15–C26), while HLP is a heavier cut (≈C20–C40).

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
**What is petroleum jelly? Briefly describe its composition and the origin from lube refining.**

**Model Answer:**
Petroleum jelly (petrolatum) is a semi-solid, translucent hydrocarbon gel obtained as a co-product during the **solvent dewaxing of lubricating oil fractions**. It consists of approximately **10–20 % microcrystalline wax** (acting as a structurant/gelling agent) dispersed in **80–90 % heavy liquid hydrocarbons** (acting as the dispersion medium). The wax forms a three-dimensional lattice that traps the oil, giving the product its characteristic consistency, thixotropy, and melting point (38–60 °C).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe with a neat flow diagram the **manufacture of pharmaceutical-grade liquid paraffin** by the **acid–clay route**. List the key quality parameters checked before batch release.

**(b) [7 Marks)** Discuss the role of **hydrofinishing** as an alternative to acid treatment. What are the advantages and limitations of each route?

##### **Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Feedstock:**
Dewaxed, solvent-extracted **neutral lube distillate** (Sp. Gr. 0.85–0.89) is the feed. It must already be low in aromatics and wax.

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Acid Treatment:**
The feed is charged to a **stirred acid reactor** at 30–60 °C. **98 % oleum (H₂SO₄ + SO₃)** or fuming sulfuric acid is added in 3–5 stages, each followed by settling.
- The acid reacts with **aromatics, olefins, and polar compounds**, polymerizing them.
- The **acid sludge** (lower layer) is drained, settled, and disposed of via cement kilns or acid regeneration plants.

**Step 3 (1 mark) — Neutralization and Washing:**
The acid-treated oil is washed with **dilute NaOH** to remove residual acidity, followed by **water washing** with demineralized water to remove salts.

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Drying:**
Vacuum dehydration (or hot air stripping) removes dissolved water.

**Step 5 (1 mark) — Clay Percolation:**
The dried oil is passed through a column of **activated bauxite or fuller's earth** at 80–120 °C. Clay adsorbs remaining polar color bodies, trace acids, and unstable compounds.

**Step 6 (1 mark) — Quality Release:**
Released only upon meeting the following parameters:
- **Saybolt color: +30 (water white)**
- **UV absorbance (260–350 nm):** within pharmacopoeia limits
- **Carbonizable substances test (USP):** passes
- **Kinematic viscosity:** within grade spec
- **Flash point:** ≥ 150 °C
- **Acidity/alkalinity:** neutral
- **Sulfur content:** ≤ 10 ppm

##### **Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Hydrofinishing (Catalytic Hydrogenation):**

The dewaxed oil is heated to **250–350 °C** and passed with **hydrogen gas** over a fixed bed of **Ni-Mo or Ni-W catalyst** (sometimes with Pt for food-grade). Pressure: **30–100 bar.** Space velocity: 0.5–2 h⁻¹. H₂/oil ratio: 100–500 Nm³/m³.

**Reactions:**
- Aromatics → Naphthenes (saturation)
- Sulfur → H₂S (removed in scrubber)
- Nitrogen → NH₃
- Olefins → Paraffins

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Acid–Clay Route | Hydrofinishing Route |
|---|---|---|
| CAPEX | Lower | Higher (H₂ plant, high-pressure reactor) |
| OPEX | Higher (acid sludge disposal) | Lower (no sludge) |
| Environmental | Acid sludge issue | Clean (H₂S → sulfur plant) |
| Color quality | Excellent (water white) | Excellent |
| UV absorbance | Sometimes requires polishing | Very low directly |
| Suitability for food/pharma | Yes (traditional) | Yes (preferred modern) |
| Yield | 95–97 % | 98–99 % |
| Operating cost driver | Acid + clay cost | H₂ + catalyst + utilities |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Modern refineries prefer **hydrofinishing** for environmental and yield reasons, while some specialty plants retain the **acid–clay route** for highest-purity, color-stable white oils.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Alternative]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **manufacture of petroleum jelly** from lube distillate feed. Discuss the role of **slack wax** and the **de-oiling step.**

**(b) [7 Marks]** What are the critical **quality parameters** of petroleum jelly? Explain the **cone penetration test** and how it relates to product consistency.

##### **Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Lube distillate (NL or NH) is fed to **solvent dewaxing** using **MEK + toluene** (typical 60:40 ratio). The mixture is cooled stepwise from 40 °C to – 20 °C in **scraped-surface chillers.**

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Wax crystals form and are filtered using **rotary drum filters.** The filter cake is **slack wax** (≈ 15–40 % oil).

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Slack wax is fed to a **de-oiling crystallizer/filter** (second stage). Cooling to – 15 °C to – 25 °C separates hard wax from a soft, oil-rich stream.

**Step 4 (1 mark):** The **soft stream** from de-oiling is the **petrolatum-grade feedstock.** It contains ~ 80 % oil and ~ 20 % microcrystalline wax — ideal for jelly consistency.

**Step 5 (1 mark):** The soft petrolatum is **blended** with selected heavy lube fractions to achieve target cone penetration (100–300 dmm).

**Step 6 (1 mark):** The blend is sent to **hydrofinishing** (or clay treatment) to improve color, odor, and oxidative stability.

**Step 7 (1 mark):** The finished petroleum jelly is **quality tested** and dispatched in drums or pails.

##### **Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Critical Quality Parameters (3 marks):**

1. **Cone Penetration (ASTM D937):** Measures consistency.
2. **Drop Point (ASTM D127):** Indicates melting behavior.
3. **Color (Lovibond, Saybolt):** Purity indicator.
4. **UV Absorbance:** USP test for polycyclic aromatics (limited for medicinal grade).
5. **Acidity/Alkalinity, Ash, Moisture, Sulfated Ash.**
6. **Odor and Appearance (homogeneous, smooth, no separation).**

**Cone Penetration Test — Detailed (4 marks):**

- A standard **cone of 102.5 g mass** is released onto the **petrolatum surface at 25 °C for 5 seconds.**
- The **depth of penetration in tenths of a millimetre (dmm)** is the penetration value.
- **Lower penetration = harder jelly**; **higher penetration = softer jelly.**
- Typical pharmaceutical grade: **100–200 dmm**; **industrial grade: 200–300 dmm.**
- The test is also run **at 0 °C** and **at 37 °C** to study **consistency–temperature behavior**, critical for **cosmetic spreadability** and **pharmaceutical ointment use.**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Liquid Paraffin & Petroleum Jelly:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse the two products' processes.** Liquid paraffin comes from the **dewaxed oil (DWO) top stream**; petroleum jelly comes from the **slack wax bottom stream** of the same dewaxing step. Examiners frequently deduct marks if students interchange them.
> 2. **Viscosity is NOT viscosity index.** A common error: students report viscosity in cSt as if it were VI. Always report viscosity with **temperature specification** (e.g., "22 cSt at 40 °C").
> 3. **Cone penetration must include units (dmm)** and **test conditions (25 °C, 100 g, 5 s).** Marks are lost if any of these are omitted.
> 4. **Petroleum jelly melting point is 38–60 °C, NOT above 100 °C.** Do not confuse with hard wax melting points.
> 5. **Acid–clay route is NOT obsolete** — it is still preferred for some ultra-high-purity grades. Do not present it as "outdated" in KTU answers.
> 6. **Yield calculations must use mass balance, not single-step %** — for multi-step processes, **multiply (1 – loss fraction) for each step sequentially.**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 4 (Liquid Paraffin & Petroleum Jellies)**

- **Liquid Paraffin = highly refined lube oil** (predominantly C15–C40, saturated hydrocarbons, near-zero aromatics).
- **Two grades:** Light (LLP, pharma) and Heavy (HLP, cosmetic/industrial), differing mainly in **viscosity** and **boiling range**.
- **Petroleum Jelly = gel of microcrystalline wax (~15–20 %) in heavy oil (~80–85 %)**, with melting point **38–60 °C.**
- **Both products originate from the lube refining train:** vacuum distillation → solvent extraction → solvent dewaxing → finishing.
- **Critical process step:** **Solvent dewaxing** — yields dewaxed oil (DWO) for liquid paraffin and slack wax for petroleum jelly.
- **Manufacturing routes for liquid paraffin:** (1) Acid–clay and (2) Hydrofinishing–clay.
- **Manufacturing of petrolatum:** Slack wax → de-oiling → blending → hydrofinishing/clay → finished.
- **Key quality tests for LLP/HLP:** Saybolt color (+30), UV absorbance (USP), carbonizable substances, kinematic viscosity, flash point, acidity, ash.
- **Key quality tests for petrolatum:** Cone penetration (100–300 dmm), drop point (38–60 °C), color, UV absorbance, ash, odor, homogeneity.
- **Hydrocarbon type analysis** (ASTM D2140): CA + CN + CP = 100 %; pharmaceutical grade demands **CA ≤ 0.5 %**.
- **Hydrofinishing advantages:** no acid sludge, higher yield, eco-friendly, lower UV absorbance.
- **Acid–clay advantages:** lower CAPEX, deep color, traditional reliability.
- **Cone penetration formula (basics):** $P = 100 \times h$ where $h$ is penetration depth in mm.
- **Viscosity conversion:** $SUS = 4.632 \times \nu_{cSt}$ for $\nu \leq 75$ cSt.
- **Mass balance in multi-step processes:** multiply $(1 - \text{loss fraction})$ sequentially across all steps.
- **Applications:** Pharma (laxatives, ointments, vaccines), cosmetics (baby oil, lotions), food (release agents), industrial (lubrication, plasticizers, corrosion protection).
- **Pharmacopoeia standards:** USP, IP, BP, EP — all limit UV absorbance, heavy metals, and polynuclear aromatics.
- **Environmental note:** Acid sludge disposal is the chief environmental challenge; modern refineries shift to hydrofinishing.
